## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Intuition

Imagine you purchase a **ceiling fan** in Kerala for the humid summer months.

| Stage | Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Quality of Design** | The engineering team decides the fan will run on **75 W**, deliver an airflow of **230 CMM**, and have a sweep of **1200 mm**. These numbers form the *design intent*. |
| **Quality of Conformance** | The factory actually produces each fan with a power draw between **73 W – 77 W** (very close to the 75 W target). The product *conforms* to design. |
| **Quality of Performance** | You install the fan. It runs quietly, cools the room effectively, the regulator works smoothly, and it lasts for **5+ years without failure**. The product *performs*. |

**Intuition Summary:** A product can be perfectly designed and perfectly built (good conformance), yet still fail in *Quality of Performance* if it is the wrong design for the customer, the wrong voltage, the wrong size, or the wrong feature set.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Memory Hook for KTU Viva:**
> *Design = Plan, Conformance = Build, Performance = Use.*

---

## 1.3 The Three Views of Quality — KTU Foundation Block

In the KTU 2024 Scheme SQC syllabus (PEPET415, Module 1), quality is introduced through three interacting components:

1. **Quality of Design (QOD):** The set of specifications that translate customer needs into measurable engineering targets. Governed by R&D, marketing, and product engineering.
2. **Quality of Performance (QOP):** The actual field behavior of the product when used by the customer. Measured by reliability, durability, uptime, and customer satisfaction scores.
3. **Quality of Conformance (QOC):** The statistical closeness of the manufactured output to the design specifications. This is the primary domain of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** — control charts, acceptance sampling, and process capability studies.

The relationship can be summarized as:

> **Customer-Perceived Quality ≈ f (Quality of Design, Quality of Performance, Quality of Conformance)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Fold Decomposition of Quality

### 2.1.1 Quality of Design (QOD)

- Established during the **product development phase**.
- Determines the **target values** ($T$) and **tolerance limits** (USL, LSL).
- Driven by **marketing research, customer requirements, and cost constraints**.
- A **low QOD** cannot be compensated for by excellent conformance or performance.
- Example: Designing an entry-level two-wheeler engine to deliver a fuel efficiency of **65 kmpl** instead of the market benchmark of **55 kmpl**.

### 2.1.2 Quality of Conformance (QOC)

- Concerns the **manufacturing process** itself.
- Measured by indices such as the **Process Capability Index** ($C_p$) and **Process Performance Index** ($P_p$).
- The statistical backbone of SQC: a process with $C_p \geq 1.33$ is considered capable, while $C_p \geq 1.67$ denotes a Six-Sigma-class process.

### 2.1.3 Quality of Performance (QOP)

- Evaluated **post-production**, often under real-world conditions.
- Depends on:
  - **Reliability** — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
  - **Durability** — Useful life until replacement.
  - **Serviceability** — Mean Time To Repair (MTTR).
  - **Safety** — Probability of catastrophic failure.
- Influenced by both QOD and QOC, but also by **uncontrollable environmental factors** (temperature, humidity, dust, usage pattern).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The interplay of QOD, QOP, and QOC is the **single most-tested conceptual question** in Module 1 of PEPET415.

---

## 2.2 Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality

**David A. Garvin** (Harvard Business School, 1987) defined eight critical dimensions that comprehensively describe **Quality of Performance** from the customer's viewpoint. These are essential for KTU descriptive answers.

| # | Dimension | Definition | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics of the product. | Top speed of a Royal Enfield motorcycle = **120 km/h**. |
| 2 | **Features** | Secondary characteristics that supplement basic function. | Bluetooth connectivity in a smart TV. |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation over a specified period. | Laptop keyboard rated for **10 million keystrokes**. |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Degree to which the product meets pre-established standards. | Bolt diameter within $\pm 0.05$ mm of nominal. |
| 5 | **Durability** | Length of useful life before replacement becomes necessary. | Refrigerator compressor life of **10+ years**. |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Speed, ease, and courtesy of repair and maintenance. | Average MTTR of an ATM machine = **2 hours**. |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal — look, feel, sound, taste, smell. | Premium matte finish on a smartphone. |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Indirect quality assessment from brand image, advertising. | A "Bosch" tool vs an unbranded tool. |

---

## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Linking Performance to Money

**Genichi Taguchi** argued that any deviation from the target value incurs a loss to society. The **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** is a cornerstone formula in SQC for measuring the cost of poor performance.

### 2.3.1 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = Quality loss in monetary units (₹) when the quality characteristic takes value $x$.
- $m$ = Target value (ideal design specification).
- $x$ = Actual measured value.
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (a constant in ₹/unit²).

The constant $k$ is determined from specification limits:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

Where:
- $A_0$ = Total loss at the functional tolerance limit (₹).
- $\Delta_0$ = Functional tolerance — typically $\vert m - USL \vert$ or $\vert m - LSL \vert$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the squared term?** Because loss grows **quadratically** as the product moves away from target — a small miss costs a little, a large miss costs disproportionately more. This is a frequent KTU 2-mark conceptual question.

### 2.3.2 Categories of Quality Costs

| Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs (P)** | Money spent to *avoid* defects. | Training, quality planning, supplier capability audits. |
| **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Money spent on *detection* of defects. | Inspection, testing, calibration, sorting. |
| **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** | Costs of defects found *before* shipment. | Scrap, rework, re-testing, production delays. |
| **External Failure Costs (EFC)** | Costs of defects found *after* shipment. | Warranty claims, recalls, legal liability, brand damage. |

The **Total Cost of Quality (TCQ)** is the sum of all four categories:

$$TCQ = P + A + IFC + EFC$$

As QOP improves, **IFC and EFC fall sharply**, but **P and A may rise**. The optimal strategy minimizes the sum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The optimal COQ point is **not** at zero defects, but at the point where the marginal increase in prevention/appraisal cost equals the marginal decrease in failure cost.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$ | Taguchi Quality Loss Function | 2 marks |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$ | Loss coefficient | 2 marks |
| 3 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index | 3 marks |
| 4 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability (shift-adjusted) | 3 marks |
| 5 | $TCQ = P + A + IFC + EFC$ | Total Cost of Quality | 2 marks |
| 6 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Performance / Serviceability ratio | 2 marks |
| 7 | $\text{Six-Sigma Level} = 3 C_{pk}$ | Process excellence benchmark | 2 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> All $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) symbols are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command to preserve the markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Performance testing of I.C. engines, pumps, gearboxes.
- **Production / Manufacturing Engineering:** SQC directly links to QOP via capability studies.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength performance, structural reliability.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Transformer efficiency, UPS performance, signal-to-noise ratio.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Ergonomic performance of human-machine systems.
- **Reliability Engineering:** MTBF and MTTR analysis bridges QOP with field-failure data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Taguchi's Quality Loss Function from Specification Limits

### Statement to Prove

For a quality characteristic $X$ with target value $m$ and a functional tolerance $\Delta_0$, the quadratic loss function is given by:

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2, \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the boundary conditions.**
At the functional tolerance limit, the deviation is $\vert x - m \vert = \Delta_0$, and the associated loss to society is $A_0$ (e.g., the cost of replacing the product under warranty).

$$L(x) \Big\vert_{x = m + \Delta_0} = A_0$$

**Step 2 — Assume a quadratic loss form.**
Taguchi postulated that loss grows as the *square of the deviation* from target. This is physically reasonable because the customer's dissatisfaction accelerates non-linearly as the product deviates from the ideal.

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition from Step 1.**

$$k(\Delta_0)^2 = A_0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $k$.**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $k$ back into the loss function.**

$$L(x) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot (x - m)^2$$

This is the final form used in every KTU textbook problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $L(x) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot (x - m)^2$. The loss is **zero at the target** and grows **parabolically** as $x$ moves away from $m$ — a symmetrical quadratic about the target.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Process Capability Index ($C_p$)

### Statement to Derive

The $C_p$ index measures the inherent capability of a process to produce output within specification limits, assuming the process mean is centered on the target.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the process spread.**
For a stable process, the natural spread of a quality characteristic under the **3-sigma rule** is:

$$\text{Process Spread} = 6\sigma$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the quality characteristic.

**Step 2 — Define the specification spread.**

$$\text{Specification Spread} = USL - LSL$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Spread}}{\text{Process Spread}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- $C_p = 1.00 \Rightarrow$ Process spread exactly equals specification spread.
- $C_p = 1.33 \Rightarrow$ Process is **capable** (margin = 4σ on each side).
- $C_p = 1.67 \Rightarrow$ Process is **excellent** (margin = 5σ on each side).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — $C_{pk}$ for Off-Center Processes

When the process mean $\mu$ shifts away from the midpoint of the specification limits, the capability deteriorates on one side.

**Step 1 — Compute the upper-side capability.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lower-side capability.**

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — The actual capability is the lesser of the two (the *bottleneck side*).**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute both expressions.**

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** If a process has $C_p = 1.5$ but the mean is shifted, $C_{pk}$ can be **less than 1.0** — making it a *not-capable* process despite high $C_p$. Always check both $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Taguchi's Loss Function

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A manufacturer produces a steel shaft. The target diameter is $m = 50.00$ mm. The functional tolerance is $\Delta_0 = 0.50$ mm. The cost of replacing a defective shaft (loss at the tolerance limit) is $A_0 = ₹400$. A specific shaft is measured to have a diameter of $x = 50.30$ mm. Calculate the quality loss.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the loss coefficient $k$.**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{400}{(0.50)^2} = \frac{400}{0.25} = 1600 \text{ ₹/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deviation from target.**

$$x - m = 50.30 - 50.00 = 0.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the loss function.**

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2 = 1600 \times (0.30)^2$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the square.**

$$(0.30)^2 = 0.09$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication.**

$$L(x) = 1600 \times 0.09 = ₹144$$

**Conclusion:** Even though the shaft is still within the $\pm 0.50$ mm tolerance, a deviation of 0.30 mm causes a loss of **₹144 per shaft** to society. Had the shaft been at $x = 50.50$ mm (the tolerance limit), the loss would be the full $A_0 = ₹400$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Taguchi's framework proves that *being inside the specification limits does NOT mean zero loss*. This is a 7-mark question every year.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Quality View

| Scenario | Dominant Quality View | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| A car factory is producing engines with very high defect rates, even though the design is excellent. | **Quality of Conformance** | The manufacturing process is poor. Apply SQC charts. |
| Customers complain that a phone's battery lasts only 4 hours, although the spec sheet says 10 hours. | **Quality of Performance** | Real-world performance is substandard despite conformance. |
| A startup designs a budget EV with range = 80 km, but customers want 200 km. | **Quality of Design** | The design itself is misaligned with customer needs. |
| An aerospace fastener passes inspection but cracks after 100 flight cycles. | **Quality of Performance** (reliability) | Field reliability is poor. |
| A textile mill's looms produce 5% defective fabric. | **Quality of Conformance** | Out-of-control process. Apply $p$-chart. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — The Three-View Quality Framework

This diagram maps the interactions between **Quality of Design**, **Quality of Performance**, and **Quality of Conformance** as a single functional architecture, suitable for reproduction in KTU answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Customer Needs and Market Voice] --> A2[Marketing Research and Voice of Customer VOC]
    A2 --> A3[Quality of Design QOD - Engineering Specifications and Target Values]
    A3 --> A4[Process Planning and Tooling Design]
    A4 --> A5[Manufacturing Process]
    A5 --> A6{Statistical Process Control SPC}
    A6 -->|In Control| A7[Quality of Conformance QOC - Meets Specifications]
    A6 -->|Out of Control| A8[Defect Detection and Rework]
    A8 --> A5
    A7 --> A9[Finished Product Release]
    A9 --> A10[Field Usage and Customer Environment]
    A10 --> A11{Quality of Performance QOP Evaluation}
    A11 -->|Satisfactory| A12[Customer Satisfaction and Brand Loyalty]
    A11 -->|Unsatisfactory| A13[Customer Complaints and Warranty Claims]
    A13 --> A14[Feedback Loop to Quality of Design]
    A14 --> A3
    A12 --> A15[Continuous Improvement Kaizen]
    A15 --> A3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **upper loop** represents *design-to-production* (QOD → QOC).
- The **right loop** represents *production-to-customer* (QOC → QOP).
- The **bottom feedback arc** represents the *continuous improvement cycle* that drives redesign.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Garvin's Eight Dimensions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Quality of Performance QOP] --> B1[1. Performance Primary Function]
    B0 --> B2[2. Features Supplemental Functions]
    B0 --> B3[3. Reliability Failure Free Operation]
    B0 --> B4[4. Conformance to Standards]
    B0 --> B5[5. Durability Useful Life]
    B0 --> B6[6. Serviceability Ease of Repair]
    B0 --> B7[7. Aesthetics Sensory Appeal]
    B0 --> B8[8. Perceived Quality Brand Image]
    B1 --> B9[Measured by Engineering Tests]
    B2 --> B10[Measured by Feature Checklist]
    B3 --> B11[Measured by MTBF]
    B4 --> B12[Measured by C sub p and C sub pk]
    B5 --> B13[Measured by Life Cycle Tests]
    B6 --> B14[Measured by MTTR]
    B7 --> B15[Measured by Customer Surveys]
    B8 --> B16[Measured by Market Research]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Cost of Quality Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Prevention Cost P] --> C4[Total Cost of Quality TCQ]
    C2[Appraisal Cost A] --> C4
    C3[Internal Failure Cost IFC] --> C4
    C5[External Failure Cost EFC] --> C4
    C4 --> C6{Is TCQ Minimized?}
    C6 -->|Yes| C7[Optimal Quality Investment]
    C6 -->|No| C8[Shift Spending from Failure to Prevention]
    C8 --> C1
    C8 --> C2
    C7 --> C9[Improved Quality of Performance QOP]
```

---

## 4.4 Mapping of Quality Gurus to Quality of Performance

| Guru | Primary Contribution | Direct Link to QOP |
|---|---|---|
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | Statistical Process Control charts (1924). | Foundation of **QOC**, indirectly supporting QOP. |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management, PDCA cycle, System of Profound Knowledge. | Drives **continuous improvement** in QOP. |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Planning, Control, Improvement trilogy; *Fitness for Use*. | Directly defines QOP. |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (TQC) — integrated organizational approach. | Holistic improvement of all three quality views. |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles, Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram. | Field-driven improvement of QOP via worker participation. |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | *Quality is Free*; concept of **Zero Defects**; **4 Absolutes of Quality**. | QOC focus that ultimately improves QOP. |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Quality Loss Function, Robust Design, off-line quality control. | Mathematical quantification of QOP losses. |
| **Shigeo Shingo** | Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), Single-Minute Exchange of Die (SMED). | Improves QOC and reduces failures, hence QOP. |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Quality of Performance" as applied to industrial products. How is it different from "Quality of Conformance"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Quality of Performance (QOP)** refers to the degree to which a product successfully performs its intended function under real operating conditions, throughout its useful life. It is a **customer-oriented** measure that captures reliability, durability, serviceability, and overall fitness for use.
>
> **Quality of Conformance (QOC)**, on the other hand, is a **manufacturing-oriented** measure. It quantifies how closely the actual manufactured output adheres to the engineering specifications set during the design phase. QOC is monitored through statistical tools such as control charts and process capability indices.
>
> **Key difference:** QOC is assessed **inside the factory** (incoming, in-process, and final inspection), whereas QOP is assessed **outside the factory** (in the hands of the customer). A product may have excellent QOC but poor QOP if the design itself is inadequate for the customer's environment.

*(Examiner's note: Award full marks only if the student mentions both factory vs customer perspective AND gives at least one example.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State and explain Taguchi's Quality Loss Function. Why is the loss function quadratic and not linear?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function** is given by:
>
> $$L(x) = k(x - m)^2$$
>
> where $L(x)$ is the monetary loss to society when the quality characteristic takes value $x$, $m$ is the target value, and $k$ is the loss coefficient.
>
> The function is **quadratic** (not linear) for two reasons:
> 1. **Physical reasoning:** Customer dissatisfaction, warranty exposure, and field-failure costs do not rise linearly with deviation — they accelerate. A small miss costs a little, a large miss is catastrophic.
> 2. **Symmetry:** Squaring ensures that losses are identical for equal deviations in either direction (above or below target), which matches observed customer behavior.
>
> At the **target** ($x = m$), the loss is **zero**. As the deviation increases, the loss grows parabolically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the three-fold view of Quality: Quality of Design, Quality of Performance, and Quality of Conformance. Illustrate with a suitable engineering example.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
According to Juran, industrial quality can be decomposed into three interrelated components. The overall customer-perceived quality is the result of how well all three are managed simultaneously.

**2. Quality of Design (2 marks):**
- Established during the **product development** stage.
- Translates *voice of customer* into measurable engineering targets (nominal value, tolerances).
- Driven by R&D, marketing, and product engineering.
- A low QOD cannot be repaired later — no amount of inspection or rework will fix a bad design.
- **Example:** Designing an air-conditioner to deliver cooling capacity of 1.5 TR at an ambient of 45 °C.

**3. Quality of Conformance (2 marks):**
- Concerns the **manufacturing process** itself.
- Measured by the **Process Capability Index** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
- Statistical Quality Control charts ($\bar{X}$–$R$, $p$, $c$) are the primary monitoring tools.
- **Example:** A factory produces 1.5 TR AC units where the actual cooling capacity lies between 1.45 TR and 1.55 TR — the product conforms to the design tolerance of $\pm 0.05$ TR.

**4. Quality of Performance (2 marks):**
- Assessed in the **field**, under actual customer conditions.
- Captures reliability (MTBF), durability, serviceability (MTTR), and overall customer satisfaction.
- Influenced by QOD, QOC, and external factors (humidity, dust, voltage fluctuations, usage pattern).
- **Example:** The same AC unit, when installed in a coastal Kerala home, cools the room from 35 °C to 24 °C within 15 minutes and runs without failure for 7 years.

**5. Conclusion:** The three views are interlinked — QOD sets the goal, QOC ensures the goal is met during production, and QOP validates that the goal was indeed the right one.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality with one engineering example for each dimension.**

**Model Answer (1 mark for the list, 0.5 mark for each definition + 0.25 mark for example, totaling 6 marks, plus 0.5 for a concluding line):**

| # | Dimension | Definition | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics. | A 1.5 TR split AC cools a 150 sq. ft. room to 24 °C. |
| 2 | **Features** | Secondary characteristics. | Wi-Fi enabled inverter AC with smartphone control. |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation. | Compressor MTBF rated at **50,000 hours**. |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Degree of meeting specifications. | Voltage within $\pm 10\%$ of 230 V. |
| 5 | **Durability** | Length of useful life. | Copper condenser coil life of **10+ years**. |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Ease of repair and maintenance. | Annual service time = **30 minutes** with tool-free panel access. |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal. | Matte white finish with hidden LED display. |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Brand-driven indirect quality. | "Daikin" or "Voltas" badge on the unit. |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Garvin's framework gives a holistic, multi-dimensional view of Quality of Performance that goes beyond mere specifications, encompassing subjective and objective attributes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Define Quality Cost. Explain the four categories of quality costs with examples. Illustrate with a simple numerical example showing how Total Cost of Quality (TCQ) varies with prevention spending.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Quality Cost (or Cost of Quality, COQ) is the total expenditure incurred by an organization in preventing, detecting, and rectifying defects, including costs arising from both internal and external failures.

**2. Four Categories (4 marks, 1 each):**
- **Prevention Costs (P):** Cost of activities to *avoid* defects. Examples: quality training, supplier audits, process design, FMEA, preventive maintenance.
- **Appraisal Costs (A):** Cost of activities to *detect* defects. Examples: incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC, calibration of measuring instruments.
- **Internal Failure Costs (IFC):** Cost of defects found *before* delivery. Examples: scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime due to defects.
- **External Failure Costs (EFC):** Cost of defects found *after* delivery. Examples: warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal liability, loss of goodwill.

**3. Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**

Suppose a company spends the following (in lakhs ₹) under three scenarios:

| Spending Level | P | A | IFC | EFC | TCQ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Prevention** | 0.5 | 1.0 | 4.0 | 6.0 | **11.5** |
| **Moderate Prevention** | 2.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | **8.0** |
| **High Prevention** | 5.0 | 2.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | **8.5** |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The optimal COQ lies at the *moderate* prevention level, where TCQ is minimized. Excessive prevention increases P and A faster than the savings in IFC and EFC.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain Taguchi's Quality Loss Function. A bolt has a target diameter of 12.00 mm. The functional tolerance is 0.40 mm, and the cost of replacement at the tolerance limit is ₹200. Compute the quality loss for a bolt of diameter 12.18 mm. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the loss function formula (1 mark).**

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2, \quad k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the loss coefficient $k$ (1 mark).**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{200}{(0.40)^2} = \frac{200}{0.16} = 1250 \text{ ₹/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the deviation (1 mark).**

$$x - m = 12.18 - 12.00 = 0.18 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the loss (1 mark).**

$$L(x) = 1250 \times (0.18)^2 = 1250 \times 0.0324 = ₹40.50$$

**Step 5 — Comment (3 marks):**
- The bolt is **within the specification limit** ($\pm 0.40$ mm), so by traditional **Quality of Conformance** it would be considered *acceptable*.
- However, by Taguchi's framework, even a 0.18 mm deviation causes a real loss of **₹40.50** to society — in the form of slightly looser fit, reduced fatigue life, and downstream customer dissatisfaction.
- The full loss of $A_0 = ₹200$ would occur only at $x = 12.40$ mm or $x = 11.60$ mm.
- **Implication:** Taguchi's loss function penalizes *all* deviations, not just out-of-spec ones. It motivates manufacturers to *center the process on target* rather than merely keep it within limits — the foundation of **Robust Design**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in PEPET415 Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Confusing QOP with QOC:** Students frequently write "Quality of Performance = how well the product meets specifications." That is **QOC**, not QOP. QOP is *field performance*, not *factory conformance*.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the unit in $k$:** The loss coefficient $k$ has units of ₹/mm² (or whatever the quality characteristic is). Always mention units — losing 0.5 marks per occurrence.
> 3. **Mixing up Garvin's dimensions:** Reliability and Durability are *not* the same. Reliability is failure-free over a *period*; Durability is *total useful life*. Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 4. **Linear vs Quadratic Loss:** Writing $L(x) = k \cdot \vert x - m \vert$ (linear) instead of $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$ (quadratic) is a frequent error. Taguchi's function is **always quadratic**.
> 5. **Omitting the conclusion line:** A 7-mark answer without a concluding line is typically docked **0.5 to 1 mark**. Always end with a one-line summary tying the concept back to Quality of Performance.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** For $C_p$ problems, students often forget to mention that the process is *in statistical control* and *normally distributed*. Examiners check this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality of Performance (QOP)** = how well a product works in the customer's hands.
- **Quality of Design (QOD)** = how well the product is *engineered* on paper.
- **Quality of Conformance (QOC)** = how well the *manufactured* product matches the design.
- **Juran's definition:** Quality = *Fitness for Use*.
- **Garvin's 8 dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$ where $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$.
- The loss function is **quadratic**, **symmetric about the target**, and **zero at the target**.
- **Cost of Quality (TCQ) = P + A + IFC + EFC** — optimize, do not maximize.
- **Process Capability:** $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$; $C_{pk}$ accounts for mean shift.
- **Six-Sigma benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ implies **Six-Sigma quality**.
- **Reliability metric:** MTBF; **Serviceability metric:** MTTR; **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- **Quality Gurus (must memorize for KTU):** Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Crosby, Taguchi, Shingo.
- **Taguchi's insight:** Being *within* specification does **not** mean zero loss — center the process on target.
- **Customer-orientation:** QOP is fundamentally judged by the customer, not by the factory.
- **Continuous improvement (Kaizen)** links QOP back to QOD through a feedback loop — the basis of Total Quality Management (TQM).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Quality Function**, as conceptualized by **Dr. Genichi Taguchi**, is a quadratic mathematical model that quantifies the economic **loss to society** incurred due to any deviation of a product's quality characteristic from its **target (nominal) value**. It is formally termed the **Taguchi Loss Function (TLF)**.

Mathematically, the Quality Function is expressed as:

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^2$$

Where:
- $L(y)$ = **Loss Function** — the monetary or societal loss when the quality characteristic takes the value $y$
- $y$ = **Actual value** of the quality characteristic produced
- $m$ = **Target (nominal) value** of the quality characteristic
- $k$ = **Quality Loss Coefficient** (a proportionality constant measured in currency² per unit, e.g., ₹/mm² or $/V²)

The Quality Function is a cornerstone of **off-line quality control** — it is applied at the **product and process design stage** rather than during manufacturing inspection.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Steering Wheel of a Car**

Imagine you are driving a car on a perfectly straight highway. Your target is to keep the steering wheel **exactly centered** (deviation = 0).

- **Zero Deviation:** Steering is perfect → No wear on tires, no stress on the driver, no fuel waste → **Zero loss**.
- **Slight Deviation (steering slightly left):** The car drifts. The driver must constantly correct → Slight tire wear, slight fatigue → **Small but non-zero loss**.
- **Large Deviation (sharply turned):** The car swerves dangerously → Severe tire wear, high fuel consumption, accident risk → **Massive loss**.

**Key Insight:** Taguchi observed that this is true for *every* quality characteristic. A bolt that is "just slightly off" from the target diameter does **not** produce zero loss — it produces a small loss. Only the **exact target value** yields zero loss. This contradicts the traditional "**Acceptance Sampling**" view, which considers everything within specification limits as "good" (loss = 0) and only "rejects" items outside the limits (loss = full cost).

---

## 1.3 The Three Categories of Quality Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Classification — Memorize This!**

| # | Characteristic Type | Symbol | Target Behavior | Loss Function Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nominal-the-Best (NTB)** | $\mathcal{N}$ | Target is a specific central value | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ |
| 2 | **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | $\mathcal{S}$ | Target is zero; any positive value is bad | $L(y) = k y^2$ |
| 3 | **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | $\mathcal{L}$ | Target is infinity; any value is acceptable, but larger is better | $L(y) = k / y^2$ |

---

## 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics Used with the Quality Function:**
> - **Tolerance (Δ):** The maximum allowable deviation from target, $m \pm \Delta$, specified by the customer.
> - **Specification Limits (LSL, USL):** Lower and Upper Specification Limits, typically $m \pm \Delta$.
> - **Average Loss per unit ($\bar{L}$):** $\bar{L} = k(\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2)$ — the expected monetary loss per item.
> - **Loss Coefficient ($k$):** $k = A_0 / \Delta^2$, where $A_0$ is the average loss at the specification limit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophical Foundation — Why a "Quality Function"?

Traditional Western SQC philosophy (Deming, Shewhart, Juran) classified products as either **"conforming"** (within specifications) or **"non-conforming"** (outside specifications). Under this binary view:

- A product at the **target** value → Loss = 0
- A product at the **specification limit** → Loss = 0
- A product **beyond** the specification limit → Loss = Replacement cost

**Taguchi's revolutionary insight (1978):** This is economically wrong. A product exactly at the specification limit already causes harm (poor performance, shorter life, customer dissatisfaction). Loss increases **smoothly and quadratically** as deviation grows — there is no "step" at the specification limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Axiom:** *"The loss to society from a product's functional variation is approximately proportional to the square of the deviation of its functional characteristic from the target value."*

---

## 2.2 The Three Operational Forms of the Quality Function

### 2.2.1 Nominal-the-Best (NTB) Quality Function

Used when the quality characteristic has an ideal central target (e.g., a resistor of 100 Ω, a shaft diameter of 25 mm, voltage of 5 V).

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^2$$

**Operational Steps:**
1. Identify the target value $m$ from engineering design.
2. Determine the loss coefficient $k$ using boundary conditions.
3. Compute loss for any observed deviation.

### 2.2.2 Smaller-the-Better (STB) Quality Function

Used when the ideal target is zero and any positive value indicates poor quality (e.g., impurities, contamination, leakage, wear, defects).

$$L(y) = k y^2$$

This is obtained from the NTB form by setting $m = 0$.

### 2.2.3 Larger-the-Better (LTB) Quality Function

Used when larger values are always better (e.g., strength, battery life, fuel efficiency, yield, safety factor).

$$L(y) = \frac{k}{y^2}$$

This is obtained by taking the reciprocal transformation of an STB characteristic. If $y$ is the characteristic, then $1/y$ is STB, so:

$$L(y) = \frac{k}{y^2}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ | Nominal-the-Best Loss Function | Currency |
| 2 | $L(y) = k y^2$ | Smaller-the-Better Loss Function | Currency |
| 3 | $L(y) = k \mid y^{-2} \mid$ | Larger-the-Better Loss Function | Currency |
| 4 | $k = A_0 / \Delta^2$ | Loss Coefficient (NTB) | Currency / unit² |
| 5 | $k = A_0 / y_0^2$ | Loss Coefficient (STB) | Currency / unit² |
| 6 | $A = \Delta^2 / y_0^2$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (STB) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\bar{L} = k(\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2)$ | Average Loss per unit (NTB) | Currency |
| 8 | $\bar{L} = k \sigma^2$ | Average Loss per unit (STB, $\bar{y} \approx 0$) | Currency |
| 9 | $\bar{L} = k (1/\bar{y}^2 + 2\sigma^2/\bar{y}^4)$ | Average Loss per unit (LTB) | Currency |
| 10 | $A = -10 \log_{10}(\bar{L})$ | S/N Ratio in decibels | dB |

**Where:**
- $A_0$ = Average loss at the **functional tolerance limit** (not specification limit)
- $\Delta$ = Functional tolerance (half-width of acceptable variation)
- $y_0$ = Functional tolerance for STB characteristics
- $\sigma^2$ = Process variance
- $\bar{y}$ = Process mean
- $m$ = Target value

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Quality Function is extensively used in:

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications (KTU-Recognized):**
> - **Automotive Engineering:** Cylinder bore diameter, brake pad thickness, fuel injector flow rate.
> - **Electronics:** Resistor values, capacitor tolerances, IC voltage levels, solder joint strength.
> - **Manufacturing:** Shaft concentricity, surface roughness (Ra), thread pitch accuracy.
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug concentration (target value critical).
> - **Telecommunications:** Signal strength (LTB), noise level (STB), frequency accuracy (NTB).
> - **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength, steel yield strength (LTB), crack length (STB).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Loss Coefficient $k$ for Nominal-the-Best

### Step 1: State the Boundary Condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Logic:**
> When the quality characteristic reaches the **functional tolerance limit** $y = m + \Delta$, the loss incurred must equal the **average loss at the tolerance limit**, denoted $A_0$.

### Step 2: Substitute the Boundary Condition

$$L(m + \Delta) = A_0$$

$$k \cdot (m + \Delta - m)^2 = A_0$$

$$k \cdot \Delta^2 = A_0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $k$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2}}$$

**Interpretation:** The loss coefficient is the monetary loss incurred per unit squared of deviation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Loss Coefficient $k$ for Smaller-the-Better

### Step 1: State the Boundary Condition

When the quality characteristic equals the **functional tolerance value** $y = y_0$ (the largest tolerable value for an STB characteristic), the loss equals $A_0$.

### Step 2: Substitute

$$L(y_0) = A_0$$

$$k \cdot y_0^2 = A_0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $k$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{A_0}{y_0^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Loss Coefficient $k$ for Larger-the-Better

For an LTB characteristic, the ideal target is $y \to \infty$. Taguchi uses the transformation $z = 1/y$, converting it to an STB form.

### Step 1: Transform the Variable

$$L(y) = k' \cdot \left(\frac{1}{y}\right)^2 = \frac{k'}{y^2}$$

### Step 2: Set $k = k'$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{A_0}{y_0^2}}$$

where $y_0$ is the **smallest acceptable value** of the LTB characteristic.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Average Loss per Unit ($\bar{L}$) for Nominal-the-Best

The average loss is the expected value of $L(y)$ over the production distribution.

### Step 1: Express the Expected Loss

$$\bar{L} = E[L(y)] = E[k(y - m)^2]$$

### Step 2: Expand the Square

$$\bar{L} = k \cdot E[(y - m)^2]$$

### Step 3: Decompose the Squared Deviation

We can write $(y - m) = (y - \bar{y}) + (\bar{y} - m)$, so:

$$(y - m)^2 = (y - \bar{y})^2 + 2(y - \bar{y})(\bar{y} - m) + (\bar{y} - m)^2$$

### Step 4: Apply the Expectation Operator

Since $E[y - \bar{y}] = 0$ and $E[(y - \bar{y})^2] = \sigma^2$:

$$\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + 2 \cdot 0 \cdot (\bar{y} - m) + (\bar{y} - m)^2]$$

### Step 5: Final Form

$$\boxed{\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2]}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The term $\sigma^2$ = **Variance Loss** — loss due to random variation (imprecision).
- The term $(\bar{y} - m)^2$ = **Bias Loss** — loss due to the process mean being off-target.
- **Taguchi's Two-Pronged Attack:** To minimize loss, you must (1) **reduce variance** AND (2) **center the mean on target**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Derivation (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example: Deriving $k$ and Computing $\bar{L}$**

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> *"The target diameter of a shaft is $m = 25.00$ mm. The functional tolerance is $\Delta = 0.50$ mm. The average loss at the functional tolerance limit is $A_0 = ₹400$ per shaft. If the process produces shafts with mean $\bar{y} = 25.05$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.15$ mm, determine (a) the loss coefficient $k$, and (b) the average loss per shaft."*

### Part (a): Computing $k$

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2} = \frac{400}{(0.50)^2} = \frac{400}{0.25} = ₹1600 \text{ per mm}^2$$

### Part (b): Computing $\bar{L}$

$$\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2]$$

$$\bar{L} = 1600 \times [(0.15)^2 + (25.05 - 25.00)^2]$$

$$\bar{L} = 1600 \times [0.0225 + 0.0025]$$

$$\bar{L} = 1600 \times 0.0250$$

$$\boxed{\bar{L} = ₹40 \text{ per shaft}}$$

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Derivation: Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio for STB

### Step 1: Express Average Loss for STB

For an STB characteristic with small $\bar{y}$ relative to $\sigma$, Taguchi approximates:

$$\bar{L} \approx k \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\bar{y}^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 2: Define the Mean-Squared-Deviation (MSD)

$$MSD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 \approx \bar{y}^2 + \sigma^2$$

### Step 3: Form the S/N Ratio

The S/N ratio is defined to be **larger when quality is better** (loss is smaller):

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}(MSD) = -10 \log_{10}\left(\bar{y}^2 + \sigma^2\right)$$

This is the **smaller-the-better S/N ratio** in decibels.

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparison Table — All Three Quality Function Types**

| Parameter | Nominal-the-Best (NTB) | Smaller-the-Better (STB) | Larger-the-Better (LTB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loss Function** | $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$ | $L(y) = k y^2$ | $L(y) = k \cdot y^{-2}$ |
| **Target Value** | $m$ (finite central value) | $0$ | $\infty$ |
| **Loss Coefficient** | $k = A_0/\Delta^2$ | $k = A_0/y_0^2$ | $k = A_0/y_0^2$ |
| **Average Loss** | $k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y}-m)^2]$ | $k \sigma^2$ (when $\bar{y} \approx 0$) | $k/\bar{y}^2$ (approx.) |
| **S/N Ratio** | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right]$ | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}[\bar{y}^2 + \sigma^2]$ | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}[1/\bar{y}^2 + \sigma^2/\bar{y}^4]$ |
| **Example** | Shaft diameter, voltage | Leakage, contamination, wear | Strength, life, efficiency |
| **Curve Shape** | Parabola opening upward | Parabola through origin | Hyperbola decreasing from $\infty$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Taguchi Quality Philosophy Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRODUCT DESIGN STAGE] --> B[Identify Quality Characteristic y]
    B --> C{What is the Target Behavior?}
    C -->|Central finite target m| D[Nominal-the-Best NTB]
    C -->|Target is ZERO| E[Smaller-the-Better STB]
    C -->|Target is INFINITY| F[Larger-the-Better LTB]
    D --> G[Apply L y = k y minus m squared]
    E --> H[Apply L y = k y squared]
    F --> I[Apply L y = k over y squared]
    G --> J[Compute Loss Coefficient k]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Calculate Average Loss L bar]
    K --> L[Minimize Variance and Bias]
    L --> M[Optimal Quality Achieved]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ffc107,color:#000000
    style E fill:#fd7e14,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#dc3545,color:#ffffff
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart traces the engineering decision path from design stage through characteristic classification to loss computation and final optimization.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Taguchi Loss Function Curve (NTB Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[m minus Delta] -->|Loss increases quadratically| B[Target m with Loss = 0]
    B -->|Loss increases quadratically| C[m plus Delta]
    B --> D[Parabola Vertex at Origin of Loss]
    A --> E[Loss equals A0 at tolerance limit]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Loss grows unbounded beyond Delta]
    style B fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#dc3545,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#6c757d,color:#ffffff
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This represents the **parabolic loss curve** of the NTB quality function. The vertex of the parabola is at $(y = m, L = 0)$. At both functional tolerance limits $(m \pm \Delta)$, the loss equals $A_0$. Beyond these limits, the loss continues to grow quadratically — there is **no "flat" acceptable region**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Traditional vs Taguchi View of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Traditional SQC View] --> B[Within specs = GOOD, Loss = 0]
    A --> C[Outside specs = BAD, Loss = Full Reject Cost]
    D[Taguchi Quality Function View] --> E[Any deviation from target causes loss]
    D --> F[Loss is a smooth quadratic function]
    E --> G[Loss exists even WITHIN specification limits]
    F --> H[Design for ROBUSTNESS, not just inspection]
    style A fill:#6c757d,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#dc3545,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This contrast diagram highlights the **philosophical shift** introduced by Taguchi — moving from a binary "accept/reject" inspection mentality to a continuous loss-based design philosophy.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Components of Total Average Loss

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Average Loss L bar] --> B[Variance Loss k times sigma squared]
    A --> C[Bias Loss k times y bar minus m squared]
    B --> D[Reduced by tightening process control]
    C --> E[Reduced by adjusting process mean]
    D --> F[Minimum Loss Achieved]
    E --> F
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#fd7e14,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ffc107,color:#000000
    style F fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The total average loss is decomposed into two orthogonal components. This decomposition is the foundation of **Taguchi's Two-Step Optimization Strategy** (also called the **"Mean First, Then Variance"** principle).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Quality Function as proposed by Taguchi. Explain its significance. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Quality Function** (also called the **Taguchi Loss Function**) is a quadratic mathematical model that quantifies the **monetary loss to society** resulting from any deviation of a product's quality characteristic from its target value. It is given by $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ for nominal-the-best characteristics, $L(y) = k y^2$ for smaller-the-better, and $L(y) = k / y^2$ for larger-the-better.

> **Significance:**
> 1. It shifts the focus from **inspection** (accept/reject) to **design for quality**.
> 2. It demonstrates that loss exists **even within specification limits**, contradicting the traditional binary view.
> 3. It provides a **monetary measure** of quality loss, enabling engineers to make cost-effective design decisions.
> 4. It enables the computation of **optimal tolerance** and **process parameter settings** to minimize societal loss.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for significance points.)*

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between the three categories of quality characteristics with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Category | Target | Loss Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nominal-the-Best (NTB)** | Central finite value $m$ | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ | Shaft diameter 25 mm, voltage 5 V |
| 2 | **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | Zero (0) | $L(y) = k y^2$ | Engine oil leakage, surface defects, wear |
| 3 | **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | Infinity ($\infty$) | $L(y) = k / y^2$ | Concrete strength, battery life, safety factor |

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark per category for correct identification of target and example.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING:**
> - Many students **lose 2 marks** by writing $L = k(y - m)^2$ without stating the **boundary conditions** used to derive $k$.
> - Students often **forget units** in $k$ computation. Always mention **₹/mm²** or **$/V²** etc.
> - Do **NOT** confuse the **functional tolerance ($\Delta$)** with the **specification limit**. Taguchi uses functional tolerance.
> - When asked for "average loss", students often write $L(y)$ instead of $\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2]$. Both are different concepts.
> - For S/N ratio questions, always show the **transformation step** (especially $z = 1/y$ for LTB) — examiners award 2 marks specifically for this.

---

### **Question A: KTU ESE 14-Mark Module 1 Question** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Derive the Quality Function for a **Nominal-the-Best** characteristic. State the loss coefficient $k$ in terms of the average loss at the functional tolerance limit $A_0$ and the functional tolerance $\Delta$. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A resistor manufacturing process has a target resistance $m = 100$ Ω. The functional tolerance is $\Delta = 5$ Ω. The average loss at the functional tolerance is $A_0 = ₹500$ per resistor. If the process mean is $\bar{y} = 101$ Ω and process standard deviation is $\sigma = 1.5$ Ω, compute: (i) the loss coefficient $k$, (ii) the average loss per resistor $\bar{L}$, and (iii) the new average loss if the process variance is reduced to $\sigma = 0.5$ Ω while keeping the same mean. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Derivation of NTB Quality Function (7 Marks)

**Step 1: State Taguchi's Postulate (1 Mark)**
Taguchi postulated that the loss to society from a product's functional variation is approximately proportional to the square of the deviation of its functional characteristic from the target value.

**Step 2: Define the Loss Function (1 Mark)**

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^2$$

where $L(y)$ is the loss, $y$ is the actual value, $m$ is the target value, and $k$ is the proportionality constant (loss coefficient).

**Step 3: State the Boundary Condition (1 Mark)**

When the quality characteristic reaches the functional tolerance limit, i.e., $y = m + \Delta$ or $y = m - \Delta$, the loss must equal $A_0$ (average loss at functional tolerance).

**Step 4: Apply the Boundary Condition (1 Mark)**

$$L(m + \Delta) = A_0$$

$$k \cdot (m + \Delta - m)^2 = A_0$$

$$k \cdot \Delta^2 = A_0$$

**Step 5: Solve for $k$ (1 Mark)**

$$\boxed{k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2}}$$

**Step 6: Final Form of the Quality Function (1 Mark)**

$$\boxed{L(y) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2} (y - m)^2}$$

**Step 7: Derive the Average Loss Expression (1 Mark)**

$$\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2] = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2}[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2]$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $m = 100$ Ω
- $\Delta = 5$ Ω
- $A_0 = ₹500$ per resistor
- $\bar{y} = 101$ Ω
- $\sigma = 1.5$ Ω

**(i) Loss Coefficient $k$ (2 Marks)**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2} = \frac{500}{(5)^2} = \frac{500}{25} = ₹20 \text{ per } \Omega^2$$

**(ii) Average Loss per Resistor (3 Marks)**

$$\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2]$$

$$\bar{L} = 20 \times [(1.5)^2 + (101 - 100)^2]$$

$$\bar{L} = 20 \times [2.25 + 1.00]$$

$$\bar{L} = 20 \times 3.25 = ₹65 \text{ per resistor}$$

**(iii) New Average Loss with Reduced Variance (2 Marks)**

With $\sigma = 0.5$ Ω, $\bar{y} = 101$ Ω, $m = 100$ Ω:

$$\bar{L}_{new} = 20 \times [(0.5)^2 + (101 - 100)^2]$$

$$\bar{L}_{new} = 20 \times [0.25 + 1.00]$$

$$\bar{L}_{new} = 20 \times 1.25 = ₹25 \text{ per resistor}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Reducing $\sigma$ from 1.5 Ω to 0.5 Ω (a 3× reduction) **reduced the loss from ₹65 to ₹25** — a **61.5% reduction** in loss. The remaining ₹25 loss is entirely due to **bias** ($(\bar{y} - m)^2 = 1$). This illustrates Taguchi's two-step strategy: **first reduce variance, then center the mean on target**.

---

### **Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question (KTU Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the three forms of the Taguchi Quality Function with suitable examples. Derive the loss coefficient expressions for each form. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A pharmaceutical tablet is required to deliver a drug dose of $m = 500$ mg (Nominal-the-Best). The specification limit is $500 \pm 50$ mg, and the average loss at the specification limit is $A_0 = ₹1000$ per batch. A new process is being designed with two options:
> - **Process P1:** Mean = 500 mg, Standard Deviation = 10 mg
> - **Process P2:** Mean = 502 mg, Standard Deviation = 4 mg
>
> Calculate the average loss per batch for both processes. Which process is economically superior, and by how much? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Three Forms of the Quality Function (7 Marks)

**Form 1: Nominal-the-Best (NTB) — 2.5 Marks**

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^2, \quad k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2}$$

**Example:** Resistor resistance, shaft diameter, voltage output, drug dosage. The target is a specific central value.

**Form 2: Smaller-the-Better (STB) — 2.5 Marks**

$$L(y) = k y^2, \quad k = \frac{A_0}{y_0^2}$$

**Example:** Engine emissions, leakage current, surface roughness, contamination level. The target is zero — the smaller the value, the better.

**Form 3: Larger-the-Better (LTB) — 2 Marks**

$$L(y) = \frac{k}{y^2}, \quad k = \frac{A_0}{y_0^2}$$

**Example:** Bond strength, battery life, fuel efficiency, insulation resistance, safety factor. The target is infinity — the larger the value, the better.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $m = 500$ mg
- $\Delta = 50$ mg (functional tolerance = specification tolerance here)
- $A_0 = ₹1000$ per batch

**Step 1: Calculate Loss Coefficient $k$ (1 Mark)**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2} = \frac{1000}{(50)^2} = \frac{1000}{2500} = ₹0.40 \text{ per mg}^2$$

**Step 2: Average Loss for Process P1 (3 Marks)**

Given: $\bar{y}_1 = 500$ mg, $\sigma_1 = 10$ mg

$$\bar{L}_1 = k[\sigma_1^2 + (\bar{y}_1 - m)^2]$$

$$\bar{L}_1 = 0.40 \times [(10)^2 + (500 - 500)^2]$$

$$\bar{L}_1 = 0.40 \times [100 + 0] = 0.40 \times 100 = ₹40 \text{ per batch}$$

**Step 3: Average Loss for Process P2 (3 Marks)**

Given: $\bar{y}_2 = 502$ mg, $\sigma_2 = 4$ mg

$$\bar{L}_2 = k[\sigma_2^2 + (\bar{y}_2 - m)^2]$$

$$\bar{L}_2 = 0.40 \times [(4)^2 + (502 - 500)^2]$$

$$\bar{L}_2 = 0.40 \times [16 + 4] = 0.40 \times 20 = ₹8 \text{ per batch}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\Delta \bar{L} = \bar{L}_1 - \bar{L}_2 = 40 - 8 = ₹32 \text{ per batch}$$

**Process P2 is economically superior** despite being off-target by 2 mg. The lower variance ($\sigma_2 = 4$ vs $\sigma_1 = 10$) more than compensates for the small bias. This is a classic illustration of why **reducing variance is more powerful than correcting bias alone** in Taguchi's quality philosophy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Quality Function**

### 🎯 Core Concepts to Master

- ✅ The **Taguchi Loss Function** quantifies monetary loss as a **quadratic function** of deviation from target.
- ✅ **Zero deviation = Zero loss** (the core axiom of Taguchi's philosophy).
- ✅ Loss exists **even within specification limits** — there is no "free zone".
- ✅ The **loss coefficient $k$** is derived from the boundary condition: loss at the functional tolerance = $A_0$.

### 📐 Key Formulas (Top 5 Must-Remember)

- ✅ $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ — **NTB form**
- ✅ $L(y) = k y^2$ — **STB form**
- ✅ $L(y) = k \cdot y^{-2}$ — **LTB form**
- ✅ $k = A_0 / \Delta^2$ — **Loss coefficient (NTB & STB)**
- ✅ $\bar{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2]$ — **Average loss (NTB)**

### ⚠️ Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid

- ❌ Do **not** confuse **functional tolerance ($\Delta$)** with **specification limit** — they are not always equal.
- ❌ Do **not** write $L = k(y - m)$ (linear) — it is always **quadratic**.
- ❌ Always state the **units** of $k$ (e.g., ₹/mm², $/V²).
- ❌ For LTB, remember the **reciprocal transformation** $z = 1/y$ before applying the STB form.
- ❌ In "average loss" calculations, use the **expected value** $\bar{L}$, not $L(y)$ for a single $y$.

### 🔑 KTU 2024 High-Yield Insights

- ✅ Taguchi's **Two-Step Strategy**: First **reduce variance**, then **center the mean on target**.
- ✅ The S/N (Signal-to-Noise) ratio is the **negative logarithm of average loss** — a higher S/N ratio = lower loss.
- ✅ The Quality Function is a tool for **off-line quality control** — applied at the **design stage**, not during production.
- ✅ The functional relationship between quality and loss is **continuous and smooth** — never stepped or binary.
- ✅ A process with **higher variance** always has **higher loss**, even if its mean is on target.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goal Post View of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Goal Post View of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Goal Post View of Quality** is the classical, traditional paradigm of quality management that defines quality strictly as **conformance to predetermined specifications** (also known as engineering tolerances or specification limits). Under this view, a product, service, or process output is evaluated by comparing its measured characteristic(s) against a fixed upper specification limit (**USL**) and a fixed lower specification limit (**LSL**). The output is classified as either **acceptable** (conforming — the measurement falls between LSL and USL) or **unacceptable** (non-conforming — the measurement falls outside either specification limit). There is no middle ground, no partial credit, and no continuous gradation of quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** *Quality is viewed as a dichotomous (binary) variable — a product either meets specifications or it does not. The specifications act as fixed "goal posts" against which every unit is judged. This view originated from the early 20th-century manufacturing mindset and is often associated with the inspection-based quality philosophy that prevailed before the modern quality revolution.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine a football (soccer) field. The **goal post** consists of two vertical posts connected by a horizontal crossbar. When a player kicks the ball towards the goal, there are only **two possible outcomes**:

- The ball **passes between the posts and under the crossbar** → **GOAL** (accepted, conforming, "good")
- The ball **hits the post, goes over the bar, or misses entirely** → **NO GOAL** (rejected, non-conforming, "bad")

There is **no such thing as "almost a goal"** or "a partial goal." Similarly, under the Goal Post View of Quality, a manufactured bolt is either within the specified diameter tolerance (good) or outside it (rejected). A resistor of nominal 100 Ω is either between 95 Ω and 105 Ω (acceptable) or not (rejected).

**Think of it this way:** A student writing an exam is either awarded a pass mark (≥ 40/100) or a fail (< 40/100). The boundary is sharp. The student who scores 39 is "failing" by just one mark, but under the Goal Post View, the difference between 39 and 41 is treated as enormous — one is "bad," one is "good" — even though the actual quality difference is negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Students:** The Goal Post View treats quality as a **step function**, not a continuous variable. Quality "jumps" from 0 to 100% the instant a measurement crosses the specification boundary — an unrealistic and mathematically discontinuous representation.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Origin

The Goal Post View emerged from the **mass-production era** (early 1900s to mid-1900s), when interchangeable parts manufacturing required standardized tolerances. Pioneers like **Walter A. Shewhart** (the father of statistical quality control) and **Harold Dodge** developed acceptance sampling and control charts within this paradigm.

However, the same Shewhart, along with his student **W. Edwards Deming**, later **criticized** this view as being too narrow and economically wasteful. Deming famously argued that the Goal Post View:

1. Ignores the **variation within specifications** (two units at the LSL and at the nominal are treated identically, though their actual performance may differ significantly).
2. Fails to account for the **economic loss to society** caused by variation.
3. Encourages **inspection and sorting** rather than process improvement.
4. Discourages continuous improvement because as long as outputs are "within specs," no action is taken.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

The Goal Post View operates on a simple, deterministic logic that can be broken into the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Define Specifications:** Engineers establish fixed numerical tolerances for each critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristic. For a shaft diameter, the nominal might be $\mu_0 = 25.00$ mm with a tolerance of $\pm 0.05$ mm.
- **Step 2 — Define Specification Limits:** From the tolerance, compute the **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** and **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)**.
- **Step 3 — Manufacture / Produce:** Manufacturing processes generate units with inherent variability. The output distribution is typically assumed Normal with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.
- **Step 4 — Measure Each Unit:** Every produced unit is measured (often via 100% inspection or sampling).
- **Step 5 — Binary Classification:** Each unit is classified using a strict rule — if its measurement falls in $[LSL, USL]$, it is **conforming**; otherwise, it is **non-conforming**.
- **Step 6 — Disposition:** Conforming units are shipped; non-conforming units are reworked, scrapped, or sold as seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Logic:** This view was operationally convenient for early manufacturers because it allowed **simple pass/fail sorting** by unskilled inspectors. It did not require statistical thinking — only a ruler, a caliper, or a go/no-go gauge.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formalism

Let $Y$ represent the measured quality characteristic of a unit. The Goal Post View defines the **Quality Status** ($Q$) of a unit as:

$$
Q(Y) = \begin{cases} 1 \quad (\text{Conforming / Acceptable}) & \text{if } LSL \leq Y \leq USL \\ 0 \quad (\text{Non-conforming / Reject}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $LSL = \mu_0 - T$ (Lower Specification Limit)
- $USL = \mu_0 + T$ (Upper Specification Limit)
- $\mu_0$ = Nominal/target value
- $T$ = Tolerance (half-width)

If $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the **probability of conformance** (yield) is:

$$
P(\text{Conforming}) = P(LSL \leq Y \leq USL) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma}\right)
$$

Where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of the standard normal distribution.

The **probability of non-conformance** (defect rate) is:

$$
P(\text{Non-conforming}) = 1 - \left[\Phi\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Specification Limit | $LSL = \mu_0 - T$ | Lower acceptable bound | Same as characteristic |
| Upper Specification Limit | $USL = \mu_0 + T$ | Upper acceptable bound | Same as characteristic |
| Tolerance (half-width) | $T = \frac{USL - LSL}{2}$ | Half of the specification window | Same as characteristic |
| Specification Width | $W = USL - LSL$ | Total specification spread | Same as characteristic |
| Process Capability Index | $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process spread vs. spec spread | Dimensionless |
| Process Capability (shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | One-sided capability with mean shift | Dimensionless |
| Probability of Conformance | $P_c = \Phi\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Yield (fraction conforming) | Dimensionless (probability) |
| Taguchi Loss Function (Contrast) | $L(y) = k(y - \mu_0)^2$ | Continuous loss (rejects Goal Post) | Currency or units² |
| Six-Sigma Equivalent | $C_p \geq 2.0$ (12σ within ±6σ spec) | World-class benchmark | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This view is still used today in **incoming inspection, supplier qualification, and acceptance sampling** (e.g., MIL-STD-105E, ANSI/ASQ Z1.4). It remains the foundation of quality contracts between buyer and seller, though modern practitioners supplement it with capability indices and Taguchi loss functions.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Conformance under Goal Post View

**Problem Setup:** Suppose a manufacturing process produces steel rods with diameter $Y \sim N(\mu = 10.02 \text{ mm}, \sigma^2 = 0.0025 \text{ mm}^2)$. The specification limits are $LSL = 9.95$ mm and $USL = 10.05$ mm. Compute the conformance probability.

**Step 1 — Standardize the Lower Limit:**

We compute the standardized Z-score for the lower specification limit:

$$
Z_L = \frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{9.95 - 10.02}{0.05} = \frac{-0.07}{0.05} = -1.40
$$

Here, $\sigma = \sqrt{0.0025} = 0.05$ mm. The negative Z-value indicates that the LSL lies 1.40 standard deviations **below** the process mean.

**Step 2 — Standardize the Upper Limit:**

Similarly, for the upper specification limit:

$$
Z_U = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{10.05 - 10.02}{0.05} = \frac{0.03}{0.05} = +0.60
$$

The positive Z-value indicates that the USL lies 0.60 standard deviations **above** the process mean.

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Normal CDF Values:**

From the standard normal table (or using a calculator):

$$
\Phi(Z_U) = \Phi(0.60) = 0.7257
$$

$$
\Phi(Z_L) = \Phi(-1.40) = 0.0808
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Conformance Probability Formula:**

$$
P_c = \Phi(Z_U) - \Phi(Z_L) = 0.7257 - 0.0808 = 0.6449
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result:**

$$
P(\text{Conforming}) = 64.49\%, \quad P(\text{Non-conforming}) = 35.51\%
$$

This means **roughly 1 in 3 rods** is outside specifications — a process with extremely poor capability. The Goal Post View would classify all 35.51% of these rods as simply "bad" with no further distinction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability Insight:** The $C_p$ for this process is $C_p = \frac{10.05 - 9.95}{6 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = 0.333$, which is far below the industry minimum of $C_p \geq 1.00$, confirming the process is **incapable**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Goal Post vs. Modern Quality Views

| **Dimension** | **Goal Post View** | **Taguchi Loss Function View** | **Deming / Juran View** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition of Quality** | Conformance to specs | Minimum societal loss | Fitness for use / Predictable variation |
| **Quality is...** | Binary (good/bad) | Continuous quadratic loss | Multi-dimensional and continuous |
| **Specification Limits** | Rigid, absolute | Flexible (loss extends beyond) | Used as guides, not absolutes |
| **Action when "in spec"** | Ship it — no action | Still minimize variation | Drive toward nominal; reduce variation |
| **Treatment of Variation** | Ignored within specs | Loss increases as $(y - \mu_0)^2$ | Variation is the enemy |
| **Role of Inspection** | Central (sort good vs. bad) | Reduced (build quality in) | Reduced (control the process) |
| **Customer Focus** | Low (internal specs only) | Medium (loss to society) | High (fitness for actual use) |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Discouraged | Encouraged | Strongly encouraged |
| **Mathematical Form** | Step function (Heaviside-like) | Parabolic ($L = k(y-m)^2$) | Statistical control theory |

## 3.3 Limitation Analysis — Why the Goal Post View Fails

**Limitation 1 — Step Function Discontinuity:**

The Goal Post View implies that a part at $Y = LSL + \epsilon$ (where $\epsilon$ is infinitesimally small) is acceptable, while a part at $Y = LSL - \epsilon$ is scrap. This is mathematically and economically nonsensical:

$$
\lim_{\epsilon \to 0^+} Q(LSL + \epsilon) = 1, \quad \lim_{\epsilon \to 0^-} Q(LSL - \epsilon) = 0
$$

Such a **discontinuous reward function** cannot represent true quality.

**Limitation 2 — Equal Treatment of Dissimilar Units:**

Two rods with diameters $9.96$ mm and $10.04$ mm are both "conforming" under the Goal Post View, yet their functional performance (e.g., fit in a bearing) may differ substantially. The view **erases meaningful variation**.

**Limitation 3 — Encouragement of "Specification Gaming":**

Managers may **manipulate specifications** (relaxing $LSL$ or tightening $USL$) to artificially improve conformance rates, rather than improving the actual process. This is a known ethical failure of the paradigm.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Goal Post View — Operational Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Raw Material Entry] --> nodeB[Manufacturing Process]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Output Distribution Normal Mean Mu SD Sigma]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Measure Each Unit}
    nodeD --> nodeE{LSL less than or equal to Y less than or equal to USL}
    nodeE --> nodeF[YES: Conforming Unit]
    nodeE --> nodeG[NO: Non-Conforming Unit]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Ship to Customer]
    nodeF --> nodeI[No Process Improvement Action]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Rework or Scrap]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Return to Process for Root Cause]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Dispose or Rework]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Process Investigation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the critical branch point `nodeE` — this is the **binary classification step** that defines the Goal Post View. A unit's quality fate is sealed at this single inequality test, with no intermediate state.

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — Three Quality Paradigms

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Quality Paradigm Universe] --> nodeB[Traditional View]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Modern Statistical View]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Japanese Quality View]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Goal Post View]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Spec: LSL to USL]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Logic: Pass or Fail]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Focus: Conformance]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Taguchi Loss Function]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Loss: k times y minus m squared]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Focus: Continuous Improvement]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Juran Fitness for Use]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Deming System of Profound Knowledge]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Focus: Customer Needs]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Focus: Variation Reduction and Predictability]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Explain the Goal Post View of Quality with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Goal Post View of Quality is the traditional approach where quality is defined as **conformance to specifications** (typically upper and lower specification limits). Under this view, a product is either acceptable or rejected — a binary classification with no intermediate quality levels.

**Example:** Consider a ball bearing with a specified inner diameter of **$20.00 \pm 0.02$ mm**. The lower specification limit ($LSL$) is **$19.98$ mm** and the upper specification limit ($USL$) is **$20.02$ mm**. Under the Goal Post View, any bearing with diameter in $[19.98, 20.02]$ mm is accepted; any bearing outside this range is rejected. There is no partial credit — a bearing of $19.981$ mm is "perfect," while one at $19.979$ mm (only $0.002$ mm smaller) is scrap. **(3 marks)**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**List any three limitations of the Goal Post View of Quality.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Binary / Discontinuous View:** Treats quality as a step function, ignoring continuous variation within specifications. **(1 mark)**
2. **No Economic Loss Consideration:** Ignores the cost to society of products that are "in spec" but far from the nominal target (contrast with Taguchi's loss function). **(1 mark)**
3. **Discourages Continuous Improvement:** As long as units fall within specifications, no action is taken; the view does not motivate variance reduction. **(1 mark)**

*(Acceptable alternative limitations: Encourages 100% inspection, ignores customer perspective, allows specification manipulation, treats products near LSL/USL identically.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Goal Post View of Quality**. Discuss its operational steps and explain why it is considered the classical view. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process produces piston rings with diameter $Y \sim N(\mu = 75.00 \text{ mm}, \sigma^2 = 0.09 \text{ mm}^2)$. The specification limits are $LSL = 74.00$ mm and $USL = 76.00$ mm. Calculate **(i)** the process capability index $C_p$, **(ii)** the probability of conformance, and **(iii)** comment on whether the Goal Post View adequately describes this process. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Goal Post View Explanation (7 Marks):**

The Goal Post View of Quality is the classical, inspection-based approach to quality that emerged in the early 20th century during the era of mass production. **(1 mark)** It defines quality as **conformance to fixed specifications**, typically expressed as an upper specification limit ($USL$) and a lower specification limit ($LSL$). **(1 mark)**

**Operational Steps:**
1. Engineers establish specifications based on design requirements. **(1 mark)**
2. The process is operated to produce units, which exhibit natural variability (assumed Normal). **(1 mark)**
3. Each unit is measured and compared against $LSL$ and $USL$. **(1 mark)**
4. Units within $[LSL, USL]$ are classified as **conforming**; units outside are **non-conforming**. **(1 mark)**
5. Non-conforming units are reworked or scrapped; conforming units are shipped. **(1 mark)**

**Why Classical:** It was the dominant view before Shewhart, Deming, Juran, and Taguchi introduced statistical and philosophical alternatives. Its simplicity made it suitable for unskilled inspection labor in mass production. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $Y \sim N(\mu = 75.00, \sigma^2 = 0.09)$, so $\sigma = 0.30$ mm. $LSL = 74.00$ mm, $USL = 76.00$ mm.

**(i) Process Capability Index $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{76.00 - 74.00}{6 \times 0.30} = \frac{2.00}{1.80} = 1.111
$$

**(1 mark)** $C_p \approx 1.11$, which is **marginally capable** ($C_p \geq 1.00$ is the industry minimum threshold).

**(ii) Probability of Conformance:**

Standardize:

$$
Z_L = \frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{74.00 - 75.00}{0.30} = \frac{-1.00}{0.30} = -3.333
$$

$$
Z_U = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{76.00 - 75.00}{0.30} = \frac{1.00}{0.30} = +3.333
$$

**(1 mark)** From standard normal tables:

$$
\Phi(3.33) \approx 0.99957, \quad \Phi(-3.33) \approx 0.00043
$$

**(1 mark)** Therefore:

$$
P_c = \Phi(Z_U) - \Phi(Z_L) = 0.99957 - 0.00043 = 0.99914
$$

**(1 mark)** So approximately **99.91%** of piston rings conform to specifications, with only **0.09%** non-conformance (about 9 in 10,000).

**(iii) Adequacy of Goal Post View:**

Even with a respectable conformance rate of 99.91%, the Goal Post View does not distinguish between a piston ring of diameter 74.01 mm (barely within $LSL$) and one of 75.00 mm (perfectly nominal). However, the ring at 74.01 mm may suffer functional issues in a high-performance engine, illustrating the Goal Post View's **insensitivity to within-spec variation**. **(1 mark)**

Furthermore, the $C_{pk}$ (with the mean centered) equals $C_p$ here since $\mu = 75.00$ mm is exactly midway, but if a shift occurred (e.g., $\mu = 75.10$ mm), the yield would drop dramatically due to the Goal Post View's sharp boundary. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to take the **square root of variance** to obtain $\sigma$ before applying formulas. Here, $\sigma^2 = 0.09$ means $\sigma = 0.30$, **not** $0.09$. Misusing $0.09$ as $\sigma$ will yield a wrong $C_p = 3.70$ (incapability index inversion) — losing **2 to 3 marks**. Always extract $\sigma$ from $\sigma^2$ first!

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE – May 2024, Model QP – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Goal Post View** with **Taguchi's Loss Function View** of quality. Use mathematical expressions where appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electrical resistor manufacturing process has nominal resistance $\mu_0 = 100$ Ω. The Goal Post View accepts all resistors in $[95, 105]$ Ω. The process distribution is $Y \sim N(100, 4)$ Ω². **(i)** Compute the conformance probability. **(ii)** If the cost of a defective resistor is ₹50 and the Taguchi loss coefficient is $k = 0.10$ ₹/Ω², compute the **expected Taguchi loss** per resistor produced. **(iii)** Which view better reflects the manufacturer's true cost burden? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Goal Post View** | **Taguchi Loss Function View** |
|---|---|---|
| Quality definition | Conformance to specs | Minimum loss to society |
| Mathematical form | Step function: $Q = 1$ if $LSL \leq Y \leq USL$, else $Q = 0$ | Quadratic: $L(y) = k(y - \mu_0)^2$ |
| In-spec treatment | No loss assumed | Continuous loss increasing with deviation from target |
| Out-of-spec treatment | Total loss (scrap/rework cost) | Loss exceeds scrap cost (extended) |
| Action focus | Inspect and sort | Improve process to reduce variation |

**(5 marks for tabulated comparison with mathematical expressions.)**

**Taguchi's Loss Function:**

$$
L(y) = k(y - \mu_0)^2
$$

Where $k$ is the loss coefficient (in ₹/Ω², for example), $y$ is the actual quality characteristic, and $\mu_0$ is the target value.

**Goal Post's Implicit Loss Function:**

$$
L_{\text{goal post}}(y) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } LSL \leq y \leq USL \\ C_{\text{scrap}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**(1 mark)** The Goal Post View's loss function is **discontinuous and piecewise constant**, whereas Taguchi's is **continuous and convex (parabolic)**. Taguchi argued that even "in-spec" parts incur loss proportional to squared deviation from target.
