**(1 mark)** Key insight: A Goal Post-accepted unit at $y = 95$ Ω or $y = 105$ Ω suffers **identical** (zero) loss under the Goal Post, but under Taguchi, it suffers loss $L = k(5)^2 = 25k$ — nonzero and economically meaningful.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $\mu_0 = 100$ Ω, $LSL = 95$ Ω, $USL = 105$ Ω, $Y \sim N(\mu = 100, \sigma^2 = 4)$ Ω². Thus $\sigma = 2$ Ω.

**(i) Conformance Probability:**

Standardize:

$$
Z_L = \frac{95 - 100}{2} = -2.50, \quad Z_U = \frac{105 - 100}{2} = +2.50
$$

From tables: $\Phi(2.50) = 0.9938$, $\Phi(-2.50) = 0.0062$. **(1 mark)**

$$
P_c = 0.9938 - 0.0062 = 0.9876
$$

**(1 mark)** So conformance probability is **98.76%**, and non-conformance is **1.24%** (1,240 defective per 100,000 units).

**(ii) Expected Taguchi Loss per Resistor:**

The expected loss is:

$$
E[L(Y)] = k \cdot E[(Y - \mu_0)^2] = k \cdot \text{Var}(Y) = k \sigma^2
$$

**(2 marks — key insight: $E[(Y-\mu_0)^2] = \text{Var}(Y)$ when $\mu = \mu_0$.)**

$$
E[L] = 0.10 \times 4 = ₹0.40 \text{ per resistor}
$$

**(1 mark)** So the expected Taguchi loss is **₹0.40 per resistor**.

**(iii) Which View Better Reflects True Cost Burden?**

Under the Goal Post View, the manufacturer only "feels" the cost of the **1.24% defectives** (₹50 each on average):

$$
\text{Goal Post Cost} = 0.0124 \times 50 = ₹0.62 \text{ per resistor (average)}
$$

**(1 mark)** Under Taguchi, the manufacturer bears ₹0.40 per resistor from **all units** (including the 98.76% "good" ones), reflecting real-world performance degradation in customer applications.

**(1 mark)** **Justification:** The Taguchi view is more accurate because customers experience performance loss from resistors that deviate from 100 Ω, even if nominally "in spec." For example, a 105 Ω resistor in a precision timing circuit may cause timing errors. Thus, Taguchi's view **better reflects the manufacturer's true cost burden** and motivates variance reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Part (b)(ii), students often compute $E[(Y - \mu_0)^2]$ by incorrectly expanding it as $E[Y^2] - 2\mu_0 E[Y] + \mu_0^2$ and then substituting values. This wastes time. The shortcut is to recognize that **when the process is centered ($\mu = \mu_0$)**, $E[(Y - \mu_0)^2] = \text{Var}(Y) = \sigma^2$. Use this identity to save 1–2 minutes per problem. **Don't lose marks by over-computing!**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Goal Post View of Quality**

- ✅ **Definition:** Quality = conformance to fixed specifications ($LSL \leq Y \leq USL$); binary classification (pass/fail).
- ✅ **Origin:** Classical/traditional 20th-century manufacturing paradigm; preceded modern SQC philosophy.
- ✅ **Specification Limits:** $LSL = \mu_0 - T$ and $USL = \mu_0 + T$, where $T$ is the tolerance (half-width).
- ✅ **Probability of Conformance:** $P_c = \Phi\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$.
- ✅ **Process Capability:** $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$; minimum acceptable $C_p = 1.00$; world-class $C_p = 2.00$ (Six-Sigma).
- ✅ **Six Limitations:** (1) Binary/discontinuous; (2) ignores within-spec variation; (3) no economic loss consideration; (4) discourages continuous improvement; (5) encourages 100% inspection; (6) ignores customer perspective.
- ✅ **Contrast with Taguchi:** Taguchi uses continuous quadratic loss $L(y) = k(y - \mu_0)^2$; Goal Post uses step function.
- ✅ **Contrast with Deming/Juran:** Deming focuses on predictable variation and process control; Juran emphasizes fitness for use (customer-centric).
- ✅ **KTU Exam Frequency:** This topic appears in **Part A** (definition/limitations) almost every semester and frequently anchors **Part B** numerical problems on capability indices and conformance probability.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert $\sigma^2$ to $\sigma$ before applying formulas — always extract the **standard deviation** first.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"G-O-A-L"** → **G**o-or-no-go, **O**nly binary, **A**bsolute specs, **L**oss-function-free.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Aspects of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Other Aspects of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Quality"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000 definition adapted for engineering).

In simpler engineering terms, **Quality** is the degree to which a product or service conforms to its design specifications and meets customer expectations. It is a multidimensional construct that combines **Fitness for Use**, **Conformance to Requirements**, and **Customer Satisfaction**.

## 1.2 Other Aspects of Quality — Scope

"Other Aspects of Quality" broadens the definition from purely statistical monitoring to the **managerial, economic, philosophical, and strategic dimensions** of quality. It includes:

| Aspect | Focus Area |
| :--- | :--- |
| Quality Costs (COQ) | Economic impact of quality |
| Quality Gurus | Philosophical foundations |
| Quality Awards | Recognition frameworks |
| ISO 9000 & Standards | International compliance |
| Six Sigma | Data-driven methodology |
| Bench Marking | Comparative performance |
| TQM | Holistic management philosophy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** Statistical Quality Control (SQC) is the *quantitative backbone*, while "Other Aspects of Quality" provides the *qualitative and managerial context* without which SQC is incomplete.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

**Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
Imagine running a restaurant. **Quality** is not only about whether the food is cooked correctly (statistical control of the process), but also:
- How much money you waste on burnt food (**Internal Failure Cost**)
- How much you spend on training chefs (**Prevention Cost**)
- How much you spend on tasting dishes before serving (**Appraisal Cost**)
- How much you lose when customers complain and never return (**External Failure Cost**)

The "Other Aspects" are the **business and cultural pillars** that ensure the kitchen runs profitably, consistently, and reputationally — not just statistically.

## 1.4 Quality: A Multi-Faceted Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Universal Views of Quality (Garvin's Framework):**
> 1. **User-Based:** Fitness for intended use (subjective, customer-driven)
> 2. **Manufacturing-Based:** Conformance to specifications (objective, measurable)
> 3. **Product-Based:** Precision in design and inherent excellence (engineering-driven)

These views are reconciled in **Total Quality Management (TQM)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The PAF Model

The **PAF Model** categorizes all quality-related expenditures into four mutually exclusive cost buckets. This is the **gold standard** in KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (P)
Costs incurred to **prevent defects** from occurring in the first place.
- Quality planning, training, process design, supplier capability assessment, statistical process control (SPC) implementation.

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (A)
Costs of **detecting defects** through inspection, testing, and measurement.
- Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final product testing, calibration of measuring equipment.

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IF)
Costs arising from defects **caught before the product reaches the customer**.
- Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime due to defects, material wastage.

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EF)
Costs arising from defects **discovered after the product reaches the customer**.
- Warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal liability, loss of goodwill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of COQ:** As prevention costs **increase**, internal and external failure costs **decrease** dramatically — leading to a lower *total* cost of quality. The optimal point minimizes the sum of all four costs.

### 2.1.5 Total Cost of Quality (TCQ) Formula

$$
TCQ = P + A + IF + EF
$$

The **COQ Ratio** is often expressed as a percentage of sales or revenue:

$$
COQ \% = \frac{TCQ}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100
$$

## 2.2 Quality Gurus and Their Contributions

| Guru | Core Philosophy | Key Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; System thinking | PDCA (Deming) Cycle |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy: Planning, Control, Improvement | Fitness for Use definition |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Quality is Free; "Do it right the first time" | Zero Defects concept |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Cause-and-Effect analysis | Fishbone Diagram |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss to Society; Robust Design | Quality Loss Function |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (TQC) | COQ concept introduction |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Process Control foundations | Control Charts |

### 2.2.1 Deming's 14 Points (Key Highlights)
1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

### 2.2.2 The Deming Cycle (PDCA)

> [!NOTE]
> **PDCA = Plan, Do, Check, Act** — a four-stage iterative management method for continuous improvement.

- **Plan:** Identify the goal and plan how to achieve it.
- **Do:** Execute the plan on a small scale.
- **Check:** Analyze the results against expected outcomes.
- **Act:** Standardize or revise; repeat the cycle.

## 2.3 Quality Awards

### 2.3.1 Deming Prize (Japan, 1951)
- Awarded to companies that demonstrate strong statistical quality control practices.
- Examines company-wide **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** implementation.

### 2.3.2 Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA, USA, 1987)
- Criteria: Leadership, Strategic Planning, Customer Focus, Measurement/Analysis, Workforce Focus, Operations Focus, Results.

### 2.3.3 European Quality Award (EQA, 1992)
- Established by EFQM (European Foundation for Quality Management).
- Model based on **Enablers** and **Results**.

## 2.4 ISO 9000 Series

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 9000** is a family of international standards for **Quality Management Systems (QMS)**. ISO 9001:2015 is the current standard for QMS certification.

**Key Clauses (ISO 9001:2015):**
- Clause 4: Context of the organization
- Clause 5: Leadership
- Clause 6: Planning
- Clause 7: Support
- Clause 8: Operation
- Clause 9: Performance evaluation
- Clause 10: Improvement

## 2.5 Six Sigma Methodology

**Six Sigma** is a data-driven methodology aimed at **near-perfect quality (3.4 DPMO)**.

### 2.5.1 DMAIC Framework (5 Phases)

| Phase | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **D**efine | Identify the problem, customer, and project scope (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality). |
| **M**easure | Quantify the problem; baseline current process performance. |
| **A**nalyze | Identify root causes of defects using statistical tools. |
| **I**mprove | Implement solutions to address root causes. |
| **C**ontrol | Sustain improvements using control plans and SPC. |

## 2.6 Bench Marking

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Bench Marking is the systematic process of comparing one's business processes and performance metrics to industry bests, then using that information to improve.

**Types of Bench Marking:**
- Internal (within organization)
- Competitive (vs. direct competitors)
- Functional (vs. best-in-class in similar function)
- Generic (vs. best processes regardless of industry)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Cost of Quality | $TCQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | Sum of all four cost categories |
| COQ as % of Sales | $COQ \% = (TCQ / R) \times 100$ | Where $R$ = Revenue/Sales |
| Six Sigma Yield | $Y = 99.99966\% \text{ (6σ)}$ | Equivalent to 3.4 DPMO |
| DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | $DPMO = (D / (N \times O)) \times 10^{6}$ | Where $D$ = Defects, $N$ = Units, $O$ = Opportunities per unit |
| Process Sigma Level | $\sigma = Z_{\text{LSL}} + 1.5 \text{ (with shift)}$ | Short-term vs. long-term |
| Taguchi Loss Function | $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ | $k$ = quality loss coefficient, $m$ = target, $y$ = actual |
| Quality Loss Coefficient | $k = A_{0} / \Delta_{0}^{2}$ | $A_{0}$ = loss at tolerance limit, $\Delta_{0}$ = tolerance |
| Defect Rate Conversion | $\text{Yield} = (1 - p)^{n}$ | Where $p$ = defect probability, $n$ = number of opportunities |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Defects Per Unit (DPU)** with **DPMO**. DPU = D / N, while DPMO = D / (N × O) × 10^6. The "M" (Million) and "O" (Opportunities) are critical multipliers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Taguchi's Quality Loss Function

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Taguchi argued that any deviation from the target value $m$ results in a **loss to society**, even if the product is within specification limits.

Let the target value be $m$ and the actual measured value be $y$. The deviation is $\Delta y = y - m$.

**Assumption (Taguchi's Postulate):** The loss to society is proportional to the **square of the deviation** from the target.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Let the loss function be $L(y)$. We assume a quadratic relationship with deviation.

$$
L(y) = k (y - m)^{2}
$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality (quality loss coefficient).

**Step 2:** Determine the value of $k$ using a known boundary condition. If the deviation reaches the tolerance limit $\Delta_{0}$ (i.e., $y - m = \pm \Delta_{0}$), the loss equals the cost of replacement/repair $A_{0}$.

$$
A_{0} = k (\Delta_{0})^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $k$.

$$
k = \frac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back to obtain the complete loss function.

$$
L(y) = \frac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}} (y - m)^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even when a product is *within* specification limits, it still incurs a quality loss. Only when $y = m$ (exact target) is the loss zero. This is a **paradigm shift** from traditional "within-spec-is-acceptable" thinking.

## 3.2 Derivation: Six Sigma DPMO Calculation

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Count the number of units produced: $N$.
**Step 2:** Count the number of defects found: $D$.
**Step 3:** Count the number of opportunities per unit for a defect to occur: $O$.
**Step 4:** Calculate the total number of defect opportunities.

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = N \times O
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the defect rate (proportion of defects).

$$
p = \frac{D}{N \times O}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to DPMO (defects per million opportunities).

$$
DPMO = p \times 10^{6} = \frac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 7:** Determine the sigma level by referring to standard normal tables (with a 1.5σ long-term shift included).

$$
Z_{\text{long-term}} = Z_{\text{short-term}} - 1.5
$$

For **Six Sigma quality**: $DPMO = 3.4$, and $\sigma = 6.0$ (with the 1.5σ shift adjustment).

## 3.3 Derivative: Total Cost of Quality Optimization

**Problem:** Minimize $TCQ(P) = P + A(P) + IF(P) + EF(P)$ where $P$ is the prevention cost.

**Step 1:** As $P$ increases:
- $A$ initially increases (more inspection), then decreases.
- $IF$ and $EF$ decrease monotonically (fewer defects).

**Step 2:** The optimal $P^*$ is found by:

$$
\frac{d(TCQ)}{dP} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** The second derivative confirms a minimum:

$$
\frac{d^{2}(TCQ)}{dP^{2}} > 0
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting empirical relationships (assuming linear decline of failure costs with prevention):

$$
IF(P) = IF_{0} - \alpha P
$$

$$
EF(P) = EF_{0} - \beta P
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are sensitivity coefficients.

**Step 5:** Total cost becomes:

$$
TCQ(P) = P + A + (IF_{0} - \alpha P) + (EF_{0} - \beta P)
$$

$$
TCQ(P) = (1 - \alpha - \beta) P + (A + IF_{0} + EF_{0})
$$

**Step 6:** Minimizing $TCQ$ w.r.t $P$:

$$
\frac{d(TCQ)}{dP} = (1 - \alpha - \beta) = 0
$$

**Step 7:** This yields a **trivial solution** only if $\alpha + \beta = 1$, which is generally not the case. The realistic model uses **nonlinear** relationships:

$$
IF(P) = IF_{0} \cdot e^{-aP}
$$

$$
EF(P) = EF_{0} \cdot e^{-bP}
$$

$$
TCQ(P) = P + A + IF_{0} e^{-aP} + EF_{0} e^{-bP}
$$

**Step 8:** Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TCQ)}{dP} = 1 - a \cdot IF_{0} e^{-aP} - b \cdot EF_{0} e^{-bP} = 0
$$

**Step 9:** Solve numerically for $P^*$. The **optimal prevention cost** is found where the marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings from reduced failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The PAF model and Taguchi's loss function both emphasize that **quality is an investment**, not a cost. The economic optimum occurs when marginal prevention investment equals marginal failure cost savings.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Six Sigma DPMO Computation

**Given:**
- Units produced: $N = 1000$
- Defects found: $D = 25$
- Opportunities per unit: $O = 4$

**Step 1:** Calculate total opportunities.

$$
N \times O = 1000 \times 4 = 4000
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate defect rate.

$$
p = \frac{D}{N \times O} = \frac{25}{4000} = 0.00625
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to DPMO.

$$
DPMO = 0.00625 \times 10^{6} = 6250
$$

**Step 4:** Using standard normal tables, find the corresponding sigma level.

For DPMO = 6250, the short-term sigma is approximately **4.15σ**, and the long-term sigma (with 1.5σ shift) is:

$$
\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = 4.15 - 1.5 = 2.65
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The process is performing at approximately **2.65 sigma**, which is far below the Six Sigma target of 6.0. Significant improvement is required.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Taguchi Loss Function

**Given:**
- Target dimension: $m = 50$ mm
- Tolerance limit: $\Delta_{0} = 0.5$ mm
- Loss at tolerance: $A_{0} = 1000$ Rs.
- Actual measurement: $y = 50.2$ mm

**Step 1:** Calculate the quality loss coefficient.

$$
k = \frac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}} = \frac{1000}{(0.5)^{2}} = \frac{1000}{0.25} = 4000 \text{ Rs/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the deviation.

$$
\Delta y = y - m = 50.2 - 50 = 0.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the quality loss.

$$
L(y) = k (\Delta y)^{2} = 4000 \times (0.2)^{2} = 4000 \times 0.04 = 160 \text{ Rs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Even though the part is within the tolerance limit ($0.2 < 0.5$ mm), there is still a **Rs. 160 loss to society**. This is the heart of Taguchi's philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quality Cost Behavior Curve (PAF Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Prevention Cost P]:::up
    nodeB[Appraisal Cost A]:::mid
    nodeC[Internal Failure Cost IF]:::down
    nodeD[External Failure Cost EF]:::down
    nodeE[Total Cost of Quality TCQ]:::total
    nodeF[Optimal P Point]:::optimum
    nodeG[Customer Dissatisfaction Rising]:::alert

    nodeA -->|Increases| nodeF
    nodeB -->|First increases then decreases| nodeF
    nodeC -->|Decreases exponentially| nodeF
    nodeD -->|Decreases exponentially| nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeG

    classDef up fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef down fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef total fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef optimum fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:4px,color:#000000
    classDef alert fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** As prevention cost **P** increases, total quality cost first decreases (because failure costs drop faster than prevention rises), reaches a **minimum**, and then rises (prevention becomes excessive). The minimum is the **economic optimum**.

## 4.2 DMAIC Six Sigma Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeD1[D1 DEFINE]:::phase1
    nodeD2[Identify Problem CTQ Voice of Customer VOC Project Charter]:::step1
    nodeD3[M2 MEASURE]:::phase2
    nodeD4[Collect Data Baseline Capability DPMO Sigma Level]:::step2
    nodeD5[A3 ANALYZE]:::phase3
    nodeD6[Root Cause Analysis Pareto Fishbone Regression]:::step3
    nodeD7[I4 IMPROVE]:::phase4
    nodeD8[Design Experiments Pilot Solutions Optimize]:::step4
    nodeD9[C5 CONTROL]:::phase5
    nodeD10[Control Plans SPC Mistake Proofing Documentation]:::step5

    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3
    nodeD3 --> nodeD4
    nodeD4 --> nodeD5
    nodeD5 --> nodeD6
    nodeD6 --> nodeD7
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8
    nodeD8 --> nodeD9
    nodeD9 --> nodeD10
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cost of Quality. List the four categories of quality costs in the PAF model. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Cost of Quality (COQ) is the sum of all costs incurred to prevent, detect, and correct defects in a product or service. It is a financial measure that quantifies the economic impact of quality-related activities.

**Four Categories (PAF Model):**

1. **Prevention Costs (P):** Costs of preventing defects (training, quality planning, process design).
2. **Appraisal Costs (A):** Costs of detecting defects (inspection, testing, calibration).
3. **Internal Failure Costs (IF):** Costs of defects found before delivery (scrap, rework).
4. **External Failure Costs (EF):** Costs of defects found after delivery (warranty, returns, loss of goodwill).

**Formula:** $TCQ = P + A + IF + EF$

### Question 2: What is Six Sigma? Briefly explain the DMAIC methodology. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Six Sigma** is a data-driven quality management methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, corresponding to a process performance of 6 standard deviations from the mean.

**DMAIC Methodology:**

| Phase | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **D**efine | Identify the problem, customer (VOC), and project CTQs. |
| **M**easure | Establish baseline; measure current process performance (DPMO, sigma level). |
| **A**nalyze | Identify root causes of defects using statistical tools. |
| **I**mprove | Develop and implement solutions to eliminate root causes. |
| **C**ontrol | Standardize improvements; maintain gains using SPC and control plans. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Question A(a):** [7 Marks] Explain the Taguchi Loss Function in detail. Derive the expression for the quality loss coefficient and compute the loss for a given example.

**Question A(b):** [7 Marks] A manufacturing process produces 5000 units per day. A quality audit reveals 80 defects in one week (5 working days). If each unit has 3 opportunities for a defect, calculate the DPMO and determine the long-term sigma level.

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Taguchi Loss Function:**

Taguchi proposed that any deviation from the target value of a quality characteristic causes a loss to society, even if the deviation is within specification limits.

**Loss Function Expression:**

$$
L(y) = k (y - m)^{2}
$$

where:
- $L(y)$ = Quality loss (Rs.)
- $y$ = Actual measured value
- $m$ = Target value
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient

**Derivation of $k$:**

When the deviation $y - m$ reaches the tolerance limit $\pm \Delta_{0}$, the loss equals $A_{0}$ (cost of rework/replacement).

$$
A_{0} = k (\Delta_{0})^{2}
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
k = \frac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}}
$$

**Complete Loss Function:**

$$
L(y) = \frac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}} (y - m)^{2}
$$

**Example Computation:**

**Given:**
- Target: $m = 100$ mm
- Tolerance: $\Delta_{0} = 1$ mm
- Loss at tolerance: $A_{0} = 500$ Rs
- Actual: $y = 100.3$ mm

**Step 1:** Compute $k$.

$$
k = \frac{500}{(1)^{2}} = 500 \text{ Rs/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute deviation.

$$
y - m = 100.3 - 100 = 0.3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute loss.

$$
L(y) = 500 \times (0.3)^{2} = 500 \times 0.09 = 45 \text{ Rs}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Despite being within tolerance, the part incurs a Rs. 45 loss. This drives Taguchi's philosophy of **minimizing variation, not just meeting specs**.

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Given:**
- Units per day: $N_{\text{day}} = 5000$
- Days: 5
- Total defects in week: $D = 80$
- Opportunities per unit: $O = 3$

**Step 1:** Total units produced in 5 days.

$$
N = 5000 \times 5 = 25000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2:** Total defect opportunities.

$$
N \times O = 25000 \times 3 = 75000
$$

**Step 3:** Defect rate.

$$
p = \frac{80}{75000} = 0.0010667
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to DPMO.

$$
DPMO = 0.0010667 \times 10^{6} = 1066.67 \approx 1067 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 5:** Find the short-term sigma level from standard normal tables.

For DPMO = 1067, the short-term Z-value is approximately $4.20\sigma$ (i.e., the area in the upper tail beyond $\pm 4.20\sigma$ is roughly 1067 per million).

**Step 6:** Compute the long-term sigma level (with 1.5σ shift).

$$
\sigma_{\text{long-term}} = 4.20 - 1.5 = 2.70 \sigma
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The process is at approximately **2.70 sigma**, far from the Six Sigma target of 6.0. Improvement initiatives (DMAIC) are required.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Question B(a):** [7 Marks] Discuss the contributions of W. Edwards Deming and Joseph M. Juran to the field of quality management. Explain the Deming Cycle (PDCA) with a suitable diagram.

**Question B(b):** [7 Marks] A company has the following annual quality costs. Calculate the Total Cost of Quality (TCQ) and the COQ as a percentage of sales, given annual sales of Rs. 5 crores. If the company invests an additional Rs. 2 lakhs in prevention, and this results in a 30% reduction in internal failure and 50% reduction in external failure, calculate the new TCQ and comment.

| Cost Category | Amount (Rs. Lakhs) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Prevention | 2.0 |
| Appraisal | 3.0 |
| Internal Failure | 6.0 |
| External Failure | 4.0 |

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993):**
- American statistician and quality pioneer.
- Introduced **14 Points for Management** to transform business effectiveness.
- Emphasized **system thinking**, statistical knowledge, and continuous improvement.
- Developed the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** based on Walter Shewhart's work.

**Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008):**
- Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — a customer-oriented definition.
- Proposed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement.
- Emphasized that quality is achieved through **management commitment** at all levels.

**The Deming PDCA Cycle:**

| Step | Action |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Plan** | Define objectives, identify processes, plan changes. |
| **Do** | Execute the plan on a small scale. |
| **Check** | Measure results and compare against expected. |
| **Act** | Standardize improvements or restart the cycle. |

The PDCA cycle is a **continuous improvement loop**. The cycle repeats iteratively to drive ongoing quality enhancement. (Refer to Section 4.3 for the diagram.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** Deming focused on **process variation reduction**, while Juran focused on **planning and breakthrough improvements**. Both are complementary philosophies in TQM.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given Data (in Rs. Lakhs):**
- Prevention ($P$) = 2.0
- Appraisal ($A$) = 3.0
- Internal Failure ($IF$) = 6.0
- External Failure ($EF$) = 4.0
- Sales ($R$) = 500 Lakhs (Rs. 5 crores)

**Step 1:** Calculate initial TCQ.

$$
TCQ_{\text{initial}} = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
TCQ_{\text{initial}} = 2.0 + 3.0 + 6.0 + 4.0 = 15.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate initial COQ %.

$$
COQ \%_{\text{initial}} = \frac{15.0}{500} \times 100 = 3.0\%
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate new values after additional prevention investment of Rs. 2 Lakhs.

New Prevention Cost:

$$
P_{\text{new}} = 2.0 + 2.0 = 4.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

New Internal Failure (30% reduction):

$$
IF_{\text{new}} = 6.0 \times (1 - 0.30) = 6.0 \times 0.70 = 4.2 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

New External Failure (50% reduction):

$$
EF_{\text{new}} = 4.0 \times (1 - 0.50) = 4.0 \times 0.50 = 2.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate new TCQ.

$$
TCQ_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{new}} + A + IF_{\text{new}} + EF_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
TCQ_{\text{new}} = 4.0 + 3.0 + 4.2 + 2.0 = 13.2 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate savings.

$$
\text{Savings} = TCQ_{\text{initial}} - TCQ_{\text{new}} = 15.0 - 13.2 = 1.8 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate new COQ %.

$$
COQ \%_{\text{new}} = \frac{13.2}{500} \times 100 = 2.64\%
$$

**Comment:**
- The TCQ decreased by **Rs. 1.8 Lakhs** despite spending Rs. 2 Lakhs more on prevention.
- Net gain in failure cost reduction (Rs. 3.8 Lakhs) exceeded the prevention cost (Rs. 2 Lakhs), validating the **PAF economic model**.
- The COQ percentage dropped from 3.0% to 2.64%, improving profitability.
- The investment in prevention is **economically justified** in this scenario.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — COQ Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Always express all costs in the **same unit** (Lakhs or Crores) before computing TCQ or COQ %.
> 2. **Forgetting appraisal cost:** Students often omit the appraisal cost from TCQ. The complete formula is $TCQ = P + A + IF + EF$.
> 3. **Percentage base error:** COQ % is computed with respect to **Sales/Revenue**, not total cost or production cost. Using the wrong denominator will lead to a full 7-mark loss in a sub-part.
> 4. **Order of operations:** Apply the percentage reduction to the **original IF/EF** values, not the post-adjustment values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Other Aspects of Quality**

- **Quality** is multi-dimensional: User-based, Manufacturing-based, Product-based (Garvin).
- **PAF Model** of Cost of Quality has **four categories**: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- **TCQ Formula:** $TCQ = P + A + IF + EF$
- **COQ %:** $COQ \% = (TCQ / \text{Sales}) \times 100$
- **Optimal COQ** occurs when marginal prevention cost equals marginal failure cost savings.
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$, where $k = A_{0} / \Delta_{0}^{2}$.
- **Key insight of Taguchi:** Even within-spec products incur quality loss — minimize variation, not just meet specifications.
- **Deming's PDCA:** Plan, Do, Check, Act — iterative continuous improvement.
- **Deming's 14 Points** focus on system thinking, leadership, training, and breaking departmental barriers.
- **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- **Crosby's "Zero Defects"** and "Quality is Free" philosophies.
- **Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram** for cause-and-effect analysis.
- **Six Sigma target:** 3.4 DPMO, corresponding to 6σ quality (with 1.5σ long-term shift).
- **DMAIC:** Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.
- **DPMO Formula:** $DPMO = (D / (N \times O)) \times 10^{6}$
- **Process Sigma Level:** $\sigma = Z_{\text{short}} - 1.5$ (long-term).
- **ISO 9001:2015** follows the PDCA structure across its 10 clauses.
- **Deming Prize** (Japan) and **Malcolm Baldrige Award** (USA) reward company-wide quality practices.
- **Bench Marking** types: Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic.
- **TQM Pillars:** Customer focus, employee involvement, continuous improvement, process approach, fact-based decisions.
- **Statistical SQC Tools Used in TQM:** Control Charts, Pareto Charts, Histograms, Scatter Diagrams, Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, Flowcharts, Check Sheets (the "7 QC Tools").
- **Prevention vs. Detection:** Prevention is **proactive** and cheaper; Appraisal is **reactive** and more expensive in the long run.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For Module 1 "Other Aspects of Quality," questions typically focus on **definitions, guru philosophies, COQ computations, and Six Sigma metrics**. Memorize the PAF formula, Taguchi loss function, and DPMO computation. Draw **clear, labeled diagrams** for PDCA and DMAIC cycles — KTU examiners allocate marks for visual quality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality and Profitability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality and Profitability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Quality

In the context of KTU's Statistical Quality Control syllabus, **Quality** is formally defined as the **totality of characteristics of an entity (product, process, or service) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs** (adapted from ISO 8402 / ISO 9000).

In simpler KTU-exam oriented terms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality = Fitness for Use (Juran)** or **Conformance to Requirements (Crosby)** or **A Predictable Degree of Uniformity and Dependability at Low Cost and Adapted to the Market (Deming)**.

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand the **economic definition of quality**: the ability of a product/service to deliver customer satisfaction at the **lowest possible total cost** to the organization and society.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Profitability

**Profitability** is the ability of a firm to generate **financial gains (profit)** relative to its revenue, operating costs, and capital base over a specific period.

$$
\text{Profit Margin (\%)} = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100
$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Coffee Shop" View of Quality

Imagine two coffee shops on the same street:

- **Shop A** serves coffee quickly, but sometimes cold, sometimes spilled. They have very few customers willing to pay more.
- **Shop B** invests in trained baristas (prevention cost) and checks every cup before serving (appraisal cost). They rarely get complaints.

Shop B spends *more* initially, but because they **don't lose customers** (lower failure costs), their **profit margin** is higher. This is the heart of **Quality and Profitability**: *Strategic quality spending INCREASES profitability*, while poor quality silently *eats* profit away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Favourite):** Quality is **NOT** free. You must *invest* in it. But the **return on that investment** comes from reduced failures, higher customer retention, and lower re-work.

### 1.4 Standard Definitions You Must Memorize

| Authority | Definition of Quality |
|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Fitness for Use |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability, at low cost, suited to the market |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Conformance to Requirements |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | The total composite product and service characteristics of marketing, engineering, manufacture, and maintenance through which the product and service in use will meet the expectations of the customer |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Quality is the loss imparted to society from the time the product is shipped |
| **ISO 9000** | Degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfils requirements |

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in SQC

Statistical Quality Control is fundamentally a **profit-engineering discipline**. You cannot justify control charts, sampling plans, or process capability studies without linking them to **monetary outcomes**. This module establishes that bridge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's Framework)

David Garvin identified **eight dimensions of quality** that are essential for KTU exams:

1. **Performance** – Primary operating characteristics.
2. **Features** – Secondary characteristics that supplement basic functioning.
3. **Reliability** – Probability of failure-free operation over a period.
4. **Conformance** – Degree to which product meets pre-established standards.
5. **Durability** – Length of product life before deterioration.
6. **Serviceability** – Speed, courtesy, and competence of repair.
7. **Aesthetics** – Sensory characteristics (look, feel, sound).
8. **Perceived Quality** – Reputation / brand image.

### 2.2 Quality and Productivity – The Core Link

Quality and productivity are **interdependent**:

- **Poor quality → Lower productivity** (due to rework, scrap, warranty claims, lost time).
- **Good quality → Higher productivity** (smooth flow, fewer stoppages, lower inspection effort over time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's Chain Reaction:** *Improve Quality → Decrease Costs → Improve Productivity → Capture the Market → Stay in Business → Provide Jobs.*

### 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) – The PAF Model

The **PAF Model** classifies all quality-related costs into **four categories**:

#### **P – Prevention Costs**
Costs incurred to **prevent defects** from occurring in the first place.
- Quality planning, training, product/process design reviews, supplier capability reviews, quality improvement projects.

#### **A – Appraisal Costs**
Costs incurred to **detect defects** through inspection and testing.
- Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final inspection, calibration of test equipment, quality audits.

#### **IF – Internal Failure Costs**
Costs incurred when defective products are found **before** reaching the customer.
- Scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure analysis, downtime due to defects.

#### **EF – External Failure Costs**
Costs incurred when defective products reach the **customer**.
- Warranty claims, returns, recalls, complaints handling, field service, loss of goodwill, legal liabilities.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$ | Total Quality Cost |
| 2 | $\text{COG} = \dfrac{\text{Sales}}{\text{TQC}}$ | Cost-of-Quality ratio (lower is better) |
| 3 | $\text{Ratio}_{P:IF:EF} = \text{typically shifts toward P over time}$ | PAF distribution trend |
| 4 | $\text{Optimal Q-Level: } \dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 0$ | Minimum total cost condition |
| 5 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{(Mfg Cost + TQC + Other Costs)}$ | Profit equation |
| 6 | $\text{ROQ} = \dfrac{\text{Quality Benefit} - \text{Quality Cost}}{\text{Quality Cost}}$ | Return on Quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** In markdown tables, absolute-value bars are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table formatting. The above formulas use NO `|` characters for this reason.

### 2.5 The "Iceberg" Model of Quality Costs

External failure costs are **visible** (warranty claims, returns) – like the tip of an iceberg. Internal failure and appraisal costs are **hidden** beneath the surface. Prevention costs are **invisible investments** that prevent the iceberg from forming.

### 2.6 The Optimal Quality Level Theory

As the quality level (defect rate) **decreases**:
- **Prevention costs increase** (exponentially)
- **Appraisal costs rise then fall** (we inspect less when we trust the process)
- **Internal failure costs decrease** (sharply)
- **External failure costs decrease** (most sharply)

The **optimal quality level** occurs where **Total Quality Cost (TQC) is minimized**. Beyond this, over-investment in prevention yields diminishing returns.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing**: COQ analysis reveals that scrap and rework often consume 15–40% of operating costs.
- **Software Industry**: Bug prevention (P) is 50–100× cheaper than fixing bugs post-release (EF).
- **Service Sector (Banking, Healthcare)**: Failure costs manifest as lost trust, lawsuits, and churn.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Quality Level (Marginal Analysis)

Let:
- $Q$ = Quality level (higher Q = fewer defects, e.g., measured as conformance percentage).
- $P(Q)$ = Prevention costs, an increasing function of $Q$.
- $A(Q)$ = Appraisal costs, often modeled as convex or decreasing.
- $IF(Q)$ = Internal failure costs, decreasing in $Q$.
- $EF(Q)$ = External failure costs, sharply decreasing in $Q$.

**Step 1:** Define Total Quality Cost.

$$
TQC(Q) = P(Q) + A(Q) + IF(Q) + EF(Q)
$$

**Step 2:** Take the first derivative of TQC with respect to Q (rate of change of cost as quality improves).

$$
\frac{d\,TQC(Q)}{dQ} = P'(Q) + A'(Q) + IF'(Q) + EF'(Q)
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $P'(Q) > 0$ (prevention rises with Q), and $IF'(Q) < 0$, $EF'(Q) < 0$ (failures fall with Q). Appraisal derivative $A'(Q)$ may be positive initially.

**Step 4:** At the **optimal quality level $Q^*$**, the marginal increase in prevention/appraisal equals the marginal decrease in failure costs. Mathematically, the slope of TQC = 0.

$$
\left.\frac{d\,TQC}{dQ}\right|_{Q=Q^*} = 0
$$

This gives:

$$
P'(Q^*) + A'(Q^*) = -IF'(Q^*) - EF'(Q^*)
$$

**Interpretation:** The marginal cost of *preventing one more unit of defect* must equal the marginal benefit of *avoiding one more failure*.

**Step 5:** Second-order condition for minimum (TQC is U-shaped or has a convex bottom).

$$
\left.\frac{d^{2}\,TQC}{dQ^{2}}\right|_{Q=Q^*} > 0
$$

### 3.2 Numerical Illustration: COQ at Three Quality Levels

Consider a manufacturing firm producing 10,000 units/month. Assume:
- Prevention cost per unit = ₹5 (training, planning)
- Appraisal cost per unit = ₹4 (inspection)
- Internal failure cost per defective unit = ₹200
- External failure cost per defective unit = ₹800

| Quality Level (% Conformance) | Defect Rate (%) | Defective Units | P (₹) | A (₹) | IF (₹) | EF (₹) | TQC (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 95% | 5% | 500 | 50,000 | 40,000 | 1,00,000 | 4,00,000 | **5,90,000** |
| 98% | 2% | 200 | 80,000 | 45,000 | 40,000 | 1,60,000 | **3,25,000** |
| 99.5% | 0.5% | 50 | 1,50,000 | 50,000 | 10,000 | 40,000 | **2,50,000** |

**Observation:** As quality improves, P and A rise, but IF and EF drop faster. **Optimal is near 99.5%**.

### 3.3 Return on Quality (ROQ) – Worked Example

**Given:**
- Annual revenue: ₹10 Crore
- Current TQC: ₹2 Crore
- Quality improvement investment (additional prevention): ₹40 Lakh
- Resulting TQC reduction: ₹80 Lakh (saved failure costs)
- Revenue gain from improved reputation: ₹50 Lakh

**Step 1: Quality Benefit**
$$
B = \text{TQC Reduction} + \text{Revenue Gain} = 80{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Additional Cost**
$$
C = 40{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Net Gain**
$$
\text{Net} = B - C = 90{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: ROQ**
$$
ROQ = \frac{B - C}{C} = \frac{90{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 2.25 \;\text{or}\; 225\%
$$

**Conclusion:** For every ₹1 invested in quality, the firm gets back ₹2.25 — a strong profitability case.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cause–Effect of Quality Spending on Profit

| Driver | Direction on Cost | Direction on Profit | Net Profit Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Prevention | ↑ Short-term cost | ↓ Long-term failure cost | **↑ Positive** |
| ↑ Appraisal | ↑ Cost | ↓ Defect leakage | **↑ Positive** (up to optimum) |
| ↓ Internal Failure | ↓ Scrap/rework | ↓ Wastage | **↑ Positive** |
| ↓ External Failure | ↓ Warranty/returns | ↑ Brand value | **↑ Strongly Positive** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Why does prevention cost money but improve profit?"* Answer with the **chain reaction**: Prevention → Fewer defects → Lower IF/EF → Lower TQC → Higher margin → Higher profit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cost of Quality (PAF) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QInput[Quality Investment Input] --> P[Prevention Costs]
    QInput --> A[Appraisal Costs]
    Defects[Defects Occur in Process] --> IF[Internal Failure Costs]
    Defects --> EF[External Failure Costs]
    P --> TQCNode[Total Quality Cost]
    A --> TQCNode
    IF --> TQCNode
    EF --> TQCNode
    TQCNode --> ProfitNode[Profitability Outcome]
    ProfitNode --> P1[Higher Customer Retention]
    ProfitNode --> P2[Lower Warranty Costs]
    ProfitNode --> P3[Better Brand Image]
    ProfitNode --> P4[Reduced Rework]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Deming's Chain Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Improve Quality] --> A2[Decrease Costs]
    A2 --> A3[Improve Productivity]
    A3 --> A4[Capture the Market]
    A4 --> A5[Stay in Business]
    A5 --> A6[Provide Jobs]
    A6 --> ProfitLink[Increase Profitability]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph: Optimal Quality Level Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    XAxis[Quality Level Q increases left to right]
    YAxis[Cost in Rupees]
    PCurve[Prevention Cost rises exponentially] --> SumNode
    ACurve[Appraisal Cost first rises then falls] --> SumNode
    IFCurve[Internal Failure Cost falls sharply] --> SumNode
    EFCurve[External Failure Cost falls very sharply] --> SumNode
    SumNode[TQC reaches minimum at Q Star]
    SumNode --> Beyond[Beyond Q Star prevention overshoot increases TQC]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Tree: Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QD[Quality Dimensions] --> QD1[Performance]
    QD --> QD2[Features]
    QD --> QD3[Reliability]
    QD --> QD4[Conformance]
    QD --> QD5[Durability]
    QD --> QD6[Serviceability]
    QD --> QD7[Aesthetics]
    QD --> QD8[Perceived Quality]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams represent **functional/architectural relationships** since this is a management-statistics topic without physical hardware to sketch. Each diagram visually maps how quality levers connect to profitability outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define quality according to Juran and Philip Crosby.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Juran** defines quality as **"Fitness for Use"** – the product's ability to meet customer needs in actual use conditions (1 mark).
- **Philip B. Crosby** defines quality as **"Conformance to Requirements"** – the product must meet the pre-specified engineering and design specifications (1 mark).
- Both definitions emphasize customer satisfaction but from different angles: Juran from market perspective, Crosby from specification perspective (1 mark for comparison).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] List the four categories of Cost of Quality (COQ) in the PAF model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **P – Prevention Costs:** Costs to prevent defects (training, planning) (0.75 mark).
- **A – Appraisal Costs:** Costs of inspection and testing (0.75 mark).
- **IF – Internal Failure Costs:** Scrap, rework before delivery (0.75 mark).
- **EF – External Failure Costs:** Warranty, returns, complaints after delivery (0.75 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** – *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept and its four components with suitable examples for a manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company produces 50,000 components per month. The following data is provided:
- Prevention cost: ₹2 per unit
- Appraisal cost: ₹3 per unit
- Internal failure cost: ₹150 per defective unit
- External failure cost: ₹500 per defective unit
- Current defect rate: 4%

The company plans to invest an additional ₹1,50,000 per month in prevention, which will reduce the defect rate to 1%. Calculate the **Total Quality Cost** at both defect rates and comment on the decision. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) – 7 Marks:**

1. **Definition of COQ** (1 mark): COQ is the total cost incurred to ensure product quality and the losses due to lack of quality.

2. **Prevention Costs (1.5 marks):** Costs incurred to prevent defects. Examples:
   - Quality training programs for employees.
   - Product and process design reviews.
   - Supplier evaluation and selection.
   - Quality improvement projects (Six Sigma, Kaizen).

3. **Appraisal Costs (1.5 marks):** Costs of detecting defects through inspection.
   - Incoming material inspection.
   - In-process and final inspections.
   - Calibration of measuring equipment.
   - Quality audits.

4. **Internal Failure Costs (1.5 marks):** Costs when defects are detected *before* shipment.
   - Scrap and rework.
   - Re-inspection.
   - Failure mode analysis.
   - Production downtime due to defects.

5. **External Failure Costs (1.5 marks):** Costs when defects reach the customer.
   - Warranty claims and field repairs.
   - Product recalls.
   - Customer complaints handling.
   - Loss of goodwill, legal liabilities, return shipping.

---

**Model Solution (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate defective units at 4% defect rate.**

$$
\text{Defects} = 50{,}000 \times 0.04 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: TQC at 4% defect rate.**

$$
P = 50{,}000 \times 2 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
A = 50{,}000 \times 3 = 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
IF = 2{,}000 \times 150 = 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
EF = 2{,}000 \times 500 = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
TQC_1 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Defective units at 1% defect rate.**

$$
\text{Defects} = 50{,}000 \times 0.01 = 500 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4: New prevention cost (including additional investment).**

$$
P_{\text{new}} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5: TQC at 1% defect rate.**

$$
P = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
A = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ (assumed unchanged)}
$$

$$
IF = 500 \times 150 = 75{,}000
$$

$$
EF = 500 \times 500 = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
TQC_2 = 2{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}25{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Savings and Decision.**

$$
\text{Savings} = TQC_1 - TQC_2 = 15{,}50{,}000 - 7{,}25{,}000 = 8{,}25{,}000
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The additional investment of ₹1,50,000 yields savings of ₹8,25,000 per month. **The investment is highly justified** – the company saves ₹5.50 for every ₹1 invested. Profitability improves significantly.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** – *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss **Deming's Chain Reaction** and explain how it links quality improvement to profitability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **concept of optimal quality level**. Derive the condition for optimality mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Deming's Chain Reaction (5 marks):**

1. **Improve Quality** (1 mark) – Through process control, statistical methods, training, design improvement.
2. **Decrease Costs** (1 mark) – Less rework, scrap, warranty claims; lower inspection cost.
3. **Improve Productivity** (1 mark) – Smooth workflow, fewer interruptions, better morale.
4. **Capture the Market** (1 mark) – Customer satisfaction builds loyalty and pulls market share.
5. **Stay in Business / Provide Jobs** (1 mark) – Long-term survival and growth.

**Link to Profitability (2 marks):** Each link in the chain reduces cost or increases revenue. Cumulative effect → higher profit margin, better competitive position, sustainable growth.

---

**Model Solution (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Diagram Description (2 marks):** Draw Total Quality Cost (TQC) as a U-shaped curve against quality level Q. Show four curves:
- Prevention cost (rising exponentially).
- Appraisal cost (gentle rise then fall).
- Internal failure (falling sharply).
- External failure (falling very sharply).
- Sum of all four = TQC with a minimum at $Q^*$.

**Mathematical Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $TQC(Q) = P(Q) + A(Q) + IF(Q) + EF(Q)$.

At optimum:
$$
\frac{d\,TQC}{dQ}\bigg|_{Q=Q^*} = 0
$$

Equivalently:
$$
\frac{dP}{dQ} + \frac{dA}{dQ} + \frac{dIF}{dQ} + \frac{dEF}{dQ} = 0
$$

Since $IF$ and $EF$ fall with Q, their derivatives are negative; $P$ and $A$ generally rise. So:
$$
\frac{dP}{dQ} + \frac{dA}{dQ} = -\left(\frac{dIF}{dQ} + \frac{dEF}{dQ}\right)
$$

**Economic Interpretation:** The marginal cost of preventing one more defect equals the marginal benefit of avoiding one more failure. Second-order condition $\frac{d^2 TQC}{dQ^2} > 0$ confirms minimum.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Quality & Profitability Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Quality is free" with "Quality costs nothing"** – KTU expects you to say: *Quality is NOT free; investment in quality yields ROI.*
> 2. **Mixing up Internal and External Failure Costs** – Internal = before customer (scrap/rework); External = after customer (warranty/returns).
> 3. **Forgetting units in COQ numericals** – Always write per-unit cost and total cost separately.
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram in optimal quality level question** – A U-curve sketch is *mandatory*; expect -1 mark if missing.
> 5. **Stating Deming's Chain Reaction out of order** – The chain is sequential; any reordering loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Ignoring Prevention cost in COQ totals** – Many students add only A + IF + EF. Always include all four.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality Definitions**: Juran (Fitness for Use), Crosby (Conformance), Deming (Uniformity at low cost), Taguchi (Loss to society).
- **Eight Dimensions (Garvin)**: Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **PAF Model**: Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure = Total Quality Cost.
- **Key Equation**: $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$.
- **Deming's Chain Reaction**: Quality → Costs↓ → Productivity↑ → Market↑ → Survival.
- **Optimal Quality Level**: Occurs where marginal prevention cost = marginal failure savings; TQC is minimized.
- **ROQ Formula**: $ROQ = \dfrac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$.
- **Iceberg Model**: External failures are visible; internal failures are hidden but larger.
- **Cost Ratio Trend**: Mature quality programs shift spending from IF/EF toward P (prevention-dominated COQ).
- **Profit Equation**: Profit = Revenue − Manufacturing Cost − TQC − Other Costs.
- **Numerical Must-Dos**: Always compute defective units first, then per-category costs, then sum.
- **Examiner Mindset**: They want to see the *link* between quality investment and profit increase — not isolated definitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** This topic is the **foundation** for all SQC modules (control charts, acceptance sampling, capability indices). Master the PAF model and Deming's chain reaction — they appear as 3-mark sub-questions in nearly every past paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Quality Control

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **Statistical Quality Control**, *Quality Control* is formally defined as the set of operational techniques and engineering activities employed to fulfil requirements for quality. It is a **systematic, measurement-based process** designed to monitor, evaluate, and maintain a product or service within specified tolerances.

$$
\text{Quality Control (QC)} = f(\text{Standards}, \text{Measurement}, \text{Corrective Action})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Definition (KTU Standard):** Quality Control is the regulatory process through which we measure actual quality performance, compare it with established standards (specifications/tolerances), and initiate corrective action when a significant deviation is observed.

A broader, related concept is **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** — the application of statistical methodology to monitor, control, and improve the quality of products and processes. SQC is the principal focus of this course and rests on the foundational concept that **variation is universal and quantifiable**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

The following definitions form the bedrock vocabulary of Module 1:

- **Quality (Q):** The totality of characteristics of an entity (product, process, or service) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (ISO 9000).
- **Specification:** A precise statement of the technical requirements that a product must satisfy.
- **Tolerance:** The permissible deviation from a specified nominal value, defining the **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** and the **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)**.
- **Process Mean ($\mu$):** The central tendency of the manufacturing or service process.
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** The fundamental measure of process dispersion (variability).
- **Capability:** The inherent ability of a process to produce output within specification limits.

> [!NOTE]
> In every KTU examination, the difference between **Quality Control**, **Quality Assurance**, and **Total Quality Management** is a **favourite 3-mark question**. Memorize the contrasts in Section 2.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Master Chef Inspector

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a master chef running a five-star restaurant. She does not wait for customers to complain about a salty soup. Instead, every half hour she walks to the kitchen, tastes a spoonful, checks the colour, smells the aroma, and compares it against her **recipe book** (the *specification*). If the soup is too salty, she immediately adjusts the salt dispenser (the *process*). This continuous, proactive checking is exactly what **Quality Control** does in an industry — it does not wait for a defective product to reach the customer; it inspects, measures, and corrects in real time.

This analogy is powerful because it captures the three pillars of QC: **(i) a defined standard**, **(ii) measurement against that standard**, and **(iii) corrective action** to bring the process back to the desired state.

### 1.4 The Need for Quality Control

Modern engineering systems demand QC because:

1. **Customer Expectations:** The market increasingly demands zero-defect products.
2. **Cost Reduction:** Detecting defects upstream is far cheaper than warranty claims downstream.
3. **Competitive Advantage:** Higher quality translates directly to brand equity.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Standards such as ISO 9001 mandate formal QC systems.
5. **Waste Minimization:** Reducing scrap, rework, and material wastage — aligned with lean manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution of the Quality Concept

The modern concept of quality has matured through several distinct stages. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be aware of this historical evolution:

| Era | Dominant Approach | Focus | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inspection Era (pre-1930)** | 100% inspection of finished goods | Detection of defects | Costly, late-stage, error-prone |
| **Quality Control Era (1930–1950)** | Sampling inspection, Shewhart control charts | Process control via statistics | Limited to manufacturing |
| **Quality Assurance Era (1950–1980)** | System-wide quality built into design | Prevention over detection | Bureaucratic if overdone |
| **Total Quality Management (TQM)** (1980–present) | Organization-wide, customer-centric culture | Continuous improvement | Requires cultural shift |
| **Six Sigma Era (1990–present)** | DMAIC methodology, $3.4$ defects per million | Near-perfect process | Heavy training overhead |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Module 1 questions frequently test the **shift from inspection → control → assurance → TQM**. This is not just history — it represents a philosophical shift from *finding defects* to *preventing defects*.

### 2.2 Quality Gurus and Their Contributions

Module 1 of the KTU syllabus emphasizes the philosophical foundations laid by the great quality thinkers. The Board regularly asks short notes on these pioneers:

- **W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993):** Advocated the **14 Points for Management** and the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** (also called the Deming Wheel or Shewhart Cycle). He taught that **94% of quality problems belong to the system, not the workers**.
- **Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008):** Defined quality as *"fitness for use"* and proposed the **Juran Trilogy** — *Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement*.
- **Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001):** Famous for the philosophy *"Quality is Free"* and the **Zero Defects** doctrine. He framed quality in terms of the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**.
- **Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989):** Developed the **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** for root-cause analysis and championed **Quality Circles** — small groups of workers who solve quality problems.
- **Genichi Taguchi (1924–2012):** Defined the **Quality Loss Function**, arguing that any deviation from target causes a loss to society, even if within specifications.
- **Armand V. Feigenbaum (1920–2014):** Coined the term **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in 1956, emphasizing that quality is everyone's responsibility.

### 2.3 Dimensions of Product Quality (David Garvin)

Garvin proposed **eight dimensions** that comprehensively define quality. Although KTU questions are usually conceptual, knowing these dimensions allows full credit in descriptive answers:

1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics.
2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that add value.
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time.
4. **Conformance** — Degree to which design and operating characteristics match standards.
5. **Durability** — Length of useful life.
6. **Serviceability** — Speed and ease of repair.
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound).
8. **Perceived Quality** — Subjective image of quality in the buyer's mind.

### 2.4 Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance vs. TQM

This is one of the **most frequently tested distinctions** in KTU Module 1:

| Parameter | **Quality Control (QC)** | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Operational, product-focused | Managerial, system-focused | Whole organization |
| **Orientation** | Detection of defects | Prevention of defects | Continuous improvement |
| **Approach** | Reactive | Proactive | Strategic and cultural |
| **Responsibility** | QC department / inspectors | All departments | Everyone in the organization |
| **Tools Used** | Control charts, sampling plans | Process audits, process mapping | FMEA, Six Sigma, 5S, Kaizen |
| **Goal** | Conform to specifications | Build quality into the process | Exceed customer expectations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** **QC** *checks the product*; **QA** *builds the system*; **TQM** *changes the culture*.

### 2.5 The Statistical Quality Control (SQC) Framework

SQC is the bridge between statistics and engineering quality. It is built on two pillars:

$$
\text{Statistical Quality Control (SQC)} =
\begin{cases}
\text{(i) Statistical Process Control (SPC)} & \text{— monitors the process via control charts} \\
\text{(ii) Acceptance Sampling} & \text{— accepts/rejects lots via sampling plans}
\end{cases}
$$

Both branches use the same statistical foundation: the **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** with its famous **68-95-99.7 rule**.

> [!TIP]
> The **68-95-99.7 Rule** states that for any normally distributed variable, approximately **68.27%** of observations lie within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$, **95.45%** within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$, and **99.73%** within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$. This $\pm 3\sigma$ band is the foundation of all Shewhart control charts.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 1. Every formula below is examinable, and many 14-mark questions combine two or more of them.

| Concept / Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Process Mean (sample average)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}{n}$ | Measurement units of variable |
| **Sample Standard Deviation** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Uses Bessel's correction $(n-1)$ |
| **Sample Range** | $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Quick estimate of dispersion |
| **Process Standard Deviation (from R-bar)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $d_2$ is a constant from control chart tables |
| **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless ratio; $C_p \geq 1.33$ is desirable |
| **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min \left[ \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right]$ | Accounts for off-center processes |
| **3-Sigma Control Limits (variable)** | $\text{UCL} = \mu + 3\sigma, \quad \text{LCL} = \mu - 3\sigma$ | Shewhart's foundational formula |
| **$\bar{X}$-Chart Control Limits** | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ depends on sample size $n$ |
| **$R$-Chart Control Limits** | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3$, $D_4$ are tabulated constants |
| **$p$-Chart Control Limits** | $\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p}$ = average fraction nonconforming |
| **$c$-Chart Control Limits** | $\text{UCL}_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c}$ = average number of defects per unit |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Notice that **Control Limits** (UCL/LCL) are **not** the same as **Specification Limits** (USL/LSL). Control Limits describe the *voice of the process*; Specification Limits describe the *voice of the customer*. Conflating these two will cost you 3 to 5 marks.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

QC is the invisible backbone of every high-technology industry:

- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** A single dust particle can ruin a chip. QC monitors cleanroom conditions and wafer dimensions to maintain defect densities below $0.01$ per $\text{cm}^2$.
- **Automotive Industry:** Six Sigma methodologies have reduced warranty claims to historically low levels (e.g., Toyota, Honda).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Every batch must pass strict SQC, including $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for critical quality attributes.
- **Software Engineering:** The CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) framework is essentially a **statistical quality control** system applied to software processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 3-Sigma Control Limits

This is the foundational derivation from which all Shewhart control charts descend. We assume the underlying quality characteristic $X$ follows a **normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — State the Assumptions**

Let $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, meaning the probability density function is:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Standardize the Variable**

Define the standardized variable:

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

This transforms $X$ into a standard normal $Z \sim N(0,1)$.

**Step 3 — Apply the 68-95-99.7 Rule**

From the standard normal table:

$$
P(-3 < Z < 3) = 0.9973
$$

This means that in the long run, **99.73%** of all observations will fall within the interval $[\mu - 3\sigma, \mu + 3\sigma]$, and only **0.27%** will lie outside — about **3 chances in 1000**.

**Step 4 — Define the Control Limits**

Shewhart argued that we should use this natural band as the boundary of "common cause" variation. Points outside are flagged as needing investigation. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Center Line (CL)} &= \mu \\
\text{Upper Control Limit (UCL)} &= \mu + 3\sigma \\
\text{Lower Control Limit (LCL)} &= \mu - 3\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — The Average Run Length (ARL) — Why 3-Sigma?**

The probability of a point falling outside these limits purely by chance is:

$$
p_{\text{out}} = 1 - 0.9973 = 0.0027
$$

The expected number of points before a false alarm is:

$$
\text{ARL} = \frac{1}{p_{\text{out}}} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370
$$

In other words, a false alarm occurs on average every **370 points** — a tolerable rate for most industrial processes. This is why Shewhart chose $\pm 3\sigma$ and not $\pm 2\sigma$ (ARL $\approx 22$, too noisy) or $\pm 4\sigma$ (ARL $\approx 31{,}000$, too slow to react).

### 3.2 Derivation of the $\bar{X}$-Chart Control Limits

In practice, the true parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown. We estimate them from *rational subgroups* of size $n$, taken at regular intervals from the process.

**Step 1 — Define the Subgroup Mean**

For subgroup $i$ of size $n$:

$$
\bar{X}_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 — The Grand Mean (CL)**

The centerline of the $\bar{X}$-chart is the average of all subgroup means:

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i
$$

**Step 3 — The Standard Error of the Mean**

By the Central Limit Theorem, $\bar{X}_i$ has standard error:

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the 3-Sigma Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Estimate $\sigma$ from $\bar{R}$**

Since the true $\sigma$ is unknown, we use the average subgroup range $\bar{R}$ and the tabulated constant $d_2$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

Substituting and defining $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constants $A_2, d_2, D_3, D_4$ are **tabulated for each $n$** in the standard KTU-recommended tables. The student is *not* expected to memorize them, but must know which one to use for which chart.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Process Capability Index $C_p$

The index $C_p$ measures how well the natural process spread fits inside the specification width.

**Step 1 — The Process Spread (Voice of the Process)**

A process whose output is normally distributed naturally occupies a band of width $6\sigma$ (i.e., $\pm 3\sigma$ around $\mu$):

$$
\text{Process Spread} = 6\sigma
$$

**Step 2 — The Specification Width (Voice of the Customer)**

The customer's requirement is the distance between the upper and lower spec limits:

$$
\text{Specification Width} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio**

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $C_p = 1.00$ means the process barely fits; $C_p = 1.33$ means the process has $33\%$ margin; $C_p = 1.50$ is excellent; $C_p < 1.00$ means the process cannot meet specifications.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Off-Center Index $C_{pk}$

The limitation of $C_p$ is that it assumes the process is perfectly centered. $C_{pk}$ corrects this by measuring the distance from the mean to the *nearest* specification limit.

**Step 1 — Distance from Mean to USL**

$$
d_{\text{upper}} = \text{USL} - \mu
$$

**Step 2 — Distance from Mean to LSL**

$$
d_{\text{lower}} = \mu - \text{LSL}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Two One-Sided Capability Ratios**

$$
C_{\text{pu}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{\text{pl}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Minimum**

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} = \min(C_{\text{pu}}, C_{\text{pl}}) = \min \left[ \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right]}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example**

Suppose a turning operation produces shafts with specification $24.0 \pm 0.5$ mm. After 50 measurements: $\mu = 24.1$ mm, $\sigma = 0.08$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

$$
C_p = \frac{24.5 - 23.5}{6(0.08)} = \frac{1.0}{0.48} \approx 2.08
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{24.5 - 24.1}{3(0.08)}, \frac{24.1 - 23.5}{3(0.08)}\right] = \min[1.667, 2.500] = 1.667
$$

Both indices exceed $1.33$, so the process is **capable**, though slightly off-center (the mean has drifted $0.1$ mm above the nominal $24.0$ mm).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Quality Control Operational Cycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop operational cycle** of a Quality Control system. This is the same logic embedded in the PDCA (Deming) cycle, but specialized for the QC function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Define Quality Standards and Specifications] --> B1[Measure Process Output via Sampling]
    B1 --> C1[Record Data on Control Chart]
    C1 --> D1{Point Within Control Limits}
    D1 -->|Yes| E1[Continue Process Conforming]
    D1 -->|No| F1[Flag Out of Control Signal]
    F1 --> G1[Identify Assignable Cause via Fishbone Analysis]
    G1 --> H1[Remove Root Cause and Recalibrate]
    H1 --> A1
    E1 --> I1[Periodic Capability Review Cp and Cpk]
    I1 --> J1{Capability Acceptable}
    J1 -->|Yes| A1
    J1 -->|No| K1[Initiate Quality Improvement Project]
    K1 --> A1
```

**How to read the diagram:**

- The cycle begins at **A1**, where engineering establishes the quality standard.
- At **B1**, the process is sampled and the output measured.
- **C1** records the data point on a control chart.
- The **diamond** at **D1** is the decision node: the test against UCL/LCL.
- If the point is in control, the process continues; if not, the branch on the right is taken, leading to root-cause analysis and corrective action.
- The bottom loop (**I1** → **J1** → **K1**) represents the periodic **capability review**, ensuring that even an "in-control" process is still *capable* of meeting specifications.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SQC System

The following schematic maps the **functional architecture** of a complete Statistical Quality Control system, showing the interactions between measurement, analysis, and decision subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Input Raw Material] --> P2[Production Process]
    P2 --> P3[Process Output]
    P3 --> S1[Sampling Subsystem]
    S1 --> S2[Measurement Subsystem]
    S2 --> D1[Database Storage]
    D1 --> A1[Statistical Analysis Engine]
    A1 --> C1[Control Chart Generator]
    A1 --> C2[Capability Calculator Cp Cpk]
    C1 --> V1{Validation against UCL and LCL}
    C2 --> V2{Capability Criterion Cp greater than 1.33}
    V1 -->|Out of Control| ACT1[Alarm and Stop Production]
    V1 -->|In Control| OK1[Continue Production]
    V2 -->|Not Capable| ACT2[Trigger Improvement Project]
    V2 -->|Capable| OK2[Document and Archive]
    ACT1 --> FEED[Feedback to Process Engineering]
    ACT2 --> FEED
    FEED --> P2
    OK1 --> P2
    OK2 --> P2
```

**Functional Mapping Notes:**

- The **left vertical chain** (P1 → P2 → P3) is the physical production flow.
- The **middle horizontal chain** (S1 → S2 → D1 → A1) is the data acquisition and statistical engine.
- The **right vertical chain** (V1, V2) implements the dual validation: *statistical control* (control chart test) and *engineering capability* (Cp/Cpk test).
- The **bottom feedback loop** (FEED → P2) closes the system by feeding corrective decisions back into the production process.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> Differentiate between **Quality Control (QC)** and **Quality Assurance (QA)**. Mention any four points of distinction.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quality Control | Quality Assurance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Product-oriented | Process / system-oriented |
| **Approach** | Reactive — detects defects | Proactive — prevents defects |
| **Responsibility** | QC department / inspectors | All departments |
| **Tools** | Control charts, sampling | Process audits, FMEA, 5S |
| **Philosophy** | "Inspect the output" | "Build quality in" |

*(Any four correct distinctions fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> State and briefly explain the **Juran Trilogy** of quality management.

**Model Answer:**

Joseph M. Juran defined quality management as consisting of three universal processes, the **Juran Trilogy**:

1. **Quality Planning:** Identify customers, determine their needs, develop product features, and establish process goals.
2. **Quality Control:** Evaluate actual quality performance, compare it with quality goals, and act on the difference.
3. **Quality Improvement:** Establish the infrastructure to continuously improve processes and reduce chronic quality problems.

This trilogy is widely considered the most concise and universal framework of quality management and is a standard KTU reference.

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving control-limit problems, students frequently (a) forget to state the formula *before* substituting values, (b) use the wrong tabulated constant for their sample size $n$, and (c) drop units. These three omissions typically cost **2 to 3 marks each** — a cumulative 6–9 mark loss on a single 14-mark sub-question. **Always write the formula, then the substitution, then the numerical answer with units.**

---

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **Q. (a)** **[7 Marks]**
> For a CNC turning process, the specification on the diameter of a shaft is $25.0 \pm 0.4$ mm. From 25 subgroups of size $n = 5$, the following statistics have been computed: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.05$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.32$ mm. Given that for $n = 5$, the tabulated constant is $A_2 = 0.577$. Compute the **trial control limits** for the $\bar{X}$-chart and the **process capability index $C_p$**. Interpret the results.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the formula for $\bar{X}$-chart limits**

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}, \quad
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 25.05 + (0.577)(0.32) \\
&= 25.05 + 0.1846 \\
&= 25.2346 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 25.05 - (0.577)(0.32) \\
&= 25.05 - 0.1846 \\
&= 24.8654 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate $\sigma$**

For $n = 5$, the constant $d_2 = 2.326$. Therefore:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.32}{2.326} \approx 0.1376 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_p$**

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.4 - 24.6}{6(0.1376)} = \frac{0.8}{0.8256} \approx 0.969
$$
