$$\text{USL} = x_0 + \Delta = 50.00 + 0.05 = 50.05 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LSL} = x_0 - \Delta = 50.00 - 0.05 = 49.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total tolerance width.**

$$T = \text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 50.05 - 49.95 = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the process capability index $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.10}{0.108} \approx 0.926$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.** Since $C_p < 1.0$, the process is **incapable** — even a perfectly centered process will produce defects.

**Step 5 — Compute defect rate using Z-values.**

$$Z_{\text{USL}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{50.05 - 50.00}{0.018} = \frac{0.05}{0.018} \approx 2.778$$

$$Z_{\text{LSL}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{\sigma} = \frac{50.00 - 49.95}{0.018} = \frac{0.05}{0.018} \approx 2.778$$

**Step 6 — Look up normal-table area (standard KTU table values).**

$$P(X > \text{USL}) = 1 - \Phi(2.778) \approx 1 - 0.99727 = 0.00273$$

By symmetry (process is centered):

$$P(X < \text{LSL}) = 0.00273$$

**Step 7 — Total defect proportion.**

$$P_{\text{defect}} = 0.00273 + 0.00273 = 0.00546 = 0.546\%$$

$$\text{In ppm: } 0.00546 \times 10^6 \approx 5460 \text{ ppm}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: $C_{pk}$ for an Off-Center Process

**Problem Setup:**

A resistor is specified at $R = 100 \; \Omega \pm 5 \; \Omega$. The process mean is $\mathbf{\mu = 102 \; \Omega}$ and $\mathbf{\sigma = 1.8 \; \Omega}$.

**Step 1 — Identify limits.**

$$\text{USL} = 105, \quad \text{LSL} = 95$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{105 - 102}{3 \times 1.8} = \frac{3}{5.4} = 0.5556$$

$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{102 - 95}{5.4} = \frac{7}{5.4} = 1.2963$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.5556,\; 1.2963) = 0.5556$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** Since $C_{pk} < 1.0$, the process is **incapable AND off-center** — defect rate will be high on the upper side. The remedy is to **re-center** the process to $\mu = 100$ (not necessarily to tighten it).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Specification Type to SQC Tool

| Specification Type | Data Type | Primary SQC Tool (Module 2–4) | Sampling Plan (Module 4–5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bilateral Tolerance | Variable | $\bar{X}$ & $R$ / $\bar{X}$ & $S$ charts | OC-curve based on $\sigma$ |
| Unilateral (≥ LSL) | Variable | $\bar{X}$ chart, capability $C_{pk}$ | MIL-STD-105E |
| Unilateral (≤ USL) | Variable | $\bar{X}$ chart, capability $C_{pk}$ | MIL-STD-105E |
| Attribute (Defective) | Attribute | $p$-chart, $np$-chart | ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 |
| Attribute (Defect count) | Attribute | $c$-chart, $u$-chart | Poisson OC-curve |
| Asymmetric Tolerance | Variable | Off-center $C_{pk}$ analysis | Modified OC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-1 Connection:** The choice of specification at the design stage *determines* the choice of control chart, sampling plan, and acceptance criterion used throughout production. A poorly written specification propagates into every subsequent quality decision.

## 3.4 Numerical Problem — Six-Sigma Specification Check

**Problem:** A bearing inner race is specified at $\mathbf{30.000 \text{ mm} \pm 0.010 \text{ mm}}$. The process exhibits $\mathbf{\sigma = 0.0015 \text{ mm}}$ with the mean drifting up to $\mathbf{1.5\sigma}$ from target.

**Step 1 — Compute $T$.**

$$T = 0.020 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute natural process spread $6\sigma$.**

$$6\sigma = 6 \times 0.0015 = 0.009 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare $T$ to $6\sigma$.**

$$\frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.020}{0.009} \approx 2.22$$

Since $T > 6\sigma$ but $T < 12\sigma$, the process is **capable but not Six-Sigma** capable.

**Step 4 — Compute $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{0.020}{6 \times 0.0015} = \frac{0.020}{0.009} \approx 2.22$$

**Step 5 — Six-Sigma requirement.** For $C_{pk} \geq 1.5$ (Six-Sigma) with $1.5\sigma$ drift allowance, we need $C_p \geq 2.0$. Here $C_p = 2.22$ — therefore it **meets** Six-Sigma spec.

**Step 6 — Implied defect rate.** Centered: $P \approx 2[1-\Phi(6)] \approx 2 \times 9.87 \times 10^{-10} = 1.97 \times 10^{-9}$, i.e., ~2 in a billion.
]<]minimax[>[</SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Specification of Quality — Conceptual Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Need] --> B[Design Intent]
    B --> C[Quality Characteristic Selection]
    C --> D[Nominal Value x0]
    D --> E[Tolerance Type Selection]
    E --> E1[Bilateral]
    E --> E2[Unilateral]
    E --> E3[Asymmetric]
    E1 --> F[Specification Limits USL LSL]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Data Type Classification]
    G --> G1[Variable Continuous]
    G --> G2[Attribute Discrete]
    G1 --> H[Control Chart Choice]
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1[Variable Charts Xbar R]
    H --> H2[Attribute Charts p np c u]
    F --> I[Process Capability Cp Cpk]
    I --> J{Six Sigma Capable}
    J -->|Yes| K[Sigma Level ge 6]
    J -->|No| L[Process Improvement]
    L --> M[Reduce Sigma Drift]
    L --> N[Re-center Mean]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Quality Specification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Product Spec] --> D1[Dimensional Spec]
    P --> D2[Functional Spec]
    P --> D3[Performance Spec]
    P --> D4[Material Spec]
    P --> D5[Aesthetic Spec]

    D1 --> M1[Length Diameter Weight]
    D2 --> M2[Fits Power Tolerance]
    D3 --> M3[Life Cycles Speed]
    D4 --> M4[Composition Grade]
    D5 --> M5[Color Finish Texture]

    M1 --> V[Variable Data]
    M2 --> V
    M3 --> V
    M4 --> AT[Attribute Data]
    M5 --> AT
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Specification-to-Inspection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SPEC["Specification Stage"]
        S1[Customer Requirement Document]
        S2[Design Engineering]
        S3[Specification Document]
        S4[Tolerance Stack-up Analysis]
    end

    subgraph PROC["Process Stage"]
        P1[Manufacturing Process]
        P2[Measurement & Inspection]
        P3[Data Acquisition System]
    end

    subgraph SQC["SQC Stage"]
        Q1[Control Charts]
        Q2[Capability Indices Cp Cpk]
        Q3[Sampling Plans]
        Q4[Acceptance Decision]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S4 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    Q1 --> Q4
    Q2 --> Q4
    Q3 --> Q4
```

## 4.4 Tolerance Visualization Schematic (ASCII)

```
                    USL (Upper Specification Limit)
     ──────────────────────────────────────────  ← +Δ_U
                ┌─────────────────────┐
                │     Tolerance       │
                │       Band          │       ← USL = x0 + Δ_U
                │                     │
                │      ┌─────┐        │       ← Process spread
                │      │  ░  │        │       ← Mean μ
                │      │░░░░░│        │
                │      │  ░  │        │
                │      └─────┘        │
                │                     │       ← LSL = x0 − Δ_L
                │                     │
                └─────────────────────┘
     ──────────────────────────────────────────  ← −Δ_L
                    LSL (Lower Specification Limit)

                  ←── Δ_U ──→        ←── Δ_L ──→
```

- **Inner band** = actual process spread ($\mu \pm 3\sigma$).
- **Outer band** = tolerance band.
- If the inner band is *entirely* within the outer band → process is **capable**.
]<]minimax[>[</SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Quality Specification". Distinguish between variable and attribute quality characteristics with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (KTU-Standard 3-Mark Format):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Quality Specification is a precise, documented statement of the technical, functional, and performance requirements that a product or service must satisfy, including nominal values and permissible deviations (tolerances).

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Variable | Attribute |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement | Measured on a numerical scale | Classified as conforming/non-conforming |
| Data type | Continuous | Discrete (count or category) |
| Information | More information per observation | Less information per observation |
| Example 1 | Diameter of a shaft (mm) | Number of defective bulbs in a lot |
| Example 2 | Tensile strength (MPa) | Number of scratches on a TV screen |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often quote only Juran's definition and skip Crosby's, or vice-versa. Mention *both* for full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the difference between a "defect" and a "defective" with one engineering example.**

**Model Answer:**

- A **defect** is a *non-conformance* of a unit of product to a specification; it is a **count** (e.g., 4 dents on a car door).
- A **defective** is a *unit of product* that contains one or more defects; it is a **classifier** (e.g., the car itself is "defective").

**Example:** A mobile phone with 2 dead pixels has **2 defects** but is **1 defective**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "defective = 1, defect = 0" or mixing the two — this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice between two questions is mandatory.

---

### ❓ Question A — Specification Types & Process Capability (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

A piston pin is specified with diameter **$25.000 \text{ mm} \pm 0.020 \text{ mm}$**. The manufacturing process is centered with $\sigma = 0.006 \text{ mm}$.

**Compute:**
1. The specification limits.
2. The process capability index $C_p$.
3. The percentage of defective parts produced.
4. Comment on whether the process is capable.

**Solution:**

**1. Specification Limits (1 mark):**

$$\text{USL} = 25.000 + 0.020 = 25.020 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LSL} = 25.000 - 0.020 = 24.980 \text{ mm}$$

**2. $C_p$ (2 marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.020 - 24.980}{6 \times 0.006} = \frac{0.040}{0.036} = 1.111$$

**3. Defect Rate (3 marks):**

$$Z = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{25.020 - 25.000}{0.006} = \frac{0.020}{0.006} = 3.333$$

From standard normal tables:

$$P(X > \text{USL}) = 1 - \Phi(3.333) \approx 0.000429$$

By symmetry, $P(X < \text{LSL}) = 0.000429$

$$P_{\text{defect}} = 2 \times 0.000429 = 0.000858 = 0.0858\% \approx 858 \text{ ppm}$$

**4. Comment (1 mark):**

Since $C_p = 1.111 > 1.0$ but $C_p < 1.33$, the process is **marginally capable**. Defects are produced but at a low rate. To achieve Six-Sigma quality ($C_p \ge 2.0$), $\sigma$ must be reduced to $0.00333 \text{ mm}$.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

A turned shaft has specification $40.00 \pm 0.08 \text{ mm}$. The process mean has drifted to $40.05 \text{ mm}$ and $\sigma = 0.025 \text{ mm}$.

**Calculate $C_{pk}$ and suggest remedial action.**

**Solution:**

**Specification limits (1 mark):**

$$\text{USL} = 40.08, \quad \text{LSL} = 39.92$$

**Compute $C_{pk}$ components (3 marks):**

$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{40.08 - 40.05}{3 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.03}{0.075} = 0.40$$

$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{40.05 - 39.92}{0.075} = \frac{0.13}{0.075} = 1.733$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.40,\; 1.733) = 0.40$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** $C_{pk} = 0.40 \ll 1.0$ → process is **incapable and off-center**.

**Remedial Action (2 marks):** Re-center the process by adjusting the machine tool offset so that $\mu \to 40.00$ mm. Once centered, $C_{pk} = C_p = \frac{0.16}{0.15} = 1.067$, making it marginally capable. For full Six-Sigma quality, additionally reduce $\sigma$ to $\le 0.0133$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part (b):** Most students compute $C_p$ instead of $C_{pk}$. The off-center condition requires **$C_{pk}$** specifically. Also, you must state the *remedy* (re-centering + variance reduction) — not merely flag the problem.

---

### ❓ Question B — Six-Sigma Quality & Defect Classification (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 — Alternative]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

Define **Six-Sigma quality**. A manufacturing process has $\sigma = 0.005 \text{ mm}$, $\mu = 50.000 \text{ mm}$, with specification $50.00 \pm 0.012 \text{ mm}$. Show that this process satisfies Six-Sigma capability (assuming no long-term drift).

**Solution:**

**Definition of Six-Sigma (2 marks):**

Six-Sigma quality refers to a process where the natural tolerance band ($\pm 6\sigma$) fits within the specification limits, resulting in a defect rate of **no more than 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, even after allowing for a long-term mean shift of up to $1.5\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Compute natural process spread (1 mark):**

$$12\sigma = 12 \times 0.005 = 0.060 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute specification width (1 mark):**

$$T = \text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 50.012 - 49.988 = 0.024 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare and compute $C_p$ (2 marks):**

$$\frac{T}{12\sigma} = \frac{0.024}{0.060} = 0.40$$

Since $0.024 < 0.060$, the **specification is tighter than the natural process spread**. Hence the process is **NOT** Six-Sigma capable.

**Correction for Six-Sigma (1 mark):**

For Six-Sigma with $1.5\sigma$ drift allowance:

$$\frac{T}{12\sigma} = \frac{0.024}{12\sigma} \ge 2.0 \Rightarrow \sigma \le 0.001 \text{ mm}$$

Hence $\sigma$ must be reduced to **at most $0.001 \text{ mm}$**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

In a batch of 500 capacitors, the inspector finds the following non-conformities: 12 have leakage, 8 have insulation failure, and 5 have damaged leads. Some units have multiple defects.

1. How many **defects** are there in total?
2. If 20 capacitors have at least one defect, how many **defectives** are there?
3. Calculate the **defect rate per unit (DPU)** and the **defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

**Solution:**

**1. Total Defects (1 mark):**

$$D = 12 + 8 + 5 = 25 \text{ defects}$$

**2. Defectives (1 mark):**

$$N_d = 20 \text{ defective capacitors}$$

**3. DPU (Defects Per Unit) (2 marks):**

$$\text{DPU} = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} = \frac{25}{500} = 0.05$$

**4. DPMO (2 marks):**

Opportunities per unit (3 defect categories) $\times$ Units = $3 \times 500 = 1500$ opportunities.

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{25}{1500} \times 10^6 = 16{,}667 \text{ ppm}$$

**5. Sigma Level (1 mark):**

For 16,667 DPMO, the corresponding sigma level (including $1.5\sigma$ drift) ≈ **3.7σ** (from standard sigma-conversion tables).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often confuse *defects* with *defectives* — recall that the **defect** count is the **sum** of all non-conformities, but the **defective** count is the **number of unique units** failing at least one criterion. Also, in DPMO the denominator uses *opportunities* (= units × categories), not just units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Specification of Quality**

- **Definitions to memorize verbatim:** Quality (Juran & Crosby), Specification, Tolerance, Defect, Defective, AQL, LTPD.
- **Types of tolerance:** Bilateral ($x_0 \pm \Delta$), Unilateral (one limit only), Asymmetric ($\Delta_U \neq \Delta_L$).
- **Two data types:** Variable (continuous, measurable) vs Attribute (discrete, countable).
- **Two attribute subtypes:** **Defective** (unit-based) vs **Defect** (count-based) — *never confuse*.
- **Capability indices:** $C_p$ for centered processes; $C_{pk}$ for off-center — $C_{pk} \le C_p$ always.
- **Six-Sigma rule:** $T \ge 12\sigma$ with $1.5\sigma$ drift → $\le 3.4$ DPMO.
- **Sigma thresholds:** $C_p \ge 1.0$ → capable; $C_p \ge 1.33$ → well-capable; $C_p \ge 2.0$ → Six-Sigma.
- **Z-value formula:** $Z = \dfrac{\text{limit} - \mu}{\sigma}$ — used with standard normal tables to find defect rates.
- **Process improvement hierarchy:** (1) Re-center mean, (2) Reduce variance, (3) Re-engineer specification.
- **Common exam keywords:** *fitness for use*, *conformance to requirements*, *inherent characteristic*, *process capability*, *ppm*.
- **Draw the spec-vs-process diagram** in any numerical answer — examiners award marks for visual clarity.
- **Cite ISO 9000:2015** definitions wherever possible — KTU prefers contemporary standards over textbook-only references.
- **Practice standard normal table lookups** — most Module-1 numerical answers end with a $\Phi(Z)$ table read-off.
]<]minimax[>[</SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Quality?

**Quality** is the totality of features and characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. In a manufacturing context, it represents the **conformance to specifications** and the **fitness for use** of a product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight**
> As per the ISO 8402 / ISO 9000 standards referenced in the KTU syllabus:
> *Quality is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfils requirements.*

In simpler terms — **Quality = Doing the right thing, the right way, every time.**

## 1.2 Quality Control (QC) vs. Quality Assurance (QA)

These two terms are frequently confused by students. The KTU examiner expects a **clear distinction** between them.

| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product-oriented | Process-oriented |
| **Stage** | Post-production / during production | Throughout the entire process |
| **Tools Used** | Inspection, Control charts, Sampling plans | Audits, Documentation, Process design |
| **Objective** | Detect & remove defects | Prevent defects from occurring |
| **Responsibility** | Inspection team / QC department | Entire organization |
| **Approach** | Reactive (corrective) | Proactive (preventive) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**
> Think of a **baker making bread daily**.
> - **Quality Assurance (QA)** = Following the **recipe and hygiene standards** correctly every time (preventing bad bread).
> - **Quality Control (QC)** = **Tasting/testing** each batch of bread before delivery to check if it meets standard (detecting bad bread).

## 1.3 What is Statistical Quality Control (SQC)?

**Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the application of statistical methods to the measurement, monitoring, and improvement of quality in manufacturing and service processes. It is the broader umbrella that contains:

1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — Using control charts to monitor processes.
2. **Acceptance Sampling** — Using sampling plans to accept/reject lots.

## 1.4 Inspection — The Backbone of QC

**Inspection** is the process of measuring, examining, testing, or gauging the product or its components to determine whether they conform to the specified requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept**
> *Quality Control without Inspection is like a teacher grading papers without reading them.* Inspection is the **operational tool** through which QC is exercised.

### Types of Inspection (Classified by Method)

1. **100% Inspection (Screening Inspection):**
   - Every single unit of the product is inspected.
   - Used when: Defects are critical, sample size is small, inspection cost is low.
   - Disadvantage: Costly, time-consuming, and prone to inspector fatigue leading to errors.

2. **Sampling Inspection:**
   - Only a portion of the units (sample) is inspected.
   - Decision (accept/reject) is made on the **lot** based on the **sample**.
   - Used when: 100% inspection is impractical or uneconomical.

3. **No Inspection:**
   - When the product is very cheap and inspection cost exceeds the product cost.
   - Or when the process is proven to be highly reliable (Six-Sigma level).

### Types of Inspection (Classified by Stage)

1. **Raw Material Inspection (Incoming Inspection):** At the receipt of materials.
2. **In-Process Inspection (Floor Inspection):** During production.
3. **Final Inspection (Pre-dispatch):** Before the product reaches the customer.

## 1.5 Objectives of Quality Control & Inspection

- ✅ To ensure customer satisfaction.
- ✅ To reduce the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — wastage, rework, warranty claims.
- ✅ To improve process capability and productivity.
- ✅ To provide a basis for **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**.
- ✅ To comply with national and international quality standards (ISO 9000, BIS, etc.).

## 1.6 The Cost of Quality (COQ) Concept

Quality has a price. Quality costs are classified into four categories by the KTU syllabus:

| Category | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs** | Costs to *prevent* defects | Training, process design, quality planning |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Costs to *detect* defects | Inspection, testing, audits |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection |
| **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, returns, loss of reputation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Increasing *prevention* and *appraisal* costs drastically *reduces* failure costs. This is the economic foundation for investing in SQC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inspection Decision Framework

When an inspector evaluates a product, the decision matrix is:

| | Item is Good | Item is Defective |
|---|---|---|
| **Inspector says "Accept"** | ✅ Correct Decision (Probability = 1 - p) | ❌ Error: **Acceptance of Defective (Producer's Risk area)** |
| **Inspector says "Reject"** | ❌ Error: **Rejection of Good (Consumer's Risk area)** | ✅ Correct Decision (Probability = p) |

Where **p = fraction defective** in the lot.

## 2.2 Comparison: 100% Inspection vs. Sampling Inspection

| Parameter | 100% Inspection | Sampling Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | High | Relatively Low |
| Time | Slow | Fast |
| Fatigue Errors | Yes (in manual inspection) | Minimal |
| Destructive Testing | Not possible | Possible (only sample destroyed) |
| Statistical Inference | Not required | Required |
| Application | Critical items (aircraft parts, surgical tools) | Mass production (bottles, bolts, chips) |

## 2.3 Acceptance Sampling — The Statistical Bridge

**Acceptance Sampling** is the statistical middle-ground between 100% inspection and no inspection. Its key features:

- A **sample of size *n*** is drawn randomly from a lot of size *N*.
- Each item is classified as **defective** or **non-defective**.
- The number of defectives *d* in the sample is counted.
- **Decision Rule:**
  - If $d \leq c$ → **Accept the lot** (where *c* = acceptance number)
  - If $d > c$ → **Reject the lot**

This is called an **$(n, c)$ sampling plan**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Terminology**
> The student must remember these exact KTU-approved terms:
> - **Lot (Batch):** The entire collection of items submitted for inspection.
> - **Sample:** A subset of the lot selected randomly.
> - **Acceptance Number (c):** Maximum allowable defectives in the sample.
> - **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve:** The graph showing the probability of acceptance $P_a$ vs. lot quality *p*.

## 2.4 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Conceptual Foundation

The OC curve is a **graphical signature** of any sampling plan. It plots:

$$P_a = P(\text{Accepting the lot}) \text{ vs. } p \text{ (fraction defective)}$$

- When $p = 0$ (perfect lot) → $P_a = 1$ (should be accepted).
- When $p = 1$ (completely defective lot) → $P_a = 0$ (should be rejected).
- The curve is **monotonically decreasing** and **S-shaped**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**
> The OC curve is like a **doctor's diagnostic test accuracy curve**. The y-axis is "Probability of correctly saying the patient is healthy," and the x-axis is "how sick the population actually is." A good sampling plan has a curve that drops sharply.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fraction Defective in Lot | $p = \dfrac{D}{N}$ | Dimensionless, 0 ≤ p ≤ 1 |
| 2 | Fraction Defective in Sample | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{d}{n}$ | Estimated value of *p* |
| 3 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $AOQ = \dfrac{(N - n)}{N} \cdot p \cdot P_a$ | Measures long-run quality after inspection + rectifying |
| 4 | Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $ATI = n + (N - n) \cdot (1 - P_a)$ | Expected items inspected per lot |
| 5 | Hypergeometric Distribution (Lot Finite) | $P(d) = \dfrac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Used when N is small, sampling without replacement |
| 6 | Binomial Approximation (N Large) | $P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Used when N ≥ 10n |
| 7 | Poisson Approximation (p small, n large) | $P(d) = \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Used when p ≤ 0.10 and np ≤ 10 |
| 8 | Acceptance Number | *c* | Integer ≥ 0 |
| 9 | Probability of Acceptance (Binomial) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Sum of binomial probabilities |
| 10 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a$ at AQL | Probability of rejecting a good lot |
| 11 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a$ at LTPD | Probability of accepting a bad lot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Caution:** In the KTU board exam, the **binomial distribution** is the most commonly tested probability model. Ensure you memorize the formula and can compute $P_a$ for small *n* and *c* manually.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Quality Control Method Used |
|---|---|
| **Automobile (Toyota)** | Statistical Process Control (SPC) with control charts |
| **Electronics (Intel, TSMC)** | Acceptance Sampling + Six-Sigma |
| **Pharmaceutical** | 100% Inspection on critical parameters; sampling for non-critical |
| **Food & Beverages (Coca-Cola)** | Sampling Inspection + AOQ-based plans |
| **Aerospace (Boeing)** | 100% Inspection on safety-critical parts |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Acceptance under Binomial Distribution

**Problem Setup:**
- Lot size: $N$ (very large, hence sampling without replacement ≈ with replacement).
- Sample size: $n$.
- True fraction defective: $p$.
- Acceptance number: $c$.

**Step 1 — Single Item Probability:**
The probability that a single randomly selected item is defective is $p$. The probability it is non-defective is $(1 - p)$.

**Step 2 — Probability of Exactly *d* Defectives in *n* Items:**
We want exactly *d* defectives (and therefore $n - d$ good items). Since items are independent (binomial assumption):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = d) &= \underbrace{\binom{n}{d}}_{\text{ways to choose positions}} \cdot \underbrace{p^d}_{\text{defective items}} \cdot \underbrace{(1-p)^{n-d}}_{\text{good items}} \\
&= \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of Acceptance $P_a$:**
The lot is accepted if the number of defectives *d* is **at most *c***. By the **additive law of probability** for mutually exclusive events:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) + \cdots + P(X = c) \\
P_a &= \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of Rejection:**
$$
P_r = 1 - P_a
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most important derivation in Module 1. KTU board questions frequently ask students to compute $P_a$ for given *n*, *c*, and *p*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Question:** A sampling plan $(n=10, c=1)$ is used. The fraction defective is $p = 0.10$. Compute $P_a$.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula with $c = 1$:**

$$
P_a = \binom{10}{0}(0.10)^0(0.90)^{10} + \binom{10}{1}(0.10)^1(0.90)^9
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1: } \binom{10}{0}(0.90)^{10} &= 1 \times 0.3487 = 0.3487 \\
\text{Term 2: } \binom{10}{1}(0.10)(0.90)^9 &= 10 \times 0.10 \times 0.3874 = 0.3874
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum them up:**

$$
P_a = 0.3487 + 0.3874 = 0.7361
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** When the lot is 10% defective, this plan accepts it about **73.6%** of the time. This means the **consumer's risk is very high** — a defective lot slips through nearly 3 out of 4 times!

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

**Assumptions (Rectifying Inspection Plan):**
- Every rejected lot is **100% inspected** and defectives are replaced with good ones.
- Rejected lots after replacement are as good as accepted lots in outgoing quality.

**Step 1 — Number of Defectives that Escape to the Customer per Lot:**
Out of $N$ items in a lot, only $n$ are inspected under the sampling plan. The remaining $(N - n)$ items go uninspected. If the lot is accepted, all defectives in the uninspected portion escape to the customer.

**Step 2 — Expected Defectives Escaping per Accepted Lot:**
$$
\text{Expected defectives escaping} = (N - n) \cdot p
$$

**Step 3 — AOQ Formula:**
The **Average Outgoing Quality** is the long-run average fraction defective in the outgoing product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOQ &= \frac{\text{Expected defectives in outgoing product}}{\text{Total items in lot}} \\
AOQ &= \frac{(N - n) \cdot p \cdot P_a}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The term $P_a$ appears because we multiply by the probability of acceptance — a rejected lot is fully inspected and rectified, contributing zero defectives to the customer.

**Step 4 — Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):**
The **AOQL** is the **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible values of *p*. It is the worst-case average quality the customer will receive:

$$
AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} [AOQ(p)]
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of Plans

| Plan Type | n | c | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strict (Small c)** | Any | $c = 0$ | Rejects lot if even 1 defective found; low $P_a$ |
| **Moderate** | Any | $c = 1$ | Allows 1 defective; balanced |
| **Lenient (Large c)** | Any | $c = n$ | Accepts all lots; essentially no inspection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Board):** Confusing *c = 0* (zero acceptance number) with "100% inspection." They are not the same! $c = 0$ means **sample inspection where the lot is rejected if the single sample item is defective**.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Computing the OC Curve Points

**Given Plan:** $n = 25, c = 1$

**Compute $P_a$ for various values of *p*:**

| $p$ | $P_a = (1-p)^{25} + 25p(1-p)^{24}$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 1.0000 | Accept |
| 0.01 | $0.99^{25} + 25(0.01)(0.99)^{24} \approx 0.7759$ | Accept (likely) |
| 0.05 | $0.95^{25} + 25(0.05)(0.95)^{24} \approx 0.6425$ | Sometimes accept |
| 0.10 | $0.90^{25} + 25(0.10)(0.90)^{24} \approx 0.2716$ | Mostly reject |
| 0.20 | $0.80^{25} + 25(0.20)(0.80)^{24} \approx 0.0234$ | Reject |

> [!NOTE]
> These five points are sufficient to sketch the **S-shaped OC curve** in the KTU exam. A good student labels the axes clearly: **x-axis = p (fraction defective)**, **y-axis = $P_a$ (probability of acceptance)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quality Management Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Management] --> B[Quality Assurance QA]
    A --> C[Quality Control QC]
    A --> D[Quality Improvement]
    B --> B1[Process Design]
    B --> B2[Documentation]
    B --> B3[Training and Audits]
    C --> C1[Inspection]
    C --> C2[Statistical Process Control]
    C --> C3[Acceptance Sampling]
    C1 --> C1a[100 percent Inspection]
    C1 --> C1b[Sampling Inspection]
    C1 --> C1c[No Inspection]
    C2 --> C2a[Control Charts for Variables]
    C2 --> C2b[Control Charts for Attributes]
    C3 --> C3a[Single Sampling Plan]
    C3 --> C3b[Double Sampling Plan]
    C3 --> C3c[Sequential Sampling Plan]
    D --> D1[Kaizen]
    D --> D2[Six Sigma]
    D --> D3[PDCA Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Inspection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot of Size N Arrives] --> B{Draw Random Sample of Size n}
    B --> C[Inspect Each Item in Sample]
    C --> D{Count Defectives d}
    D --> E{Is d less than or equal to c}
    E -->|Yes| F[Accept the Lot]
    E -->|No| G[Reject the Lot]
    F --> H[Send to Customer]
    G --> I{Is 100 percent Rectification Possible}
    I -->|Yes| J[100 percent Screen and Replace Defectives]
    I -->|No| K[Return Lot to Supplier]
    J --> L[Send Rectified Lot to Customer]
```

## 4.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cost of Quality COQ] --> B[Prevention Costs]
    A --> C[Appraisal Costs]
    A --> D[Internal Failure Costs]
    A --> E[External Failure Costs]
    B --> B1[Quality Training]
    B --> B2[Process Design]
    B --> B3[Quality Planning]
    C --> C1[Inspection and Testing]
    C --> C2[Equipment Calibration]
    C --> C3[Quality Audits]
    D --> D1[Scrap and Rework]
    D --> D2[Re inspection]
    D --> D3[Production Downtime]
    E --> E1[Warranty Claims]
    E --> E2[Product Returns]
    E --> E3[Loss of Reputation]
```

## 4.4 Acceptance Sampling Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Population or Lot N] --> B[Random Sampling n]
    B --> C[Sample Items Inspected]
    C --> D[Number of Defectives d Recorded]
    D --> E{Compare d with c}
    E -->|d less than or equal to c| F[Accept Lot]
    E -->|d greater than c| G[Reject Lot]
    F --> H[Pass to Customer]
    G --> I[Rectify or Return]
    I --> J[Disposition Decision]
```

## 4.5 Inspection Type Selection Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Inspection Type] --> B{Are Tests Destructive}
    B -->|Yes| C[Sampling Inspection]
    B -->|No| D{Is Lot Size Small}
    D -->|Yes| E[100 percent Inspection]
    D -->|No| F{Is Defect Cost Critical}
    F -->|Yes| E
    F -->|No| G{Is Inspection Cost Low}
    G -->|Yes| E
    G -->|No| C
    C --> H{Is Process Highly Reliable}
    H -->|Yes| I[Skip Inspection]
    H -->|No| J[Apply Sampling Plan n c]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Quality Control. How is it different from Quality Assurance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Quality Control (QC):** A set of operational techniques and activities used to fulfil requirements for quality. It is **product-oriented** and focuses on detecting defects through inspection, control charts, and sampling. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Quality Assurance (QA):** A planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the quality system to provide confidence that the product will satisfy quality requirements. It is **process-oriented** and focuses on preventing defects through proper process design, training, and audits. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any three types of inspection based on the extent of inspection. State one advantage of sampling inspection over 100% inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **100% Inspection** — Every item inspected. **(1 mark)**
2. **Sampling Inspection** — Only a random sample is inspected. **(1 mark)**
3. **No Inspection** — Inspection skipped due to very low cost items or highly reliable processes. **(1 mark)**
- **Advantage of sampling over 100%:** Sampling inspection is suitable for **destructive testing** (e.g., testing the life of a bulb), where 100% inspection is impossible. *(This is an additional point often rewarded as a bonus.)*

---

## 📝 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Define inspection. Explain the different types of inspection based on the stage of production. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a manufacturing process, a lot of size $N = 2000$ items is inspected using a single sampling plan with $n = 50$ and $c = 2$. The fraction defective is $p = 0.05$. Using the binomial distribution, compute the probability of acceptance $P_a$. Also compute the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Definition:** Inspection is the process of examining, measuring, testing, or gauging raw materials, work-in-progress, or finished products to determine whether they conform to the specified quality standards. **(2 marks)**

**Types of Inspection Based on Stage:** **(5 marks)**

1. **Raw Material Inspection (Incoming Inspection):**
   - Conducted when raw materials are received from suppliers.
   - Purpose: To prevent defective inputs from entering the production process.
   - Methods: Sampling, supplier certification, visual checks.

2. **In-Process Inspection (Floor/Patrol Inspection):**
   - Conducted during production at various stages.
   - Purpose: To detect defects early and prevent the production of large quantities of defective items.
   - Types: *Patrol inspection* (inspector roams), *Station inspection* (inspection at fixed points).

3. **Final Inspection (Pre-dispatch Inspection):**
   - Conducted on the finished product before it is dispatched to the customer.
   - Purpose: To ensure that only conforming products reach the customer.
   - Often the last line of defense; includes functional testing, packaging checks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A three-stage inspection system (raw → in-process → final) ensures defects are caught at the earliest possible stage, reducing **Cost of Poor Quality**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $N = 2000, n = 50, c = 2, p = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Apply Binomial $P_a$ formula with $c = 2$:** **(3 marks)**

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{50}{d} (0.05)^d (0.95)^{50-d}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:** **(3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 0) &= \binom{50}{0}(0.05)^0(0.95)^{50} = 1 \times 1 \times 0.0769 = 0.0769 \\
P(X = 1) &= \binom{50}{1}(0.05)^1(0.95)^{49} = 50 \times 0.05 \times 0.0810 = 0.2025 \\
P(X = 2) &= \binom{50}{2}(0.05)^2(0.95)^{48} = 1225 \times 0.0025 \times 0.0853 = 0.2611
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum for $P_a$:** **(0.5 mark)**

$$
P_a = 0.0769 + 0.2025 + 0.2611 = 0.5405
$$

**Step 4 — Compute AOQ:** **(3 marks, includes formula)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
AOQ &= \frac{(N - n) \cdot p \cdot P_a}{N} \\
&= \frac{(2000 - 50) \times 0.05 \times 0.5405}{2000} \\
&= \frac{1950 \times 0.05 \times 0.5405}{2000} \\
&= \frac{52.70}{2000} \\
&= 0.02635
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
- $P_a \approx 0.5405$ (54.05% chance of acceptance)
- $AOQ \approx 0.0264$ (i.e., 2.64% outgoing defectives)

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** What is Acceptance Sampling? Explain the terms *Lot*, *Sample*, *Acceptance Number*, and *Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve* with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sampling plan $(n = 15, c = 1)$ is used for inspection. Compute $P_a$ for $p = 0.05$ and $p = 0.20$ using the binomial distribution. Comment on the trend. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Definition:** Acceptance Sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a **sample** is drawn from a **lot**, and based on the inspection results of the sample, a decision is made to either **accept or reject** the entire lot. **(2 marks)**

**Key Terms:** **(5 marks, ~1.25 each)**

1. **Lot (Batch):** A collection of items produced under uniform conditions, submitted together for inspection. E.g., 5000 bottles produced in one shift.

2. **Sample:** A subset of the lot, chosen **randomly**, that represents the lot. E.g., 100 bottles drawn randomly from 5000.

3. **Acceptance Number (*c*):** The maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample for the lot to be accepted. E.g., $c = 2$ means up to 2 defectives in the sample is acceptable.

4. **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve:** A graph that plots the **probability of acceptance $P_a$** against the **true fraction defective *p***. It is the "fingerprint" of a sampling plan — it tells us how good the plan is at distinguishing good from bad lots. Shape: **S-shaped, monotonically decreasing**. When $p = 0$, $P_a = 1$. When $p = 1$, $P_a = 0$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:** $n = 15, c = 1$

**For $p = 0.05$:** **(3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= (0.95)^{15} + 15(0.05)(0.95)^{14} \\
&= 0.4633 + 15 \times 0.05 \times 0.4877 \\
&= 0.4633 + 0.3658 \\
&= 0.8291
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $p = 0.20$:** **(3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= (0.80)^{15} + 15(0.20)(0.80)^{14} \\
&= 0.0352 + 15 \times 0.20 \times 0.0440 \\
&= 0.0352 + 0.1321 \\
&= 0.1673
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Trend:** **(1 mark)**
- At $p = 0.05$ (good lot), $P_a = 0.829$ → the plan accepts good lots most of the time. ✓
- At $p = 0.20$ (poor lot), $P_a = 0.167$ → the plan rejects poor lots most of the time. ✓
- As *p* increases, $P_a$ decreases, confirming the **monotonically decreasing** nature of the OC curve.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the binomial coefficient:** Students often write $(0.95)^{15}$ but forget the $\binom{15}{1} = 15$ factor in the second term. **Full 3 marks can be lost.**
> 2. **Using Poisson without checking validity:** Poisson approximation is only valid when $p \leq 0.10$ AND $np \leq 10$. For $p = 0.20$, the binomial is mandatory.
> 3. **Confusing $P_a$ with $P_r$:** $P_a$ is the probability of acceptance. Some students calculate it as the probability of rejection and lose a mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to label the OC curve axes:** Always label **x-axis = p**, **y-axis = $P_a$**. The S-shape and the points $(0, 1)$ and $(1, 0)$ are mandatory for full marks in sketch questions.
> 5. **Mixing up Quality Control with Quality Assurance:** This is a classic 1-mark loser in definition questions. Stick to **product-oriented** for QC and **process-oriented** for QA.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Quality Control (QC)** = Product-oriented, detection-based. **Quality Assurance (QA)** = Process-oriented, prevention-based.
- 📌 **Inspection** is the operational tool of QC. Three types: **100%**, **Sampling**, **No Inspection**.
- 📌 **Three stages of inspection:** Raw Material → In-Process → Final Inspection.
- 📌 **Acceptance Sampling Plan (n, c):** Sample size *n*, acceptance number *c*. Accept if $d \leq c$.
- 📌 **Probability of Acceptance (Binomial):** $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
- 📌 **OC Curve:** S-shaped, monotonically decreasing plot of $P_a$ vs. *p*. Passes through $(0, 1)$ and $(1, 0)$.
- 📌 **AOQ** = $\dfrac{(N-n) \cdot p \cdot P_a}{N}$ — measures long-run outgoing quality.
- 📌 **AOQL** = Maximum of AOQ over all *p* — the worst-case quality the customer sees.
- 📌 **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot. **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot.
- 📌 **Cost of Quality (COQ):** Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- 📌 **KTU Board Pattern:** Always use the **binomial distribution** unless $p$ is very small and $np \leq 10$ (then Poisson).
- 📌 **Key Rule of Thumb:** 100% inspection is **impossible for destructive testing** → sampling is mandatory (e.g., testing life of a light bulb, tensile strength of steel rods).
- 📌 **Random Sampling** is the cornerstone of valid acceptance sampling — biased sampling destroys the statistical basis.
- 📌 **Remember:** Increasing $n$ or decreasing $c$ makes the OC curve **steeper** (better discrimination).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Policy # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Quality Policy** is a documented formal statement of the overall intentions, strategic direction, and commitment of an organization's top management with respect to **quality**, as defined under the **ISO 9000:2015** family of standards and consistent with the **Total Quality Management (TQM)** philosophy endorsed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for Statistical Quality Control. According to **ISO 9000:2015 (Clause 3.5.9)**, a quality policy is defined as: > *"Policy related to quality."* More operationally, it is the **overarching commitment** of an organization to consistently deliver products and services that satisfy customer and applicable statutory/regulatory requirements, and to continually improve the effectiveness of the **Quality Management System (QMS)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A quality policy is NOT the same as quality objectives. The **policy** is the *what* and *why*; the **objectives** are the *measurable how* and *by when*. This distinction is a frequently tested KTU question. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a country's **Constitution** — it lays down the guiding principles, values, and commitments of the nation. It does not specify how many roads to build next year or which school to upgrade. Those specifics are handled by **legislation and budgets** (the objectives). A **Quality Policy** is exactly this constitutional layer for a business: - It is a **broad promise** made by the top leadership ("we commit to customer satisfaction"). - It is **not a tactical target** ("reduce defects to 50 ppm by Q3") — that is a quality objective. - It is a **publicly visible declaration** that signals intent to employees, customers, regulators, and partners. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Maruti Suzuki vs. a Local Garage:** > A car manufacturer (Maruti Suzuki) publishes a formal quality policy: *"Deliver world-class, fuel-efficient, safe vehicles that delight Indian customers through continuous innovation."* A local garage does not have a written policy. The manufacturer's competitive advantage, brand trust, and process consistency are directly traceable to that documented commitment. ## 1.3 The "Big Five" Elements of a Quality Policy (KTU Frequently Tested) > [!IMPORTANT] > A KTU-compliant quality policy MUST address these five elements (as per ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2): 1. **Customer Focus** — Alignment with customer needs and expectations. 2. **Statutory & Regulatory Compliance** — Conformance to applicable legal requirements. 3. **Commitment to Continual Improvement** — The PDCA / Kaizen philosophy. 4. **Framework for Setting Quality Objectives** — Bridge from policy to measurable goals. 5. **Communication & Understanding** — Within the organization and to interested parties. ## 1.4 Terminology Snapshot (Bold the Key Metrics) | Term | Bolded Standard Metric / Phrase | Meaning | |------|-------------------------------|---------| | **QMS** | **Quality Management System** | The integrated system to direct and control an organization regarding quality | | **TQM** | **Total Quality Management** | A management philosophy centred on customer satisfaction through continuous improvement | | **DPMO** | **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | The **Six Sigma benchmark metric** for quality performance | | **COPQ** | **Cost of Poor Quality** | The cost incurred due to defects, rework, and warranty claims | | **CTQ** | **Critical-to-Quality** | The key measurable characteristics of a product/process | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchy of Quality Documents (Top-Down Flow) The quality policy sits at the **apex** of a structured documentation pyramid. Understanding this hierarchy is **critical for KTU theory questions**. **Step 1 — Quality Policy (Top Tier)** - The *strategic umbrella*. What we believe in. - Drafted and signed by **top management** (CEO, MD, Board). - Reviewed periodically (typically annually). **Step 2 — Quality Objectives (Second Tier)** - *Tactical and measurable*. SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound). - Cascaded from policy across departments and functions. **Step 3 — Quality Manual (Third Tier)** - Describes the scope of the QMS, references procedures, and demonstrates compliance. - Acts as the "operating manual" of the QMS. **Step 4 — Procedures & Work Instructions (Fourth Tier)** - Documented step-by-step instructions for routine activities. - Closely tied to **SPC charts, control limits, and acceptance sampling plans** (the core of SQC). **Step 5 — Records & Data (Base Tier)** - Raw evidence: inspection logs, control charts, Pareto charts, etc. - Forms the empirical basis for statistical analysis. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind a Quality Policy A quality policy is **not a ceremonial poster on the office wall**. It serves four engineering-economic purposes: 1. **Alignment** — Ensures every employee, from a shop-floor operator to a C-suite executive, pulls in the same quality direction. 2. **Accountability** — Top management is *publicly* committed; this creates governance pressure. 3. **Framework for Improvement** — It is the *parent document* for measurable quality objectives, which in turn drive statistical process control (SPC) initiatives. 4. **Stakeholder Trust** — Customers, investors, and auditors use the policy as the *first screening* to judge a company's quality maturity. ## 2.3 Quality Policy vs. Quality Objectives — The Critical Distinction This is **the single most important KTU question** on this topic. Commit this comparison to memory. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic: Policy = Promise, Objectives = Performance Numbers** | Dimension | Quality Policy | Quality Objectives | |-----------|---------------|--------------------| | **Nature** | Qualitative, declarative | Quantitative, measurable | | **Level** | Strategic (corporate) | Tactical / Operational (departmental) | | **Time-frame** | Long-term (3–5 years) | Short-term (annual / quarterly) | | **Form** | Sentence / paragraph | Numbered targets with KPIs | | **Example** | *"Achieve zero-defect manufacturing culture."* | *"Reduce in-process defect rate from 1.2% to 0.5% by Dec 2025."* | | **Derived from** | Mission, Vision, Customer needs | Quality Policy + Customer requirements | | **Measurability** | Not directly measurable | Must be measurable (SMART) | ## 2.4 The Deming / Juran / Crosby TQM Roots Modern quality policy is the convergence of three quality gurus' philosophies: - **W. Edwards Deming** — *"The customer is the most important part of the production line."* → **System thinking + continual improvement (PDCA).** - **Joseph Juran** — *"Fitness for use."* → Quality is about **meeting customer needs**, not just specifications. - **Philip Crosby** — *"Quality is free."* → **Prevention**, not detection, drives cost reduction. These three philosophies are baked into any ISO-aligned quality policy. ## 2.5 Quality Policy in the SQC Context Although the **Quality Policy** is a management declaration, it directly drives the **statistical machinery** of SQC: $$ \text{Quality Policy} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Quality Objectives} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CTQ Characteristics} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SPC / Control Charts} $$ Concretely, a policy like *"Continually reduce process variation"* translates into objectives that are tracked using: - $\bar{X} - R$ charts (variables data) - $p$-charts, $c$-charts, $u$-charts (attributes data) - **Process Capability Indices** like $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ - **Six Sigma** levels (DPMO targets) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > While Quality Policy is largely conceptual, KTU often links it to SQC metrics. The following table is a **cheat sheet** for the related statistical concepts that may appear in a 14-mark question. | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Standard | |------------------|---------|-------------------| | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index | $\geq 1.33$ = Capable | | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability (centred) | $\geq 1.33$ = Capable | | $\sigma$ | Standard deviation (process spread) | Six Sigma $= 6\sigma$ | | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | **3.4 DPMO = Six Sigma** | | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | Cost of Poor Quality | Minimize all four | | $C_{pk} \geq 1.00$ | Industry minimum | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ preferred | | $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | World-class (Six Sigma) | Aim for $\geq 1.67$ | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Rule:** Use $\vert$ for absolute value notation. Never use the bare pipe `|` inside a table row — it breaks the table syntax. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Quality Policy in Engineering - **Manufacturing (Automotive):** Drives TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) and Six Sigma deployment. Example: *Hyundai's "Think 4-2-1" policy.* - **Software (IT Services):** Anchors CMMI and ISO 27001 certifications. Example: *Tata Consultancy Services' quality policy.* - **Healthcare (KTU Reference Lab):** Anchors NABL accreditation and patient-safety KPIs. - **Civil Engineering:** Anchors BIS / IS quality standards for concrete (M-sand, M30 grade), rebar, and structural steel. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Cascading Logic: From Policy to SPC The KTU board expects students to demonstrate **the logical flow** from a high-level policy to a measurable statistical process. Let us derive this connection step by step. > **Scenario:** A leading Kerala-based electrical switchgear manufacturer ("AARAV Switches Pvt. Ltd.") wishes to formalize its quality policy. ### Step 1 — Draft the Quality Policy > *"At AARAV Switches, we are committed to delivering safe, durable, and BIS-compliant electrical accessories that exceed customer expectations in performance and reliability. We shall achieve this through:* > > *(a) Adherence to IS 3854 and IS 1293 standards.* > *(b) Continual improvement of our manufacturing processes.* > *(c) Empowerment of every employee to halt production in case of quality concerns.* > *(d) Periodic review of this policy by top management."* **Why this matters:** The policy is **qualitative**. It is impossible to score a switch on "exceed customer expectations" — that is why we need objectives. ### Step 2 — Derive Quality Objectives from the Policy From the policy above, we can derive measurable objectives: $$ \text{Objective 1: Reduce in-process rejection rate from 2.5\% to 0.8\% by Q4 2025.} $$ $$ \text{Objective 2: Achieve on-time delivery } \geq 98\% \text{ for the next 12 months.} $$ $$ \text{Objective 3: Maintain customer complaint ratio } \leq 0.5\% \text{ of total shipments.} $$ ### Step 3 — Identify Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) Characteristics For an electrical switch, the CTQs are typically: $$ CTQ_1 = \text{Dimensional accuracy of pin diameter (mm)} \quad [LSL = 4.7,\; USL = 5.0] $$ $$ CTQ_2 = \text{Insulation resistance (M}\Omega\text{)} \quad [LSL = 100,\; USL = \infty] $$ $$ CTQ_3 = \text{Tensile strength of housing (MPa)} \quad [LSL = 40,\; USL = 80] $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Process Capability Formula Let us compute $C_p$ for $CTQ_1$ (pin diameter). Suppose: $$ \bar{X} = 4.85\ \text{mm}, \quad \sigma = 0.05\ \text{mm}, \quad LSL = 4.7\ \text{mm}, \quad USL = 5.0\ \text{mm} $$ $$ C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{5.0 - 4.7}{6 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.3}{0.30} = 1.00 $$ Now let us compute $C_{pk}$ (centred capability). Since $\bar{X} = 4.85$ is exactly at the midpoint of $[4.7, 5.0]$, the process is centred, so $C_{pk} = C_p = 1.00$. > **Interpretation:** $C_{pk} = 1.00$ means the process is *just barely* meeting specification limits. The quality policy of "continual improvement" mandates that the operations team improve this to $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ by reducing $\sigma$. ### Step 5 — Required Improvement in $\sigma$ We want $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. Solve for $\sigma$: $$ \sigma_{\text{new}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6 \times C_{pk}^{\text{target}}} = \frac{0.3}{6 \times 1.33} \approx 0.0376\ \text{mm} $$ > **Result:** Standard deviation must drop from $0.050$ mm to $0.0376$ mm. This becomes a **quality objective** for the production manager, cascaded from the policy. ### Step 3.2 — DPMO Derivation (Six Sigma Linkage) > **Scenario:** In a 30-day period, a KTU beverage manufacturer produces $N = 50{,}000$ bottles. Each bottle has $O = 8$ opportunities for defects. A total of $D = 200$ defects are observed. $$ DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{50{,}000 \times 8} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{400{,}000} $$ $$ DPMO = 500 $$ > **Interpretation:** 500 DPMO corresponds roughly to a **4.4 $\sigma$** quality level. The corporate quality policy might state "achieve Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO) by FY 2027," which is the **delta** the company must close statistically. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Policy Compliance Audit Checklist | KTU Quality Policy Clause | Evidence Required | Statistical Tool / Metric | |--------------------------|--------------------|---------------------------| | Customer focus | Customer satisfaction surveys (CSAT) | Trend chart of CSAT score | | Regulatory compliance | BIS / ISO audit reports | Audit pass rate % | | Continual improvement | Year-on-year reduction in rejection rate | Pareto, Ishikawa, SPC charts | | Objective setting | Documented SMART objectives | QMS records | | Communication | Training records, intranet posts | Attendance % | | Review | Minutes of Management Review Meeting | Action-item closure rate | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Quality Policy — Cascading Architecture The following Mermaid block depicts how a single quality policy cascades through an organization into measurable statistical outcomes. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Top Management
CEO and Board of Directors] --> B[Quality Policy
Strategic Declaration] B --> C[Quality Objectives
SMART and Measurable] C --> D[CTQ Characteristics
Critical to Quality] D --> E[SPC Plan
Control Charts and Sampling] E --> F[Data Collection
Variables and Attributes] F --> G[Statistical Analysis
Capability and Hypothesis] G --> H[Management Review
Feedback Loop] H --> A B --> I[ISO 9001 Compliance
Clause 5.2] I --> J[External Audit
Certification Body] J --> H style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#264478,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style J fill:#7f6000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Interpretation of the flow:** - The **CEO** (node A) signs the policy. The **policy** (node B) is the strategic declaration. - It cascades into **objectives** (node C) and then to **CTQ characteristics** (node D) — the measurable technical specs. - These drive the **SPC plan** (node E): choice of $\bar{X}-R$ or $p$-charts, sampling frequency, etc. - Data is collected (node F) and analyzed (node G) using capability indices like $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. - Results feed into the **Management Review** (node H), which closes the loop back to top management. - The **ISO 9001** framework (node I) ensures the policy is compliant with international standards, validated by an **external audit** (node J). ## 4.2 Policy vs. Objectives — Side-by-Side Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Quality Policy
Qualitative and Strategic] -->|cascades into| O1[Objective 1
Rejection Rate below 0.8%] P --> O2[Objective 2
On Time Delivery above 98%] P --> O3[Objective 3
CSAT above 4.5 by 5 scale] O1 --> S1[SPC Chart 1
p chart for rejection] O2 --> S2[Logistics KPI
OTD tracker] O3 --> S3[Survey Tool
NPS software] style P fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style O1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style O2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style O3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 PDCA Cycle — Engine of Continual Improvement The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, embedded inside any ISO-aligned quality policy, is the **operational engine** of SQC. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Plan
Set Objectives and Process] --> D1[Do
Implement the Process] D1 --> C1[Check
Monitor via SPC Charts] C1 --> A1[Act
Take Corrective Action] A1 --> P1 C1 -.-> CAPA[CAPA Log
Corrective and Preventive Action] A1 -.-> CAPA style P1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style CAPA fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define the term "Quality Policy" as per ISO 9001:2015. Highlight any two essential elements it must contain.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A quality policy is a formal statement of an organization's overall commitments and intentions related to quality, consistent with the ISO 9001:2015 framework. It is drafted and signed by top management. Two essential elements: 1. **Customer focus** — commitment to meeting customer and applicable statutory/regulatory requirements. 2. **Continual improvement** — a pledge to enhance the QMS using tools such as PDCA and SPC. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. Distinguish between Quality Policy and Quality Objectives. Give one example of each.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A *Quality Policy* is a qualitative, strategic declaration of intent. A *Quality Objective* is a quantitative, measurable target derived from that policy. **Example Policy:** *"Achieve zero-defect manufacturing culture."* **Example Objective:** *"Reduce in-process defect rate from 1.5% to 0.6% by December 2025."* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Provided. Attempt any ONE of the following.** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain in detail the essential elements of a Quality Policy as required by ISO 9001:2015. How does the policy cascade into measurable quality objectives? Illustrate with one industrial example.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** A quality policy, to be ISO 9001:2015 compliant, must contain **six essential elements** (Clause 5.2.1): 1. **Appropriate to the purpose and context of the organization** — it must align with the company's strategy and market. 2. **Provide a framework for setting quality objectives** — it is the parent of all measurable goals. 3. **Include a commitment to satisfy applicable requirements** — both customer and statutory. 4. **Include a commitment to continual improvement** — explicit mention of improvement philosophy (PDCA, Kaizen, Six Sigma). 5. **Be available as documented information** — written, version-controlled, and retrievable. 6. **Be communicated, understood, and applied within the organization** — through training and induction. 7. **Be available to relevant interested parties** — published on the website or in tender documents. **Cascading into objectives — Industrial Example (AARAV Switches):** The corporate quality policy states: *"Deliver BIS-compliant, durable electrical switches through continual process improvement."* Cascading objectives: - *Rejection rate < 0.8% by Q4 2025.* - *$C_{pk}$ for pin diameter $\geq 1.33$.* - *On-time delivery $\geq 98\%$.* Each objective is cascaded to a function (production, QA, logistics) with a target, owner, and review date. This forms the operational layer of the QMS. **(b) A switch manufacturing process has $USL = 5.00$ mm, $LSL = 4.70$ mm. The sample mean is $\bar{X} = 4.78$ mm and the standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.05$ mm.** **Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability. If the company policy mandates $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$, what should be the new standard deviation?** *(7 Marks)* **Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:** $$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{5.00 - 4.70}{6 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.30}{0.30} = 1.00$$ **Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$:** $$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) = \min\left(\frac{5.00 - 4.78}{3 \times 0.05},\; \frac{4.78 - 4.70}{3 \times 0.05}\right) = \min(1.467,\; 0.533)$$ $$C_{pk} = 0.533$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** $C_p = 1.00$ indicates the process spread is just equal to the specification spread. However, $C_{pk} = 0.533 < 1.00$ means the process is **off-centre** (mean = 4.78 is closer to LSL = 4.70). The process is producing many items below the lower specification limit, which is unacceptable. **Step 4 — New $\sigma$ for $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (assuming the process is re-centred to $\bar{X} = 4.85$ mm, the midpoint):** $$C_{pk} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{new}}} \geq 1.33 \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma_{\text{new}} \leq \frac{0.30}{6 \times 1.33}$$ $$\sigma_{\text{new}} \leq 0.0376\ \text{mm}$$ > The process must reduce its standard deviation from 0.050 mm to $\leq 0.0376$ mm — a **24.8% reduction** in variation. This becomes a quality objective cascading from the policy. ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a) "Quality Policy is the constitution of the Quality Management System." Critically analyse this statement with reference to the ISO 9001:2015 framework. Discuss the role of top management in formulating and reviewing the quality policy.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** The statement is a powerful analogy. A constitution is the supreme guiding document; similarly, a quality policy is the **topmost strategic document** in the QMS hierarchy, from which all other quality documents descend. **Critical Analysis (ISO 9001:2015 Context):** - **Hierarchy:** The quality policy sits above the quality manual, which sits above procedures, which sit above records. Any deviation in the policy cascades downwards and can destabilize the entire QMS. - **Alignment:** Like a constitution, the policy must be aligned with the organization's mission, vision, and values. ISO 9001:2015 demands that it be "appropriate to the purpose and context" of the organization. - **Flexibility:** A constitution is rigid in principle but allows for amendments. The quality policy must be reviewed periodically (typically annually) by top management to reflect changing customer, market, and regulatory conditions. - **Public declaration:** Like a preamble that is published and read, the quality policy is a *public* statement — communicated to customers, suppliers, and regulators. **Role of Top Management (ISO 9001 Clause 5.1 & 5.2):** - **Accountability:** Top management is *ultimately accountable* for the QMS. They must draft, sign, and own the policy. - **Communication:** They must ensure the policy is *understood* and *applied* at all levels, not just displayed in a reception area. - **Review:** They must conduct a **Management Review Meeting (MRM)** at planned intervals, evaluating the policy's continued suitability. - **Resource Provision:** The policy is meaningless without resources. Top management must allocate budget, personnel, and infrastructure for policy implementation. - **Integration:** The policy must be integrated into business processes, not treated as a standalone compliance exercise. **(b) A bottling plant has the following data for the month of April 2025:** - *Bottles produced: $N = 100{,}000$* - *Opportunities for defect per bottle: $O = 5$* - *Observed defects: $D = 350$* - *Specification: fill volume must lie in $[495, 505]$ ml.* **Compute the DPMO, identify the approximate $\sigma$ level, and suggest a quality policy objective to improve the current quality level to Six Sigma ($3.4$ DPMO).** *(7 Marks)* **Step 1 — Compute DPMO:** $$DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O} = \frac{350 \times 10^6}{100{,}000 \times 5} = \frac{350 \times 10^6}{500{,}000} = 700$$ **Step 2 — Identify $\sigma$ level (using the standard Six Sigma conversion table):** | DPMO | $\sigma$ Level | |------|----------------| | 3.4 | 6.0 $\sigma$ | | 233 | 5.0 $\sigma$ | | 3,210 | 4.5 $\sigma$ | | 6,210 | 4.0 $\sigma$ | | 66,807 | 3.0 $\sigma$ | $DPMO = 700$ lies between 233 and 3,210, corresponding to approximately **4.6 $\sigma$ to 4.7 $\sigma$** (i.e., below world-class). **Step 3 — Policy objective to reach Six Sigma:** The current DPMO is 700, but Six Sigma requires 3.4 DPMO. The reduction factor is: $$\text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{700}{3.4} \approx 206$$ > The defects must be reduced by a factor of approximately **206** to reach Six Sigma. The corporate quality policy should state: > > *"Achieve Six Sigma ($3.4$ DPMO) operational performance across all critical bottling lines by FY 2027, through the deployment of DMAIC projects, automated vision inspection, and statistical process control."* This becomes a measurable quality objective cascaded from the policy. ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing policy with objective:** Saying "reduce defects to 0.5%" is an objective, NOT a policy. A policy is qualitative. (*Lose 1 mark in Part A; lose 2–3 marks in Part B if confused.*) > 2. **Omitting top management's role:** The policy *must* be owned by top management. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the review clause:** A policy must be reviewed periodically. Students often omit this. > 4. **Skipping statistical evidence:** When asked to discuss the link to SQC, students write a generic paragraph without quoting $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, or DPMO. *Always link the policy to a numerical metric.* > 5. **Miswriting $C_{pk}$ formula:** Some students forget the "min" function. The correct formula uses $\min$, not addition or subtraction. > 6. **Unit errors in DPMO:** DPMO is dimensionless. Students sometimes write "%" or "ppm" — be precise. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Policy** - **Definition (ISO 9000:2015):** A formal statement of overall quality intentions and commitments, aligned with the organization's purpose. - **Owner:** **Top management** (CEO / Board). Always mention this in any KTU answer. - **Six ISO 9001:2015 Must-Haves:** (1) context-appropriate, (2) framework for objectives, (3) commitment to compliance, (4) commitment to continual improvement, (5) documented & communicated, (6) available to interested parties. - **Policy vs. Objective:** Policy = **qualitative & strategic**; Objective = **quantitative & SMART**. - **Cascade:** Policy $\rightarrow$ Objectives $\rightarrow$ CTQs $\rightarrow$ SPC Plan $\rightarrow$ Data $\rightarrow$ Analysis $\rightarrow$ Management Review $\rightarrow$ (back to Policy). - **TQM Roots:** Deming (system, PDCA), Juran (fitness for use), Crosby (prevention). - **PDCA Cycle:** Plan-Do-Check-Act. The operational engine of any quality policy. - **Process Capability link:** $C_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$; $C_{pk} = \min\bigl((USL - \bar{X})/3\sigma,\;(\bar{X} - LSL)/3\sigma\bigr)$. Target: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. - **Six Sigma link:** $DPMO = (D \times 10^6) / (N \times O)$. Six Sigma = **3.4 DPMO** at $\pm 1.5\sigma$ shift. - **Review frequency:** Typically **annually**, or whenever there is a major change in strategy, regulation, or customer base. - **Communication:** Must be communicated *to* and *understood by* every employee — not merely displayed. - **Real-world example (KTU-style):** Kerala's own KELTRON, V-Guard, or MRF — all have published ISO 9001-aligned quality policies. - **Mnemonic for the 5 elements:** **"C-C-O-C-C"** → **C**ustomer focus, **C**ompliance, **O**bjective framework, **C**ontinual improvement, **C**ommunication. ---

topics.title: Statistical Quality Control (SQC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statistical Quality Control (SQC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is a branch of applied statistics that uses statistical techniques (such as control charts, acceptance sampling plans, and process capability analysis) to monitor, control, and improve the quality of manufacturing and service processes. It operates on the principle that **all processes exhibit variation**, and this variation can be measured, analyzed, and managed using probability and statistical inference.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PEPET415**, SQC encompasses two major subdivisions:
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — Online monitoring of a process *during* production.
- **Acceptance Sampling (AS)** — Offline decision-making on a *lot/batch* basis after production.

The discipline was pioneered by **Walter A. Shewhart** of Bell Telephone Laboratories in **1924**, who developed the first control chart. It was later championed globally by quality gurus like **W. Edwards Deming**, **Joseph M. Juran**, and **Kaoru Ishikawa**, whose contributions transformed Japanese (and later worldwide) manufacturing standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Thermometer for Processes"**

Imagine you are boiling milk on a stove. You don't have to taste it every second; you just glance at the **bubbling pattern**. If the milk is simmering steadily, all is well. If suddenly it boils over or barely heats, you adjust the flame. 

**Statistical Quality Control is exactly that "thermometer"** for any industrial process:
- The **process** is the stove + milk setup.
- The **quality characteristic** (e.g., bottle weight, bolt diameter, pH of a chemical) is the "bubble level."
- The **control chart** is the constant visual glance.
- The **control limits** are like the "min" and "max" acceptable bubble levels.

When a sample measurement crosses the control limits, it is the statistical equivalent of the milk boiling over — an alarm that the process has gone "out of control."

### 1.3 Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's 8 Dimensions)

A product or service is judged on **8 dimensions of quality**, as defined by **David Garvin (1987)**:

| # | Dimension | Brief Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics |
| 2 | **Features** | Added "bells and whistles" |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Degree to which product meets specifications |
| 5 | **Durability** | Length of useful life |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Ease/speed of repair |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Look, feel, sound, taste |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Brand image / reputation |

### 1.4 Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QC (Quality Control)** is *reactive* and *product-oriented* — it inspects the finished product to weed out defects.
> **QA (Quality Assurance)** is *proactive* and *process-oriented* — it builds quality into the system so defects are prevented.
> **SQC bridges both** by using statistical data to drive preventive action.

### 1.5 Why Variation Exists in Every Process

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Theorem of SQC (Shewhart):** *"No two manufactured items are exactly alike. Variation in quality is inherent in any production process."*

Variation arises from two broad sources:
- **Common Cause Variation (Chance Cause):** Inherent to the process, expected, random. Example: minor tool wear, micro humidity changes.
- **Special Cause Variation (Assignable Cause):** External, unexpected, identifiable. Example: broken machine, untrained operator, contaminated raw material.

The central job of SQC is to **distinguish** between these two — so engineers can decide whether to **leave the process alone** (common cause) or **intervene** (special cause).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives and Functions of SQC

The **operational objectives** of SQC, in a logical order, are:

1. **Maintain the process at a stable, capable level** of performance.
2. **Detect assignable causes** of variation as early as possible.
3. **Reduce variability** around the target value.
4. **Provide a common language** between operators, engineers, and management based on data.
5. **Enable continuous improvement** (the Deming–PDCA cycle).
6. **Reduce waste, rework, scrap, and warranty costs.**

### 2.2 The Deming Cycle (PDCA)

Deming's famous **Plan–Do–Check–Act** loop is the engine that drives SQC-based improvement:

- **Plan:** Identify the problem, hypothesize a cause, design an experiment.
- **Do:** Implement the change on a small scale and collect data.
- **Check:** Apply statistical tools (control charts, capability indices) to verify the result.
- **Act:** If successful, standardize; if not, revise hypothesis and loop back.

### 2.3 Advantages of SQC

- ✅ **Reduces wastage** and rework → lower cost of poor quality (COPQ).
- ✅ **Improves product uniformity** → higher customer satisfaction.
- ✅ **Provides early warning** of process drift.
- ✅ **Builds an objective database** for management decisions.
- ✅ **Reduces dependence on 100% inspection** (saves labour and time).
- ✅ **Satisfies ISO 9001 / Six Sigma / Industry 4.0 compliance** requirements.

### 2.4 Limitations of SQC

- ❌ Requires **trained personnel** in statistics.
- ❌ **Initial setup cost** (gauges, software, training) can be high.
- ❌ **Not a substitute for poor design** — cannot fix a fundamentally incapable process.
- ❌ Only effective when **data is reliable and the sample is random**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core formulas** tested in KTU Module-1 questions. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Grand Mean of Means** | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | $k$ = number of subgroups | Central line on $\bar{X}$-chart |
| 2 | **Average Range** | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$ | $R_i$ = range of $i$-th subgroup | Central line on $R$-chart |
| 3 | **$\bar{X}$-chart UCL** | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ = constant for sample size $n$ | $3\sigma$ upper control limit |
| 4 | **$\bar{X}$-chart LCL** | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ tabulated | $3\sigma$ lower control limit |
| 5 | **$R$-chart UCL** | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_4$ constant | Upper limit on variability |
| 6 | **$R$-chart LCL** | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3$ constant | Lower limit on variability |
| 7 | **Standard Deviation (X̄ method)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $d_2$ = bias constant | Estimates process $\sigma$ |
| 8 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL, LSL$ = spec limits | Two-sided capability |
| 9 | **Process Capability (offset)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ = process mean | Accounts for centering |
| 10 | **Natural Tolerance Limits** | $NTL = \mu \pm 3\sigma$ | — | Defines "in-control" voice of process |
