| 11 | **Probability of Type-I error** | $\alpha$ | — | False alarm probability (≈ 0.27% for 3σ) |
| 12 | **Sample Mean Standard Error** | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $n$ = subgroup size | Used in $\bar{X}$-chart construction |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

In modern industries — semiconductor fabrication (TSMC), automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors), pharmaceuticals (Cipla, Sun Pharma), aerospace (Boeing, HAL) — SQC is the **backbone of zero-defect manufacturing**. It is the statistical engine inside **Six Sigma** ($C_{pk} \geq 1.67$) and **ISO/TS 16949** automotive quality systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why "3-Sigma" Control Limits?

The cornerstone of SQC is Shewhart's choice of **$\pm 3\sigma$** as control limits. Let us derive its statistical basis.

**Given:** A quality characteristic $X$ drawn from a **Normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

$$
X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\ \sigma^2)
$$

**Step 1 — Standardize the variable** to obtain the standard normal $Z$:

$$
Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

So $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\ 1)$.

**Step 2 — Probability that a measurement lies within $\pm 3\sigma$:**

$$
P(\vert X - \mu \vert \leq 3\sigma) = P(\vert Z \vert \leq 3)
$$

This is computed from the standard normal cumulative distribution function $\Phi(\cdot)$:

$$
P(\vert Z \vert \leq 3) = \Phi(3) - \Phi(-3)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the symmetry of the standard normal distribution.** Because $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$:

$$
\Phi(3) - \Phi(-3) = \Phi(3) - [1 - \Phi(3)] = 2\Phi(3) - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the tabulated value** $\Phi(3) = 0.99865$:

$$
2(0.99865) - 1 = 0.9973
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the probability of being outside $\pm 3\sigma$:**

$$
1 - 0.9973 = 0.0027 = 0.27\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** If a process is truly in statistical control, the probability of any single point falling outside the 3σ limits is only **0.27%** (≈ 3.4 per million by modern standards for long-run analysis). Shewhart chose $3\sigma$ as a practical balance — wide enough to **avoid false alarms**, narrow enough to **catch assignable causes quickly**.

### 3.2 Derivation: $\bar{X}$-chart Limits from $\bar{R}$

We want control limits for the **sample mean** $\bar{X}$ of subgroups of size $n$.

**Step 1 — Distribution of the sample mean** (by Central Limit Theorem):

$$
\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\ \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

The standard error of the mean is:

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the $3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ rule:**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \dfrac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate $\mu$ by $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\sigma$ by $\bar{R}/d_2$** (a standard unbiased estimator of process standard deviation based on subgroup range):

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \dfrac{3\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the constant $A_2$:**

$$
A_2 \triangleq \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

By symmetry:

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_p$

**Definition:** $C_p$ measures how well the **spread of the process** fits inside the **engineering specification window**.

**Step 1 — Total specification width:**

$$
W = USL - LSL
$$

**Step 2 — Natural (statistical) width of the process** is $\pm 3\sigma$, i.e., $6\sigma$ in total.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Interpretation Scale:**

$$
C_p \begin{cases} < 1.00 & \Rightarrow \text{Process is NOT capable} \\ 1.00 - 1.33 & \Rightarrow \text{Marginally capable} \\ 1.33 - 1.67 & \Rightarrow \text{Capable (typical industry)} \\ \geq 1.67 & \Rightarrow \text{Six-Sigma class} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> A bottle-filling machine fills 500 mL bottles. Subgroups of size $n = 5$ are taken every hour; $k = 25$ subgroups yield $\bar{\bar{X}} = 501.2$ mL and $\bar{R} = 1.8$ mL. For $n = 5$, $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.

**Compute $\bar{X}$-chart limits:**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = 501.2 + 0.577 \times 1.8 = 501.2 + 1.0386 = 502.2386 \text{ mL}
$$

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = 501.2 - 1.0386 = 500.1614 \text{ mL}
$$

**Compute $R$-chart limits:**

$$
UCL_R = 2.114 \times 1.8 = 3.8052 \text{ mL},\quad LCL_R = 0 \times 1.8 = 0
$$

**Compute process capability** (assume $LSL = 498$, $USL = 502$):

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{1.8}{2.326} = 0.7740 \text{ mL}
$$

$$
C_p = \dfrac{502 - 498}{6 \times 0.7740} = \dfrac{4}{4.644} = 0.861
$$

**Inference:** $C_p < 1$ ⇒ the process is **not capable**; the operator must **reduce variability** before declaring the system in control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level SQC Framework

The following block-level functional architecture summarizes the **two pillars of SQC** and their inter-relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Material Input] --> nodeB[Production Process]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Process In Control?}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Acceptable Product Output]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Special Cause Identified]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Corrective Action]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG[Sample Measurement]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Statistical Process Control SPC]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Acceptance Sampling AS]
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Lot Acceptance Decision]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Accept Lot]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Reject Lot]
```

### 4.2 Control Chart Anatomy

The following schematic captures the **anatomy of a Shewhart control chart** — the visual heart of SQC.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Time or Sample Sequence on X axis] --> nodeB[Plotted Sample Statistic on Y axis]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Center Line CL equals process mean]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Upper Control Limit UCL equals CL plus 3 sigma]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Lower Control Limit LCL equals CL minus 3 sigma]
    nodeF[Points inside UCL and LCL band] --> nodeG[Common cause variation only]
    nodeH[Points outside UCL or LCL] --> nodeI[Special cause variation signal]
```

### 4.3 PDCA Improvement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[PLAN Identify problem and hypothesis] --> nodeDO[DO Implement small scale trial]
    nodeDO --> nodeCH[CHECK Apply SQC tools to verify]
    nodeCH --> nodeAC[ACT Standardize or revise]
    nodeAC --> nodeP
```

### 4.4 Sources of Process Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeV[Process Variation] --> nodeC1[Common Cause Chance]
    nodeV --> nodeS1[Special Cause Assignable]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE1[Inherent random noise]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE2[Expected within control limits]
    nodeS1 --> nodeF1[Machine failure]
    nodeS1 --> nodeF2[Operator error]
    nodeS1 --> nodeF3[Material defect]
    nodeS1 --> nodeF4[Environmental change]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**  
Define Statistical Quality Control. List any **four** objectives of SQC.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** SQC is the application of statistical techniques to monitor, control, and improve the quality of products and processes, based on the principle that *variation in quality is inherent and measurable*.

> **Four objectives (1.5 marks):**  
> 1. To maintain the process at a stable, capable level.  
> 2. To detect assignable causes of variation at the earliest.  
> 3. To reduce variability and thus wastage/rework.  
> 4. To provide a factual basis for managerial decision-making.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**  
Differentiate between **Quality Control (QC)** and **Quality Assurance (QA)** in a tabular form.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Focus | Product-oriented | Process-oriented |
| Approach | Reactive (detect defects) | Preventive (avoid defects) |
| Tools | Inspection, gauges, testing | SOPs, audits, training |
| Stage | End of production | Across the value chain |
| Aim | Separate good from bad | Build quality in |

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the **historical evolution** of SQC. Discuss the contributions of Walter Shewhart, W. Edwards Deming, and Joseph Juran. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The contents of 10 bottles (in mL) taken from a filling machine are: 502, 498, 501, 500, 499, 503, 497, 502, 500, 498. The process mean is $\mu = 500$ mL and $\sigma = 2$ mL. Construct the **3σ control limits** for the $\bar{X}$-chart and comment on whether the process is in control. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Evolution of SQC (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-1920 — Craft Inspection Era:** Quality judged by the master craftsman; no formal data.
2. **1924 — Walter A. Shewhart (Bell Labs)** introduced the **control chart** while working on the Western Electric Hawthorne plant. Published *Economic Control of Quality of Manufactured Product* (1931) — the foundation text of SQC.
3. **World War II Era — Acceptance Sampling.** H.F. Dodge and H.G. Romig developed sampling plans to inspect large lots of war material economically.
4. **1950s — W. Edwards Deming** taught SQC to Japanese engineers; his **14 Points for Management** and **PDCA cycle** revolutionized global manufacturing. He emphasized *"constancy of purpose for improvement."*
5. **1950s — Joseph M. Juran** complemented Deming with the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) and the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**.
6. **1970s Onwards — Kaoru Ishikawa (Cause-and-Effect diagrams)**, **Genichi Taguchi (Quality Loss Function)**, and the rise of **Six Sigma** (Motorola, 1987).
7. **2000s–Today:** Integration with **Industry 4.0, IoT, AI-driven SPC dashboards**.

**(b) Control chart computation (7 marks):**

Given $\mu = 500$, $\sigma = 2$, $n = 1$ (individual readings), so $A = 3$ (for $n = 1$).

The 3σ limits for individual measurements are:

$$
UCL = \mu + 3\sigma = 500 + 3(2) = 506 \text{ mL}
$$

$$
LCL = \mu - 3\sigma = 500 - 3(2) = 494 \text{ mL}
$$

**Check each reading:** The maximum = 503, minimum = 497. All values lie between 494 and 506.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** No point falls outside the control limits → **the process appears to be in statistical control**. However, the spread of 6 mL suggests checking **process capability** against the specification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **two subdivisions of SQC**: Statistical Process Control (SPC) and Acceptance Sampling (AS). Compare them on **five points**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process has specification limits $LSL = 98$ mm and $USL = 102$ mm. From 25 subgroups of size $n = 5$, the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.8$ mm. Compute the **process capability index $C_p$** and comment on the capability of the process. Use $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Two subdivisions of SQC (7 marks):**

| # | SPC (Statistical Process Control) | AS (Acceptance Sampling) |
|---|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Online (during production) | Offline (after production) |
| 2 | Monitors process stability | Decides lot acceptance/rejection |
| 3 | Uses **control charts** (X-bar, R, p, c, etc.) | Uses **sampling plans** (single, double, sequential) |
| 4 | Goal: prevent defects | Goal: accept/reject lots |
| 5 | Continuous feedback to operator | One-time decision per lot |
| 6 | Reduces reliance on inspection | Economical for destructive testing |
| 7 | Requires real-time data | Requires lot formation |

(Use the **Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1** as the supporting block diagram — 2 marks.)

**(b) $C_p$ computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Estimate process standard deviation:**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.8}{2.326} = 0.3439 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the specification width:**

$$
USL - LSL = 102 - 98 = 4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $6\sigma$ process width:**

$$
6\hat{\sigma} = 6 \times 0.3439 = 2.0634 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{4}{2.0634} = 1.939
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**

> Since $C_p \approx 1.94 \gg 1.33$, the process is **highly capable** and comfortably exceeds typical industry benchmarks.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply $\bar{R}$ by $A_2$** — students often write $UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using wrong constants** ($A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$) for the given sample size $n$. Always look up the **control chart constants table**.
> 3. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** — $C_p$ ignores mean offset; $C_{pk}$ does not. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — always mention that the data is **normally distributed and independent** before applying 3σ limits.
> 5. **Skipping the conclusion** in numerical problems — a one-line "process is/is not in control" statement is mandatory.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ SQC = the **statistical engineering** of quality — built on Shewhart's 1924 invention.
- ✅ Two pillars: **SPC (process control)** + **AS (acceptance sampling)**.
- ✅ **Variation is universal** — classify it as **common cause** (ignore) or **special cause** (investigate).
- ✅ **Deming's PDCA cycle** is the engine of SQC-based improvement.
- ✅ **3σ control limits** give a **0.27% false-alarm rate** under normality.
- ✅ Always compute **$A_2, D_3, D_4$ from the constants table** for the exact subgroup size.
- ✅ **$C_p \geq 1.33$** ⇒ capable; **$C_p < 1.0$** ⇒ not capable; **$C_p \geq 1.67$** ⇒ Six-Sigma class.
- ✅ Garvin's **8 dimensions** of quality help translate customer needs into measurable characteristics.
- ✅ SQC is the **statistical backbone of ISO 9001, Six Sigma, and Industry 4.0 smart manufacturing**.
- ✅ Always conclude numerical answers with a **clear "in-control / out-of-control / capable / not-capable"** remark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Feedback in Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Feedback in Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, **Feedback** refers to the systematic process of collecting, measuring, and returning information about the output performance of a manufacturing or service process back to the input stage, so that corrective or preventive actions can be initiated to keep the process aligned with established quality standards.

Mathematically, the feedback loop in a quality control system can be represented as a closed-loop transfer relation:

$$E(t) = R(t) - Y(t)$$

where $E(t)$ is the **error signal**, $R(t)$ is the **reference (target quality)**, and $Y(t)$ is the **measured actual quality output**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Feedback is the cornerstone of the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) Cycle** of Deming and the **Shewhart Control Chart Loop**. Without feedback, statistical process control reduces to a passive recording exercise with no corrective authority.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car** on a highway while blindfolded. You press the accelerator, but you cannot see where the car is going. The result is almost always a crash. Now remove the blindfold — you can now see the road (the *desired path*), compare it with where the car actually is (the *current position*), and turn the steering wheel accordingly. That visual comparison and the resulting steering correction is **feedback**.

In a factory:
- The **desired path** = product specifications / tolerance limits (e.g., diameter = $10 \pm 0.1$ mm).
- The **current position** = actual measured diameter of the produced part.
- The **steering wheel** = the corrective action taken by the operator or engineer.

**Feedback converts a "blind" process into a self-correcting, intelligent one.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bold

The following metrics are universally accepted by KTU examiners for this topic:

- **Specification Limits (LSL, USL):** Lower and Upper Specification Limits defining acceptable quality.
- **Control Limits (LCL, UCL):** Statistical limits derived from process data (typically at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$).
- **Process Capability Index ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$):** Numerical indicators of how well the process meets specifications.
- **Assignable vs. Common Cause Variation:** Feedback distinguishes between these two fundamental sources of variability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> *Specification Limits* are set by the **customer / designer** (external voice).
> *Control Limits* are derived from the **process itself** (internal voice).
> Feedback bridges these two voices continuously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Feedback in Quality Control

A complete feedback mechanism in SQC rests on three operational pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Measurement (Data Acquisition)**
- Samples are drawn from the process at defined intervals.
- Quality characteristics (length, weight, hardness, defects per unit) are measured.
- Tools: Calipers, gauges, **Shewhart control charts**, $\bar{X}$–$R$ charts, $p$–charts, $c$–charts.

**Pillar 2 — Comparison (Diagnosis)**
- The measured value is compared against:
  - **Target value** ($T$) — the nominal specification.
  - **Control limits** ($\mu \pm 3\sigma$) — the natural process boundaries.
- A deviation triggers a decision: *in-control* or *out-of-control*.

**Pillar 3 — Action (Corrective Response)**
- If in-control → continue production.
- If out-of-control → identify **assignable cause**, eliminate it, and restart.
- The action itself is fed back as a learning loop into the next cycle.

### 2.2 Types of Feedback in Quality Systems

| Feedback Type | Description | Typical Use in SQC |
|---------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Negative Feedback** | Output deviation is *opposed* by corrective action to bring the process back to target. | Standard SPC control charts; thermostat analogy. |
| **Positive Feedback** | Output deviation is *amplified*, leading to escalation or system shutdown. | Alarm systems, automatic line stoppage when out-of-control signal is detected. |
| **Feed-Forward Control** | Anticipates disturbance *before* it affects output (not strictly feedback). | Pre-production design reviews, FMEA. |
| **Closed-Loop SPC** | Continuous, automated feedback from in-line sensors to controllers. | Modern Industry 4.0 manufacturing cells. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In SQC literature, "feedback" almost always refers to **Negative Feedback** — it is the stabilizing force that keeps the process centered on the target.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Where Used |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | Error Signal | $E(t) = R(t) - Y(t)$ | Definition of feedback error |
| 2 | Process Mean | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Centerline of $\bar{X}$ chart |
| 3 | Control Limits (X-bar) | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper Control Limit |
| 4 | Control Limits (X-bar) | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower Control Limit |
| 5 | Control Limits (R chart) | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$, $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Range chart limits |
| 6 | Process Capability | $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Capability Index |
| 7 | Process Capability (shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Capability with centering |
| 8 | Six Sigma Level | $Z = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Defects per million (DPMO) |
| 9 | OC Curve Function | $\beta = \Phi\!\left(\frac{A_2 \sqrt{n} - k \cdot d}{1}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{-A_2 \sqrt{n} - k \cdot d}{1}\right)$ | Type II error for shifts |
| 10 | ARL (in-control) | $ARL_0 = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ | Average Run Length baseline |

**Notation Note:** In the formulas above, $\Phi(\cdot)$ denotes the cumulative standard normal distribution function, $d$ is the standardized shift in process mean, $k$ is a multiplier, and $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are standard tabulated constants dependent on sample size $n$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Real-time torque feedback on engine assembly ensures bolts meet the $80 \pm 5$ Nm specification.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Tablet weight feedback loop maintains dosage uniformity within $\pm 5\%$ of label claim.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Sub-nanometer feedback control in lithography is impossible without closed-loop SQC.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength feedback determines formwork removal timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Feedback Error Signal in a Shewhart Control Chart

The Shewhart control chart is, in essence, an *analog* feedback controller sampled in discrete time. Let us derive its governing equation explicitly.

**Step 1 — Define the Sampling Instant**

Let $t_k = k \cdot h$ denote the time at which the $k$-th sample of size $n$ is drawn from the process. Here $h$ is the sampling interval (e.g., every hour, every 50 units).

**Step 2 — Define the Sample Statistic**

For each sample, compute the sample mean:

$$\bar{X}_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{i,k}$$

This $\bar{X}_k$ is the *measured output* $Y(t_k)$ of the feedback system at time $t_k$.

**Step 3 — Define the Reference (Set Point)**

The reference (target) is the grand average of all sample means, denoted $\bar{\bar{X}}$:

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^{m} \bar{X}_k$$

where $m$ is the total number of preliminary samples used to establish the baseline.

**Step 4 — Form the Error Signal**

Following the standard feedback definition $E(t) = R(t) - Y(t)$, we obtain:

$$E_k = \bar{\bar{X}} - \bar{X}_k$$

This is the **discrete feedback error** at sample $k$. If $\vert E_k \vert$ exceeds a critical threshold, an out-of-control signal is generated.

**Step 5 — Establish Control Limits via Standard Deviation**

The standard deviation of $\bar{X}_k$ is $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$. The standard feedback rule uses the $3\sigma$ boundary:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The decision rule is:

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Continue Production (In-Control)} & \text{if } LCL_{\bar{X}} \le \bar{X}_k \le UCL_{\bar{X}} \\[4pt] \text{Trigger Corrective Action (Out-of-Control)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — The Corrective Action as the "Control Input"**

Let $U_k$ denote the corrective adjustment made after sample $k$. The process mean evolves as:

$$\mu_{k+1} = \mu_k + U_k - \delta_k$$

where $\delta_k$ is the random assignable-cause disturbance entering the process. The feedback law that perfectly eliminates the disturbance is:

$$U_k = \delta_k = \bar{X}_k - \bar{\bar{X}} = -E_k$$

This shows that **the corrective action magnitude equals the negative of the error**, which is the defining property of *negative feedback*.

**Step 7 — Stability of the Feedback Loop**

For stability, we require the closed-loop gain $\vert G \vert < 1$. Substituting:

$$\bar{X}_{k+1} - \bar{\bar{X}} = (\bar{X}_k - \bar{\bar{X}}) \cdot (1 - G)$$

where $G$ is the controller gain. For asymptotic convergence to $\bar{\bar{X}}$, we need:

$$\vert 1 - G \vert < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0 < G < 2$$

In a Shewhart chart, $G$ corresponds to the standardized distance of $\bar{X}_k$ from $\bar{\bar{X}}$ in units of $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$, and the $3\sigma$ rule sets the maximum allowed $G = 3$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Feedback vs. No Feedback

| Performance Aspect | Without Feedback | With Feedback (SPC) |
|--------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Defect Rate | High and unpredictable | Reduced (typically $4.5\sigma$ to $6\sigma$) |
| Process Mean Drift | Undetected for long periods | Detected within $ARL_1$ samples |
| Customer Complaints | Reactive (after delivery) | Proactive (before shipment) |
| Cost of Quality | High (scrap, rework, warranty) | Low (prevention-driven) |
| Operator Confidence | Low | High |
| Continuous Improvement | Absent | Built into PDCA |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ARL (Average Run Length)** is the *expected number of samples* before the chart signals. For an in-control process, $ARL_0 = 1/\alpha = 370.4$ (for $\alpha = 0.0027$). For a $1.5\sigma$ shift, $ARL_1 \approx 43.9$. This is the *quantitative payoff* of feedback.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Feedback Architecture for SQC

The following Mermaid block renders the block-level functional architecture of a feedback-driven quality control system, showing how data flows from the process through measurement, comparison, decision, and back to corrective action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Reference Input\nTarget Specification T] --> node2[Comparator\nCompares T vs Y]
    node3[Process Under Control\nManufacturing Operation] --> node4[Measurement Block\nSample Mean and Range]
    node4 --> node5[Measured Output\nY of t]
    node5 --> node2
    node2 --> node6[Error Signal\nE of t equals T minus Y]
    node6 --> node7[Decision Logic\nIn-control vs Out-of-control]
    node7 --> node8{In-Control State}
    node7 --> node9{Out-of-Control State}
    node8 --> node10[Continue Production\nNo Action Needed]
    node9 --> node11[Identify Assignable Cause\nRoot Cause Analysis]
    node11 --> node12[Apply Corrective Action\nAdjustment U of t]
    node12 --> node3
    node10 --> node3
```

### 4.2 PDCA Cycle with Embedded Feedback

The Deming Wheel (PDCA) is the operational embodiment of feedback in quality management.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    planNode[PLAN\nDefine Quality Standard\nSet Specification Limits] --> doNode
    doNode[DO\nExecute the Process\nProduce Output] --> checkNode
    checkNode[CHECK\nMeasure Output\nCompare Against Standard\nThis is the FEEDBACK Step] --> actNode
    actNode[ACT\nTake Corrective Action\nIf Deviation Detected\nStandardize if No Deviation] --> planNode
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow of Information in SPC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData[Raw Sample Data X1 X2 ... Xn] --> sampleMean[Compute Sample Mean X-bar]
    rawData --> sampleRange[Compute Sample Range R]
    sampleMean --> chartX[X-bar Control Chart]
    sampleRange --> chartR[R Control Chart]
    chartX --> compareX[Compare to UCL and LCL]
    chartR --> compareR[Compare to UCL and LCL]
    compareX --> decision{Points Within Limits?}
    compareR --> decision
    decision --> yesPath[In-Control Signal to Operator]
    decision --> noPath[Out-of-Control Alarm\nto Quality Engineer]
    noPath --> rca[Root Cause Investigation]
    rca --> actionLoop[Corrective Action and Re-feed to Process]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **Industry 4.0** smart factories, the *measurement* and *comparison* blocks are replaced by IoT sensors and machine-learning classifiers operating in real time, but the fundamental feedback structure remains identical to the Shewhart 1924 architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*Define the term "feedback" in the context of statistical quality control. List any two key elements of a feedback loop.*

**Model Answer:**

Feedback in SQC is the process of measuring the output of a manufacturing process, comparing it with the established quality standard, and using the resulting deviation (error signal) to initiate corrective action so that the process returns to or remains within specification.

**Two key elements of a feedback loop:**
1. **Measurement system** — devices and procedures for capturing the quality characteristic.
2. **Comparator / Decision rule** — the logic that compares measured value against specification or control limit and decides whether action is required.

*(Other acceptable elements: Reference/Set Point, Error Detector, Controller/Corrective Action, Process itself.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Differentiate between specification limits and control limits. Why is this distinction important for feedback-based quality control?*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Specification Limits | Control Limits |
|-------|----------------------|----------------|
| Set by | Customer / Design Engineer | Process itself (statistical) |
| Based on | Engineering requirement | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ of process data |
| Purpose | Define *what is acceptable* | Define *what the process naturally delivers* |
| Variability | Fixed once product is designed | Changes as process improves |

**Importance for Feedback:**
If a process operates *within* control limits but *outside* specification limits, the process is statistically stable but incapable — feedback indicates that the **process itself must be improved**, not merely corrected. Conversely, a process within specifications but outside control limits is **about to fail**, and feedback prevents future rejections. This dual diagnosis is the power of feedback-based SQC.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the role of feedback in maintaining and improving quality in a manufacturing process. Discuss the Shewhart control chart as a feedback mechanism with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process manufactures piston rings with a target inner diameter of **50 mm**. The standard deviation is known to be $\sigma = 0.8$ mm. Samples of size $n = 5$ are taken every hour. Compute the upper and lower control limits for the $\bar{X}$ chart. If a sample mean of **51.2 mm** is observed, what feedback action should be triggered? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **role of feedback** in quality control is threefold:

1. **Detection of Deviations:** Feedback continuously monitors the process and detects when the output drifts away from the target specification.
2. **Diagnosis of Causes:** By analysing patterns in the feedback signal (e.g., trends, runs, cycles), engineers can distinguish *common cause* (inherent) variation from *assignable cause* (special) variation.
3. **Correction and Learning:** Feedback initiates corrective action and feeds lessons learned back into process design — closing the loop of continuous improvement (Kaizen).

The **Shewhart control chart** functions as a discrete-time feedback controller:

- **Reference input** = Center Line (CL) at $\bar{\bar{X}}$.
- **Measured output** = Sample statistic $\bar{X}_k$ or $R_k$.
- **Error signal** = $\bar{X}_k - \bar{\bar{X}}$.
- **Control limits** = UCL and LCL at $\pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.
- **Controller action** = Stop / investigate when $\vert \bar{X}_k - \bar{\bar{X}} \vert > 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.

*Block diagram: Refer to the flowchart in Section 4.1 above.*

This feedback structure ensures that quality is **built into the process** rather than **inspected into the product**, consistent with Deming's philosophy.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Target (process mean) $\mu = 50$ mm
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 0.8$ mm
- Sample size $n = 5$

**Step 1 — Compute Standard Error of the Mean**

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.8}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.8}{2.2361} = 0.3578 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Control Limits using the $3\sigma$ rule**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 50 + 3(0.3578) = 50 + 1.0734 = 51.0734 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 50 - 3(0.3578) = 50 - 1.0734 = 48.9266 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare Observed Sample Mean**

Observed $\bar{X} = 51.2$ mm.

Since $51.2 > UCL_{\bar{X}} = 51.0734$, the point lies **above** the upper control limit.

**Step 4 — Feedback Action Triggered**

The control chart signals an **out-of-control condition**. Required feedback actions:
1. **Immediate** — Stop the production line and quarantine the last batch.
2. **Investigation** — Identify the assignable cause (e.g., tool wear, raw material batch change, operator error, machine misalignment).
3. **Corrective Action** — Adjust the process to bring the mean back to 50 mm; re-verify with a fresh sample.
4. **Documentation** — Record the incident in the control log and update the FMEA database.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to take the **square root of $n$** when computing $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. Do **not** confuse **Control Limits (UCL, LCL)** with **Specification Limits (USL, LSL)**. They are computed differently and serve different purposes. Mixing them is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets.
> 3. Always state the *decision* and the *corrective action*, not just the numerical limit. Examiners award the final 1–2 marks for logical closure of the feedback loop.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain how the **PDCA (Deming) cycle** acts as a feedback loop for continuous quality improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following 10 sample means (each from $n=4$ observations) have been collected from a filling process whose target weight is **500 g** and standard deviation is $\sigma = 4$ g:

$$\{ 501, 498, 502, 499, 503, 497, 504, 496, 505, 495 \}$$

Construct the $\bar{X}$ control chart and identify whether the process is in statistical control. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **PDCA cycle** is the canonical model of feedback-driven quality management:

| Stage | Action | Feedback Element |
|-------|--------|------------------|
| **PLAN** | Define quality objectives, identify customers, set specifications. | Sets the *reference input*. |
| **DO** | Execute the production process at small scale. | The *process under control*. |
| **CHECK** | Measure outputs, compare against the plan. | The *feedback measurement* and *error detection*. |
| **ACT** | If deviation found → take corrective action and standardize; if not found → standardize current best practice and proceed to next cycle. | The *corrective controller*. |

The cycle then **repeats**, with each iteration refining the standard. This is a **closed-loop negative feedback system** with a learning memory — each cycle's *ACT* output becomes the next cycle's *PLAN* input. Through this iterative feedback, the process mean converges toward the target and variance reduces, embodying **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**.

*(Block diagram: Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram above.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Target (center) $\mu = 500$ g
- $\sigma = 4$ g
- $n = 4$
- 10 sample means provided.

**Step 1 — Compute Grand Average**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{501 + 498 + 502 + 499 + 503 + 497 + 504 + 496 + 505 + 495}{10}$$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{5000}{10} = 500 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Standard Error**

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Control Limits**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 500 + 3(2) = 506 \text{ g}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 500 - 3(2) = 494 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Plot and Check All 10 Points**

| Sample # | $\bar{X}_k$ | Within [494, 506]? |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 501 | Yes |
| 2 | 498 | Yes |
| 3 | 502 | Yes |
| 4 | 499 | Yes |
| 5 | 503 | Yes |
| 6 | 497 | Yes |
| 7 | 504 | Yes |
| 8 | 496 | Yes |
| 9 | 505 | Yes |
| 10 | 495 | Yes |

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

All 10 sample means lie within the control limits [494 g, 506 g]. However, note a **monotonically increasing trend** from sample 2 to 5 (498 → 503) and a **monotonically decreasing trend** from sample 7 to 10 (504 → 495). While no individual point violates the $3\sigma$ rule, the **runs test** indicates non-random behaviour.

**Step 6 — Feedback Conclusion**

The process is **statistically in-control** by the $3\sigma$ Western Electric Rule 1, but the **trend patterns** suggest an emerging systematic cause (possibly tool wear or raw material drift). Recommended feedback action: **Increase sampling frequency** and investigate the underlying cyclic pattern before it breaches control limits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. When $\sigma$ is given directly (not $\bar{R}$), do **not** use $A_2$ — use the formula $UCL = \mu + 3\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. Using $A_2$ here is a common error.
> 2. Examiners expect students to **interpret** the chart, not just compute numbers. Always close your answer with a clear statement: *in-control* or *out-of-control*, and the action to be taken.
> 3. Trend detection (runs test) is a high-value answer for full marks on KTU papers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Role of Feedback in Quality Control**

- **Feedback Definition:** Returning process output information to the input stage for corrective action; mathematically $E(t) = R(t) - Y(t)$.
- **Negative Feedback** is the *stabilizing* type used in SQC; it opposes deviation.
- **PDCA Cycle** = Plan → Do → Check → Act is the *managerial embodiment* of feedback.
- **Shewhart Control Chart** = the *engineering embodiment* of feedback, using $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ decision boundaries.
- **Error Signal** in SQC = deviation of sample statistic from grand average (e.g., $\bar{X}_k - \bar{\bar{X}}$).
- **Specification Limits (USL, LSL)** ≠ **Control Limits (UCL, LCL)** — never interchange.
- **Corrective Action Magnitude** $U_k = -E_k$ (negative feedback law).
- **Stability Condition** for the closed-loop SQC system: controller gain $G$ must satisfy $0 < G < 2$.
- **ARL** quantifies feedback speed: $ARL_0 = 1/\alpha = 370.4$ in-control; $ARL_1 \approx 43.9$ for $1.5\sigma$ shift.
- **Feedback Distinguishes** *common cause* (inherent) variation from *assignable cause* (special) variation.
- **Closed-Loop SPC** in Industry 4.0 = automated sensor → comparator → controller feedback in real time.
- **Capability Indices** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are *outcomes* of an effective feedback system.
- **Always** conclude a KTU answer with: (i) numerical result, (ii) interpretation, (iii) corrective action — this sequence fetches full marks.
- The **Deming philosophy** positions feedback not as inspection but as a *learning mechanism* for systemic improvement.
- Modern extensions: **EWMA charts** and **CUSUM charts** are *integral* feedback controllers (memory-based), unlike Shewhart's *instantaneous* (memoryless) feedback.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Perspective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Perspective # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is "Quality"? — The Engineering Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > **Quality** is the totality of features and characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. *(Ref: ISO 8402 / IS 13979)* In simpler engineering terms, **Quality** is the **degree of conformance** of a product or service to its design specifications and the **degree of fitness for use** by the customer. A high-quality product is one that: - Meets design specifications (Conformance Quality) - Satisfies customer expectations (Performance Quality) - Is delivered on time and at the agreed cost (Service Quality) --- ## 1.2 The Five Classical Viewpoints of Quality (Perspective of Quality) Quality is not a single dimension — it is viewed differently by different stakeholders. The **KTU 2024 syllabus** explicitly tests the following five classical perspectives: | # | Perspective | Core Idea | Typical Stakeholder | |---|-------------|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Transcendent View** | Quality is an innate, absolute, universally recognized excellence | Philosopher / End-user feeling | | 2 | **Product-Based View** | Quality is a precise, measurable variable (differences in quantity of an attribute) | Engineer / Designer | | 3 | **User-Based View** | Quality lies in the eyes of the beholder — fitness for intended use | Customer | | 4 | **Manufacturing-Based View** | Quality is conformance to specifications, standards, and engineering drawings | Production / Shop-floor engineer | | 5 | **Value-Based View** | Quality is the degree of excellence at an acceptable price — the cost–performance trade-off | Business / Procurement manager | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Quality Like a Restaurant Meal" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Learning Aid):** > > Imagine you order a **Masala Dosa at a Kerala restaurant**. > > - The **transcendent view** is the *feel* of the taste — you simply *know* it's a great dosa. > - The **product-based view** measures: weight (g), diameter (cm), oil content (ml), crispness index. > - The **user-based view** says: "I love this dosa, even if it's slightly oily." > - The **manufacturing-based view** says: "The dosa must be exactly 28 cm in diameter, weigh 220 g, and contain 15 ml oil per the recipe." > - The **value-based view** asks: "Is ₹60 a fair price for a dosa of this size and taste?" > > **Quality is the balanced satisfaction of ALL five views simultaneously.** --- ## 1.4 Why Quality Perspective Matters in SQC Statistical Quality Control (SQC) is **rooted in the manufacturing-based and product-based views**, but it **serves** the user-based and value-based views. Without understanding *whose* quality we are measuring, the statistical tools (control charts, acceptance sampling) become mathematically correct but commercially meaningless. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** > Questions like *"Differentiate between the product-based and user-based views of quality"* are **guaranteed 5-mark/short-answer items** in the KTU ESE. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality David A. Garvin (1987) broadened the concept of quality into **eight strategic dimensions** — a frequently tested topic in KTU. > [!NOTE] > **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality (Must-Memorize List):** > > 1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics (e.g., mileage of a car). > 2. **Features** — Secondary, supplemental characteristics (e.g., touchscreen in a budget car). > 3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time. > 4. **Conformance** — Degree to which product meets pre-established standards. > 5. **Durability** — Length of product life under normal use. > 6. **Serviceability** — Speed, ease, and courtesy of repair. > 7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal — look, feel, sound, taste. > 8. **Perceived Quality** — Reputation, brand image, indirect measures. --- ## 2.2 Quality Terminology — KTU Board-Standard Glossary | Term | Definition (KTU 2024) | |------|------------------------| | **Quality of Design** | The degree to which the design specification reflects the *intended use* and customer requirements. | | **Quality of Conformance** | The degree to which the manufactured product *actually* follows the design specifications. | | **Quality Control (QC)** | The operational techniques and activities used to *fulfill* requirements for quality. (Detection-oriented.) | | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | All planned and systematic activities *implemented* within the quality system that can be demonstrated to *provide confidence* that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. (Prevention-oriented.) | | **Quality Planning** | Strategic activity that identifies quality standards and determines the processes to meet them. | | **Quality Improvement (Kaizen)** | Formal methodology (PDCA / DMAIC) for continuously reducing variation and waste. | | **TQM (Total Quality Management)** | A *company-wide* management philosophy focused on continuously improving quality through customer satisfaction. | | **Six Sigma** | A data-driven methodology targeting **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — i.e., 4.5 standard deviations from the mean. | --- ## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The KTU High-Yield Numerical Section The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the sum of all costs incurred to prevent poor quality, plus the costs of detecting and rectifying poor quality. It has **four classical categories**: 1. **Prevention Costs (P)** — Costs of *avoiding* defects. *Examples:* training, quality planning, supplier capability studies. 2. **Appraisal Costs (A)** — Costs of *detecting* defects. *Examples:* inspection, testing, equipment calibration. 3. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** — Costs of defects found *before* delivery. *Examples:* scrap, rework, re-inspection. 4. **External Failure Costs (EFC)** — Costs of defects found *after* delivery. *Examples:* warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits. ### 2.3.1 Key Quality Cost Formulas > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Use these directly in numerical problems):** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|----------|---------|-------| | 1 | **Total Quality Cost (TQC)** | $TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC$ | ₹ (Rupees) | | 2 | **Total Cost of Quality as % of Sales** | $COQ\% = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Total Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 3 | **Quality Cost per Unit** | $QPU = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Number of Units Produced}}$ | ₹ / unit | | 4 | **Quality Loss Function (Taguchi)** | $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ | ₹ | | 5 | **Six Sigma DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | defects / million | | 6 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | **Process Capability (centered)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | dimensionless | > **Legend (for above formulas):** > - $k$ = quality loss coefficient (a cost constant) > - $y$ = actual quality characteristic value > - $m$ = target value of the quality characteristic > - $D$ = number of defects > - $U$ = number of units > - $O$ = number of opportunities per unit > - $USL, LSL$ = Upper / Lower Specification Limits > - $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = process standard deviation ### 2.3.2 Engineering Utility In manufacturing (e.g., **Kerala's cashew, spices, or electronics industries**), SQC engineers compute **TQC** to convince management that: - Increasing *Prevention costs* by ₹1 Lakh can *reduce* failure costs by ₹5–10 Lakh. - The **optimum quality cost** occurs when the sum of (Prevention + Appraisal) ≈ (Internal + External Failure). - Taguchi's **Loss Function** shows that even a product *within* specifications causes customer-society loss — driving the Six Sigma philosophy. --- ## 2.4 Historical Evolution of Quality (KTU Frequently Asked) > [!NOTE] > **Quality Gurus & Their Contributions (Mandatory for KTU):** > > | Guru | Period | Key Contribution | > |------|--------|------------------| > | **Walter A. Shewhart** | 1920s | Father of Statistical Quality Control; Control Charts (1931). | > | **W. Edwards Deming** | 1940s | PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act); 14 Points for Management. | > | **Joseph M. Juran** | 1950s | Juran Trilogy — Quality Planning, Control, Improvement. | > | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956 | Concept of *Total Quality Control* (TQC). | > | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1960s | Quality Circles; Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram. | > | **Genichi Taguchi** | 1980s | Quality Loss Function; Robust Design. | > | **Philip B. Crosby** | 1970s | "Quality is Free"; Zero Defects; Quality is Conformance. | > | **Motorola Engineers** | 1986 | Six Sigma Methodology (Bill Smith). | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: The Taguchi Quality Loss Function Taguchi redefined quality as the **financial loss imparted to society** from the time a product is shipped. He proposed that any deviation from the target value $m$ incurs a loss proportional to the *square* of the deviation. ### Step 1 — Define the Loss Function Let $L(y)$ be the loss incurred when a product's quality characteristic takes the value $y$. Taguchi's empirical model is: $$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$$ where: - $y$ = the actual measured value of the quality characteristic. - $m$ = the target (nominal) value. - $k$ = the loss coefficient (a constant with units of ₹ per unit²). ### Step 2 — Determine the Constant $k$ Using the Tolerance Limit Suppose the customer's loss (or cost of repair/replacement) when the characteristic reaches the **functional tolerance limit** $\Delta_{0}$ from target is $A_{0}$. That is, when $(y - m) = \pm \Delta_{0}$, the loss $L(y) = A_{0}$. Substituting into the loss function: $$A_{0} = k(\Delta_{0})^{2}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for the Loss Coefficient $k$ Re-arranging the above equation: $$k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $k$ Back to Obtain the Operational Loss Function $$L(y) = \left[\dfrac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}}\right] (y - m)^{2}$$ ### Step 5 — Average Quality Loss Over a Production Batch If $n$ units are produced with measured values $y_{1}, y_{2}, \dots, y_{n}$, the **average loss per unit** is: $$\bar{L} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k(y_{i} - m)^{2} = k \cdot \overline{(y - m)^{2}}$$ Since $\overline{(y - m)^{2}}$ is the **second central moment** about the target, this represents the **Mean Squared Deviation (MSD)** from target. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** > Taguchi's loss function **refutes** the old "Goal Post Mentality" (any value within specs is acceptable). It proves that a product *at the specification limit* causes loss $A_{0}$, and a product *exactly at target* causes zero loss. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Six Sigma DPMO Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem (Modelled after KTU past papers):** > A mobile phone assembly line produces **5,000 units/day**. Each phone has **250 opportunities** for defects. At the end of a day, **142 defects** are observed. Calculate the DPMO and the corresponding sigma level. ### Step 1 — Identify the Given Values $$D = 142 \text{ defects},\quad U = 5{,}000 \text{ units},\quad O = 250 \text{ opportunities per unit}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Total Opportunity Count $$\text{Total Opportunities} = U \times O = 5{,}000 \times 250 = 1{,}250{,}000$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the DPMO Formula $$DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$$ Substituting: $$DPMO = \dfrac{142 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}250{,}000}$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate Numerically $$DPMO = \dfrac{142{,}000{,}000}{1{,}250{,}000} = 113.6 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert DPMO to Sigma Level Using the standard **Six Sigma conversion table** (yield-vs-sigma): | Yield (%) | DPMO | Sigma Level | |-----------|------|-------------| | 99.99966 | 3.4 | 6.0 | | 99.999 | 10 | 5.6 | | 99.99 | 100 | 5.2 | | 99.98 | 200 | 5.0 | | 99.0 | 10,000 | 3.8 | | 95.0 | 50,000 | 3.0 | | **99.9886** | **113.6** | **≈ 5.4** | For $DPMO = 113.6$, the corresponding sigma level is approximately **5.4 σ**. > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This is a world-class process (above the industry average of ~4 σ ≈ 6,210 DPMO), but the company should still target 6 σ = 3.4 DPMO. --- ## 3.3 Quality Cost Trade-Off Derivation (Economic Model) The total quality cost as a function of prevention investment $P$ can be modelled as: $$TQC(P) = P + A(P) + IFC(P) + EFC(P)$$ As $P$ increases: - Prevention and Appraisal costs **increase** monotonically. - Internal and External Failure costs **decrease** monotonically. The **optimum** occurs where: $$\dfrac{d\,TQC}{dP} = \dfrac{dA}{dP} + \dfrac{dIFC}{dP} + \dfrac{dEFC}{dP} = -1$$ This is a **first-order optimality condition** — the marginal cost of additional prevention equals the marginal benefit from reduction in failures. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 The Five Classical Quality Perspectives — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Quality — A Multi-Viewed Concept]) --> V1 Start --> V2 Start --> V3 Start --> V4 Start --> V5 V1["Perspective 1: TRANSCENDENT
Innate excellence, absolute goodness"] --> Stake1[Stakeholder: Philosopher, End-User Feeling] V2["Perspective 2: PRODUCT-BASED
Measurable attributes, quantifiable variables"] --> Stake2[Stakeholder: Engineer, Designer] V3["Perspective 3: USER-BASED
Fitness for intended use, customer satisfaction"] --> Stake3[Stakeholder: Customer, Market] V4["Perspective 4: MANUFACTURING-BASED
Conformance to specifications and drawings"] --> Stake4[Stakeholder: Production Engineer, QA] V5["Perspective 5: VALUE-BASED
Excellence at an acceptable price and cost"] --> Stake5[Stakeholder: Procurement, Business Manager] Stake1 --> SQC[Statistical Quality Control Tools] Stake2 --> SQC Stake3 --> SQC Stake4 --> SQC Stake5 --> SQC SQC --> Outcome([Improved Product and Customer Satisfaction]) ``` ## 4.2 Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality — Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Q([Quality of a Product]) --> D1 Q --> D2 Q --> D3 Q --> D4 Q --> D5 Q --> D6 Q --> D7 Q --> D8 D1["1. PERFORMANCE
Primary characteristics"] --> P1[Example: Car mileage] D2["2. FEATURES
Supplementary traits"] --> P2[Example: Touchscreen display] D3["3. RELIABILITY
Failure-free operation"] --> P3[Example: MTBF of laptop] D4["4. CONFORMANCE
Adherence to specs"] --> P4[Example: Tolerance on shaft diameter] D5["5. DURABILITY
Length of product life"] --> P5[Example: Cycle life of battery] D6["6. SERVICEABILITY
Ease of repair"] --> P6[Example: Service center network] D7["7. AESTHETICS
Sensory appeal"] --> P7[Example: Mobile phone finish] D8["8. PERCEIVED QUALITY
Reputation and image"] --> P8[Example: Apple brand trust] ``` ## 4.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Functional Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD COQ[Total Cost of Quality TQC] --> CatA COQ --> CatB COQ --> CatC COQ --> CatD CatA["PREVENTION COSTS P
Training, planning, supplier audits"] --> Effect1[REDUCES failure costs] CatB["APPRAISAL COSTS A
Inspection, testing, calibration"] --> Effect1 CatC["INTERNAL FAILURE IFC
Scrap, rework, re-inspection"] --> Effect2[BEFORE delivery] CatD["EXTERNAL FAILURE EFC
Warranty, returns, lawsuits"] --> Effect3[AFTER delivery] Effect1 --> Optimum Effect2 --> Optimum Effect3 --> Optimum Optimum([Optimum Quality Cost where dTQC/dP equals zero]) ``` ## 4.4 Evolution of Quality Management — Timeline Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR E1[1920s Inspection Era] --> E2[1930s Shewhart Control Charts] E2 --> E3[1940s Deming PDCA and SQC in Japan] E3 --> E4[1950s Juran Trilogy and Feigenbaum TQC] E4 --> E5[1960s Ishikawa Quality Circles] E5 --> E6[1970s Crosby Zero Defects and TQM] E6 --> E7[1980s Taguchi Loss Function and Robust Design] E7 --> E8[1986 Six Sigma at Motorola] E8 --> E9[2000s Lean Six Sigma and Industry 4.0 Quality] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model] **Define quality. List and briefly explain any three views of quality.** **Model Answer (Board-Standard):** > **Quality** is the totality of features and characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs *(IS 13979 / ISO 8402)*. **Three views of quality:** 1. **User-based view:** Quality is the fitness of a product for its intended use as perceived by the customer. The *customer defines* quality. 2. **Manufacturing-based view:** Quality is the degree of conformance of the manufactured product to its design specifications and engineering drawings. 3. **Value-based view:** Quality is the degree of excellence offered at an *acceptable cost*. It balances performance against price. *(For full 3 marks, students must define quality + explain 3 views with one example each.)* --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model] **Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC).** **Model Answer (Board-Standard):** | Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) | |-----------|------------------------|-----------------------| | **Focus** | *Prevention* of defects | *Detection* of defects | | **Approach** | Process-oriented | Product-oriented | | **Scope** | Company-wide, all activities | Specific to production/inspection | | **Stage** | Throughout the value chain | After manufacture (in-process/final) | | **Tools** | Audits, training, process design | Control charts, sampling, inspection | | **Mindset** | "Do it right the first time" | "Find and remove the defects" | > **One-liner trick to remember:** *QA is before the defect occurs; QC is after it occurs.* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** [KTU ESE – Module 1 Typical Pattern] **(a) Explain the five classical perspectives of quality in detail. Discuss how each perspective influences the design of a Statistical Quality Control system in a manufacturing organization. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The five classical perspectives of quality are: 1. **Transcendent View** — Quality is an absolute, universally recognized standard of excellence. It is *abstract* and *subjective*; cannot be measured directly. Example: the perceived craftsmanship of a hand-made Swiss watch. 2. **Product-Based View** — Quality is treated as a *precise, measurable variable*; differences in quality reflect differences in the quantity of some attribute (e.g., thread count in fabric, octane number of fuel). 3. **User-Based View** — Quality is *fitness for use* as judged by the customer. A product meeting customer expectations has high quality; otherwise, low quality — regardless of the engineering spec. 4. **Manufacturing-Based View** — Quality is *conformance to specifications*. The SQC engineer measures each unit against the drawing/specs and rejects non-conformities. 5. **Value-Based View** — Quality is the *degree of excellence at an acceptable price*. Trade-off between cost and performance. **Influence on SQC Design:** - The **manufacturing-based view** justifies the use of **control charts** ($\bar{X}, R, p, c$) — these directly monitor conformance to specifications. - The **product-based view** drives the use of **process capability indices** ($C_{p}$, $C_{pk}$) which measure attribute-level quality. - The **user-based view** motivates **acceptance sampling** plans where customer risk is balanced with producer risk. - The **value-based view** drives the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** analysis and the Taguchi Loss Function. - The **transcendent view** underpins the **brand equity** and is indirectly served by all the above. > *For full 7 marks: explain all 5 views with examples + link each to at least one SQC tool.* --- **(b) The following data relates to a small-scale electronic component manufacturer for a year:** | Cost Category | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) | |---------------|---------------------| | Quality Planning & Training (Prevention) | 3.2 | | Incoming & In-process Inspection (Appraisal) | 4.5 | | Scrap and Rework (Internal Failure) | 6.8 | | Warranty Claims and Returns (External Failure) | 9.5 | | **Total Sales Revenue for the year** | **240** | **Calculate: (i) Total Cost of Quality (TQC), (ii) Cost of Quality as a % of Sales, (iii) Quality Cost per Unit, if 80,000 units were produced. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step (i) — Total Cost of Quality:** $$TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC = 3.2 + 4.5 + 6.8 + 9.5 = 24.0 \text{ Lakhs}$$ So, **TQC = ₹ 24.0 Lakhs**. **Step (ii) — COQ as % of Sales:** $$COQ\% = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{24.0}{240} \times 100 = 10.0\%$$ **Step (iii) — Quality Cost per Unit (QPU):** $$QPU = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Units Produced}} = \dfrac{24{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = ₹ 30 \text{ per unit}$$ **Inference:** With external failure costs (₹ 9.5 L) being the largest contributor (39.6% of TQC), the firm should invest more in **prevention** (training, robust design) to reduce warranty burden. --- ### **Question B2 — Choice B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice for Module 1] **(a) Explain the Taguchi Quality Loss Function. A shaft has a target diameter of 50.00 mm. The customer's loss (replacement cost) when the diameter deviates to the tolerance limit of ±0.50 mm is ₹ 4,000. Calculate: (i) the loss coefficient $k$, (ii) the loss when the actual diameter is 50.30 mm. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **The Taguchi Quality Loss Function** states that any deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value $m$ causes a loss to society proportional to the **square of the deviation**: $$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$$ The model **refutes the "Goal-Post Mentality"** — a product within specs is *not* zero-loss, only the product *exactly at target* is zero-loss. **Given data:** - Target $m = 50.00$ mm - Tolerance limit $\Delta_{0} = \pm 0.50$ mm - Loss at tolerance limit $A_{0} = ₹ 4{,}000$ **Step (i) — Loss Coefficient $k$:** $$A_{0} = k(\Delta_{0})^{2} \implies k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{(0.50)^{2}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{0.25} = 16{,}000 \text{ ₹ / mm}^2$$ **Step (ii) — Loss when $y = 50.30$ mm:** Deviation: $(y - m) = 50.30 - 50.00 = 0.30$ mm $$L(y) = 16{,}000 \times (0.30)^{2} = 16{,}000 \times 0.09 = ₹ 1{,}440$$ > **Key Insight:** Even a diameter of 50.30 mm — well *within* the ±0.50 mm tolerance — causes a loss of ₹ 1,440 per shaft. This is the foundation of **Robust Design** in SQC. --- **(b) List and briefly explain Garvin's eight dimensions of product quality. Why is it important for an SQC engineer to consider all eight dimensions while designing a quality system? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality:** 1. **Performance** — The primary operating characteristics of a product. *Example: maximum speed, fuel efficiency of a car.* 2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that supplement the basic functioning. *Example: a rear-view camera in a car.* 3. **Reliability** — The probability of a product performing its intended function *without failure* over a specified time period. 4. **Conformance** — The degree to which a product meets pre-established engineering specifications and standards. 5. **Durability** — The length of useful life of a product before it deteriorates beyond acceptable use. 6. **Serviceability** — The speed, competence, and courtesy with which a product is restored to working order after a failure. 7. **Aesthetics** — The sensory appeal of the product — appearance, feel, sound, taste. 8. **Perceived Quality** — The customer's indirect assessment of quality based on reputation, brand image, and past experience. **Importance for the SQC Engineer:** - **Conformance** and **Reliability** are *directly measurable* through **control charts** and **reliability testing**. - **Performance** and **Features** are validated through **design-of-experiments (DOE)** and **engineering testing**. - **Durability** and **Serviceability** require **accelerated life testing** and **field data analysis**. - **Aesthetics** and **Perceived Quality** drive **customer satisfaction surveys** and **voice-of-customer (VOC)** analysis. - A *narrow* focus on conformance alone (the manufacturing view) ignores 7 of 8 dimensions, leading to a technically perfect but commercially unsuccessful product. > *For full 7 marks: list all 8 + explain at least 4 with examples + justify why the SQC engineer needs all 8.* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 1 — Quality Perspective):** > > 1. **Confusing "Quality" with "Grade":** Grade refers to *categorical* ranking (A-grade, B-grade steel); Quality refers to *degree of excellence*. Many students write the definition of *grade* and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Missing the 5 perspectives:** Students often write only 3 views. The KTU board *explicitly tests all 5* — losing a view = losing 1 mark each. > 3. **Mixing up QA and QC:** They are *not* synonyms. QA is *prevention*, QC is *detection*. Many students use them interchangeably → 3-mark penalty. > 4. **Taguchi Loss Function — forgetting the squaring:** Writing $L(y) = k(y - m)$ instead of $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ → 0 marks for the formula. > 5. **Unit errors in COQ numericals:** Students forget to convert Lakhs → Rupees before dividing by number of units → wrong QPU answer. > 6. **Garvin's dimensions incomplete:** Listing only 4–5 dimensions instead of 8. Each missing dimension = 0.5 mark lost. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Perspective:** > > - ⭐ **Quality = Conformance + Fitness for Use + Value for Price** (3-in-1 definition). > - ⭐ **Five Views:** Transcendent, Product-based, User-based, Manufacturing-based, Value-based — remember the acronym **T-PUMV**. > - ⭐ **Quality is NOT the same as Grade** (Grade = category, Quality = excellence). > - ⭐ **QA = Prevention, QC = Detection** — never interchange these. > - ⭐ **Cost of Quality has 4 components:** P, A, IFC, EFC. > - ⭐ **TQC Formula:** $TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC$. > - ⭐ **COQ % of Sales:** A good benchmark is **2.5% – 5%** for manufacturing; >10% signals poor quality. > - ⭐ **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ — loss is **proportional to squared deviation**, not binary. > - ⭐ **Loss Coefficient:** $k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{(\Delta_{0})^{2}}$. > - ⭐ **Six Sigma DPMO Formula:** $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$. > - ⭐ **Garvin's 8 Dimensions** — must be memorised in correct order: *Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality*. > - ⭐ **Quality Gurus & Eras:** Shewhart (Control Charts) → Deming (PDCA) → Juran (Trilogy) → Ishikawa (Fishbone) → Taguchi (Loss) → Motorola (Six Sigma). > - ⭐ **Goal-Post Mentality** is *rejected* by Taguchi — even within-spec products cause societal loss. > - ⭐ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO** (not zero) because of the 1.5 σ long-term drift allowance. > - ⭐ **Optimum quality cost** is achieved when the marginal cost of prevention = marginal benefit from reduced failures. > **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** > If you remember only **one sentence** for the exam: > *"Quality is the balanced satisfaction of transcendent, product, user, manufacturing, and value-based perspectives, measured through cost-of-quality and Taguchi's loss function, and improved through statistical process control."*
