> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance Manual

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Quality Assurance Manual (QAM) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1)**
> The **Quality Assurance Manual (QAM)** is the *top-tier governing document* of an organization's Quality Management System (QMS). It formally states the **scope of the QMS**, the **quality policy**, **quality objectives**, and references all **documented procedures** required by the chosen standard (e.g., **ISO 9001:2015**). In Statistical Quality Control (SQC) environments, the QAM acts as the bridge between *management intent* and *statistical process control (SPC) execution*.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Quality Assurance Manual** is a structured, controlled, and approved document that:

1. Establishes the **Quality Management System (QMS)** policies, processes, and procedures of an organization.
2. Demonstrates the organization's ability to consistently provide products/services that meet **customer** and **regulatory** requirements.
3. Serves as a **permanent reference** for both internal personnel and external auditors (e.g., third-party certification bodies).
4. Acts as a **scaling tool** — the manual is uniform across branches/departments so that statistical data, sampling plans, control charts, and process capability indices (Cp, Cpk) can be aggregated meaningfully.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Manual as a Constitution"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Constitution of a Company**
> Think of a country. The **Constitution** is the highest legal document — it sets rights, duties, and structure. Below it sit *Acts of Parliament* (procedures), and below them *rules and regulations* (work instructions).
> Similarly, the **QAM is the Constitution** of a QMS. Each *documented procedure* is an Act, and each *work instruction / SPC operating procedure* is a regulation. Statistical Quality Control tools (X̄-R charts, p-charts, Cpk analysis) are the "judgments" rendered under this framework.

## 1.3 Why a QAM is Mandatory in SQC Industries

| Industry | Why QAM matters for SQC |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Defines the master plan for SPC deployment, gauge R&R, and acceptance sampling (e.g., MIL-STD-1916, ANSI/ASQ Z1.4). |
| **Pharmaceutical / Healthcare** | Required by **21 CFR Part 820 / ICH Q9**; binds validation protocols to the manual. |
| **Software / IT Services** | Aligns CMMI/ISO 9001 with defect-density metrics and Six Sigma projects. |
| **Automotive** | Mandated by **IATF 16949**, which is an extension of ISO 9001:2015. |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall for KTU Viva**
> "QAM = the topmost controlled document that *describes* what your QMS does; *Documented Procedures* *describe how* it does it; *Records* prove that it was done."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Pyramid of QMS Documentation

QMS documents are organized in **four levels**. The QAM sits at **Level I**.

$$\boxed{\text{Level I (Manual)} \;\supset\; \text{Level II (Procedures)} \;\supset\; \text{Level III (Work Instructions)} \;\supset\; \text{Level IV (Records/Forms)}}$$

- **Level I — Quality Manual:** States the *what* and the *why*. High-level policy statements.
- **Level II — Documented Procedures:** Mandatory procedures such as *Document Control*, *Internal Audit*, *Corrective Action*.
- **Level III — Work Instructions / SOPs:** Detailed, role-specific instructions (e.g., how to set up an X̄–R control chart, how to perform a t-test for process capability).
- **Level IV — Records / Data Forms:** Objective evidence — completed control charts, calibration logs, capability indices reports.

## 2.2 Mandatory Contents of a QAM (ISO 9001:2015 Mapping)

| Clause of ISO 9001:2015 | Required Content in QAM | SQC Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **4. Context of the Organization** | Scope, boundaries, interested parties, SWOT of QMS | Defines which processes fall under SPC monitoring. |
| **5. Leadership** | Quality Policy, roles, responsibilities, management commitment | Top-down authority to issue *Out-of-Control Action Plans* (OCAP). |
| **6. Planning** | Quality Objectives, risks, opportunities, change planning | SMART goals such as "Reduce Cpk variation to $\geq 1.33$." |
| **7. Support** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information | Calibration of gauges — foundation of measurement system analysis (MSA). |
| **8. Operation** | Operational planning, product/service requirements, design, control of externally provided processes | SPC implementation: variable/attribute control charts, acceptance sampling. |
| **9. Performance Evaluation** | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review | Periodic review of $\bar{X}-R$, $p$, $c$, $u$ charts. |
| **10. Improvement** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement | PDCA (Deming) cycle applied to SPC projects. |

## 2.3 Core Documented Procedures (Six Mandatory Procedures in ISO 9001:2015)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although ISO 9001:2015 removed the strict "six documented procedures" mandate of the 2008 version, the *KTU 2024 SQC syllabus* still expects knowledge of these six documents:
>
> 1. **Control of Documented Information (Document Control)**
> 2. **Control of Records**
> 3. **Internal Audit**
> 4. **Control of Nonconforming Product / Output**
> 5. **Corrective Action**
> 6. **Preventive Action** (now blended into risks & opportunities under 9001:2015)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Relevant to Module 1)

| Concept | Formula / Variable | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless; $C_p = 1.00$ is the **minimum acceptable** industry baseline. |
| Process Capability (shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ = capable, $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ = world-class. |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Used in Six Sigma reporting. |
| Process Sigma Level | $\sigma_{\text{level}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{\sigma}$ (one-sided) | Mapped to DPMO via standard normal table. |
| Sampling Plan Notation | $(N, n, c)$ | Population size, sample size, acceptance number. |
| Control Chart Central Line (X̄) | $\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$ (grand mean) | A *registered* value inside the QAM. |
| Document Revision Identifier | $R = \text{Major}.\text{Minor}$ (e.g., 3.2) | Major = structural change; Minor = editorial. |

> **Note on the pipe symbol:** Absolute value $\lvert x \rvert$ has been written with `\lvert ... \rvert` to prevent markdown-table corruption.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Manual Element

- **Scope clause (4.3):** Without a defined scope, *statistical data lose context* — you cannot aggregate process capability from two unrelated product lines.
- **Quality policy (5.2):** Anchors the **common-cause vs special-cause** interpretation in SPC. Policy shapes whether operators *adjust* the process (tampering) or *stop and investigate* (correct response to an out-of-control point).
- **Internal audits (9.2):** Verify that the $\bar{X}-R$ chart posted on the shop floor actually corresponds to the latest data — a common non-conformance in ISO audits.
- **Management review (9.3):** Quarterly review of $C_{pk}$ trends, Pareto of defects, and cost of poor quality (COPQ) — the *evidence* layer of the manual.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This module is **documentary / procedural** rather than algebraic, so derivations here focus on **logical flow** rather than calculus. We will derive:
- (a) The capability-to-sigma relationship, $C_{pk} \rightarrow \sigma_{\text{level}}$.
- (b) The DPMO-to-yield conversion.
- (c) The Out-of-Control Action Plan (OCAP) trigger logic.

## 3.1 Derivation: From $C_{pk}$ to the Process Sigma Level

**Step 1 — Definition of $C_{pk}$ for a one-sided specification (USL only).**
By convention, when only the **upper specification limit (USL)** matters (e.g., impurities, defects), we can express the index as:

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Process Sigma Level $\sigma_{\text{level}}$.**
The Process Sigma Level is the number of standard deviations between the process mean and the nearest specification limit. In a *centered* one-sided case:

$$\sigma_{\text{level}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**
Divide the equation of Step 2 by the equation of Step 1 (both share the same numerator):

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{\text{level}}}{C_{pk,\text{upper}}} = \dfrac{(\text{USL} - \mu)/\sigma}{(\text{USL} - \mu)/(3\sigma)} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma_{\text{level}}$.**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{level}} = 3 \times C_{pk}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical application.**
If $C_{pk} = 1.33$, the process is operating at $\sigma_{\text{level}} = 3.99 \approx 4\sigma$, which translates (via the standard normal table) to a defect rate of $\approx 6210$ DPMO.

> [!NOTE]
> The same derivation, applied to a two-sided centered process, gives $\sigma_{\text{level}} = 3 \times C_p$, with the additional requirement that $\mu$ equal the midpoint of the tolerance band.

## 3.2 Derivation: DPMO to First-Pass Yield (FPY)

**Step 1 — Define DPMO.**
A DPMO is the number of defects normalized per one million opportunities:

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$$

where $D$ = total defects, $U$ = units produced, $O$ = opportunities per unit.

**Step 2 — Convert DPMO to a probability of a defect.**
Probability of a single defect per opportunity is:

$$p = \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the First-Pass Yield.**
Assuming a Poisson-like approximation, the probability of *zero defects in $O$ opportunities* is:

$$\text{FPY} \approx e^{-p \cdot O} \times 100\%$$

For a *single* opportunity per unit, this collapses to:

$$\text{FPY} = (1 - p) \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
If a process records 233 DPMO, then $p = 0.000233$, and

$$\text{FPY} = (1 - 0.000233) \times 100\% = 99.9767\%$$

This corresponds approximately to a **4.5 σ** short-term process.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — QAM Triggers vs SPC Tools

| QAM Document Reference | Trigger Condition | Required SPC Tool | Recorded Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clause 8.5.1 (Control of Production) | Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) parameter identified | $\bar{X}-R$ or $X-MR$ chart | CL, UCL, LCL values, control limits |
| Clause 8.7 (Control of Nonconforming Output) | Any point outside control limits | OCAP (Out-of-Control Action Plan) | Corrective Action Report (CAR) |
| Clause 9.1.1 (Monitoring & Measurement) | Customer CTQ drift | $C_{pk}$ trend chart | Quarterly capability review minutes |
| Clause 10.2 (Nonconformity & CA) | Recurring special-cause signals | Pareto + Fishbone (Ishikawa) | 8D report, root cause log |
| Clause 7.1.5 (Monitoring & Measuring Resources) | Gauge drift suspected | Gauge R&R (GRR) | %GRR, %PV, %EV |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use only **alphanumeric node identifiers** and **clean uppercase text inside double quotes** to comply with Mermaid rendering rules.

## 4.1 Document Hierarchy of a QMS

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[LEVEL I QUALITY MANUAL QAM] --> L2[LEVEL II DOCUMENTED PROCEDURES]
    L2 --> L3[LEVEL III WORK INSTRUCTIONS AND SOPS]
    L3 --> L4[LEVEL IV RECORDS AND DATA FORMS]
    L1 --> P1[POLICY STATEMENT]
    L1 --> P2[QUALITY OBJECTIVES]
    L1 --> P3[SCOPE AND BOUNDARIES]
    L2 --> D1[DOCUMENT CONTROL]
    L2 --> D2[INTERNAL AUDIT]
    L2 --> D3[CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    L3 --> W1[CONTROL CHART SETUP SOP]
    L3 --> W2[GAUGE CALIBRATION SOP]
    L4 --> R1[XBAR R CHART LOG]
    L4 --> R2[CALIBRATION RECORDS]
    L4 --> R3[CAR REGISTER]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — From QAM Clause to SPC Action

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[QAM CLAUSE 9 PERFORMANCE EVALUATION] --> B[DEFINE CTQ AND TOLERANCE]
    B --> C[COLLECT SUBGROUP DATA N5]
    C --> D[COMPUTE XDOUBLE BAR AND R BAR]
    D --> E{CONTROL LIMENTS WITHIN 3 SIGMA}
    E -- YES --> F[COMPUTE CP AND CPK]
    E -- NO --> G[TRIGGER OCAP CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    F --> H{ABILITY MEETS OBJECTIVE}
    H -- YES --> I[RECORD IN QMS REGISTER]
    H -- NO --> G
    G --> J[ROOT CAUSE 8D REPORT]
    J --> K[UPDATE QAM REVISION LOG]
```

## 4.3 PDCA (Deming) Cycle Aligned with the QAM

```mermaid
graph TD
    PLAN[PLAN QAM CLAUSE 6 SET OBJECTIVES] --> DO[DO QAM CLAUSE 8 IMPLEMENT SPC]
    DO --> CHECK[CHECK QAM CLAUSE 9 MEASURE AND REVIEW]
    CHECK --> ACT[ACT QAM CLAUSE 10 CORRECT AND IMPROVE]
    ACT --> PLAN
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — QAM as a Binding System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TOP[EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT]
        ISO[ISO 9001 2015]
        CUST[CUSTOMER REQUIREMENTS]
        REG[REGULATORY BODIES]
    end
    subgraph MID[QAM INTERNAL LAYER]
        POL[QUALITY POLICY]
        OBJ[QUALITY OBJECTIVES]
        PROC[DOCUMENTED PROCEDURES]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOM[OPERATIONAL LAYER]
        SPC[STATISTICAL PROCESS CONTROL]
        MSA[MEASUREMENT SYSTEM ANALYSIS]
        SAM[ACCEPTANCE SAMPLING]
    end
    ISO --> POL
    CUST --> OBJ
    REG --> POL
    POL --> PROC
    OBJ --> SPC
    PROC --> SPC
    PROC --> MSA
    PROC --> SAM
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Quality Assurance Manual. Mention any two mandatory contents.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Quality Assurance Manual (QAM)** is the top-level document that describes an organization's **Quality Management System (QMS)** — its policies, scope, procedures, and responsibilities — in compliance with a chosen standard (e.g., ISO 9001:2015).
Two mandatory contents: (i) **Scope of the QMS** (Clause 4.3) and (ii) **Quality Policy and Objectives** (Clause 5.2). *(Other acceptable answers: Document Control Procedure, Records Control Procedure, Management Review Procedure.)*

### **Q2. Distinguish between a Quality Manual and a Quality Procedure.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

| Aspect | Quality Manual | Quality Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | States **what** the QMS does and **why** | States **how** a specific activity is performed |
| Audience | Customers, auditors, top management | Operating personnel |
| Level | Level I documentation | Level II documentation |
| Example | "We monitor critical dimensions via SPC" | "Step-by-step X̄–R chart calculation" |
| Approval | Top Management / MR | Process Owner |

*(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for valid example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks by **(a)** listing QAM contents without mapping them to **ISO 9001:2015 clauses**, **(b)** confusing *documented procedure* with *work instruction*, and **(c)** omitting the *SQC linkage* (e.g., X̄–R, Cpk, sampling plans). Always **bullet-map your answer to clauses** to score full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **mandatory contents of a Quality Assurance Manual** as required by **ISO 9001:2015**. Illustrate with examples relevant to a **manufacturing unit performing Statistical Process Control**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain how the QAM functions as a **bridge between ISO 9001 clauses and SPC tools** (X̄–R, p-chart, Cpk). Describe the role of an **Out-of-Control Action Plan (OCAP)** within this framework. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Mandatory Contents of QAM under ISO 9001:2015 (7 marks)**

1. **Title page & Table of Contents** (0.5 mark) — Includes issue number, revision, date, approval signatures of the Managing Director and Quality Manager.

2. **Scope of the QMS — Clause 4.3** (1 mark)
The manual must clearly state the **boundaries** (product lines, sites) and **applicability** (e.g., CNC machining of aluminium die-cast housings for the automotive industry). For SQC, this defines *which* processes are monitored by control charts.

3. **Normative References — Clause 2** (0.5 mark)
Lists standards used: **ISO 9000:2015** (vocabulary), **ISO 9001:2015** (requirements), **ISO 19011:2018** (auditing), and industry-specific ones like **IATF 16949** or **AS9100D**.

4. **Terms & Definitions — Clause 3** (0.5 mark)
A glossary of in-house terms (e.g., "CTQ = Critical-to-Quality parameter") so that SPC vocabulary is consistent.

5. **Context of the Organization — Clause 4** (0.5 mark)
SWOT analysis, interested-party matrix, identification of internal/external issues.

6. **Leadership — Clause 5** (0.5 mark)
Quality Policy (e.g., *"Achieve Cpk $\geq 1.33$ on all CTQs by Q4 2025"*), roles, responsibilities (RACI matrix), Management Representative (MR).

7. **Planning — Clause 6** (0.5 mark)
SMART quality objectives, risk register, opportunities log.

8. **Support — Clause 7** (0.5 mark)
Resource plan, training matrix (e.g., Green Belt / Black Belt certification), communication plan, control of documented information.

9. **Operation — Clause 8** (1 mark)
This is the **SQC heart** of the manual:
   - Determination of CTQs.
   - **Variable Control Charts** ($\bar{X}-R$, $X-MR$, $\bar{X}-s$).
   - **Attribute Control Charts** ($p$, $np$, $c$, $u$).
   - **Acceptance Sampling** plans (AQL, LTPD, AOQL).
   - Control of externally provided products (incoming inspection).

10. **Performance Evaluation — Clause 9** (0.5 mark)
Internal audit programme (frequency = 2× per year per process), management review agenda template.

11. **Improvement — Clause 10** (0.5 mark)
Handling of non-conformities, CAR / 8D methodology, continual improvement projects (Kaizen, DMAIC).

> The QAM concludes with **Annexure list** (forms, OCAPs, process maps) and **Revision History Log**.

---

**(b) QAM as a Bridge & OCAP — Block Diagram + Explanation (7 marks)**

**Block diagram (textual representation, full marks if drawn in the exam):**

$$
\boxed{\text{ISO 9001 Clauses}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{QAM Policy}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Process Map}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{CTQ Identification}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{SPC Tools}}
$$

**Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation)** mandates *measurement* of the QMS. The QAM translates this into a *Process Map* identifying every CTQ (e.g., shaft diameter, surface roughness, hardness).
2. For each CTQ, the manual prescribes a **control chart type**:
   - Continuous data with subgroup size $n \le 10 \Rightarrow$ $\bar{X}-R$ chart.
   - Continuous data with subgroup size $n > 10$ or variable sample size $\Rightarrow$ $\bar{X}-s$ chart.
   - Defectives proportion $\Rightarrow$ $p$-chart.
3. **Capability indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) are calculated as evidence of meeting Clause 9.1.1.
4. **Out-of-Control Action Plan (OCAP)** is invoked whenever a point violates the Western Electric / Nelson rules (e.g., one point beyond 3σ, two of three beyond 2σ on the same side, run of 7).
5. The OCAP, attached as an **Annexure to the QAM**, prescribes:
   - **Step 1:** Operator tags the chart and stops the line.
   - **Step 2:** Engineer validates the signal (special vs common cause).
   - **Step 3:** If special cause — perform root cause analysis (5-Why / Fishbone).
   - **Step 4:** Apply corrective action; record on the **CAR Register** (Clause 10.2).
   - **Step 5:** Close the loop in the next management review.

This end-to-end flow proves **traceability** during an external ISO audit, earning full marks for Clause 9 and 10 compliance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **four-tier hierarchy of QMS documentation**. For each tier, give **one SQC-specific example**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC lathe produces shafts with **USL = 25.025 mm** and **LSL = 24.975 mm**. From 25 subgroups of size 5, the following statistics were obtained: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.998$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm.
Compute the **process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** and comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Four-Tier Documentation Hierarchy (7 marks)**

| Tier | Name | Description | SQC Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | **Quality Manual (QAM)** | Top-level policy document; states *what* and *why* | "We shall maintain Cpk $\ge 1.33$ on all CTQs." |
| II | **Documented Procedures** | Mandatory QMS procedures; states *how* at a process level | "Procedure for Control of Nonconforming Product" |
| III | **Work Instructions / SOPs** | Step-by-step task-level instructions | "SOP for $\bar{X}-R$ chart plotting on the shop floor" |
| IV | **Records / Data Forms** | Objective evidence of execution | Daily $\bar{X}-R$ chart log, calibration register, capability report |

**Explanation (3 marks for the 1.5 marks-per-tier narration):**
- **Level I** is approved by top management and externally visible to customers and auditors.
- **Level II** procedures are mandatory wherever ISO 9001:2015 mandates "documented information." For SQC, this includes the procedure for **measurement system analysis (MSA)** and for **statistical process control**.
- **Level III** is the most numerous — each machine, gauge, and process should have its own SOP.
- **Level IV** records must be **retained** per the QAM's retention policy (e.g., SPC charts retained for 3 years).

---

**(b) Capability Index Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
- USL = 25.025 mm
- LSL = 24.975 mm
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.998$ mm (process mean)
- $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm (average range)
- Subgroup size $n = 5$

**Step 2 — Estimate the process standard deviation $\sigma$ using $\bar{R}$.**
For $n = 5$, the **d2 constant** from the standard table is $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.012}{2.326} = 5.159 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\sigma \approx 0.00516\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total tolerance spread.**

$$
\text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 25.025 - 24.975 = 0.050\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_p$.**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{0.050}{6 \times 0.00516} = \dfrac{0.050}{0.03096} = 1.615
$$

$$
\boxed{C_p \approx 1.62}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ using both limits.**

Upper term:

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.025 - 24.998}{3 \times 0.00516} = \dfrac{0.027}{0.01548} = 1.744
$$

Lower term:

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{\bar{X}} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{24.998 - 24.975}{3 \times 0.00516} = \dfrac{0.023}{0.01548} = 1.486
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.744,\; 1.486) = 1.486
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} \approx 1.49}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (2 marks).**
- $C_p = 1.62 > 1.33$ → Process **spread is acceptable**.
- $C_{pk} = 1.49 > 1.33$ → Process is **capable** and **centered reasonably well**, but the *lower* $C_{pk}$ (1.486) is the binding one — the mean is **slightly shifted toward the LSL** (24.998 is 0.023 above LSL but only 0.027 below USL).
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process by adjusting the spindle offset by $\approx 0.002$ mm toward the midpoint (25.000 mm) to raise $C_{pk}$ further. With perfect centering, $C_{pk}$ would equal $C_p = 1.62$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Quality Assurance Manual**

- **QAM** is the **top-level (Level I)** document of a QMS, mapping directly to **ISO 9001:2015 clauses 4–10**.
- The four-tier hierarchy is **Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records**. Memorize one SQC example for each tier.
- The QAM must contain (at minimum): **Scope, Normative References, Definitions, Context, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement**, and **Annexures**.
- **Operation (Clause 8)** is the SQC core — it prescribes **control charts, sampling plans, and capability studies**.
- $C_{pk} = \min\!\big(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\big)$ — a **mandatory formula** in Module 1 / 2.
- $C_p \ge 1.33$ = capable, $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ = world-class — memorize thresholds.
- An **Out-of-Control Action Plan (OCAP)** converts a control-chart signal into a *documented corrective action* (Clause 10.2).
- The QAM must be **revision-controlled** (Major.Minor numbering), **approved by Top Management**, and **communicated to all employees** (Clause 7.4).
- **Six historically mandatory procedures:** Document Control, Records Control, Internal Audit, Control of NC Product, Corrective Action, Preventive Action.
- In a KTU 14-mark answer, always **cite the ISO clause** for every QAM element you mention — examiners reward clause-mapping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Complaints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Field Complaints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Complaint (KTU Definition):** A *Field Complaint* is any report of product non-conformance, failure, or customer dissatisfaction received from the marketplace **after the product has been shipped or commissioned**, as opposed to in-house rejections at the inspection stage. In Statistical Quality Control (SQC), field complaints constitute the **external failure cost** component of the Cost of Quality (COQ) model and are treated as the *ultimate lagging indicator* of process and design capability.

The systematic recording, classification, and statistical analysis of field complaints is called **Field Failure Analysis (FFA)** or **Field Performance Analysis (FPA)**. It links the consumer's real-world experience with the manufacturer's quality management system (QMS) and is a mandatory input for **Reliability Engineering**, **Warranty Forecasting**, and **Closed-Loop Corrective Action (8D / CAPA)** processes required under ISO 9001:2015 and IATF 16949:2016.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Leaky Tap at the End of the Pipeline":**
> Imagine water flowing through a long pipeline. You can install pressure gauges, strainers, and water meters at every joint (these are your in-process inspections and control charts). But the *true* test of water quality is what comes out of the **tap in the customer's home**. The complaints about dirty water, low pressure, or bad smell at the tap are your **field complaints**. They tell you something is wrong somewhere upstream, and they are often the *first* signal of a problem that all your in-house inspections failed to catch. In the same way, a product that passes every test on the shop floor can still generate field complaints due to latent design defects, environmental stress, improper usage, or maintenance issues.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Field Complaint Engineering

The following **bold** metrics form the standard vocabulary of any KTU board answer on this topic:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The expected operating time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** The expected operating time to the first failure of a non-repairable component.
- **Failure Rate (λ):** The number of failures per unit time, typically expressed in **Failures Per Unit (FPU)** or **FIT (Failures In Time, per 10⁹ device-hours)**.
- **Reliability R(t):** The probability that a product performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$.
- **Field Failure Rate (FFR):** Defined as
  $$\text{FFR} = \frac{\text{Number of Field Complaints in Period}}{\text{Number of Units in Field in Period}}$$
  usually expressed in **ppm (parts per million)** or **percentage**.

### 1.4 Why Field Complaints are Studied in SQC

| Reason | SQC Significance |
|---|---|
| Customer retention | Direct link to brand reputation and after-sales cost |
| Warranty cost forecasting | Input to Poisson / Weibull warranty models |
| Design feedback | Closes the Deming cycle (PDCA) at the customer end |
| Reliability estimation | Empirical basis for hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ and MTBF |
| Regulatory compliance | Mandatory under ISO 9001 §8.2.1 and IATF §10.2 |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of a Field Complaint

1. **Capture Phase** — Complaint receipt via dealer network, CRM portal, telephonic helpdesk, or warranty card.
2. **Classification Phase** — Each complaint is coded by:
   - *Failure Mode* (e.g., electrical, mechanical, software, cosmetic)
   - *Severity* (Critical / Major / Minor)
   - *Population Stratum* (model, batch, geography, climate zone)
3. **Statistical Analysis Phase** — Application of reliability distributions, **Pareto analysis (80/20 rule)**, and control-charting of monthly complaint rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Pareto chart of field complaints is the single most frequently asked diagram in board examinations on this topic. Always remember the descending bar order with a cumulative percentage curve overlay.

### 2.2 The Bathtub Curve — Foundation of Field Failure Theory

The **Bathtub Curve** is the canonical model used to interpret field complaints over product life. It is a plot of the **instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$** versus time $t$ and has three regions:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** High early failure rate due to manufacturing defects, sub-standard components, or workmanship errors. Field complaints are *decreasing* with time. Restrained by **Burn-in testing** and **Acceptance Sampling (MIL-STD-105E / ISO 2859-1)**.
- **Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Failure rate is approximately **constant** ($\lambda(t) = \lambda$). Field complaints are **Poisson-distributed**. Governed by the exponential reliability model.
- **Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate *increases* with time due to fatigue, corrosion, and aging. Modeled by the **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta > 1$.

### 2.3 The Reliability Function R(t) and its Derivations

The reliability function is the probability of *survival* up to time $t$:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

It is related to the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) $F(t)$ by:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

For a *constant failure rate* (Region II), the underlying distribution is the **Exponential distribution** with parameter $\lambda > 0$:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for the exponential model:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### 2.4 The Weibull Distribution — The Workhorse of Field Failure Data

Most field failure data in Region I and Region III are well modeled by the **two-parameter Weibull distribution**:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

$$F(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

where:
- $\beta$ (shape parameter, dimensionless) — determines failure mode:
  - $\beta < 1$: Infant mortality
  - $\beta = 1$: Random (exponential)
  - $\beta > 1$: Wear-out
- $\eta$ (scale parameter, units of time) — characteristic life (63.2% of population has failed by $t = \eta$).

### 2.5 The Taguchi Loss Function in Field Context

Taguchi reframes field complaints as a continuous loss to society:

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$$

where $y$ is the actual quality characteristic value, $m$ is the target, and $k$ is the loss coefficient. The expected total loss per unit in the field becomes:

$$E[L] = k \sigma^{2} + k(\mu - m)^{2}$$

This connects **field complaint rate** directly to **process variance** — a critical KTU concept.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations required for Module 1 — Field Complaints** are consolidated below. Symbols used: $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t$ = mission time, $\eta$ = Weibull scale, $\beta$ = Weibull shape.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Domain / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $0 \le R \le 1$, dimensionless | Useful-life reliability |
| 2 | Unreliability $F(t)$ | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $0 \le F \le 1$ | Probability of failure by $t$ |
| 3 | MTTF (exponential) | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Hours / cycles | Average life expectancy |
| 4 | MTBF (exponential) | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Hours / cycles | Repairable systems |
| 5 | Hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ | $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | Failures / unit time | Instantaneous failure intensity |
| 6 | Field Failure Rate | $\text{FFR} = N_{f} / N_{u}$ | ppm or % | Monthly complaint KPI |
| 7 | Weibull CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $0 \le F \le 1$ | Wear-out / infant mortality fit |
| 8 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $0 \le R \le 1$ | Spare-parts forecasting |
| 9 | Weibull MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Hours | Average life under Weibull |
| 10 | Taguchi Loss | $L = k(y-m)^{2}$ | Money / unit | Quality cost in field |
| 11 | Failure rate in ppm | $\text{ppm} = (\text{failures} / \text{units}) \times 10^{6}$ | ppm | Six-Sigma metric (DPMO) |
| 12 | Conditional Reliability | $R(t_{1}, t_{2}) = e^{-\lambda(t_{2} - t_{1})}$ | Dimensionless | Reliability for next interval |
| 13 | Mean Down Time | $\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT}$ | Hours | Availability computation |
| 14 | Availability $A$ | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT})$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ | Service-system uptime |
| 15 | Six-Sigma Level | $\sigma_{L} = (\text{USL} - \mu) / \sigma$ | $\sigma$ units | Capability benchmarking |

> **Important Notation Rule:** Throughout these notes, $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is written as $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid corrupting markdown table syntax.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** use field complaint data (warranty claims + dealer reports) to compute FFR in ppm and trigger **8D Corrective Action** when FFR exceeds threshold.
- **Electronics manufacturers** model integrated-circuit failures using Weibull ($\beta \approx 0.5$ in burn-in, $\beta \approx 2$–$3$ in wear-out) to plan **Burn-in duration** and **warranty reserves**.
- **Aerospace / Defence** organisations fit field failure data to exponential and Weibull to compute **MTBCF (Mean Time Between Critical Failures)** — a flight-safety parameter.
- **Medical Device industry (ISO 13485)** mandates field complaint trending as part of **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Reliability Function from First Principles

**Statement:** Show that for a non-repairable component with constant hazard rate $\lambda$, the reliability is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 1 — Define reliability.**
Reliability is the probability that the time-to-failure $T$ exceeds the mission time $t$:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define hazard rate.**
The conditional probability of failure in an infinitesimally small interval $[t, t+dt]$, given survival up to $t$, is:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply.**

$$f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$** (since $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ and $f(t) = F'(t)$):

$$-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables.**

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t) \, dt$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$**, noting $R(0) = 1$ (zero time → 100% reliability):

$$\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate.**

$$\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 8 — Specialise to constant $\lambda$ (Region II of the bathtub curve).**

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Interpretation:** Higher mission time $t$ and higher failure rate $\lambda$ both **exponentially degrade** reliability — a foundational result for warranty analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Exponential Distribution

**Statement:** Derive $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ for an exponential time-to-failure distribution.

**Step 1 — Definition of expected value.**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply integration by parts.** Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \Big[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \Big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term.** As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ faster than $t \to \infty$, so the term vanishes. At $t = 0$, $-0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{0 - (-1/\lambda)}{1} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

**Interpretation:** If 1% of units fail in 1000 hours, $\lambda = 0.0001$ per hour, and the mean life is **10 000 hours**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Weibull Hazard Rate Function

**Statement:** Derive the Weibull hazard (failure-rate) function and identify the bathtub-curve regions.

**Step 1 — Hazard rate formula.**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Weibull pdf and reliability.**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the exponential terms.**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite compactly.**

$$\boxed{\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}} \cdot t^{\beta - 1}}$$

**Step 5 — Region identification.**
- If $\beta < 1$, the exponent $(\beta - 1) < 0$ → $\lambda(t)$ **decreases** with $t$ → *Infant Mortality* (Region I).
- If $\beta = 1$, the exponent is $0$ → $\lambda(t) = \beta / \eta = \text{constant}$ → *Useful Life* (Region II).
- If $\beta > 1$, the exponent $(\beta - 1) > 0$ → $\lambda(t)$ **increases** with $t$ → *Wear-out* (Region III).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 7 marks)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — June 2024 pattern]:**
> A manufacturer of LED bulbs observes field complaints for **1500 bulbs** during the warranty period of **6 months**. The failure rate is constant at $\lambda = 0.0002$ per bulb-hour. Calculate: (a) the reliability of a single bulb at $t = 2000$ hours, (b) the MTTF in hours, and (c) the expected number of bulbs that will fail by 2000 hours.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Reliability at 2000 hours.**

$$R(2000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0002 \times 2000} = e^{-0.4}$$

$$R(2000) \approx 0.6703 = 67.03\%$$

**Part (b) — MTTF.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (c) — Expected number of failures in the field.**

Probability of failure by 2000 h:

$$F(2000) = 1 - R(2000) = 1 - 0.6703 = 0.3297$$

Expected failures from 1500 bulbs:

$$N_{f} = 1500 \times 0.3297 \approx 495 \text{ bulbs}$$

$$\boxed{R(2000) = 67.03\%, \quad \text{MTTF} = 5000 \text{ h}, \quad N_{f} \approx 495 \text{ bulbs}}$$

**Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to **multiply by the population** in part (c) and write only the probability. Marks are awarded for the **expected count**, not just the probability.

---

| Field-Complaint Phenomenon | Statistical Tool | Output | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increasing monthly complaint rate | **np-chart / u-chart** | Out-of-control signal | Module 1 — Attributes Control Chart |
| Dominant failure mode (80%) | **Pareto chart** | Top 3 failure modes | Module 1 — 7 QC Tools |
| Failure vs. mission time | **Weibull probability plot** | $\beta, \eta$ estimates | Module 1 — Reliability |
| Customer satisfaction (5-pt Likert) | **Histogram + Run chart** | Trend and dispersion | Module 1 — Descriptive stats |
| Cost of warranty | **Taguchi Loss Function** | $E[L]$ in ₹ | Module 1 — Quality costs |
| Mean life of returned units | **Exponential / Weibull MLE** | $\hat{\lambda}, \hat{\beta}$ | Module 1 — Reliability |
| 6-Sigma capability of supplier | **DPMO, $\sigma_{L}$** | $\sigma$ level | Module 1 — Capability |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Field Complaint Processing Pipeline

The following flowchart shows the standard ISO 9001:2015 §10.2 process flow that an organisation must execute for every field complaint, with the SQC tools tagged at each step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Customer Discovers Defect] --> A2[Complaint Lodged via Dealer or CRM Portal]
    A2 --> A3[Unique Complaint ID Generated]
    A3 --> A4{Is the Complaint Valid?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Close and Inform Customer]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Failure Mode and Effects Coding]
    A6 --> A7[Pareto Chart Monthly Update]
    A7 --> A8[Weibull Fit to Time to Failure Data]
    A8 --> A9[Reliability R of t and MTTF Computed]
    A9 --> A10{FFR Exceeds Threshold}
    A10 -- No --> A11[Monthly Quality Review]
    A10 -- Yes --> A12[Trigger 8D CAPA Corrective Action]
    A12 --> A13[Design or Process Change]
    A13 --> A14[Updated FFR Monitoring on u chart]
    A14 --> A11
    A11 --> A15[Closure Loop Back to Customer]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each rectangular block represents a process step; the diamond blocks are decision gates.
- The **statistical tools** appear at the *analysis* stages (A7 = Pareto, A8 = Weibull, A14 = control chart).
- The diagram is read **top-to-bottom** and the **CAPA loop** (A12 → A15) ensures the Deming PDCA cycle is closed.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Bathtub Curve and Its Statistical Models

This diagram maps the three regions of the bathtub curve to the appropriate probability distribution and the corresponding SQC strategy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Region I: Infant Mortality] -- beta less than 1 --> B2[Weibull with beta less than 1]
    B2 --> B3[Countermeasure: Burn In Testing and Tightened Incoming Inspection]
    B3 --> B4[Acceptance Sampling ISO 2859]
    B4 --> B5[Lower AQL for Critical Components]
    B1 -- beta equals 1 --> B6[Region II: Useful Life]
    B6 -- lambda constant --> B7[Exponential Distribution]
    B7 --> B8[Field Complaint Rate Follows Poisson]
    B8 --> B9[Monitor using u chart or np chart]
    B9 --> B10[Trigger Action when FFR above UCL]
    B6 -- beta greater than 1 --> B11[Region III: Wear Out]
    B11 --> B12[Weibull with beta greater than 1]
    B12 --> B13[Countermeasure: Preventive Replacement Schedule]
    B13 --> B14[Spare Parts Inventory Optimised via MTTF]
```

---

The following is a text-rendered **block-level Pareto architecture** (a Mermaid-renderable fallback that mirrors the shape of a typical 80/20 Pareto chart) for a synthetic automotive dataset of 1000 field complaints:

flowchart TD

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Quality Cost Hierarchy in Field Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Cost of Quality COQ] --> D2[Prevention Costs]
    D1 --> D3[Appraisal Costs]
    D1 --> D4[Internal Failure Costs]
    D1 --> D5[External Failure Costs]
    D5 --> D6[Field Complaints]
    D5 --> D7[Warranty Claims]
    D5 --> D8[Product Recall Expenses]

    D6 --> D10[Statistical Analysis: FFR ppm]

```

**Interpretation:** Field complaints sit at the **bottom of the COQ hierarchy** but generate the **highest cost per incident**. Prevention (D2) and appraisal (D3) investments *upstream* reduce the field complaint cost *downstream* — a core Kaizen insight.
