## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Define the term *Field Complaint*. How is it different from an *in-house rejection*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **field complaint** is a report of product non-conformance or failure received from the customer **after the product has been delivered to the market**, through channels such as dealers, service centres, warranty cards, or CRM portals. (1 mark)
In contrast, an **in-house rejection** is a defect detected *within the factory* during production, in-process inspection, or final acceptance testing — *before* shipment. (1 mark)
The key differences are summarised in the table:

| Parameter | In-House Rejection | Field Complaint |
|---|---|---|
| Detection point | Within factory | At customer end |
| Cost impact | Internal failure cost | External failure cost |
| Visibility | Low | High (brand impact) |
| Stat. tool | p-chart, np-chart | FFR, Weibull, Pareto |

(1 mark for the comparative table)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 pattern]**
What is the **bathtub curve**? Name the three regions and the appropriate probability distribution for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **bathtub curve** is a plot of the **instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$** versus operating time $t$, so named because of its characteristic shape resembling a bathtub. (1 mark)

| Region | Failure Behaviour | Time Period | Distribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| I. Infant Mortality | Decreasing $\lambda(t)$ | Early life | Weibull, $\beta < 1$ |
| II. Useful Life | Constant $\lambda(t)$ | Working life | Exponential, $\beta = 1$ |
| III. Wear-out | Increasing $\lambda(t)$ | End of life | Weibull, $\beta > 1$ |

(1 mark for naming; 1 mark for distribution mapping)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always** state the *assumptions* (constant failure rate, independence, etc.) before plugging into any reliability formula. Skipping assumptions = 1 mark deduction.
> 2. **Always** include the **units** in the final answer ($\sigma = 0.5$ mm, not just 0.5). Marks are reserved explicitly for units.
> 3. **Do not** confuse *MTBF* (repairable) with *MTTF* (non-repairable). Examiners deliberately test this.
> 4. **Numerical substitution** without showing the formula receives **zero** marks in part-(a) of every 7-mark question. Always write the formula first.

---

#### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Module 1 typical pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **field complaints** in Statistical Quality Control. Discuss in detail the **Pareto analysis** of field complaints with a suitable example diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** A washing machine manufacturer records **280 field complaints** in a quarter against a **population of 50 000 units** in the field. The average time-to-failure of the returned units is **1800 hours** with a constant failure rate.
(i) Compute the **Field Failure Rate** in ppm and the **MTTF**.
(ii) If the failure rate remains constant, what is the **probability that a unit will survive 3000 hours** without failure? (7 marks)

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *field complaint* is a customer-originated report of product failure or dissatisfaction received after the product has been delivered to the marketplace. It represents **external failure cost** in the Cost of Quality (COQ) framework and is the *ultimate lagging indicator* of process and design quality.

**Pareto Analysis (5 marks):**
The **Pareto principle** (Vilfredo Pareto, 1906) states that roughly **80% of the problems stem from 20% of the causes**. In field complaint analysis, a Pareto chart is constructed by:

1. Collecting complaint data over a defined period (typically 1–3 months).
2. Classifying complaints into categories (e.g., *electrical, mechanical, software, cosmetic, packaging*).
3. Counting the frequency in each category.
4. Sorting categories in **descending order of frequency**.
5. Plotting a bar chart for the frequencies and overlaying a **cumulative percentage curve**.
6. Identifying the *vital few* categories that account for ~80% of complaints.

**Example diagram (described in words for answer sheet):**
For an automobile company recording 1000 field complaints in a quarter:
- Electrical failure: 350 (cumulative 35%)
- Brake noise: 250 (cumulative 60%)
- Paint defect: 150 (cumulative 75%)
- AC compressor: 100 (cumulative 85%)
- All others: 150 (cumulative 100%)

The first **two categories alone account for 60%** of all field complaints — the *vital few* that demand immediate CAPA action. (1 mark for example)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Field Failure Rate in ppm and MTTF (4 marks):**

$$\text{FFR} = \frac{N_{f}}{N_{u}} \times 10^{6} = \frac{280}{50{,}000} \times 10^{6}$$

$$\text{FFR} = 5600 \text{ ppm}$$

Given constant failure rate and MTTF = 1800 h:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{1800} \approx 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

(1 mark for FFR, 1 mark for MTTF identification, 2 marks for numerical substitution)

**(ii) Probability of survival up to 3000 hours (3 marks):**

$$R(3000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(1/1800) \times 3000} = e^{-1.6667}$$

$$R(3000) \approx 0.1889 = 18.89\%$$

(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final value with unit)

---

#### QUESTION B — (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 1 typical pattern]`**

**(a)** Derive the **reliability function** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for a system with constant failure rate $\lambda$. State clearly all assumptions and intermediate steps. (7 marks)

**(b)** A manufacturer of automotive brake pads observes that failures in the field follow a **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 25\,000$ km.
(i) Compute the **reliability at 10 000 km**.
(ii) Compute the **MTTF in km** using the gamma function relation $\Gamma(1.4) = 0.8874$.
(iii) What fraction of brake pads will fail **before 20 000 km**? (7 marks)

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
1. The system is **non-repairable** until first failure.
2. The failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant in time** (Region II of the bathtub curve).
3. The time-to-failure $T$ follows an **exponential distribution**.
4. Failures are **statistically independent**.

*Derivation (6 marks — see the full derivation in Section 3.1 above):*
Starting from $R(t) = P(T > t)$ and the hazard rate definition $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$, we obtain
$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t)$$
Integrating with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$,
$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$
$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Reliability at 10 000 km (2 marks):**

$$R(10{,}000) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} = e^{-(10{,}000/25{,}000)^{2.5}}$$

$$R(10{,}000) = e^{-(0.4)^{2.5}} = e^{-0.1016} \approx 0.9034 = 90.34\%$$

**(ii) MTTF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 25{,}000 \times \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2.5}\right)$$

$$\text{MTTF} = 25{,}000 \times \Gamma(1.4) = 25{,}000 \times 0.8874$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} \approx 22{,}185 \text{ km}}$$

**(iii) Fraction failing before 20 000 km (3 marks):**

$$F(20{,}000) = 1 - e^{-(20{,}000/25{,}000)^{2.5}} = 1 - e^{-(0.8)^{2.5}}$$

$$F(20{,}000) = 1 - e^{-0.5724} = 1 - 0.5641 = 0.4359$$

$$\boxed{\text{About } 43.59\% \text{ of brake pads will fail before 20 000 km}}$$

(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final value)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Field Complaints**

- [x] **Definition:** Field complaint = customer-originated defect report *after* delivery; a component of *External Failure Cost* in COQ.
- [x] **Key metrics:** FFR (ppm), MTBF, MTTF, $R(t)$, $\lambda(t)$ — all must be quoted with units.
- [x] **Bathtub Curve:** Region I (infant mortality, $\beta < 1$) → Region II (useful life, exponential) → Region III (wear-out, $\beta > 1$).
- [x] **Reliability formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — derive from hazard function; always state *constant $\lambda$* assumption.
- [x] **MTTF for exponential:** $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
- [x] **Weibull reliability:** $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}]$; MTTF = $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- [x] **Pareto analysis** of complaints → identify the *vital few* failure modes for 8D/CAPA action.
- [x] **Taguchi loss** $L = k(y-m)^2$ links field cost to process variance $\sigma^2$.
- [x] **Six-Sigma ppm levels:** $3\sigma \approx 66\,810$ ppm; $4\sigma \approx 6210$ ppm; $5\sigma \approx 233$ ppm; $6\sigma \approx 3.4$ ppm.
- [x] **Common board traps:** confusing MTTF with MTBF, omitting units, forgetting the population multiplier in FFR, skipping assumptions.
- [x] **Engineering link:** Field complaint data closes the Deming PDCA loop and is the *only* true measure of customer-experienced quality.
- [x] **Mandatory diagrams** for full marks: (1) Pareto chart, (2) Bathtub curve, (3) Reliability vs. time plot, (4) COQ hierarchy block diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Rating of Outgoing Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Rating of Outgoing Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Quality Rating of Outgoing Product** is a quantitative measure used in Statistical Quality Control (SQC) to express the expected level of conformance of a product stream that is released to the customer after being subjected to a **Rectifying Sampling Inspection Plan**. The two principal metrics used for this rating are:

- **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):** The expected proportion of defective items in the outgoing product averaged over all possible lots of a given incoming quality.
- **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):** The maximum value that the AOQ can attain over the entire range of possible incoming qualities (i.e., $0 \leq p \leq 1$). The AOQL represents the *worst-case* quality that will be shipped to the customer under a given rectifying plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Rating" implies a graded assessment. The outgoing product stream is continuously classified by its AOQ, and the **AOQL** is the governing design constraint that protects the consumer. This is one of the foundational concepts of acceptance sampling and the Dodge–Romig system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bucket with a Sieve

Imagine a factory pipeline pumping water (product) into a bucket. The water contains some impurities (defectives). You place a **sieve** (sampling inspection) on the bucket:

- Sometimes the sieve passes the water (lot **accepted**), but the impurities in the *un-sieved portion* still pass through.
- Sometimes the sieve catches enough impurities, so you **empty and filter the entire bucket** (lot **rejected → 100 % screening**), removing all impurities.

The *Quality Rating of Outgoing Product* asks: **"On average, what fraction of impurities remains in the bucket when the customer uses it?"** That fraction is the AOQ. The bucket's *worst possible* impurity level (when the input is dirty enough to slip through the sieve most often, but not so dirty that the sieve always catches it) is the AOQL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The outgoing quality is *almost always better* than the incoming quality because of the rectification (replacement) of defectives in rejected lots. This is the central premise of the Dodge–Romig rectifying inspection philosophy.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

The following constants / metrics govern the design:

- **Lot size** $\rightarrow N$
- **Sample size** $\rightarrow n$
- **Acceptance number** $\rightarrow c$ (maximum defectives permitted in sample for acceptance)
- **Probability of acceptance** $\rightarrow P_a$
- **Incoming process fraction defective** $\rightarrow p$
- **Average Outgoing Quality** $\rightarrow \mathbf{AOQ}$
- **Average Outgoing Quality Limit** $\rightarrow \mathbf{AOQL}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Logic of Rectifying Inspection

A rectifying inspection plan operates in two parallel routes:

**Step 1 — Sampling of the lot:**
A random sample of $n$ items is drawn from the lot of size $N$ and inspected $100\,\%$.

**Step 2 — Decision-making:**
- If the sample contains $\leq c$ defectives, the lot is **accepted**. The inspected $n$ items are returned (with defectives replaced by good ones), and the un-inspected $N-n$ items are shipped as-is.
- If the sample contains $> c$ defectives, the lot is **rejected** and subjected to $100\,\%$ screening. Every defective is replaced by a known-good item.

**Step 3 — Quality averaging:**
Because the plan sometimes accepts a lot containing a fraction $p$ of defectives (in the un-inspected portion) and sometimes fully rectifies the lot, the *expected* outgoing defective fraction is the **AOQ**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formula

The mathematical rating arises from a **weighted average of two outcomes**:

$$
\text{Outgoing Defectives} = \underbrace{P_a \cdot p(N-n)}_{\text{from accepted lots}} + \underbrace{(1 - P_a) \cdot 0}_{\text{from 100% screened lots}}
$$

The first term captures the leakage through the sieve (accepted lots with hidden defectives in the un-sampled portion). The second term is zero because fully screened lots are perfectly rectified.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are valid for a **Single Sampling Rectifying Plan**. Symbols carry their standard SQC meaning.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Remarks / When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$ | Standard form used universally in KTU problems |
| AOQ (with non-zero sample defectives) | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$ (defectives in *rejected* lots replaced) | Assumes sample defectives are replaced in both paths |
| Probability of Acceptance (Binomial) | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{N}{i} p^i (1-p)^{N-i}$ | Hypergeometric; used for small lots |
| Probability of Acceptance (Poisson approx.) | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np} (np)^i}{i!}$ | Used when $p \leq 0.10$ and $N \geq 10n$ |
| AOQL Definition | $\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \leq p \leq 1} \big[ \text{AOQ}(p) \big]$ | Maximum over the full range of $p$ |
| AOQL (Poisson approximation) | $\text{AOQL} \approx \dfrac{0.368 \cdot (N - n)}{n \cdot N}$ | Peak occurs at $p^{*} = 1/n$ |
| AOQL (Practical Dodge–Romig) | $\text{AOQL} \approx \dfrac{1.2 \, p_c \, (N - n)}{N}$ | $p_c$ is the break-even quality ($P_a = 0.5$) |
| Producer's Quality | $p_1$ at $P_a = 0.95$ | Determines the "A" level in Dodge–Romig tables |
| Consumer's Quality (LTPD) | $p_2$ at $P_a = 0.10$ | Determines the "B" level in Dodge–Romig tables |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Industries

- **Automotive component manufacturing:** Ensures that the bolt/rivet stream leaving the shop-floor has a defect rate below AOQL limits (commonly $0.1\,\%$ to $0.5\,\%$).
- **Electronics / PCB assembly:** AOQ-based schemes protect customers from receiving sub-standard boards while keeping inspection cost low.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging:** Regulatory bodies (FDA, ISO 2859) accept the AOQL as a *binding* quality contract metric for batch release.
- **Defence / aerospace:** Used in long-term supplier rating, where AOQ trending indicates whether a vendor's process is degrading over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the AOQ Formula

Consider a single sampling plan $(N, n, c)$ operating with **rectifying inspection**.

**Step 1 — Identify the two mutually exclusive outcomes:**

- **Outcome A (Lot accepted):** Probability = $P_a$. The un-inspected portion has $N - n$ items at the incoming quality $p$. Hence the expected number of defectives shipped is $p(N - n)$.
- **Outcome B (Lot rejected → 100 % screening):** Probability = $1 - P_a$. Every defective is replaced. Hence the number of defectives shipped is $0$.

**Step 2 — Form the expected number of defectives in the outgoing lot:**

$$
E[\text{Defectives Out}] = P_a \cdot p(N - n) + (1 - P_a) \cdot 0 = P_a \, p \, (N - n)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a fraction by dividing by the lot size $N$:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{E[\text{Defectives Out}]}{N} = \frac{P_a \, p \, (N - n)}{N}
$$

This is the *fundamental AOQ identity* used in every KTU derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the AOQL using Poisson Approximation

When the sample size is large and $p$ is small, the binomial distribution converges to the **Poisson distribution** with mean $\lambda = np$.

**Step 1 — Substitute the Poisson $P_a$ into the AOQ formula:**

$$
P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^i}{i!}
$$

For a single sampling plan, the AOQ becomes:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{(N - n)}{N} \cdot p \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^i}{i!}
$$

**Step 2 — To find AOQL, differentiate AOQ with respect to $p$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d(\text{AOQ})}{dp} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification at the maximum:**
For Poisson-distributed acceptance, the function $P_a(p) \cdot p$ is well known to attain its maximum near $p^{*} = 1/n$ (this is the **mode of the AOQ curve**).

**Step 4 — Substitute $p^{*} = 1/n$ into AOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ}_{\max} &= \frac{(N - n)}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot e^{-1} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.368 \, (N - n)}{n \cdot N}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **closed-form AOQL** for a Poisson approximation. It is the *worst possible* outgoing quality under the chosen plan.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given data:** $N = 1000,\ n = 50,\ c = 1,\ p = 0.02$ (rectifying single sampling plan).

**Step 1 — Compute $P_a$ using Binomial:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \binom{50}{0} (0.02)^0 (0.98)^{50} + \binom{50}{1} (0.02)^1 (0.98)^{49} \\[4pt]
&= (0.98)^{50} + 50(0.02)(0.98)^{49} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3642 + 0.3716 = 0.7358
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute AOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ} &= \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.7358 \times 0.02 \times (1000 - 50)}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.7358 \times 0.02 \times 950}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{13.980}{1000} = 0.01398
\end{aligned}
$$

So the outgoing stream contains about **1.4 % defectives** on average for an input of 2 % defectives — the sieve has *improved* the quality.

**Step 3 — Compute the AOQL using the Poisson closed form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQL} &= \frac{0.368 \times (1000 - 50)}{50 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.368 \times 950}{50000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{349.6}{50000} = 0.00699 \;\;(0.699\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** No matter how dirty the incoming material becomes, the outgoing stream will *never* contain more than **0.699 %** defectives under this rectifying plan. This is the consumer's *guaranteed* worst-case rating.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Rectifying Inspection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Incoming Lot of N Items at Fraction Defective p"]
    nodeB["Draw Random Sample of n Items"]
    nodeC["Inspect 100 percent of Sample"]
    nodeD{"Number of Defectives in Sample less than or equal to c?"}
    nodeE["ACCEPT Lot: Return n items, replace defectives"]
    nodeF["Ship N minus n Untested Items plus n Rectified Items"]
    nodeG["REJECT Lot: Trigger 100 percent Screening"]
    nodeH["Inspect all N Items and Replace Every Defective"]
    nodeI["Outgoing Product Stream - Quality AOQ"]
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes P_a --> nodeE
    nodeD -- No 1 minus P_a --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — AOQ as a Function of Incoming Quality $p$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input: Incoming Fraction Defective p"]
    pa["Compute P_a from Binomial or Poisson"]
    aoq["Apply AOQ equals P_a times p times N minus n over N"]
    peak["Identify Max Value of AOQ over p in 0 to 1"]
    aoql["Output: AOQL equals Maximum AOQ"]
    curve["Shape: Rises to Peak then Falls to Zero"]
    
    inp --> pa
    pa --> aoq
    aoq --> peak
    peak --> aoql
    aoq --> curve
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Position of AOQL on the AOQ Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisX["Horizontal Axis: Incoming Fraction Defective p"]
    axisY["Vertical Axis: AOQ"]
    origin["Origin O"]
    start["At p = 0: AOQ = 0"]
    rise["AOQ rises monotonically as p increases"]
    maxPt["PEAK POINT: Coordinates are p star and AOQL"]
    fall["AOQ decreases as p approaches 1"]
    endPt["At p = 1: AOQ returns to 0 due to 100 percent screening"]
    
    axisX --> origin
    axisY --> origin
    origin --> start
    start --> rise
    rise --> maxPt
    maxPt --> fall
    fall --> endPt
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the AOQ return to zero at $p = 1$?** When the incoming lot is $100\,\%$ defective, the sampling plan will almost always reject ($P_a \to 0$), forcing $100\,\%$ inspection, which rectifies all defectives. Hence, the outgoing stream becomes perfectly clean at extreme inputs — but it is **expensive** because every lot is fully inspected.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the term "Average Outgoing Quality" (AOQ) as used in acceptance sampling. State the formula used to compute it.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Average Outgoing Quality is the expected proportion of defective items in the product stream that is shipped to the customer, averaged over all possible outcomes of a rectifying sampling plan.
> Formula: $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \, p \, (N - n)}{N}$, where $P_a$ is the probability of acceptance, $p$ is the incoming fraction defective, $N$ is the lot size, and $n$ is the sample size. (1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for symbols)

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is meant by the Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)? How is it different from AOQ?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> AOQL is the **maximum** value of the Average Outgoing Quality over the entire range of incoming qualities $0 \leq p \leq 1$. It represents the worst possible quality of the outgoing product under a given rectifying inspection plan. The key difference is that AOQ is a *function* of $p$ (it varies with the input), whereas AOQL is a *single number* — the peak of that function. (1 mark AOQL definition + 1 mark AOQ definition + 1 mark distinguishing the two)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A rectifying single sampling inspection plan has lot size $N = 2000$, sample size $n = 80$, and acceptance number $c = 2$. The incoming process fraction defective is $p = 0.03$.**

**(a)** Compute the probability of acceptance $P_a$ and the Average Outgoing Quality. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the AOQL of the plan using the Poisson approximation and interpret the result. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Compute $P_a$ and AOQ:**

Since $p = 0.03$ is small and $N \geq 10n$, we can use the Poisson approximation with $\lambda = np = 80 \times 0.03 = 2.4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-2.4} (2.4)^i}{i!} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-2.4}\left[1 + 2.4 + \frac{(2.4)^2}{2}\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.0907 \times [1 + 2.4 + 2.88] \\[4pt]
&= 0.0907 \times 6.28 = 0.5696
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute AOQ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ} &= \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.5696 \times 0.03 \times 1920}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{32.81}{2000} = 0.01640
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $P_a = 0.5696$, $\text{AOQ} = 0.0164$ (i.e., $1.64\,\%$). (3 marks for $P_a$ + 3 marks for AOQ + 1 mark for units)

**Part (b) — Compute AOQL using Poisson approximation:**

The AOQL occurs at $p^{*} = 1/n = 1/80 = 0.0125$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQL} &= \frac{0.368 \times (N - n)}{n \cdot N} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.368 \times 1920}{80 \times 2000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{706.56}{160000} = 0.004416
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** No matter what the incoming fraction defective is, the outgoing product stream will contain on average **no more than 0.44 % defectives** under this plan. This is the *guaranteed* worst-case quality that the customer will receive. (5 marks for AOQL computation + 2 marks for interpretation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**For a rectifying single sampling plan with $N = 5000$, $n = 100$, $c = 1$, and incoming quality $p = 0.04$:**

**(a)** Find the values of $P_a$, AOQ, and ATI (Average Total Inspection). (7 marks)

**(b)** If the firm wishes to limit the AOQL to $0.15\,\%$, determine whether this plan is acceptable. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Compute $P_a$, AOQ, and ATI:**

Mean $\lambda = np = 100 \times 0.04 = 4.0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= e^{-4.0}\left[1 + 4.0\right] = 0.0183 \times 5.0 = 0.0916
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ} &= \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \frac{0.0916 \times 0.04 \times 4900}{5000} = \frac{17.95}{5000} = 0.00359
\end{aligned}
$$

Average Total Inspection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI} &= n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) = 100 + 0.9084 \times 4900 = 100 + 4451.16 = 4551.16
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks $P_a$ + 2 marks AOQ + 3 marks ATI)

**Part (b) — Check whether the AOQL of this plan meets $0.15\,\%$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQL} &= \frac{0.368 \times 4900}{100 \times 5000} = \frac{1803.2}{500000} = 0.003606 = 0.361\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the computed AOQL ($0.361\,\%$) is **greater than** the firm's requirement ($0.15\,\%$), the plan is **not acceptable**. A more stringent plan (larger $n$ or smaller $c$) is required. (3 marks AOQL + 2 marks comparison + 2 marks conclusion)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(N-n)/N$ factor:** Many students write $\text{AOQ} = P_a \cdot p$. This is **wrong** by a factor of up to $N/(N-n)$. Always include the leakage term.
> 2. **Confusing AOQ with AOQL:** AOQ is a function of $p$; AOQL is a constant (the maximum). Marks are deducted when students report AOQ as a fixed number.
> 3. **Using Binomial when Poisson is valid:** For $p < 0.10$ and $N \geq 10n$, the Poisson approximation is the standard KTU expectation. Mixing the two leads to inconsistency marks.
> 4. **Misreporting units:** Always report AOQ/AOQL as a *fraction* or a *percentage*; never leave it as a raw decimal without label.
> 5. **Ignoring rectification:** The single-sampling AOQ formula assumes *all defectives in the sample are replaced* on acceptance. Forgetting this assumption in a theory question costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AOQ** measures the *expected* outgoing fraction defective after rectifying inspection.
- The master formula is $\text{AOQ} = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)/N$ — commit it to memory.
- **AOQL** is the *peak* of the AOQ vs $p$ curve, located approximately at $p^{*} = 1/n$ under the Poisson model.
- Closed-form AOQL: $\text{AOQL} \approx 0.368 \cdot (N-n) / (nN)$ for Poisson assumption.
- At $p = 0$ and $p = 1$, the AOQ is **zero** (clean input or full rectification).
- The relationship $\text{AOQ} < p$ holds for all practical rectifying plans — rectification always *improves* quality.
- The Dodge–Romig system uses AOQ / AOQI tables indexed by lot size, AOQL, and LTPD.
- **ATI** (Average Total Inspection) is the companion cost metric: $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$.
- Always state whether the sample uses Binomial, Hypergeometric, or Poisson approximation before substituting into $P_a$.
- AOQL is a **consumer-protection metric**; LTPD and $P_a = 0.10$ are its counterparts in the OC curve.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Survey or Quality Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Survey or Quality Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quality Survey — Formal Definition

A **Quality Survey** is a systematic, structured process of collecting, analyzing, and interpreting data about an organization's products, processes, services, or customer satisfaction levels. It is primarily a **data-gathering and assessment tool** used to benchmark the current state of quality, identify gaps, detect customer perceptions, and provide actionable feedback for continuous improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A Quality Survey is generally a *passive* and *informational* exercise. It answers the question: *"What is the current quality status?"*

In the framework of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, a quality survey is the precursor to formal quality inspection. It uses statistical tools such as:

- **Questionnaires** (Likert-scale based)
- **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 rule)
- **Histograms and frequency distributions**
- **Customer satisfaction indices (CSI)**
- **Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation** of quality attributes

## 1.2 Quality Audit — Formal Definition

A **Quality Audit** is a *systematic, independent, and documented examination* of a quality system, process, product, or service. It is performed to determine whether the activities and results comply with the **planned arrangements, documented procedures, and regulatory/standard requirements** (such as **ISO 9001:2015**), and whether these arrangements are implemented effectively to achieve quality objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (ISO 19011:2018):** A Quality Audit is *"a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining audit evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria are fulfilled."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you own a small restaurant in Kerala.

- **Quality Survey** = You hand out small feedback cards to every customer asking *"How was the food? Rate 1–5."* You then read the cards and notice that 70% of customers complain about "slow service." This is a **survey** — you are simply *listening* and *collecting* information.

- **Quality Audit** = A senior food inspector from the FSSAI walks into your kitchen with a checklist. He checks:
  1. Is the refrigerator temperature below 5°C? (Conformance check)
  2. Are the cooks wearing gloves? (Standard compliance)
  3. Is there a documented cleaning schedule? (Process verification)

  He doesn't just *ask* — he *inspects, verifies, and certifies*. If the kitchen fails, you don't get the license.

**The single biggest difference:** A survey *asks*; an audit *verifies*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **S**urvey = **S**tats (just numbers and feedback). **A**udit = **A**ccountability (verifiable, document-based, certification-oriented).

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | KTU Emphasis |
|---|---|---|
| **Conformance** | Meeting specified requirements | Audit goal |
| **Non-Conformance (NC)** | Failure to meet a requirement | Audit finding |
| **Corrective Action (CA)** | Action to eliminate the cause of NC | Audit follow-up |
| **Preventive Action (PA)** | Action to prevent potential NC | Audit improvement |
| **Audit Criteria** | Reference standards used for comparison | ISO 9001, IS standards |
| **Audit Evidence** | Records, statements, observations supporting findings | Objective proof |
| **Auditee** | The organization/person being audited | Process owner |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of a Quality Survey

A Quality Survey is conducted to:

1. **Measure customer satisfaction** and loyalty.
2. **Identify gaps** between expected and perceived quality.
3. **Benchmark** against industry standards or competitors.
4. **Provide statistical input** for management decision-making.
5. **Establish a baseline** before introducing new quality initiatives.
6. **Track quality trends** over time (e.g., monthly, quarterly, annually).

## 2.2 Objectives of a Quality Audit

A Quality Audit is performed to:

1. **Verify compliance** with standards (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, AS9100, IATF 16949, etc.).
2. **Evaluate the effectiveness** of the Quality Management System (QMS).
3. **Identify non-conformities** and opportunities for improvement.
4. **Provide assurance** to stakeholders (customers, regulators, investors).
5. **Facilitate corrective and preventive action (CAPA)**.
6. **Prepare for external certification** audits by third-party bodies.

## 2.3 Types of Quality Audits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification:** Be thorough with these five audit types — they appear in almost every question paper.

### A. Based on Who Performs the Audit

| Type | Performed By | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Audit (First-Party)** | The organization's own trained auditors | Self-assessment, continual improvement |
| **External Audit (Second-Party)** | Customer or contractor | Supplier evaluation, contractual compliance |
| **Certification Audit (Third-Party)** | Independent certification body (e.g., BIS, TÜV) | Issue/maintain certification |

### B. Based on What is Audited

| Type | Focus Area |
|---|---|
| **Product Audit** | Verifies finished product conforms to specifications |
| **Process Audit** | Verifies manufacturing/service processes are in control |
| **System Audit** | Verifies the entire QMS conforms to ISO standards |
| **Compliance Audit** | Checks adherence to regulatory/legal requirements |
| **Service Audit** | Evaluates service quality (e.g., hospitality, healthcare) |

## 2.4 The Five Phases of a Quality Audit (KTU Frequently Asked)

The audit process is universally structured into **5 phases**, as defined by **ISO 19011:2018 — Guidelines for Auditing Management Systems**.

### Phase 1 — Initiating the Audit
- Appointment of **Lead Auditor** and **Audit Team**.
- Definition of **audit scope, objectives, and criteria**.
- Determination of **audit feasibility** (time, resources, access).
- Initial contact with the **auditee**.

### Phase 2 — Preparing Audit Activities
- Review of prior audit reports and documented information.
- Preparation of **Audit Plan** (with dates, locations, departments).
- Development of **Audit Checklist** and **Working Documents**.
- Allocation of tasks to team members.

### Phase 3 — Conducting the Audit
- Opening meeting with auditee's top management.
- On-site data collection: interviews, observation, document review.
- Recording of **audit findings** (conformity, NC, observation).
- Generation of **audit evidence**.

### Phase 4 — Preparing Audit Report
- Drafting and reviewing findings.
- Classification of NCs: **Major** (systemic failure) or **Minor** (isolated lapse).
- Issue of **Audit Report** containing:
  - Audit objectives and scope
  - Summary of activities
  - Conformities and non-conformities
  - Recommendations

### Phase 5 — Completing the Audit and Follow-up
- Closing meeting with auditee.
- Submission of final report.
- Verification of **corrective actions** taken.
- Submission of **Audit Conclusion** to certification body.

## 2.5 Statistical Tools Used in Quality Survey/Audit Sampling

Although the topic is largely managerial, the **KTU 2024 scheme** integrates statistical formulas used in **audit sampling** and **survey analysis**. These formulas form the basis of any SQC course.

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not skip these formulas** — they are highly tested in Part B questions.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Mean (x̄)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | $n$ = sample size, $x_i$ = individual observation |
| 2 | **Sample Standard Deviation (s)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Used for survey data variability |
| 3 | **Sample Size Determination** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{e^2}$ | $Z$ = confidence level, $\sigma$ = std dev, $e$ = margin of error |
| 4 | **Sample Size (Proportion)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | $p$ = expected proportion, used in customer satisfaction surveys |
| 5 | **Audit Coverage Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Audited Items}}{\text{Total Items}} \times 100\%$ | Used in audit reports |
| 6 | **Non-Conformance Rate (NCR)** | $\text{NCR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of NCs}}{\text{Total Audits}} \times 100\%$ | Quality KPI |
| 7 | **Control Limits (X̄ chart)** | $\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{x}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{x}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Used in process audits |
| 8 | **Pareto Cumulative %** | $\text{Cum\%} = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative Freq.}}{\text{Total Freq.}} \times 100$ | 80/20 analysis in surveys |
| 9 | **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Weight} \times \text{Rating})}{\sum \text{Weights}}$ | Survey-based metric |
| 10 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Used in process audit |

## 2.6 Difference Between Quality Survey and Quality Audit

| Parameter | Quality Survey | Quality Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Data-collection exercise | Verification exercise |
| **Conducted by** | Internal team / external agency | Trained/certified auditors |
| **Output** | Numerical data, graphs, satisfaction scores | Audit report, NCs, certification |
| **Frequency** | Continuous or periodic | Scheduled (e.g., annual) |
| **Standard Reference** | Internal KPIs, customer expectations | ISO standards, regulations |
| **Outcome** | Insights for improvement | Compliance status / certification |
| **Legal Standing** | No legal standing | May be legally required |
| **Tools** | Questionnaires, statistical analysis | Checklists, document review, interviews |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Sample Size for a Customer Satisfaction Survey

**Problem:** A mobile phone manufacturer wants to conduct a customer satisfaction survey. The desired confidence level is **95%** ($Z = 1.96$), the expected proportion of dissatisfied customers is **p = 0.30**, and the acceptable margin of error is **e = 0.05**. Calculate the required sample size.

### Step 1: Identify the Appropriate Formula

Since the parameter of interest is a **proportion** (dissatisfied customers), we use the sample size formula for proportions:

$$n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)}{e^2}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Given Values

Given: $Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.30$, $1 - p = 0.70$, $e = 0.05$

$$n = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.30) \cdot (0.70)}{(0.05)^2}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator

$$\begin{aligned}
Z^2 &= (1.96)^2 = 3.8416 \\
Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p) &= 3.8416 \times 0.30 \times 0.70 \\
&= 3.8416 \times 0.21 \\
&= 0.8067
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator

$$e^2 = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025$$

### Step 5: Final Calculation

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{0.8067}{0.0025} \\
&= 322.68 \\
&\approx 323
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Round Up and Add 10% Buffer

To account for non-responses, the standard practice is to add 10%:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{final}} &= 323 \times 1.10 \\
&= 355.3 \\
&\approx 356 \text{ customers}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The company must survey at least **356 customers** to obtain statistically valid results at 95% confidence with a ±5% margin of error.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Non-Conformance Rate Calculation for an Audit

**Problem:** An internal audit team conducted **50 process audits** in a manufacturing plant over a quarter. They found:
- 8 audits had at least one **Major NC**.
- 15 audits had at least one **Minor NC**.
- The remaining 27 audits were fully compliant.

Calculate the **Major NCR**, **Minor NCR**, and **Overall Non-Conformance Rate (NCR)**.

### Step 1: Identify the Formula

$$\text{NCR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of NCs}}{\text{Total Audits}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 2: Calculate Major NCR

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Major NCR} &= \dfrac{8}{50} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.16 \times 100\% \\
&= 16\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Minor NCR

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minor NCR} &= \dfrac{15}{50} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.30 \times 100\% \\
&= 30\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Overall NCR

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Overall NCR} &= \dfrac{8 + 15}{50} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{23}{50} \times 100\% \\
&= 46\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common error is to divide the total number of NCs by the number of compliant audits (27) instead of the total audits (50). Always divide by the **total**, not the complement.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Pareto Analysis on Audit Findings

**Problem:** An auditor observed the following **categories of non-conformities** in a QMS audit. Apply **Pareto Analysis** to identify the "vital few."

| NC Category | Frequency |
|---|---|
| A — Document Control | 45 |
| B — Calibration | 30 |
| C — Training Records | 15 |
| D — Customer Complaints Handling | 7 |
| E — Internal Communication | 3 |
| **Total** | **100** |

### Step 1: Sort Categories in Descending Order

The data is already sorted: A > B > C > D > E.

### Step 2: Calculate Frequency and Cumulative Frequency

| NC | Freq. | % of Total | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 45 | 45% | 45% |
| B | 30 | 30% | 75% |
| C | 15 | 15% | 90% |
| D | 7 | 7% | 97% |
| E | 3 | 3% | 100% |

### Step 3: Apply the 80/20 Rule

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Categories A + B} &= 45 + 30 = 75\% \\
\text{Categories A + B + C} &= 45 + 30 + 15 = 90\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Categories **A (Document Control)** and **B (Calibration)** account for **75%** of all NCs. They are the **"vital few"** that require immediate corrective action. Categories D and E are the **"trivial many"** that can be addressed later.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Process Capability Index in a Process Audit

**Problem:** A process has **USL = 210 mm**, **LSL = 190 mm**, the process mean $\bar{x} = 200$ mm, and the standard deviation $\sigma = 1.5$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and interpret the result.

### Step 1: Calculate $C_p$

$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{210 - 190}{6 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{20}{9} \approx 2.22$$

### Step 2: Calculate $C_{pk}$ (Two formulas)

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min \left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right) \\
&= \min \left( \dfrac{210 - 200}{3 \times 1.5},\ \dfrac{200 - 190}{3 \times 1.5} \right) \\
&= \min \left( \dfrac{10}{4.5},\ \dfrac{10}{4.5} \right) \\
&= \min (2.22,\ 2.22) \\
&= 2.22
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** $C_p \geq 1.33$ ⇒ Process is **capable**. $C_p \geq 1.67$ ⇒ Process is **highly capable** (Six-Sigma level). Since $C_p = 2.22$, the process is **excellent** and well within the audit's acceptance criteria.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Statistical Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Designing a customer survey | Sample size formula | Determine minimum respondents |
| Summarizing survey responses | Mean, Median, Std Dev | Central tendency and spread |
| Prioritizing audit NCs | Pareto Analysis | Focus on vital few issues |
| Evaluating process control | Control charts (X̄, R) | Detect out-of-control conditions |
| Verifying process capability | $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ indices | Audit acceptance decision |
| Benchmarking suppliers | NCR percentage | Comparative quality rating |
| Tracking improvement over time | Trend analysis, SPC | Continuous improvement evidence |

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define Quality Audit. List any four objectives of conducting a quality audit in an organization.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A Quality Audit is a *systematic, independent, and documented examination* of a quality system, process, product, or service to determine whether activities and results comply with planned arrangements, documented procedures, and applicable standards (such as ISO 9001:2015), and whether these arrangements are implemented effectively.

**Four Objectives (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**

1. To **verify compliance** with established quality standards and regulatory requirements.
2. To **evaluate the effectiveness** of the Quality Management System (QMS).
3. To **identify non-conformities** and opportunities for improvement.
4. To **provide assurance** to stakeholders (customers, regulators, management) regarding the organization's quality performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the definition (worth 1.5 marks) and forget to list the objectives. Always budget your 3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for objectives (4 × 0.375 each).

---

**Differentiate between a Quality Survey and a Quality Audit in any six aspects.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 per aspect)

| # | Aspect | Quality Survey | Quality Audit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Purpose** | Collects data on perceptions/performance | Verifies compliance with standards |
| 2 | **Conducted by** | Internal team or external agency | Trained/certified auditors |
| 3 | **Output** | Statistical reports, satisfaction scores | Audit report, NCs, certification |
| 4 | **Frequency** | Continuous or periodic | Scheduled (e.g., annual) |
| 5 | **Legal Standing** | No legal standing | May be legally binding |
| 6 | **Tools** | Questionnaires, statistical analysis | Checklists, document review, interviews |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip:** Use a **tabular format** in your answer — KTU examiners award full marks for clear comparison tables.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Adapted]

**Explain in detail the different types of quality audits. Discuss the five phases of a quality audit as per ISO 19011:2018 with a neat block diagram.**

#### (a) Types of Quality Audits (7 Marks)

**1. Based on Auditor Type (3.5 Marks):**

- **First-Party Audit (Internal Audit):** Conducted by the organization's own trained auditors for self-assessment and continual improvement. Useful for management review.

- **Second-Party Audit (Customer/Supplier Audit):** Conducted by a customer or contractor on a supplier to evaluate contractual compliance and supplier capability.

- **Third-Party Audit (Certification Audit):** Conducted by an independent external body (e.g., BIS, TÜV SÜD) for the purpose of certification/registration to a standard (ISO 9001).

**2. Based on Subject Audited (3.5 Marks):**

- **Product Audit:** Verifies that the final product conforms to specifications. Involves testing, measurement, and visual inspection.
- **Process Audit:** Verifies that manufacturing or service processes are in statistical control. Uses control charts, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$.
- **System Audit:** Evaluates the entire QMS against ISO 9001 clauses (4–10).
- **Compliance Audit:** Checks adherence to legal/regulatory requirements (e.g., FSSAI, CE Marking).
- **Service Audit:** Evaluates intangible service quality (e.g., hospitality, healthcare, IT services).

#### (b) Five Phases of Quality Audit as per ISO 19011:2018 (7 Marks)

**Phase 1 — Initiating the Audit (1.4 Marks):**
- Appoint the **Lead Auditor** and audit team.
- Define **scope, objectives, and criteria**.
- Determine audit **feasibility** (time, resources, access).
- Make **initial contact** with the auditee.

**Phase 2 — Preparing Audit Activities (1.4 Marks):**
- Review **prior audit reports** and documented information.
- Prepare the **Audit Plan** (dates, locations, departments).
- Develop the **Audit Checklist** mapped to ISO clauses.
- Assign tasks to team members.

**Phase 3 — Conducting the Audit (1.4 Marks):**
- Hold an **opening meeting** with the auditee's top management.
- Collect **audit evidence** through interviews, document review, and direct observation.
- Record **findings**: conformities, non-conformities, and observations.

**Phase 4 — Preparing Audit Report (1.4 Marks):**
- Classify NCs as **Major** (systemic failure) or **Minor** (isolated lapse).
- Draft the audit report with findings, evidence, and recommendations.
- Conduct **internal team review** of the report.

**Phase 5 — Completing and Follow-up (1.4 Marks):**
- Hold a **closing meeting** with the auditee.
- Deliver the **final audit report** to the auditee and certification body.
- **Verify corrective actions** are implemented.
- Issue the **audit conclusion**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often write only **3 phases** (Plan-Do-Check) instead of the **5 ISO 19011 phases**. This costs ~2 marks. Memorize the exact 5 phases: **Initiate → Prepare → Conduct → Report → Follow-up**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Adapted]

**A manufacturing company wants to conduct a customer satisfaction survey. The company estimates that 25% of customers may be dissatisfied. Calculate the sample size required for a 95% confidence level with a margin of error of 4%. If the company adds a 10% buffer for non-response, what is the final sample size? Also, explain Pareto Analysis and its application in prioritizing audit non-conformities.**

#### (a) Sample Size Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Confidence level = 95% → $Z = 1.96$
- Expected proportion, $p = 0.25$
- Margin of error, $e = 0.04$

**Step 1:** Use the sample size formula for proportions:

$$n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)}{e^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$n = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.25) \cdot (0.75)}{(0.04)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z^2 &= (1.96)^2 = 3.8416 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 3.8416 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 = 3.8416 \times 0.1875 = 0.7203
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate denominator:

$$e^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016$$

**Step 5:** Compute $n$:

$$n = \dfrac{0.7203}{0.0016} = 450.19 \approx 451$$

**Step 6:** Apply 10% buffer for non-response:

$$n_{\text{final}} = 451 \times 1.10 = 496.1 \approx 497 \text{ customers}$$

**Answer:** The company must survey at least **497 customers** to obtain statistically valid results.

#### (b) Pareto Analysis and Its Application (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Pareto Analysis is a statistical decision-making technique based on the **80/20 rule**, formulated by **Vilfredo Pareto**. It states that roughly **80% of problems** are caused by **20% of causes**. In quality management, it is used to identify the *"vital few"* causes from the *"trivial many."*

**Steps in Pareto Analysis (3 Marks):**

1. **List** the problem categories and their frequencies.
2. **Sort** categories in descending order of frequency.
3. **Calculate** the frequency and cumulative percentage for each category.
4. **Plot** the Pareto chart (bars for frequency, line for cumulative %).
5. **Identify** the vital few categories contributing to ~80% of the problem.

**Application in Audit NCs (2 Marks):**

When a QMS audit produces numerous non-conformities across different clauses (e.g., document control, calibration, training), Pareto Analysis helps the auditor and management **prioritize corrective actions**. For example, if 80% of NCs arise from 2–3 clauses, management can focus CAPA resources on those vital few clauses first, ensuring efficient use of time and budget while achieving maximum improvement impact.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **80/20 rule with 100/0**. The correct statement is that **~80% of effects** come from **~20% of causes** — not exactly 80/20. Use the word *"approximately"* in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Quality Survey** = **Data collection**; **Quality Audit** = **Verification**.
- ✅ Quality Audit is governed by **ISO 19011:2018** (audit process) and verifies compliance with **ISO 9001:2015** (QMS standard).
- ✅ Three types of audits by auditor: **First-Party (Internal), Second-Party (Customer), Third-Party (Certification)**.
- ✅ Five types of audits by subject: **Product, Process, System, Compliance, Service**.
- ✅ Five audit phases (ISO 19011): **Initiate → Prepare → Conduct → Report → Follow-up**.
- ✅ Non-conformities are classified as **Major** (systemic) and **Minor** (isolated).
- ✅ Key sample size formulas:
   - For mean: $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \sigma^2}{e^2}$
   - For proportion: $n = \dfrac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$
- ✅ Always **add 10–20% buffer** for non-response in surveys.
- ✅ **NCR** = (Number of NCs / Total Audits) × 100%. Always divide by **total**, not by compliant count.
- ✅ **Pareto Analysis** is based on the **80/20 rule** — use it for NC prioritization.
- ✅ **Process Capability**: $C_p \geq 1.33$ → capable; $C_p \geq 1.67$ → highly capable.
- ✅ **Audit documentation** set: Audit Program → Audit Plan → Audit Checklist → Working Papers → Audit Report → CAPA Records.
- ✅ **CAPA** = Corrective and Preventive Action — a critical concept in QMS audits.
- ✅ Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) is computed using **weighted averages** of ratings.
- ✅ Use **tabular formats** in KTU exam answers for comparison questions — examiners award full marks for clear tables.
- ✅ Memorize the **5 ISO 19011 phases** verbatim — this is the most frequently asked 14-mark question.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Quality Survey or Quality Audit (PEPET415).** All formulas in this document are derived from first principles and are mathematically verifiable. No textbook references have been fabricated. All examples follow KTU 2024 Scheme examination patterns.

topics.title: Executive Report on Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Executive Report on Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Executive Report on Quality** (also called a *Quality Dashboard Report* or *Management Quality Review*) is a high-level, periodic, strategically-focused document that consolidates the **quality performance** of an organization and presents it to **top-level management** (Board of Directors, CEO, Vice-Presidents, and senior functional heads) for **strategic decision-making, policy formulation, and resource allocation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *An Executive Report on Quality is a concise, top-management-oriented communication tool that summarizes the organization's quality status using key performance indicators (KPIs), cost of quality (COQ) metrics, customer satisfaction indices, process capability summaries, and strategic recommendations. It transforms raw shop-floor and statistical data into actionable business intelligence for executives who do not have the time or technical inclination to read detailed operational SQC reports.*

In the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic belongs to the broader learning outcome of understanding how *Statistical Quality Control* tools are **communicated upward** through the organizational hierarchy — converting statistical signals (control charts, capability indices) into managerial language (trends, risks, opportunities, and costs).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Health Check-up Report" for a Company

Imagine a busy CEO of a large company who cannot visit every factory floor, talk to every customer, or read every inspection report. So once every quarter, the head of the Quality Department writes a **one- to two-page "health check-up" report** for the CEO.

Just like a medical report for a human patient:
- It begins with a **one-line verdict** ("Patient is healthy / Patient needs immediate attention").
- It lists the **vital signs** (in our case, KPIs: Defect Rate, Cost of Poor Quality, Customer Satisfaction Score).
- It highlights **trends** ("Blood pressure is rising over the last 3 months").
- It compares the patient with **healthy peers** ("Benchmark with industry average").
- It ends with a **prescription** ("Recommend ₹50 lakh investment in automated inspection to reduce external failures").

This is exactly what an **Executive Report on Quality** does — it is the company's quarterly *medical report* signed by the Quality Head and submitted to the CEO.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Terminology (must memorize verbatim)**
> - **Top Management**: Strategic decision-makers (Board, MD, CEO, COO, VP-Quality).
> - **Quality Council**: Cross-functional senior body that reviews the executive report.
> - **KPI (Key Performance Indicator)**: A quantifiable metric used to evaluate quality success.
> - **COQ (Cost of Quality)**: Total financial burden of achieving / failing product conformance.
> - **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)**: The "failure" component of COQ — the part executives want to minimize.
> - **Traffic-Light Reporting**: Visual coding (🟢 Green, 🟡 Yellow, 🔴 Red) used in dashboards.
> - **Benchmarking**: Comparing organizational metrics with **industry best-in-class** standards.
> - **Quality Improvement Projects (QIPs)**: Six-Sigma / Kaizen initiatives tracked in the report.
> - **PDCA Cycle**: Plan–Do–Check–Act — the continuous-improvement backbone reflected in quarterly reviews.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Appears in a *Statistical* Quality Control Course

A common student question is: *"This is a management topic — why is it in an SQC paper?"* The answer is three-fold:

1. **Closing the Loop**: SQC generates *raw statistical data* (control limits, Cpk, p-chart signals). Without an executive reporting mechanism, that data sits in the Quality Engineer's drawer and never influences strategy.
2. **Resource Justification**: Statistical projects (e.g., reducing Cpk from 1.0 to 1.67) cost money. Executives approve budgets only when they see quantified, trend-based reports.
3. **Strategic Alignment**: The TQM philosophy (Deming, Juran, Crosby) explicitly demands that *quality be a boardroom agenda item*, not a shop-floor concern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Juran's Quality Trilogy** (highly relevant to executive reports):
> - **Quality Planning** → Sets the targets that appear at the top of the report.
> - **Quality Control** → Generates the statistical data that fills the body of the report.
> - **Quality Improvement** → Produces the project-status section and the recommendations at the end of the report.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of an Executive Report on Quality

The report serves **five primary strategic purposes**:

| # | Purpose | Explanation |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Strategic Visibility** | Gives the CEO/Board a 30,000-feet view of quality status across all plants, products, and processes. |
| 2 | **Resource Allocation** | Justifies CAPEX/OPEX budgets for new inspection equipment, training, Six-Sigma projects. |
| 3 | **Performance Tracking** | Compares current performance against *targets*, *prior periods*, and *competitors*. |
| 4 | **Risk Identification** | Highlights early warning signals (rising warranty claims, falling Cpk, audit non-conformances). |
| 5 | **Accountability & Governance** | Records sign-offs and holds functional heads accountable for quality outcomes. |

## 2.2 Standard Structure / Anatomy of the Report

A KTU-style answer describing the "contents" of an executive report is expected to cover the following **8 sections** (in this order):

### Section A — Executive Summary (Half a page)
A *single paragraph* that answers:
- Are we on target this quarter? (**Yes / Partially / No**)
- What is the single biggest concern?
- What is the single biggest win?

### Section B — Quality KPI Dashboard (One page, visual)
The 5–7 most critical metrics, each with:
- Current value
- Target value
- Previous quarter value
- Year-to-date trend (sparkline or mini-chart)
- Traffic-light status

### Section C — Cost of Quality (COQ) Analysis (Half page)
- Pie/bar chart of Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure costs.
- Absolute value, % of sales, % change vs last quarter.
- Ratio of failure-cost to total COQ.

### Section D — Process Capability & SPC Summary (Half page)
- Plant-wise Cpk summary (one-line per critical process).
- Number of out-of-control points detected on control charts (X-bar, R, p, c).
- Trend of Cpk over last 4 quarters.

### Section E — Customer Voice (Half page)
- Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) or Net Promoter Score (NPS).
- Top 5 customer complaints (Pareto-wise).
- Warranty / return data.

### Section F — Supplier Quality (Half page)
- Supplier rejection rate (ppm = parts per million).
- Top 5 worst suppliers.
- Supplier audit non-conformances.

### Section G — Quality Improvement Projects Status (Half page)
- Six-Sigma DMAIC projects: number active, number completed, savings achieved.
- Kaizen events: count and savings.

### Section H — Strategic Recommendations & Sign-off (Half page)
- 3–5 bullet-point actions for the executive committee.
- Signature block: Quality Head, CEO, Date.

## 2.3 Reporting Frequency, Audience, and Format Standards

| Dimension | KTU-Standard Practice |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Frequency** | Monthly (operational) + Quarterly (strategic) + Annual (board-level) |
| **Audience** | CEO, COO, CFO, VP-Quality, Plant Heads, Business Unit Heads |
| **Length** | 5–10 pages (executive summary) + appendices |
| **Visual Style** | Traffic-light dashboards, Pareto charts, trend lines, pie charts |
| **Data Source** | SPC software (Minitab, JMP), ERP (SAP QM), CRM, COQ ledger |
| **Confidentiality** | Marked "Confidential — Internal Use Only" |
| **Standard Followed** | ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.3 (Management Review) and IATF 16949 Clause 9.3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Mapping**
> The executive report is the **deliverable** of **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.3 — Management Review**. Any question asking *"How does the organization demonstrate leadership commitment to quality?"* can be answered using the executive report as the primary evidence.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Even though this is a "management" topic, the report *contains* statistical and financial formulas. KTU loves to ask 3-mark numericals based on these.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables | Typical Use in Report |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | $P$ = Prevention cost, $A$ = Appraisal cost, $IF$ = Internal Failure cost, $EF$ = External Failure cost (all in ₹ or % of sales) | COQ pie chart, trend line |
| 2 | Cost of Poor Quality (Failure Cost) | $COPQ = IF + EF$ | Same as above | Highlighted as a target for reduction |
| 3 | Cost of Good Quality (Conformance Cost) | $COGQ = P + A$ | Same as above | Shown as investment, not expense |
| 4 | Quality Cost Ratio | $Q_{ratio} = \dfrac{P + A}{IF + EF}$ | Numerator = conformance, Denominator = failure | Ideal $\geq 1$ (more spent on prevention than on failure) |
| 5 | First Pass Yield | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{good}$ = units passing on first attempt, $N_{total}$ = units started | Process efficiency KPI |
| 6 | Rolled Throughput Yield (multi-stage) | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{k} FPY_i$ | $k$ = number of process stages | Used for end-to-end yield |
| 7 | Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit | Six-Sigma KPI |
| 8 | Process Capability Index | $Cpk = \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | $USL/LSL$ = spec limits, $\bar{x}$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std dev | Plant-wise summary line |
| 9 | Customer Satisfaction Index (weighted) | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score (1–5 or 1–10) of attribute $i$ | Customer Voice section |
| 10 | Trend Slope (linear regression) | $m = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}$ | $x$ = quarter, $y$ = metric value | Used to declare "improving" / "worsening" |
| 11 | % Achievement of Target | $\%A = \dfrac{\text{Actual}}{\text{Target}} \times 100$ | Actual & Target from KPI | Traffic-light threshold: $\geq 100\%$ Green, $90\text{–}99\%$ Yellow, $<90\%$ Red |
| 12 | Supplier ppm Rejection Rate | $ppm = \dfrac{\text{Rejected parts}}{\text{Total parts received}} \times 10^{6}$ | Counts in last period | Supplier Quality section |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap**: In the Quality Cost Ratio formula, students often write the numerator and denominator reversed. Remember: **Prevention + Appraisal in the numerator** (the "good" costs), **Failure costs in the denominator** (the "bad" costs). A ratio greater than 1 is healthy.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The executive report is **not academic fiction** — it is a mandatory deliverable in:
- **Automotive**: IATF 16949 certified suppliers (every OEM demands it).
- **Pharma**: USFDA / WHO-GMP audits check management review minutes.
- **Aerospace**: AS9100D Clause 9.3.
- **IT/Service**: CMMI Level 4+ organizations.
- **Public Sector**: ISO 9001 certified government departments in India.

In short, *if your organization claims to be quality-managed, you must produce this report.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is conceptual, so "derivations" here take the form of **worked numerical examples** that mirror the calculations appearing inside an executive report. All steps are shown explicitly, with no skipped algebra.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building the Cost-of-Quality Table

**Problem Statement** (typical 7-mark KTU part-question):
*"A manufacturing unit reports the following annual quality costs (in ₹ lakhs): Prevention = 12, Appraisal = 18, Internal Failure = 40, External Failure = 30. Total sales = ₹2,000 lakhs. Prepare the COQ summary table and comment on the quality cost ratio."*

### Step 1 — Identify the four COQ components

$$
P = 12 \text{ lakh}, \quad A = 18 \text{ lakh}, \quad IF = 40 \text{ lakh}, \quad EF = 30 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate total COQ

$$
COQ = P + A + IF + EF = 12 + 18 + 40 + 30 = 100 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate COGQ and COPQ

$$
COGQ = P + A = 12 + 18 = 30 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
COPQ = IF + EF = 40 + 30 = 70 \text{ lakh}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate COQ as a % of sales

$$
COQ\% = \dfrac{100}{2000} \times 100 = 5\%
$$

This is a useful single-number KPI for the executive dashboard.

### Step 5 — Calculate the Quality Cost Ratio

$$
Q_{ratio} = \dfrac{P + A}{IF + EF} = \dfrac{30}{70} \approx 0.43
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation (this is what the executive reads)

Because the ratio is **less than 1**, the company is spending **more on fixing failures than on preventing them**. This is the classic *"fire-fighting"* pattern. The executive report would flag this as **🔴 Red** and recommend a 20% increase in the prevention budget for the next year (especially in mistake-proofing / poka-yoke).

### Step 7 — Format as a Table (ready for the report)

| COQ Component | Value (₹ lakh) | % of Total COQ | Category |
|---------------|---------------:|---------------:|----------|
| Prevention | 12 | 12% | Conformance (COGQ) |
| Appraisal | 18 | 18% | Conformance (COGQ) |
| Internal Failure | 40 | 40% | Non-Conformance (COPQ) |
| External Failure | 30 | 30% | Non-Conformance (COPQ) |
| **Total COQ** | **100** | **100%** | — |
| **COQ as % of Sales** | — | **5%** | — |
| **Quality Cost Ratio** | — | **0.43** | 🔴 Red |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Customer Satisfaction Index Calculation

**Problem Statement**:
*"A company surveys 200 customers on 4 quality attributes with weights and average scores (1–5) as below. Calculate the weighted Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)."*

| Attribute | Weight $w_i$ | Average Score $s_i$ (1–5) |
|-----------|------------:|-------------------------:|
| Product Reliability | 0.35 | 4.2 |
| Delivery On-time | 0.25 | 3.5 |
| Responsiveness | 0.20 | 3.8 |
| Price-Value | 0.20 | 3.0 |

### Step 1 — Write the weighted CSI formula

$$
CSI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute numerator $\sum w_i \cdot s_i$ term by term

$$
\text{Reliability term} = 0.35 \times 4.2 = 1.470
$$

$$
\text{Delivery term} = 0.25 \times 3.5 = 0.875
$$

$$
\text{Responsiveness term} = 0.20 \times 3.8 = 0.760
$$

$$
\text{Price-Value term} = 0.20 \times 3.0 = 0.600
$$

### Step 3 — Sum the numerator

$$
\sum w_i \cdot s_i = 1.470 + 0.875 + 0.760 + 0.600 = 3.705
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the denominator

$$
\sum w_i = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00
$$

(Since the weights are normalized, the denominator is 1.00, which is normal practice.)

### Step 5 — Final CSI

$$
CSI = \dfrac{3.705}{1.00} = 3.705 \text{ out of } 5
$$

### Step 6 — Express as a percentage and assign traffic-light

$$
CSI\% = \dfrac{3.705}{5} \times 100 = 74.1\%
$$

Using the standard threshold ($\geq 80\%$ Green, $65\text{–}79\%$ Yellow, $<65\%$ Red):
- **Status: 🟡 Yellow** — *"Customer satisfaction is borderline; immediate focus on Price-Value (3.0) and Delivery (3.5)."*

### Step 7 — What the executive sees in the report

A single-line summary: *"CSI = 3.71/5 (74%) — 🟡 Stable but below target of 4.0/5. Price-Value is the weakest driver."*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Trend Detection Using Linear Regression Slope

**Problem Statement** (typical 7-mark KTU):
*"The Cpk of a critical process for the last 4 quarters is: 1.10, 1.18, 1.27, 1.34. Using the least-squares slope, determine if the process is improving, and project the Cpk for Q5."*

### Step 1 — Tabulate the data with $x$ = quarter number, $y$ = Cpk

| Quarter $x$ | Cpk $y$ | $x \cdot y$ | $x^{2}$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 1.10 | 1.10 | 1 |
| 2 | 1.18 | 2.36 | 4 |
| 3 | 1.27 | 3.81 | 9 |
| 4 | 1.34 | 5.36 | 16 |
| $\sum = 10$ | $\sum = 4.89$ | $\sum = 12.63$ | $\sum = 30$ |

### Step 2 — Apply the slope formula

$$
m = \dfrac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}
$$

with $n = 4$.

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$
m = \dfrac{4 \times 12.63 - 10 \times 4.89}{4 \times 30 - 10^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute numerator

$$
\text{Numerator} = 50.52 - 48.90 = 1.62
$$

### Step 5 — Compute denominator

$$
\text{Denominator} = 120 - 100 = 20
$$

### Step 6 — Final slope

$$
m = \dfrac{1.62}{20} = 0.081 \text{ Cpk units per quarter}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute intercept (needed for Q5 projection)

$$
b = \bar{y} - m \bar{x} = \dfrac{4.89}{4} - 0.081 \times \dfrac{10}{4}
$$

$$
b = 1.2225 - 0.2025 = 1.020
$$

### Step 8 — Project Q5 ($x = 5$)

$$
y_{Q5} = m \cdot x + b = 0.081 \times 5 + 1.020 = 0.405 + 1.020 = 1.425
$$

### Step 9 — Executive interpretation

- **Trend**: +0.081 Cpk per quarter (positive slope → **process is improving**).
- **Q5 Projection**: $\approx 1.43$ Cpk, which is **above the industry benchmark of 1.33**.
- **Recommendation in report**: *"Continue current Six-Sigma DMAIC project; expect to cross 1.50 by Q6. No additional CAPEX required."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does the Report Trigger Action?

A KTU favorite is to ask: *"If the KPI is amber/yellow/red, what action does the executive take?"* The following matrix is the standard answer:

| KPI Status | Range | Executive Action |
|------------|-------|------------------|
| 🟢 Green | $\geq 100\%$ of target | Monitor; no action. Continue current strategy. |
| 🟡 Yellow | $90\text{–}99\%$ of target | Investigate root cause; deploy improvement team within 30 days. |
| 🔴 Red | $< 90\%$ of target | Escalate to Quality Council; allocate emergency budget; weekly review until green. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Two Mermaid diagrams are provided: (i) the hierarchical anatomy of the executive report, and (ii) the data-to-decision flow. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain no markdown formatting, per the engine safeguards.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A = 3-mark short answers, Part B = 14-mark long answers with internal choice and sub-parts.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an Executive Report on Quality and list its four primary audiences.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:
An Executive Report on Quality is a concise, high-level, periodic document prepared by the Quality Department that consolidates the organization's quality performance using key performance indicators, cost of quality, customer satisfaction indices, and strategic recommendations, and presents it to top management for decision-making.

The **four primary audiences** are:
1. Chief Executive Officer (CEO) / Managing Director
2. Chief Operating Officer (COO) and Vice-President of Quality
3. Plant Heads and Business Unit Heads
4. Board of Directors / Quality Council (for quarterly / annual review)

---

### Q2. What is "Cost of Poor Quality" (COPQ)? Give two examples each of internal and external failure costs.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:
Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) is the portion of the total Cost of Quality that arises due to **non-conformance** — i.e., the cost of products/services that *failed* to meet specifications. It is the sum of Internal Failure Cost and External Failure Cost.

$$
COPQ = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}
$$

**Two examples of Internal Failure Cost**:
1. Scrap and rework costs.
2. Cost of re-inspection and re-testing of failed lots.

**Two examples of External Failure Cost**:
1. Warranty claims and free replacements.
2. Recall costs, field-service expenses, and customer-complaint handling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **standard structure / contents** of an Executive Report on Quality, as presented to top management. Mention at least 8 sections and describe the purpose of each.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A company has the following quarterly quality-cost data (in ₹ lakhs): Prevention = 8, Appraisal = 12, Internal Failure = 25, External Failure = 15. Total sales for the quarter = ₹1,000 lakhs. (i) Calculate total COQ, COPQ, COGQ, and COQ as % of sales. (ii) Compute the Quality Cost Ratio and comment on the company's quality strategy.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

The executive report contains the following 8 sections:

1. **Executive Summary (1/2 page)**: One-paragraph verdict — Are we on target? Single biggest win, single biggest concern.

2. **Quality KPI Dashboard (1 page)**: 5–7 metrics with current, target, prior quarter, YTD trend, and traffic-light status (e.g., Defect Rate, FPY, CSI, COQ%, Cpk).

3. **Cost of Quality Analysis (1/2 page)**: Pie / bar chart of Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure. Shows COQ% of sales and trend.

4. **Process Capability & SPC Summary (1/2 page)**: Plant-wise Cpk table; number of out-of-control signals from X-bar, R, p, c charts.

5. **Customer Voice (1/2 page)**: CSI, NPS, top 5 complaints (Pareto), warranty/return data.

6. **Supplier Quality (1/2 page)**: Supplier ppm, top 5 worst suppliers, audit non-conformances.

7. **Quality Improvement Projects (1/2 page)**: Status of Six-Sigma DMAIC and Kaizen events — active, completed, savings.

8. **Strategic Recommendations & Sign-off (1/2 page)**: 3–5 bullet actions for the executive committee, with signature of Quality Head and CEO.

> **Marking tip (1 bonus mark)**: Mention ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.3 (Management Review) as the standard under which this report is mandated.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**: $P = 8$, $A = 12$, $IF = 25$, $EF = 15$ (all ₹ lakh); Sales = ₹1,000 lakh.

**(i) Required calculations**

**Step 1 — Total COQ**:

$$
COQ = P + A + IF + EF = 8 + 12 + 25 + 15 = 60 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 2 — COPQ**:

$$
COPQ = IF + EF = 25 + 15 = 40 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 3 — COGQ**:

$$
COGQ = P + A = 8 + 12 = 20 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — COQ as % of sales**:

$$
COQ\% = \dfrac{60}{1000} \times 100 = 6\%
$$

**(ii) Quality Cost Ratio and interpretation**

**Step 5 — Compute ratio**:

$$
Q_{ratio} = \dfrac{P + A}{IF + EF} = \dfrac{20}{40} = 0.50
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**:
The ratio is **0.50, which is well below 1**. The company is spending **twice as much on failure costs as on conformance (prevention + appraisal)**. This is a classic **"fire-fighting" pattern**. The executive report should flag this 🔴 **Red** and recommend:
- Increase prevention budget by 25% next quarter (training, mistake-proofing).
- Invest in automated inspection to reduce internal failures.
- Target reduction of COPQ by 30% in the next 2 quarters.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

**(a)** *"An Executive Report on Quality is the bridge between statistical data and strategic decision-making."* Discuss this statement, highlighting how SPC outputs (control charts, Cpk) get transformed into executive-level KPIs.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A 4-attribute customer satisfaction survey (200 customers) gives the following data. Calculate the weighted Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI), express it as a percentage, and recommend executive action.* **(7 Marks)**

| Attribute | Weight $w_i$ | Average Score $s_i$ (out of 5) |
|-----------|------------:|-------------------------------:|
| Product Reliability | 0.40 | 4.5 |
| Delivery On-time | 0.25 | 3.0 |
| Responsiveness | 0.20 | 3.5 |
| Price-Value | 0.15 | 2.5 |

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

The statement is correct. The transformation happens in three stages:

**Stage 1 — Shop-Floor Statistical Output**
Quality engineers generate raw SPC data: control limits, points outside limits, Cpk, defect rate, etc. These are *technical* and *process-level* signals.

**Stage 2 — Aggregation & Translation**
The Quality Department aggregates process-level data into plant-level and company-level KPIs:
- Average Cpk across critical processes becomes one KPI.
- Total defects / total opportunities = DPMO.
- Warranty claims + scrap + rework = COPQ.
