- Customer survey responses = CSI.

**Stage 3 — Executive Visualization**
The aggregated KPIs are visualized using **traffic-light dashboards, sparkline trends, and Pareto charts** — formats a CEO can scan in 60 seconds.

**Why this bridge is essential**:
1. **Without the report**, statistical data stays in the engineer's drawer and never influences strategy.
2. **Without the report**, executives cannot justify quality budgets to the Board.
3. **Without the report**, the PDCA cycle's "Check" and "Act" phases have no executive owner.

Thus, the Executive Report on Quality is the **only document** that closes the loop between SQC data and corporate strategy.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply the weighted CSI formula**:

$$
CSI = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum w_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each $w_i \cdot s_i$ term**:

$$
\text{Reliability} = 0.40 \times 4.5 = 1.800
$$

$$
\text{Delivery} = 0.25 \times 3.0 = 0.750
$$

$$
\text{Responsiveness} = 0.20 \times 3.5 = 0.700
$$

$$
\text{Price-Value} = 0.15 \times 2.5 = 0.375
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the numerator**:

$$
\sum w_i \cdot s_i = 1.800 + 0.750 + 0.700 + 0.375 = 3.625
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the denominator**:

$$
\sum w_i = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00
$$

**Step 5 — CSI value**:

$$
CSI = \dfrac{3.625}{1.00} = 3.625 \text{ out of } 5
$$

**Step 6 — Express as %**:

$$
CSI\% = \dfrac{3.625}{5} \times 100 = 72.5\%
$$

**Step 7 — Traffic-light and action**:
Using the standard threshold: 🟡 **Yellow (65–79%)**.

**Executive recommendation in the report**:
- Price-Value (2.5) is the **weakest** driver — investigate pricing strategy.
- Delivery (3.0) is also weak — review logistics and on-time performance.
- Reliability is strong (4.5) — leverage in marketing.
- **Action**: Form a cross-functional tiger team to address Price-Value and Delivery within 60 days; re-survey next quarter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (verified by KTU valuation key analysis):**
> 1. **Confusing the four COQ components** — Prevention & Appraisal are *conformance* (good) costs; Internal & External Failure are *non-conformance* (bad) costs. Many students mix them up in formulas. (-2 marks typical).
> 2. **Forgetting the units in COQ% of sales** — Always express as a % of total sales, not in absolute ₹ only. (-1 mark).
> 3. **Writing the Quality Cost Ratio upside-down** — Numerator = $P + A$, Denominator = $IF + EF$. A ratio $\geq 1$ is healthy; $< 1$ is fire-fighting. (-1 mark).
> 4. **Listing report contents without explaining their purpose** — KTU wants the *strategic purpose* of each section, not just the heading. A bare list scores 3/7; a purpose-explained list scores 7/7.
> 5. **No mention of ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.3** — This is the "examiner's delight" linkage. Including it adds 1 impression mark and shows depth.
> 6. **In numericals, showing only the final answer** — Always show the *formula*, *substitution*, and *final value*. KTU follows a stepwise marking scheme.
> 7. **Confusing CSI with NPS** — They are different. CSI is a weighted average of attribute scores; NPS = % Promoters − % Detractors. Don't mix them in the same formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- **Definition**: Executive Report on Quality = concise, top-management-oriented, periodic communication of quality status using KPIs, COQ, and recommendations.
- **Primary Audience**: CEO, COO, VP-Quality, Plant Heads, Quality Council, Board.
- **Standard Length**: 5–10 pages plus appendices.
- **Frequency**: Monthly (operational) + Quarterly (strategic) + Annual (board).
- **8 Standard Sections**: (1) Executive Summary, (2) KPI Dashboard, (3) COQ, (4) Cpk & SPC, (5) Customer Voice, (6) Supplier Quality, (7) QIP Status, (8) Recommendations & Sign-off.
- **COQ Formula**: $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$.
- **COPQ Formula**: $COPQ = IF + EF$.
- **COGQ Formula**: $COGQ = P + A$.
- **Quality Cost Ratio**: $Q_{ratio} = (P + A) / (IF + EF)$; healthy if $\geq 1$.
- **CSI Formula**: $CSI = \sum(w_i \cdot s_i) / \sum w_i$.
- **Cpk Formula**: $Cpk = \min[(USL - \bar{x})/(3\sigma), (\bar{x} - LSL)/(3\sigma)]$.
- **DPMO Formula**: $DPMO = (D \times 10^6) / (N \times O)$.
- **Traffic-Light Rule**: $\geq 100\%$ Green, $90\text{–}99\%$ Yellow, $< 90\%$ Red.
- **ISO Linkage**: ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.3 (Management Review) — the executive report is its primary deliverable.
- **Juran's Trilogy Mapping**: Planning → targets; Control → SPC data; Improvement → QIP status.
- **TQM Gurus Linked**: Deming (PDCA), Juran (Trilogy), Crosby (Quality is Free / COPQ), Feigenbaum (Total Quality Control).
- **Visual Tools Used**: Traffic-light dashboard, Pareto chart, pie chart of COQ, Cpk trend line, sparklines.
- **Strategic Value**: Closes the loop between shop-floor SQC and boardroom strategy; justifies CAPEX; identifies risks; tracks continuous improvement.
- **Examiner's Bonus Word**: Always mention **"ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.3 Management Review"** when explaining the purpose — it instantly adds depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inspection Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inspection Planning** is the systematic, engineering-oriented process of deciding **what to inspect, where to inspect, when to inspect, how much to inspect, and how to organize the inspection function** within a manufacturing or service environment, with the goal of minimizing total quality cost while ensuring conformance to specifications.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Inspection Planning* is treated as the **strategic backbone of Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** — it precedes the actual selection of control charts or sampling plans. The decisions made at the planning stage directly govern the cost-effectiveness of the entire quality assurance program.

The four classical decision dimensions (per Montgomery's *Introduction to Statistical Quality Control*) are:
1. **Decision 1:** Where in the process flow to place inspection stations.
2. **Decision 2:** Whether to use **100% inspection** or **sampling inspection**.
3. **Decision 3:** What **sampling plan** to use (single, double, multiple, or sequential).
4. **Decision 4:** Whether inspection is to be **centralized** or **decentralized**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Inspection Planning is a *Module 1* foundational topic. Direct 14-mark questions are frequently asked linking inspection planning to **AOQ, AOQL, ATI, and ASN curves** — students are expected to derive inspection cost trade-offs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine you are the **Operations Head of a major international airport**. Your job is to make sure every passenger boarding the aircraft is safe. You cannot personally frisk every one of the 80,000 daily passengers — that would paralyze the airport (high inspection cost). Yet you also cannot allow *zero* checking, because a single undetected threat could be catastrophic (high cost of defectives).

So you design an **inspection plan**:
- **Where?** At the boarding gate (final product), not at the entrance door (raw material) — because the threat must be neutralized just before aircraft entry.
- **How much?** Random X-ray scanning for 1 in 4 bags (sampling inspection), but **100% checking** for international flights (high risk).
- **How to organize?** Centralized command center monitors CCTV, while decentralized guards patrol gates.

This is **exactly** what an industrial inspection planner does — balancing **inspection cost** against the **cost of passing defectives**, to design a robust, cost-optimal inspection system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Inspection is *not* a cost — it is a **cost-avoidance investment**. The goal of inspection planning is to find the **economic minimum** of the *Total Quality Cost curve* (prevention + appraisal + failure costs).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Inspection Planning

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fraction defective incoming | $p$ | dimensionless | Proportion of defectives in submitted lot |
| Probability of acceptance | $P_a$ | dimensionless | Chance the lot is accepted by the plan |
| Average Outgoing Quality | AOQ | fraction defective | Expected fraction defective after inspection |
| Average Outgoing Quality Limit | AOQL | fraction defective | Maximum value of AOQ over all $p$ |
| Average Total Inspection | ATI | units inspected/lot | Expected number of items inspected per lot |
| Producer's Risk | $\alpha$ | probability | P(rejecting a good lot) |
| Consumer's Risk | $\beta$ | probability | P(accepting a bad lot) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Inspection Planning

### Pillar 1 — Decision on **Where to Inspect** (Position of Inspection Station)

Inspection can be placed at:
- **Raw material stage** (vendor inspection)
- **In-process stage** (after each critical operation)
- **Finished-goods stage** (before shipment)
- **Field/Field-return stage** (after customer use)

> **Why it matters:** Earlier detection = lower cost of failure. The cost of a defect discovered at the **raw-material** stage may be ₹10, but the *same* defect at the **field** stage may cost ₹10,000 (warranty, recall, brand damage).

### Pillar 2 — Decision on **How Much to Inspect**

The fundamental choice is between:

$$ \text{Inspection Strategy} = \begin{cases} 100\% \text{ Inspection (Screening)} \\ \text{Sampling Inspection} \\ \text{No Inspection (Skip-lot)} \end{cases} $$

**Selection Rule:**
- Use **100% inspection** when:
  - Cost of a defective passing is very high (aerospace, medical devices).
  - Defectives are easily detected by automated sensors.
  - Lot size is small and inspection is cheap.
- Use **Sampling inspection** when:
  - Inspection is destructive (light bulbs, ammunition).
  - Defect rate is low and the cost of inspection exceeds the cost of defectives.
  - Lots are large and continuous flow production is used.

### Pillar 3 — Decision on **Sampling Plan Type**

| Plan Type | Inspection Effort | Information per Item | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Sampling | Fixed $n$, single decision | Moderate | General purpose |
| Double Sampling | Two stages, $n_1$ then $n_2$ | Moderate | When lot sizes vary |
| Multiple Sampling | Up to 7 stages | High | Expensive-to-inspect items |
| Sequential Sampling | Item-by-item | Maximum | Very destructive tests |
| Skip-Lot | Skip after several accepts | Low | Trusted supplier (rectifying inspection) |

### Pillar 4 — Decision on **Centralized vs. Decentralized Inspection**

| Aspect | Centralized | Decentralized |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | One central QC lab | Multiple inspection stations on shop floor |
| **Authority** | QC manager | Production supervisor |
| **Feedback Speed** | Slow (delays in routing) | Fast (immediate) |
| **Best For** | Complex lab tests (chemical, metallurgical) | Visual/dimensional checks, SPC charts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Model (most common in industry):** Centralized for *lab tests*, decentralized for *shop-floor control charts*. KTU often tests this in 3-mark questions.

## 2.2 Economic Trade-Off in Inspection Planning

The total cost of a quality system has three components:

$$ C_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Failure}} $$

For inspection planning, we focus on **Appraisal Cost** ($C_A$) vs **Failure Cost** ($C_F$):

- **Appraisal cost** ($C_A$): cost of inspectors, equipment, testing time $\propto$ fraction inspected $f$.
- **Internal failure cost** ($C_{IF}$): scrap, rework $\propto$ defectives *not caught* before shipping.
- **External failure cost** ($C_{EF}$): warranty, recall $\propto$ defectives reaching customer.

$$ C_{\text{Inspection}} = C_A \cdot f + C_{IF} \cdot p(1-f) \cdot (1-P_a) + C_{EF} \cdot p(1-f) \cdot P_a $$

The **optimal inspection fraction** $f^*$ balances $C_A$ against failure costs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{Total}} = C_A + C_P + C_F$ | Total quality cost (Juran's trilogy) | 2 |
| 2 | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Average Outgoing Quality (rectifying plan) | 7–14 |
| 3 | $\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \text{AOQ}(p)$ | Maximum AOQ — worst case after rectification | 7 |
| 4 | $\text{ATI} = n + (N-n)(1-P_a)$ | Average Total Inspection per lot | 7 |
| 5 | $\text{ASN} = \dfrac{n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_1}{1 - P_1}$ | Average Sample Number (double sampling) | 7 |
| 6 | $f^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_F \cdot p}$ | Optimal fraction to inspect (continuous case) | 7 |
| 7 | $E(L) = N \cdot p \cdot P_a$ | Expected number of defectives *passed* per lot | 3–7 |
| 8 | $\alpha = P(\text{reject} \vert p = p_1)$ | Producer's risk | 3 |
| 9 | $\beta = P(\text{accept} \vert p = p_2)$ | Consumer's risk | 3 |
| 10 | $P_d = 1 - P_a$ | Probability of rejection | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The formula $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a (N-n)}{N}$ assumes **rectifying inspection** — i.e., all defectives in the sample are replaced with good ones. KTU students frequently lose marks by forgetting the $(N-n)$ correction.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Inspection planning is foundational in:
- **Automotive industry** (IATF 16949 mandates inspection plans for every Critical-to-Quality characteristic).
- **Pharmaceutical industry** (FDA's Process Validation Guidance requires staged inspection: raw → in-process → finished → stability).
- **Semiconductor manufacturing** (wafer-level inspection with sampling at each layer).
- **Reliability engineering** (skip-lot plans for MIL-STD-105 / MIL-STD-414).
- **Supply chain management** (vendor inspection plans, AQL-based acceptance).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

**Statement:** For a rectifying single-sampling plan with lot size $N$, sample size $n$, acceptance number $c$, the AOQ is the long-run fraction defective in the output stream.

**Derivation:**

Consider a single lot of size $N$ with incoming fraction defective $p$.

- Number of defectives in the lot (incoming) = $N \cdot p$.
- After sampling, the lot is either **accepted** or **rejected**.

**Case 1 — Lot Accepted (Probability = $P_a$):**
- All $N$ items are shipped.
- Number of defectives shipped = $N \cdot p$.
- Output size = $N$.

**Case 2 — Lot Rejected (Probability = $1 - P_a$):**
- Assume **rectification** — the rejected lot undergoes 100% screening, all defectives are replaced by good ones.
- Number of defectives shipped = $0$.
- Output size = $N$ (after replacement).

**Expected number of defectives shipped (per lot):**

$$ E(D) = N \cdot p \cdot P_a + 0 \cdot (1 - P_a) = N \cdot p \cdot P_a $$

**Expected number of good items shipped per lot:**

$$ E(G) = N \cdot (1 - p) \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - p) \cdot (1 - P_a) = N \cdot (1 - p) $$

**Average Outgoing Quality:**

$$ \text{AOQ} = \dfrac{E(D)}{E(G)} = \dfrac{N \cdot p \cdot P_a}{N \cdot (1 - p)} $$

This reduces (for small $p$) to the **classical AOQ formula**:

$$ \boxed{\text{AOQ} \approx \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N - n)}{N}} $$

> **Note on the $(N-n)$ term:** This refined form accounts for the fact that during sampling, the $n$ inspected items have *already* been removed; if defectives are replaced, the *remaining* $(N-n)$ items may still contain $(N-n) \cdot p$ defectives that are accepted without further inspection. This is the form KTU expects in 14-mark problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Total Inspection (ATI)

**Statement:** ATI is the expected number of items inspected per lot, accounting for both the sample and the 100% screening of rejected lots.

**Derivation:**

- If lot is **accepted** (probability $P_a$): only the $n$ sampled items are inspected.
- If lot is **rejected** (probability $1 - P_a$): the entire lot of $N$ items is inspected under rectification.

$$ \text{ATI} = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) $$

$$ \boxed{\text{ATI} = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)} $$

**Special cases:**

- **Tight plan** ($P_a \approx 0$ for all $p$): ATI $\to N$ (100% inspection).
- **Loose plan** ($P_a \approx 1$ for all $p$): ATI $\to n$ (only sampling).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[Problem Statement]:** A lot of $N = 1000$ items is submitted to a rectifying single-sampling plan with $n = 50$ and $c = 1$. If the incoming fraction defective is $p = 0.04$, compute the AOQ and ATI. Assume $P_a$ is given by the binomial table as $0.40$ for this $p$.

**Step 1 — Compute AOQ:**

$$ \text{AOQ} = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 0.40 \cdot (1000 - 50)}{1000} $$

$$ \text{AOQ} = \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 950}{1000} = \dfrac{15.2}{1000} = 0.0152 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{AOQ} = 0.0152 \text{ or } 1.52\%} $$

**Step 2 — Compute ATI:**

$$ \text{ATI} = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a) = 50 + (1000 - 50)(1 - 0.40) $$

$$ \text{ATI} = 50 + 950 \cdot 0.60 = 50 + 570 = 620 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{ATI} = 620 \text{ items inspected per lot (on average)}} $$

**Step 3 — Interpret:** With a 4% incoming defect rate, the **output** defect rate drops to **1.52%** because the rectifying plan catches and replaces most defectives. The inspector must, on average, examine **620 items out of 1000** to achieve this — meaning sampling with rectification is cheaper than 100% inspection (1000 items) for moderate defect rates.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Inspection Plan to Use

| Scenario | Cost of Defective ($C_F$) | Cost of Inspection ($C_A$) | Defect Rate ($p$) | Recommended Plan |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace turbine blade | Very High (₹10,00,000) | Moderate (₹5,000) | Low ($<1\%$) | 100% inspection + automated vision |
| Pharmaceutical tablets | Very High (life-risk) | High | Very Low | 100% electronic + sampling |
| Light bulb filaments | Moderate | Very High (destructive) | Low | **Sequential sampling** |
| Automobile fasteners | Moderate | Low | Moderate | **Single sampling with rectification** |
| Bulk cement bags | Low | Low | High ($>5\%$) | **Double sampling** with AOQL constraint |
| Vendor of trusted long-term supplier | Low | Moderate | Very Low | **Skip-lot inspection** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inspection Planning Decision Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **logical decision flow** an inspection planner follows when designing a quality control system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Product Spec Ready]) --> A1{Is Inspection Cost Greater than Failure Cost?}
    A1 -- Yes --> A2[Use Sampling Inspection]
    A1 -- No --> A3[Use 100 percent Inspection]
    A2 --> B1{Is Test Destructive?}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Use Sequential or Double Sampling]
    B1 -- No --> B3[Use Single or Multiple Sampling]
    B2 --> C1[Decide Sampling Plan n and c]
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1{Compute AOQL Requirement}
    D1 -- Tight --> D2[Small n, Small c]
    D1 -- Loose --> D3[Large n, Large c]
    D2 --> E1[Choose Centralized or Decentralized]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 -- Complex Lab Tests --> F1[Centralized QC Lab]
    E1 -- Shop Floor Checks --> F2[Decentralized Stations]
    F1 --> G1([Implement Rectifying Plan])
    F2 --> G1
    A3 --> E1
    G1 --> H1([Monitor AOQ and ATI Monthly])
    H1 --> I1{AOQ within Limit?}
    I1 -- No --> A1
    I1 -- Yes --> J1([Plan Approved])
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The planner first compares *inspection cost* to *failure cost*. If inspection is expensive, sampling is preferred. The plan is then refined based on test destructiveness, AOQL targets, and organizational structure. Continuous monitoring (AOQ, ATI) ensures the plan adapts to process drift.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inspection Planning System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[Input Layer]
        INa[Product Specification]
        INb[Process Capability Data]
        INc[Cost Data: CA, CF]
        INd[Customer AQL Requirement]
    end

    subgraph PR1[Planning Engine]
        PRa[Cost Tradeoff Analyzer]
        PRb[Sampling Plan Selector]
        PRc[Risk Calculator alpha and beta]
    end

    subgraph EX1[Execution Layer]
        EXa[Raw Material Inspection Station]
        EXb[In Process Control Station]
        EXc[Final Goods Inspection Station]
        EXd[Rectification and Rework Cell]
    end

    subgraph MO1[Monitoring Layer]
        MOa[AOQ Curve Generator]
        MOb[ATI Curve Generator]
        MOc[OC Curve Plotter]
    end

    INa --> PRa
    INb --> PRa
    INc --> PRa
    INd --> PRb
    PRa --> PRb
    PRb --> PRc
    PRc --> EXa
    PRc --> EXb
    PRc --> EXc
    EXa --> EXb --> EXc
    EXc --> EXd
    EXa --> MOa
    EXb --> MOa
    EXc --> MOa
    EXa --> MOb
    EXb --> MOb
    EXc --> MOb
    EXa --> MOc
    EXb --> MOc
    EXc --> MOc
    MOa --> PRa
    MOb --> PRa
    MOc --> PRa
```

> **Architecture interpretation:** The system is a **closed-loop feedback controller** — the *Planning Engine* generates the plan, the *Execution Layer* implements it, and the *Monitoring Layer* feeds performance metrics (AOQ, ATI, OC curve) back to the *Planning Engine* for continuous improvement (PDCA cycle).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between centralized and decentralized inspection systems. Under what conditions is each preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Centralized Inspection | Decentralized Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| Location | One central QC laboratory | Multiple stations on shop floor |
| Authority | Quality Control Manager | Production Supervisor |
| Feedback Speed | Slower (transport delay) | Immediate (real-time) |
| Best For | Destructive tests, complex chemical/metallurgical analysis | Visual checks, dimensional checks, SPC chart updates |

**Centralized** is preferred when inspection requires **expensive equipment or skilled analysts** (e.g., spectrometry, tensile testing). **Decentralized** is preferred for **high-volume, fast-paced, visual or dimensional** checks where the operator can feed the data into control charts in real time. *(1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for conditions.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)? Why is it called a "limit"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** is the expected fraction defective in the output stream *after* rectifying inspection. As $p$ varies from 0 to 1, AOQ first increases, reaches a **maximum**, and then decreases. The **AOQL** is defined as:

$$ \text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \text{AOQ}(p) $$

It is called a "limit" because it represents the **worst-case** average quality that the consumer can ever expect, regardless of how poor the incoming quality becomes. It is a **guaranteed upper bound** on the average fraction defective leaving the inspection system. *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for worst-case interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** *Explain the four major decisions involved in inspection planning. For each decision, state the key criteria that guide the choice.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A lot of $N = 2000$ items is inspected using a rectifying single-sampling plan with $n = 100$ and $c = 2$. The incoming fraction defective is $p = 0.03$. Given that $P_a = 0.42$ for this $p$ value:*
- *(i) Compute the AOQ and the ATI.* **(4 marks)**
- *(ii) Interpret the practical meaning of your results. Would you recommend this plan?* **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The four major decisions in inspection planning are:

1. **Decision 1 — Where to inspect (3 typical locations):**
   - At raw material receipt (vendor inspection) → catches defects at the source.
   - At in-process stages (after each critical operation) → catches drift early.
   - At finished-goods stage (before shipment) → final gate.
   - **Key criteria:** *Cost of failure escalates* the later the defect is found; early detection is preferred when rework cost is high. *(2 marks)*

2. **Decision 2 — How much to inspect (100% vs sampling):**
   - **100% inspection** when defectives are catastrophic, easily detectable, and lots are small.
   - **Sampling inspection** when inspection is destructive, expensive, or the process is highly capable.
   - **Key criterion:** Compare cost of inspection $C_A$ with cost of passing defectives $C_F$. *(2 marks)*

3. **Decision 3 — Type of sampling plan:**
   - Single, double, multiple, or sequential.
   - **Key criterion:** Test destructiveness, lot size variability, ASN requirements. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Decision 4 — Centralized vs decentralized inspection:**
   - **Key criterion:** Complexity of the test, speed of feedback required, and equipment cost. *(1.5 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $N = 2000$, $n = 100$, $c = 2$, $p = 0.03$, $P_a = 0.42$.

**(i) Computation:**

$$ \text{AOQ} = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \dfrac{0.03 \cdot 0.42 \cdot (2000 - 100)}{2000} $$

$$ \text{AOQ} = \dfrac{0.03 \cdot 0.42 \cdot 1900}{2000} = \dfrac{23.94}{2000} = 0.01197 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{AOQ} = 0.01197 \approx 1.20\%} \quad \text{(3 marks for AOQ + 1 mark for ATI)} $$

$$ \text{ATI} = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a) = 100 + (2000 - 100)(1 - 0.42) $$

$$ \text{ATI} = 100 + 1900 \cdot 0.58 = 100 + 1102 = 1202 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{ATI} = 1202 \text{ items per lot (on average)}} \quad \text{(1 mark for ATI)} $$

**(ii) Interpretation (3 marks):**

- The output fraction defective drops from $3\%$ to about $1.2\%$, a **60% reduction** in defectives reaching the customer.
- On average, **1202 items must be inspected** out of 2000 to achieve this — significantly less than 100% inspection (2000 items), saving roughly **40% of inspection effort**.
- The plan is **recommended** if $1.2\%$ outgoing quality meets customer AQL. If the customer demands $\le 0.5\%$, the plan must be tightened (lower $c$ or larger $n$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** *Discuss the economic model of inspection planning. Derive the condition for the optimal inspection fraction $f^*$ that minimizes total cost per unit produced.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A manufacturing process has the following parameters:*
- *Cost of inspecting one unit: $C_A = ₹5$*
- *Cost of a defective reaching a customer: $C_F = ₹500$*
- *Incoming fraction defective: $p = 0.02$*

*Find:*
- *(i) The optimal fraction $f^*$ to inspect.* **(2 marks)**
- *(ii) The minimum total quality cost per unit.* **(2 marks)**
- *(iii) If the firm chooses 100% inspection, what is the extra cost per unit compared to the optimum?* **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Economic Model Setup (3 marks):**

Let $f$ = fraction of units inspected ($0 \le f \le 1$).
- Total cost per unit = (Appraisal cost) + (Expected failure cost).
- Appraisal cost per unit = $C_A \cdot f$.
- Failure cost per unit = $C_F \cdot p \cdot (1 - f)$ [a fraction $1-f$ escapes inspection, and $p$ of those are defective, each costing $C_F$].

$$ C_{\text{Total}}(f) = C_A \cdot f + C_F \cdot p \cdot (1 - f) $$

**Optimization (2 marks):**

Take derivative with respect to $f$ and set to zero:

$$ \dfrac{dC_{\text{Total}}}{df} = C_A - C_F \cdot p = 0 $$

$$ C_A = C_F \cdot p $$

**Optimal Inspection Fraction (2 marks):**

$$ \boxed{f^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_F \cdot p}} $$

provided $C_A \le C_F \cdot p$; otherwise $f^* = 0$ (skip inspection).

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $C_A = ₹5$, $C_F = ₹500$, $p = 0.02$.

**(i) Optimal fraction (2 marks):**

$$ f^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_F \cdot p} = 1 - \dfrac{5}{500 \cdot 0.02} = 1 - \dfrac{5}{10} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 $$

$$ \boxed{f^* = 0.5 \text{ (inspect 50\% of units)}} $$

**(ii) Minimum total cost per unit (2 marks):**

$$ C_{\text{Total}}(f^*) = C_A \cdot f^* + C_F \cdot p \cdot (1 - f^*) = 5 \cdot 0.5 + 500 \cdot 0.02 \cdot 0.5 $$

$$ C_{\text{Total}} = 2.5 + 5 = ₹7.50 $$

$$ \boxed{C_{\text{Total, min}} = ₹7.50 \text{ per unit}} $$

**(iii) Cost at 100% inspection (3 marks):**

$$ C_{\text{Total}}(f = 1) = C_A \cdot 1 + C_F \cdot p \cdot 0 = 5 + 0 = ₹5.00 $$

Wait — at $f=1$ the failure cost is **zero** (no defective escapes), but inspection cost is $C_A = ₹5$. The **extra cost** of 100% vs optimum is:

$$ \Delta C = C_{\text{Total}}(f=1) - C_{\text{Total}}(f^*) = 5 - 7.5 = -₹2.50 $$

**Surprise result:** At these parameter values, $C_F \cdot p = 10 \ge C_A = 5$, so 100% inspection is *actually cheaper* than the optimal $f^* = 0.5$. The economic model prescribes $f^* = 1$ in this case. This is a classic KTU trap: the *constrained* optimum must respect the upper bound $f \le 1$.

$$ \boxed{\text{Correct conclusion: } f^*_{\text{constrained}} = 1 \text{ (100% inspection); cost} = ₹5/\text{unit; saving} = ₹2.50/\text{unit}} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting rectification:** The AOQ formula $\dfrac{p \cdot P_a (N-n)}{N}$ assumes *rectifying inspection*. Without rectification, AOQ is simply $p \cdot P_a$ — students lose **2–3 marks** by omitting the rectification clause.
> 2. **Confusing AOQ with AOQL:** AOQ is a *function of $p$*; AOQL is the *maximum* of that function. Drawing the AOQ curve and marking the peak is worth full marks in graphical questions.
> 3. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** $\alpha$ (Producer's risk) is the probability of *rejecting* a good lot at $p = p_1$ (AQL). $\beta$ (Consumer's risk) is the probability of *accepting* a bad lot at $p = p_2$ (LTPD). Swap them and you lose 3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Ignoring the constraint $f^* \le 1$:** In the economic model, the unconstrained optimum $f^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_F \cdot p}$ must be **clipped to $[0, 1]$**. KTU papers frequently test this corner-case solution.
> 5. **Not drawing the OC curve:** When asked to "comment on the discriminating power" of a plan, always sketch the **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** — its steepness indicates plan sensitivity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Inspection Planning (Module 1)**

- ✅ Inspection planning is a **strategic precursor** to sampling plan selection and control charting — never an afterthought.
- ✅ The **four core decisions** are: *Where? How much? What type? Centralized or decentralized?*
- ✅ **100% inspection** is justified when $C_F \cdot p$ (expected failure cost per unit) $\ge C_A$ (inspection cost per unit).
- ✅ **Rectifying inspection** replaces all defectives in rejected lots with good ones — required assumption for the classical AOQ formula.
- ✅ Key formula trio to memorize: **AOQ, ATI, AOQL** — they always appear together in KTU numericals.
- ✅ **AOQ** is a *function* of $p$; **AOQL** is the *maximum* of that function (geometric peak of the AOQ curve).
- ✅ **ATI** measures inspection effort per lot; **ASN** measures average sample size for multi-stage plans.
- ✅ Producer's risk $\alpha$ ↔ AQL ($p_1$); Consumer's risk $\beta$ ↔ LTPD ($p_2$). Never swap them.
- ✅ **Centralized** inspection for complex/destructive lab tests; **decentralized** for shop-floor visual/dimensional checks.
- ✅ Economic optimum $f^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_F \cdot p}$, **clipped** to $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ Sequential sampling is **mandatory** when inspection is destructive and expensive (ammunition, fuses, light bulbs).
- ✅ Skip-lot plans are used for *trusted* suppliers with proven long-term quality history (CSP-1 plans under MIL-STD-105).
- ✅ KTU's *favorite* 14-mark structure: **Conceptual question on the four decisions (7 marks) + Numerical on AOQ/ATI (7 marks)**.
- ✅ Always **draw the OC curve** for any plan you discuss — the steepness around $p_1$ and $p_2$ defines the plan's discrimination.
- ✅ AOQL-based design: choose the plan whose AOQL just satisfies the consumer's worst-case quality requirement.
- ✅ AQL is **not** a defect rate to *achieve* — it is the **maximum acceptable** defect rate at the *producer's* risk level.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Budget
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Budget

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Quality Budget** is a quantitative financial plan that allocates monetary resources across the various categories of quality-related activities within an organization for a defined planning period (typically quarterly or annually). It is a forward-looking instrument derived from the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** framework, structured around the classical **PAF Model** (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) propounded by quality gurus such as **Joseph Juran** and later formalized by **Philip Crosby** and the **American Society for Quality (ASQ)**.

Mathematically, the Total Quality Cost (TQC) function governing the Quality Budget is expressed as:

$$
TQC \;=\; C_{P} \;+\; C_{A} \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}
$$

where each component represents a distinct allocation bucket of the Quality Budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Quality Budget is the financial instrument that translates the philosophical goals of Total Quality Management (TQM) into measurable, controllable rupee/dollar allocations. Without a Quality Budget, quality initiatives become unfunded rhetoric.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Family Monthly Budget" Analogy:**

Imagine a household where the father earns ₹1,00,000 per month. Before spending, he creates a **budget**:
- **₹15,000** → Prevention (insurance premiums, health check-ups, children's tuition) — *prevent problems*
- **₹5,000** → Appraisal (car servicing, doctor visits) — *detect problems early*
- **₹10,000** → Internal Failure (food wastage, phone repairs) — *failures caught at home*
- **₹8,000** → External Failure (traffic fines, late payment penalties) — *failures escaping home*

If the family notices **Prevention spending is only ₹2,000**, the father intuitively increases it to ₹15,000, knowing it will slash future failure costs. This exact rebalancing act — driven by data — is what an industrial **Quality Budget** accomplishes at the factory floor level.

### 1.3 Quality Cost Categories — The PAF Model

The Quality Budget is partitioned into **four mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE)** cost categories:

> [!NOTE]
> **PAF Model Categorization (Standard KTU Reference)**

| **Budget Category** | **Symbol** | **Nature of Cost** | **Examples** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prevention Costs** | $C_P$ | Proactive / Discretionary | Quality training, supplier capability programs, design reviews, statistical process control (SPC) installation |
| **Appraisal Costs** | $C_A$ | Detection / Measurement | Incoming inspection, in-process testing, calibration of gauges, final QC audits |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | $C_{IF}$ | Reactive (before shipment) | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, defect analysis, machine downtime due to defects |
| **External Failure Costs** | $C_{EF}$ | Reactive (after shipment) | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, legal liabilities, lost goodwill |

The first two ($C_P$, $C_A$) are often termed **"Costs of Conformance"**, while the latter two ($C_{IF}$, $C_{EF}$) are termed **"Costs of Non-Conformance"**.

### 1.4 Physical & Administrative Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Benchmarks (used in KTU numericals):**
> - Optimal prevention-to-failure cost ratio target: **$C_P : (C_{IF}+C_{EF}) \approx 1:1$** (Juran's principle)
> - Quality cost as % of sales for world-class manufacturers: **2.5% – 5%**
> - Quality cost as % of sales for poor-quality firms: **15% – 40%**
> - **Baldrige-indexed** benchmark: External failure costs should be **< 25%** of total quality cost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Decomposition

**Step 1 — Recognition of Hidden Costs:**
Traditional accounting treats quality costs as **"overhead"** or hides them inside COGS (Cost of Goods Sold). SQC demands they be **isolated and made visible** through a Quality Cost Report (QCR), which feeds the Quality Budget.

**Step 2 — PAF Allocation Logic:**
Management allocates funds based on the **dominant failure type** observed in the prior period:
- If scrap rates > 5% → Shift funds from $C_A$ → $C_P$
- If warranty claims rise > 10% YoY → Shift funds from $C_{IF}$ → $C_P$
- If customer audit failures occur → Boost $C_A$ (third-party audits)

**Step 3 — The Optimum Quality Level (OQL) Search:**
Plot total quality cost $TQC$ as a function of conformance level $q$. The optimum occurs at the **minimum point** of the curve where the marginal cost of additional prevention equals the marginal savings in failure costs.

**Step 4 — Budgetary Approval Cycle:**
Quality Budget is approved by top management, executed by the Quality Assurance (QA) department, and reviewed monthly via the **Quality Cost Variance (QCV)** report:

$$
QCV \;=\; (BQ_{i} - AQ_{i})
$$

where $BQ_i$ is the budgeted amount and $AQ_i$ is the actual spent for cost category $i$.

**Step 5 — Continuous Re-Engineering (Kaizen):**
The Quality Budget is a **living document**, revisited every quarter. As the organization matures, the typical trend is:
- $C_P$ and $C_A$ rise initially (investment phase)
- $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ fall sharply (maturity phase)
- $TQC$ overall declines as the **optimum quality cost curve** is approached.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

In manufacturing industries (automotive, electronics, aerospace), the Quality Budget directly influences:
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — a key KPI on the Balanced Scorecard
- **Six Sigma project selection** — projects are prioritized based on the COPQ they can recover
- **ISO 9001:2015** clause 7.1.5 (Monitor & Measuring Resources) compliance
- **Supplier Quality Assurance (SQA)** — incoming material testing budget
- **Warranty reserves** — actuarial projections for $C_{EF}$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examinations**

| **Formula ID** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **F-1** | $TQC = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Quality Cost identity | ₹ or \$ |
| **F-2** | $COQ = TQC / \text{Sales Revenue}$ | Cost of Quality as % of sales | % |
| **F-3** | $COPQ = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Cost of Poor Quality (failure-only) | ₹ or \$ |
| **F-4** | $QCV = BQ - AQ$ | Quality Cost Variance (per category) | ₹ or \$ |
| **F-5** | $ROI_{Q} = \dfrac{\Delta COPQ}{\Delta C_{P}}$ | Return on Quality Investment | Ratio |
| **F-6** | $\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dq} = 0$ at optimum | First-order optimality condition | — |
| **F-7** | $C_P^{\text{opt}} = C_A^{\text{opt}} \;\; \text{(at maturity)}$ | Juran's mature-firm balance | ₹ or \$ |
| **F-8** | $QCL = \dfrac{C_P + C_A}{TQC} \times 100$ | Conformance cost loading index | % |
| **F-9** | $EFL = \dfrac{C_{EF}}{TQC} \times 100$ | External Failure Loading index | % |
| **F-10** | $B = \sum_{i=1}^{4} B_i + B_{admin}$ | Total Quality Budget with admin overhead | ₹ or \$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Warning (Avoid in LaTeX markdown tables):** Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead of the bare pipe symbol `\| x \|` inside markdown tables to prevent parser breakage.

### 2.4 Crosby's "Zero Defects" Quality Budget Doctrine

**Philip B. Crosby (1979)** in *Quality is Free* proposed that the *only* acceptable Quality Budget allocation target is:

$$
\text{Conformance Cost Loading} \;\rightarrow\; 100\% \quad \text{as} \quad C_{IF}, C_{EF} \rightarrow 0
$$

Crosby's *Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)* was the conceptual opposite of *Price of Conformance (POC)*:

$$
PONC \;=\; C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

$$
POC \;=\; C_P + C_A
$$

$$
TQC \;=\; POC \;+\; PONC
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Total Quality Cost Function

In classical SQC theory, the Total Quality Cost as a function of quality level $q$ (where $q \in [0, 1]$ represents the proportion of conforming units) can be derived from first principles.

**Assumption Block (Standard Textbook):**
- Prevention cost $C_P(q)$ rises exponentially as $q \rightarrow 1$ (because achieving 100% conformance demands infinite investment)
- Appraisal cost $C_A(q)$ is approximately constant, $C_A = k_A$
- Internal failure cost $C_{IF}(q)$ falls linearly with $q$: $C_{IF}(q) = k_{IF}(1 - q)$
- External failure cost $C_{EF}(q)$ falls linearly with $q$: $C_{EF}(q) = k_{EF}(1 - q)$

**Derivation Step 1 — Total Quality Cost Identity:**

$$
TQC(q) \;=\; C_P(q) \;+\; C_A(q) \;+\; C_{IF}(q) \;+\; C_{EF}(q)
$$

Substituting each component:

$$
TQC(q) \;=\; \dfrac{k_P}{q^2} \;+\; k_A \;+\; k_{IF}(1 - q) \;+\; k_{EF}(1 - q)
$$

**Derivation Step 2 — Group Constants for Clarity:**

Let $k_F = k_{IF} + k_{EF}$ (combined failure rate constant):

$$
TQC(q) \;=\; \dfrac{k_P}{q^2} \;+\; k_A \;+\; k_F (1 - q)
$$

**Derivation Step 3 — First-Order Optimality Condition:**

Differentiate $TQC(q)$ with respect to $q$ and equate to zero (FOC for unconstrained minimum):

$$
\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dq} \;=\; -\dfrac{2k_P}{q^3} \;-\; k_F \;=\; 0
$$

Solving for the optimum quality level $q^{*}$:

$$
q^{*} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{-2k_P}{k_F}\right)^{1/3}
$$

Since $k_P, k_F > 0$, and we require $q > 0$, we discard the negative real root. The economically meaningful optimum is:

$$
\boxed{\;q^{*} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{2 k_P}{k_F}\right)^{1/3}\;}
$$

**Derivation Step 4 — Verify Minimum via Second Derivative:**

$$
\dfrac{d^2(TQC)}{dq^2} \;=\; \dfrac{6 k_P}{q^4} \;>\; 0 \quad \forall \; q > 0
$$

The second derivative is positive → the FOC yields a **minimum** (not a maximum). ✓ Optimum confirmed.

**Derivation Step 5 — Minimum Total Quality Cost:**

$$
TQC_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{k_P}{(q^{*})^2} \;+\; k_A \;+\; k_F (1 - q^{*})
$$

Substituting $q^{*}$:

$$
TQC_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{k_P \cdot k_F^{2/3}}{(2k_P)^{2/3}} \;+\; k_A \;+\; k_F \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{2k_P}{k_F}\right)^{1/3}\right)
$$

Simplifying the first term:

$$
\dfrac{k_P \cdot k_F^{2/3}}{2^{2/3} \cdot k_P^{2/3}} \;=\; \dfrac{k_P^{1/3} \cdot k_F^{2/3}}{2^{2/3}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;TQC_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{k_P^{1/3} \, k_F^{2/3}}{2^{2/3}} \;+\; k_A \;+\; k_F \;-\; (2k_P k_F^2)^{1/3}\;}
$$

This is the **Economic Balance Equation** — the foundation of the Quality Budget at the firm-wide optimum.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Numerical

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

A precision bearing manufacturer has compiled the following annual quality cost data:
- Prevention cost: ₹4,00,000
- Appraisal cost: ₹2,50,000
- Internal failure cost: ₹6,00,000
- External failure cost: ₹3,50,000

Annual sales = ₹1,00,00,000. Determine: (a) Total Quality Cost, (b) COPQ, (c) COQ as % of sales, (d) Quality Cost Variance if budgeted $C_P$ was ₹5,00,000.

**Solution Step (a) — Total Quality Cost:**

$$
TQC \;=\; C_P \;+\; C_A \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}
$$

$$
TQC \;=\; 4,00,000 \;+\; 2,50,000 \;+\; 6,00,000 \;+\; 3,50,000
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TQC \;=\; ₹16,00,000\;}
$$

**Solution Step (b) — Cost of Poor Quality (Failure-Only):**

$$
COPQ \;=\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF} \;=\; 6,00,000 \;+\; 3,50,000
$$

$$
\boxed{\;COPQ \;=\; ₹9,50,000\;}
$$

**Solution Step (c) — Cost of Quality as % of Sales:**

$$
COQ \;=\; \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{16,00,000}{1,00,00,000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\;COQ \;=\; 16\%\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This figure (16%) is in the **"poor-quality firm"** category (benchmark: 15–40%). The firm should reallocate budget toward prevention to lower $C_{IF}$.

**Solution Step (d) — Quality Cost Variance:**

$$
QCV_P \;=\; BQ_P \;-\; AQ_P \;=\; 5,00,000 \;-\; 4,00,000
$$

$$
\boxed{\;QCV_P \;=\; +₹1,00,000\;\text{(favourable variance — under-spent prevention)}\;}
$$

**Analytical Insight:**
A favourable $QCV$ on prevention is actually *unfavourable strategically* — it indicates insufficient prevention investment, which will surface as higher $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ in subsequent periods. KTU examiners often test this interpretation.

### 3.3 The Juran Optimization Trade-off Table

| **Quality Level $q$** | **$C_P$ (₹)** | **$C_A$ (₹)** | **$C_{IF}$ (₹)** | **$C_{EF}$ (₹)** | **$TQC$ (₹)** |
| :---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 0.70 | 50,000 | 2,00,000 | 9,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 17,50,000 |
| 0.80 | 1,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 13,00,000 |
| 0.85 | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 4,50,000 | 3,00,000 | 11,50,000 |
| **0.90** | **4,00,000** | **2,00,000** | **3,00,000** | **2,00,000** | **11,00,000** ✓ |
| 0.95 | 8,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 1,00,000 | 12,50,000 |
| 0.98 | 20,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 60,000 | 40,000 | 23,00,000 |

**Reading the table:** The optimum is at $q^{*} = 0.90$ with $TQC_{\min} = ₹11,00,000$. Above this, prevention costs escalate super-linearly (note: $C_P$ jumps from ₹4L → ₹8L → ₹20L). Below 0.85, failure costs dominate. **This is the Quality Budget inflection point.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quality Budget — PAF Allocation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ANNUAL QUALITY BUDGET] --> B[CONFORMANCE COSTS]
    A --> C[NON CONFORMANCE COSTS]
    A --> D[ADMIN OVERHEAD Badmin]

    B --> E[PREVENTION CP]
    B --> F[APPRAISAL CA]

    C --> G[INTERNAL FAILURE CIF]
    C --> H[EXTERNAL FAILURE CEF]

    E --> E1[Quality Training]
    E --> E2[Design Reviews]
    E --> E3[SPC Programs]
    E --> E4[Supplier Dev]

    F --> F1[Incoming Inspection]
    F --> F2[In Process Testing]
    F --> F3[Gauge Calibration]
    F --> F4[Final QC]

    G --> G1[Scrap]
    G --> G2[Rework]
    G --> G3[Re Inspection]
    G --> G4[Defect Analysis]

    H --> H1[Warranty Claims]
    H --> H2[Returns Recalls]
    H --> H3[Field Service]
    H --> H4[Legal Liability]

    D --> D1[QA Salaries]
    D --> D2[Quality Systems Software]
    D --> D3[Audit Costs]
```

### 4.2 Quality Budget Approval & Control Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Prior Period Actual Costs] --> P2[Quality Cost Report QCR]
    P2 --> P3[Trend Analysis Pareto]
    P3 --> P4[Management Review]
    P4 --> P5[New Quality Budget Allocation]
    P5 --> P6[Departmental Sub Budgets]
    P6 --> P7[Monthly Execution]
    P7 --> P8[Variance Analysis QCV]
    P8 --> P2
```

### 4.3 Cost-of-Quality Behavioral Model (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Quality Level q target]
        I2[Sales Revenue S]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS
        M1[Prevention Cost Model CP q]
        M2[Appraisal Cost Model CA q]
        M3[Internal Failure Model CIF q]
        M4[External Failure Model CEF q]
        S1[Summer: TQC sum]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[TQC optimum q star]
        O2[COQ percent TQC by S]
        O3[Budget Allocation Vectors]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I1 --> M2
    I1 --> M3
    I1 --> M4
    M1 --> S1
    M2 --> S1
    M3 --> S1
    M4 --> S1
    S1 --> O1
    I2 --> O2
    S1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Quality Budget Reallocation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Monthly QCR Review] --> R1{Scrap Rate greater than 5 percent}
    R1 -- YES --> X1[Boost CP by 20 percent]
    R1 -- NO --> R2{Warranty Claims up by 10 percent YoY}
    R2 -- YES --> X2[Boost CP and CA jointly]
    R2 -- NO --> R3{External Failure greater than 25 percent of TQC}
    R3 -- YES --> X3[Boost CA third party audits]
    R3 -- NO --> X4[Maintain Current Allocation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

Define the term **"Quality Budget"** and list the four classical categories of Cost of Quality (COQ) as per the PAF model.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Quality Budget** is a forward-looking financial plan that allocates an organization's monetary resources across the various cost categories of quality for a defined period (typically one year). It is derived from the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** framework and is used by management to plan, control, and optimize expenditures on quality-related activities.

The four PAF-model categories are:

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_P$)** — costs of proactive measures to prevent defects (e.g., training, design reviews, supplier development).
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$)** — costs of detecting defects through inspection, testing, and auditing.
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$)** — costs incurred before delivery due to non-conforming products (e.g., scrap, rework, re-inspection).
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$)** — costs incurred after delivery (e.g., warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal liabilities).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (1 mark each):** Definition — 1 mark; 4 categories listed with one-line description — 2 marks (½ × 4).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Frequently Asked]**

Distinguish between **"Price of Conformance (POC)"** and **"Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)"** as introduced by Philip Crosby. Why is reducing PONC considered the primary objective of a Quality Budget?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Price of Conformance (POC)** | **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Composition** | $POC = C_P + C_A$ | $PONC = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ |
| **Nature** | Investment (proactive) | Penalty (reactive) |
| **Visibility in accounts** | Often booked as overhead | Often hidden in COGS |
| **Customer impact** | Indirect (capability building) | Direct (dissatisfaction, warranty) |

**Why PONC reduction is the primary Quality Budget objective:**
PONC represents **wasteful expenditure** — money spent correcting mistakes that should never have occurred. Every rupee of PONC recovered adds directly to profit. In contrast, POC is a *planned investment* whose return is measured by PONC reduction. Crosby's dictum *"Quality is Free"* rests on the principle that $PONC \rightarrow 0$ generates a profit dividend exceeding POC investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Tabular distinction — 2 marks; Justification — 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Practice (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A medium-scale engineering firm reports the following annual quality cost data: Prevention = ₹3,00,000; Appraisal = ₹2,00,000; Internal Failure = ₹5,00,000; External Failure = ₹4,00,000. Annual sales revenue = ₹80,00,000. Calculate: (i) Total Quality Cost (TQC), (ii) Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ), (iii) Cost of Quality as a percentage of sales (COQ%), and (iv) the ratio of Conformance Costs to Non-Conformance Costs. Comment on the firm's quality maturity level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain Juran's classical **Cost-of-Quality Behaviour Model**. Show how the **optimum quality level** $q^{*}$ is mathematically determined. State two management decisions that can be drawn from such a model. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total Quality Cost:**

$$
TQC \;=\; C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

$$
TQC \;=\; 3,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 5,00,000 + 4,00,000 \;=\; \boxed{₹14,00,000}
$$

**(ii) Cost of Poor Quality (Failure-Only):**

$$
COPQ \;=\; C_{IF} + C_{EF} \;=\; 5,00,000 + 4,00,000 \;=\; \boxed{₹9,00,000}
$$

**(iii) Cost of Quality as % of Sales:**

$$
COQ\% \;=\; \frac{TQC}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{14,00,000}{80,00,000} \times 100 \;=\; \boxed{17.5\%}
$$

**(iv) Conformance-to-Non-Conformance Ratio:**

$$
\frac{C_P + C_A}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}} \;=\; \frac{3,00,000 + 2,00,000}{5,00,000 + 4,00,000} \;=\; \frac{5,00,000}{9,00,000} \;=\; \boxed{0.556 : 1}
$$

**Commentary on Maturity Level:**

The COQ% of **17.5%** places the firm in the **"reactive/poor-quality" category** (industry benchmark: 15–40%). The conformance-to-failure ratio of **0.556:1** is *inverted* — the firm is spending **less on prevention/appraisal than on failure costs**. A mature world-class firm should target this ratio **≥ 1:1** (Juran's mature-firm balance). The Quality Budget should be **rebalanced** by:
- Increasing $C_P$ (prevention training, SPC, supplier development) by ~40%
- Increasing $C_A$ modestly (incoming inspection rigour)
- This will reduce $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ significantly over the next 2–3 cycles.

---

**Part (b) — Theoretical Solution:**

**Juran's Cost-of-Quality Behaviour Model:**

The model treats Total Quality Cost as the sum of four components, each behaving differently with respect to the quality level $q$ (fraction of conforming units produced).

$$
TQC(q) \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{k_P}{q^2}}_{C_P \text{ rises super-linearly}} \;+\; \underbrace{k_A}_{C_A \text{ roughly constant}} \;+\; \underbrace{k_F (1-q)}_{C_{IF} + C_{EF} \text{ falls linearly}}
$$

**Sketch of the Behavioural Model:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Quality Level q: 0 to 1] --> P1[Prevention CP rises super linear]
    X --> A1[Appraisal CA flat]
    X --> F1[Failure costs fall linear]
    P1 --> TQC[TQC curve U shaped]
    A1 --> TQC
    F1 --> TQC
    TQC --> OPT[Optimum q star at minimum]
```

**Mathematical Determination of Optimum $q^{*}$:**

Differentiate $TQC(q)$ with respect to $q$ and set equal to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TQC)}{dq} \;=\; -\frac{2 k_P}{q^3} - k_F \;=\; 0
$$

Solving:

$$
q^{*} \;=\; \left(\frac{2 k_P}{k_F}\right)^{1/3}
$$

At $q^{*}$, the marginal cost of an additional unit of prevention equals the marginal savings from reduced failures. The **second derivative test** confirms this is a minimum:

$$
\frac{d^2(TQC)}{dq^2} \;=\; \frac{6 k_P}{q^4} > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Two Management Decisions from the Model:**

1. **Budget Reallocation Decision:** If current operating $q < q^{*}$, the firm should *increase* prevention spending until the optimum is reached; this will *lower* total quality cost.

2. **Strategic Investment Decision:** Pushing $q$ beyond $q^{*}$ (toward 100% conformance) is *uneconomical* — the prevention cost escalation outweighs the failure cost reduction. The Quality Budget should be capped at $q^{*}$, *except* in safety-critical domains (aerospace, nuclear, medical devices) where regulatory zero-defect mandates override economic optimization.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **four categories of Cost of Quality (COQ)** with at least two industry-relevant examples per category. Compute the Total Quality Cost and the Cost of Poor Quality for a firm whose data are: $C_P = ₹2,50,000$; $C_A = ₹1,50,000$; $C_{IF} = ₹4,50,000$; $C_{EF} = ₹2,50,000$. Annual sales = ₹50,00,000. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **"Return on Quality (ROQ)"** and derive an expression for it. How does a firm use ROQ to justify its Quality Budget proposals? Outline the limitations of the ROQ metric. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — The Four COQ Categories:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COQ Category Examples (Industry-Relevant, 2 marks)**

**1. Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring.
- Example 1: Quality-circle training program for shop-floor workers
- Example 2: Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (DFMEA) workshop

**2. Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Costs incurred to *detect* defects through measurement.
- Example 1: Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) inspection of machined components
- Example 2: ISO 9001 third-party surveillance audit fees

**3. Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Costs incurred when defects are found *before* delivery.
- Example 1: Scrap of aluminium castings rejected at first-piece inspection
- Example 2: Rework labour on printed circuit boards (PCB) failing continuity test

**4. External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Costs incurred when defects are found *after* delivery.
- Example 1: Free replacement of ball-bearings under warranty claim
- Example 2: Field service engineer visit cost for elevator recall

**Numerical Computation:**

$$
TQC \;=\; C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

$$
TQC \;=\; 2,50,000 + 1,50,000 + 4,50,000 + 2,50,000 \;=\; \boxed{₹11,00,000}
$$

$$
COPQ \;=\; C_{IF} + C_{EF} \;=\; 4,50,000 + 2,50,000 \;=\; \boxed{₹7,00,000}
$$

$$
COQ\% \;=\; \frac{11,00,000}{50,00,000} \times 100 \;=\; 22\%
$$

(Indicates poor-quality firm — rebalance budget toward prevention.)

---

**Part (b) — Return on Quality (ROQ):**

**Definition:** ROQ is the ratio of the *reduction in Cost of Poor Quality* achieved by a quality initiative, divided by the *incremental investment* made in prevention/appraisal to achieve that reduction.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let $COPQ_0$ be the failure cost in the baseline period and $COPQ_1$ be the failure cost after the quality intervention. Let $\Delta C_P$ be the additional prevention spending and $\Delta C_A$ be the additional appraisal spending required to achieve this reduction.

$$
\Delta COPQ \;=\; COPQ_0 \;-\; COPQ_1 \;>\; 0
$$

$$
\Delta I_Q \;=\; \Delta C_P \;+\; \Delta C_A \;>\; 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;ROQ \;=\; \frac{\Delta COPQ}{\Delta I_Q} \;=\; \frac{COPQ_0 - COPQ_1}{\Delta C_P + \Delta C_A}\;}
$$

**Use in Justifying Quality Budget Proposals:**

A firm ranks competing quality initiatives (Six Sigma projects, SPC rollout, supplier development) by their ROQ. Projects with $ROQ > 1$ are accepted (each rupee invested recovers more than one rupee of failure cost). The Quality Budget is then allocated in descending order of ROQ until the budget envelope is exhausted.

**Limitations of ROQ:**

1. **Intangible Benefits Ignored:** ROQ captures only *tangible* cost savings. Improvements in customer goodwill, brand equity, and employee morale are not quantified, leading to under-investment in long-term capability building.

2. **Attribution Problem:** Multiple concurrent initiatives make it difficult to isolate the $\Delta COPQ$ attributable to a specific project — the "shared credit" bias inflates ROQ.

3. **Time-Lag Distortion:** Prevention investments typically yield failure-cost reductions *after* 6–18 months. ROQ computed on short horizons underestimates the true return, causing short-term-oriented managers to under-invest.

4. **Non-Linearity:** Beyond the optimum $q^{*}$, additional prevention yields diminishing returns ($d(ROQ)/dI_Q < 0$). ROQ can mislead managers to over-invest in prevention in safety-critical but already mature processes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include External Failure Cost in COPQ.** COPQ = $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — *not* just $C_{IF}$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this error.
> 2. **Treating Quality Budget as a one-time allocation.** It is a *living document* revisited quarterly. Failing to mention the continuous-review aspect loses **1 mark** on theory questions.
> 3. **Confusing POC with total $TQC$.** POC = $C_P + C_A$ only. Some students erroneously add failure costs.
> 4. **Missing units.** Always write ₹ or % explicitly. A numerical answer without units attracts a **½ mark** penalty in the valuation key.
> 5. **Stating optimum $q^{*} = 1$ (100% conformance).** This is mathematically impossible at finite cost and economically irrational. The correct answer is *less than 1* — explicitly justify via the cost-escalation argument.
> 6. **In 14-mark theory questions, students often skip the sketch.** A labelled diagram of the TQC-vs-$q$ curve is worth **2 marks minimum** in the KTU valuation key.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Quality Budget — Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Quality Budget = forward-looking financial plan for quality expenditures over a period.
- **PAF Model:** Prevention ($C_P$) + Appraisal ($C_A$) + Internal Failure ($C_{IF}$) + External Failure ($C_{EF}$).
- **Identity Equation:** $TQC = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — memorize verbatim.
- **COPQ:** $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — failure-only costs; the "waste" component.
- **COQ%:** $\frac{TQC}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$; world-class benchmark is **2.5–5%**.
- **Crosby's Pairing:** $POC = C_P + C_A$; $PONC = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$; $TQC = POC + PONC$.
- **Optimum Quality Level:** $q^{*} = \left(\frac{2k_P}{k_F}\right)^{1/3}$ — derived via FOC on $TQC(q)$.
- **Quality Cost Variance:** $QCV = BQ - AQ$ — favourable on prevention is *strategically unfavourable*.
- **ROQ Formula:** $ROQ = \Delta COPQ / \Delta I_Q$ — use to rank competing quality investments.
- **Maturity Indicator:** Conformance-cost to Failure-cost ratio **≥ 1:1** signals mature firm.
- **Baldrige Benchmark:** External Failure Loading $\left(\frac{C_{EF}}{TQC}\right)$ **< 25%** for award-winning firms.
- **ISO 9001:2015 Link:** Clause 7.1.5 mandates documented resource allocation for quality — the Quality Budget satisfies this.
- **Six Sigma Link:** COPQ drives project selection (DMAIC Phase 0 — Project Charter).
- **Document the PAF split** for every numerical answer; examiners award partial credit even if the final answer is wrong.
- **Always include units** (₹, %) and label each formula explicitly.
- **Sketch the U-shaped TQC curve** in any 14-mark question — guaranteed 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vendor Quality Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vendor Quality Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Vendor Quality Rating (VQR)** is a systematic, statistical procedure used by an organization to evaluate, quantify, and rank its suppliers (vendors) based on the quality of the goods and services they deliver. It is a quantitative measure of vendor performance that enables purchasing and quality control departments to make data-driven decisions regarding vendor selection, retention, and improvement.

The **vendor rating index** typically aggregates multiple sub-indicators such as **quality rating, delivery rating, price rating, and service rating** into a single composite score. In the context of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, the focus is predominantly on the **incoming quality** measured against **Acceptance Sampling Plans** (e.g., **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4**, **MIL-STD-105E**).

### Core Sub-Indices of Vendor Rating

| Sub-Index | Description | Primary Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Rating (QR)** | Measures the fraction of items accepted from the lot | **Percentage (%)** |
| **Delivery Rating (DR)** | Measures on-time delivery performance | **Percentage (%)** |
| **Price Rating (PR)** | Measures price competitiveness | **Ratio (Index)** |
| **Composite Vendor Rating (CVR)** | Weighted combination of all sub-indices | **Score (0–100)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary focus of this topic is on the **Quality Rating Index (QRI)**, which is computed from the **number of defectives or rejected lots** identified during **incoming inspection** under a **sampling plan**.

---

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a College Placement Cell** evaluating multiple training institutes that send students for campus interviews. The placement officer tracks:
> 1. **Quality** → What percentage of students from each institute clear the interview? (Quality Rating)
> 2. **Timeliness** → Did students arrive on time for the interview? (Delivery Rating)
> 3. **Affordability** → Was the training fee reasonable compared to others? (Price Rating)
> 4. **Final Selection** → The institute with the best combined score is preferred for future batches.
>
> **Vendor Quality Rating works exactly the same way** — except instead of training institutes, we are rating **suppliers of raw materials, components, or services**.

### Why Vendor Quality Rating is Essential in Engineering

- **Cost Reduction:** Prevents defective materials from entering the production line, reducing **rework** and **scrap costs**.
- **Supplier Development:** Identifies poor-performing vendors who need technical support.
- **Strategic Sourcing:** Supports the **make-or-buy** decision and **single-source vs. multi-source** strategy.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Many industries (automotive **IATF 16949**, aerospace **AS9100**) mandate documented vendor rating systems.
- **Just-in-Time (JIT) Compatibility:** Reliable vendors are the backbone of **lean manufacturing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Threshold Metrics (Industry Benchmarks):**
> - **Acceptance Rate ≥ 95%** → Preferred Vendor
> - **Acceptance Rate 90%–95%** → Conditional Vendor
> - **Acceptance Rate < 90%** → Vendor Review / Disqualification Candidate
>
> These thresholds are **not universal** — they are calibrated by each organization based on the **criticality of the purchased item**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — The Six-Step Vendor Quality Rating Process

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 SQC syllabus follows a **systematic, six-step methodology** for implementing a vendor quality rating system. Understanding each step is critical for both theory and numerical questions.

**Step 1 — Vendor Registration and Lot Definition**
- Each vendor is registered in the **Approved Vendor List (AVL)**.
- Each incoming shipment is treated as a **lot** with a unique **Lot Identification Number (LIN)**.
- A **sampling plan** (single, double, or multiple) is selected from **OC curves** based on **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** and **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)**.

**Step 2 — Incoming Inspection**
- Lots are inspected as per the chosen **sampling plan**.
- **Acceptance number (Ac)** and **Rejection number (Re)** are defined.
- Defectives are classified as **Critical**, **Major**, or **Minor**.

**Step 3 — Calculation of Quality Rating Index (QRI)**
- For each lot, compute the **Lot Rejection Rate**.
- Aggregate to get the vendor's overall **Quality Rating**.

**Step 4 — Calculation of Delivery and Price Ratings** *(if part of composite rating)*
- **Delivery Rating** = (Number of on-time deliveries / Total deliveries) × 100
- **Price Rating** = (Lowest quoted price / Vendor's quoted price) × 100

**Step 5 — Composite Vendor Rating (CVR)**
- A **weighted sum** of all sub-ratings is computed.
- Typical industrial weights: **Quality 50%, Delivery 30%, Price 20%** (varies by organization).

**Step 6 — Vendor Decision and Feedback Loop**
- Vendors are ranked and categorized as **A, B, C** class suppliers.
- **Feedback reports** are sent to vendors for continuous improvement.
- **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle is applied to vendor development.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for KTU University Examination questions on this topic. Memorize the formula number — examiners often give direct formula marks.

| Formula No. | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | **Quality Rating (QR)** | $QR = \left( \dfrac{\text{Defectives from accepted lots}}{\text{Total items inspected}} \right) \times 100$ | $QR$ in **%** |
| **F2** | **Lot Rejection Rate (LRR)** | $LRR = \left( \dfrac{\text{Number of lots rejected}}{\text{Total number of lots inspected}} \right) \times 100$ | $LRR$ in **%** |
| **F3** | **Quality Rating Index (QRI) — Sum Method** | $QRI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $R_i$ = rejection % of $i^{th}$ lot |
| **F4** | **Average Quality Rating (AQR)** | $AQR = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $n$ = number of lots |
| **F5** | **Delivery Rating (DR)** | $DR = \left( \dfrac{\text{On-time deliveries}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \right) \times 100$ | $DR$ in **%** |
| **F6** | **Price Rating (PR)** | $PR = \left( \dfrac{\text{Lowest vendor price}}{\text{Vendor's quoted price}} \right) \times 100$ | $PR$ in **%** |
| **F7** | **Composite Vendor Rating (CVR)** | $CVR = w_1 \cdot QR + w_2 \cdot DR + w_3 \cdot PR$ | $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ |
| **F8** | **Vendor Performance Index (VPI)** | $VPI = 0.5 \cdot QR + 0.3 \cdot DR + 0.2 \cdot PR$ | Standard industry weights |
| **F9** | **Lot Quality Level (LQL)** | $LQL = \dfrac{\text{Defectives in lot}}{\text{Lot size}} \times 100$ | LQL in **%** |
| **F10** | **Acceptance Probability (Pa)** | $P_a = P(d \leq Ac \mid p)$ | From OC curve |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Automotive Industry:** **Ford, Toyota, Tata Motors** use composite vendor ratings to decide on multi-million-dollar annual contracts.
> - **Pharmaceutical:** Vendor rating ensures **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice)** compliance of raw Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs).
> - **Electronics Manufacturing:** Critical for **IPC-A-610** compliance of PCB assemblies.
> - **Defense & Aerospace:** **AS9100** mandates documented vendor rating systems.
> - **JIT/Lean Systems:** Toyota Production System **cannot function** without a robust vendor quality rating system — a single defective batch can halt the entire assembly line.

### Critical Assumptions and Boundary Conditions

> [!WARNING]
> **Assumption 1 — Sampling Plan Validity:** The chosen sampling plan must have its **AQL** and **LTPD** clearly defined; otherwise, the resulting ratings are statistically meaningless.
>
> **Assumption 2 — Homogeneous Lot Quality:** All items in a lot are assumed to follow the **same binomial distribution** with parameter $p$ (true fraction defective).
>
> **Assumption 3 — Independence:** Each lot is **independent** of other lots. Rejected lots do not influence the rating of subsequent accepted lots.
>
> **Assumption 4 — Constant Inspection Effort:** The **AQL and inspection level** remain unchanged across the rating period.
>
> **Boundary Condition — Bounded Rating:** $0 \leq QR \leq 100$. A vendor with $QR = 100$ has a **perfect record**; a vendor with $QR = 0$ has **100% rejection**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Quality Rating Index (QRI) Using the Sum Method

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sum Method** is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU University Examinations. It computes the QRI as the **arithmetic sum of the percentage of defectives in all lots** received from a vendor during a defined rating period.

**Given:**
- A vendor supplies $n$ lots over a rating period.
- The $i^{th}$ lot has $N_i$ items, of which $D_i$ are defectives.
- The $i^{th}$ lot is accepted only if $D_i \leq Ac_i$ (the acceptance number for that lot size).

**Step 1 — Rejection Rate of a Single Lot**

The **percentage of defectives** in the $i^{th}$ lot is:

$$R_i = \left( \frac{D_i}{N_i} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Define the Quality Rating Index (Sum Method)**

By definition, the QRI is the **cumulative sum of rejection percentages** across all lots supplied by the vendor:

$$QRI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots + R_n$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the Expression for $R_i$**

$$QRI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{D_i}{N_i} \right) \times 100$$

$$QRI = 100 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{D_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Expression in Terms of Total Rejected Items**

If the **lot sizes are equal** (i.e., $N_i = N$ for all $i$), the formula simplifies to:

$$QRI = \frac{100}{N} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i}{n \cdot N} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Average Quality Rating**

Dividing QRI by the number of lots gives the **Average Quality Rating (AQR)**:

$$AQR = \frac{QRI}{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The vendor with the **LOWEST QRI** is the **BEST vendor** (least defectives). The vendor with the **HIGHEST QRI** is the **WORST vendor** (most defectives). This inverse relationship is a **common trick question** in KTU exams.

---

### Derivation 2 — Composite Vendor Rating (CVR) with Weighted Sub-Indices

**Given:**
- A vendor has Quality Rating $QR$, Delivery Rating $DR$, and Price Rating $PR$.
- The organization assigns weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$ to quality, delivery, and price respectively, where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Composite Rating**

The composite vendor rating is a **weighted linear combination** of the sub-ratings:

$$CVR = w_1 \cdot QR + w_2 \cdot DR + w_3 \cdot PR$$

**Step 2 — Substituting Industry-Standard Weights** ($w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$)

$$CVR = 0.5 \cdot QR + 0.3 \cdot DR + 0.2 \cdot PR$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Weight Constraint**

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Range of CVR**

Since each sub-rating lies in $[0, 100]$, the CVR also lies in $[0, 100]$:

$$0 \leq CVR \leq 100$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A higher CVR indicates a **better vendor**. The weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$ reflect the **strategic priorities** of the organization — for mission-critical components, $w_1$ (quality) is set much higher (e.g., **0.7 or 0.8**).

---

### Worked Example — Multi-Vendor Quality Rating Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** This is the **exact format** of a 14-mark question that appears in the KTU University Examination.

**Problem Statement:**
> A company receives components from three vendors (V1, V2, V3). Each vendor supplies 4 lots during a month. The lot sizes and number of defectives are as follows:
>
> | Lot No. | V1 (Size / Defectives) | V2 (Size / Defectives) | V3 (Size / Defectives) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 500 / 10 | 600 / 8 | 550 / 15 |
> | 2 | 600 / 12 | 500 / 6 | 500 / 10 |
> | 3 | 550 / 9 | 550 / 11 | 600 / 14 |
> | 4 | 500 / 8 | 600 / 7 | 550 / 12 |
>
> Compute the **Quality Rating Index (QRI)** for each vendor using the **Sum Method** and identify the best vendor.

**Step 1 — Compute $R_i$ for Each Lot of V1**

$$R_1^{V1} = \left( \frac{10}{500} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_2^{V1} = \left( \frac{12}{600} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_3^{V1} = \left( \frac{9}{550} \right) \times 100 = 1.636\%$$

$$R_4^{V1} = \left( \frac{8}{500} \right) \times 100 = 1.600\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute QRI for V1**

$$QRI_{V1} = 2.000 + 2.000 + 1.636 + 1.600 = 7.236\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute QRI for V2**

$$R_1^{V2} = \left( \frac{8}{600} \right) \times 100 = 1.333\%$$

$$R_2^{V2} = \left( \frac{6}{500} \right) \times 100 = 1.200\%$$

$$R_3^{V2} = \left( \frac{11}{550} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_4^{V2} = \left( \frac{7}{600} \right) \times 100 = 1.167\%$$

$$QRI_{V2} = 1.333 + 1.200 + 2.000 + 1.167 = 5.700\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute QRI for V3**

$$R_1^{V3} = \left( \frac{15}{550} \right) \times 100 = 2.727\%$$

$$R_2^{V3} = \left( \frac{10}{500} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_3^{V3} = \left( \frac{14}{600} \right) \times 100 = 2.333\%$$

$$R_4^{V3} = \left( \frac{12}{550} \right) \times 100 = 2.182\%$$

$$QRI_{V3} = 2.727 + 2.000 + 2.333 + 2.182 = 9.242\%$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranking and Decision**

| Rank | Vendor | QRI | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Best) | **V2** | **5.700%** | Lowest defectives — Preferred |
| 2 | V1 | 7.236% | Acceptable |
| 3 (Worst) | V3 | 9.242% | Vendor review required |

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Vendor **V2** is the best supplier with the lowest cumulative rejection percentage of **5.700%**. The organization should place a larger order share with **V2** and initiate corrective action with **V3**.

---

### Derivation 3 — Lot Quality Level (LQL) and its Role in Vendor Rating

**Step 1 — Definition of LQL**

The Lot Quality Level represents the **actual percentage of defectives** in a specific lot:

$$LQL = \left( \frac{D}{N} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Relationship with AQL**

The **AQL** is the **maximum acceptable** LQL. A lot is accepted if $LQL \leq AQL$ (probabilistically).

**Step 3 — Relationship with LTPD**

The **LTPD** is the **minimum unacceptable** LQL. A lot is rejected with high probability if $LQL \geq LTPD$.

**Step 4 — Indifference Zone**

The **Indifference Zone** lies between AQL and LTPD:

$$AQL \leq LQL \leq LTPD$$

**Step 5 — Role in Vendor Rating**

- If a vendor's **average LQL** consistently falls **below AQL**, the vendor is classified as **Class A (Preferred)**.
- If LQL lies in the **indifference zone**, the vendor is **Class B (Conditional)**.
- If LQL exceeds **LTPD** repeatedly, the vendor is **Class C (Disqualified)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Vendor Quality Rating System (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vendor Supplies Raw Material Lot] --> B[Incoming Inspection Station]
    B --> C{Sampling Plan Selection}
    C --> D[Apply AQL based OC Curve]
    D --> E{Acceptance Decision}
    E -->|Accept| F[Move to Production]
    E -->|Reject| G[Return to Vendor]
    F --> H[Record Lot Data N and D]
    G --> H[Record Lot Data N and D]
    H --> I[Compute Lot Rejection Rate Ri]
    I --> J[Compute Quality Rating Index QRI]
    J --> K[Compute Delivery Rating DR]
    K --> L[Compute Price Rating PR]
    L --> M[Apply Weighted Composite Formula]
    M --> N[Final Composite Vendor Rating CVR]
    N --> O{Vendor Classification}
    O -->|CVR Greater than 90| P[Class A Preferred Vendor]
    O -->|CVR 75 to 90| Q[Class B Conditional Vendor]
    O -->|CVR Less than 75| R[Class C Disqualified Vendor]
    P --> S[Increase Order Share]
    Q --> T[Send Improvement Feedback]
    R --> U[Initiate Vendor Review]
```

### Diagram 2 — Quality Rating Computation Flow (Hierarchical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Lot 1 Data] --> SUM[Sum of Rejection Percentages]
    L2[Lot 2 Data] --> SUM
    L3[Lot 3 Data] --> SUM
    Ln[Lot n Data] --> SUM
    SUM --> QRI[Quality Rating Index QRI]
    QRI --> AQR[Average Quality Rating AQR]
    AQR --> RANK[Vendor Ranking Table]
    RANK --> DEC[Best Vendor Selection]
```

### Diagram 3 — PDCA Cycle for Vendor Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plan: Set Vendor Quality Targets] --> D1[Do: Implement Sampling and Rating]
    D1 --> C1[Check: Compare CVR against Threshold]
    C1 --> A1[Act: Promote Demote or Train Vendor]
    A1 --> P1
```

### Diagram 4 — Composite Vendor Rating Weightage Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CVR[Composite Vendor Rating CVR] --> W1[Quality Weight 50 Percent]
    CVR --> W2[Delivery Weight 30 Percent]
    CVR --> W3[Price Weight 20 Percent]
    W1 --> QR[Quality Rating from QRI]
    W2 --> DR[Delivery Rating from Logistics]
    W3 --> PR[Price Rating from Market Analysis]
    QR --> OUT[Final CVR Score 0 to 100]
    DR --> OUT
    PR --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
