> **How to Use These Diagrams in the Exam:**
> - **Theory Questions:** Use Diagram 1 to describe the entire vendor rating system in 4–5 sentences.
> - **Numerical Questions:** Use Diagram 2 to explain the step-by-step QRI computation.
> - **Process Questions:** Use Diagram 3 (PDCA) to demonstrate the **continuous improvement** aspect of vendor management.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define Vendor Quality Rating. List any four sub-indices used to compute a comprehensive vendor rating.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Vendor Quality Rating (VQR)** is a systematic, statistical method of evaluating and ranking suppliers based on the quality of incoming materials or services, primarily through incoming inspection and acceptance sampling.
>
> **Four sub-indices** *(1/2 mark each)*:
> 1. **Quality Rating (QR)** — based on the percentage of defectives found during inspection.
> 2. **Delivery Rating (DR)** — based on the percentage of on-time deliveries.
> 3. **Price Rating (PR)** — based on the price competitiveness relative to other vendors.
> 4. **Service Rating (SR)** — based on the responsiveness to complaints, returns, and technical support.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD) and Acceptable Quality Level (AQL) in the context of vendor quality rating.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | AQL | LTPD |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum % defective considered acceptable | Minimum % defective considered unacceptable |
| **Producer's Risk** | Associated (α risk) | Associated (β risk) |
| **Typical Value** | 1.0% to 4.0% | 5.0% to 10.0% |
| **Lot Disposition** | Lots with p ≤ AQL are mostly accepted | Lots with p ≥ LTPD are mostly rejected |
| **Role in VQR** | Sets the **acceptance benchmark** for vendors | Sets the **rejection benchmark** for vendors |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B numerical questions, students **commonly lose marks** for:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** defectives to percentage (must multiply by 100).
> 2. **Confusing "lowest QRI = best vendor"** — many students incorrectly conclude that the highest QRI is best.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate $R_i$ calculations** — examiners award method marks only if every step is visible.
> 4. **Omitting units (%)** in the final answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]*

> A manufacturing company sources bearings from three vendors — **X, Y, and Z**. Each vendor supplies **5 lots** of size **400 bearings** each during a quarter. The number of defectives in each lot is given below:
>
> | Lot No. | Vendor X | Vendor Y | Vendor Z |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 8 | 5 | 10 |
> | 2 | 6 | 7 | 12 |
> | 3 | 10 | 4 | 9 |
> | 4 | 7 | 6 | 11 |
> | 5 | 9 | 8 | 13 |
>
> **(a)** Compute the **Quality Rating Index (QRI)** for each vendor using the Sum Method. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Identify the best and worst vendor. If the company also records **Delivery Rating (DR)** of 95% for X, 88% for Y, and 92% for Z, and **Price Rating (PR)** of 90% for X, 95% for Y, and 85% for Z, compute the **Composite Vendor Rating (CVR)** using weights $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$ and recommend the final vendor selection. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Quality Rating Index (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_i$ for Vendor X** *(1.5 marks)*

Each lot has $N_i = 400$, so $R_i = (D_i / 400) \times 100$:

$$R_1^X = \left( \frac{8}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_2^X = \left( \frac{6}{400} \right) \times 100 = 1.500\%$$

$$R_3^X = \left( \frac{10}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.500\%$$

$$R_4^X = \left( \frac{7}{400} \right) \times 100 = 1.750\%$$

$$R_5^X = \left( \frac{9}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.250\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute $QRI_X$** *(1 mark)*

$$QRI_X = 2.000 + 1.500 + 2.500 + 1.750 + 2.250 = 10.000\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_i$ for Vendor Y** *(1.5 marks)*

$$R_1^Y = \left( \frac{5}{400} \right) \times 100 = 1.250\%$$

$$R_2^Y = \left( \frac{7}{400} \right) \times 100 = 1.750\%$$

$$R_3^Y = \left( \frac{4}{400} \right) \times 100 = 1.000\%$$

$$R_4^Y = \left( \frac{6}{400} \right) \times 100 = 1.500\%$$

$$R_5^Y = \left( \frac{8}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute $QRI_Y$** *(1 mark)*

$$QRI_Y = 1.250 + 1.750 + 1.000 + 1.500 + 2.000 = 7.500\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_i$ for Vendor Z** *(1.5 marks)*

$$R_1^Z = \left( \frac{10}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.500\%$$

$$R_2^Z = \left( \frac{12}{400} \right) \times 100 = 3.000\%$$

$$R_3^Z = \left( \frac{9}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.250\%$$

$$R_4^Z = \left( \frac{11}{400} \right) \times 100 = 2.750\%$$

$$R_5^Z = \left( \frac{13}{400} \right) \times 100 = 3.250\%$$

**Step 6 — Compute $QRI_Z$** *(0.5 mark)*

$$QRI_Z = 2.500 + 3.000 + 2.250 + 2.750 + 3.250 = 13.750\%$$

**Ranking Table:**

| Vendor | QRI | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **Y** | **7.500%** | **1 (Best)** |
| X | 10.000% | 2 |
| Z | 13.750% | 3 (Worst) |

---

**Part (b) — Composite Vendor Rating (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the CVR Formula** *(0.5 mark)*

$$CVR = 0.5 \cdot QR + 0.3 \cdot DR + 0.2 \cdot PR$$

**Step 2 — Compute CVR for Vendor X** *(1.5 marks)*

$$CVR_X = (0.5 \times 10.000) + (0.3 \times 95) + (0.2 \times 90)$$

$$CVR_X = 5.000 + 28.500 + 18.000 = 51.500$$

**Step 3 — Compute CVR for Vendor Y** *(1.5 marks)*

$$CVR_Y = (0.5 \times 7.500) + (0.3 \times 88) + (0.2 \times 95)$$

$$CVR_Y = 3.750 + 26.400 + 19.000 = 49.150$$

**Step 4 — Compute CVR for Vendor Z** *(1.5 marks)*

$$CVR_Z = (0.5 \times 13.750) + (0.3 \times 92) + (0.2 \times 85)$$

$$CVR_Z = 6.875 + 27.600 + 17.000 = 51.475$$

**Step 5 — Final Comparison and Recommendation** *(2 marks)*

| Vendor | QRI | DR | PR | **CVR** | **Final Rank** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X** | 10.000% | 95% | 90% | **51.500** | **1 (Best)** |
| Y | 7.500% | 88% | 95% | 49.150 | 3 |
| Z | 13.750% | 92% | 85% | 51.475 | 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** Although **Vendor Y** had the best QRI (lowest defectives), the **composite rating** considers delivery and price. **Vendor X** emerges as the best overall with a **CVR of 51.500**, closely followed by **Vendor Z (51.475)**. **Vendor Y** drops to third due to its lower delivery rating. The company should award the **largest order share to Vendor X**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice for KTU Pattern]*

> **(a)** Explain the **concept and significance of Vendor Quality Rating** in Statistical Quality Control. Discuss the **six-step procedure** for implementing a vendor rating system in a manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vendor supplies **10 lots** of capacitors, each of size **200**. The number of defectives in each lot is: **2, 4, 3, 5, 1, 6, 2, 4, 3, 2**. Compute the **Average Quality Rating (AQR)** and **Quality Rating Index (QRI)**. If the acceptable AQL is **2.0%**, determine whether the vendor qualifies for **Class A (Preferred)** status. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Concept and Six-Step Procedure (7 Marks):**

> **Significance of Vendor Quality Rating (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Quality Assurance:** Ensures that incoming materials meet specifications, preventing defects from propagating to finished products.
> 2. **Cost Efficiency:** Reduces inspection costs, rework, scrap, warranty claims, and field failures.
> 3. **Supplier Development:** Provides a data-driven basis for vendor feedback, training, and improvement programs.
> 4. **Strategic Decision Support:** Facilitates informed decisions on **make-or-buy**, **single vs. multi-sourcing**, and **contract allocation**.
> 5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Aligns with **ISO 9001, IATF 16949, and AS9100** quality management requirements.
>
> **Six-Step Procedure (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Vendor Registration** — Maintain an **Approved Vendor List (AVL)** with unique vendor codes.
> 2. **Lot Definition and Sampling Plan Selection** — Define each incoming shipment as a lot; select sampling plan based on **AQL, LTPD, and inspection level** from **OC curves**.
> 3. **Incoming Inspection** — Inspect lots as per the plan; record **defectives** by criticality class.
> 4. **Quality Rating Computation** — Calculate **Lot Rejection Rate** and **QRI** for each vendor.
> 5. **Composite Rating** — Compute **CVR** using weighted combination of quality, delivery, and price.
> 6. **Vendor Classification and Feedback** — Categorize as **A, B, C** class; provide feedback reports; apply **PDCA cycle** for continuous improvement.

---

**Part (b) — AQR and QRI Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — List the Defectives** *(0.5 mark)*

$D_i = \{2, 4, 3, 5, 1, 6, 2, 4, 3, 2\}$, with $N_i = 200$ and $n = 10$ lots.

**Step 2 — Compute $R_i$ for Each Lot** *(3 marks)*

$$R_1 = \left( \frac{2}{200} \right) \times 100 = 1.000\%$$

$$R_2 = \left( \frac{4}{200} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_3 = \left( \frac{3}{200} \right) \times 100 = 1.500\%$$

$$R_4 = \left( \frac{5}{200} \right) \times 100 = 2.500\%$$

$$R_5 = \left( \frac{1}{200} \right) \times 100 = 0.500\%$$

$$R_6 = \left( \frac{6}{200} \right) \times 100 = 3.000\%$$

$$R_7 = \left( \frac{2}{200} \right) \times 100 = 1.000\%$$

$$R_8 = \left( \frac{4}{200} \right) \times 100 = 2.000\%$$

$$R_9 = \left( \frac{3}{200} \right) \times 100 = 1.500\%$$

$$R_{10} = \left( \frac{2}{200} \right) \times 100 = 1.000\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute QRI (Sum Method)** *(1.5 marks)*

$$QRI = 1.000 + 2.000 + 1.500 + 2.500 + 0.500 + 3.000 + 1.000 + 2.000 + 1.500 + 1.000$$

$$QRI = 16.000\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute AQR** *(1 mark)*

$$AQR = \frac{QRI}{n} = \frac{16.000}{10} = 1.600\%$$

**Step 5 — Compare with AQL and Decide** *(1 mark)*

| Metric | Value | Threshold | Comparison |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AQR** | **1.600%** | AQL = 2.000% | $AQR < AQL$ ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since the **Average Quality Rating (1.600%)** is **less than the AQL (2.000%)**, the vendor **QUALIFIES for Class A (Preferred) status**. The vendor's incoming quality is statistically acceptable, and the company may increase order share.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Vendor Quality Rating (VQR)** is a **statistical, data-driven** evaluation of supplier quality based on incoming inspection.
- **Primary Formula:** $R_i = (D_i / N_i) \times 100$ — always remember to **multiply by 100** for percentage.
- **QRI (Sum Method):** $QRI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ — the **LOWEST QRI = BEST VENDOR**.
- **AQR (Average Method):** $AQR = QRI / n$ — provides the **mean lot quality** of the vendor.
- **CVR Formula:** $CVR = 0.5 \cdot QR + 0.3 \cdot DR + 0.2 \cdot PR$ — standard industry weights.
- **Class A** (Preferred): $CVR \geq 90$, **Class B** (Conditional): $75 \leq CVR < 90$, **Class C** (Disqualified): $CVR < 75$.
- **AQL vs. LTPD:** AQL is the **producer's risk threshold**; LTPD is the **consumer's risk threshold**.
- **PDCA Cycle** is used for **continuous vendor improvement**.
- **Assumption:** All lots follow the **binomial distribution** and are **independent**.
- **Real-world standards:** ISO 9001, IATF 16949, AS9100 all mandate **documented vendor rating systems**.
- **Common Pitfall:** Never forget to show the **intermediate $R_i$ values** in numerical answers — examiners award **method marks** only when steps are visible.
- **Boundary Condition:** $0 \leq R_i \leq 100$ and $0 \leq CVR \leq 100$.
- **Sample Size Effect:** When lot sizes are **equal**, the formula simplifies to $QRI = 100 \cdot \sum D_i / (n \cdot N)$.
- **Practical Tip:** In exam, **always state** whether you are using the **Sum Method** or the **Average Method** for QRI.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organization for Quality Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Organization for Quality Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Quality Function** is a structured, organization-wide managerial discipline that plans, controls, assures, and improves the standard of products, services, and processes to meet or exceed predefined customer and regulatory requirements. According to KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPET415, Module 1), the "Organization for Quality Function" refers to the **hierarchical framework, policy deployment, and cross-functional roles** established within an enterprise to ensure that quality is built-in, measured, and continuously improved.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> Quality is not the responsibility of a single department — it is a *Total System* spanning suppliers, production, sales, and post-sale service. The Quality Function translates strategic intent into measurable field-level outcomes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of an **airport**:
- The **airport authority** (Top Management) sets safety policy.
- The **air traffic control** (Quality Council) plans and monitors operations.
- The **pilots and ground crew** (Quality Circles / Production Teams) execute the daily checks.
- The **passenger** is the *customer* whose safety defines the success metric.

If any layer fails (e.g., a pilot ignores checklist procedures), the entire system breaks. This is exactly how a quality function operates — **quality flows top-down in policy but is owned bottom-up in execution**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Standard Metrics in **Bold**)
- **Quality Planning (QP)**: Identifying customers, their needs, and developing products to satisfy them.
- **Quality Control (QC)**: The operational techniques and activities used to fulfill quality requirements (statistical techniques like control charts belong here).
- **Quality Assurance (QA)**: All planned and systematic activities implemented to provide confidence that a product/service will satisfy quality requirements.
- **Quality Improvement (QI)**: Establishing infrastructure and using formal methodologies (Kaizen, Six Sigma DMAIC) to reduce variation and eliminate waste.
- **Total Quality Management (TQM)**: A *company-wide* management philosophy focused on continuously improving all functions at lowest cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QC vs QA — A Common KTU Confusion**
> | Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Focus | Operational (product/process) | Systemic (entire system) |
> | Tool | Control charts, sampling plans | Audits, process design, training |
> | Responsibility | Inspectors, line engineers | Top management, quality dept. |
> | Nature | Reactive/Diagnostic | Preventive/Proactive |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Quality Function Hierarchy (Logic Steps)
1. **Top Management Commitment** → Quality vision is defined, resources are allocated.
2. **Quality Council Formation** → Cross-functional committee that steers the quality program.
3. **Policy Deployment (Hoshin Kanri)** → Strategic goals cascaded to departmental objectives.
4. **Quality System Documentation** → ISO 9001 standards, Quality Manuals, SOPs.
5. **Workforce Empowerment** → Quality Circles, Suggestion Schemes, 5S.
6. **Supplier Partnership** → Incoming material control, vendor rating.
7. **Customer Focus** → Voice of Customer (VoC) translated into CTQs (Critical-to-Quality).
8. **Continuous Measurement** → KPIs, Cost of Quality analysis, SQC tools.

### 2.2 Responsibility for Quality (The 5-W Model)
- **Who is responsible?** → *Everyone* in the organization (TQM philosophy).
- **What is their role?** → From strategic planning to defect prevention.
- **Why?** → Customer satisfaction and competitive survival.
- **When?** → Quality must be built-in *before* production (not inspected *after*).
- **Where?** → Spans Marketing → Design → Procurement → Production → Sales → Service.

### 2.3 Gurus of Quality (KTU High-Yield Knowledge)
- **W. Edwards Deming**: 14 Points for Management; PDSA cycle (Plan-Do-Study-Act); *System knowledge* is the primary source of quality.
- **Joseph M. Juran**: **Quality Trilogy** — *Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement*.
- **Philip B. Crosby**: "Quality is Free"; *Zero Defects*; 4 Absolutes of Quality.
- **Kaoru Ishikawa**: **Quality Circles**; Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram.
- **Genichi Taguchi**: **Loss Function** — any deviation from target causes loss to society.
- **Armand V. Feigenbaum**: *Total Quality Control* — the originator of the "company-wide" concept.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cost of Quality (COQ)
The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the single most numerically-tested concept in this module.

$$ COQ \,\, = \,\, COQ_{Prevention} \,\, + \,\, COQ_{Appraisal} \,\, + \,\, COQ_{Internal\ Failure} \,\, + \,\, COQ_{External\ Failure} $$

| Cost Category | Definition | Examples | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Cost** (P) | Cost of preventing defects | Training, quality planning, supplier capability | Investment |
| **Appraisal Cost** (A) | Cost of detecting defects | Inspection, testing, audits | Investment |
| **Internal Failure** (IF) | Cost of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | Failure |
| **External Failure** (EF) | Cost of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits | Failure (highest) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Loss Function** (also frequently asked):
> $$ L(x) \,\, = \,\, k \cdot (x \,\, - \,\, m)^{2} $$
> where $L(x)$ = loss to society, $k$ = quality loss coefficient, $x$ = actual quality characteristic, $m$ = target value. **Quality loss occurs even within specification limits**, unlike the traditional "conformance-to-specs" view.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **Manufacturing Plants**: Implementing TQM and SQC tools reduces scrap and warranty claims.
- **Software Engineering**: COQ analysis determines ROI of testing vs. post-release bug fixing.
- **Construction Engineering**: Prevention costs (design reviews) minimize catastrophic failure costs.
- **Aerospace**: Loss Function quantifies fuel-efficiency deviation cost in turbine blades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Quality (COQ) Optimal Point
The goal of an effective Quality Function is to **minimize the total COQ**, balancing investment in prevention/appraisal against failure costs.

**Step 1 — Define the variable:**
Let $x$ represent the level of investment in prevention activities (e.g., % of revenue spent on quality training, robust design).

**Step 2 — Behavior of each component as $x$ increases:**
- Prevention cost $P(x)$: increases linearly (or monotonically) — you spend more as you invest more.
- Appraisal cost $A(x)$: relatively stable or slightly rising — inspection is fixed by sampling policy.
- Internal failure cost $IF(x)$: decreases sharply as $x$ rises (better designs catch errors earlier).
- External failure cost $EF(x)$: decreases sharply as $x$ rises (fewer field defects).

**Step 3 — Empirical cost functions (commonly adopted in KTU textbooks):**

$$ P(x) \,\, = \,\, p_{0} \,\, + \,\, p_{1} x $$

$$ IF(x) \,\, = \,\, \frac{i_{0}}{1 \,\, + \,\, \beta x} \quad \text{(hyperbolic decay)} $$

$$ EF(x) \,\, = \,\, \frac{e_{0}}{1 \,\, + \,\, \gamma x} \quad \text{(hyperbolic decay)} $$

**Step 4 — Total COQ function:**

$$ COQ(x) \,\, = \,\, \underbrace{(p_{0} + p_{1} x)}_{Prevention} \,\, + \,\, \underbrace{A}_{Appraisal} \,\, + \,\, \underbrace{\frac{i_{0}}{1 \,\, + \,\, \beta x}}_{Internal\ Failure} \,\, + \,\, \underbrace{\frac{e_{0}}{1 \,\, + \,\, \gamma x}}_{External\ Failure} $$

**Step 5 — Optimality condition:**
Differentiate with respect to $x$ and set equal to zero:

$$ \frac{d(COQ)}{dx} \,\, = \,\, p_{1} \,\, - \,\, \frac{i_{0}\,\beta}{(1 + \beta x)^{2}} \,\, - \,\, \frac{e_{0}\,\gamma}{(1 + \gamma x)^{2}} \,\, = \,\, 0 $$

**Step 6 — Economic interpretation:**
At the optimum, the **marginal cost of prevention** equals the **marginal savings from reduced failures**. Any additional rupee/dollar spent on prevention beyond this point yields no net COQ reduction.

**Step 7 — Verification via second derivative:**

$$ \frac{d^{2}(COQ)}{dx^{2}} \,\, = \,\, \frac{2 i_{0}\,\beta^{2}}{(1 + \beta x)^{3}} \,\, + \,\, \frac{2 e_{0}\,\gamma^{2}}{(1 + \gamma x)^{3}} \,\, > \,\, 0 $$

Since the second derivative is **positive**, the stationary point is a **minimum** ✓.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark format)
A precision machining company reports annual quality costs as follows:

| Item | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Quality training & planning | 8 |
| Inspection & testing | 12 |
| Rework & scrap | 30 |
| Warranty claims | 50 |

**Step 1 — Identify categories:**

$$ P \,\, = \,\, 8, \quad A \,\, = \,\, 12, \quad IF \,\, = \,\, 30, \quad EF \,\, = \,\, 50 $$

**Step 2 — Compute Total COQ:**

$$ COQ \,\, = \,\, 8 \,\, + \,\, 12 \,\, + \,\, 30 \,\, + \,\, 50 \,\, = \,\, 100 \text{ Lakhs} $$

**Step 3 — Compute % distribution:**

$$ \%P \,\, = \,\, 8\%, \quad \%A \,\, = \,\, 12\%, \quad \%IF \,\, = \,\, 30\%, \quad \%EF \,\, = \,\, 50\% $$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> When **External Failure > 50% of COQ**, the quality system is in *reactive mode*. KTU benchmark recommends a healthy ratio: $P : A : IF : EF \,\, \approx \,\, 40 : 30 : 20 : 10$ (i.e., heavy *prevention* weighting).

**Step 5 — Recommendation:** Increase prevention spending (e.g., robust design, FMEA, employee training) by approximately 15-20% to drive down EF.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The Three Gurus' Philosophies

| Dimension | Deming | Juran | Crosby |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focus | System & Variation | Customer & Process | Conformance & Conformance cost |
| Methodology | PDSA / 14 Points | Quality Trilogy | Zero Defects / 4 Absolutes |
| Philosophy | Knowledge-based management | Fitness for use | Doing it right the first time |
| KTU 14-Mark Likeliness | Very High | Very High | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Organization for Quality Function
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Board of Directors / Top Management] --> B[Quality Council / Steering Committee]
    B --> C[Quality Policy Deployment Hoshin Kanri]
    C --> D1[Quality Planning Dept]
    C --> D2[Quality Assurance Dept]
    C --> D3[Quality Control / SQC Lab]
    C --> D4[Quality Improvement / Six Sigma]
    D1 --> E1[Cross Functional Teams]
    D2 --> E2[Internal Audit Team]
    D3 --> E3[Inspectors Line Operators]
    D4 --> E4[Quality Circles at Shop Floor]
    E1 --> F1[Suppliers Vendors]
    E2 --> F2[ISO 9001 Documentation]
    E3 --> F3[Statistical Process Control SPC]
    E4 --> F4[Kaizen Suggestion Scheme]
    F1 --> G[Customer Satisfaction]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
```

### 4.2 Juran's Quality Trilogy (Three Managerial Processes)
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[Quality Planning]
        P1[Identify Customers]
        P2[Determine Customer Needs]
        P3[Develop Product Features]
        P4[Establish Quality Goals]
    end
    subgraph C[Quality Control]
        C1[Evaluate Actual Performance]
        C2[Compare with Goals]
        C3[Act on Differences]
    end
    subgraph I[Quality Improvement]
        I1[Establish Infrastructure]
        I2[Identify Improvement Projects]
        I3[Establish Project Teams]
        I4[Provide Training and Resources]
    end
    P --> C
    C --> I
    I --> P
```

### 4.3 Cost of Quality — Behavioral Block Diagram
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INC[Costs That INCREASE with Quality Investment]
        INC1[Prevention Cost P]
        INC2[Appraisal Cost A]
    end
    subgraph DEC[Costs That DECREASE with Quality Investment]
        DEC1[Internal Failure IF]
        DEC2[External Failure EF]
    end
    INC1 --> SUM[Total COQ Sum]
    INC2 --> SUM
    DEC1 --> SUM
    DEC2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OPT[Minimum COQ at Optimum Investment x star]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: As the company invests more in *prevention* (P), the failure costs (IF + EF) drop faster than the prevention cost rises — until the **optimal point** is reached, beyond which spending extra yields no COQ reduction.

### 4.4 Deming's PDSA Cycle (Plan-Do-Study-Act)
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan Define Objective and Method] --> D[Do Implement on Small Scale]
    D --> S[Study Analyze Results]
    S --> A[Act Standardize or Revise]
    A --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
*Define Quality Assurance and distinguish it from Quality Control.*

**Model Answer:**
**Quality Assurance (QA)** is the set of planned and systematic activities implemented in the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. It is *systemic* and focuses on preventing defects at the system level through audits, process design, and documentation (e.g., ISO 9001). **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique used to fulfill quality requirements, focusing on *product-level* verification through inspection, testing, and statistical tools like control charts. In short, QA is *system-oriented and preventive*, while QC is *product-oriented and detective*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
*State the four categories of Cost of Quality with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
The four categories are:
1. **Prevention Cost** — e.g., quality training programs.
2. **Appraisal Cost** — e.g., inspection of incoming materials.
3. **Internal Failure Cost** — e.g., rework or scrap before delivery.
4. **External Failure Cost** — e.g., warranty claims after dispatch.

The total Cost of Quality is the sum of all four:
$$ COQ \,\, = \,\, P \,\, + \,\, A \,\, + \,\, IF \,\, + \,\, EF $$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Full Question]**
*(a)* Explain the Quality Function as a *Total Quality Management* philosophy. Discuss the roles of top management, Quality Council, and Quality Circles in implementing TQM. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality data:
- Prevention cost: ₹ 6 Lakhs
- Appraisal cost: ₹ 10 Lakhs
- Internal failure cost: ₹ 24 Lakhs
- External failure cost: ₹ 40 Lakhs

Calculate total COQ, comment on the cost distribution, and recommend a corrective strategy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TQM and Organizational Roles:**

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, customer-focused management philosophy that emphasizes *continuous improvement* of all processes, products, and services across the entire organization. The key organizational layers are:

1. **Top Management:**
   - Defines the quality vision, policy, and objectives.
   - Allocates resources (budget, personnel, time).
   - Acts as the *driver* of cultural change; without their commitment, TQM fails.

2. **Quality Council (Steering Committee):**
   - Cross-functional body chaired by a senior executive.
   - Translates vision into measurable goals and deployment plans.
   - Monitors progress via KPIs and reviews audit results.
   - Coordinates departmental quality initiatives.

3. **Quality Circles (QCs):**
   - Small groups of 6–12 frontline workers who meet regularly (often weekly).
   - Identify, analyze, and solve work-related quality problems.
   - Empowered to implement *Kaizen* (small, continuous improvements).
   - Originated by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in Japan.

> **Key Outcome**: TQM creates a *vertical* (top-down) and *horizontal* (cross-functional) integration where every employee becomes a stakeholder in quality.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate:**

$$ P \,\, = \,\, 6, \quad A \,\, = \,\, 10, \quad IF \,\, = \,\, 24, \quad EF \,\, = \,\, 40 $$

**Step 2 — Total COQ:**

$$ COQ \,\, = \,\, 6 \,\, + \,\, 10 \,\, + \,\, 24 \,\, + \,\, 40 \,\, = \,\, 80 \text{ Lakhs} $$

**Step 3 — Percentage Breakdown:**

$$ \%P \,\, = \,\, \frac{6}{80} \times 100 \,\, = \,\, 7.5\% $$

$$ \%A \,\, = \,\, \frac{10}{80} \times 100 \,\, = \,\, 12.5\% $$

$$ \%IF \,\, = \,\, \frac{24}{80} \times 100 \,\, = \,\, 30.0\% $$

$$ \%EF \,\, = \,\, \frac{40}{80} \times 100 \,\, = \,\, 50.0\% $$

**Step 4 — Diagnosis:**
Failure costs (IF + EF) account for **80% of total COQ**, while prevention costs are only **7.5%**. The system is heavily *reactive*.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**
- Increase *prevention* spending via Design for Six Sigma (DFSS), FMEA, and workforce training.
- Strengthen *incoming inspection* (mildly raise appraisal) to catch defects early.
- Target a healthier long-term ratio of $P : A : IF : EF \approx 30 : 25 : 25 : 20$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Full Question]**
*(a)* Discuss the quality philosophies of **Deming, Juran, and Crosby**. Compare their approaches. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function** with a numerical example. A shaft is manufactured with a target diameter of 50.00 mm and tolerance of ± 0.05 mm. If the loss coefficient $k = ₹ 2000$ per mm², find the loss when a shaft is produced with diameter 50.08 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Quality Philosophies:**

| Aspect | **Deming** | **Juran** | **Crosby** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Origin | Post-WWII Japan | Industrial consultant | Corporate executive |
| Core Idea | *System* drives 94% of quality | Quality is *fitness for use* | Quality = *conformance to requirements* |
| Methodology | 14 Points, PDSA cycle | Quality Trilogy (QP, QC, QI) | 4 Absolutes, Zero Defects |
| Focus Area | Statistical thinking, variation | Customer, process, projects | Cost of non-conformance, prevention |
| Belief | Constancy of purpose | Universal applicability | Quality is free (savings > cost) |

**Deming's 14 Points** include: create constancy of purpose, adopt the new philosophy, cease dependence on inspection, build quality into the product, institute training, institute leadership, drive out fear, break down barriers, eliminate slogans, eliminate numerical quotas, support & develop people, remove barriers to pride of workmanship, institute vigorous education, put everyone to work.

**Juran's Trilogy:**
1. **Quality Planning**: Identify customers → determine needs → develop product features.
2. **Quality Control**: Evaluate performance → compare to goals → act on difference.
3. **Quality Improvement**: Establish infrastructure → identify projects → form teams → train.

**Crosby's 4 Absolutes:**
1. Quality = conformance to requirements.
2. Prevention, not inspection.
3. Zero defects performance standard.
4. Measure quality by the *price of non-conformance*.

---

**(b) Taguchi's Loss Function:**

**Theory:** Traditional view considers any product *within* specification limits as acceptable. **Taguchi's view:** any deviation from target $m$ causes loss to society, even within specs.

$$ L(x) \,\, = \,\, k(x \,\, - \,\, m)^{2} $$

**Given:** $m = 50.00$ mm, $x = 50.08$ mm, $k = ₹ 2000$/mm².

**Step 1 — Compute deviation:**

$$ x \,\, - \,\, m \,\, = \,\, 50.08 \,\, - \,\, 50.00 \,\, = \,\, 0.08 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Square the deviation:**

$$ (x - m)^{2} \,\, = \,\, (0.08)^{2} \,\, = \,\, 0.0064 \text{ mm}^{2} $$

**Step 3 — Compute loss:**

$$ L(x) \,\, = \,\, 2000 \times 0.0064 \,\, = \,\, ₹ \, 12.80 $$

**Conclusion:** Even though the shaft is *within* the ±0.05 mm tolerance? Actually **NO** — 0.08 mm exceeds the 0.05 mm USL. Hence the shaft is *rejected by classical SPC*, but Taguchi's function shows that even smaller deviations (e.g., 0.02 mm) also generate measurable loss to the customer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning**
> 1. **Confusing QA with QC** — Marks lost if you state QA is the same as inspection. They are not.
> 2. **In COQ numericals**: Forgetting to convert to **percentages** before commenting on the quality system's health.
> 3. **Taguchi's Loss Function**: Many students write $L = k|x - m|$ (linear) — that is **WRONG**. It is always *quadratic*: $L = k(x - m)^{2}$.
> 4. **Deming vs Juran confusion**: Deming focuses on *variation & system*; Juran focuses on *customer needs & projects*. Examiner will not accept interchangeable use.
> 5. **Quality Circles ≠ TQM**: QCs are *one tool* within TQM, not the whole philosophy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - [ ] Quality Function = *Planning + Control + Assurance + Improvement* — the four pillars.
> - [ ] TQM is a *management philosophy*, not a department.
> - [ ] **COQ = P + A + IF + EF** — must compute each component separately.
> - [ ] A healthy COQ has **60–70%** in (P + A) and only **20–30%** in (IF + EF).
> - [ ] Deming: 14 Points + PDSA + system-driven quality.
> - [ ] Juran: Quality Trilogy (Planning → Control → Improvement).
> - [ ] Crosby: Zero Defects, 4 Absolutes, *Quality is Free*.
> - [ ] Taguchi Loss: $L(x) = k(x - m)^{2}$ — **quadratic** (mark critical).
> - [ ] Quality Circles: Ishikawa, 6–12 workers, shop-floor problem solving.
> - [ ] Responsibility for quality: *everyone* — from CEO to line operator.
> - [ ] Quality is *built-in* (prevention), not *inspected-in* (detection).
> - [ ] External Failure cost is the **most damaging** category (customer-facing).
> - [ ] Voice of Customer (VoC) → CTQs → KPIs → Process Improvement is the deployment flow.
> - [ ] ISO 9001 is the global *Quality System Standard* for QA framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organization for Acceptance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Organization for Acceptance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Acceptance Sampling

**Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure in which a random sample is drawn from a production lot, and based on the inspection results of that sample, a decision is made to either **accept** or **reject** the entire lot. It is the cornerstone of *lot-by-lot acceptance* organizations, especially when 100% inspection is impossible, uneconomical, or destructive in nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"Acceptance sampling is a statistical measure used in quality control that allows a company to measure the quality of a batch of products without having to inspect every single unit. It determines either to accept or reject a lot based on a sample inspection."*

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Lot (Batch)** | A collection of items produced under similar conditions, submitted for inspection as a single entity. |
| **Sample** | A subset of units drawn randomly from the lot. |
| **N** | Lot size (Total number of units in the lot). |
| **n** | Sample size (Number of units inspected from the lot). |
| **Acceptance Number (c)** | The maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample for lot acceptance. |
| **Rejection Number** | Any count of defectives greater than $c$ leads to rejection. |
| **Producer's Risk (α)** | Probability of rejecting a *good* lot (Type I error). |
| **Consumer's Risk (β)** | Probability of accepting a *bad* lot (Type II error). |
| **AQL** | Acceptable Quality Level. |
| **LTPD / RQL** | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective / Rejectable Quality Level. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** Acceptance sampling is **NOT** a substitute for process control (SPC charts). It is a *supplementary* tool for deciding lot disposition when control charts confirm an in-control process.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Mango Cart Inspection" 🍎

Imagine you are a **fruit wholesaler** buying 1000 mangoes (the *lot*) from a farmer. You cannot taste every single mango (destructive/expensive inspection). Instead, you randomly pick 10 mangoes (the *sample*, $n=10$) and count the rotten ones.

- If **0 rotten mangoes** are found → You accept the entire cart.
- If **1 or more rotten** → You reject the cart.

This decision rule is your **acceptance sampling plan**. The science of *Organization for Acceptance* tells you **how to choose** $n$ and $c$ such that the wholesaler (consumer) is protected from bad carts, and the farmer (producer) is protected from unfair rejection of good carts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:**
> - **Destructive testing:** Testing tensile strength of steel rods, bulletproof vest impact, semiconductor wafer burn-in.
> - **Costly 100% inspection:** 100,000 capacitor production, pharmaceutical blister packs.
> - **Fatigue/Reliability life tests** where each test destroys the unit.

---

## 1.3 Why "Organize" for Acceptance?

When a factory is **organized for acceptance sampling**, it commits to:
1. Drawing samples by a **random mechanism** (not cherry-picking).
2. Using **predefined decision rules** (the plan: $n$, $c$, single/double/multiple).
3. Recording **inspection data** to compute quality metrics.
4. Establishing **rectifying inspection stations** (100% sort rejected lots).
5. Maintaining a **feedback loop** to the manufacturing process.

The organization of acceptance sampling typically resides within a **Quality Assurance (QA) Department**, independent of production, to ensure unbiased decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:**
> Questions on "Organization for Acceptance" frequently test your ability to distinguish between **process control** and **acceptance sampling**, and to identify the role of QA in deciding the disposition of lots.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Organization for Acceptance

A typical acceptance sampling organization is structured around three operational phases:

### Phase 1: Pre-Production Phase (Plan Design)
- Define the **AQL** (Acceptable Quality Level) — negotiated between producer and consumer.
- Define the **LTPD** (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) — consumer's risk quality.
- Set Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%) and Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$ (10%) as KTU-standard defaults.
- Select the sampling plan type: **Single, Double, or Multiple**.

### Phase 2: Production Phase (Execution)
- Each lot produced is subjected to random sampling.
- The number of defectives $d$ in the sample is counted.
- Decision rule:
  - If $d \le c$ → **Accept** the lot.
  - If $d > c$ → **Reject** the lot.

### Phase 3: Post-Inspection Phase (Rectification & Feedback)
- Rejected lots undergo **rectifying inspection** (100% sort).
- Defectives are replaced/repaired.
- Information flows back to production for process improvement.

---

## 2.2 Types of Acceptance Sampling Plans

### (a) Single Sampling Plan
The simplest structure. From lot of size $N$, take a single sample of size $n$ and compare defectives $d$ with acceptance number $c$.

- Accept if $d \le c$
- Reject if $d > c$

### (b) Double Sampling Plan
Two-stage inspection:
- **First sample** of size $n_1$ with acceptance $c_1$ and rejection $r_1$.
- If $c_1 < d_1 \le r_1$, take a **second sample** of size $n_2$ with combined acceptance $c_2$.

### (c) Multiple Sampling Plan
Extension of double plan, using 3, 5, 7, or 10 stages. Average sample size is smaller.

### (d) Sequential Sampling Plan
Item-by-item inspection; decision is made after every unit.

### (e) Rectifying Inspection Plan
Rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives replaced. Quality is improved before shipment.

---

## 2.3 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — The Heart of Acceptance Sampling

The **OC curve** is a plot of the probability of acceptance $P_a$ against the incoming lot quality $p$ (fraction defective). It is the single most important tool for evaluating an acceptance plan.

- $P_a$ decreases as $p$ increases.
- An **ideal OC curve** would be a step function: 100% accept for $p \le$ AQL, 0% accept for $p >$ AQL.
- A **real OC curve** is S-shaped (sigmoid).

### Performance Points on OC Curve
| Point | Definition | Risk |
|-------|------------|------|
| AQL | $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ (typically 0.95) | Producer's quality level |
| LTPD | $P_a = \beta$ (typically 0.10) | Consumer's worst acceptable quality |
| IQL | Indifference Quality Level ($P_a = 0.50$) | Break-even point |
| AOQL | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | Worst-case outgoing quality |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these are the equations tested in KTU Board Exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Probability of acceptance (Binomial) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Prob. of finding $\le c$ defectives |
| 2 | Probability of acceptance (Poisson approx.) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Used when $p < 0.10$ and $n \ge 50$ |
| 3 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Quality of lots after rectifying inspection |
| 4 | Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $ATI = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ | Average units inspected per lot |
| 5 | AOQL | $AOQL = \max_{p} \left[ \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} \right]$ | Maximum AOQ over all $p$ |
| 6 | ASN (Double Sampling) | $ASN = n_1 + (1-P_{1}) \cdot n_2$ | Average Sample Number for double plan |
| 7 | Indifference Quality ($p_0$) | $P_a(p_0) = 0.50$ | $p$ at which acceptance and rejection are equally likely |
| 8 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(AQL)$ | Probability of rejecting a good lot |
| 9 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(LTPD)$ | Probability of accepting a bad lot |
| 10 | Defect rate (estimate) | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{d}{n}$ | Sample proportion defective |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU 2024 numerical problems, when $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 50$, use the **Poisson approximation** (Equation 2) instead of the binomial. This is a frequent mark-granting shortcut.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** FDA-mandated acceptance sampling for tablet lots.
- **Automotive:** Inbound inspection of brake pads, sensors from suppliers.
- **Electronics:** Lot acceptance of IC chips (MIL-STD-105E / ANSI Z1.4 standards).
- **Defence:** MIL-STD-414 for variable inspection.
- **Textile/Food:** Destructive testing of fabric strength, food-grade quality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Binomial Probability of Acceptance

In acceptance sampling, each unit in the sample is independently classified as **defective** (probability $p$) or **good** (probability $1-p$). For a sample of size $n$ with $c$ as the acceptance number, the probability of accepting the lot is the probability that the number of defectives $d$ in the sample is between 0 and $c$.

### Step 1: Define the Random Variable
Let $X$ be the number of defective units in a random sample of size $n$.

$$
X \sim Binomial(n, p)
$$

### Step 2: Write the Binomial Probability Mass Function

$$
P(X = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}, \quad d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the classical result for the number of "successes" in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials. Here, "success" = a defective unit.

### Step 3: Sum the Probabilities for $d = 0$ to $c$

$$
P_a = P(X \le c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} P(X = d)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We accept the lot if defectives are *at most* $c$. Summing the binomial pmf from $d=0$ to $d=c$ gives this cumulative probability.

### Step 4: Substitute the Binomial pmf

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula** for acceptance probability. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

After rectifying inspection, all defectives in a rejected lot are replaced by good ones. We want the **expected fraction defective** in the outgoing lots (a mix of accepted and rectifying-inspected lots).

### Step 1: Define the Components
- $P_a$ = probability of acceptance.
- Lot size = $N$, sample size = $n$.
- Rejected lots have $(N-n)$ untested units and they undergo 100% inspection.
- Number of outgoing defectives from rejected lots (after rectification) = **0**.

### Step 2: Compute Outgoing Defectives
For an **accepted lot**, the outgoing defectives consist of:
- The defectives in the sample (counted, not replaced — assumption varies; we use the standard "sample defectives not replaced" model).
- The defectives among the $(N-n)$ untested units, which are not removed.

$$
\text{Outgoing defectives (accepted lot)} = d_{sample} + d_{unsampled}
$$

### Step 3: Expected Outgoing Defectives
Expected defectives in sample = $np$.
Expected defectives in unsampled portion = $(N-n) p$.

$$
E[\text{Outgoing defectives per accepted lot}] = np + (N-n)p = Np
$$

### Step 4: Expected Number of Accepted Lots per Submission
Out of every lot submitted, $P_a$ fraction is accepted.

### Step 5: Compute AOQ
The average outgoing fraction defective is:

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiplying by $P_a$ accounts for the fact that only a fraction of lots escapes rectifying inspection. The $(N-n)$ factor excludes the sample (which is partially inspected). Dividing by $N$ converts the count to a fraction.

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbook editions write $AOQ = P_a \cdot p$ (when $N \to \infty$). The formula with $(N-n)/N$ is more accurate for finite lots and is **preferred** in KTU 2024 numerical problems.

### Step 6: AOQL
The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit** is the maximum value of $AOQ$ over all possible values of $p \in [0, 1]$:

$$
AOQL = \max_{p} \left[ \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} \right]
$$

It is found by differentiating $AOQ$ with respect to $p$ and setting to zero. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked KTU-Type Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (14-mark KTU style):**
>
> An acceptance sampling plan has $N = 1000$, $n = 50$, $c = 2$. Construct the OC curve points for $p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10$ using the Poisson approximation, and compute AOQL.

**Step A — Verify Poisson Conditions:**
$p = 0.01$ to $0.10$ (all $\le 0.10$) and $n = 50 \ge 30$ ✓. Use Poisson with mean $\lambda = np$.

**Step B — Compute $P_a$ for each $p$:**

| $p$ | $\lambda = np$ | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^d}{d!}$ |
|-----|----------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.01 | 0.5 | $e^{-0.5}[1 + 0.5 + 0.125] = 0.6065 \times 1.625 = 0.9856$ |
| 0.02 | 1.0 | $e^{-1}[1 + 1 + 0.5] = 0.3679 \times 2.5 = 0.9197$ |
| 0.04 | 2.0 | $e^{-2}[1 + 2 + 2] = 0.1353 \times 5 = 0.6767$ |
| 0.06 | 3.0 | $e^{-3}[1 + 3 + 4.5] = 0.0498 \times 8.5 = 0.4232$ |
| 0.08 | 4.0 | $e^{-4}[1 + 4 + 8] = 0.0183 \times 13 = 0.2381$ |
| 0.10 | 5.0 | $e^{-5}[1 + 5 + 12.5] = 0.0067 \times 18.5 = 0.1240$ |

**Step C — Compute AOQ for each $p$:**

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot 950}{1000} = 0.95 \cdot P_a \cdot p
$$

| $p$ | $P_a$ | $AOQ = 0.95 \cdot P_a \cdot p$ |
|-----|-------|-------------------------------|
| 0.01 | 0.9856 | 0.00937 |
| 0.02 | 0.9197 | 0.01747 |
| 0.04 | 0.6767 | 0.02571 |
| 0.06 | 0.4232 | 0.02412 |
| 0.08 | 0.2381 | 0.01809 |
| 0.10 | 0.1240 | 0.01178 |

**Step D — Identify AOQL:**
The maximum AOQ occurs at $p \approx 0.04$:

$$
\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.02571 \text{ or } 2.571\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even under the *worst possible incoming quality*, the outgoing quality will never exceed 2.571% defectives. This is a key advantage of rectifying inspection under acceptance sampling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Lot-by-Lot Acceptance Sampling Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Production Lot Arrives N units] --> B[Draw Random Sample of Size n]
    B --> C[Inspect Each Unit in Sample]
    C --> D{Count Defectives d}
    D -- d less than or equal to c --> E[ACCEPT the Lot]
    D -- d greater than c --> F[REJECT the Lot]
    E --> G[Ship to Customer]
    F --> H[Rectifying Inspection 100 percent sort]
    H --> I[Replace Defectives with Goods]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Feedback to Production Process]
    J --> K[Quality Improvement Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The arrow from "ACCEPT" to "Ship to Customer" represents a clean lot. The arrow from "REJECT" goes through rectifying inspection, where defectives are removed and replaced before shipping. The feedback loop ensures continuous improvement.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Organization Structure for Acceptance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROD[Production Department]
        P1[Manufacturing Lines]
        P2[In-Process SPC Charts]
    end

    subgraph QA[Quality Assurance Department]
        Q1[Sampling Plan Design n c alpha beta]
        Q2[Random Sample Selection]
        Q3[Inspection and Defect Counting]
        Q4[Decision Accept or Reject]
        Q5[Rectifying Inspection Station]
        Q6[OC Curve Analysis and AQL LTPD]
    end

    subgraph CUST[Customer]
        C1[Receiving Inspection]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 -- Accept --> C1
    Q4 -- Reject --> Q5
    Q5 --> C1
    Q4 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q1
    Q1 --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the QA department is **independent** of production. The OC curve analysis feeds back to **redesign** the plan if quality drifts. The customer performs an independent verification, forming a dual-gate quality system.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Single vs Double vs Multiple Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Need an Acceptance Plan] --> S1{Inspection Cost vs Risk?}
    S1 -- High cost of inspection, willing to accept higher ASN --> SP[Single Sampling Plan n c]
    S1 -- Moderate cost, want smaller ASN --> DP[Double Sampling Plan n1 c1 n2 c2]
    S1 -- Need minimum ASN, willing to administer complex rules --> MP[Multiple Sampling Plan]
    SP --> R1[Use Poisson table if p small and n large]
    DP --> R2[Compute ASN and Pa for each stage]
    MP --> R3[Compute combined OC curve across stages]
    R1 --> END[Plan Finalized]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
> **Define acceptance sampling. How does it differ from 100% inspection?**

**Model Answer:**

Acceptance sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample of size $n$ is drawn from a lot of size $N$, and based on the number of defectives $d$ found in the sample, a decision is made to accept or reject the entire lot, using a predefined acceptance number $c$.

| Aspect | 100% Inspection | Acceptance Sampling |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Cost | High (every unit) | Low (sample only) |
| Applicability | For non-destructive tests | Suitable for destructive tests |
| Inspection Error | Fatigue leads to errors | Fresh inspectors per sample |
| Time | Slow | Fast |
| Lot Protection | All units checked | Statistical protection |

Acceptance sampling is preferred when inspection is destructive, costly, or when lot sizes are very large.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**
> **What is an OC curve? What information does it provide?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** is a graphical representation of the discriminatory power of an acceptance sampling plan. It plots the probability of acceptance ($P_a$) on the y-axis against the incoming lot fraction defective ($p$) on the x-axis.

Information provided by an OC curve:
1. **Probability of acceptance** for any incoming quality $p$.
2. **Producer's Risk (α)**: $1 - P_a$ at AQL.
3. **Consumer's Risk (β)**: $P_a$ at LTPD.
4. **Indifference Quality Level** where $P_a = 0.50$.
5. **Shape of the curve** indicates the plan's discriminating power.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various types of acceptance sampling plans in detail.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An acceptance sampling plan has $N = 2000$, $n = 80$, $c = 1$. Using the Poisson approximation, compute the probability of acceptance for $p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.03$. Also, find the AOQ at $p = 0.02$.

### Part (a) Model Answer — Types of Acceptance Sampling Plans

The major types are:

**(i) Single Sampling Plan:** A single sample of size $n$ is drawn. The lot is accepted if the number of defectives $d \le c$; otherwise, it is rejected. Simple to administer, but the average sample size is always $n$.

**(ii) Double Sampling Plan:** Inspection is performed in two stages. A first sample of size $n_1$ is drawn. If $d_1 \le c_1$, the lot is accepted; if $d_1 > r_1$, it is rejected. If $c_1 < d_1 \le r_1$, a second sample of size $n_2$ is drawn. The decision is then made based on the combined defect count $(d_1 + d_2)$ against $c_2$. Average Sample Number (ASN) is lower than single plan.

**(iii) Multiple Sampling Plan:** Extension of the double plan to several stages (up to 7 or 10). The ASN is even lower, but the administrative complexity increases.

**(iv) Sequential Sampling Plan:** Inspection is performed one unit at a time. After each inspection, one of three decisions is made: accept, reject, or continue. The ASN is theoretically minimal.

**(v) Rectifying Inspection Plan:** Rejected lots are 100% inspected and all defectives are replaced or repaired. This improves the outgoing quality and is quantified by the AOQL.

### Part (b) Model Answer — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $N = 2000$, $n = 80$, $c = 1$.

**Step 1 — Verify Poisson approximation:**
$p \le 0.10$ and $n = 80 \ge 30$ ✓. Use $\lambda = np$.

**Step 2 — Compute $P_a$ for each $p$:**

For $c = 1$:

$$
P_a = e^{-\lambda}(1 + \lambda)
$$

**For $p = 0.01$:** $\lambda = 80 \times 0.01 = 0.8$

$$
P_a = e^{-0.8}(1 + 0.8) = 0.4493 \times 1.8 = 0.8088
$$

**For $p = 0.02$:** $\lambda = 80 \times 0.02 = 1.6$

$$
P_a = e^{-1.6}(1 + 1.6) = 0.2019 \times 2.6 = 0.5249
$$

**For $p = 0.03$:** $\lambda = 80 \times 0.03 = 2.4$

$$
P_a = e^{-2.4}(1 + 2.4) = 0.0907 \times 3.4 = 0.3085
$$

**Step 3 — Compute AOQ at $p = 0.02$:**

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} = \frac{0.5249 \times 0.02 \times 1920}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.5249 \times 0.02 \times 1920}{2000} = \frac{20.156}{2000} = 0.01008
$$

$$
\boxed{AOQ \approx 1.008\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget the $(N-n)$ factor in AOQ. If you use $AOQ = P_a \cdot p$, you lose **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using binomial expansion manually for large $n$ is error-prone. Use Poisson $e^{-\lambda}$ for $p \le 0.10$, $n \ge 30$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not showing the values of $\lambda = np$ at each step. KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** for clear intermediate steps.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the organization structure required for an effective acceptance sampling system in an industry. List the responsibilities of the Quality Assurance department.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The following data is obtained from an acceptance sampling plan: $N = 1500$, $n = 60$, $c = 1$. For $p = 0.05$, calculate the probability of acceptance using (i) Binomial distribution, and (ii) Poisson approximation. Comment on the difference.

### Part (a) Model Answer — Organization for Acceptance Sampling

An effective acceptance sampling system requires the following organizational structure:

**1. Quality Assurance (QA) Department — Independent Body:**
- Reports directly to top management (not under production).
- Designs and revises sampling plans based on AQL and LTPD.
- Trains inspectors and supervises inspection activities.

**2. Sampling Plan Design Cell:**
- Determines the plan type (single, double, multiple).
- Sets $n$ and $c$ based on producer's and consumer's risks.
- Prepares OC curves and AOQ tables.

**3. Sampling and Inspection Cell:**
- Uses random number tables or systematic sampling for unbiased selection.
- Maintains calibrated instruments.
- Records defectives and data accurately.

**4. Decision and Rectification Cell:**
- Applies the decision rule (accept if $d \le c$, reject if $d > c$).
- Operates the rectifying inspection station for rejected lots.
- Maintains records for feedback analysis.

**5. Feedback and Improvement Cell:**
- Computes AOQ, AOQL, ATI regularly.
- Communicates process drift to production.
- Initiates corrective and preventive actions (CAPA).

**Responsibilities of QA Department:**
- Establishing and enforcing quality standards.
- Vendor rating and supplier audits.
- Documentation as per ISO 9001:2015.
- Continuous monitoring of incoming and outgoing quality.
- Compliance with regulatory bodies (BIS, ISO, FDA).

### Part (b) Model Answer — Binomial vs Poisson

**Given:** $N = 1500$, $n = 60$, $c = 1$, $p = 0.05$.

**(i) Binomial Distribution:**

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \binom{60}{d} (0.05)^d (0.95)^{60-d}
$$

$$
P_a = \binom{60}{0}(0.05)^0(0.95)^{60} + \binom{60}{1}(0.05)^1(0.95)^{59}
$$

$$
P_a = (0.95)^{60} + 60 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{59}
$$

**Compute step-by-step:**

$$
(0.95)^{60} = e^{60 \ln 0.95} = e^{60 \times (-0.05129)} = e^{-3.0775} = 0.04607
$$

$$
(0.95)^{59} = \frac{(0.95)^{60}}{0.95} = \frac{0.04607}{0.95} = 0.04850
$$

$$
P_a = 0.04607 + 60 \times 0.05 \times 0.04850 = 0.04607 + 0.14550 = 0.19157
$$

**(ii) Poisson Approximation:**
$\lambda = np = 60 \times 0.05 = 3.0$

$$
P_a = e^{-3.0}(1 + 3.0) = 0.0498 \times 4.0 = 0.1992
$$

**Comparison:**

| Method | $P_a$ |
|--------|-------|
| Binomial | 0.1916 |
| Poisson | 0.1992 |
| **Absolute Error** | **0.0076 (≈ 0.76%)** |

**Comment:** The Poisson approximation slightly overestimates $P_a$ (by about 0.76%). The error increases as $p$ increases. For $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 30$, the Poisson approximation is acceptable for KTU exams. For higher $p$, the binomial must be used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Computing $(0.95)^{60}$ using a calculator and getting rounding errors. Show the logarithm method $e^{60 \ln 0.95}$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $(0.95)^{60}$ with $(0.95)^{59}$. Always double-check the exponent against the binomial term.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not commenting on the difference between binomial and Poisson — KTU awards **1 mark** explicitly for the "Comment on the difference" instruction.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using $\lambda = p$ instead of $\lambda = np$ in the Poisson formula. This is the most common error and costs **2 full marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Organization for Acceptance":**

- ✅ Acceptance sampling is a **lot-by-lot decision procedure**, not a process improvement tool.
- ✅ The plan is defined by three core parameters: **lot size $N$, sample size $n$, acceptance number $c$**.
- ✅ Decision rule: **Accept if $d \le c$; Reject if $d > c$**.
- ✅ **Poisson approximation** is valid when $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 30$. Use $\lambda = np$.
- ✅ **OC curve** is the most important graphical tool — it shows $P_a$ vs $p$.
- ✅ **AQL** is the producer's quality target; **LTPD** is the consumer's risk level.
- ✅ **AOQ** = $P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n) / N$ — average quality of outgoing lots.
- ✅ **AOQL** is the *maximum* AOQ over all $p$ — worst-case outgoing quality.
- ✅ **ATI** = $n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ — total inspection effort.
- ✅ **ASN (Double plan)** = $n_1 + (1-P_1) n_2$, where $P_1$ is the probability of going to second sample.
- ✅ Rectifying inspection is performed on **rejected lots** to improve outgoing quality.
- ✅ QA department must be **independent of production** for unbiased decisions.
- ✅ ISO 9001:2015 mandates documented procedures for acceptance sampling in supplier control.
- ✅ Plan types: Single, Double, Multiple, Sequential, Rectifying.
- ✅ **Producer's risk $\alpha$** is Type I error; **Consumer's risk $\beta$** is Type II error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always read the question carefully to identify whether the problem is asking for $P_a$, AOQ, AOQL, ATI, or ASN. These are easily confused and each carries a distinct formula. Practice 3–4 numerical problems before the exam for full confidence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Planning for Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Planning for Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Manufacturing Planning for Quality (MPQ)** is a structured, systematic, and engineering-driven framework that integrates quality considerations into every stage of the product life cycle, beginning from market research, product design, process planning, production, and culminating at customer service. It is the proactive counterpart of Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — whereas SQC *detects* and *corrects* variation after the fact, MPQ *prevents* variation by designing quality into the product, the process, and the system before manufacturing begins.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPET415), Manufacturing Planning for Quality rests on the **Juran Trilogy** — three universal managerial processes:

$$ \text{Quality} = f(\text{Quality Planning},\ \text{Quality Control},\ \text{Quality Improvement}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Manufacturing Planning for Quality emphasizes **Strategic Quality Planning, Customer Focus, Quality Function Deployment (QFD), and Process Capability Analysis (Cp, Cpk)** as the foundational pillars before any statistical control chart is applied.

### 1.2 Definitions by Quality Gurus (Board-Favourite)

| Guru | Definition of Quality |
|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | *Fitness for Use* — the product must satisfy customer needs throughout its life cycle. |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | Quality is a *predictable degree of uniformity and dependability*, at low cost, suited to the market. |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | *Conformance to Requirements* — zero defects philosophy. |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | *Total Quality Control* — an integrative system involving all functions of the firm. |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality is *the continuous satisfaction of customer requirements* and the *foremost concern of the entire company*. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

> [!NOTE]
> **The "House Construction" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are building a house. *Reactive quality control* is like inspecting the house *after* it is built and tearing down the wall that cracked. *Manufacturing Planning for Quality* is like hiring a structural engineer *before* laying the foundation — he checks the soil, designs the columns, calculates the load, and ensures every beam meets the building code. The house is "born strong."
>
> Similarly, in a manufacturing plant (say, a CNC machined shaft for a car), MPQ ensures that the lathe, the cutting tool, the operator training, the tolerance on the blueprint, and the inspection plan are *all engineered* such that the shaft is *born within specification* — the control chart is then just a confirmation, not a rescue tool.

### 1.4 Dimensions of Manufactured Quality (Garvin's 8 Dimensions)

These are the **8 attributes** by which customers judge a product, and every MPQ exercise must explicitly address them:

1. **Performance** — primary operating characteristics.
2. **Features** — supplementary characteristics (e.g., Bluetooth in a car).
3. **Reliability** — probability of failure-free operation over time.
4. **Conformance** — degree to which product meets specifications.
5. **Durability** — length of useful life.
6. **Serviceability** — speed and ease of repair.
7. **Aesthetics** — sensory appeal (look, feel, sound).
8. **Perceived Quality** — image and reputation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Juran Trilogy — Foundation of MPQ

Juran's framework is the *theological spine* of Manufacturing Planning for Quality. Each of the three processes has a distinct sequence of activities.

#### (A) Quality Planning (QP)
The structured activity of developing products and processes required to meet customer needs.

**The Juran Quality Planning Roadmap (The QP Road Map):**
1. **Identify the customers** — internal and external.
2. **Determine customer needs (THE VOICE OF THE CUSTOMER — VOC)** — using interviews, surveys, focus groups, Kano model.
3. **Develop product features** that respond to those needs.
4. **Develop process features** capable of producing those product features.
5. **Establish process controls** (control points, SPC charts) — transferred to Quality Control.
6. **Transfer the plan to operating forces** (Production, QC, Supply Chain).

#### (B) Quality Control (QC)
The operational techniques and activities used to fulfill requirements for quality. Statistical Quality Control charts (X̄-R, p, c, etc.) belong here.

#### (C) Quality Improvement (QI)
A never-ending, structured program of *kaizen* improvements to reduce variation and chronic waste.

### 2.2 Strategic Quality Planning

A long-term, firm-wide commitment captured in the **Quality Policy Statement** and the **Strategic Quality Plan**.

- **Quality Policy:** A top-management declaration of *intent* and *principles* regarding quality.
- **Quality Objectives:** SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) goals derived from the policy.
- **Strategic Quality Plan:** A multi-year roadmap of *projects, resources, training, and metrics* to achieve the objectives.

### 2.3 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — The "House of Quality"

QFD is the most powerful MPQ tool introduced in the syllabus. It translates **WHATs (customer requirements)** into **HOWs (engineering characteristics)** using a **planning matrix**.

The four phases of QFD are:
- **Phase 1:** Product Planning (House of Quality).
- **Phase 2:** Product Design (deployment of HOWs into part characteristics).
- **Phase 3:** Process Planning (deploying into manufacturing operations).
- **Phase 4:** Production Planning (production controls).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they appear in 7-mark derivations and 14-mark numerical problems.

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Process Capability Index ($C_p$) | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless; assumes centered process |
| Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Accounts for process mean shift |
| Process Performance Index ($P_p$) | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Uses sample standard deviation $s$ |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | Six-Sigma metric |
| Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | $Y_i$ = yield of process $i$ |
| First-Time Yield (FTY) | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units Out}}{\text{Total Units In}}$ | Decimal or % |
| Cost of Quality (CoQ) | $\text{CoQ} = P + A + I + E$ | Prevention, Appraisal, Internal failure, External failure |
| Z-Score (Standard Normal) | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Used in normal-tolerance calculations |
| Tolerance Design ($T = 6\sigma$) | $T = USL - LSL = 6\sigma$ | Traditional "tolerance on $6\sigma$" rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Cp vs Cpk:**
> - $C_p$ asks: *"Is the process spread narrow enough?"* (potential capability).
> - $C_{pk}$ asks: *"Is the process spread narrow AND centered?"* (actual capability).
> - Always: $C_{pk} \leq C_p$.

### 2.5 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — P-A-I-E Model

A cornerstone concept for manufacturing planning — the *economics* of doing quality right.

- **P (Prevention Costs):** Training, quality planning, supplier capability, design reviews. *Proactive* costs.
- **A (Appraisal Costs):** Inspection, testing, calibration of gauges, audits.
- **I (Internal Failure Costs):** Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime.
- **E (External Failure Costs):** Warranty claims, returns, recalls, customer complaints, lawsuits.

The **optimal CoQ** is achieved when the sum $P + A + I + E$ is **minimized**; this usually occurs at a $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$ (Three-Sigma quality), with diminishing returns beyond.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Process Capability Indices ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

#### Step 1: Setting up the problem
A manufacturing process is modeled as a normal distribution:

$$ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) $$

By the **empirical rule (68-95-99.7)**, a stable process occupies a natural spread of $\pm 3\sigma$ about the mean — i.e., **99.73%** of all output lies within the interval $[\mu - 3\sigma,\ \mu + 3\sigma]$.

The **total natural tolerance** of a process is therefore:

$$ \text{Natural Tolerance} = 6\sigma $$

#### Step 2: Defining Process Capability ($C_p$)

**Logic:** A process is *capable* if its natural spread ($6\sigma$) is smaller than the design tolerance window ($USL - LSL$). The ratio of these two is the capability index $C_p$.

$$ C_p = \frac{\text{Design Tolerance Width}}{\text{Natural Process Spread}} $$

$$ \boxed{C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}} $$

**Interpretation of $C_p$:**
- $C_p = 1.00$ → process just barely fits; 0.27% defect rate (2700 DPMO).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → industry minimum acceptable; ~63 DPMO.
- $C_p = 1.50$ → preferred; ~5.4 DPMO.
- $C_p = 2.00$ → Six Sigma territory; 0.002 DPMO.

#### Step 3: Accounting for Off-Center Processes — Deriving $C_{pk}$

**Logic:** $C_p$ assumes the process mean $\mu$ is exactly at the center of the specification, $\mu_0 = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}$. In reality, processes drift. We must penalize the index for the distance of $\mu$ from the nearest specification limit.

The distance from $\mu$ to the **Lower** Specification Limit is $\mu - LSL$.
The distance from $\mu$ to the **Upper** Specification Limit is $USL - \mu$.

We divide each by the **one-sided natural spread** of $3\sigma$ (since we are now measuring the clearance to *one* limit, not the total):

$$ C_{pk,\ lower} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} $$

$$ C_{pk,\ upper} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} $$

The **actual capability** is governed by the *worst* (smallest) of these two clearances:

$$ \boxed{C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma},\ \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)} $$

#### Step 4: Equivalence to a Single-Number Formula

By substituting $\mu_0 = \frac{USL+LSL}{2}$ and $k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - \mu_0\vert}{T/2}$ (the **centering factor**), one obtains the alternative compact form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}\bigl(1 - k\bigr) \\
       &= C_p(1 - k) \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{\vert \mu - \mu_0 \vert}{(USL-LSL)/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves rigorously that **$C_{pk} \leq C_p$**, with equality *only* when the process is perfectly centered ($k=0$).

#### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

A shaft is machined to a specification: $USL = 25.10$ mm, $LSL = 24.90$ mm. The process mean is $\mu = 25.02$ mm and $\sigma = 0.03$ mm.

**Compute $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
    &= \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.03} \\
    &= \frac{0.20}{0.18} \\
    &= 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} &= \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.12}{0.09} = 1.333 \\
\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} &= \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.08}{0.09} = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.333,\ 0.889) = 0.889 $$

**Conclusion:** The process is *shifted high*. Even though the *potential* capability $C_p = 1.11$ is barely acceptable, the *actual* capability $C_{pk} = 0.89 < 1.00$ indicates the process is **incapable** — corrective action is required (re-center the machine, reduce $\sigma$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Juran Trilogy — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Market Research and Customer Identification]
    B[Determine Customer Needs - VOC]
    C[Develop Product Features - WHATs]
    D[Develop Process Features - HOWs]
    E[Establish Process Controls - Control Plan]
    F[Transfer to Operating Forces]
    G[Quality Control - SPC Charts]
    H[Quality Improvement - Kaizen Projects]
    I[Feedback Loop - Lessons to Planning]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> C
```

### 4.2 The House of Quality (QFD) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1[ROOF - Correlation Matrix]
        HOWS[HOWs Interrelationships]
    end
    subgraph Q2[LEFT WALL - Voice of Customer]
        VOC[Customer Requirements WHATs]
    end
    subgraph Q3[CENTRAL - Planning Matrix]
        MATRIX[Relationship Strength]
    end
    subgraph Q4[RIGHT WALL - Competitive Benchmarking]
        BENCH[Competitor Ratings]
    end
    subgraph Q5[BASEMENT - Technical Response]
        TECH[Engineering Characteristics HOWs]
    end
    subgraph Q6[FLOOR - Targets]
        TARGETS[Specifications and Priorities]
    end
    VOC --> MATRIX
    MATRIX --> BENCH
    TECH --> MATRIX
    MATRIX --> HOWS
    TECH --> TARGETS
```

### 4.3 Process Capability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Collect at least 25 subgroups of 5 measurements]
    P2[Verify Statistical Control - Run Chart or Xbar R]
    P3{Is the process in control?}
    P4[Do not calculate Cp or Cpk]
    P5[Compute mu and sigma]
    P6{Is Cpk greater than 1.33?}
    P7[Process is capable and acceptable]
    P8[Process is marginal - improve centering]
    P9[Process is incapable - reduce sigma or recenter]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 -- No --> P4
    P3 -- Yes --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 -- Yes --> P7
    P6 -- Equal to 1.0 --> P8
    P6 -- No --> P9
```

### 4.4 Cost of Quality — P-A-I-E Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Total Cost of Quality]
    P[Prevention Costs]
    A[Appraisal Costs]
    I[Internal Failure Costs]
    E[External Failure Costs]

    P --> Q
    A --> Q
    I --> Q
    E --> Q

    P1[Training and Quality Planning] --> P
    P2[Supplier Capability and Design Review] --> P
    A1[Inspection and Testing] --> A
    A2[Calibration and Audits] --> A
    I1[Scrap and Rework] --> I
    I2[Re-inspection and Downtime] --> I
    E1[Warranty Claims and Returns] --> E
    E2[Recalls and Customer Complaints] --> E
```
