## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Manufacturing Planning for Quality. State the three processes of the Juran Trilogy.**

**Model Answer:**
Manufacturing Planning for Quality (MPQ) is a systematic engineering approach that integrates quality considerations into every stage of the product and process life cycle, ensuring that products are designed and produced to meet customer needs reliably and economically.

Juran's Trilogy consists of three universal managerial processes:
1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Developing products and processes to meet customer needs (the QP Road Map: identify customers → determine needs → develop product features → develop process features → establish controls → transfer to operations).
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Operational techniques to evaluate performance and compare with goals, taking corrective action.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** A structured, continuous program to reduce chronic waste and variation, raising performance to unprecedented levels.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three processes with brief explanation.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Differentiate between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ with formulas. Why is $C_{pk}$ preferred in modern industry?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$ |
| Assumes | Process is centered | Process may be off-center |
| Measures | *Potential* capability | *Actual* capability |
| Value | $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ | Always $\leq C_p$ |

$C_{pk}$ is preferred because in real manufacturing, processes are never perfectly centered. $C_{pk}$ reflects *actual* performance including shift, whereas $C_p$ overstates capability when the mean drifts.

*(3 marks: 1 each for formula, difference, and justification.)*

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the Juran Quality Planning Road Map in detail. How does it differ from the Deming PDCA cycle? **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A precision bearing race has specifications $USL = 30.025$ mm and $LSL = 29.975$ mm. After gathering 25 subgroups of size 5, the grand average is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 30.008$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm. The process is verified to be in statistical control. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Juran's Quality Planning Road Map vs. Deming's PDCA (7 marks):**

Juran's Quality Planning (QP) Road Map is a structured **6-step sequence** for designing quality into a new product or process before production begins:

1. **Identify the customers** — both internal (next workstation) and external (end-user).
2. **Determine customer needs** — capture the *Voice of the Customer* (VOC) through surveys, interviews, focus groups, and the Kano model. Output: a list of prioritized *WHATs*.
3. **Develop product features** — translate WHATs into measurable engineering characteristics (*HOWs*) using QFD / House of Quality.
4. **Develop process features** — design the manufacturing process capable of achieving the product features; identify critical process parameters.
5. **Establish process controls** — set up control points, SPC charts, FMEAs, and operator instructions.
6. **Transfer to operating forces** — hand over the plan to Production, Quality, and Supply Chain with full training.

**Comparison with Deming's PDCA:**
- **PDCA** is a *continuous improvement* loop (Plan → Do → Check → Act) suited for **Quality Improvement** of an existing process.
- **Juran's QP Road Map** is a *linear, project-based* planning sequence suited for **Quality Planning** of a *new* product or process.
- PDCA is reactive-cyclical; Juran's Roadmap is proactive-linear.
- They are **complementary, not substitutes** — Juran's Trilogy (QP, QC, QI) and Deming's PDCA together form a complete quality system.

*(Award 5 marks for the 6 steps with 1-line explanations, 1 mark for PDCA, 1 mark for comparison.)*

---

**Part (b) — Capability Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $USL = 30.025$ mm, $LSL = 29.975$ mm
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = 30.008$ mm
- $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm
- Subgroup size $n = 5$; for $n=5$, the control-chart constant $d_2 = 2.326$.

**Step 1: Estimate the process standard deviation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.012}{2.326} = 0.00516\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the capability index $C_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} \\
    &= \frac{30.025 - 29.975}{6 \times 0.00516} \\
    &= \frac{0.050}{0.03096} \\
    &= 1.615
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} &= \frac{30.008 - 29.975}{3 \times 0.00516} = \frac{0.033}{0.01548} = 2.131 \\
\frac{USL - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} &= \frac{30.025 - 30.008}{3 \times 0.00516} = \frac{0.017}{0.01548} = 1.098
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ C_{pk} = \min(2.131,\ 1.098) = 1.098 $$

**Step 4: Comment.**
- $C_p = 1.62$ indicates the *spread* of the process is excellent (well above 1.33).
- $C_{pk} = 1.10$ indicates the process is **shifted high** (mean 30.008 vs. target midpoint 30.000).
- The process is **capable** ($C_{pk} > 1.00$) but not optimally centered. Action: re-center the machine toward $\mu = 30.000$ mm to push $C_{pk}$ toward 1.62.

*(Award 1 mark for $\hat{\sigma}$, 2 for $C_p$, 3 for $C_{pk}$ calculations, 1 for comment.)*

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATE CHOICE) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q. (a)** What is Quality Function Deployment (QFD)? With a neat sketch, explain the **House of Quality** and list the information it contains. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** The following data refer to a manufacturing line of 4 sequential processes. The unit cost of rework is ₹40, and the unit cost of scrap is ₹80. The selling price is ₹500 per good unit. Input to the line is 1000 units.

| Process | Output (Good) | Rework | Scrap |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 900 | 60 | 40 |
| 2 | 820 | 50 | 30 |
| 3 | 770 | 30 | 20 |
| 4 | 740 | 20 | 10 |

Calculate the **First-Time Yield (FTY)** and the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**. Compute the total cost of quality for the line. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — QFD and the House of Quality (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured planning methodology, developed by Yoji Akao in Japan in 1966, that translates customer needs (the *Voice of the Customer* / *WHATs*) into specific engineering and manufacturing requirements (*HOWs*) at every stage of product development. Its primary visual tool is the **House of Quality**.

**Components of the House of Quality (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):**

1. **Left Wall (Customer Requirements / WHATs):** Prioritized list of customer needs (e.g., "easy to use", "long battery life").
2. **Central Matrix (Planning Matrix):** Cells indicating the *strength of relationship* between each WHAT and each HOW — typically scored as 9 (strong), 3 (medium), 1 (weak), 0 (none).
3. **Ceiling / Roof (Correlation Matrix):** Shows how the HOWs relate to *each other* — positive (synergy) or negative (trade-off).
4. **Right Wall (Competitive Benchmarking):** Ratings of competitors on the same WHATs — identifies market gaps.
5. **Basement (Technical Response / HOWs):** Measurable engineering characteristics (e.g., "battery capacity in mAh", "weight in grams").
6. **Floor (Targets and Priorities):** Numerical targets for each HOW, weighted by importance to customers.
7. **Output:** The "Weight" of each HOW — a ranked list of which engineering characteristics most deserve engineering effort.

**Significance:** QFD prevents *over-engineering* and *under-engineering* simultaneously by focusing R&D on features customers actually value.

*(Award 3 marks for definition and concept, 2 marks for the six parts of the house, 2 marks for significance.)*

---

**Part (b) — Yield and Cost-of-Quality Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate yield $Y_i$ for each process.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= \frac{900}{1000} = 0.900 \\
Y_2 &= \frac{820}{900} = 0.911 \\
Y_3 &= \frac{770}{820} = 0.939 \\
Y_4 &= \frac{740}{770} = 0.961
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\
           &= 0.900 \times 0.911 \times 0.939 \times 0.961 \\
           &= 0.7411 \\
           &= 74.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: First-Time Yield (FTY) — overall.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FTY} &= \frac{740\ \text{good units}}{1000\ \text{input units}} = 0.740 = 74.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cost of Quality (Internal Failure Costs).**

Total rework units = $60 + 50 + 30 + 20 = 160$.
Total scrap units = $40 + 30 + 20 + 10 = 100$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Internal Failure Cost} &= (160 \times ₹40) + (100 \times ₹80) \\
                            &= ₹6{,}400 + ₹8{,}000 \\
                            &= ₹14{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** RTY = 74.11% means that only **741 units out of every 1000 inputs** survive the entire line without *any* rework. The opportunity cost (lost contribution margin) for the 100 scrapped units is $100 \times ₹500 = ₹50{,}000$ in lost sales — a strong motivator for MPQ investments in prevention.

*(Award 1 mark per yield, 2 marks for RTY, 1 for FTY, 2 for cost.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** Students often use the *sample* standard deviation $s$ for $C_p$ and the *process* $\sigma$ for $C_{pk}$. Use $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ consistently when subgroup data is given.
> 2. **Forgetting the centering factor:** $C_p$ can never exceed $C_{pk}$ — if your answer does, it is wrong; re-check the math.
> 3. **Juran vs. Deming confusion:** Juran's Trilogy is *not* PDCA. PDCA is a single improvement loop; the Trilogy is the *managerial* umbrella under which PDCA lives.
> 4. **House of Quality labels:** A common sketch error is to swap the "Roof" (HOW-HOW correlation) and the "Right Wall" (competitive benchmark). Memorize the layout.
> 5. **RTY vs. FTY:** RTY is the *product of yields*; FTY is the *ratio of good output to total input*. Many students report them as equal — they are NOT.
> 6. **Unit confusion in $C_p$:** Both numerator and denominator must be in the *same* units; do not mix mm and cm.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MPQ = Juran Trilogy + QFD + Capability Analysis + CoQ.**
- **Juran Trilogy = QP + QC + QI** — QP happens *before* production; QC happens *during*; QI is *continuous*.
- **Quality Planning Road Map has 6 steps** — VOC is Step 2, the most critical.
- **Garvin's 8 dimensions** of quality are exam-favourites for 3-mark questions.
- **$C_p$ measures spread; $C_{pk}$ measures spread + centering.** Always report both.
- **Benchmark $C_{pk}$ values:** $<1$ incapable; $1\text{–}1.33$ marginal; $>1.33$ capable; $>1.67$ excellent; $>2.0$ Six-Sigma.
- **For subgroup data:** use $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ (with $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n=5$, $d_2 = 2.574$ for $n=4$, $d_2 = 3.078$ for $n=3$).
- **QFD = House of Quality** — six components: WHATs, HOWs, Central Matrix, Roof, Right Wall, Floor.
- **RTY = product of stage yields**; **FTY = good-out / total-in**; **RTY $\leq$ FTY always**.
- **Cost of Quality = P + A + I + E**; prevention cost is the *only* category that *decreases* all others.
- **DPMO** is the Six-Sigma unit for defect rate; **target for Six-Sigma quality** is $3.4$ DPMO.
- **Juran's view:** quality is *fitness for use* — focus on the *customer*, not the *product*.
- **Crosby's view:** quality is *conformance to requirements* — focus on *zero defects* and *prevention*.
- **Deming's view:** quality is *predictable uniformity* — focus on *the system*, not the worker (85% of problems are system-related).
- **Feigenbaum's view:** quality is the *responsibility of everyone* — Total Quality Control.
- **The 4-Phase QFD deployment:** Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning.
- **Remember the "P-A-I-E" model order** — Prevention, Appraisal, Internal failure, External failure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning for Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planning for Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Process Control** is a statistical methodology used to monitor, evaluate, and maintain a manufacturing or business process at its desired performance level by studying the inherent variation in output through carefully designed sampling procedures and graphical tools (control charts).

**Planning for Process Control** is the systematic, pre-implementation phase in which the quality engineer decides:

- Which quality characteristic is to be controlled,
- What type of data will be collected (variable or attribute),
- Which control chart is most appropriate,
- The **subgroup size** $(n)$,
- The **sampling frequency** $(h)$,
- The **rational subgrouping** strategy,
- The **Action Limits** and rules for interpretation,
- The required **process capability** before deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Planning for Process Control precedes the actual use of any Shewhart control chart. It transforms a vague "monitor the process" instruction into a measurable, statistically valid quality control plan.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. You do not steer by looking at every single pebble on the road. Instead:

- You set a **lane** (the specification limits),
- You glance at the road every few seconds (the **sampling frequency**),
- You watch the steering wheel (the **control chart**),
- If the car drifts more than a small amount, you correct it; if the car is wobbling, something is wrong with the wheels (special cause).

**Planning for Process Control** is precisely this: deciding *what to glance at, how often, in what groups, and what to do if a deviation is detected*, *before* the actual journey (production) begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** A process that operates with only **common cause variation** is said to be in a state of **statistical control**. The goal of planning is to ensure we can distinguish routine noise (common cause) from real problems (special cause).

## 1.3 The Two Faces of Variation

Every process exhibits variation. Walter Shewhart classified this variation into two types:

| Variation Type | Description | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Common Cause** | Inherent, built into the process; predictable, random | Manage the **system** (re-engineer) |
| **Special Cause** | External, assignable, unusual, non-random | Investigate the **specific event** |

A process control plan must be powerful enough to **signal special causes** promptly, yet robust enough to **not react to common cause noise**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Stage Planning Framework

### Stage 1 — Identification of Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) Characteristics
Identify which dimensions or attributes directly affect customer satisfaction. Pareto analysis or **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** is typically used.

### Stage 2 — Determination of Data Type
- **Variable (Continuous) Data:** Length, weight, viscosity, tensile strength.
- **Attribute (Discrete) Data:** Pass/Fail, number of defects, defectives per unit.

### Stage 3 — Selection of the Appropriate Control Chart
The decision rule is summarized in the **Control Chart Selection Matrix** (see Section 4).

### Stage 4 — Determination of Subgroup Size $(n)$
Subgroup size $n$ governs the chart's ability to detect process shifts. Two contradictory objectives must be balanced:

- **Small $n$** → easy to collect, but the chart is *insensitive* to small shifts.
- **Large $n$** → powerful detection, but *expensive* and may include within-subgroup variability.

### Stage 5 — Determination of Sampling Frequency $(h)$
The interval between successive subgroups. It depends on:
- The **process stability** history,
- The **cost of inspection**,
- The **rate of production**,
- The **magnitude of shift** the chart must detect.

### Stage 6 — Rational Subgrouping
A **rational subgroup** is a set of observations selected in such a way that:

1. Each subgroup represents a "snapshot" of the process at one instant.
2. Variation *within* a subgroup contains only **common cause** variation.
3. Variation *between* subgroups maximizes the chance of detecting a **special cause**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of Rational Subgrouping (Wheeler):** *"Subgroups should be selected so that if a special cause is present, its chance of appearing between subgroups is maximized, while its chance of appearing within subgroups is minimized."*

### Stage 7 — Phase I vs Phase II Deployment
- **Phase I (Retrospective / Pilot):** Use $20$ to $25$ preliminary subgroups to estimate the process mean and standard deviation, then establish *trial* control limits.
- **Phase II (Prospective / Routine):** Use the validated limits from Phase I to monitor live production and detect future special causes.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **must-know** formulas for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) under the 2024 scheme. Memorize the boundary conditions and the meaning of every constant.

### A. Control Limits — Variable Data

For an X-bar and R chart with subgroup size $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \, \bar{R} \\
\text{Center Line}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \, \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{R} &= D_4 \, \bar{R} \\
\text{Center Line}_{R} &= \bar{R} \\
\text{LCL}_{R} &= D_3 \, \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are **tabulated control chart constants** that depend only on $n$.

### B. Control Limits — Attribute Data (p-chart)

For a fraction non-conforming chart with sample size $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{p} &= \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} \\
\sigma_p &= \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}\,(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
\text{UCL}_p &= \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p \\
\text{LCL}_p &= \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p
\end{aligned}
$$

### C. Control Limits — Attribute Data (c-chart, defects per unit)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{c} &= \frac{\text{Total defects}}{\text{Number of units}} \\
\text{UCL}_c &= \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} \\
\text{LCL}_c &= \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### D. Process Capability Indices

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

If only one specification limit exists (e.g., minimum strength), use $C_{pu}$ or $C_{pl}$:

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
$$

### E. Sample Size to Detect a Specified Shift

To detect a shift from $p_1$ to $p_2$ in fraction non-conforming (two-sided test):

$$
n = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}\right)^2 \, p_1(1-p_1)}{\left(p_2 - p_1\right)^2}
$$

To detect a shift $\delta$ in the process mean:

$$
n = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}\right)^2 \, \sigma^2}{\delta^2}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Subgroup size | items |
| $A_2, D_3, D_4$ | Shewhart control chart constants | dimensionless |
| $\bar{p}$ | Average fraction non-conforming | dimensionless |
| $C_p$ | Process Capability (potential) | dimensionless |
| $C_{pk}$ | Process Capability (actual) | dimensionless |
| $Z_{\alpha/2}$ | Standard normal critical value | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpreting Capability:** $C_p \ge 1.33$ → capable; $C_p \ge 1.67$ → excellent; $C_p \le 1.00$ → **not capable**.
> **Interpreting $C_{pk}$:** $C_{pk} = C_p$ → process is **centered**; $C_{pk} \ll C_p$ → process is **off-center**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **automotive industry** (e.g., Bosch, Maruti Suzuki), process control plans dictate every machining cell. The crankshaft diameter X-bar and R chart with $n = 5$ is run every 30 minutes.

In **pharmaceutical manufacturing** (US FDA-cGMP environment), $C_{pk}$ targets of $\ge 1.50$ are mandated. The planning phase ensures the right chart and sample size are chosen *before* validation batches begin.

In **semiconductor fabrication**, attribute charts (defects per wafer) are used because critical dimensions are sub-nanometer and cannot be economically measured on every unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Control Limits for the X-bar Chart

The X-bar chart monitors the **mean of subgroups** of size $n$. By the Central Limit Theorem, the subgroup means are normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.

**Step 1:** Define the standard error of the subgroup mean.

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 2:** The control limits are set at $\pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ from the process mean (the famous **three-sigma limits** introduced by Shewhart).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \mu - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** In practice, $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown and are replaced by sample estimates from $k$ preliminary subgroups.

The grand average is:

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i
$$

The average range is:

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i
$$

**Step 4:** Relate $\sigma$ to $\bar{R}$ using the constant $d_2$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

Substitute into the UCL/LCL expressions:

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3 \bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

where $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$ is a tabulated constant. (For $n=5$, $A_2 = 0.577$.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Process Capability Index $C_p$

**Step 1:** A capable process must fit within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean inside the specification window. The **natural tolerance** of the process is therefore:

$$
\text{Natural Tolerance} = 6\sigma
$$

**Step 2:** The specification width is:

$$
\text{Specification Width} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}
$$

**Step 3:** The capability index is the *ratio* of specification width to process spread:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

> **Logic Explanation:** A value of $C_p = 1.00$ means the process exactly fills the specification window. Any shift will produce non-conforming output. $C_p > 1.33$ indicates a comfortable margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$

$C_p$ assumes the process is **perfectly centered**. To penalize off-center processes, we evaluate the *one-sided* distances:

**Step 1:** Distance from mean to upper specification limit:

$$
d_U = \text{USL} - \mu
$$

**Step 2:** Distance from mean to lower specification limit:

$$
d_L = \mu - \text{LSL}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert each to "sigma units":

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{d_U}{3\sigma} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{d_L}{3\sigma} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 4:** $C_{pk}$ is the *minimum* of the two — the *limiting* capability:

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})
$$

> **Logic Explanation:** A process can be $C_p = 1.50$ (very capable) but $C_{pk} = 0.80$ (in trouble) if it has drifted toward the specification limit. $C_{pk}$ is therefore the **true engineering metric**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sample Size to Detect a Shift in a p-chart

We want the probability of a Type II error $\beta$ (failing to detect a real shift) to be small.

**Step 1:** For a process with true fraction non-conforming $p_1$, the standard error of $p$ is:

$$
\sigma_{p_1} = \sqrt{\frac{p_1(1-p_1)}{n}}
$$

**Step 2:** For the same chart to detect a shift to $p_2$, the distance between the two means (in standard error units) must equal $Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}$:

$$
\frac{\vert p_2 - p_1 \vert}{\sigma_{p_1}} = Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}
$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides and rearrange:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p_2 - p_1)^2 &= (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \frac{p_1(1-p_1)}{n} \\
n &= \frac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \, p_1(1-p_1)}{(p_2 - p_1)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation:** This is the same logic that underlies power analysis in classical statistics. Larger $Z_{\beta}$ (greater power) → larger $n$. Smaller shift $(p_2 - p_1)$ → very large $n$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

A precision lathe produces shafts with $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.995$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.018$ mm, based on $k = 20$ subgroups of size $n = 5$. The specification limits are $24.90$ mm and $25.10$ mm.

**Step 1:** From the table of control chart constants, for $n = 5$:
- $A_2 = 0.577$
- $d_2 = 2.326$

**Step 2:** Compute X-bar chart limits.

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 24.995 + 0.577 \times 0.018 = 24.995 + 0.0104 = 25.0054 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 24.995 - 0.0104 = 24.9846 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Estimate process standard deviation.

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.018}{2.326} = 0.00774 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $C_p$.

$$
C_p = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.00774} = \frac{0.20}{0.04644} = 4.31
$$

> **Interpretation:** $C_p = 4.31$ is extraordinarily capable. The process spread $(0.0464$ mm$)$ is only 23% of the specification window $(0.20$ mm$)$.

**Step 5:** Compute $C_{pk}$ assuming $\mu = 24.995$ mm.

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{25.10 - 24.995}{3 \times 0.00774} = \frac{0.105}{0.02322} = 4.52
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{24.995 - 24.90}{0.02322} = \frac{0.095}{0.02322} = 4.09
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(4.52,\ 4.09) = 4.09
$$

> **Interpretation:** $C_{pk} = 4.09$ indicates the process is **centered** (close to the midpoint 25.00) and **very capable**. No immediate action is needed.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Planning for Process Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start of Planning]) --> stepA[Stage 1: Identify CTQ Characteristics]
    stepA --> stepB[Stage 2: Classify Data Type Variable or Attribute]
    stepB --> stepC[Stage 3: Select Appropriate Control Chart]
    stepC --> stepD[Stage 4: Determine Subgroup Size n]
    stepD --> stepE[Stage 5: Determine Sampling Frequency h]
    stepE --> stepF[Stage 6: Apply Rational Subgrouping Rule]
    stepF --> stepG[Stage 7: Phase I Retrospective Data Collection]
    stepG --> stepH[Compute Trial Control Limits and Sigma]
    stepH --> stepI{Capability Acceptable Cpk greater than threshold}
    stepI -->|No| stepJ[Re Engineer Process then Loop Back]
    stepJ --> stepG
    stepI -->|Yes| stepK[Validate and Freeze Control Limits]
    stepK --> stepL[Stage 8: Phase II Routine Monitoring]
    stepL --> stepM[Apply Out of Control Action Rules]
    stepM --> endA([Process Under Statistical Control])
```

> **Reading Guide:** The feedback loop from "Re-Engineer" back to "Phase I Data Collection" represents the iterative nature of capability improvement. Control limits are not sacred; they are revised when the process itself is fundamentally changed.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Control Chart Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dataA[Quality Data Available] --> dataB{Data Type}
    dataB -->|Variable Continuous| dataC{Subgroup Size n}
    dataC -->|n equals 1| chart1[Individuals I and Moving Range MR Chart]
    dataC -->|n between 2 and 10| chart2[X bar and R Chart]
    dataC -->|n greater than 10| chart3[X bar and S Chart]
    dataC -->|Need to detect small shifts| chart4[EWMA or CUSUM Chart]
    dataB -->|Attribute Discrete| dataD{Sample Size}
    dataD -->|Constant n, count defectives| chart5[np Chart]
    dataD -->|Variable n, fraction defectives| chart6[p Chart]
    dataD -->|Count of defects c| chart7[c Chart]
    dataD -->|Defects per unit u, variable n| chart8[u Chart]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Variable charts exploit the *magnitude* of measurements (more information per unit), while attribute charts are simpler and cheaper. Choose the most **powerful** chart that is **economically feasible** for the application.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Phase I vs Phase II Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phaseA[Phase I Retrospective Setup] --> phaseB[Collect 20 to 25 Subgroups]
    phaseB --> phaseC[Compute Trial Limits Using X bar bar and R bar]
    phaseC --> phaseD[Remove Out of Control Points and Recompute]
    phaseD --> phaseE{Final Limits Stable}
    phaseE -->|No| phaseB
    phaseE -->|Yes| phaseF[Freeze Control Limits and Sigma Estimate]
    phaseF --> phaseG[Phase II Prospective Monitoring]
    phaseG --> phaseH[Plot New Subgroups on Frozen Chart]
    phaseH --> phaseI{Any Point Outside Limits or Non Random Pattern}
    phaseI -->|No| phaseG
    phaseI -->|Yes| phaseJ[Trigger Corrective Action Investigation]
    phaseJ --> phaseK[Document Special Cause and Remove]
    phaseK --> phaseG
```

> **Reading Guide:** Phase I establishes the "voice of the process." Phase II listens for changes to that voice. The two phases have *different* statistical properties and *different* goals.

## 4.4 Process Variation Behavior (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Raw Material Input] --> procA[Manufacturing Process]
    procA --> outputA[Finished Output]
    subgraph Causes[Sources of Variation]
        c1[Common Cause Noise] --> c2[Inherent to Process]
        c3[Special Cause Signal] --> c4[External Disruption]
    end
    subgraph Control[Statistical Control Tools]
        s1[Control Chart UCL and LCL] --> s2[Capability Indices Cp and Cpk]
        s3[Sampling Plan n and h] --> s2
    end
    subgraph Outcomes[Decision Matrix]
        o1{In Control and Capable} -->|Yes| o2[Continue Production]
        o1 -->|No Off Center| o3[Adjust Process Mean]
        o1 -->|No Too Much Variation| o4[Reduce Sigma via Six Sigma Tools]
    end
```

> **Reading Guide:** This figure links *cause* → *measurement* → *decision*. The heart of process control planning is to map every cause category to a specific measurement tool and a specific decision rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Style: 2 Questions of 3 marks each, no choice**

**Q1.** Define *process control*. List any four key activities involved in planning for process control. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

*Process control* is a statistical technique used to monitor and stabilize a process by distinguishing common cause variation from special cause variation, primarily through the use of control charts.

The four key activities in planning are:

1. **Identification of the critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristic.**
2. **Determination of data type** (variable or attribute).
3. **Selection of an appropriate control chart** (e.g., X-bar/R, p, c).
4. **Determination of subgroup size $n$ and sampling frequency $h$.**

---

**Q2.** Distinguish between *common cause variation* and *special cause variation* in a manufacturing process. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Inherent to the process | External / assignable |
| **Predictability** | Stable, predictable distribution | Unpredictable, irregular |
| **Action required** | Re-engineer the system | Remove the specific cause |
| **Frequency of occurrence** | Continuous, background | Sporadic, intermittent |
| **Control chart response** | Points within limits | Points outside limits or non-random patterns |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Module Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice given. Each question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Theory + Capability Calculation

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the seven stages of planning for process control. [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

The seven stages are:

1. **Identification of CTQ characteristics** — Pareto and FMEA are used to isolate dimensions or attributes that most affect customer satisfaction.

2. **Data type classification** — Determine whether data is *variable* (continuous, measured on a scale) or *attribute* (discrete, go/no-go).

3. **Control chart selection** — Use the standard decision tree: $\rightarrow$ variable with $n=1$: I-MR; $n=2$–$10$: X-bar/R; $n>10$: X-bar/S; attribute: p, np, c, or u chart.

4. **Subgroup size determination** — Balance statistical power (large $n$ for shift detection) against cost and time (small $n$).

5. **Sampling frequency determination** — Fast, high-volume production requires more frequent sampling; long, stable processes require less.

6. **Rational subgrouping** — Select units so that within-subgroup variation contains only common cause noise; between-subgroup variation captures special causes.

7. **Phase I and Phase II deployment** — Phase I uses $20$–$25$ preliminary samples to establish trial limits; Phase II uses frozen limits to monitor the live process.

---

**Q. (b)** For a manufacturing process, the grand average $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.0$ mm, average range $\bar{R} = 1.2$ mm, with $k = 25$ subgroups of size $n = 4$. The specification limits are $48.0$ mm and $52.0$ mm. Calculate the control limits for the X-bar and R charts, and hence compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on process capability. [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.0$ mm, $\bar{R} = 1.2$ mm, $n = 4$.

**Step 1 — Tabulated constants for $n=4$:** $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$, $d_2 = 2.059$.

**Step 2 — X-bar chart limits:**

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.0 + 0.729 \times 1.2 = 50.0 + 0.8748 = 50.8748 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.0 - 0.8748 = 49.1252 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — R chart limits:**

$$
\text{UCL}_R = 2.282 \times 1.2 = 2.7384 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_R = 0 \times 1.2 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Estimate $\sigma$:**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{1.2}{2.059} = 0.5828 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{52.0 - 48.0}{6 \times 0.5828} = \frac{4.0}{3.4968} = 1.144
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (process is centered at $\mu = 50.0$):**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{52.0 - 50.0}{3 \times 0.5828} = \frac{2.0}{1.7484} = 1.144
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{50.0 - 48.0}{1.7484} = 1.144
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.144, 1.144) = 1.144
$$

**Comment:** $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.144 > 1.00$, so the process is **just capable** but has no margin of safety. Recommended action: reduce $\sigma$ by $\approx 15\%$ to achieve $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Rational Subgrouping + Sample Size Design

**Q. (a)** Define *rational subgrouping*. State and explain Wheeler's principle of rational subgrouping with two engineering examples. [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A rational subgroup is a set of measurements selected such that each subgroup represents a "snapshot" of the process at one instant, with within-subgroup variation containing only common cause variation.

**Wheeler's Principle:** *"Subgroups should be selected so that if a special cause is present, its chance of appearing between subgroups is maximized, while its chance of appearing within subgroups is minimized."*

**Engineering Example 1 — Bottle Filling Machine:** Take 4 consecutive bottles every 30 minutes. The subgroup captures a moment in time, so any filler-valve problem will show up as variation *between* subgroups, not within.

**Engineering Example 2 — Surface Roughness of Castings:** Select 3 castings from the same mold at a time, not from different molds. This way, mold-to-mold variation (special cause) is captured between subgroups, while within-mold noise (common cause) is captured within subgroups.

---

**Q. (b)** A process produces items with an average fraction non-conforming $\bar{p} = 0.02$. A p-chart is to be designed with sample size $n = 200$. Compute the trial control limits. Also, determine the sample size required to detect a shift to $p_2 = 0.05$ with $\alpha = 0.0027$ (i.e., $Z_{\alpha/2} = 3$) and power $1 - \beta = 0.95$ (i.e., $Z_\beta = 1.645$). [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\bar{p} = 0.02$, $n = 200$, $p_2 = 0.05$, $Z_{\alpha/2} = 3.0$, $Z_\beta = 1.645$.

**Part 1 — Trial control limits for the p-chart:**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.02 \times 0.98}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0196}{200}} = \sqrt{0.000098} = 0.00990
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_p = 0.02 + 3 \times 0.00990 = 0.02 + 0.0297 = 0.0497
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_p = 0.02 - 0.0297 = -0.0097 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{LCL}_p = 0 \text{ (negative set to 0)}
$$

**Part 2 — Sample size to detect shift from $0.02$ to $0.05$:**

$$
n = \frac{(3.0 + 1.645)^2 \times 0.02 \times 0.98}{(0.05 - 0.02)^2} = \frac{(4.645)^2 \times 0.0196}{(0.03)^2}
$$

$$
n = \frac{21.576 \times 0.0196}{0.0009} = \frac{0.4229}{0.0009} = 469.9 \approx 470
$$

**Interpretation:** With $n = 200$, the p-chart **cannot reliably detect** a shift from 2% to 5% defectives (the upper limit 4.97% is too close to 5%). To achieve 95% power of detection, the sample size must be increased to **470**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid These Errors!**
>
> 1. **Confusing Control Limits with Specification Limits** — Control limits are calculated from the *process data* ($\pm 3\sigma$); specification limits are dictated by the *customer/design*. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if you interchange them.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Set Negative LCL to Zero** — For p-charts and c-charts, LCL can be negative. Always set it to 0; the fraction non-conforming *cannot* be negative. A common 1-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Misinterpretation of $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$** — Reporting $C_p = 1.5$ as "process is excellent" without computing $C_{pk}$ is incomplete. A 7-mark answer demands *both* indices.
>
> 4. **Wrong Tabulated Constant** — Using $A_2$ value for $n = 5$ in a problem where $n = 4$ is a classic error. Always *state the constant and its source* ($n = 4 \Rightarrow A_2 = 0.729$).
>
> 5. **Reporting $C_{pk}$ without Units / Interpretation** — A bare number is worth 1 mark less than a number with a clear engineering interpretation ("capable", "marginal", "not capable").
>
> 6. **Forgetting Rationale in Rational Subgrouping** — The 7-mark question on rational subgrouping demands the *principle* (Wheeler's rule) plus examples. Just defining it earns only 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final Reading Before Exam)**

- [x] **Process Control** = using statistical tools (charts) to separate **common cause** noise from **special cause** signals.
- [x] **Planning** is the *pre-implementation* design of the chart, sample size, and frequency — *not* the chart itself.
- [x] **Two faces of variation:** common cause (inherent, manage the system) vs special cause (assignable, manage the point).
- [x] **Data type determines chart type:** Variable → X-bar/R, X-bar/S, I-MR, EWMA, CUSUM; Attribute → p, np, c, u.
- [x] **Tabulated constants** for $n$: memorize $A_2$ for $n = 2$ to $6$ at minimum (0.577, 0.483, 0.419, 0.337, 0.308 for $n=3,4,5,6,7$ approx).
- [x] **Control limits ≠ Specification limits** — control limits describe the *process*; specification limits describe the *customer's need*.
- [x] **Phase I (retrospective)** establishes limits from $20$–$25$ pilot subgroups; **Phase II (prospective)** uses those limits to monitor live production.
- [x] **Rational subgrouping rule (Wheeler):** *Maximize between-subgroup variation, minimize within-subgroup variation* — to expose special causes between, not hide them within.
- [x] **Process Capability:** $C_p$ measures *potential* (spread only); $C_{pk}$ measures *actual* (spread + centering). Always report both.
- [x] **Capability thresholds:** $C_{pk} < 1.00$ (incapable), $1.00$–$1.33$ (marginal), $1.33$–$1.67$ (capable), $> 1.67$ (excellent).
- [x] **Sample size formula** for shift detection: $n = (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_\beta)^2 \, p_1(1-p_1) / (p_2 - p_1)^2$ for attribute data.
- [x] **Negative LCL** for p and c charts → set to **0** (physical constraint).
- [x] **Three-sigma limits** are 99.73% probability bounds when the process is in control.
- [x] **Engineer's first step** in any process control question: *identify data type, then choose the right chart, then compute limits, then interpret.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Process Control (SPC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statistical Process Control (SPC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is a data-driven, statistical methodology used to monitor, measure, and control a manufacturing or business process through the application of **control charts**, **process capability indices**, and **variance analysis**. It was pioneered by **Walter A. Shewhart** of Bell Telephone Laboratories in **1924**, and is formally defined by the American Society for Quality (ASQ) as the use of statistical techniques to control a process.

The fundamental objective of SPC is to distinguish between two categories of process variation:

- **Common Cause Variation (Chance Cause):** Inherent, natural, random fluctuations within the **Voice of the Process (VOP)**. These are present in every process and can only be eliminated by fundamental changes to the system itself.
- **Special Cause Variation (Assignable Cause):** External, non-random, identifiable disruptions that cause the process to behave abnormally. These must be detected, diagnosed, and removed to restore process stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** A process is said to be in a state of **Statistical Control** when only common cause variation is present. Predictability and capability analysis can ONLY be performed on statistically stable processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thermostat of Manufacturing"

Imagine a home **thermostat** set to maintain 24°C. The heating system constantly switches on and off, causing small, expected fluctuations around 24°C (this is **common cause variation** — it is the natural, designed behaviour of the system). The thermostat is the *control chart* — it continuously monitors the room temperature (the *quality characteristic*).

Now, suppose a window is left open on a cold day. The temperature suddenly drops to 18°C. This is an **assignable (special) cause** — a specific, external event that the normal system cannot absorb. A good control chart detects this *out-of-control signal* and triggers a response, much like a thermostat alarm or a homeowner's reaction.

> [!TIP]
> **SPC = Continuous process "health monitoring."** Control charts are the stethoscopes, the variable readings are the vital signs, and the control limits are the thresholds of clinical concern.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Characteristic** | The measurable property of a product/process being monitored (e.g., diameter, weight, tensile strength). |
| **Subgroup / Sample** | A small, rationally selected set of measurements (typically n = 2 to 10) taken under homogeneous conditions. |
| **Rational Subgrouping** | Shewhart's principle: subgroups are selected so that variation **within** a subgroup reflects only common causes, while variation **between** subgroups reveals special causes. |
| **Center Line (CL)** | The grand average of the statistic being plotted. |
| **Control Limits (UCL, LCL)** | Statistical boundaries, set at ±3σ from the center line, within which the process is expected to operate if only common cause variation exists. |
| **Process Capability (Cp, Cpk)** | The ability of a stable process to produce output within **specification limits** (set by the customer/engineer). |
| **Voice of the Process (VOP)** | What the process is actually capable of doing, expressed by its statistical distribution. |
| **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** | What the customer requires, expressed by the specification limits. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Philosophy of SPC

SPC operates on a four-stage **Deming–Shewhart Cycle (PDCA)** logic:

1. **Plan:** Define the critical quality characteristic (CTQ — Critical-to-Quality) and the sampling plan.
2. **Do:** Collect subgroup data from the process at regular intervals.
3. **Check:** Plot the data on a control chart. Apply the **Western Electric / Nelson Rules** to detect non-random patterns.
4. **Act:** Investigate out-of-control signals, remove special causes, and recalculate control limits.

### 2.2 Control Chart Anatomy

Every Shewhart control chart contains three parallel lines drawn across the time axis:

- **Center Line (CL):** The grand mean of the sample statistic.
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** $\mu + 3\sigma$ (statistical upper bound).
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** $\mu - 3\sigma$ (statistical lower bound).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Control limits (±3σ) are **NOT** specification limits (USL/LSL). Control limits describe what the *process* is doing; specification limits describe what the *customer wants*. Conflating the two is a classic KTU board-exam blunder.

### 2.3 Selection of the Appropriate Control Chart

| Data Type | Subgroup Size (n) | Chart for Central Tendency | Chart for Dispersion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Variables (Continuous)** | n = 2 to 10 | $\bar{X}$ Chart | R Chart (range) / S Chart (std. dev.) |
| **Attributes (Discrete)** | Variable | p Chart (proportion defective) | n/a |
| **Attributes** | Fixed n | np Chart (number defective) | n/a |
| **Attributes (Defects)** | Variable | u Chart (defects per unit) | n/a |
| **Attributes (Defects)** | Fixed inspection area | c Chart (number of defects) | n/a |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Statistic | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Grand Mean of Means | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | Average of k subgroup means. Center line of $\bar{X}$ chart. |
| 2 | Mean of Ranges | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k}$ | Center line of R chart. |
| 3 | $\bar{X}$ Chart UCL | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for means. |
| 4 | $\bar{X}$ Chart LCL | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for means. |
| 5 | R Chart UCL | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for range. |
| 6 | R Chart LCL | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for range. |
| 7 | p Chart CL | $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Average proportion defective. |
| 8 | p Chart UCL | $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{\bar{n}}}$ | Upper limit, attribute proportion. |
| 9 | p Chart LCL | $LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{\bar{n}}}$ | Lower limit, attribute proportion. |
| 10 | c Chart CL | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{k}$ | Average number of defects. |
| 11 | c Chart UCL | $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Upper limit for defect count. |
| 12 | c Chart LCL | $LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Lower limit for defect count. |
| 13 | u Chart CL | $\bar{u} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Average defects per unit. |
| 14 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (centred). |
| 15 | Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual capability (accounts for off-centring). |
| 16 | Process Performance Index | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Overall performance (unstable process). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The constants **$A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$** depend on the subgroup size n and are tabulated in the AIAG/SPC reference tables. For n = 5: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. The ±3σ choice gives a **0.27%** total probability of false alarm (Type I error), which Shewhart judged as the optimal economic balance between "searching for trouble that does not exist" and "missing real trouble."

### 2.5 Why ±3σ? The Statistical Rationale

If the process statistic is approximately normally distributed, then the probability of a single point falling outside ±3σ is:

$$P(\vert X - \mu \vert > 3\sigma) = 2 \times [1 - \Phi(3)] \approx 0.0027$$

This translates to roughly **1 false alarm every 370 points** (on average, 1 in 370 plotted points will fall outside the limits purely by chance). Shewhart chose this as the practical engineering balance between detecting real shifts promptly and tolerating a manageable level of false alarms.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (IATF 16949):** SPC is mandatory for critical characteristics (CC/SC) — every KTU mechanical/industrial student must remember this.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Cpk monitoring of line-width and film thickness.
- **Pharmaceutical (FDA 21 CFR 211):** Batch-process control of tablet weight and dissolution.
- **Civil Engineering:** Monitoring concrete cube strength using $\bar{X}$–R charts.
- **Service Industries:** Call-center wait times, banking transaction errors (np charts).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the $\bar{X}$ Chart Control Limits

**Starting Premise:** Let the process output $X$ be normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. We draw random subgroups of size $n$.

**Step 1 — Distribution of the Subgroup Mean:**
By the Central Limit Theorem, the subgroup mean $\bar{X}$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

*This is the foundational relation of all variable control charts — the standard error of the mean decreases with the square root of the subgroup size.*

**Step 2 — Substitute into the ±3σ Formula:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \dfrac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu - \dfrac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Replace Population Parameters with Sample Statistics:**
In practice, $\mu$ is unknown and is estimated by $\bar{\bar{X}}$. The unknown $\sigma$ is estimated from the average subgroup range $\bar{R}$ using the relationship:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant (e.g., $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$) representing the expected value of the range of $n$ standard normal variates.

**Step 4 — Form the Final Working Equation:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \dfrac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \left(\dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}\right)\bar{R}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Tabulated Constant $A_2$:**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \qquad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}}$$

**Step 7 — R Chart Limits (Analogous Derivation):**
The range $R$ has its own distribution with mean $d_2 \sigma$ and standard deviation $d_3 \sigma$, where $d_3$ is a tabulated constant.

$$UCL_R = d_2 \sigma + 3 d_3 \sigma = (d_2 + 3 d_3)\sigma = D_4 \bar{R}$$

$$LCL_R = d_2 \sigma - 3 d_3 \sigma = (d_2 - 3 d_3)\sigma = D_3 \bar{R}$$

where $D_3 = 1 - \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}$ and $D_4 = 1 + \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU problems assume n is a small integer (typically 3, 4, or 5) and that the table of constants is provided in the question paper. If a constant is missing in a problem, derive it from the basic definitions of $d_2$ and $d_3$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Process Capability Indices

**Step 1 — Define the Specification Spread:**

$$S = USL - LSL$$

**Step 2 — Define the Process Spread (Six Sigma Width):**

$$P = 6\sigma$$

This represents ±3σ on either side of the process mean — the natural working tolerance of the process.

**Step 3 — Form the Potential Capability Ratio $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \dfrac{S}{P} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

*Interpretation:* $C_p = 1.00$ means the process spread *exactly* fits the specification width. $C_p \geq 1.33$ is generally considered capable; $C_p \geq 1.50$ is preferred for critical characteristics.

**Step 4 — Account for Off-Centring (The $C_{pk}$ Adjustment):**
$C_p$ assumes the process is *perfectly centered* on the specification midpoint. In reality, the mean $\mu$ often drifts. Define the off-centring index:

$$k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}, \quad \text{where } M = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$$

**Step 5 — One-Sided Capability Indices:**

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \quad \text{(upper side capability)}$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \quad \text{(lower side capability)}$$

**Step 6 — Final $C_{pk}$ Definition:**

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)}$$

**Step 7 — Relationship Between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$$

When $k = 0$ (perfectly centered), $C_{pk} = C_p$. As the process drifts off-centre, $C_{pk} < C_p$.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — $\bar{X}$–R Chart

**Problem (KTU-style):** A piston-ring manufacturer takes 5 samples of size n = 5 each. The subgroup means (in mm) are: 34.0, 34.5, 33.8, 34.2, 34.0. The subgroup ranges are: 4, 5, 3, 4, 4. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and R charts using $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$ (for n = 5).

**Step 1 — Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$:**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{34.0 + 34.5 + 33.8 + 34.2 + 34.0}{5} = \dfrac{170.5}{5} = 34.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{R}$:**

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{4 + 5 + 3 + 4 + 4}{5} = \dfrac{20}{5} = 4.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — $\bar{X}$ Chart Limits:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 34.1 + (0.577)(4.0) = 34.1 + 2.308 = 36.408 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 34.1 - 2.308 = 31.792 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = 34.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — R Chart Limits:**

$$UCL_R = (2.114)(4.0) = 8.456 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = (0)(4.0) = 0 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_R = 4.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:** All 5 subgroup means (33.8 to 34.5) lie within (31.792, 36.408) and all 5 ranges (3 to 5) lie within (0, 8.456). The process is in **statistical control**. ✓

### 3.4 Worked Example — Process Capability

Using the same piston-ring data, suppose the specification is LSL = 30 mm, USL = 38 mm, and the overall process standard deviation is $\sigma = 1.7$ mm (estimated from $\bar{R}/d_2 = 4.0/2.326$).

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \dfrac{38 - 30}{6 \times 1.7} = \dfrac{8}{10.2} \approx 0.784$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (process mean $\mu = 34.1$):**

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{38 - 34.1}{3 \times 1.7} = \dfrac{3.9}{5.1} \approx 0.765$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{34.1 - 30}{5.1} = \dfrac{4.1}{5.1} \approx 0.804$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.765, 0.804) = 0.765$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** Both $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are below 1.0, indicating the process is **NOT capable** of meeting specifications as-is. The manufacturing engineer must either reduce $\sigma$ (improve the process) or shift $\mu$ closer to the midpoint (34.0) and reduce variation to achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SPC Operational Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define CTQ Critical to Quality Characteristic] --> B[Establish Sampling Plan Rational Subgroups]
    B --> C[Collect Subgroup Data from Process]
    C --> D[Compute Subgroup Statistics Xbar and R]
    D --> E[Calculate Grand Mean Xdoublebar and Mean Range Rbar]
    E --> F[Determine Control Limits UCL LCL using 3 sigma]
    F --> G[Plot Points on Control Chart with Center Line]
    G --> H{Is Point Within Control Limits?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Process In Statistical Control]
    H -- No --> J[Out of Control Signal Detected]
    J --> K[Apply Western Electric and Nelson Rules]
    K --> L[Investigate Assignable Special Cause]
    L --> M[Remove Special Cause and Document]
    M --> N[Recalculate Control Limits if Needed]
    N --> C
    I --> O[Estimate Process Capability Cp and Cpk]
    O --> P{Cpk greater than or equal to 1.33}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Process Capable and Stable Continue Production]
    P -- No --> R[Implement Process Improvement DMAIC]
    R --> S[Re-evaluate Capability]
    S --> O
```

### 4.2 Control Chart Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[UCL Upper Control Limit] --- B[CL Center Line at Xdoublebar]
    B --- C[LCL Lower Control Limit]
    A --> D[Zone A between 2 sigma and 3 sigma]
    D --> E[Zone B between 1 sigma and 2 sigma]
    E --> F[Zone C within 1 sigma]
    E --> G[Zone B between 1 sigma and 2 sigma]
    G --> H[Zone A between 2 sigma and 3 sigma]
    H --> C
    I[Sample 1] -.-> B
    J[Sample 2] -.-> A
    K[Sample 3] -.-> F
    L[Sample 4] -.-> E
    M[Sample 5] -.-> G
    N[Sample k] -.-> B
```

### 4.3 Variation Type Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Output Variation Observed] --> B{Is Variation Random and Predictable}
    B -- Yes --> C[Common Cause Variation]
    C --> D[Inherent to System Design]
    D --> E[Requires Management Action to Reduce]
    B -- No --> F[Special Cause Variation]
    F --> G[Due to External Disturbance]
    G --> H[Requires Operator Action to Remove]
    H --> I[Example Tool Breakage Power Surge]
    E --> J[Example Machine Wear Material Variability]
```

### 4.4 Control Chart Selection Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Data Type] --> B{Continuous or Attribute}
    B -- Continuous --> C[Subgroup Size n fixed and small]
    C --> D[Use Xbar Chart for Mean]
    C --> E[Use R Chart for Dispersion]
    B -- Attribute --> F{What is Counted}
    F -- Proportion Defective variable n --> G[Use p Chart]
    F -- Number Defective fixed n --> H[Use np Chart]
    F -- Defects per Unit variable n --> I[Use u Chart]
    F -- Number of Defects fixed area --> J[Use c Chart]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Statistical Process Control (SPC). State any two objectives of SPC.**

**Model Answer:**
Statistical Process Control is the application of statistical techniques — particularly control charts — to monitor, measure, and control a process so that it operates at its full capability to produce conforming product.

Objectives of SPC:
1. To detect and eliminate assignable (special) causes of variation in a process, thereby achieving and maintaining statistical control.
2. To monitor process performance and verify that the process remains stable and capable of producing output within specification limits (i.e., to reduce variability and improve quality).
3. To provide a common basis for quality communication between supplier and customer.

*(Any two well-stated objectives earn full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between control limits and specification limits.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Control Limits (UCL, LCL) | Specification Limits (USL, LSL) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Statistical boundaries of the process at ±3σ. | Engineering/design boundaries set by the customer. |
| **Basis** | Calculated from process data. | Determined by customer requirements. |
| **Purpose** | Detect *change* in the process. | Define what is *acceptable* output. |
| **Variability** | Vary as the process improves. | Usually fixed by design. |
| **Voice** | Voice of the Process (VOP). | Voice of the Customer (VOC). |

*(A 3-row tabular comparison with definitions earns full 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of variation in processes. Distinguish between common cause and special cause variation with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data represent the inside diameter (in mm) of 20 samples of size 5 each, taken from a manufacturing process. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and R control charts and comment on the state of control. For n = 5, use $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. **(7 marks)**

| Sample | $\bar{X}$ | R |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 34.5 | 3.0 |
| 2 | 34.2 | 2.5 |
| 3 | 33.9 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 34.4 | 3.5 |
| 5 | 33.8 | 2.0 |
| 6 | 34.3 | 2.5 |
| 7 | 34.0 | 2.0 |
| 8 | 34.5 | 2.5 |
| 9 | 33.9 | 2.0 |
| 10 | 34.2 | 3.0 |
| 11 | 34.4 | 2.0 |
| 12 | 33.6 | 2.5 |
| 13 | 34.1 | 2.0 |
| 14 | 34.0 | 2.5 |
| 15 | 34.3 | 3.0 |
| 16 | 34.5 | 2.0 |
| 17 | 34.2 | 2.5 |
| 18 | 34.0 | 2.0 |
| 19 | 33.7 | 2.5 |
| 20 | 34.4 | 3.0 |

##### Model Solution

**(a) Concept of Variation (7 marks):**

Every process, no matter how well-designed, exhibits variation. **Dr. W. Edwards Deming** stated: *"If I had to reduce my message for management to just a few words, I'd say it all has to do with reducing variation."*

**Common Cause Variation (Chance Causes):**
- Inherent, built-in randomness within the system.
- Affects every individual output of the process.
- Forms a stable, predictable distribution.
- Removing it requires **fundamental changes to the system** (new equipment, retraining, redesign) — management's responsibility.
- *Example:* Slight variations in piston-ring diameter caused by inherent machine vibration and material batch differences.

**Special Cause Variation (Assignable Causes):**
- External, intermittent, and identifiable disturbances.
- Affect specific subgroups or specific time periods.
- Indicated by points outside control limits or non-random patterns.
- Removing it is the **operator's responsibility** (repair, recalibrate, replace tool).
- *Examples:* A broken cutting tool, sudden drop in power supply voltage, contamination of raw material.

*Strategy:* First, eliminate all special causes until the process is stable; only then address common causes.

**(b) Construction of $\bar{X}$ and R Charts (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$:**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{20} = \dfrac{34.5 + 34.2 + 33.9 + 34.4 + 33.8 + 34.3 + 34.0 + 34.5 + 33.9 + 34.2 + 34.4 + 33.6 + 34.1 + 34.0 + 34.3 + 34.5 + 34.2 + 34.0 + 33.7 + 34.4}{20}$$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{681.7}{20} = 34.085 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{R}$:**

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{20} = \dfrac{3.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 3.5 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 3.0}{20}$$

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{49.0}{20} = 2.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — $\bar{X}$ Chart Limits:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 34.085 + (0.577)(2.45) = 34.085 + 1.414 = 35.499 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 34.085 - 1.414 = 32.671 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = 34.085 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — R Chart Limits:**

$$UCL_R = (2.114)(2.45) = 5.179 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = (0)(2.45) = 0 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_R = 2.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on State of Control:**
- $\bar{X}$ values range from **33.6 to 34.5 mm**, all comfortably within (32.671, 35.499) ✓
- R values range from **2.0 to 3.5 mm**, all within (0, 5.179) ✓
- **Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control** — no special causes are present. Capability analysis can now be performed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define process capability. Explain $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ indices with their industrial interpretation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces shafts with a target diameter of 25.00 mm. The specification limits are LSL = 24.90 mm and USL = 25.10 mm. The process mean is $\mu = 24.97$ mm and the process standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.04$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Process Capability (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Process capability is the inherent ability of a *stable* (statistically controlled) process to produce output that conforms to the engineering specifications. It is quantified by the **capability indices**, which compare the natural spread of the process (6σ) to the specification width (USL − LSL).

**$C_p$ — Potential Capability Index:**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

- Assumes the process is *perfectly centered* on the specification midpoint.
- Industrial interpretation:
    - $C_p < 1.00$ → Process is **not capable** (spread too wide).
    - $C_p = 1.00$ → Process just meets specifications marginally.
    - $1.00 < C_p < 1.33$ → Marginal capability.
    - $C_p \geq 1.33$ → **Capable**.
    - $C_p \geq 1.50$ → **Highly capable** (typical Six-Sigma target: $C_p = 2.00$).

**$C_{pk}$ — Actual (Critical) Capability Index:**

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

- Accounts for *off-centring* of the process mean.
- $C_{pk} = C_p$ only when the process is perfectly centered.
- When $C_{pk} > 1.33$, the process is **capable and reliable** in production.
- Note: $C_{pk}$ can never exceed $C_p$.

**Key Insight:** $C_p$ tells you *what the process could do*; $C_{pk}$ tells you *what it is actually doing*. Both must be ≥ 1.33 for a production-ready process.

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm, $\mu = 24.97$ mm, $\sigma = 0.04$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.24} = 0.833$$

**Step 2 — Compute One-Sided Indices:**

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 24.97}{3 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.12} = 1.083$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{24.97 - 24.90}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.12} = 0.583$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.083, 0.583) = 0.583$$

**Step 4 — Off-centring Index k:**

$$M = \dfrac{25.10 + 24.90}{2} = 25.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$k = \dfrac{\vert 24.97 - 25.00 \vert}{0.20/2} = \dfrac{0.03}{0.10} = 0.30$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 0.833 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.833 \times 0.70 = 0.583 \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Comments:**
- $C_p = 0.833 < 1.00$ → The process spread is **wider than the specification width** — variation must be reduced.
- $C_{pk} = 0.583 < C_p$ → The process is **off-center** (shifted toward the LSL).
- The process is producing shafts that **violate the LSL** — diameter is too small on average.
- **Recommendations:**
    1. **Re-center** the process: shift $\mu$ from 24.97 mm to 25.00 mm (adjust machine offset).
    2. **Reduce variation:** Lower $\sigma$ to approximately $0.025$ mm to achieve $C_p = 1.33$ (since $6\sigma \le 0.20$).
    3. After both steps, re-verify $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ before approving for production.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
> 1. **Misapplying constants:** Using $A_2$ for the R chart (or vice-versa) is an instant 1-mark deduction. Memorize: $A_2 \to \bar{X}$ chart, $D_3/D_4 \to$ R chart.
> 2. **Confusing control limits with specification limits:** A common error. The question on "control vs specification limits" is a KTU favourite — draw a clear VOP vs VOC distinction.
> 3. **Wrong choice of chart:** Using a $\bar{X}$ chart for attribute (defect) data loses 1 mark. Always classify the data type first.
> 4. **Forgetting rational subgrouping:** When asked to "design a sampling plan," many students skip the rationale for subgroup size. KTU values the *reasoning*, not just the number.
> 5. **Cpk vs Cp confusion:** Writing $C_{pk} = C_p$ always (even when the process is off-center) is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Missing units:** $\bar{X}$, R, USL/LSL must always carry the correct unit (mm, kg, MPa, etc.) — units are checked stringently in KTU board valuation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SPC = Statistical Process Control**, founded by **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)**.
- ✅ **Two variation types:** *Common cause* (system-inherent, fix the system) vs *Special cause* (assignable, fix the operator/process).
- ✅ **3-sigma limits** give a **0.27%** false-alarm rate — Shewhart's economic balance.
- ✅ **Rational Subgrouping** is the foundational sampling principle of SPC.
- ✅ Control chart constants for n = 5: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- ✅ **Variable charts:** $\bar{X}$ (location) + R (dispersion) for n ≤ 10; $\bar{X}$ + S for n > 10.
- ✅ **Attribute charts:** p (variable n, proportion), np (fixed n, count), c (defects in fixed area), u (defects per unit, variable n).
- ✅ **$C_p$ measures potential capability; $C_{pk}$ measures actual capability** — $C_{pk} \le C_p$ always.
- ✅ $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = C_p(1-k)$, where $k$ is the off-centring index.
- ✅ **Acceptance benchmark:** $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ for general production, $\ge 1.50$ for critical characteristics.
- ✅ **Process Performance Index $P_{pk}$** uses sample standard deviation $s$ — used *only* for unstable processes; capability indices are for stable processes.
- ✅ **Western Electric Rules** (1956) and **Nelson Rules** (1984) are the run-tests for non-random patterns — beyond the basic ±3σ test.
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** is the engine of continuous SPC improvement.
- ✅ **VOP vs VOC:** VOP = voice of the process (control limits); VOC = voice of the customer (spec limits).
- ✅ **First stabilize, then optimize:** Capability analysis is meaningful *only* on a process in statistical control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a systematic, customer-driven, team-based **structured planning and product development methodology** that uses a series of interlinked matrices (the most famous being the **House of Quality – HOQ**) to translate the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, measurable **engineering and technical requirements** at every stage of the product life-cycle — from concept design to manufacturing to service.

QFD is classified under the umbrella of **Quality Management Systems (QMS)** and is recognized by international standards (notably **ISO 9001**, **Six Sigma / DFSS** — *Design for Six Sigma*, and **QCC/Kaizen frameworks** in Japanese industry).

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)
- **VOC** — Voice of the Customer (the "WHATS")
- **CTQs** — Critical-to-Quality characteristics
- **Kano Model** — categorizes customer needs into Must-be, One-dimensional, and Attractive
- **HOQ** — House of Quality (the central matrix of QFD)
- **DFSS** — Design for Six Sigma (the framework in which QFD often operates)

## 1.2 Historical Origin

> [!NOTE]
> QFD was originally developed in **Japan in 1966–1972** by quality pioneers **Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno** at the *Mitsubishi Heavy Industries Kobe Shipyard*. It was popularized in the West by **John Hauser** and **Don Clausing** in their landmark **1988 Harvard Business Review article "The House of Quality"**, which described its use at **Toyota, Ford, Mazda, Boeing, NASA, and the US Navy**.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Translator at the Border"

Imagine a **bilingual translator stationed at the border between two countries**:

- **Country A (the Customer)** speaks only the language of *needs and feelings*: *"I want a phone that doesn't feel slippery, has a long battery, and looks classy."*
- **Country B (the Engineer)** speaks only the language of *specifications and numbers*: *"surface friction coefficient ≥ 0.4, battery capacity = 5000 mAh, anodized aluminum chassis with Ra = 0.8 µm."*

Without a translator, both sides get frustrated, the product is designed blind, and quality suffers. **QFD is the disciplined, documented translator.** The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the **translation dictionary** that ensures *nothing is lost, misinterpreted, or over-prioritized* during the conversation.

### Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners
QFD answers four questions, in order, for every product:
1. **WHAT** does the customer want? (the "Whats")
2. **HOW** will we technically deliver it? (the "Hows")
3. **HOW STRONGLY** is each How linked to each What? (the relationship matrix)
4. **HOW MUCH** should we focus on each How? (the computed technical weights)

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in QFD (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Relationship strength symbols: 9 (strong ●), 3 (moderate ○), 1 (weak △), 0 (none —)**
- **Correlation (Roof) symbols: ++ (strong positive), + (positive), -- (strong negative), - (negative), blank (independent)**
- **Customer Importance Weight: 1 to 5** (5 = most critical)
- **Sales Point: 1.0, 1.2, 1.5** (multiplier reflecting market competitive pull)
- **Technical Difficulty: 1 to 5** (5 = most difficult to achieve)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **6 canonical components of the House of Quality**. Memorize them: *Customer Requirements, Technical Requirements, Relationship Matrix, Roof (Correlation) Matrix, Customer Competitive Assessment, Technical Competitive Assessment.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architectural "Why" Behind QFD

QFD is built on three foundational engineering management theories:

1. **Customer-Driven Engineering** — The product specification begins *outside* the company (with the market), not inside the engineering department.
2. **Cross-Functional Teamwork** — Marketing, Design, Manufacturing, Quality, and Finance all populate the matrix together.
3. **Concurrent / Simultaneous Engineering** — All product, part, and process decisions are made in **parallel**, slashing development lead time (typical reduction: **30 %–50 %**).

## 2.2 The Four Phases of QFD (Akao Deployment Chart)

Each phase has its own House of Quality, and the *Hows* of one phase become the *Whats* of the next.

| Phase | Name | Input ("Whats") | Output ("Hows") | Output Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Product Planning** | Customer Requirements | Engineering characteristics of the product | R&D / Design |
| 2 | **Parts Deployment** | Critical product features | Component / part specifications | Component engineering |
| 3 | **Process Planning** | Critical part characteristics | Manufacturing process parameters | Manufacturing engineering |
| 4 | **Production Planning** | Process parameters | Production controls, tooling, work instructions | Production / Quality |

> [!NOTE]
> A complete QFD study uses **4 interlinked Houses of Quality**, though Module 1 typically focuses only on **Phase 1 — the Product-Planning HOQ**.

## 2.3 The Six Components of the House of Quality (Phase 1 HOQ)

| # | Component | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Requirements (WHATS)** | Left wall | List of VOC items with importance weights |
| 2 | **Technical Requirements (HOWS)** | Ceiling / top matrix | Engineering / measurable parameters |
| 3 | **Relationship Matrix** | Central body | Strength of link between each What and each How (scored **9, 3, 1, 0**) |
| 4 | **Roof — Technical Correlation Matrix** | Triangular ceiling | Hows-vs-Hows interaction (synergy / trade-off) |
| 5 | **Customer Competitive Assessment** | Right wall | Benchmark CRs against competitor products |
| 6 | **Technical Competitive Assessment** | Bottom row | Benchmark TRs against competitor products |

Auxiliary rows (KTU frequently asked): **Importance Weights, Sales Points, Technical Difficulty, Target Values, Absolute & Relative Technical Importance**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Engineering Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer Importance (raw) | $C_j$ (j = 1 to m) | Direct customer rating | Score **1–5** |
| 2 | Sales Point Multiplier | $S_j \in \{1.0, 1.2, 1.5\}$ | Market pull weight | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Weighted Customer Importance | $W_j = C_j \times S_j$ | Final adjusted weight of CR j | Score |
| 4 | Relationship Strength | $R_{ij} \in \{0, 1, 3, 9\}$ | Strength of link between CR j and TR i | Score |
| 5 | **Absolute Technical Importance** | $T_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} W_j \cdot R_{ij}$ | Total engineering focus for TR i | Score |
| 6 | **Relative Technical Importance** | $RT_i = \dfrac{T_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i} \times 100\%$ | % priority of TR i | **%** |
| 7 | Normalized Technical Importance | $NT_i = \dfrac{T_i}{\max(T_i)} \times 5$ | Scaled to 1–5 for plotting | Score |
| 8 | Target Value | $TV_i$ | Engineering specification of TR i | Engineering units (mm, W, etc.) |
| 9 | Technical Difficulty | $D_i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Effort to achieve $TV_i$ | Score |
| 10 | Roof Correlation | $\text{++}, \text{+}, \text{--}, \text{-}$ | Synergy / trade-off between TRs | Categorical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚠️ **KTU pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse *Absolute Technical Importance* $T_i$ with *Customer Importance Weight* $W_j$. They live in different rows of the matrix and use different symbols (i = row index for TRs, j = column index for CRs).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of QFD

- **Automotive industry (Toyota, Ford, Tata Motors):** Reduces concept-to-launch time by mapping 100+ customer voices into ~30 engineering targets.
- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Aligns 5 million+ part specifications to passenger comfort, fuel economy, and FAA regulations.
- **Software (Microsoft, SAP):** Replaced the term "Voice of Customer" with "User Stories" in QFD-derived agile frameworks.
- **Healthcare (Mayo Clinic):** Maps patient-safety requirements to medical-device engineering parameters.
- **Six Sigma / DFSS:** QFD is the front-end *Define* phase of the **DMADV** cycle (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is dedicated to the **complete mathematical machinery** of QFD. We will derive the **Absolute and Relative Technical Importance** formulas and then execute a **fully-worked industrial example** (an electric-kettle design study) so that the student can solve any KTU 14-mark numerical question.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Absolute Technical Importance Formula

### Step 1 — Setup
Let there be:
- $m$ customer requirements indexed by $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$
