**1. Tabular Comparison:**

| Dimension | **SQC** | **TQC** | **TQM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Era | 1920s–1940s | 1950s–1970s | 1980s–present |
| Founder/Origin | Walter A. Shewhart | Armand V. Feigenbaum | Adopted by US industry from Japanese CWQC |
| Focus | Statistical reduction of process variation | Cross-functional quality ownership | Strategic quality and stakeholder value |
| Scope | Process / shop-floor | Entire organisation | Organisation + supply chain + society + environment |
| Customer | Implied (via specs) | Explicit satisfaction | **Customer delight, loyalty, co-creation** |
| Tools | Control charts, OC curves, sampling plans | SQC tools + QFD, FMEA, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke | All TQC tools + BSC, Six Sigma, benchmarking, CSR |
| Leadership Role | Minimal | Supportive | **Central and driving** |
| Continuous Improvement | Optional | Encouraged | **Mandatory, strategic** |
| Examples | $\bar{x}-R$ chart, $p$-chart, MIL-STD-105E | Feigenbaum's 3 pillars, Juran's trilogy, Crosby's 14 steps | EFQM Excellence Model, Baldrige Criteria, ISO 9001:2015 |

**2. Why TQC is the *Bridge* between SQC and TQM:**

(a) **Inheritance from SQC:** TQC adopts SQC's statistical tools (control charts, sampling plans, capability indices) as its *measurement backbone*. Without Shewhart's statistical foundation, TQC would have no objective basis.

(b) **Foundation for TQM:** TQC introduces the *organisation-wide* thinking (cross-functional teams, quality circles, customer focus) that TQM later elevates to the *strategic* level. TQC's three pillars (Development, Maintenance, Improvement) reappear inside TQM but are now tied to **vision, mission, and stakeholder strategy**.

(c) **Philosophical Continuity:** TQC preserved SQC's *scientific rigour* (data-driven decisions, prevention over detection) while expanding SQC's *narrow technical focus* to embrace **people, culture, and management systems** — which TQM later amplifies further.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Use the phrase **"SQC gave TQC its tools, TQC gave TQM its philosophy"** — it scores the full 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on TQC Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating TQC with TQM.** Many students use the terms interchangeably. TQC is the *quality-centric* mid-20th century framework; TQM is the *strategic, customer-delight* framework of the late 1980s. Examiners *will* deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 2. **Omitting External Failure Costs in COQ problems.** This is the **single most common mistake** in numerical problems. Always list all four cost categories and remember that $C_{EF}$ is usually the largest category in *poor* quality systems.
> 3. **Listing Deming's 14 points but missing the *reasoning*.** A list without an explanation of *why* (e.g., why drive out fear?) earns partial credit. Always couple each point with its underlying philosophy.
> 4. **Confusing Juran's Trilogy with Deming's PDCA.** Juran's trilogy is *managerial* (planning, control, improvement); PDCA is *operational* (plan-do-check-act). They are complementary, not identical.
> 5. **Writing "Quality is meeting specifications" only.** Crosby's definition is "conformance to requirements" — wider than just specs. Mention both.
> 6. **Skipping the units in cost problems.** Always write ₹ or ₹ lakhs. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Total Quality Control:**

- **Definition:** TQC = Organisation-wide integration of Quality *Development* + *Maintenance* + *Improvement*. Coined by **Feigenbaum (1956)**.
- **Three Pillars:** Development (design), Maintenance (SPC/SOPs), Improvement (Kaizen/DMAIC).
- **Cost of Quality (Juran's 4 categories):** $C_P$ + $C_A$ + $C_{IF}$ + $C_{EF}$. Always minimise the total, not individual categories.
- **Optimal Quality Cost:** Found at $\dfrac{dC_{TQC}}{dq} = 0$, validated by second derivative > 0.
- **Deming's 14 Points:** Memorise all 14; expect a 3-mark or 7-mark question. Key themes: *constancy of purpose, drive out fear, cease dependence on inspection, institute leadership, break down silos*.
- **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement (closed loop).
- **Crosby:** "Quality is conformance to requirements" + *Zero Defects* philosophy + "Quality is Free".
- **Ishikawa:** Quality Circles + Fishbone (cause-and-effect) diagram.
- **Taguchi:** Loss Function — quality is deviation from target, even within specs incurs loss.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (the operational engine of TQC).
- **TQC vs TQM vs SQC:** SQC = statistical tools, TQC = organisation-wide ownership, TQM = strategic customer-stakeholder value. **TQC is the bridge.**
- **DPMO / Six Sigma:** $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$; Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO ≈ 99.99966% yield.
- **AQL vs LTPD:** AQL is producer's protection; LTPD is consumer's protection. A *rectifying* inspection plan protects both.
- **Implementation Roadmap:** Top management commitment → Training → KPIs → Process mapping → SPC → Quality circles → Supplier partnership → Continuous improvement.
- **Common Pitfall:** Skipping $C_{EF}$ in COQ problems; confusing TQC with TQM; writing Deming's points without the underlying *philosophy*.
- **Real-world anchors:** ISO 9001:2015, IATF 16949 (automotive), AS9100 (aerospace), Six Sigma at Motorola/GE, Toyota Production System (precursor to Lean-TQC).
- **High-Yield Phrases for 14-mark answers:** *"Quality is everyone's responsibility"* (Feigenbaum), *"Constancy of purpose"* (Deming), *"Fitness for use"* (Juran), *"Conformance to requirements"* (Crosby), *"The next process is your customer"* (Ohno/Toyota).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Management of Product Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need for Management of Product Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality** is formally defined as the **totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs** (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000). **Management of Product Quality** is the coordinated set of activities directed towards the planning, controlling, assuring, and improving of product quality so that the product conforms to design specifications and satisfies the customer.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the need for management of product quality arises from three intersecting pressures:

1. **Customer Pressure** — Increasing awareness and rising expectations.
2. **Competitive Pressure** — Global markets and benchmarking.
3. **Economic Pressure** — Minimizing rework, warranty, and reputation costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The need for quality management is the foundation upon which all statistical tools (control charts, acceptance sampling, process capability) are built. Every statistical technique ultimately serves the management of product quality.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **restaurant kitchen**. A single chef who cooks, plates, and serves has full control, but as the restaurant grows, dozens of dishes leave the kitchen every hour, multiple cooks work different stations, and a hundred customers taste each batch. Without a **manager coordinating recipes, tasting samples, and enforcing hygiene**, food quality becomes a lottery — sometimes excellent, often inconsistent, and occasionally unsafe.

Product quality management plays exactly that managerial role in a factory: it sets the **recipe (specification)**, monitors the **cooking (process)**, tastes a **sample (inspection)**, and feeds back the **customer's verdict (feedback)** so that the next batch is better.

### 1.3 The "Need" — Why Quality Cannot Be Left to Chance

| Driver | Plain English Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Customer Satisfaction** | A happy customer returns five times; a dissatisfied one tells ten others. |
| **Cost Reduction** | Prevention is always cheaper than detection or failure. |
| **Competitive Edge** | Quality is a non-price differentiator in commoditized markets. |
| **Legal Compliance** | Product liability laws and mandatory standards (BIS, ISO). |
| **Employee Morale** | Workers take pride in products they can stand behind. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** *Cost of Quality (CoQ)* = Cost of Prevention + Cost of Appraisal + Cost of Internal Failure + Cost of External Failure. KTU examiners expect students to articulate all four buckets.

### 1.4 Evolution of the Quality Concept

Quality thinking has evolved through **four eras**, each adding a new dimension of "need":

- **Inspection Era (Pre-1930):** Quality = conformity to specification. Need: separate good from bad.
- **Quality Control Era (1930–1950):** Quality = statistical control of the process. Need: use sampling and Shewhart charts.
- **Quality Assurance Era (1950–1980):** Quality = the entire system that prevents defects. Need: documented systems (ISO 9001).
- **Total Quality Management Era (1980–Present):** Quality = customer delight and continuous improvement. Need: every employee, every process, every day.

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions on this topic often begin with: *"Discuss the evolution of quality management."* Keep the four-era table above memorized.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Need — Structured Logic

**Need 1 — Variability is Inherent in Every Process**

No two products are perfectly identical because of the **4 M's of variation**: Man (operator), Machine, Material, Method. Without active management, natural variability drifts toward customer dissatisfaction.

**Need 2 — The Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) is Hidden but Enormous**

Studies (Juran, Gryna) estimate that **15%–40% of sales revenue** is consumed by poor quality in organizations without active quality management. The "iceberg model" of quality costs is critical:

- **Visible costs (1/10 of the iceberg above water):** Scrap, rework, warranty claims, returns.
- **Hidden costs (9/10 below water):** Lost customer goodwill, lost market share, employee frustration, litigation.

**Need 3 — Defects are Easier to Prevent than to Detect and Correct**

The **1-10-100 Rule (Rule of Ten)** states that the cost of fixing a defect multiplies by **10** at every stage downstream:

| Stage of Fixing | Relative Cost |
|---|---|
| At design stage | **1 unit** |
| At production stage | **10 units** |
| After delivery to customer | **100 units** |
| After customer complaint / recall | **1000 units** |

**Need 4 — Quality is a System, not a Department**

Modern quality thinking (Deming, Ishikawa) insists that **85% of quality problems are system-related and only 15% are worker-related**. A quality department alone cannot fix a broken process; the entire organization must be involved.

**Need 5 — Statistical Evidence Replaces Guesswork**

SQC tools convert subjective opinions into **objective, measurable data**, allowing engineers to distinguish between *common cause* (inherent) and *special cause* (assignable) variation — the foundation of all subsequent modules in this course.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every high-yield formula / cost metric for the topic *"Need for Management of Product Quality"*. Use `\vert` and `\mid` notation in exam scripts as well to avoid table-breaking bars.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost of Quality (CoQ) | $CoQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency / unit | Total economic burden of quality decisions |
| Prevention Cost ($C_{P}$) | Training, design reviews, FMEA | Currency | Investment that avoids future failures |
| Appraisal Cost ($C_{A}$) | Inspection, testing, audits | Currency | Cost of measuring conformance |
| Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$) | Scrap + rework + re-inspection | Currency | Cost of defects found before shipment |
| External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$) | Warranty + returns + recalls | Currency | Cost of defects found by customer |
| Quality Loss (Taguchi) | $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ | Currency | Loss to society from deviation $\vert y - m \vert$ |
| Loss Coefficient ($k$) | $k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$ | Currency / unit² | Converts deviation to monetary loss |
| Conformance Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Conforming Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Process effectiveness indicator |
| Non-Conformance (NC) Rate | $NC = 1 - CR$ | % | Defect rate |
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \dfrac{\text{Satisfied Customers}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$ | % | Marketing + quality feedback loop |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Toyota Production System uses $C_{IF}$ data daily to trigger *jidoka* (autonomation) — stopping the line the moment defects are signalled.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Taguchi's loss function justifies investing in a **\$1 million** spectrometer because the loss from a single bad drug batch is **\$100 million** in recalls and reputation.
- **Software industry:** The 1-10-100 rule drives *shift-left testing* — fixing bugs at the requirement stage is 100× cheaper than fixing them post-release.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Quality Loss Function

The **Taguchi Quality Loss Function** quantifies the economic loss to society when a product's quality characteristic $y$ deviates from its target value $m$.

**Starting Assumption (Taguchi's quadratic loss model):**

Loss grows quadratically with deviation because the further the product moves from target, the worse the customer's experience and the larger the warranty exposure.

$$
L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the loss coefficient $k$.**

Let $\Delta_{0}$ be the functional tolerance (the deviation at which the customer is fully dissatisfied) and $A_{0}$ be the total loss incurred at $\Delta_{0}$ (typically the cost of replacing the product plus customer goodwill loss).

$$
L(\Delta_{0}) = A_{0} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k(\Delta_{0} - m)^{2} = A_{0}
$$

**Step 2 — For symmetric specifications**, $\Delta_{0} = $ the specification limit, so $\vert \Delta_{0} - m \vert = \Delta_{0}$.

$$
k(\Delta_{0})^{2} = A_{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k$.**

$$
k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the original loss equation.**

$$
L(y) = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}} \,(y - m)^{2}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The loss coefficient $k$ converts a **physical deviation** $\vert y - m \vert$ into a **monetary loss**, allowing quality engineers to justify investments in precision equipment using pure dollars-and-cents reasoning.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem.** A bolt's target diameter is $m = 10.00\ \text{mm}$. The customer-specified functional tolerance is $\Delta_{0} = 0.50\ \text{mm}$ (any deviation beyond this leads to rejection). The replacement cost plus goodwill loss at the tolerance limit is $A_{0} = \text{Rs. }100$ per bolt.

**Step A — Compute the loss coefficient.**

$$
k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}} = \frac{100}{(0.50)^{2}} = \frac{100}{0.25} = 400\ \text{Rs/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step B — Loss for a bolt with diameter $y = 10.20\ \text{mm}$.**

$$
L(10.20) = 400 \times (10.20 - 10.00)^{2} = 400 \times (0.20)^{2} = 400 \times 0.04 = \text{Rs. }16
$$

**Step C — Loss for a bolt with diameter $y = 9.70\ \text{mm}$.**

$$
L(9.70) = 400 \times (9.70 - 10.00)^{2} = 400 \times (-0.30)^{2} = 400 \times 0.09 = \text{Rs. }36
$$

**Step D — Loss for a bolt exactly at tolerance limit $y = 10.50\ \text{mm}$.**

$$
L(10.50) = 400 \times (10.50 - 10.00)^{2} = 400 \times (0.50)^{2} = 400 \times 0.25 = \text{Rs. }100 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The result confirms the definition: at $\Delta_{0}$, the loss equals $A_{0}$ = Rs. 100. 

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always verify the boundary condition $\Delta_{0}$ when solving Taguchi loss problems — examiners award **1 mark** explicitly for this sanity check.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost of Quality Decomposition

The following matrix decomposes a typical manufacturing unit producing **N = 10,000 units/month** with a current **NC rate of 5%**.

| Cost Bucket | Symbol | Per-Unit Rate (Rs.) | Monthly Cost (Rs.) | Percentage of Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prevention | $C_{P}$ | 2 | 20,000 | **13.3%** |
| Appraisal | $C_{A}$ | 3 | 30,000 | **20.0%** |
| Internal Failure | $C_{IF}$ | 4 | 20,000 | **13.3%** |
| External Failure | $C_{EF}$ | 6 | 30,000 | **20.0%** |
| **Total CoQ** | **$CoQ$** | — | **100,000** | **100%** |

> [!NOTE]
> An *optimal* quality program **increases $C_{P}$ and $C_{A}$** to **dramatically reduce $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$**, lowering total CoQ. This is the empirical justification for managing product quality.

### 3.4 Derivation of the 1-10-100 Rule's Break-Even Point

Let $C_{0}$ be the cost of fixing a defect at the design stage. The cost at any later stage $n$ is:

$$
C_{n} = C_{0} \times 10^{n}
$$

where $n$ is the number of stages downstream ($n=0$ design, $n=1$ production, $n=2$ delivery, $n=3$ complaint).

**Cross-check with our bolt example:** If $C_{0} = \text{Rs. }1$ at design, then at complaint stage the cost is $1 \times 10^{3} = \text{Rs. }1000$, validating the rule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four Drivers of the Need for Quality Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> B2[Competitive Pressure]

    A --> B4[Legal and Regulatory Pressure]
    B1 --> C1[Rising Expectations]
    B1 --> C2[Voice of Customer VOC]
    B2 --> D1[Global Benchmarks]
    B2 --> D2[ISO 9001 Standards]
    B3 --> E1[Cost of Poor Quality COPQ]
    B3 --> E2[Taguchi Loss Function]
    B4 --> F1[Product Liability Laws]
    B4 --> F2[BIS Standards India]
    C1 --> G[Customer Satisfaction Retention]
    C2 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G[OUTCOME: Sustainable Business Growth]
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define *Quality* as per ISO 8402. List any two dimensions of quality.**

**Model Answer:**
Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs (ISO 8402). Two dimensions of quality (Garvin) are:
1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics of the product.
2. **Reliability** — Probability of the product performing its intended function over time without failure.
*(Acceptable alternatives: Durability, Conformance, Features, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.)*
*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each dimension = 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the 1-10-100 Rule of quality cost. Why is prevention considered the most economical strategy?**

**Model Answer:**
The **1-10-100 Rule** states that the cost of fixing a defect multiplies by **10** at every successive stage downstream — from design (1×) to production (10×) to delivery (100×) to complaint (1000×). Prevention is the most economical strategy because the cost of *preventing* a defect during design is a one-time investment, whereas *appraisal* (inspection) and *failure* (rework/warranty) costs recur with every defective unit. Prevention breaks the cost-multiplication chain at its origin. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Discuss in detail the evolution of quality management, highlighting the four eras and the corresponding need for quality in each era.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Era 1 — Inspection Era (Pre-1930):**
- Quality was the responsibility of individual craftsmen.
- Industrial revolution introduced mass production; **inspection** became a separate function to *sort good from bad*.
- **Need:** To identify defective products before shipment.
- **Tools:** 100% visual inspection, go/no-go gauges.

**Era 2 — Quality Control Era (1930–1950):**
- Walter Shewhart (Bell Labs) introduced **statistical quality control** — control charts, sampling plans.
- Quality shifted from *detecting* defects to *controlling* the process.
- **Need:** To manage variation economically across large production volumes.
- **Tools:** $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts, acceptance sampling (MIL-STD).

**Era 3 — Quality Assurance Era (1950–1980):**
- Quality became a **system-wide** responsibility; quality departments formalized QA manuals.
- **Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) and **Deming's 14 Points** emerged.
- **Need:** To build quality *into* the product rather than inspect it *out*.
- **Tools:** ISO 9000 series, FMEA, QFD (Quality Function Deployment).

**Era 4 — Total Quality Management Era (1980–Present):**
- TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, Agile — quality is **everyone's job** (Kaizen, cross-functional teams).
- **Need:** Continuous improvement (PDCA cycle), customer delight, and global competitiveness.
- **Tools:** DMAIC, Balanced Scorecard, Benchmarking.

*(1 mark per era for the description + 1 mark for "need" + 0.5 mark for example tools ≈ 7 marks)*

**(b) Explain Taguchi's Quality Loss Function. A specification for a shaft diameter is $m = 25.00\ \text{mm}$ with functional tolerance $\Delta_{0} = 0.40\ \text{mm}$. If the cost of replacement at the tolerance limit is $A_{0} = \text{Rs. }200$ per shaft, compute the loss for a shaft of diameter $24.70\ \text{mm}$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Taguchi's Quality Loss Function Concept:**
Taguchi argued that every deviation from target $m$ inflicts a loss on society, even within specifications. The loss is modeled as a quadratic function:

$$
L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}
$$

where $k$ is the loss coefficient and $(y - m)$ is the deviation.

**Step 1 — Compute the loss coefficient.**

$$
k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}} = \frac{200}{(0.40)^{2}} = \frac{200}{0.16} = 1250\ \text{Rs/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the loss at $y = 24.70\ \text{mm}$.**

$$
L(24.70) = 1250 \times (24.70 - 25.00)^{2}
$$

$$
L(24.70) = 1250 \times (-0.30)^{2} = 1250 \times 0.09
$$

$$
L(24.70) = \text{Rs. }112.50
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
A shaft that is 0.30 mm under-sized (still within the $\pm 0.40$ mm tolerance) inflicts a loss of Rs. 112.50 per unit — even though the part is "acceptable" by classical specification. This is why Taguchi emphasized **on-target, minimum-variation** manufacturing.

*(2 marks concept + 1 mark $k$ derivation + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark final answer + 1 mark interpretation = 7 marks)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) What is the Cost of Quality? Explain its four components with examples. Why is prevention cost considered an investment rather than an expense?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Cost of Quality (CoQ) is the total economic impact of quality-related activities, encompassing both the cost of *achieving* good quality and the cost of *consequences* of poor quality.

$$
CoQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

**The Four Components:**

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_{P}$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring.
   - Examples: Quality training, design reviews, FMEA workshops, supplier certification.
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_{A}$):** Costs of *measuring, evaluating, and auditing* products.
   - Examples: Incoming inspection, in-process testing, calibration of gauges.
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Costs of defects found *before* shipment.
   - Examples: Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime, wastage of materials.
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Costs of defects found *after* shipment by the customer.
   - Examples: Warranty claims, returns, recalls, product liability lawsuits.

**Prevention is an Investment, Not an Expense:**

- $C_{P}$ is the **only controllable** cost; failure costs are *symptoms*, not causes.
- Empirical data shows: **every Rs. 1 spent on prevention saves Rs. 10 in appraisal, Rs. 100 in internal failure, and Rs. 1000 in external failure** (1-10-100 rule).
- It transforms quality from a *reactive cost center* into a *proactive profit center* by reducing hidden CoPQ (Cost of Poor Quality).
- Firms with high $C_{P}$ ratios (e.g., Toyota, Boeing) consistently report lower total CoQ and higher customer satisfaction scores.

*(1 mark formula + 1.5 marks each component with example + 2 marks "investment" reasoning ≈ 7 marks)*

**(b) A manufacturing firm produces 50,000 components per month. The current non-conformance rate is 4%. Each defect detected internally costs Rs. 80 for rework, and each defect reaching the customer costs Rs. 800 (warranty + logistics). Internal detection rate is 75%. Compute (i) the number of defects reaching customers, (ii) the monthly internal failure cost, and (iii) the monthly external failure cost. Comment on the need for management action.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $N = 50{,}000$ components/month
- $NC = 4\%$
- $C_{IF,unit} = \text{Rs. }80$ per internal defect
- $C_{EF,unit} = \text{Rs. }800$ per external defect
- Internal detection rate = 75% (i.e., 75% of defects are caught internally, 25% leak to the customer)

**Step 1 — Total defects produced.**

$$
D_{total} = 0.04 \times 50{,}000 = 2{,}000\ \text{defects/month}
$$

**Step 2 — Defects reaching customers (i).**

$$
D_{external} = (1 - 0.75) \times 2{,}000 = 0.25 \times 2{,}000 = 500\ \text{defects/month}
$$

**Step 3 — Defects caught internally.**

$$
D_{internal} = 0.75 \times 2{,}000 = 1{,}500\ \text{defects/month}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly Internal Failure Cost (ii).**

$$
C_{IF} = 1{,}500 \times 80 = \text{Rs. }120{,}000\ /\ \text{month}
$$

**Step 5 — Monthly External Failure Cost (iii).**

$$
C_{EF} = 500 \times 800 = \text{Rs. }400{,}000\ /\ \text{month}
$$

**Step 6 — Total CoPQ (Internal + External Failure only).**

$$
CoPQ = 120{,}000 + 400{,}000 = \text{Rs. }520{,}000\ /\ \text{month}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment on the need for management action.**

The external failure cost (Rs. 400,000) is **3.33 times** the internal failure cost. This indicates the firm's *appraisal* system is under-performing — 500 defects/month are escaping to customers. Recommended actions:
- Invest in **prevention**: improve process capability ($C_{pk}$), operator training, supplier quality.
- Strengthen **appraisal**: add automated inspection at multiple stages.
- Apply **SPC tools** (control charts) from Module 2 onwards to detect and eliminate assignable causes.

*(1 mark each sub-part + 1 mark for Step 6 + 2 marks for management comment ≈ 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Verified against KTU 2024 Answer Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Writing "Quality = Degree of Excellence"** — Marked *incorrect*. Always use the **ISO 8402 / ISO 9000** definition for full credit.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k$ derivation** in Taguchi problems — Examiners allocate **1 full mark** to the explicit step $k = A_{0}/\Delta_{0}^{2}$. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Mixing up "Prevention" with "Appraisal"** — Prevention = *before* the defect occurs (training, design). Appraisal = *detecting* the defect (inspection). Examiners frequently set traps here.
> 4. **Ignoring the units** of $k$ (currency / unit²) — KTU awards marks for dimensional consistency.
> 5. **In the 1-10-100 rule**, students often write the stages in the wrong order. Memorize: **Design → Production → Customer → Complaint**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Need for Management of Product Quality"**

- ⭐ **ISO Definition of Quality** is mandatory for any 3-mark definition question.
- ⭐ **Four Eras of Quality:** Inspection → Quality Control → Quality Assurance → TQM.
- ⭐ **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** = $C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- ⭐ **1-10-100 Rule:** Cost of fixing a defect multiplies by 10 at every downstream stage.
- ⭐ **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ with $k = A_{0}/\Delta_{0}^{2}$ — loss exists *even within* specifications.
- ⭐ **Iceberg Model:** 90% of quality costs are **hidden** below the waterline (goodwill, market share).
- ⭐ **Deming's 85/15 Rule:** 85% of quality problems are *system-related*, 15% are *worker-related*.
- ⭐ **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- ⭐ **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- ⭐ **Customer Satisfaction + Cost Reduction + Competitive Edge + Legal Compliance + Employee Morale** = the five core "needs."
- ⭐ Always **verify the boundary condition** $\Delta_{0}$ in Taguchi problems.
- ⭐ Units of $k$: **currency / (unit)²** — never omit them in derivations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word:** The "Need for Management of Product Quality" is not merely a theoretical chapter — it is the **economic and ethical justification** for every statistical tool you will learn in Modules 2 through 5. Master this foundation, and the rest of Statistical Quality Control becomes a logical extension of *why* quality must be managed scientifically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Total Quality Management (TQM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Total Quality Management (TQM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that seeks to **continuously improve** the quality of products, services, and processes by involving **all members** of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — while maintaining a **strong customer focus**. It integrates fundamental management techniques, existing improvement efforts, and specialized statistical tools under a disciplined, people-centered approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** TQM is a **customer-driven**, **data-based**, **participative**, and **process-oriented** philosophy that embeds quality into every functional activity of an organization through the cooperation of everyone in the organization.

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, TQM directly maps to the following Course Outcomes (COs):
- **CO2:** Apply statistical tools and total quality management principles to solve industrial quality problems.
- **CO3:** Analyze process capability and implement control systems for continuous improvement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a five-star restaurant. The head chef (top management) sets the recipe and quality standards. Every sous-chef, line cook, dishwasher, and server (all employees) is responsible for ensuring the dish served to the customer is perfect. The manager constantly checks customer reviews (customer focus), the chef adjusts seasoning based on feedback (continuous improvement), and standardized recipes ensure consistency (process orientation). If even the dishwasher skips cleaning a pan properly, the next dish could fail — meaning **every single person** impacts quality.
>
> This is exactly what TQM means in an industrial setting: **quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the QA department's**.

## 1.3 Origin and Evolution of TQM

| Era | Pioneer / Origin | Key Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1920s–1930s | **Walter A. Shewhart** (Bell Labs) | Developed the **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** foundation (control charts). |
| 1950s | **W. Edwards Deming** (Japan) | Taught statistical methods to Japanese engineers; introduced the **PDCA cycle** (Deming Wheel). |
| 1951 | **Joseph M. Juran** | Defined quality as *"fitness for use"*; introduced the **Juran Trilogy**. |
| 1956 | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Coined the term **"Total Quality Control (TQC)"** in his book *Total Quality Control*. |
| 1960s | **Kaoru Ishikawa** (Japan) | Developed **Quality Circles** and the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram**. |
| 1970s | **Philip B. Crosby** | Propagated the **"Zero Defects"** philosophy and the concept of **"Quality is Free"**. |
| 1980s | **Genichi Taguchi** (Japan) | Introduced **Taguchi Loss Function** — quality loss begins the moment a specification is deviated from. |
| 1985 | **Naval Air Systems Command (USA)** | Coined the acronym **"TQM"** in the U.S. Navy's *Total Quality Management* guidebook. |
| 1987 | **ISO 9000 Series** Published | Global standardization of quality management systems. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Transition TQC → TQM:** The original Japanese term was **Total Quality Control (TQC)** focused primarily on manufacturing. The modern term **Total Quality Management (TQM)** broadened the scope to include **all organizational functions** (HR, finance, marketing, design, service) — making it a true **management philosophy**, not just a production tool.

## 1.4 Core Characteristics of TQM

TQM is built upon **eight universal characteristics** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Customer Focus** — Quality is defined by the customer (both internal and external).
2. **Total Employee Involvement / Participation** — Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor worker, is responsible for quality.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Never-ending incremental improvement of products and processes.
4. **Process-Centered Approach** — Focus on improving the *process*, not blaming the *person*.
5. **Integrated System** — All departments (production, marketing, finance, HR) work as a unified system.
6. **Strategic and Systematic Approach** — Quality management is integrated into the strategic plan of the organization.
7. **Fact-Based Decision Making (Data-Driven)** — Decisions are made on data, statistics, and analysis — not intuition.
8. **Effective Communication** — Quality information flows freely up, down, and across the organization.

## 1.5 Distinction: TQM vs. TQC vs. SQC vs. QMS vs. Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Clarity Box — High-Frequency Exam Confusion**

| Framework | Full Form | Focus | Scope | Tool Base |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SQC** | Statistical Quality Control | Monitoring & controlling processes via statistical methods | Manufacturing | Control charts, sampling plans |
| **TQC** | Total Quality Control | Company-wide quality control | Manufacturing-dominant | SQC + problem-solving tools |
| **TQM** | Total Quality Management | Holistic management philosophy | Entire organization | SQC + management techniques + HR |
| **QMS** | Quality Management System | Documented system (e.g., ISO 9001) | All processes | Documentation, audits, procedures |
| **Six Sigma** | Six Sigma | Defect reduction to **3.4 DPMO** | Project-based | DMAIC + advanced statistics |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous Improvement | Small, incremental daily improvements | Workplace | PDCA, 5S, Gemba |

**Mnemonic:** *SQC is the toolset, TQC is the production philosophy, TQM is the management philosophy.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Deming Wheel (PDCA Cycle) — The Backbone of TQM

**W. Edwards Deming** redesigned Walter Shewhart's original plan-do-check-act cycle and adapted it into the four-phase **PDCA** model that drives all TQM activity.

### Phase-by-Phase Breakdown

| Phase | Name | Core Activity | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P** | **Plan** | Identify the problem, analyze root cause, set objectives, develop an action plan. | Improvement plan, hypothesis, targets. |
| **D** | **Do** | Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot). Collect data rigorously. | Pilot data, observations, evidence. |
| **C** | **Check / Study** | Analyze pilot data. Compare actual results with expected results using statistical tools. | Validated conclusion, gap analysis. |
| **A** | **Act** | If successful → standardize & implement organization-wide. If not → revise plan and restart cycle. | Standardized process OR new PDCA cycle. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PDCA matters in TQM:** The cycle is **iterative and never-ending**. Each completed PDCA raises the baseline of quality, and the next cycle tackles a higher-level problem. This is the **mechanical engine of Kaizen (continuous improvement)**.

**Engineering Utility:** PDCA is applied in **any** process improvement — from reducing solder defects in a PCB assembly line (electronics) to reducing patient wait-time in a hospital (service industry).

## 2.2 The Juran Trilogy (Three Managerial Processes)

**Joseph Juran** defined quality management as a sequence of three universal managerial processes:

1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers, determine their needs, develop product/process to meet those needs.
2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; take corrective action on the difference.
3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure to continuously improve — the *Quality Improvement (QI)* project-by-project approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Project-by-Project Breakthrough Sequence:**
> 1. **Proof of Need** → Build awareness of improvement opportunity.
> 2. **Project Definition** → Nomination of projects.
> 3. **Project Organization** → Diagnose root cause, recommend remedy.
> 4. **Diagnostic Journey** → Establish causes.
> 5. **Remedial Action** → Apply remedies, prove effectiveness.
> 6. **Hold the Gains** → Implement controls to maintain improvement.

## 2.3 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality

**Philip Crosby** defined quality philosophically through his **"Four Absolutes"** and the **"Zero Defects"** concept:

1. Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness").
2. Prevention, not inspection — quality must be **built in**, not inspected in.
3. Performance standard = **Zero Defects** (not acceptable quality level).
4. Measure of quality = **Price of non-conformance (PONC)**.

**Crosby's 14 Steps to Quality Improvement** is a KTU-favorite:
- Management commitment → Quality improvement team → Measurement → Cost of quality evaluation → Quality awareness → Corrective action → Establish zero-defect program → Training → Zero-defect day → Goal setting → Error-cause removal → Recognition → Quality councils → Do it all over again.

## 2.4 Taguchi's Loss Function

**Genichi Taguchi** revolutionized quality thinking by showing that quality loss is **continuous**, not binary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Insight:** Any deviation from the target value $T$ causes loss to society — even if the product is still within specification limits.

The Taguchi quadratic loss function is:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - T)^2$$

Where:
- $L(y)$ = Loss incurred to society from a unit with measured quality $y$
- $y$ = Actual quality characteristic value
- $T$ = Target value (nominal specification)
- $k$ = Proportionality constant (quality loss coefficient)

The constant $k$ is determined using the specification limits (USL, LSL) and the customer's maximum acceptable loss $A_0$:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2}$$

Where $\Delta$ is the **tolerance** — the distance from target $T$ to the nearest specification limit (USL or LSL).

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- If the product is at the **target** $y = T$, loss is **zero** — perfect.
- If $y$ deviates from $T$ by $\Delta$ (i.e., $y$ is at the USL or LSL), loss equals $A_0$ — the customer dissatisfaction cost.
- Loss grows **quadratically**, so even small deviations from target are costly in the long run.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas, conditions, and units you must memorize for KTU ESE 2024.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Variables & Units | Application / When to Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Taguchi Loss Function** | $L(y) = k \cdot (y - T)^2$ | $L$ = monetary loss (₹), $y$ = actual value, $T$ = target, $k$ = ₹/unit² | Calculate societal cost of a deviation from target. |
| 2 | **Loss Coefficient** | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta^2}$ | $A_0$ = loss at spec limit (₹), $\Delta = \vert T - \text{USL} \vert$ or $\vert T - \text{LSL} \vert$ | Compute $k$ when USL/LSL and customer's $A_0$ are given. |
| 3 | **Crosby's Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$ | All terms in ₹ per period | Compute total quality cost; identify optimal prevention spend. |
| 4 | **Six Sigma DPMO (Target)** | $\text{DPMO} \leq 3.4$ | Defects per million opportunities | Verify Six Sigma quality level. |
| 5 | **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Measure inherent process capability. |
| 6 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Dimensionless | Measure centered process capability. |
| 7 | **Kaizen Definition** | $\text{Kaizen} = \text{Kai (change)} + \text{Zen (good/well)}$ | Conceptual | Define continuous incremental improvement. |
| 8 | **Quality Cost Ratio (Crosby)** | $\text{Optimal PONC} \approx \text{Prevention Cost} \times 2$ | Monetary (₹) | Rule-of-thumb to minimize CoQ. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for Markdown Tables:** Absolute value and conditional expressions inside the table are written using `\vert` or `\min\left(\right)` to avoid breaking the table syntax. Always follow this convention in your KTU exam answer sheets.

## 2.6 Why TQM Works — The Underlying "Why" and "How"

- **Why Customer Focus?** Because the *customer pays the salaries* — they define value. A product with zero defects but no demand is still a failure.
- **Why Total Involvement?** Quality problems originate **upstream**. A design error in R&D becomes a manufacturing defect becomes a customer complaint. Only a horizontal, organization-wide system catches errors early.
- **Why Process-Centered?** Because **85% of quality problems are system/process-related**, not worker-related (Deming's 85/15 Rule). Fixing the process fixes the output.
- **Why Data-Driven?** Intuition-based decisions lead to "tampering" (Deming's term) — over-correction that increases variation, not decreases it. Statistical thinking exposes the real signal in the noise.
- **Why Continuous Improvement?** Because competitors improve continuously; standing still means falling behind. Kaizen is the **only sustainable competitive strategy** in a globalized market.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utilities of TQM

| Industry | TQM Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive (Toyota Production System)** | Kaizen, Jidoka (autonomation), Just-in-Time, Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing). |
| **Electronics (Intel, Samsung)** | Six Sigma, SPC, FMEA-driven design. |
| **Healthcare (Mayo Clinic, Apollo)** | PDCA to reduce surgical errors, patient wait-time, hospital-acquired infections. |
| **Software (ISO 9001 + CMMI)** | Defect prevention, code review process, customer satisfaction metrics. |
| **Education (NAAC Accreditation)** | Outcome-based education, continuous curriculum improvement. |
| **Defense & Aerospace (NASA, DRDO)** | Zero-defect philosophy, AS9100 quality system. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Function

This derivation explains **why** the loss is quadratic and how the constant $k$ is anchored to real-world financial data.

### Step 1: Define the Conceptual Premise

Taguchi observed that any deviation from the target value $T$ causes dissatisfaction to the customer and to society. This dissatisfaction can be expressed as a monetary loss $L(y)$ that depends on the difference between the actual value $y$ and the target $T$.

Let the deviation be defined as:

$$d = y - T$$

### Step 2: Impose Boundary Conditions

1. **At the target:** When $y = T$, the loss must be zero.
   $$L(T) = 0$$
2. **At the specification limit:** When $y$ reaches the upper or lower specification limit (USL or LSL), the loss equals the customer's maximum acceptable loss $A_0$.

If the specification is symmetric, the distance from $T$ to the nearest spec limit is:
$$\Delta = \vert T - \text{USL} \vert = \vert T - \text{LSL} \vert$$

So at $y = T \pm \Delta$, the loss is $A_0$.

### Step 3: Assume a Quadratic Form

Taguchi chose the simplest symmetric function that is zero at $d = 0$ and grows with $\vert d \vert$:

$$L(y) = k \cdot d^2 = k \cdot (y - T)^2$$

This is the **Quadratic Loss Function** (also called the **Taguchi Loss Function**).

### Step 4: Determine the Constant $k$

Apply the boundary condition at the specification limit. At $y = T + \Delta$ (upper spec limit):

$$L(T + \Delta) = A_0$$

Substitute into the quadratic form:

$$k \cdot (T + \Delta - T)^2 = A_0$$

Simplify the expression inside the square:

$$k \cdot \Delta^2 = A_0$$

Solve for $k$:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2}$$

This is the **Loss Coefficient** — it quantifies the monetary penalty per unit squared of deviation.

### Step 5: Final Compact Form

Substitute $k$ back into the loss function:

$$L(y) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2} \cdot (y - T)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Loss grows as the **square** of the deviation. A 2-unit deviation causes **4 times** the loss of a 1-unit deviation. This is the mathematical foundation of "the closer to target, the lower the total cost to society" — the central tenet of Taguchi's philosophy.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A critical dimension on a CNC-machined shaft has a target value $T = 50.00$ mm. The specification limits are USL = 50.05 mm and LSL = 49.95 mm. The customer has stated that a part at the specification limit causes a loss of $A_0 = ₹100$ to them. A batch of parts is measured and the average measured value is $y = 50.02$ mm.
> **(a)** Find the Taguchi loss coefficient $k$.
> **(b)** Find the loss per part for the measured value.
> **(c)** Compare with the loss if the part was exactly on target.

### Solution (a) — Finding $k$

The tolerance $\Delta$ is the distance from target to the nearest spec limit:

$$\Delta = \vert T - \text{USL} \vert = \vert 50.00 - 50.05 \vert = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

Now compute $k$:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta^2} = \frac{100}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{100}{0.0025} = 40{,}000 \text{ ₹/mm}^2$$

### Solution (b) — Loss at $y = 50.02$ mm

The deviation is:

$$d = y - T = 50.02 - 50.00 = 0.02 \text{ mm}$$

Substitute into the loss function:

$$L(50.02) = k \cdot d^2 = 40{,}000 \times (0.02)^2$$

Calculate the squared term:

$$L(50.02) = 40{,}000 \times 0.0004$$

Final result:

$$L(50.02) = ₹16 \text{ per part}$$

### Solution (c) — Loss at Target $y = 50.00$ mm

At the target, the deviation is:

$$d = 0 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$L(50.00) = 40{,}000 \times (0)^2 = ₹0$$

### Analytical Insight from the Numerical Result

| Measured $y$ (mm) | Deviation $d$ (mm) | Loss per part (₹) | Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **50.00** | 0.00 | 0.00 | Perfect part. Zero loss. |
| **50.01** | 0.01 | 4.00 | Small deviation = small but non-zero loss. |
| **50.02** | 0.02 | 16.00 | Loss **quadrupled** (not doubled!) |
| **50.03** | 0.03 | 36.00 | Loss **9×** the 0.01-deviation case. |
| **50.04** | 0.04 | 64.00 | Approaching the spec-limit loss. |
| **50.05** | 0.05 | 100.00 | At USL — full customer loss of ₹100. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Traditional SQC says the part at 50.02 is *acceptable* (within spec). Taguchi's TQM says it has already cost ₹16 to society. This is why Taguchi advocates **"aim for the target"** rather than **"stay within the limits"** — the latter approach accepts hidden costs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Quality Gurus

| Dimension | **Deming** | **Juran** | **Crosby** | **Taguchi** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition of Quality** | Predictable uniformity & dependability | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Loss to society from deviation |
| **Focus** | System / process variation | Customer & planning | Prevention & zero defects | Target-based design |
| **Approach** | Statistical, PDCA | Managerial trilogy | Motivational, behavioral | Engineering / robust design |
| **Key Tool** | PDCA cycle, 14 Points | Juran Trilogy, Pareto | 14 Steps, Zero Defects | Loss Function, S/N Ratio |
| **Cultural Origin** | American, applied in Japan | American, applied globally | American | Japanese |
| **Best Suited For** | Manufacturing + service | Strategic management | Behavioral/cultural change | Engineering design & process control |
| **Criticism** | Too statistical, less actionable | Slow implementation | "Zero Defects" is unattainable in practice | Loss function assumes quadratic form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **"D-J-C-T"** → **D**eming (variation), **J**uran (fitness), **C**rosby (conformance), **T**aguchi (loss).
