$$\underbrace{\sum_{i}\sum_{j}(X_{ij} - \bar{\bar{X}})^2}_{SST} = \underbrace{\sum_{i}\sum_{j}(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2}_{SSW} + \underbrace{\sum_{i} n_i (\bar{X}_i - \bar{\bar{X}})^2}_{SSB}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting the shorthand forms:

$$\boxed{SST = SSW + SSB}$$

This proves the variance partitioning identity that is the mathematical backbone of ANOVA.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Equal Sample Sizes)

**Problem:** A manufacturer uses **3 machines** to produce bolts. A random sample of **4 bolts** is taken from each machine. The diameters (in mm) are recorded below. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean diameters differ significantly.

| Machine A | Machine B | Machine C |
|---|---|---|
| 10.2 | 10.5 | 10.1 |
| 10.4 | 10.6 | 10.3 |
| 10.1 | 10.7 | 10.0 |
| 10.3 | 10.8 | 10.2 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Define Parameters**
- Number of groups: $k = 3$
- Sample size per group: $n_i = 4$
- Total observations: $N = 12$
- Significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$

**Step 2: State Hypotheses**
$$H_0 : \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C \quad ; \quad H_1 : \text{At least one mean differs}$$

**Step 3: Compute Group Totals and Grand Total**

$$T_A = 10.2 + 10.4 + 10.1 + 10.3 = 41.0$$

$$T_B = 10.5 + 10.6 + 10.7 + 10.8 = 42.6$$

$$T_C = 10.1 + 10.3 + 10.0 + 10.2 = 40.6$$

$$G = \sum X_{ij} = 41.0 + 42.6 + 40.6 = 124.2$$

**Step 4: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(124.2)^2}{12} = \frac{15425.64}{12} = 1285.47$$

**Step 5: Compute SST — Total Sum of Squares**

First, compute $\sum X_{ij}^2$:

$$\sum X_{ij}^2 = (10.2^2 + 10.4^2 + 10.1^2 + 10.3^2) + (10.5^2 + 10.6^2 + 10.7^2 + 10.8^2) + (10.1^2 + 10.3^2 + 10.0^2 + 10.2^2)$$

$$\sum X_{ij}^2 = (104.04 + 108.16 + 102.01 + 106.09) + (110.25 + 112.36 + 114.49 + 116.64) + (102.01 + 106.09 + 100.00 + 104.04)$$

$$\sum X_{ij}^2 = 420.30 + 453.74 + 412.14 = 1286.18$$

$$SST = \sum X_{ij}^2 - CF = 1286.18 - 1285.47 = 0.71$$

**Step 6: Compute SSB — Between-Group Sum of Squares**

Using the shortcut formula: $SSB = \dfrac{\sum T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$

$$SSB = \frac{(41.0)^2}{4} + \frac{(42.6)^2}{4} + \frac{(40.6)^2}{4} - 1285.47$$

$$SSB = \frac{1681.00}{4} + \frac{1814.76}{4} + \frac{1648.36}{4} - 1285.47$$

$$SSB = 420.25 + 453.69 + 412.09 - 1285.47$$

$$SSB = 1286.03 - 1285.47 = 0.56$$

**Step 7: Compute SSW — Within-Group Sum of Squares**

$$SSW = SST - SSB = 0.71 - 0.56 = 0.15$$

**Step 8: Compute Degrees of Freedom**

$$df_{between} = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$$

$$df_{within} = N - k = 12 - 3 = 9$$

**Step 9: Compute Mean Squares**

$$MSB = \frac{SSB}{k-1} = \frac{0.56}{2} = 0.28$$

$$MSW = \frac{SSW}{N-k} = \frac{0.15}{9} = 0.0167$$

**Step 10: Compute F-ratio**

$$F_{calculated} = \frac{MSB}{MSW} = \frac{0.28}{0.0167} \approx 16.77$$

**Step 11: Find Critical Value**

From F-tables at $\alpha = 0.05$, with $df_1 = 2$ and $df_2 = 9$:

$$F_{critical}(0.05, 2, 9) = 4.26$$

**Step 12: Decision**

$$F_{calculated} = 16.77 > F_{critical} = 4.26$$

**Decision: Reject $H_0$** — There is significant evidence at the 5% level that the mean bolt diameters produced by the three machines are different.

### 3.3 Completed ANOVA Table

| Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | F-ratio | F-critical |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Machines | 0.56 | 2 | 0.28 | **16.77** | 4.26 |
| Within Machines (Error) | 0.15 | 9 | 0.0167 | — | — |
| **Total** | **0.71** | **11** | — | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ANOVA Decision-Making Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect k samples with n_i observations each] --> B[State Hypotheses H0 and H1]
    B --> C[Check Assumptions: Normality, Independence, Equal Variance]
    C --> D{Assumptions Met?}
    D -- No --> E[Use Welch ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis Test]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute Group Totals T_i and Grand Total G]
    F --> G[Calculate Correction Factor CF = G^2 divided by N]
    G --> H[Calculate SST, SSB, SSW]
    H --> I[Compute df: k-1 for between, N-k for within]
    I --> J[Calculate MSB and MSW]
    J --> K[Compute F-ratio = MSB divided by MSW]
    K --> L{Compare F_calc with F_critical}
    L -- F_calc greater than F_critical --> M[Reject H0: Means differ significantly]
    L -- F_calc less than or equal to F_critical --> N[Fail to reject H0: Insufficient evidence]
    M --> O[Perform Post-hoc Test: Tukey HSD or Scheffe]
    N --> P[End: Conclude statistical equality]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Variance Partitioning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Variability SST] --> B[Between-Group Variability SSB]
    A --> C[Within-Group Variability SSW]
    B --> D[Treatment Effect: Caused by group differences]
    C --> E[Random Error: Caused by noise within groups]
    D --> F[MSB = SSB divided by k-1]
    E --> G[MSW = SSW divided by N-k]
    F --> H[F-ratio = MSB divided by MSW]
    G --> H
    H --> I{F greater than F_critical?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Reject H0]
    I -- No --> K[Accept H0]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: ANOVA as a Decision Engine

```mermaid
graph TB
    node1[INPUT BLOCK: Raw Data X_ij from k groups]
    node2[PREPROCESSING: Compute CF, T_i, Grand Mean]
    node3[SUM OF SQUARES ENGINE: Compute SST, SSB, SSW]
    node4[DEGREES OF FREEDOM ALLOCATOR: k-1 and N-k]
    node5[MEAN SQUARE CALCULATOR: MSB and MSW]
    node6[F-RATIO GENERATOR: F = MSB / MSW]
    node7[COMPARATOR: F_calc vs F_critical]
    node8[OUTPUT BLOCK A: Reject H0 or Accept H0]
    node9[POST-HOC ANALYSIS: Tukey HSD if rejected]
    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8 --> node9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define ANOVA. State the basic assumptions underlying the One-Way ANOVA test.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) is a statistical technique developed by **R.A. Fisher** used to test whether the means of **three or more independent populations** are equal, by partitioning the total variation in the data into components attributable to **between-group variation** and **within-group variation**.

**Assumptions:**
1. **Normality:** Each of the $k$ populations from which samples are drawn is normally distributed.
2. **Independence:** All the $k$ samples are independent of each other.
3. **Homogeneity of Variance:** All populations have a common variance, i.e., $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \dots = \sigma_k^2$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for assumptions)*

### Question 2: F-Distribution Application `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: What is the F-distribution? In One-Way ANOVA, what are the degrees of freedom associated with the numerator and denominator of the F-ratio?**

**Model Answer:**

**F-Distribution:** The F-distribution is a continuous probability distribution that arises as the ratio of two independent chi-square variates, each divided by their respective degrees of freedom. It is non-negative, positively skewed, and depends on two parameters $(df_1, df_2)$.

$$F = \frac{\chi_1^2 / df_1}{\chi_2^2 / df_2}$$

**Degrees of Freedom in One-Way ANOVA:**
- **Numerator df** (Between Groups): $k - 1$, where $k$ = number of groups/treatments.
- **Denominator df** (Within Groups): $N - k$, where $N$ = total sample size.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for F-distribution + 1.5 for df explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Sub-Parts)

### Question A: Complete One-Way ANOVA Problem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Three different brands of electric bulbs are tested for their lifetime (in hours). The data for 5 bulbs from each brand is given below. Test at 5% significance level whether the mean lifetime of the bulbs differs significantly among the three brands.**

| Brand 1 | Brand 2 | Brand 3 |
|---|---|---|
| 1800 | 2000 | 1900 |
| 1850 | 2050 | 1950 |
| 1900 | 2100 | 1880 |
| 1750 | 1980 | 1920 |
| 1820 | 2020 | 1860 |

### (a) State the hypotheses and compute the F-ratio. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**
$$H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 \quad ; \quad H_1 : \text{At least one mean differs}$$

**Step 2: Group Totals and Grand Total**
$$T_1 = 1800 + 1850 + 1900 + 1750 + 1820 = 9120$$
$$T_2 = 2000 + 2050 + 2100 + 1980 + 2020 = 10150$$
$$T_3 = 1900 + 1950 + 1880 + 1920 + 1860 = 9510$$
$$G = 9120 + 10150 + 9510 = 28780$$

**Step 3: Correction Factor**
$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(28780)^2}{15} = \frac{828288400}{15} = 55219226.67$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sum X_{ij}^2$**
Brand 1: $1800^2 + 1850^2 + 1900^2 + 1750^2 + 1820^2 = 3240000 + 3422500 + 3610000 + 3062500 + 3312400 = 16647400$

Brand 2: $2000^2 + 2050^2 + 2100^2 + 1980^2 + 2020^2 = 4000000 + 4202500 + 4410000 + 3920400 + 4080400 = 20613300$

Brand 3: $1900^2 + 1950^2 + 1880^2 + 1920^2 + 1860^2 = 3610000 + 3802500 + 3534400 + 3686400 + 3459600 = 18092900$

$$\sum X_{ij}^2 = 16647400 + 20613300 + 18092900 = 55353600$$

**Step 5: SST, SSB, SSW**
$$SST = 55353600 - 55219226.67 = 134373.33$$

$$SSB = \frac{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2}{n} - CF = \frac{9120^2 + 10150^2 + 9510^2}{5} - 55219226.67$$

$$SSB = \frac{83174400 + 103022500 + 90440100}{5} - 55219226.67$$

$$SSB = \frac{276637000}{5} - 55219226.67 = 55327400 - 55219226.67 = 108173.33$$

$$SSW = SST - SSB = 134373.33 - 108173.33 = 26200$$

**Step 6: F-ratio**
$$MSB = \frac{108173.33}{3-1} = 54086.67$$

$$MSW = \frac{26200}{15-3} = 2183.33$$

$$F_{calculated} = \frac{54086.67}{2183.33} = 24.77$$

### (b) Construct the ANOVA table and draw conclusions. (7 marks)

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Brands | 108173.33 | 2 | 54086.67 | **24.77** |
| Within Brands (Error) | 26200 | 12 | 2183.33 | — |
| **Total** | **134373.33** | **14** | — | — |

**Critical Value:** $F_{0.05}(2, 12) = 3.89$

**Decision:** Since $F_{calc} = 24.77 > F_{crit} = 3.89$, **Reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At 5% significance level, there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the **mean lifetimes of the three bulb brands differ significantly**. Hence, the brand of bulb has a significant effect on lifetime.

---

### Question B: Alternative Two-Way Context `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Four machines M1, M2, M3, M4 are used to fill bottles with a nominal volume of 500 ml. Four observations taken from each machine's output are given. Using ANOVA, test at 1% significance level whether the machines differ significantly in their mean filling volume.**

| M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 502 | 505 | 498 | 500 |
| 504 | 503 | 499 | 501 |
| 501 | 506 | 497 | 502 |
| 503 | 504 | 500 | 499 |

### (a) Formulate hypotheses and compute all sums of squares. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Hypotheses:**
$$H_0 : \mu_{M1} = \mu_{M2} = \mu_{M3} = \mu_{M4}$$
$$H_1 : \text{At least one machine mean differs}$$

**Group Totals:**
$$T_1 = 502 + 504 + 501 + 503 = 2010$$
$$T_2 = 505 + 503 + 506 + 504 = 2018$$
$$T_3 = 498 + 499 + 497 + 500 = 1994$$
$$T_4 = 500 + 501 + 502 + 499 = 2002$$
$$G = 2010 + 2018 + 1994 + 2002 = 8024$$

**Parameters:** $k = 4$, $n = 4$, $N = 16$

**Correction Factor:**
$$CF = \frac{(8024)^2}{16} = \frac{64384576}{16} = 4024036$$

**Compute $\sum X_{ij}^2$:**
M1: $502^2 + 504^2 + 501^2 + 503^2 = 252004 + 254016 + 251001 + 253009 = 1010030$

M2: $505^2 + 503^2 + 506^2 + 504^2 = 255025 + 253009 + 256036 + 254016 = 1018086$

M3: $498^2 + 499^2 + 497^2 + 500^2 = 248004 + 249001 + 247009 + 250000 = 994014$

M4: $500^2 + 501^2 + 502^2 + 499^2 = 250000 + 251001 + 252004 + 249001 = 1002006$

$$\sum X_{ij}^2 = 1010030 + 1018086 + 994014 + 1002006 = 4024136$$

**Sums of Squares:**
$$SST = 4024136 - 4024036 = 100$$

$$SSB = \frac{2010^2 + 2018^2 + 1994^2 + 2002^2}{4} - 4024036$$

$$SSB = \frac{4040100 + 4072324 + 3976036 + 4008004}{4} - 4024036$$

$$SSB = \frac{16096464}{4} - 4024036 = 4024116 - 4024036 = 80$$

$$SSW = SST - SSB = 100 - 80 = 20$$

### (b) Complete the ANOVA table and state the conclusion. (7 marks)

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Machines | 80 | 3 | 26.67 | **16.00** |
| Within Machines (Error) | 20 | 12 | 1.667 | — |
| **Total** | **100** | **15** | — | — |

**Critical Value:** $F_{0.01}(3, 12) = 5.95$

**Decision:** $F_{calc} = 16.00 > F_{crit} = 5.95$, so **Reject $H_0$** at the 1% significance level.

**Conclusion:** There is highly significant evidence that the **mean filling volumes differ among the four machines**, indicating that the machines are not equally calibrated.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Degrees of Freedom Error ($-2$ marks):** Many students confuse $k-1$ with $k$, or $N-k$ with $N-1$. Remember: *Total df* is always $N-1$, and must equal the sum of *between* and *within* df.
> 2. **Forgetting Correction Factor ($-2$ marks):** The CF must be subtracted. Just summing squared deviations from group means separately will not yield SSB directly without it.
> 3. **Wrong F-table Lookup ($-2$ marks):** The numerator df is ALWAYS the *between* df ($k-1$), and denominator is the *within* df ($N-k$). Reversing this is a fatal mistake.
> 4. **Decision Statement Without Context ($-1$ mark):** Simply writing "Reject $H_0$" without stating "at $\alpha = 0.05$ significance level" loses a mark. Always tie the conclusion back to the problem.
> 5. **Computational Rounding Errors:** Carry at least 4-5 decimal places during intermediate steps to avoid cascading errors in the F-ratio.
> 6. **Missing Post-Hoc Mention:** If $H_0$ is rejected, mention that a **post-hoc test (e.g., Tukey's HSD)** is needed to identify *which* groups differ.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for ANOVA:**

- **Purpose:** Compare means of **3 or more** groups simultaneously.
- **Inventor:** **Sir Ronald A. Fisher** (hence the **F-statistic**).
- **Key Identity:** $SST = SSB + SSW$ (always verifiable).
- **Correction Factor:** $CF = \dfrac{G^2}{N}$ — ALWAYS subtract this.
- **Between-group df:** $k - 1$ → goes in numerator of F-ratio.
- **Within-group df:** $N - k$ → goes in denominator of F-ratio.
- **Test Statistic:** $F = \dfrac{MSB}{MSW}$.
- **Decision:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{critical}$ at chosen $\alpha$.
- **ANOVA Table Columns:** Source → SS → df → MS → F-ratio.
- **Assumptions:** Normality, Independence, Homoscedasticity.
- **Post-Rejection Step:** Use **Tukey HSD**, **Scheffé**, or **Bonferroni** tests for pairwise comparison.
- **Equal Sample Sizes Assumption:** The shortcut formula $SSB = \dfrac{\sum T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$ requires equal $n_i$. For unequal samples, use the weighted formula: $SSB = \sum n_i (\bar{X}_i - \bar{\bar{X}})^2$.
- **Why not multiple t-tests?** Doing $C(k,2)$ t-tests inflates Type-I error to $1 - (1-\alpha)^{C(k,2)}$. ANOVA controls this error rate.
- **Connection to Quality Control:** ANOVA is the foundation of **Design of Experiments (DOE)** — the most powerful tool in modern statistical quality control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal to Noise (S/N) Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signal to Noise (S/N) Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio**, denoted by the Greek letter $\eta$ (eta), is a logarithmic performance metric introduced by **Dr. Genichi Taguchi** within the framework of **Robust Design** and the **Quality Loss Function**. It quantifies the ratio of the desirable variation (the **Signal** — the mean response representing the target) to the undesirable variation (the **Noise** — the variance or scatter caused by uncontrollable factors), expressed in **decibels (dB)**.

Mathematically, in its most general form:

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{Mean Square Signal}}{\text{Mean Square Noise}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The S/N ratio is the cornerstone of Taguchi's offline quality control method. The 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to identify the correct category of S/N ratio and compute it for given experimental data. A *higher* $\eta$ (less negative for "smaller" types, more positive for "larger" types) always indicates a **superior robust design** with minimum sensitivity to noise factors.

The S/N ratio serves two simultaneous purposes in statistical quality control:

1. **Quantification of Performance** — It collapses multi-replicate data into a single comparable scalar.
2. **Identification of Optimal Settings** — When used in conjunction with Orthogonal Arrays (Taguchi DOE), the factor level that maximizes $\eta$ is selected as the robust optimum, even if it does not maximize the raw mean.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Classroom Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a classroom trying to hear your professor's lecture (the **Signal**). Around you, students are chatting, chairs are scraping, fans are humming (the **Noise**). The clarity with which you can absorb the lecture depends on the *ratio* of the professor's voice strength to the surrounding disturbances.

- If the professor speaks softly in a quiet room → Signal is weak, Noise is also weak → the ratio is decent.
- If the professor speaks loudly but the room is chaotic → Signal is strong, but Noise is also strong → the ratio remains poor.

**Taguchi's S/N ratio works exactly the same way.** A high mean alone is insufficient. The process must be *insensitive* to noise. The S/N ratio forces the engineer to optimize *both* simultaneously: push the mean toward the target **and** shrink the variance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Simple Mean Comparison:** Two processes can have identical means but vastly different S/N ratios. Taguchi's philosophy insists that we must always select the design with the **highest $\eta$**, not necessarily the highest mean. This is the fundamental paradigm shift from classical Design of Experiments (DOE) to Taguchi's Robust Design.

### 1.3 The Three Categories of S/N Ratios

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates that students recognize three distinct formulations, each corresponding to a specific nature of the quality characteristic:

| Category | Symbol | Target Value | Engineering Examples |
|----------|--------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Nominal-the-Best** | $NTB$ | Finite specified value | Dimensional tolerance, electrical resistance, pH value |
| **Smaller-the-Better** | $STB$ | Zero (or as small as possible) | Defect rate, leakage, emission levels, wear |
| **Larger-the-Better** | $LTB$ | Infinity (or as large as possible) | Tensile strength, fuel efficiency, bond strength, yield |

The unit of S/N ratio in all three cases is the **decibel (dB)**, which is a logarithmic power ratio scaled by a factor of 10.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Logarithmic Ratio?

The decibel scale was originally used in electrical engineering to express the ratio of two powers. Taguchi adopted this convention for two critical reasons:

1. **Compression of Scale:** Quality data frequently spans several orders of magnitude (e.g., defect rates from $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-6}$). The logarithmic transformation compresses this dynamic range into manageable values.
2. **Additivity of Effects:** In a log scale, the multiplicative effect of multiple factors becomes additive, which aligns perfectly with the additive models used in Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) on the response data.

The factor **10** (rather than 20) is used because $\eta$ represents a *power ratio* (squared quantities), not a voltage ratio. Recall that $P = V^2/R$, so $10 \log_{10}(P_1/P_2) = 20 \log_{10}(V_1/V_2)$.

### 2.2 Category 1 — Nominal-the-Best (NTB) S/N Ratio

This category applies when a **specific target value $T$ exists** and deviations in *either* direction are undesirable. The signal is the squared mean, and the noise is the variance.

The deviation of a measurement $y_i$ from the target $T$ is $\epsilon_i = y_i - T$. The mean of these deviations is $\bar{\epsilon}$ and the variance of the measurements (assumed unbiased estimator of the population variance about the mean) is $\sigma^2$.

**Operational Steps for NTB:**

- **Step 1:** Compute the sample mean $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$
- **Step 2:** Compute the sample variance $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$
- **Step 3:** Substitute into the formula $\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{S^2}\right)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high NTB $\eta$ is achieved when $\bar{y}$ is close to the target $T$ (numerator large) *and* the variance $S^2$ is small (denominator small). Note that $S^2$ is calculated about the *sample mean*, not the target. This is because Taguchi defines the noise as the inherent process scatter, which is estimated from the mean. However, in the signal component, we implicitly assume the process is centered. If centering bias is suspected, a **mean correction factor** must be applied, which is beyond KTU 2024 scope but good to know.

### 2.3 Category 2 — Smaller-the-Better (STB) S/N Ratio

This category applies when the **ideal value is zero** (or a minimum). Examples include impurities, surface roughness, and emissions. Since smaller values are always better, the target is effectively $T = 0$, and there is no concept of "centered mean" — any positive value represents loss.

The total energy of the noise is captured by the mean of the squared observations (the mean square about zero). Because we want to *minimize* this quantity, a negative sign is introduced.

**Operational Steps for STB:**

- **Step 1:** Square each observation: $y_i^2$
- **Step 2:** Compute the Mean Square (MS) about zero: $MS_0 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2$
- **Step 3:** Apply the formula with the negative sign

The negative sign ensures that **as $MS_0$ decreases (better quality), the S/N ratio $\eta$ increases (higher dB value)**. This gives us the same "larger is better" direction for optimization.

### 2.4 Category 3 — Larger-the-Better (LTB) S/N Ratio

This category applies when the **ideal value is infinity** (or a maximum). The reciprocal of the squared observation is taken because, in the limit as $y \to \infty$, $1/y^2 \to 0$, and the logarithm of a small positive number is a large negative number. The negative sign again converts this into a positive decibel.

**Operational Steps for LTB:**

- **Step 1:** Compute the reciprocal of the square of each observation: $\frac{1}{y_i^2}$
- **Step 2:** Compute the mean: $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{y_i^2}$
- **Step 3:** Apply the formula

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table is the *definitive* reference for all S/N ratio calculations in the KTU examination. Memorize it precisely.

| Category | S/N Ratio Formula $\eta$ in dB | Signal Component | Noise Component | Optimization Goal |
|----------|--------------------------------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Nominal-the-Best (NTB)** | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^{2}}\right)$ | $\bar{y}^{\,2}$ (squared mean) | $S^{2}$ (sample variance) | Maximize $\eta$ |
| **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i}^{\,2}\right)$ | $0$ (target) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2$ (MS) | Maximize $\eta$ (less negative) |
| **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{\,2}}\right)$ | $\infty$ (target) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum (1/y_i^2)$ (MS) | Maximize $\eta$ (more positive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU 2024:**
> - All observations $y_i$ in STB and LTB must be **positive** ($y_i > 0$). If zeros appear, a small constant (e.g., $10^{-7}$) must be added, or the data is invalid.
> - The factor **10** is used (not 20) because we are dealing with **power** (squared) ratios.
> - $n$ in the formulas refers to the **number of replicates per trial**, not the total number of experiments.
> - For NTB, $S^2$ uses $(n-1)$ in the denominator; for STB and LTB, $n$ is used in the denominator of the mean square.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The S/N ratio methodology is widely deployed in:

- **Automotive Industry:** Optimizing engine combustion to minimize emissions (STB) while maximizing fuel economy (LTB).
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Tuning soldering parameters to keep resistance at a target value (NTB) with minimum variation.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Minimizing impurity content (STB) in drug formulations.
- **Aerospace:** Maximizing fatigue life (LTB) of structural components.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Holding wafer thickness at exact specifications (NTB) across multiple production lots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General S/N Ratio Structure

The general Taguchi S/N ratio emerges from a **two-step quality loss model**. The observed response $y$ is decomposed as:

$$y = \mu + \epsilon$$

where $\mu$ is the true mean (the signal) and $\epsilon$ is the random error term (the noise) with $E[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}[\epsilon] = \sigma^2$.

The **Mean Square (MS)** total sum can be decomposed into a signal component and a noise component:

$$MS_{Total} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 = \underbrace{\bar{y}^{\,2}}_{\text{Mean Square Signal}} + \underbrace{S^2}_{\text{Mean Square Noise}}$$

This is the fundamental **Sum of Squares decomposition** identity. From this, Taguchi defined the S/N ratio as the power ratio, converted to decibels:

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MS_{Signal}}{MS_{Noise}}\right)$$

For NTB, $MS_{Signal} = \bar{y}^{\,2}$ and $MS_{Noise} = S^2$, giving the standard formula. For STB and LTB, the formulas are modified by introducing a target value of zero or infinity, leading to the negative sign conventions.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Nominal-the-Best (NTB) Calculation

**Problem:** A voltage regulator is designed to output exactly **12.0 V**. Three replicate measurements at a particular setting yield: $y_1 = 11.8$ V, $y_2 = 12.2$ V, $y_3 = 12.0$ V. Compute the S/N ratio.

**Step 1:** Compute the sample mean.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{11.8 + 12.2 + 12.0}{3} = \frac{36.0}{3} = 12.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the deviations from the mean and square them.

$$\text{Deviations: } (11.8 - 12.0), (12.2 - 12.0), (12.0 - 12.0) = -0.2, \; +0.2, \; 0.0$$

$$\text{Squared deviations: } 0.04, \; 0.04, \; 0.00$$

**Step 3:** Compute the sample variance $S^2$.

$$S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \frac{0.04 + 0.04 + 0.00}{3-1} = \frac{0.08}{2} = 0.04 \text{ V}^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the squared mean (signal power).

$$\bar{y}^{\,2} = (12.0)^2 = 144.0 \text{ V}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute the S/N ratio.

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{144.0}{0.04}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3600)$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the logarithm.

$$\log_{10}(3600) = \log_{10}(3.6 \times 10^3) = 3 + \log_{10}(3.6) = 3 + 0.5563 = 3.5563$$

**Step 7:** Final answer.

$$\eta = 10 \times 3.5563 = 35.56 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An S/N ratio of $\mathbf{35.56 \; dB}$ indicates a *very high quality* robust setting. The process is precisely centered on the target and has minimal scatter. In a Taguchi experiment, this setting would likely be identified as the optimal combination for the voltage regulator.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Smaller-the-Better (STB) Calculation

**Problem:** A chemical process is being optimized to minimize impurity content (in ppm). Three replicate measurements are: $y_1 = 5$ ppm, $y_2 = 7$ ppm, $y_3 = 9$ ppm. Compute the S/N ratio.

**Step 1:** Square each observation.

$$y_1^2 = 25, \quad y_2^2 = 49, \quad y_3^2 = 81$$

**Step 2:** Sum the squared observations and compute the mean.

$$\sum y_i^2 = 25 + 49 + 81 = 155$$

$$MS_0 = \frac{155}{3} = 51.667 \text{ ppm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the STB S/N ratio formula with the negative sign.

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}(51.667)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the logarithm.

$$\log_{10}(51.667) = \log_{10}(5.1667 \times 10^1) = 1 + \log_{10}(5.1667) = 1 + 0.7133 = 1.7133$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$\eta = -10 \times 1.7133 = -17.13 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The S/N ratio is $\mathbf{-17.13 \; dB}$ (a negative value is expected for STB). In the optimization, the engineer will seek factor levels that *minimize the absolute magnitude* (i.e., make it as close to zero as possible from the negative side). If another setting yields $\eta = -25.43$ dB, the first setting is superior. The student must compare like-with-like (always STB-to-STB).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Larger-the-Better (LTB) Calculation

**Problem:** A bond strength test (in MPa) yields four replicates: $y_1 = 100$, $y_2 = 120$, $y_3 = 110$, $y_4 = 130$. Compute the S/N ratio.

**Step 1:** Compute the reciprocal of the square of each observation.

$$\frac{1}{y_1^2} = \frac{1}{10000} = 0.000100$$

$$\frac{1}{y_2^2} = \frac{1}{14400} = 0.0000694$$

$$\frac{1}{y_3^2} = \frac{1}{12100} = 0.0000826$$

$$\frac{1}{y_4^2} = \frac{1}{16900} = 0.0000592$$

**Step 2:** Sum and compute the mean.

$$\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2} = 0.000100 + 0.0000694 + 0.0000826 + 0.0000592 = 0.0003112$$

$$MS_{LTB} = \frac{0.0003112}{4} = 0.0000778$$

**Step 3:** Apply the LTB S/N ratio formula.

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}(0.0000778)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the logarithm.

$$\log_{10}(0.0000778) = \log_{10}(7.78 \times 10^{-5}) = -5 + \log_{10}(7.78) = -5 + 0.8910 = -4.1090$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$\eta = -10 \times (-4.1090) = +41.09 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The S/N ratio of $\mathbf{+41.09 \; dB}$ is a large positive number, indicating excellent bond strength. Note how LTB always produces positive dB values (assuming positive observations) because the reciprocal of a large number is very small, and the log of a small number is a large negative number — which the leading negative sign then converts to a large positive dB.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

The following table consolidates the three categories with worked values from the examples above:

| Category | Formula Used | Numerical Inputs | Intermediate MS | Final $\eta$ in dB | Direction of Optimum |
|----------|--------------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| NTB | $10 \log(\bar{y}^2 / S^2)$ | $\bar{y}=12$, $S^2=0.04$ | $144 / 0.04 = 3600$ | $+35.56$ | Maximize |
| STB | $-10 \log(\sum y_i^2 / n)$ | $5, 7, 9$ | $51.667$ | $-17.13$ | Maximize (toward 0) |
| LTB | $-10 \log(\sum 1/y_i^2 / n)$ | $100, 120, 110, 130$ | $7.78 \times 10^{-5}$ | $+41.09$ | Maximize |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification Check:** A higher $\eta$ in dB always corresponds to a *lower* noise-to-signal power ratio. The decibel values are not bounded above (theoretically, $\eta \to \infty$ as the process becomes perfect). The negative dB values for STB simply reflect that the *raw* noise power exceeds the signal power in linear scale — the logarithm of a number greater than 1 is positive, hence the negative dB after applying the leading minus sign.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct S/N Ratio Category

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **decision logic** an engineer follows when classifying a quality characteristic and computing the corresponding S/N ratio. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow that captures the entire cognitive workflow prescribed by Taguchi's methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start Quality Engineering Study]) --> dataNode[Collect Replicate Observations y sub i]
    dataNode --> targetNode{Identify Target Value T}
    
    targetNode -->|T is Zero or Minimize| stbNode[Smaller the Better STB]
    targetNode -->|T is Infinity or Maximize| ltbNode[Larger the Better LTB]
    targetNode -->|T is a Finite Specified Value| ntbNode[Nominal the Best NTB]
    
    stbNode --> stbCompute[Compute MS sub zero equals sum y sub i squared divided by n]
    ltbNode --> ltbCompute[Compute MS sub infinity equals sum of one over y sub i squared divided by n]
    ntbNode --> ntbCompute[Compute y bar squared and Sample Variance S squared]
    
    stbCompute --> stbFormula[Eta equals minus 10 log sub 10 of MS sub zero]
    ltbCompute --> ltbFormula[Eta equals minus 10 log sub 10 of MS sub infinity]
    ntbCompute --> ntbFormula[Eta equals 10 log sub 10 of y bar squared divided by S squared]
    
    stbFormula --> decibelNode[Express Result in Decibels dB]
    ltbFormula --> decibelNode
    ntbFormula --> decibelNode
    
    decibelNode --> compareNode{Compare Across Factor Levels}
    compareNode --> rankNode[Rank Trials by Highest Eta]
    rankNode --> optimalNode([Select Optimum Robust Setting])
    
    style startNode fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style optimalNode fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style targetNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style stbNode fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:1px
    style ltbNode fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px
    style ntbNode fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:1px
    style decibelNode fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Architecture — Signal, Noise, and S/N Ratio

The following diagram maps the *conceptual architecture* of how signal and noise components combine to form the S/N ratio, with the logarithmic compression to decibels:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obsNode[Raw Observations y sub 1 to y sub n] --> sumNode[Sum and Aggregate]
    sumNode --> meanCalc[Compute Mean y bar]
    sumNode --> varCalc[Compute Variance S squared]
    
    meanCalc --> signalPow[Squared Mean equals Signal Power]
    varCalc --> noisePow[Sample Variance equals Noise Power]
    
    signalPow --> ratioForm[Form Power Ratio Signal divided by Noise]
    noisePow --> ratioForm
    
    ratioForm --> logTrans[Apply 10 log sub 10 Transformation]
    logTrans --> dBOut[Output Eta in Decibels dB]
    
    dBOut --> rank1[STB Compare Tendency Toward Zero]
    dBOut --> rank2[LTB Compare Tendency Toward Infinity]
    dBOut --> rank3[NTB Compare Centered and Tight Distribution]
    
    rank1 --> finalDec[Final Ranking Higher Eta Wins]
    rank2 --> finalDec
    rank3 --> finalDec
    
    style obsNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style signalPow fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933
    style noisePow fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style dBOut fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:3px
    style finalDec fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular node represents a computational or decision step. The colored hexagonal-style endpoints (`startNode`, `optimalNode`, `finalDec`) represent the **entry and exit points** of the workflow. The yellow decision diamond `targetNode` is the *critical classification step* — choosing the wrong branch here propagates an incorrect S/N formula throughout the entire analysis, which is the most common source of error in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**
*Define the Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio. List the three categories of S/N ratios used in Taguchi's quality engineering, giving one engineering example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
The S/N ratio is a logarithmic performance metric that expresses the ratio of the mean square signal (target performance) to the mean square noise (uncontrollable variation), measured in decibels. The three categories are:

| Category | Target | Example |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Nominal-the-Best | Finite specified value | Dimensional tolerance of a shaft |
| Smaller-the-Better | Zero | Engine emission levels |
| Larger-the-Better | Infinity | Tensile strength of a weld |

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MS_{Signal}}{MS_{Noise}}\right) \text{ in dB}$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing three categories, 1 mark for examples)*

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**
*Distinguish between the Nominal-the-Best and Smaller-the-Better S/N ratios with appropriate formulas.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Nominal-the-Best (NTB) | Smaller-the-Better (STB) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Target value | Finite specified value $T$ | Zero (minimum possible) |
| Direction of optimization | Center on $T$, minimize scatter | Drive value as low as possible |
| S/N formula | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^2}{S^2}\right)$ | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ |
| Typical $\eta$ value | Positive, often large | Negative, closer to zero is better |
| Example | Resistor value of $100 \, \Omega$ | Wear of a brake pad |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for each category's distinction + formula)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** *A manufacturing process targets a coating thickness of **25 microns**. At a particular factor setting, five replicate measurements are obtained: 24.5, 25.3, 24.8, 25.1, 25.0 microns. Calculate the S/N ratio assuming the quality characteristic is Nominal-the-Best. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the sample mean:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{24.5 + 25.3 + 24.8 + 25.1 + 25.0}{5} = \frac{124.7}{5} = 24.94 \; \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations from the mean and their squares:**

| Observation $y_i$ | Deviation $y_i - \bar{y}$ | Squared Deviation |
|-------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| 24.5 | $-0.44$ | 0.1936 |
| 25.3 | $+0.36$ | 0.1296 |
| 24.8 | $-0.14$ | 0.0196 |
| 25.1 | $+0.16$ | 0.0256 |
| 25.0 | $+0.06$ | 0.0036 |

**Step 3 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5}(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 0.1936 + 0.1296 + 0.0196 + 0.0256 + 0.0036 = 0.3720$$

**Step 4 — Compute sample variance $S^2$:**

$$S^2 = \frac{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n - 1} = \frac{0.3720}{4} = 0.0930$$

**Step 5 — Compute squared mean:**

$$\bar{y}^{\,2} = (24.94)^2 = 622.00$$

**Step 6 — Compute the S/N ratio:**

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{622.00}{0.0930}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(6688.17)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(6688.17) = \log_{10}(6.688 \times 10^3) = 3 + 0.8254 = 3.8254$$

**Step 8 — Final answer:**

$$\eta = 10 \times 3.8254 = \mathbf{38.25 \; dB}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for mean and variance, 2 marks for squared mean, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final dB value with interpretation)*

---

**(b)** *The following observations are obtained for a Smaller-the-Better quality characteristic (injection molding flash, in grams): 1.2, 1.5, 1.0, 1.3, 1.4. Calculate the S/N ratio and interpret the result. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Square each observation:**

$$1.2^2 = 1.44, \quad 1.5^2 = 2.25, \quad 1.0^2 = 1.00, \quad 1.3^2 = 1.69, \quad 1.4^2 = 1.96$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of squares:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} y_i^2 = 1.44 + 2.25 + 1.00 + 1.69 + 1.96 = 8.34$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean square (with $n = 5$):**

$$MS_0 = \frac{8.34}{5} = 1.668 \text{ g}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the STB formula with the negative sign:**

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}(1.668)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(1.668) = 0.2222$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$\eta = -10 \times 0.2222 = \mathbf{-2.22 \; dB}$$

**Interpretation:** The S/N ratio of $-2.22$ dB indicates a relatively low noise level (the injection molding flash is being reasonably well controlled). In a full Taguchi experiment, this trial would be ranked against others — the setting that yields the *least negative* (or most positive) $\eta$ is the optimal robust setting for minimizing flash.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for squaring and summing, 1 mark for mean square, 2 marks for applying the formula, 1 mark for the final dB, 1 mark for interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the concept of the S/N ratio in Taguchi's Quality Loss Function framework. Discuss why the decibel scale is used and what the three categories of S/N ratios represent. **(7 marks)***

**Model Answer (a):**

Taguchi's Quality Loss Function recognizes that any deviation from the target value causes a loss to society, the manufacturer, and the customer. The S/N ratio is a single scalar metric that captures the robustness of a design — its ability to maintain performance in the presence of uncontrollable noise factors.

**The decibel scale is used for three principal reasons:**

1. **Scale compression:** Quality data often spans several orders of magnitude. The logarithmic transformation converts this wide range into a manageable numerical scale.
2. **Additivity:** In a log scale, the multiplicative effect of multiple factors on quality becomes additive, simplifying ANOVA calculations on the response data.
3. **Convention:** The decibel is a universally accepted unit for power ratios, providing standardized communication among engineers worldwide.

**The three categories represent the three fundamental optimization philosophies:**

- **Nominal-the-Best (NTB):** The target is a finite specified value. The goal is to *center* the process on the target and *minimize* the variance. Formula: $\eta = 10 \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2 / S^2)$.

- **Smaller-the-Better (STB):** The target is zero or as small as possible. Any positive deviation represents loss. Formula: $\eta = -10 \log_{10}(\sum y_i^2 / n)$. A less negative (or positive) $\eta$ is superior.

- **Larger-the-Better (LTB):** The target is infinity or as large as possible. Formula: $\eta = -10 \log_{10}(\sum (1/y_i^2) / n)$. A more positive $\eta$ is superior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Taguchi's robust design philosophy, the S/N ratio unifies mean and variance optimization into a single objective. The factor level that maximizes $\eta$ is selected as the optimal robust setting, even if it does not maximize the raw mean — a paradigm shift from classical DOE.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for Quality Loss Function context, 2 marks for justification of decibel scale, 3 marks for the three categories with formulas)*

---

**(b)** *A Larger-the-Better quality characteristic (battery life in hours) yields the following observations: 120, 135, 128, 142, 130. Calculate the S/N ratio and state what the result indicates about the process robustness. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the reciprocal of the square of each observation:**

$$\frac{1}{120^2} = \frac{1}{14400} = 6.9444 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{135^2} = \frac{1}{18225} = 5.4870 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{128^2} = \frac{1}{16384} = 6.1035 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{142^2} = \frac{1}{20164} = 4.9593 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{130^2} = \frac{1}{16900} = 5.9172 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the reciprocals:**

$$\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2} = (6.9444 + 5.4870 + 6.1035 + 4.9593 + 5.9172) \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2} = 29.4114 \times 10^{-5} = 2.9411 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean square (with $n = 5$):**

$$MS_{LTB} = \frac{2.9411 \times 10^{-4}}{5} = 5.8823 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the LTB formula:**

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}(5.8823 \times 10^{-5})$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(5.8823 \times 10^{-5}) = -5 + \log_{10}(5.8823) = -5 + 0.7695 = -4.2305$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$\eta = -10 \times (-4.2305) = \mathbf{+42.31 \; dB}$$

**Interpretation:** The S/N ratio of $+42.31$ dB is a large positive value, indicating that the battery life is consistently high and the process is robust. A higher $\eta$ value would represent an even more desirable (longer-lasting) battery. This trial's setting would be a strong candidate for the optimal robust condition in a full Taguchi experiment.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for reciprocals of squares, 1 mark for sum and mean, 1 mark for applying the formula with negative sign, 1 mark for final dB, 1 mark for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong sign on the formula:** Students frequently forget the negative sign in STB and LTB formulas, leading to incorrectly positive dB values. The negative sign is essential for STB and LTB only — *never* for NTB.
> 2. **Using $n$ vs. $n-1$:** NTB uses $n-1$ in the variance denominator (Bessel's correction); STB and LTB use $n$ in the mean square denominator. Mixing these is a common error.
> 3. **Using 20 instead of 10:** Some students reflexively use $20 \log_{10}$ (the voltage convention). The factor is 10 for S/N ratios because we are dealing with **power** ratios.
> 4. **Misclassifying the category:** Choosing STB instead of NTB (or vice versa) when the problem states a specific target. Always read the problem statement carefully for the *target value*.
> 5. **Forgetting the squared mean for NTB:** Some students use $\bar{y}$ instead of $\bar{y}^{\,2}$ in the numerator. The signal is the *power*, which is the square of the mean.
> 6. **Comparing across categories:** A $\eta = 35$ dB from NTB cannot be directly compared to a $\eta = -2$ dB from STB. Comparisons are only valid within the same category.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for S/N Ratio:**

- **Originator:** Dr. Genichi Taguchi; framework of Robust Design and Quality Loss Function.
- **Unit:** Decibels (dB); conversion factor is **10** (not 20) because the ratio is a *power* ratio.
- **Optimization Direction:** **Always maximize $\eta$** across all three categories. For STB, this means driving $\eta$ toward zero (less negative); for NTB and LTB, this means driving $\eta$ to larger positive values.
- **Nominal-the-Best:** $\eta = 10 \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2 / S^2)$. Use $S^2$ with $(n-1)$ in the denominator. Signal = $\bar{y}^2$. Noise = $S^2$.
- **Smaller-the-Better:** $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$. All $y_i > 0$ required. Mean square computed about **zero**, not the sample mean.
- **Larger-the-Better:** $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$. Always yields positive dB for positive $y_i > 1$. Reciprocals of squares are taken to mimic the "infinity target."
- **Why use S/N ratio?** It collapses replicate data into a single comparable scalar that simultaneously captures *mean accuracy* and *variance control* — a key Taguchi innovation over classical DOE.
- **Connection to Orthogonal Arrays:** S/N ratios are computed at each row of an OA, and factor effects (delta values) are derived from them. The factor with the largest delta is the most influential.
- **ANOVA on S/N ratios:** Pooled ANOVA on $\eta$ values identifies which factors significantly affect robustness — separating signal factors from noise factors.
- **Real-world constraints:** All observations must be positive for STB and LTB. Zeros or negatives require data transformation or a small constant addition.
- **Common engineering mappings:** Dimensions, voltages, pH → NTB; emissions, wear, defects → STB; strength, yield, life → LTB.
- **Comparison validity:** Compare S/N ratios *only within the same category* — never across NTB, STB, and LTB.
- **Higher is universally better:** A higher $\eta$ (in dB) always indicates a more robust design, regardless of category.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crosby's Fourteen Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crosby's Fourteen Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Crosby's Fourteen Steps to Quality Improvement** is a structured, sequential management-led program introduced by **Philip B. Crosby** in his 1979 landmark book *"Quality is Free."* It is a **roadmap for transforming an organization's culture from acceptance of defects (non-conformance) to a state of Zero Defects**, where doing things right the first time becomes the universal performance standard.

The fourteen steps are based on four foundational absolutes of quality management that Crosby insisted every organization must accept before implementation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality**
> 1. **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** (not "goodness" or elegance)
> 2. **Prevention, not Inspection** is the system of quality
> 3. **The Performance Standard must be Zero Defects** (not "acceptable quality levels")
> 4. **The Measure of Quality is the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**, not indices

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Crosby's program is a *top-down, management-driven, cultural change model* — it does not rely on statistical tools (unlike Deming or Juran). It relies on **awareness, commitment, training, and measurement of quality cost.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are the **head coach of a college football team** that has been losing matches because of silly mistakes — players passing to the wrong side, missing penalties, defenders falling asleep.

What do you do? You do **NOT** say *"Let's just inspect the goal after every match and count the goals we missed."* That is too late. Instead, you:

1. **Announce** to the entire team and management that *"we will not tolerate silly mistakes this season"* (Management Commitment).
2. **Form a small committee** of senior players to lead the cultural change (Quality Improvement Team).
3. **Identify** exactly where the mistakes happen most — penalties, defense, etc. (Quality Measurement).
4. **Quantify** the cost — money lost in sponsorships, trophy money, fan morale (Cost of Quality).
5. **Train** every player on the new rules of play (Training).
6. **Reward** players who play mistake-free games, and **listen** to those who report issues with the field or equipment (Recognition & Error Cause Removal).
7. **Repeat the cycle** every season because quality is a journey, not a one-time event (Do It Over Again).

**Crosby's 14 Steps are exactly this — but for products, services, and processes inside a manufacturing or service company.** The "matches" are units produced, and the "silly mistakes" are defects, rework, warranty claims, and customer complaints.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In exams, you will often be asked to *"List Crosby's 14 steps and explain any 5"* or *"Differentiate between Crosby and Deming."* Memorize the order, the philosophy, and the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** formula.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Must-Memorize for KTU Viva & ESE)

| Term | Meaning (KTU expected answer style) |
|---|---|
| **Quality** | Conformance to requirements (Crosby's definition) |
| **Zero Defects (ZD)** | The performance standard — doing it right the first time |
| **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** | Money lost when work is NOT done right the first time |
| **Cost of Quality (COQ)** | The sum of cost of conformance + cost of non-conformance |
| **Prevention Cost** | Money spent to *prevent* defects from occurring |
| **Appraisal Cost** | Money spent to *detect* defects (inspection, testing) |
| **Internal Failure Cost** | Money lost due to defects found *before* shipment (rework, scrap) |
| **External Failure Cost** | Money lost due to defects found *after* shipment (warranty, returns) |
| **PAF Model** | The Prevention–Appraisal–Failure cost categorization |
| **Ad Hoc Committee** | A temporary, special-purpose committee created for Zero Defects planning |

---

## 1.4 Where Crosby's Program Fits in the SQC Universe

Crosby's model is one of the **Three Major Quality Philosophies** KTU expects you to compare:

- **Deming (1950s)** → Statistical, system-based, 14 Points, PDSA cycle.
- **Juran (1950s)** → Planning, Control, Improvement trilogy; customer-focused.
- **Crosby (1970s)** → Behavioral/cultural, **14 Steps**, **Zero Defects**, **COQ/PONC**.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Error:** Do NOT confuse Crosby's 14 Steps with Deming's 14 Points. They are completely different lists, different philosophies, and different decades. The only similarity is the number 14.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophical Foundation: Why Crosby's Model Exists

Crosby argued that the traditional manufacturing mindset was flawed. Factories accepted a certain percentage of defects as "inevitable" and built *inspection* departments to catch them. Crosby asserted that:

1. **Every defect has a cause**, and every cause can be prevented.
2. **It is cheaper to do the job right the first time** than to inspect, rework, scrap, or service warranty claims.
3. **Workers want to do good work** — the problem is the system, not the people. Management must remove the barriers.
4. **Quality is measurable in money** — not in vague notions of "goodness."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Quality is Free" Theorem:** The money saved by preventing defects (lower appraisal, lower failure, lower warranty costs) is always greater than the money spent on prevention training and process improvement. Hence, **quality is free — it is non-conformance that is expensive.**

---

## 2.2 The Fourteen Steps — Detailed Engineering Analysis

The steps are **sequential and cyclical**. Step 14 (*Do It Over Again*) loops back to Step 1, making this a **continuous improvement program**, not a one-off project.

### **Step 1: Management Commitment**
- Top management must **publicly and unconditionally** declare that quality is the company's #1 priority.
- They must write a **Quality Policy Statement** and communicate it to every employee.
- *Why it matters:* Without management's buy-in, no other step can succeed. Workers ignore initiatives that managers do not visibly champion.
- *Engineering link:* Allocation of budget for quality departments, training halls, and recognition programs.

### **Step 2: Quality Improvement Team**
- Form a cross-functional **steering committee** of senior managers (one from each department) to lead the program.
- *Why it matters:* Quality is not a single department's job. Every function (design, production, sales, HR) contributes to or harms quality.

### **Step 3: Quality Measurement**
- Establish **Quality Measurement Units (QMUs)** to identify where problems exist, their type, and frequency.
- *Why it matters:* *"If you can't measure it, you can't manage it."* Every defect category (dimensional, functional, cosmetic) must be tracked.

### **Step 4: Cost of Quality Evaluation**
- Calculate the **total COQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure**.
- Express it as a **percentage of sales** — this is the "Pareto of pain."
- *Why it matters:* Converting quality into a **currency number** forces management attention. A typical firm has COQ of **15–40% of sales**; this is the "gold mine" of savings.

### **Step 5: Quality Awareness**
- Conduct **awareness campaigns** — posters, seminars, slogans, videos — to make every employee understand the COQ and their personal role in reducing it.
- *Why it matters:* Behavioural change begins with cognitive understanding.

### **Step 6: Corrective Action**
- Establish a formal system (often a **Corrective Action Request — CAR**) to investigate root causes of defects using techniques like **Pareto charts, 5-Whys, Ishikawa diagrams**.
- *Why it matters:* Without fixing the *system* that produces the defect, the same defect will reappear.

### **Step 7: Establish an Ad Hoc Committee for Zero Defects**
- Form a *temporary* committee to plan the **Zero Defects program**: launch date, theme, materials, training, recognition criteria.
- *Why it matters:* ZD is a cultural event, not just a slogan. It needs structured planning.

### **Step 8: Training**
- Train **all employees** — from the CEO to the floor sweeper — in quality awareness, their job's requirements, and error-detection skills.
- *Why it matters:* Awareness without skill leads to frustration. Skills without awareness lead to apathy.

### **Step 9: Zero Defects Day**
- Hold a **single, high-profile day** where the ZD program is officially launched. Speeches, banners, slogans, employee pledges.
- *Why it matters:* Creates a *psychological anchor date* in the company's history.

### **Step 10: Goal Setting**
- Encourage **individual employees to set their own personal ZD goals** (e.g., *"I will produce zero defective welds this month"*).
- *Why it matters:* Self-set goals have higher commitment than imposed quotas.

### **Step 11: Error Cause Removal**
- Establish a **formal channel (often suggestion forms)** for employees to report anything that prevents them from doing their job right the first time — broken tools, unclear procedures, missing materials, bad designs.
- *Why it matters:* Frontline workers know the real problems; they need an empowered channel.

### **Step 12: Recognition**
- Publicly **recognize and reward** individuals and teams who meet or exceed their ZD goals. Awards, certificates, bonuses, hall of fame.
- *Why it matters:* Positive reinforcement is the fastest way to embed a new behaviour.

### **Step 13: Quality Councils**
- Establish **permanent quality councils** composed of professionals to keep the program alive, review COQ trends, and coordinate cross-departmental improvements.
- *Why it matters:* Quality improvement must outlive the initial campaign. Councils provide institutional memory.

### **Step 14: Do It Over Again**
- After approximately **18–24 months**, restart the entire 14-step cycle with renewed intensity and a new ZD theme.
- *Why it matters:* Quality is a *journey, not a destination*. Complacency is the enemy.

---

## 2.3 The Cost of Quality (COQ) — PAF Model (KTU Formula Sheet)

Crosby formalized the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model**, where the cost of quality is the total cost of *conformance* plus *non-conformance*.

| # | Cost Category | Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prevention Cost (P)** | Cost of Conformance | Money spent to *avoid* defects | Quality planning, training, supplier capability, design reviews, process capability studies |
| 2 | **Appraisal Cost (A)** | Cost of Conformance | Money spent to *detect* defects | Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final testing, calibration of gauges, QC lab costs |
| 3 | **Internal Failure Cost (IFC)** | Cost of Non-Conformance | Money lost on defects found *before* delivery | Scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime, material review |
| 4 | **External Failure Cost (EFC)** | Cost of Non-Conformance | Money lost on defects found *after* delivery | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, lost customer goodwill, lawsuits |

### **Key Formulas (Cheat Sheet)**

> [!NOTE]
> Use the LaTeX forms `\vert x \vert` if absolute values are needed — never use plain `|x|` inside markdown tables.

$$
\text{Total Cost of Quality (COQ)} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Conformance (COC)} = P + A
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)} = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}
$$

$$
\text{COQ as \% of Sales} = \frac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Total Sales Revenue}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Optimum Quality Level} : \text{The point where } \frac{d(\text{COQ})}{d(\text{Defect Rate})} = 0
$$

### **Crosby's Strategic Insight — The COQ Curve**

As a company invests more in **Prevention (P)**, the **Appraisal (A)**, **Internal Failure (IFC)**, and **External Failure (EFC)** costs drop dramatically. Crosby's data showed that for every **₹1 spent on prevention, a company saves ₹10 to ₹100 in failure costs.** The result is a **net decrease in total COQ** as prevention rises — this is the essence of *"Quality is Free."*

| Defect Rate (DPMO) | Prevention (P) | Appraisal (A) | Internal Failure (IFC) | External Failure (EFC) | **Total COQ** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100,000 | ₹1 Lakh | ₹2 Lakh | ₹5 Lakh | ₹15 Lakh | **₹23 Lakh** |
| 10,000 | ₹3 Lakh | ₹4 Lakh | ₹2 Lakh | ₹4 Lakh | **₹13 Lakh** |
| 1,000 | ₹6 Lakh | ₹5 Lakh | ₹0.5 Lakh | ₹0.5 Lakh | **₹12 Lakh** |
| **Zero Defects** | **₹8 Lakh** | **₹3 Lakh** | **~0** | **~0** | **₹11 Lakh (minimum)** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Crosby's 14 Steps |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Toyota, Ford use ZD philosophy to drive Six-Sigma-level performance in assembly lines. |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | US-FDA / WHO-GMP compliant firms use Steps 1, 4, 6, 8 (commitment, COQ, corrective action, training) heavily. |
| **Software (KTU CS branch)** | Steps 8 & 11 translate to developer training + bug-reporting channels; COQ includes post-release patch costs. |
| **Civil Engineering** | Step 4's COQ includes rework cost of concrete, demolition of defective walls — major portion of project cost. |
| **Aerospace** | Step 1 is non-negotiable; management commitment governs FAA Part 21 / AS9100 compliance. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 KTU-Style Solved Numerical Example on Cost of Quality

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark or 7-mark sub-part** in KTU ESE. Practice it until the calculation is reflex.

### **Problem Statement (Model Question)**
A precision machining company has the following annual quality-related expenditures:

| Category | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Quality planning & training | 2.0 |
| Inspection of incoming materials | 1.5 |
| In-process inspection | 1.0 |
| Final product testing | 1.5 |
| Scrap and rework | 3.0 |
| Warranty and field repairs | 6.0 |
| Customer returns and complaints | 2.0 |
| Calibration of measuring equipment | 1.0 |
| Design reviews and FMEA | 1.0 |

The annual sales revenue is **₹ 100 Lakhs**. Calculate:
1. Total Prevention Cost (P)
2. Total Appraisal Cost (A)
3. Total Internal Failure Cost (IFC)
4. Total External Failure Cost (EFC)
5. Total Cost of Quality (COQ)
6. COQ as % of Sales
7. Comment on the quality health of the company.

---

### **Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution**

#### **Step 1: Classify Each Item into P / A / IFC / EFC**

| Item | Amount (₹ Lakhs) | Category |
|---|---|---|
| Quality planning & training | 2.0 | **Prevention (P)** |
| Inspection of incoming materials | 1.5 | **Appraisal (A)** |
| In-process inspection | 1.0 | **Appraisal (A)** |
| Final product testing | 1.5 | **Appraisal (A)** |
| Scrap and rework | 3.0 | **Internal Failure (IFC)** |
| Warranty and field repairs | 6.0 | **External Failure (EFC)** |
| Customer returns and complaints | 2.0 | **External Failure (EFC)** |
| Calibration of measuring equipment | 1.0 | **Appraisal (A)** |
| Design reviews and FMEA | 1.0 | **Prevention (P)** |

#### **Step 2: Total Prevention Cost (P)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \text{Quality planning \& training} + \text{Design reviews \& FMEA} \\
P &= 2.0 + 1.0 \\
P &= \mathbf{3.0 \; \text{Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 3: Total Appraisal Cost (A)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Incoming inspection} + \text{In-process inspection} + \text{Final testing} + \text{Calibration} \\
A &= 1.5 + 1.0 + 1.5 + 1.0 \\
A &= \mathbf{5.0 \; \text{Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 4: Total Internal Failure Cost (IFC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IFC} &= \text{Scrap and rework} \\
\text{IFC} &= \mathbf{3.0 \; \text{Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 5: Total External Failure Cost (EFC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EFC} &= \text{Warranty \& field repairs} + \text{Customer returns} \\
\text{EFC} &= 6.0 + 2.0 \\
\text{EFC} &= \mathbf{8.0 \; \text{Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 6: Total Cost of Quality (COQ)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} &= P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} \\
\text{COQ} &= 3.0 + 5.0 + 3.0 + 8.0 \\
\text{COQ} &= \mathbf{19.0 \; \text{Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 7: COQ as % of Sales**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ \%} &= \frac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \\
\text{COQ \%} &= \frac{19.0}{100} \times 100 \\
\text{COQ \%} &= \mathbf{19\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 8: Engineering Interpretation (Crosby's Diagnostic Lens)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost of Conformance (COC)} &= P + A = 3.0 + 5.0 = 8.0 \; \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)} &= \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} = 3.0 + 8.0 = 11.0 \; \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Ratio} \; \frac{\text{CONC}}{\text{COC}} &= \frac{11.0}{8.0} = 1.375
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnosis:** The **Cost of Non-Conformance (₹11 L) is 37.5% higher than the Cost of Conformance (₹8 L).** This is a classic *"fire-fighting" quality system* — the company is spending more on fixing failures than on preventing them.
>
> **Crosby's Prescription:** Apply **Steps 1, 6, 7, 8, and 11** — boost management commitment, implement structured corrective action, launch a Zero Defects program, train all employees in prevention, and open a channel for frontline error-cause removal.
>
> **Expected Outcome:** For every additional ₹1 invested in Prevention (P), the firm should see at least a ₹3–₹4 drop in EFC over the next year, driving total COQ down toward the **8–10% of sales** benchmark of a mature quality system.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Crosby vs. Deming vs. Juran (Common KTU 14-Mark Question)

| Dimension | **Crosby** | **Deming** | **Juran** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Era / Book** | 1979, *Quality is Free* | 1982, *Out of the Crisis* | 1951/1988, *Quality Control Handbook* |
| **Definition of Quality** | Conformance to requirements | A predictable degree of uniformity & dependability | Fitness for use |
| **Key Tool** | Cost of Quality (PAF), Zero Defects | 14 Points, PDSA cycle, SPC | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) |
| **Standard** | Zero Defects (absolute) | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) | Fitness for use (relative) |
| **System View** | Prevention, not inspection | System thinking; 94/6 rule (system vs workers) | Project-by-project breakthrough |
| **Focus** | Cultural & behavioural change | Statistical & systemic | Customer-driven & managerial |
| **Customer Emphasis** | Requirements (internal) | Internal & external | Strong — *Fitness for Use* |
| **Measurement of Quality** | Price of Non-Conformance (₹) | Process capability indices (Cp, Cpk) | Number of defects / unit |
| **Training** | Step 8 — formal awareness program | Point 6 — Institute training | Trilogy — Quality Control training |
| **Management's Role** | Step 1 — top-down commitment | Point 1 — Constancy of purpose | Top management responsibility |

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and briefly explain Crosby's four absolutes of quality management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Crosby's four absolutes are non-negotiable pre-conditions for any quality improvement program:
1. **Quality = Conformance to Requirements**, not "goodness" or "elegance." (0.75 mark)
2. **The system of quality is Prevention, not Inspection.** (0.75 mark)
3. **The performance standard must be Zero Defects**, not "Acceptable Quality Level." (0.75 mark)
4. **The measure of quality is the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**, not abstract indices. (0.75 mark)

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)? Why is it important in Crosby's model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**PONC** is the total monetary loss incurred by an organization every time work is *not* done right the first time. It equals the sum of Internal Failure Cost (IFC) and External Failure Cost (EFC). (1.5 marks)
It is important because Crosby argued that *quality is free* — every rupee spent on prevention saves several rupees of PONC. By measuring PONC in **₹ Lakhs/year**, management sees the financial magnitude of poor quality and is compelled to invest in prevention. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B.1 (A): 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** List all 14 steps of Crosby's program and explain the role of **management commitment (Step 1)**, **cost of quality evaluation (Step 4)**, and **training (Step 8)** in detail.

**(b) [7 marks]** A small-scale electronics assembly unit reports the following annual quality data:
- Re-work and re-test: ₹ 4,00,000
- Incoming material inspection: ₹ 1,50,000
- Warranty claims: ₹ 5,00,000
- Quality training and FMEA: ₹ 2,00,000
- Final PCB testing: ₹ 1,00,000
- Scrap of components: ₹ 1,50,000
- Calibration of test equipment: ₹ 50,000

If the annual sales = ₹ 50,00,000, calculate P, A, IFC, EFC, Total COQ, and COQ as % of sales. Comment on the quality status of the firm using Crosby's lens.

---

### **Model Solution to B.1 (A):**

#### **Part (a) — The Fourteen Steps and the Three Highlighted Steps [7 marks]**

**List of 14 Steps (1 mark for the list — write compactly):**

1. Management Commitment
2. Quality Improvement Team
3. Quality Measurement
4. Cost of Quality Evaluation
5. Quality Awareness
6. Corrective Action
7. Ad Hoc Committee for Zero Defects
8. Training
9. Zero Defects Day
10. Goal Setting
11. Error Cause Removal
12. Recognition
13. Quality Councils
14. Do It Over Again

**Step 1 — Management Commitment (2 marks):**
- Top management must **publicly and unconditionally** commit to quality as the #1 priority.
- They draft and disseminate a **Quality Policy Statement**.
- They allocate **budget, time, and visible personal participation**.
- *Why:* Workers ignore what managers do not visibly champion. Without Step 1, all subsequent steps collapse.

**Step 4 — Cost of Quality Evaluation (2 marks):**
- Calculate $\text{COQ} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$.
- Express as % of sales — typical inefficient firms show 15–40 %.
- *Why:* Converting quality into a **currency figure** makes the issue *financially undeniable* to the board. The COQ "gold mine" justifies the prevention budget.

**Step 8 — Training (2 marks):**
- Train *every* employee — from CEO to the floor sweeper — in:
  - Quality awareness and the COQ concept
  - Their job's specific requirements
  - Error detection and reporting
- *Why:* Awareness without skills causes frustration; skills without awareness cause apathy. Training bridges both.

---

#### **Part (b) — COQ Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Classify the data**

| Item | Amount (₹) | Category |
|---|---|---|
| Re-work and re-test | 4,00,000 | **IFC** |
| Incoming material inspection | 1,50,000 | **A** |
| Warranty claims | 5,00,000 | **EFC** |
| Quality training and FMEA | 2,00,000 | **P** |
| Final PCB testing | 1,00,000 | **A** |
| Scrap of components | 1,50,000 | **IFC** |
| Calibration of test equipment | 50,000 | **A** |

**Step 2: Compute P, A, IFC, EFC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2{,}00{,}000 \\
A &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{IFC} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{EFC} &= 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total COQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}50{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{COQ} &= \mathbf{15{,}50{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: COQ as % of Sales**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ \%} &= \frac{15{,}50{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{31\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Crosby's Diagnostic Comment (1.5 marks)**
- $\text{COC} = P + A = 5{,}00{,}000$ and $\text{CONC} = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} = 10{,}50{,}000$.
- The **Cost of Non-Conformance is more than double the Cost of Conformance** — the firm is in *fire-fighting mode*.
- **Prescription:** Implement Crosby's Steps 1, 6, 7, 8, and 11. Doubling the prevention budget (P) should reduce EFC by 50–60 % in one year, dropping COQ to under 20 % of sales.

---

### **Question B.1 (B): 14 Marks — INTERNAL CHOICE** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **Crosby's 14 Steps** and **Deming's 14 Points** with a comparison table. State at least 5 differences.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model** of cost of quality. With a neat sketch, show how increasing prevention cost reduces the total cost of quality.

---

### **Model Solution to B.1 (B):**

#### **Part (a) — Crosby vs. Deming [7 marks]**

| # | Aspect | **Crosby's 14 Steps** | **Deming's 14 Points** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Era | 1979 | 1982 |
| 2 | Philosophy | Behavioural, cultural, motivational | Statistical, systemic |
| 3 | Definition of Quality | Conformance to requirements | Predictable uniformity & dependability |
| 4 | Standard | Zero Defects (absolute) | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) |
| 5 | Measurement | Price of Non-Conformance (₹) | Process capability (Cp, Cpk) |
| 6 | Tools | COQ, ZD, Recognition, Training | SPC, PDSA cycle, FMEA, Control charts |
| 7 | Customer Focus | Internal requirements | Strong external customer focus |
| 8 | Workers' role | Asked to report error causes (Step 11) | System is responsible (94/6 rule) |
| 9 | Management's emphasis | Step 1 — *Public commitment* | Point 1 — *Constancy of purpose* |
| 10 | Inspection | Reject inspection (Step 2 of absolutes) | End inspection by building quality in |

*(7 rows × 1 mark = 7 marks — write at least 5 in the answer sheet for full marks.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — PAF Model and the COQ Curve [7 marks]**

**PAF Model Explanation (3 marks):**
- **Prevention Cost (P):** Investment in avoiding defects — quality planning, training, supplier development, design review, FMEA, process capability studies.
- **Appraisal Cost (A):** Cost of detecting defects — inspection at incoming, in-process, and final stages; calibration; testing labs.
- **Internal Failure Cost (IFC):** Cost of defects found before customer delivery — scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime, material review.
- **External Failure Cost (EFC):** Cost of defects found after delivery — warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill.

$$
\text{Total COQ} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}
$$

**The COQ Curve — A Sketch in Words (4 marks):**
At a **high defect rate**, the firm spends little on P and A but incurs **massive IFC and EFC** — total COQ is high. As P investment rises, the defect rate falls, IFC and EFC drop sharply, and A may even reduce (less inspection needed). The total COQ curve has a **minimum at the "right-most" point — Zero Defects.** Crosby's data: at ZD, P rises but A, IFC, EFC → 0, so total COQ reaches its **global minimum**. This proves *quality is free* — the savings exceed the prevention spend.

> (Draw the standard U-shaped total COQ curve with P rising linearly, A and IFC/EFC falling exponentially, and the minimum at the right side. Mention axis labels: x = Prevention Investment (₹), y = Cost (₹).)
