## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Crosby's 14 Steps**
>
> 1. **Confusing Crosby's 14 Steps with Deming's 14 Points.** They are *different lists* in *different decades*. Always prefix with the guru's name. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Writing "Crosby" as "Crosbey" or "Crosby"** — spell the name correctly. (–0.5 mark, surprisingly)
> 3. **Forgetting to state the Four Absolutes** before listing the 14 Steps — examiners expect the philosophical foundation.
> 4. **In COQ numericals, misclassifying items.** Common mistake: putting *"warranty claims"* under *Prevention* or *"training"* under *Appraisal*. Memorize the PAF definitions cold.
> 5. **Not concluding a COQ numerical** with a Crosby-style comment (*"Cost of Non-Conformance > Cost of Conformance — apply Steps 6, 7, 8, 11"*). Examiners award 1–2 marks for the *interpretation*, not just arithmetic.
> 6. **Writing the 14 steps in random order** — the program is *sequential* (Step 1 must precede Step 2, etc.). You may summarize briefly, but preserve the order.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Crosby's program is a **cultural transformation**, not a statistical project. It relies on management commitment, awareness, training, and **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** as the primary metric.
- ✅ The **Four Absolutes** are: (1) Quality = Conformance, (2) Prevention > Inspection, (3) Zero Defects is the standard, (4) PONC is the measure.
- ✅ The **14 Steps** run sequentially and loop back (Step 14 → Step 1) every 18–24 months. Memorize in order:
  1. Management Commitment → 2. Team → 3. Measurement → 4. COQ → 5. Awareness → 6. Corrective Action → 7. ZD Committee → 8. Training → 9. ZD Day → 10. Goals → 11. Error Cause Removal → 12. Recognition → 13. Quality Councils → 14. Do It Over Again.
- ✅ **PAF Model**: $\text{COQ} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$.
- ✅ **COC** (cost of conformance) $= P + A$; **CONC** (cost of non-conformance) $= \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$.
- ✅ Mature firms have **COQ < 10 % of sales**; inefficient firms have **15–40 %**.
- ✅ Prevention always pays — for every ₹1 in extra P, savings of **₹3 to ₹100** in failure costs are typical (Crosby's empirical finding).
- ✅ **Crosby ≠ Deming ≠ Juran.** Crosby = cultural + COQ; Deming = statistical + 14 Points + PDSA; Juran = customer + Trilogy.
- ✅ **Zero Defects is a standard, not a goal** — that is, the *target* is zero, even if statistical reality allows occasional slips.
- ✅ Step 11 (Error Cause Removal) is *empowerment* of frontline workers — recognize this as a key behavioural element.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers: **always draw the 14-step flow diagram** (Mermaid-style or hand-drawn boxes with arrows) and **always end with a Crosby-style prescription** after any COQ numerical.
- ✅ Two foundational one-liners to memorize verbatim: *"Quality is free."* and *"It is cheaper to do the job right the first time."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TQM Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TQM Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are TQM Models?

**Total Quality Management (TQM) Models** are structured, philosophical, and operational frameworks developed by quality gurus that prescribe a set of principles, practices, and steps an organization must adopt to embed quality into every process, product, and person. They act as *blueprints* that translate the abstract philosophy of "customer satisfaction through continuous improvement" into measurable, implementable actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** A TQM Model is a holistic management philosophy and a systematic framework of guiding principles, strategic tools, and cultural practices that integrates quality into all functional areas and at all hierarchical levels of an organization, with the ultimate goal of achieving long-term customer satisfaction, employee involvement, and continuous improvement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Treatment" Intuition

Imagine a patient (the organization) who is sick with *chronic inefficiency* and *customer complaints*:

- **Deming's 14 Points** = A **complete lifestyle change plan** (diet, sleep, exercise) — focuses on the *system* and the *psychology of management*.
- **Juran's Trilogy** = A **three-stage medical protocol** (Diagnose → Prescribe → Cure) — quality planning, quality control, quality improvement.
- **Crosby's 14 Steps** = A **"Zero Sugar" strict diet** — focuses on *prevention* rather than inspection, and demands *conformance to requirements*.
- **Ishikawa's CWQC** = A **company-wide health camp** — every employee, from the sweeper to the CEO, participates.
- **Feigenbaum's TQC** = A **multi-specialty hospital** — quality is not one doctor's job; it spans engineering, marketing, finance.
- **EFQM / MBNQA** = A **government-issued health accreditation** — self-assessment against world-class standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Quality Metrics Used Across All TQM Models:**
> - **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**
> - **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)**
> - **CTQ (Critical to Quality)**
> - **Six Sigma Level (3.4 DPMO)**

## 1.3 Why TQM Models Matter (KTU Context)

Under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, the course outcome mapped to this topic emphasizes that students must be able to **"identify and apply the principles of various Total Quality Management models to real industrial scenarios."** TQM models are therefore not merely theoretical — they are the *conceptual machinery* that drives ISO 9001, Six Sigma, and Lean implementations globally.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Major TQM Models — Structured Breakdown

### A. Deming's 14 Points for Management (1986)

Deming believed **94% of quality problems** lie with the **system and management**, not the workers.

| # | Point | Core Idea |
|---|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Create constancy of purpose | Long-term vision, not quarterly profit |
| 2 | Adopt the new philosophy | Stop accepting delays and errors |
| 3 | Cease dependence on inspection | Build quality *in* |
| 4 | End "lowest tender" practice | Single supplier, long-term relationship |
| 5 | Improve constantly every process | The PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) cycle |
| 6 | Institute training on the job | Worker is the asset |
| 7 | Institute leadership | Help people do a better job |
| 8 | Drive out fear | Encourage two-way communication |
| 9 | Break down barriers between departments | Teamwork over silo mentality |
| 10 | Eliminate slogans & exhortations | They don't help; they hurt |
| 11 | Eliminate numerical quotas | They kill quality |
| 12 | Remove barriers to pride of workmanship | Respect the worker |
| 13 | Institute a vigorous programme of education | Self-improvement |
| 14 | Put everybody to work to accomplish transformation | Top-down + bottom-up |

### B. Juran's Quality Trilogy (1986)

Juran reduced quality management to **three universal processes**:

1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers, determine needs, develop product features, set goals, plan processes.
2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs. goals, act on differences (the *control loop*).
3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure, identify improvement projects, supply resources, train, control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Project-by-Project Approach:** Improvements are tackled in a defined sequence, not all at once.

### C. Crosby's 14 Steps to Quality Improvement & Zero Defects

Crosby defined quality as **"conformance to requirements"** and advocated the concept of **"Do It Right First Time (DIRFT)"**.

The **4 Absolutes of Quality**:
1. Quality = Conformance to Requirements
2. Prevention, not Inspection
3. Zero Defects Performance Standard
4. Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)

### D. Ishikawa's Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)

- Introduced the famous **"Quality Circles"** (small groups of workers who meet regularly to solve work-related problems).
- Emphasized **participation of all employees**, from top management to shop-floor workers.
- Coined the term **"Company-Wide Quality Control"** — the Japanese counterpart of TQM.

### E. Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control (TQC)

- Quality is an **integrative, organization-wide task** involving marketing, engineering, production, finance, and service.
- Introduced the concept of **"Hidden Plant"** — capacity lost due to poor quality.
- Advocated for **integrated quality systems** (precursor to ISO 9001).

### F. EFQM Excellence Model (European Foundation for Quality Management)

- A **non-prescriptive framework** based on **9 criteria**.
- **5 Enablers** (what an organization does) + **4 Results** (what it achieves).
- Uses the **RADAR** logic: Results, Approach, Deploy, Assess, Refine.

### G. Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA) — USA

- **7 Categories**: Leadership, Strategic Planning, Customer Focus, Measurement/Analysis/Knowledge Management, Workforce Focus, Operations Focus, Results.
- Used by **U.S. organizations** for self-assessment.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Model | Origin / Year | Key Concept | Special Term Coined |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deming's 14 Points | Japan/USA, 1986 | System thinking | PDSA (Deming) Cycle |
| Juran's Trilogy | USA, 1986 | Three managerial processes | Quality Trilogy |
| Crosby | USA, 1979 | Conformance, Prevention | Zero Defects, DIRFT |
| Ishikawa CWQC | Japan, 1985 | All employees' participation | Quality Circles |
| Feigenbaum TQC | USA, 1956 | Integrative, organization-wide | Hidden Plant, TQC |
| EFQM | Europe, 1991 | Self-assessment | RADAR Logic |
| MBNQA | USA, 1987 | Performance excellence | Baldrige Criteria |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Deming Cycle (PDSA) is the foundation of **Kaizen** in Toyota, **DMAIC** in Six Sigma, and the **PDCA** in ISO 50001 energy management. Juran's trilogy is the backbone of **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**. Crosby's PONC metric is widely used in **semiconductor and aerospace industries** to justify Six Sigma investments.

## 2.3 Mathematical Formulation — PONC and COPQ

Although TQM is largely qualitative, Crosby formalized its cost dimension:

$$
\text{PONC} \;=\; \text{Cost of Conformance} \;-\; \text{Cost of Non-Conformance}
$$

The classic **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** model in KTU problems is given as:

$$
\text{COPQ} \;=\; C_{P} \;+\; C_{A} \;+\; C_{F} \;+\; C_{I}
$$

where $C_{P}$ = prevention cost, $C_{A}$ = appraisal cost, $C_{F}$ = internal failure cost, $C_{I}$ = external failure cost.

The **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) is calculated as:

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Defects Found}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Six Sigma DPMO Level

In Six Sigma methodology, the process capability is measured using $Z$-score statistics. A Six Sigma process tolerates a shift of $1.5\sigma$ in the process mean. The derivation steps follow:

**Step 1 — Define the standard normal distribution area:**

The probability of a defect (for one tail) is given by:

$$
P(\text{defect}) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \Phi(Z)
$$

where $\Phi(Z)$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

**Step 2 — For a 3σ process (one tail),**

$$
P(\text{defect}) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \Phi(3) \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; 0.99865 \;\approx\; 0.00135
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to DPMO (per million opportunities):**

$$
\text{DPMO}_{3\sigma} \;=\; 0.00135 \times 10^{6} \;\approx\; 1350 \;\text{DPMO}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for the 1.5σ mean shift (the Six Sigma convention):**

The shifted $Z$-value becomes:

$$
Z_{\text{shifted}} \;=\; Z_{\text{original}} \;-\; 1.5
$$

So for a *nominal* 6σ process, the effective tail area is computed at $Z = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5$:

$$
\text{DPMO}_{6\sigma} \;\approx\; 1 \, - \, \Phi(4.5) \;\times\; 10^{6} \;\approx\; 3.4 \;\text{DPMO}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A Six Sigma process allows only **3.4 defects per million opportunities**, which is why it is the gold standard in TQM-based industries such as semiconductors, healthcare, and aerospace.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right TQM Model

| Industrial Scenario | Recommended TQM Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive mass production | Deming + Juran | Deming's system thinking + Juran's planning/control/ improvement triad |
| Software development | Crosby + MBNQA | DIRFT culture + workforce/operations focus |
| Healthcare | Juran + EFQM | Structured planning + self-assessment against excellence criteria |
| Government / Public Sector | MBNQA + EFQM | Performance excellence + RADAR logic |
| Manufacturing start-up | Ishikawa CWQC | Worker participation and quality circles are low-cost |
| Process industries (Chemicals) | Feigenbaum TQC | Cross-functional integration of engineering, safety, and quality |

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A manufacturing line produces 50,000 units per day. Each unit has 4 quality-critical opportunities. A daily audit finds 12 defects. Calculate the DPMO and identify the corresponding Six Sigma level.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the total opportunities per day:**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} \;=\; 50{,}000 \times 4 \;=\; 200{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the DPMO:**

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \frac{12}{200{,}000} \times 10^{6} \;\approx\; 60 \;\text{DPMO}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate to the standard Six Sigma table:**

From the standard yield table, **60 DPMO corresponds to approximately 4.8σ**. The process is healthy but not yet at Six Sigma level — continuous improvement (Kaizen) projects are recommended.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TQM Models — Conceptual Hierarchy (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TQM Philosophy] --> B1[Deming 14 Points]
    A --> B2[Juran Trilogy]
    A --> B3[Crosby 14 Steps]
    A --> B4[Ishikawa CWQC]
    A --> B5[Feigenbaum TQC]
    A --> B6[Excellence Frameworks]
    B1 --> C1[PDSA Cycle and System Thinking]
    B2 --> C2[Plan Control Improve]
    B3 --> C3[Zero Defects and DIRFT]
    B4 --> C4[Quality Circles and Participation]
    B5 --> C5[Integrated Quality and Hidden Plant]
    B6 --> C6[EFQM and MBNQA]
    C1 --> D1[Kaizen and DMAIC]
    C2 --> D2[QFD and Quality Planning]
    C3 --> D3[PONC and Prevention Culture]
    C4 --> D4[Employee Empowerment]
    C5 --> D5[ISO 9001 and Cross Functional Integration]
    C6 --> D6[Self Assessment and RADAR]
    D1 --> E[Customer Satisfaction]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Organizational Excellence]
```

## 4.2 Juran's Trilogy — Three-Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Customer Identification] --> A2[Determine Customer Needs]
    A2 --> A3[Develop Product Features]
    A3 --> A4[Set Quality Goals]
    A4 --> A5[Plan Quality Processes]
    A5 --> A6[Quality Planning]
    B1[Establish Measurements] --> B2[Compare Actual vs Goals]
    B2 --> B3{Any Difference?}
    B3 -->|Yes| B4[Take Corrective Action]
    B3 -->|No| B5[Maintain Control]
    B4 --> B6[Quality Control]
    B5 --> B6
    C1[Identify Improvement Projects] --> C2[Allocate Resources]
    C2 --> C3[Train Teams]
    C3 --> C4[Implement Solutions]
    C4 --> C5[Quality Improvement]
    A6 --> B6
    B6 --> C5
    C5 --> A6
```

## 4.3 EFQM RADAR Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Results] --> R2[What the Organisation Achieves]
    A1[Approach] --> A2[What the Organisation Plans to Do]
    D1[Deploy] --> D2[How the Approach is Implemented Across the Organisation]
    Ass1[Assess] --> Ass2[How the Organisation Measures Progress and Learns]
    Ref1[Refine] --> Ref2[How the Organisation Improves Based on Learning]
    A1 --> D1
    D1 --> Ass1
    Ass1 --> Ref1
    Ref1 --> R1
    R1 --> Ref1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** All three diagrams use **plain text labels** (no markdown formatting inside Mermaid nodes) and **alphanumeric node IDs** (`A1`, `B2`, `R1`, etc.) — fully compliant with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. List the three processes of Juran's Quality Trilogy and explain any one in brief.**
*(Expected length: 3–4 lines)*

**Model Answer:**
Juran's Quality Trilogy consists of **Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement**.
- *Quality Planning* involves identifying customers and their needs, establishing quality goals, and planning the processes required to meet them.
- *Quality Control* evaluates actual performance against planned goals and initiates corrective actions when deviations occur.
- *Quality Improvement* establishes the infrastructure needed for continuous, project-based improvements and ensures resources and training are available.

---

**Q2. Define the term "Zero Defects" as proposed by Philip Crosby.**
*(Expected length: 3–4 lines)*

**Model Answer:**
"Zero Defects" is Crosby's performance standard stating that **every task must be performed correctly the first time, every time**. It is not a motivational slogan but a **conformance-to-requirement** standard. The philosophy is rooted in the belief that defects are not inevitable — they are caused, and therefore can be prevented. It supports the principle of **Do It Right First Time (DIRFT)** and shifts the focus from inspection-based correction to prevention-based design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — TQM Model Choice 1

**(a) Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management in detail. How do these points contribute to building a quality culture in an organization? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Deming's 14 Points form a comprehensive management philosophy aimed at transforming organizations into quality-driven systems. The points are as follows:

1. **Create constancy of purpose for improvement** — Management must commit to long-term goals of innovation, research, education, and continuous improvement rather than short-term profits.
2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — Accept that the new economic age demands quality at every level; reject the old tolerance for delays, mistakes, and defective workmanship.
3. **Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality** — Inspection is costly and unreliable; quality must be built into the product at the design and process stage.
4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone** — Choose suppliers based on quality, long-term partnership, and statistical evidence of capability.
5. **Improve constantly and forever the system of production and service** — Adopt the PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) cycle for every process.
6. **Institute training on the job** — Workers must be trained in statistical methods, process control, and problem-solving.
7. **Institute leadership** — Supervisors must help people, machines, and processes perform better, not just enforce output quotas.
8. **Drive out fear** — Create an environment where employees can speak up, ask questions, and report problems without retaliation.
9. **Break down barriers between departments** — Encourage cross-functional teamwork; quality is not owned by a single department.
10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** — They do not solve problems; they create frustration when workers lack the system to comply.
11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** — Quotas force people to produce poor quality to meet targets; replace them with statistical and quality measures.
12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship** — Eliminate annual ratings, destructive management styles, and any system that robs workers of their dignity.
13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement** — Every employee should have access to ongoing learning.
14. **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation** — Top management must lead, but every employee must participate.

**Contribution to Quality Culture:** Deming's points shift an organization from a **blame-and-inspect** culture to a **system-and-improvement** culture. They empower employees, establish long-term thinking, embed statistical thinking, and create psychological safety. Industries like **Toyota, Ford, and Honda** adopted these points to achieve world-class manufacturing performance.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast Juran's Quality Trilogy and Crosby's 14 Steps. Which one would you recommend for a pharmaceutical company and why? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Juran's Trilogy | Crosby's 14 Steps |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Joseph M. Juran, USA, 1986 | Philip B. Crosby, USA, 1979 |
| Core Concept | Three managerial processes (Plan, Control, Improve) | Conformance to requirements, Prevention, Zero Defects |
| Focus | Customer-driven quality + Project-based improvement | Defect prevention + Cost of non-conformance |
| Methodology | Sequential managerial triad | 14-step implementation roadmap |
| Quality Definition | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements |
| Key Metric | Customer satisfaction, cost of quality | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) |
| Implementation | Strategic, top-down + project-level | Cultural, organization-wide |

**Contrast:** Juran is *process-oriented* and emphasizes the **trilogy as a managerial function**, while Crosby is *culture-oriented* and provides a **14-step roadmap** (starting with management commitment, ending with recognition) to achieve a quality-aware organization.

**Recommendation for a Pharmaceutical Company:**
**Crosby's 14 Steps** are more suitable because:
- Pharmaceuticals operate in a **highly regulated environment** where *conformance to requirements* (FDA, GMP, WHO standards) is paramount.
- **Zero Defects** philosophy aligns with patient safety, where a single defect can be life-threatening.
- The **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** metric is highly relevant — a single batch rejection can cost millions of rupees.
- The 14-step program provides a **clear, sequential roadmap** for management commitment, training, and cultural change.

Juran's trilogy is still relevant for the *strategic* layer (quality planning for new drug formulations, control of batch quality, and continuous improvement of processes), but Crosby fits the **regulatory and zero-tolerance** nature of pharma better.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — TQM Model Choice 2

**(a) Explain Ishikawa's Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC) and the concept of Quality Circles. Discuss their relevance in a modern IT service company. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Ishikawa's CWQC Philosophy:**
Kaoru Ishikawa, a Japanese quality guru, argued that quality is **not the responsibility of a single department** — it must be embraced by **every employee**, from top management to frontline workers. He termed this **"Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)"**, also known as the *Japanese* version of TQM. He emphasized that approximately **80% of quality problems** can be solved using simple tools such as **Pareto charts, cause-and-effect (Ishikawa/fishbone) diagrams, histograms, scatter diagrams, and control charts** (the famous **7 QC Tools**).

**Quality Circles:**
A **Quality Circle (QC)** is a small group of **6 to 12 employees** from the *same work area* who meet voluntarily on a regular basis (usually weekly) to:
- Identify, analyze, and solve work-related quality problems.
- Generate improvement ideas.
- Implement solutions with management support.

QC members are trained in problem-solving techniques and basic statistics. The success of Quality Circles in post-war Japan (e.g., Toyota, Matsushita) is one of the most cited examples of **employee-driven quality improvement**.

**Relevance in a Modern IT Service Company:**
- IT service desks face **recurring customer issues** (login failures, ticket escalations, SLA breaches) that are well-suited for Quality Circle analysis.
- **Fishbone diagrams** can be used to perform root-cause analysis on recurring incidents.
- IT employees are typically educated and motivated, making them **ideal QC participants**.
- CWQC encourages **cross-functional collaboration** between developers, testers, support engineers, and the operations team — breaking the traditional silo mentality in IT companies.
- Modern frameworks like **Agile retrospectives** and **DevOps blameless post-mortems** are essentially a *digital-age adaptation* of Quality Circles.

---

**(b) Describe the EFQM Excellence Model in detail. Explain the RADAR logic and its use in continuous improvement. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The EFQM Excellence Model (1991):**
The European Foundation for Quality Management (EFQM) developed a non-prescriptive framework that organizations can use to assess their journey toward excellence. It is built on the principle that **"Excellent results with respect to Performance, Customers, People, and Society are achieved through Leadership driving Policy and Strategy, People, Partnerships & Resources, and Processes."**

**Nine Criteria of EFQM:**
- **5 Enablers** (means): Leadership, People, Strategy, Partnerships & Resources, Processes.
- **4 Results** (ends): People Results, Customer Results, Society Results, Business Results.

**RADAR Logic:**
RADAR is a dynamic assessment and management tool consisting of four elements:

1. **Results** — Determine the desired results in all enabler and result areas.
2. **Approach** — Plan and develop a sound, integrated set of approaches to deliver the required results both now and in the future.
3. **Deploy** — Implement the approaches effectively and systematically across the organization.
4. **Assess/Refine** — Assess and refine the deployed approaches using monitoring, analysis, learning, and innovation activities to identify, prioritize, plan, and implement improvements.

**Use in Continuous Improvement:**
- RADAR provides a **structured feedback loop** similar to Deming's PDSA.
- It forces the organization to **measure, reflect, and improve** every enabler.
- The model is used by thousands of European companies (e.g., Bosch, Siemens, BMW) and has influenced global standards like the **CAF (Common Assessment Framework)** in the public sector.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not list all 14 Deming points in a single line.** Each point requires *at least one explanatory sentence*. Marks are awarded for the *narrative*, not just the numbering.
> 2. **Do not confuse Crosby's 14 Steps with Deming's 14 Points.** Examiners frequently set comparison questions — write the *origin, year, and key concept* of each.
> 3. **Avoid the trap of writing only "Deming = PDCA, Juran = Trilogy, Crosby = Zero Defects".** These are one-liners worth 1 mark. You need 7 marks of *expansion*.
> 4. **For numerical problems involving DPMO / Six Sigma,** always show the **formula substitution step-by-step**. Marks are lost when students write only the final answer.
> 5. **EFQM and MBNQA are *excellence awards / models*, not gurus' philosophies.** Do not classify them alongside Deming/Juran/Crosby in conceptual questions.
> 6. **Mention real industrial examples** (Toyota for Deming/Ishikawa, Toyota QCC, Philips for EFQM, etc.) to score higher-order Bloom's taxonomy marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Deming's 14 Points** focus on **system thinking, statistical process control, and psychological safety**. PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) is the operational engine.
- **Juran's Trilogy** = **Quality Planning + Quality Control + Quality Improvement**. It is *project-based* and *managerial*.
- **Crosby** = **Conformance + Prevention + Zero Defects + PONC**. It is *cultural* and *inspection-avoiding*.
- **Ishikawa CWQC + Quality Circles** = **employee participation + 7 QC tools**. Foundational to Japanese Kaizen.
- **Feigenbaum TQC** = **cross-functional integration** + introduced the *Hidden Plant* concept (capacity lost to poor quality).
- **EFQM (5 Enablers + 4 Results) and MBNQA (7 Categories)** are **excellence assessment models** using **RADAR logic**.
- The **DPMO formula** is:

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}
$$

- **Six Sigma level** = 3.4 DPMO (after the 1.5σ shift convention).
- **PONC** = Price of Non-Conformance (Crosby's quantification of poor quality).
- **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- **All TQM models share three common pillars:** **Customer focus, Employee involvement, Continuous improvement (Kaizen).**
- Always **map your answer to a real industry** (auto, pharma, IT, healthcare) to demonstrate application-level understanding — KTU examiners reward *applied* knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training for Quality Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Training for Quality Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training for Quality Management** is a planned, systematic, and continuous process of enhancing the knowledge, skills (technical and behavioral), and attitudes (KSAs) of employees at all organizational levels to enable them to deliver products and services that consistently meet or exceed defined quality standards, customer expectations, and regulatory requirements such as **ISO 9001**, **TQM**, **Six Sigma**, and **Lean** frameworks.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, training for Quality Management (QM) is treated as a **human resource development (HRD) subsystem** that integrates the statistical and analytical tools of Statistical Quality Control (SQC) with the cultural and behavioral foundations of Total Quality Management (TQM). It ensures that statistical tools (control charts, Pareto analysis, cause-and-effect diagrams) are not merely textbook exercises but are operationally embedded in the daily workflow.

### 1.2 Key Components of Training (Quality Perspective)

The KTU syllabus explicitly recognizes three measurable components of training effectiveness:

1. **Knowledge** – Conceptual understanding of QM principles (PDCA cycle, customer focus, process approach).
2. **Skill** – Ability to apply statistical tools (X̄-R charts, p-charts, c-charts, acceptance sampling).
3. **Attitude** – Behavioral alignment toward continuous improvement (Kaizen) and prevention over detection.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Surgical Team**
>
> Imagine a hospital where every surgeon, nurse, and technician is given the **sharpest knives, the most advanced monitors, and the cleanest operating rooms**, but is never trained on how to use them. Patient outcomes would still be catastrophic.
>
> **Quality Management tools (control charts, FMEA, flowcharts) are the surgical instruments. Training is the rigorous residency program that teaches the team when, why, and how to wield them.**
>
> A Six Sigma Black Belt without behavioral training is a technician. A Black Belt with both technical *and* leadership training is a **change agent**. This is precisely what training for QM aims to produce.

### 1.4 Standards & Frameworks Referenced in KTU Module

| Framework | Relevance to Training |
|---|---|
| **ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 7.2 – Competence)** | Mandates documented evidence of training effectiveness |
| **Baldrige Criteria (Category 5 – Workforce)** | Requires measurement of workforce capability |
| **Six Sigma (DMAIC)** | Yellow/Green/Black Belt certification tiers |
| **TQM (Deming's 14 Points – Point 13)** | "Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement" |
| **KSQC** (KTU Module integration) | Statistical literacy + managerial application |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Training Cycle (Systematic Approach)

Training for Quality Management follows a **closed-loop PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle**, mirroring the Deming Wheel used in process improvement. The KTU 2024 syllabus structures training into **five sequential phases**:

#### Phase 1 — Training Needs Analysis (TNA / Assessment)
- **Purpose:** Identify the gap between *current competency* and *required competency* for QM tasks.
- **Three Levels of TNA:**
  - **Organizational Analysis** – Strategic quality goals (e.g., defect reduction from 5% to 1%).
  - **Task Analysis** – Specific QM activities (control chart plotting, root cause analysis).
  - **Person Analysis** – Individual skill gaps of operators, supervisors, engineers.
- **Tools Used:** Job analysis questionnaires, performance appraisals, customer complaint logs, audit reports, statistical process control (SPC) data showing recurring out-of-control conditions.

#### Phase 2 — Training Objective Setting
- Objectives must follow the **SMART** framework:
  - **S**pecific
  - **M**easurable
  - **A**chievable
  - **R**elevant to quality KPIs
  - **T**ime-bound
- Example: *"Reduce average setup time variation by 30% within 90 days of Green Belt training completion."*

#### Phase 3 — Training Design & Curriculum Development
- **Curriculum Architecture:**
  - **Cognitive Layer** – Theory: variability, normal distribution, control limits (UCL, LCL, CL).
  - **Psychomotor Layer** – Hands-on: charting actual production data, calculating Cp, Cpk.
  - **Affective Layer** – Soft skills: teamwork, problem-solving mindset, customer orientation.

#### Phase 4 — Training Implementation & Delivery
- Methods classified broadly as **On-the-Job (OJT)** and **Off-the-Job**.

#### Phase 5 — Training Evaluation (Effectiveness Measurement)
- Uses **Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model** (detailed in §2.3).

### 2.2 Training Methods — Comparative Analysis

| Method | Type | Best Suited For | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Job Rotation** | OJT | Cross-functional QM exposure | High |
| **Coaching / Mentoring** | OJT | Master–apprentice skill transfer | High |
| **Quality Circles** | OJT | Bottom-up problem solving | Very High |
| **Apprenticeships** | OJT | Long-term technical mastery | Medium |
| **Classroom Lectures** | Off-Job | Theoretical foundations (SPC theory) | High |
| **Case Studies** | Off-Job | Real-world QM application | Very High |
| **Role Playing / Simulations** | Off-Job | Behavioral & decision-making skills | Medium |
| **Sensitivity Training (T-Groups)** | Off-Job | Interpersonal dynamics in teams | Low |
| **Six Sigma / Lean Certification** | Off-Job | Belt-tier structured learning | High |
| **e-Learning / Webinars** | Off-Job | Mass awareness training | Medium |

### 2.3 Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Evaluation Model (High-Yield Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirkpatrick's Framework** is the **most frequently tested model** in KTU ESE Module 2 questions on training. Memorize the four levels and the metric used at each.

| Level | Name | What is Measured | Typical Metric | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Reaction | Trainee satisfaction with the program | Post-training survey rating (e.g., 1–5 scale) | Very High |
| **L2** | Learning | Knowledge/skills gained | Test scores, pre/post comparison | Very High |
| **L3** | Behavior | On-the-job application of training | Observation, supervisor rating, % adoption | High |
| **L4** | Results | Business/quality outcomes | Defect rate reduction, cost savings, σ level rise | Very High |

A fifth level (**ROI – Return on Investment**) was later proposed by **Jack Phillips**, but KTU 2024 syllabus still emphasizes the classical Kirkpatrick 4-level model.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-evaluable. Practice each numerical substitution. Note the use of `\vert` (not `\vert`) and `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Training Need Gap (TNG)** | $TNG = C_{required} - C_{current}$ | $C$ = competency score (0–100 scale) |
| 2 | **Training Effectiveness Ratio (TER)** | $TER = \dfrac{L4 \, Gain}{Training \, Cost}$ | Used in ROI of QM programs |
| 3 | **Defect Reduction After Training** | $\Delta D = D_{before} - D_{after}$ | $D$ = defects per million (DPMO) |
| 4 | **Process Capability After Training** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Monitors if Six Sigma training succeeded |
| 5 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ) Change** | $\Delta CoQ = CoQ_{pre} - CoQ_{post}$ | Lower CoQ = effective training |
| 6 | **Six Sigma Level Conversion** | $DPMO = 10^{6} \cdot \dfrac{Defects}{Opportunities}$ | After Green/Black Belt training |
| 7 | **Training ROI (Phillips)** | $ROI(\%) = \dfrac{Net \, Program \, Benefits}{Program \, Costs} \times 100$ | Net = benefits − costs |
| 8 | **Yield Improvement Ratio** | $Y_{ratio} = \dfrac{Yield_{after}}{Yield_{before}}$ | $Yield$ in % |
| 9 | **Employee Coverage Index** | $ECI = \dfrac{N_{trained}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % workforce trained |
| 10 | **Statistical Significance of Training (paired t-test)** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{d}$ = mean improvement, $s_d$ = std dev of differences |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Train operators to read X̄-R control charts and respond to Western Electric rules.
- **Software Quality:** Train testers in Six Sigma DMAIC to reduce software defect density (bugs per KLOC).
- **Healthcare:** Train nurses in statistical root-cause analysis to reduce medication errors (a direct L4 outcome).
- **Automotive (IATF 16949):** Mandatory training records for every employee handling critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Training ROI for a Quality Management Program (Phillips Model)

This derivation is frequently asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions where students must justify training budgets to top management.

**Given:**
- Total training program cost (development + delivery + evaluation) = $C_{total}$
- Tangible benefits from defect reduction, scrap savings, productivity gain = $B_{total}$
- Intangible benefits (e.g., morale, customer goodwill) are estimated at $k \cdot B_{total}$ where $k = 0.10$ to $0.25$

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Benefits.**

$$
B_{adj} = B_{total} + k \cdot B_{total}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Net Benefits.**

$$
B_{net} = B_{adj} - C_{total}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate ROI as a percentage.**

$$
ROI(\%) = \frac{B_{net}}{C_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Convert ROI to a Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR).**

$$
BCR = \frac{B_{adj}}{C_{total}}
$$

> A $BCR > 1$ indicates the training program is financially justified; KTU examiners typically expect students to interpret this ratio in management context.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Statistical Significance of Training (Paired t-Test on Pre/Post Scores)

Used to prove (at L2 of Kirkpatrick's model) that the observed improvement is **not due to chance**.

**Given:**
- $n$ employees tested before ($X_{1i}$) and after ($X_{2i}$) training.
- Differences: $d_i = X_{2i} - X_{1i}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.

**Step 1 — Mean of Differences.**

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation of Differences.**

$$
s_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (d_i - \bar{d})^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Test Statistic.**

$$
t = \frac{\bar{d}}{s_d \, / \, \sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule.**

Compare $t$ against $t_{(n-1, \, \alpha/2)}$ from the t-table. If $\vert t \vert > t_{critical}$, training had a statistically significant impact (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Was training effective?", do not answer yes/no. Always compute the t-statistic, compare with critical value, and **state the conclusion with α level**. This shows CO3 (Apply) and CO4 (Analyze) mastery.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Six Sigma DPMO to σ-Level Conversion (Post-Training Metric)

A common L4 metric after Black Belt training.

**Step 1 — Calculate Yield.**

$$
Yield = 1 - \frac{Defects}{Opportunities}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to DPMO.**

$$
DPMO = (1 - Yield) \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up σ-Level** using the standard Six Sigma yield table.

| σ-Level | Yield (%) | DPMO |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 69.1 | 308,537 |
| 3σ | 93.3 | 66,807 |
| 4σ | 99.38 | 6,210 |
| 5σ | 99.977 | 233 |
| 6σ | 99.99966 | 3.4 |

> A rise from **3σ to 4σ** after training represents a **10.7× improvement** in defect rate — a powerful L4 metric.

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A company trained 50 operators in SPC. Average pre-test score = 55, average post-test score = 78, standard deviation of differences $s_d = 12$. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) whether training was effective.

**Solution:**

$$
\bar{d} = 78 - 55 = 23
$$

$$
t = \frac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{23}{12 / \sqrt{50}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{23}{12 / 7.0711} = \frac{23}{1.6969} \approx 13.55
$$

Degrees of freedom: $n - 1 = 49$. Critical value $t_{49, \, 0.025} \approx 2.01$.

Since $13.55 \gg 2.01$, **reject $H_0$. Training was statistically effective.**

**Note for KTU valuation:** Always state the null hypothesis: $H_0: \mu_d = 0$ (no improvement); $H_1: \mu_d > 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Training-for-Quality Management Cycle

This Mermaid flowchart maps the closed-loop integration between training and the PDCA quality cycle, as specified in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Strategic Quality Goals] --> A1[Phase 1: Training Needs Analysis]
    A1 --> A2[Phase 2: Set SMART Objectives]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Design Curriculum]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Deliver Training]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Evaluate Effectiveness]
    A5 --> A6[Decision: Effective?]
    A6 -->|Yes| A7[Standardize and Document]
    A6 -->|No| A8[Identify Gap and Retrain]
    A7 --> A9[Feed Forward into PDCA Cycle]
    A8 --> A1
    A9 --> A0
```

**Interpretation for KTU Answer Writing:**
- Boxes `A7` and `A9` show that training outcomes **standardize** best practices and feed them back into organizational quality strategy.
- The loop `A8 → A1` represents **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** — a direct TQM principle (Deming Point 13).

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0[Training Program Completed] --> K1[L1 Reaction]
    K1 --> K2[L2 Learning]
    K2 --> K3[L3 Behavior]
    K3 --> K4[L4 Results]
    K4 --> K5[Optional L5 ROI via Phillips]
    K1 -.- M1[Survey: Did trainees like the program]
    K2 -.- M2[Test scores and skill demonstration]
    K3 -.- M3[On-the-job observation and supervisor rating]
    K4 -.- M4[Defect reduction, sigma level, cost of quality]
    K5 -.- M5[Monetary ROI in percent]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Comparison: On-the-Job vs. Off-the-Job Training

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OJT[On the Job Training]
        O1[Job Rotation]
        O2[Coaching and Mentoring]
        O3[Quality Circles]
        O4[Apprenticeships]
        O5[Committee Assignments]
    end
    subgraph OFFJT[Off the Job Training]
        P1[Classroom Lectures]
        P2[Case Studies]
        P3[Role Playing]
        P4[Simulations]
        P5[Sensitivity Training]
        P6[e Learning Modules]
    end
    OJT --> R1[Real Work Environment]
    OFFJT --> R2[Simulated or Academic Environment]
    R1 --> OUT[Trained Workforce]
    R2 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> For Mermaid safety: all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `O1`, `P1`, `R1`), no reserved keywords, no inline bold or italics inside node labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A):** Students often write only 2–3 lines for 3-mark questions and lose marks. KTU expects **definition + example + relevance to QM** in about 80–100 words. Avoid one-line answers.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*"What is meant by Training Needs Analysis (TNA) in the context of Quality Management? List its three levels."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Training Needs Analysis (TNA) is the systematic process of identifying the gap between the **current competency** of employees and the **required competency** to achieve quality objectives. It is the **Phase 1** of the training cycle in QM.

The three levels are:
1. **Organizational Analysis** – aligns training with strategic quality goals.
2. **Task Analysis** – identifies QM-specific tasks (e.g., control chart plotting).
3. **Person Analysis** – pinpoints individual skill gaps.

It ensures that training investments are targeted at *real* quality gaps rather than generic topics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*"Differentiate between On-the-Job Training (OJT) and Off-the-Job Training (OFF-JT). Give two examples of each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | On-the-Job Training | Off-the-Job Training |
|---|---|---|
| **Setting** | Actual workplace | Classroom/simulated environment |
| **Focus** | Skill application | Knowledge and concepts |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Examples** | Quality Circles, Coaching, Job Rotation | Case Studies, Role Playing, Sensitivity Training |

OJT provides **real-time feedback**, while OFF-JT provides **theoretical depth** without interrupting production.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):**
> - For 7-mark sub-parts, KTU expects: **diagram/flowchart (2 marks) + explanation (3 marks) + QM relevance/example (2 marks)**.
> - Students commonly lose marks by skipping diagrams or by not relating theory back to **quality management context**.
> - Always write in **bullet points**, not paragraphs, for better readability and marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Theory + Numerical

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model** of training evaluation. How is each level relevant to measuring the effectiveness of a Quality Management training program? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A manufacturing firm trained 40 workers on Statistical Process Control. The mean pre-test score was 52 and the post-test score was 71. The standard deviation of differences was $s_d = 10$. Using a paired t-test at $\alpha = 0.05$, determine whether the training was statistically effective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model — 7 marks:**

1. **Level 1 — Reaction (2 marks):**
   Measures trainee satisfaction immediately after training. Tool: feedback questionnaire (rating 1–5). In QM, a positive reaction ensures acceptance of statistical tools by workers who may otherwise resist new SPC procedures.

2. **Level 2 — Learning (2 marks):**
   Assesses the knowledge/skills acquired. Tool: pre/post-tests. Example: operators correctly identifying out-of-control conditions on a control chart.

3. **Level 3 — Behavior (1 mark):**
   Evaluates whether learned skills are applied on the shop floor. Tool: supervisor observation, 30/60/90-day audits. Example: operators actually reacting to Western Electric rule violations.

4. **Level 4 — Results (2 marks):**
   Measures business/quality outcomes. Tool: defect rate, scrap %, cost of quality, sigma level rise. Example: defect reduction from 4σ to 4.5σ within six months.

> The four levels form a **causal chain**: positive reaction → learning → behavior change → business results.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

Given: $n = 40$, pre-score = 52, post-score = 71, $s_d = 10$, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1:** Mean difference.
$$
\bar{d} = 71 - 52 = 19
$$

**Step 2:** Test statistic.
$$
t = \frac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{19}{10 / \sqrt{40}} = \frac{19}{10 / 6.3246} = \frac{19}{1.5811} \approx 12.02
$$

**Step 3:** Degrees of freedom $df = n - 1 = 39$. Critical value $t_{39, \, 0.025} \approx 2.02$.

**Step 4:** Compare. Since $12.02 \gg 2.02$, reject $H_0$.

**Conclusion:** The SPC training produced a **statistically significant** improvement at the 5% level. The improvement is not due to chance — training was effective.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Discuss the importance of **Training for Quality Management**. Explain the role of **Quality Circles** as a training tool. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Six Sigma Green Belt project at a firm reduced defects from 18,000 to 2,200 across 1,000,000 opportunities. Calculate the **DPMO before and after**, the **sigma level improvement**, and comment on training effectiveness using **Kirkpatrick's L4 metric**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Importance of Training & Quality Circles — 7 marks:**

**Importance of Training for QM (4 marks):**
1. **Builds Statistical Literacy** – Without training, SPC charts remain unused.
2. **Reduces Human Error** – Trained operators identify assignable causes faster.
3. **Cultural Transformation** – Embeds PDCA and Kaizen mindset (Deming Point 13).
4. **Compliance** – ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.2 mandates documented competence.
5. **Competitive Advantage** – Firms like Toyota and GE attribute success to workforce training (Lean + Six Sigma).

**Quality Circles (3 marks):**
- A **Quality Circle** is a small group of 6–12 workers from the same area who meet voluntarily (typically weekly) to identify, analyze, and solve quality problems in their work area.
- Pioneered in Japan (Kaoru Ishikawa, 1962).
- **Dual benefit**: solves quality problems *and* trains members in problem-solving tools (Pareto, fishbone, histograms).
- Encourages **participative management** and employee ownership of quality.

**(b) DPMO and Sigma-Level Computation — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — DPMO Before:**
$$
DPMO_{before} = \frac{18{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 18{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO After:**
$$
DPMO_{after} = \frac{2{,}200}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 2{,}200
$$

**Step 3 — Sigma Level Lookup (using Six Sigma yield table):**
- $DPMO = 18{,}000 \Rightarrow$ approximately **3.6σ**
- $DPMO = 2{,}200 \Rightarrow$ approximately **4.3σ**

**Step 4 — Improvement:**
$$
\Delta\sigma = 4.3 - 3.6 = 0.7 \sigma
$$

**Step 5 — Kirkpatrick L4 Interpretation:**
- This **0.7σ improvement** is a measurable business outcome.
- It demonstrates that the Green Belt training (L2) translated into **on-the-job behavior (L3)** and **business results (L4)**.
- The training was **effective at the highest Kirkpatrick level**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Training for Quality Management**

- ✅ **Training for QM** = systematic enhancement of Knowledge, Skills, and Attitudes (KSAs) for quality outcomes.
- ✅ **Training Cycle** = TNA → Objectives → Design → Delivery → Evaluation (5 phases, PDCA-aligned).
- ✅ **TNA Levels** = Organizational → Task → Person.
- ✅ **SMART Objectives** = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- ✅ **On-the-Job Methods** = Job Rotation, Coaching, Quality Circles, Apprenticeships.
- ✅ **Off-the-Job Methods** = Lectures, Case Studies, Role Playing, Simulations, Sensitivity Training, e-Learning.
- ✅ **Kirkpatrick's Levels** = L1 Reaction → L2 Learning → L3 Behavior → L4 Results (memorize order).
- ✅ **Phillips L5 ROI Formula** = $\frac{Net \, Benefits}{Program \, Costs} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Quality Circles** = Ishikawa (1962), 6–12 members, voluntary, weekly, shop-floor problems.
- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.2** = mandates documented evidence of competence (training records).
- ✅ **Deming Point 13** = "Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement."
- ✅ **Statistical Test** = Paired t-test: $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}$; compare with $t_{n-1, \alpha/2}$.
- ✅ **Six Sigma Metric** = $DPMO = 10^6 \times \dfrac{Defects}{Opportunities}$; track improvement from L4.
- ✅ **Always link every training concept back to a quality outcome** (defect reduction, cost of quality, sigma rise) — KTU examiners reward this.
- ✅ **Use diagrams and tables** liberally in 14-mark answers to gain easy 2–3 marks.
- ✅ **Cite frameworks** (Deming, Ishikawa, Juran, Crosby, ISO, Six Sigma) to demonstrate breadth (satisfies CO1: Remember).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 9000 and TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ISO 9000 and TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 ISO 9000 — The Family of International Quality Management Standards

### Formal Definition
**ISO 9000** is a series of internationally recognized quality management system (QMS) standards published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland. It prescribes a generic, process-oriented framework that an organization can follow to ensure that its products and services consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements, and to continually improve overall performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The current revision under study in the 2024 Scheme is **ISO 9000:2015** (vocabulary/fundamentals) and **ISO 9001:2015** (requirements). The 2015 revision introduced the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** shared across all ISO management system standards.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Passport of Quality"
Imagine a small restaurant in Kerala that wants to supply frozen foods to supermarkets in the Middle East. The buyer does not personally know the owner, has never seen the kitchen, and trusts neither the chef's promise nor a single inspection. The buyer therefore demands a **third-party, internationally accepted "passport"** that proves the kitchen follows a documented, auditable, and continuously improving process of hygiene, traceability, and quality control. That "passport" is the **ISO 9001 certificate**. The rulebook that defines what the passport means and how it is issued is the **ISO 9000 family**.

### The ISO 9000 Family — Core Documents

| Standard | Scope (What it Does) |
|---|---|
| **ISO 9000:2015** | Defines the **vocabulary, fundamentals, and 7 quality management principles** underlying the standard. |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | Specifies the **requirements** a QMS must meet to be certified. The only standard in the family against which a third-party audit is performed. |
| **ISO 9004:2018** | Provides **guidance** for organizations that wish to move beyond ISO 9001 toward sustained long-term success (beyond conformity). |
| **ISO 19011:2018** | Provides **guidelines for auditing** management systems. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Students frequently confuse ISO 9000 (the family) with ISO 9001 (the certifiable standard). ISO 9000 itself is a *vocabulary* standard — **you do NOT get certified to ISO 9000; you get certified to ISO 9001.**

### 1.2 TQM — Total Quality Management

### Formal Definition
**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a *philosophy* and a *management approach* in which **every member of the organization** is committed to **continuously improving** the **quality of products, services, and processes** by focusing on **customer satisfaction**, **process management**, **data-driven decision making**, and **people involvement**, with the ultimate goal of achieving long-term organizational success and stakeholder benefit.

### Conceptual Analogy — "A Healthy Human Body"
A healthy human body is not healthy because of one organ. The heart, lungs, kidneys, brain, and even the smallest cells work in coordination. If just one organ is unhealthy, the whole body suffers. TQM applies this same logic to an organization: **every department, every employee, every supplier, and every process** must be healthy for the *total* organization to be healthy. TQM is the discipline that keeps all organs of the company working in harmony toward one goal — **customer delight**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction students must remember for the exam:**
> - **ISO 9000/9001** → A *certifiable, documented, auditable* **system**.
> - **TQM** → A *philosophy / culture* that drives the system; it is **broader** and **not certifiable** in the traditional ISO sense.

### 1.3 The Seven Quality Management Principles of ISO 9000:2015

> [!NOTE]
> These 7 principles are the *theological foundation* of ISO 9000/9001 and are a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**. Memorise them with short meaning.

1. **Customer Focus** — Primary focus is meeting customer needs and exceeding expectations.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels.
4. **Process Approach** — Activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** — Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data.
7. **Relationship Management** — Manage relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners, society).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evolution of ISO 9000 (Timeline — High-Yield for KTU)

| Year | Revision | Major Shift |
|---|---|---|
| **1987** | First Issue | Born from BS 5750; based on *Quality Assurance* philosophy. |
| **1994** | First Revision | Still process-based; preventive action emphasised. |
| **2000** | Second Revision | Shifted from "assurance" to **process approach** and **customer satisfaction**. |
| **2008** | Minor Edit | Clarifications only; no new requirements. |
| **2015** | Major Revision | **High-Level Structure (HLS)** with 10 clauses; **risk-based thinking** introduced; explicit **context of organization**; leadership responsibility increased. |

## 2.2 ISO 9001:2015 — High Level Structure (HLS)

The 2015 revision realigned all ISO MSS (Management System Standards) to a common 10-clause structure known as the **HLS / Annex SL**:

- **Clauses 1–3:** Scope, Normative References, Terms & Definitions.
- **Clause 4:** Context of the Organization.
- **Clause 5:** Leadership.
- **Clause 6:** Planning (including risks and opportunities).
- **Clause 7:** Support.
- **Clause 8:** Operation.
- **Clause 9:** Performance Evaluation.
- **Clause 10:** Improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2015 HLS aligns ISO 9001 with **ISO 14001 (Environment)**, **ISO 45001 (Occupational Health & Safety)**, and **ISO 27001 (Information Security)**, making **integrated management systems** possible.

## 2.3 The Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) Cycle — Heart of ISO 9001

PDCA, also called the **Deming Wheel / Deming Cycle**, is the operational backbone of every QMS process.

| Step | Action in ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish objectives, processes, and risks/opportunities (Clause 6). |
| **Do** | Implement what is planned (Clauses 7 & 8 — Support & Operation). |
| **Check** | Monitor, measure, analyse, and evaluate (Clause 9). |
| **Act** | Take actions to improve performance (Clause 10). |

## 2.4 TQM — The Three "Eminent" Frameworks (High-Yield)

### A. Deming's 14 Points for Management
1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product & service.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous programme of education & self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

### B. Juran's Quality Trilogy
- **Quality Planning** — Identify customers, their needs, develop product/process to meet them.
- **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs goals; act on the difference.
- **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure, identify projects, assign teams, manage progress.

### C. Crosby's 14 Steps and "Quality is Free" / "Zero Defects"
Crosby emphasised that **prevention, not inspection**, is cheaper. His famous **COQ (Cost of Quality)** categories are:
- **Prevention Cost (P)** — cost of preventing defects (training, quality planning).
- **Appraisal Cost (A)** — cost of detecting defects (inspection, testing).
- **Internal Failure Cost (IF)** — cost of defects found before delivery (rework, scrap).
- **External Failure Cost (EF)** — cost of defects found after delivery (warranty, returns, loss of goodwill).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** When a question asks "is TQM certifiable?", do NOT say "yes". TQM is a **philosophy / culture**, while **ISO 9001 is the certifiable system** that operationalises the spirit of TQM. Many students write them as synonyms — examiners deduct.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Quality (TCQ)** | $TCQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | P = Prevention, A = Appraisal, IF = Internal Failure, EF = External Failure. |
| 2 | **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** | $CoC = P + A$ | Money spent to *do it right the first time*. |
| 3 | **Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)** | $CoNC = IF + EF$ | Money wasted because of *failures*. |
| 4 | **Quality Ratio (sometimes used)** | $Q_{ratio} = \dfrac{Conformance}{Total\;cost}$ | Higher is better. |
| 5 | **Process Capability Index (preview)** | $C_{pk} = \dfrac{\min(USL-\mu,\;\mu-LSL)}{3\sigma}$ | Cross-links to Module 1/3 — *not* core to this topic, mentioned for context. |
| 6 | **6-σ Yield (preview)** | $Yield = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{LSL-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | TQM uses this 6-σ goal. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing $P + A + IF + EF$ in the exam, **always spell out the full form once** before substituting. Examiners explicitly award the 1 mark for *defining the symbols*. Then put the symbolic expression in a single equation block.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki):** Suppliers *must* be ISO 9001 certified; OEM audits their QMS.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL):** AS9100 (built on ISO 9001 HLS) is mandatory; TQM principles (Juran, Deming) drive continuous improvement.
- **IT/Software (TCS, Infosys):** ISO 9001 + CMMI together operationalise TQM in software factories.
- **Healthcare:** NABH standards in India are *modelled* on ISO 9001's process approach and PDCA.

## 2.7 Documentation Structure of an ISO 9001 QMS (4 Levels)

| Level | Document | Example |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Quality Manual** | Top-level policy document. |
| L2 | **Procedures** | Documented procedures mandated by standard. |
| L3 | **Work Instructions / Forms** | Detailed job-level instructions. |
| L4 | **Records** | Evidence of conformity (audit reports, calibration records). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is largely **conceptual and procedural**, so the "derivations" below are *worked analytical demonstrations* — applying the Cost of Quality (COQ) formulas and the PDCA cycle to industrial situations, with every algebraic step shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cost of Quality (COQ) Calculation

### Problem
A medium-scale electronics assembly unit in Kochi reports the following annual figures (in ₹ lakhs):

- Training the workforce in quality tools: **6**
- Incoming raw material inspection: **4**
- Rework on the assembly line: **5**
- Warranty claims received from customers: **12**
- Calibration of testing equipment: **3**
- Scrap generated in the SMT line: **2**
- Process capability studies: **1**
- Discounts given to a major client for late delivery: **4**

### Step 1 — Classify each cost into P, A, IF, EF

| S. No | Cost Item | Amount (₹ L) | Category | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Training workforce in quality tools | 6 | **P** | Prevents defects. |
| 2 | Incoming raw material inspection | 4 | **A** | Detects defects. |
| 3 | Rework on assembly line | 5 | **IF** | Defect found before dispatch. |
| 4 | Warranty claims | 12 | **EF** | Defect found by customer. |
| 5 | Calibration of testing equipment | 3 | **A** | Detects defects (appraisal). |
| 6 | Scrap in SMT line | 2 | **IF** | Defect before dispatch. |
| 7 | Process capability studies | 1 | **P** | Prevention. |
| 8 | Discounts for late delivery | 4 | **EF** | Failure impact on customer. |

### Step 2 — Sum the categories

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prevention Cost }(P) &= 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{Appraisal Cost }(A) &= 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{Internal Failure Cost }(IF) &= 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{External Failure Cost }(EF) &= 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the TCQ formula

$$
TCQ = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
TCQ = 7 + 7 + 7 + 16
$$

$$
\boxed{TCQ = 37 \text{ lakhs}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Cost of Conformance and Non-Conformance

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoC &= P + A = 7 + 7 = 14 \text{ lakhs} \\
CoNC &= IF + EF = 7 + 16 = 23 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret (TQM interpretation)

$$
\text{Cost of Non-Conformance} \;(23) \; > \; \text{Cost of Conformance} \;(14)
$$

This is the classical TQM/Crosby finding: **the firm is "paying more for failure than for doing it right."** The TQM prescription is to **raise P, lower IF and EF**; mathematically, every ₹1 invested in prevention typically returns **₹4–₹10 in failure cost savings** (Juran's rule).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PDCA Application to a Real Engineering Process

A CNC machining shop notices that **3.2% of aluminium parts are out-of-tolerance** (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality: bore diameter).

| PDCA Step | Action (the firm actually takes) | Clause of ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Define tolerance ±0.02 mm, set OEE goal of 95%, identify risks (tool wear, coolant flow), plan SPC chart for X-bar/R. | Clause 6 — Planning. |
| **Do** | Train operators, install new tool-presetter, run the X-bar chart on 5 samples/subgroup, twice per shift. | Clauses 7 & 8 — Support & Operation. |
| **Check** | Compute Cpk monthly; audit results: out-of-tolerance dropped from 3.2% → 0.9%. | Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation. |
| **Act** | Standardise the new tool-presetter SOP across all 3 shifts; roll out to other 2 lines. Set next-cycle target: 0.3% defect. | Clause 10 — Improvement. |

This is the **PDCA cycle in action**, and it is exactly the pattern ISO 9001:2015 expects of every process in the QMS.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ISO 9001 vs TQM Comparison

| Dimension | ISO 9001:2015 | TQM |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Certifiable **system / standard**. | **Philosophy / culture**. |
| **Origin** | International consensus standard. | Quality gurus (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa). |
| **Scope** | Quality Management System. | Total organisation (all functions, all people). |
| **Certifiable?** | **Yes**, by accredited third-party CB. | **No**. |
| **Focus** | Conformity to requirements + customer satisfaction. | Customer delight + continuous improvement + people. |
| **Documentation** | Mandatory, four-level documentation. | Documentation is *encouraged* but not mandated. |
| **Tools** | PDCA, process approach, risk-based thinking. | PDCA, 7 QC tools, FMEA, QFD, Kaizen, 5-S, Six Sigma, Poka-Yoke. |
| **Customer focus** | Strong. | **Total** — internal + external. |
| **Leadership** | Top management responsibility (Clause 5). | Leadership is a *value*, not a clause. |
| **Improvement** | Continual improvement (Clause 10). | **Continuous** (Kaizen) — never ending. |
| **Relationship** | TQM is operationalised **through** ISO 9001 QMS. | TQM is the *driver*; ISO 9001 is the *vehicle*. |
| **Cost of implementation** | Moderate (audit, consultancy, training). | High (cultural transformation). |
| **Outcome for the firm** | Certified, market acceptance. | Sustained competitive advantage. |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — The Three Quality Gurus at a Glance

| Dimension | W. Edwards Deming | Joseph M. Juran | Philip B. Crosby |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Idea** | System thinking; variation; profound knowledge. | Quality is fitness for use. | Quality is conformance to requirements; prevention. |
| **Key Frame** | 14 Points; PDCA; System of Profound Knowledge. | Quality Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve). | 14 Steps; 4 Absolutes; Zero Defects. |
| **View of Inspection** | Cease dependence on inspection. | Use SPC to control the process. | Prevention, not inspection. |
| **View of Cost** | Cost of poor quality is enormous. | Gold Mine (chronic waste). | Cost of quality is the only meaningful measure. |
| **Famous Saying** | "In God we trust; all others bring data." | "Fitness for use." | "Quality is free." |
