# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part A in KTU 2024 ESE is **2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks**. Answers must be **crisp, definition-style**, with one short application sentence.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 model]**
*Distinguish between Quality Assurance and Quality Control. Relate both to the context of ISO 9001:2015.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Quality Assurance (QA):** A *proactive, system-wide* set of planned activities that ensure processes are capable of producing conforming output. Focuses on **process design, prevention, and confidence**. Implemented at the **QMS level** (e.g., ISO 9001 Clause 4–10).
- **Quality Control (QC):** A *reactive* set of operational techniques and activities used to **inspect, measure, and test** a product or service to verify conformity. Implemented at the **operational level** (e.g., Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation; Clause 8 — Operation).
- **ISO 9001:2015** is fundamentally a **QA** standard (it assures the *system* can deliver quality), but it mandates that the QMS use **QC tools** (SPC, inspection) to evaluate performance — hence QA and QC are *complementary, not competing*, inside an ISO 9001 QMS. (3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 model]**
*List the seven quality management principles of ISO 9000:2015 and explain any two in one line each.*

**Model Answer:**
The **7 QMPs** (1 mark for naming all 7):

1. Customer focus.
2. Leadership.
3. Engagement of people.
4. Process approach.
5. Improvement.
6. Evidence-based decision making.
7. Relationship management.

**Any two explained** (1 mark each):
- **Customer Focus:** The primary focus of QMS is to meet customer needs and to *exceed* customer expectations.
- **Process Approach:** Consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively when activities are understood and managed as *interrelated processes* that function as a coherent system (this is the basis of the PDCA cycle in ISO 9001).
- *Improvement:* Successful organisations have an ongoing focus on continual improvement — Clause 10 of ISO 9001:2015 operationalises this.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part B in KTU 2024 ESE is **Module Internal Choice**: 1 question (with sub-parts) for **14 marks** from the module. To mirror the choice pattern, **two alternative questions** are provided below.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *CO1 / CO2 / Apply level*

**[KTU University Exam — Model as per 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the structure of **ISO 9001:2015** with reference to its **High-Level Structure (HLS)**. Discuss how **risk-based thinking** is integrated. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable engineering example, describe how the **PDCA cycle** is applied to control a manufacturing process. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Question A

**(a) ISO 9001:2015 — High Level Structure and Risk-Based Thinking (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
ISO 9001:2015 is the certifiable requirement standard of the ISO 9000 family. The 2015 revision aligned all ISO Management System Standards (MSS) to a **common 10-clause structure** called the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** or **Annex SL**, enabling easier integration of multiple MSSs (e.g., ISO 9001 + ISO 14001).

**The 10 Clauses (4 marks):**

| Clause | Title | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope | Defines applicability. |
| 2 | Normative References | Lists referenced standards. |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Uses ISO 9000:2015 vocabulary. |
| 4 | Context of the Organization | Identify internal/external issues, needs of interested parties, define QMS scope. |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management leadership & commitment; customer focus; policy; roles. |
| 6 | Planning | Address risks & opportunities; quality objectives; planning of changes. |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning & control; design; production; control of externally provided processes. |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, evaluation; internal audit; management review. |
| 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement. |

**Risk-Based Thinking (2 marks):**
The 2015 revision made **risk-based thinking** a *proactive, organisation-wide* responsibility. Earlier versions treated risk only under "Preventive Action" (which is now removed). Now:
- **Clause 4** requires the organisation to determine risks and opportunities from its context.
- **Clause 6** explicitly requires planning actions to address risks and opportunities and to plan how to integrate and implement these actions into QMS processes.
- **PDCA** is the vehicle — Plan includes risk, Do includes controls, Check includes monitoring, Act includes corrective action.

> **Conclusion (0 marks, optional):** This structure transforms ISO 9001 from a "tick-box" assurance standard into a **strategic, risk-aware, leadership-driven** system.

**(b) PDCA Applied to a CNC Machining Process (7 marks)**

**Example: Controlling the bore diameter (CTQ) of an aluminium housing on a CNC VMC.**

**Plan (2 marks):**
- **Define CTQ:** Bore diameter = 50.00 ± 0.03 mm.
- **Identify risks:** Tool wear, coolant temperature, fixture clamping force.
- **Quality objective:** Achieve Cpk ≥ 1.33 within 3 months; reduce rejection from 3.2% to < 1%.
- **Plan SPC tool:** $\bar{X}$-R chart, subgroup size 5, frequency twice per shift.
- **Plan resources:** New tool-presetter, refresher training for operators.

**Do (2 marks):**
- Install the new tool-presetter; calibrate the bore micrometer.
- Train all 3 shift operators on reading the SPC chart and reaction rules.
- Operate the process and start collecting subgroup data.

**Check (1.5 marks):**
- Plot $\bar{X}$ and R; compute Cpk monthly.
- After 1 month, Cpk = 0.9 (rejection 2.1%); after 3 months, Cpk = 1.36 (rejection 0.8%).
- Conclude: Improvement is real; sustained.

**Act (1.5 marks):**
- **Standardise:** Write a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for tool offset and document it as a Level-3 work instruction.
- **Replicate:** Roll out the same SOP and SPC discipline to two other CNC lines.
- Set the **next PDCA cycle** target: Cpk = 1.50 and rejection < 0.3%.

This is a textbook **PDCA implementation in line with ISO 9001:2015 Clauses 6 → 7,8 → 9 → 10.**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *CO2 / Understand–Apply level*

**(a)** Define **TQM**. Explain the contributions of **Deming, Juran, and Crosby** to the evolution of TQM. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing company reports the following annual expenditure (₹ in thousands): Quality training = 50, Incoming inspection = 30, Rework = 60, Warranty = 100, Equipment calibration = 20, Scrap = 40, Process improvement projects = 30, Customer returns = 20. Calculate **Total Cost of Quality**, **Cost of Conformance**, and **Cost of Non-Conformance**. Comment on what management should do. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Question B

**(a) TQM and the Three Gurus (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Total Quality Management is a *comprehensive, customer-focused, participative, continuous-improvement-driven* management philosophy that seeks to embed quality into every activity, every process, and every person of the organisation, with the goal of long-term success through stakeholder satisfaction.

**W. Edwards Deming (2 marks):**
- **Philosophy:** A system of profound knowledge — appreciation of a system, knowledge of variation, theory of knowledge, and psychology.
- **14 Points** (see Section 2.4) emphasising constancy of purpose, training, leadership, breaking down departmental silos.
- **PDCA Cycle** is Deming's central operational tool.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is Deming's central statistical tool — control the process, not inspect the output.

**Joseph M. Juran (2 marks):**
- **Quality = Fitness for Use** — a customer-oriented definition.
- **Quality Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement. Every manager must perform all three.
- **Chronic vs Sporadic waste:** Most losses are *chronic* (built into the system) and require structured projects to remove (Juran's "Gold Mine").
- Universal *Sequence of Improvement* (also called Juran's Roadmap): Build awareness → Set goals → Organise to reach goals → Provide training → Implement projects → Report progress.

**Philip B. Crosby (1.5 marks):**
- **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** (not elegance).
- **Four Absolutes of Quality:** Quality = conformance, prevention not inspection, zero defects performance standard, measure quality by the price of non-conformance.
- **Quality is Free** — every rupee saved in P&IF is a rupee of profit.
- **14 Steps to Quality Improvement** and **Zero Defects** programme.

**Synthesis (0.5 mark):**
While their vocabularies differ, all three converge on the same operational truths: **prevention, leadership, process discipline, data, and people**.

**(b) Cost of Quality Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Classification (3 marks):**

| Cost Item | ₹ '000 | Category |
|---|---|---|
| Quality training | 50 | P |
| Incoming inspection | 30 | A |
| Rework | 60 | IF |
| Warranty | 100 | EF |
| Equipment calibration | 20 | A |
| Scrap | 40 | IF |
| Process improvement projects | 30 | P |
| Customer returns | 20 | EF |

**Step 2 — Sums (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 50 + 30 = 80 \\
A &= 30 + 20 = 50 \\
IF &= 60 + 40 = 100 \\
EF &= 100 + 20 = 120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — TCQ, CoC, CoNC (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCQ &= P + A + IF + EF = 80 + 50 + 100 + 120 = \mathbf{350\ \text{thousand rupees}} \\
CoC &= P + A = 80 + 50 = \mathbf{130\ \text{thousand rupees}} \\
CoNC &= IF + EF = 100 + 120 = \mathbf{220\ \text{thousand rupees}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Management Comment (0.5 mark):**
The firm is spending **₹220 k (63%)** on failures and only **₹130 k (37%)** on conformance. The firm should **shift budget from IF and EF toward P and A** — a classic TQM recommendation (Crosby/Juran).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks on ISO 9000 / TQM questions:**
> 1. **Writing ISO 9000 = ISO 9001** — Wrong. ISO 9000 is the *vocabulary* standard; ISO 9001 is the *requirements* standard. You are *certified* to ISO 9001, not to ISO 9000. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Saying TQM is certifiable** — Wrong. TQM is a *philosophy/culture*. ISO 9001 is the certifiable *system*. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the 7 QMPs of ISO 9000:2015** — If a question asks "list the principles" and you list only 4–5, you lose a mark. Memorise all 7 in order.
> 4. **Confusing Crosby's "Quality is Free" with "Quality is Cheap"** — Crosby means the *cost of prevention* is lower than the *cost of failure*, not that quality has no cost.
> 5. **In COQ sums, mis-classifying Calibration as P** — Calibration is an **Appraisal (A)** cost (it detects defects), not Prevention. Same with Incoming Inspection. Only *Training, Quality Planning, Process Improvement, FMEA, Design Reviews* are Prevention.
> 6. **In PDCA, skipping the "Act" stage** — Many students write only Plan, Do, Check, and forget the feedback loop back to Plan. Always show the loop.
> 7. **Not mapping the answer to the ISO 9001:2015 clause number** — If a question says "as per ISO 9001:2015, where is this handled?", you lose 1–2 marks by giving only a generic answer without the clause reference (e.g., Clause 6 — Planning, Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation).

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final rapid-revision checklist** for this topic. Master these and you are exam-ready.

- **ISO 9000** = Family of standards; **ISO 9001** = The certifiable requirement standard; **ISO 9000:2015** = vocabulary + 7 QMPs.
- **The 7 QMPs** (in order): Customer focus, Leadership, Engagement of people, Process approach, Improvement, Evidence-based decision making, Relationship management.
- **ISO 9001:2015 has 10 clauses** (HLS / Annex SL). Clauses 4–10 are auditable. Clauses 1–3 are introductory.
- **Risk-based thinking** is a major change in 2015; "preventive action" clause was removed because risk is now proactive and pervasive.
- **PDCA = Deming Wheel** = ISO 9001:2015's operational engine. Plan → Do → Check → Act, with a feedback loop from Act back to Plan.
- **TQM = Philosophy, NOT a standard.** It is operationalised through ISO 9001 QMS, but it goes beyond — people, culture, suppliers, and society.
- **Deming** = System, variation, 14 Points, PDCA, SPC. **Juran** = Fitness for use, Quality Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve). **Crosby** = Conformance, prevention, Zero Defects, Quality is Free.
- **COQ categories** — **P (Prevention)** = training, planning, FMEA, design reviews. **A (Appraisal)** = inspection, testing, calibration. **IF (Internal Failure)** = rework, scrap, re-inspection. **EF (External Failure)** = warranty, returns, loss of goodwill, complaints.
- **Cost formulas** — $TCQ = P + A + IF + EF$, $CoC = P + A$, $CoNC = IF + EF$. TQM goal: **raise P, lower IF + EF**.
- **ISO 9000 series timeline** — 1987 → 1994 → 2000 → 2008 → 2015. The 2015 revision is current and HLS-based.
- **4 Levels of QMS Documentation** — Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- **TQM Tools covered across the topic** — PDCA, 7 QC tools, FMEA, QFD, Kaizen, 5-S, Poka-Yoke, Six Sigma, Benchmarking.
- **Auditing standard** — ISO 19011:2018.
- **Beyond-conformity guidance** — ISO 9004:2018.
- **Exam answer template for Part B** — Definition (1 m) → 7 QMPs / Gurus (3 m) → Application / example (2 m) → Map to ISO 9001:2015 clause (1 m). This structure gives full marks.
- **Always show the PDCA feedback loop** in any diagram — examiners reward it.
- **Final 1-line mnemonic** — "**ISO = System, TQM = Spirit, PDCA = Engine, Risk = 2015's Soul**." Use this to anchor long answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Benchmarking # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Benchmarking (KTU 2024 Standard Definition)** > Benchmarking is the *systematic, continuous, and structured process* of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and performance metrics against those of recognized **best-in-class** organizations (within the same industry or across industries) to identify performance gaps, understand the practices that produce superior results, and implement improvements to close those gaps. The term originates from the surveying practice of establishing a *benchmark* (a standard reference point) on a fixed physical object against which all other measurements are calibrated. In the context of Statistical Quality Control (SQC), benchmarking transforms qualitative "good practice" into **quantifiable, measurable, and statistically analyzable performance targets**. **Key KTU 2024 Nomenclature:** - **Best Practice:** A technique, method, or process that has consistently produced superior results in a given context. - **Performance Gap:** The numerical difference between the organization's current performance and the best-in-class performance. - **Process Maturity:** The degree to which a process is explicitly defined, managed, measured, controlled, and continually improved. - **Comparative Unit (CU):** The organization or internal department used as the reference point. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Explanation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain English Analogy — "The Class Topper Method"** > Imagine three students — *Aman, Balu, and Chandu* — preparing for the same KTU university exam. Aman scores 65/100, Balu scores 78/100, and Chandu scores 92/100. If Aman studies how Chandu prepares (his schedule, his notes, his revision strategy), he is essentially **benchmarking**. He is not copying; he is identifying the *performance gap* (27 marks), analyzing the *root cause* (preparation method), and implementing *best practices* to close that gap. In exactly the same way, an organization uses benchmarking to find its "Chandu" (best-in-class), measure the gap, learn the method, and improve. In SQC terminology, this is equivalent to comparing a process's **Cpk**, **DPMO**, or **control chart parameters** against the industry-leading equivalent, and implementing the superior control methodology. ## 1.3 Why Benchmarking is a Cornerstone of SQC Benchmarking is the *external anchor* that prevents an organization from drifting into a state of *internal complacency* — a common failure mode where continuous improvement efforts are measured only against the organization's own past, allowing absolute mediocrity to be perceived as excellence. In SQC, an organization can have all control charts within statistical limits and still be operating at a **6-sigma quality** level *worse* than its industry peer. Benchmarking introduces an *absolute reference frame* to the relative measures of process control. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Benchmarking (KTU 2024 Taxonomy) The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies benchmarking into **five major categories**, distinguished by the *type of comparator* and the *depth of comparison*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Type 1 — Internal Benchmarking** > Comparison of similar processes, departments, or units *within the same organization*. E.g., comparing the defect rate of Plant A (Kochi) with Plant B (Thrissur) of the same company. Advantage: data is easy to obtain; cultural barriers are minimal. Limitation: limited to the organization's existing capability ceiling — cannot break the *internal* capability barrier. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Type 2 — Competitive (External) Benchmarking** > Direct head-to-head comparison against the **specific competitors** serving the same market. E.g., comparing Maruti's paint shop defects with Hyundai's. This is the *most actionable* form for SQC, as it directly measures competitive standing. Limitation: data is often proprietary and difficult to obtain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Type 3 — Functional Benchmarking** > Comparison of a *specific function* (e.g., procurement, logistics, order fulfillment) against the best-in-class performer of that function, **regardless of industry**. E.g., a hospital benchmarking its patient-admission workflow against an airline's check-in process. Useful for breakthrough innovation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Type 4 — Generic (Process) Benchmarking** > Comparison of *discrete business processes* (e.g., order-to-cash, procure-to-pay) against the absolute best process performers in the world. Highly process-focused; most useful for SQC practitioners because process metrics are directly transferable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Type 5 — Strategic Benchmarking** > Comparison of *long-term strategic decisions* — how companies compete, not how they execute. E.g., benchmarking Toyota's lean philosophy against GE's Six Sigma deployment at the strategic level. ## 2.2 The Five-Phase Benchmarking Model (Robert C. Camp's Model) This is the **gold standard model** tested in KTU examinations. It has five sequential, non-skipping phases: **Phase 1 — Planning** - Identify the *subject* of benchmarking (what to benchmark). - Identify the *comparative* organizations. - Define the *data collection method* and metrics. - Obtain senior management sponsorship. **Phase 2 — Analysis** - Collect internal performance data. - Collect comparative performance data. - Calculate the **performance gap** quantitatively. - Identify the *root causes* of the gap (using fishbone, Pareto, etc., from SQC toolkit). **Phase 3 — Integration** - Communicate findings to stakeholders using control charts and gap charts. - Establish *revised, benchmarked performance targets*. - Gain organizational acceptance of the new goals. **Phase 4 — Action** - Develop specific, time-bound action plans. - Pilot the best practices in a controlled process zone. - Monitor using **SPC tools** (X-bar, R, p-charts) to verify statistical equivalence with the best-in-class. - Roll out organization-wide. **Phase 5 — Maturity** - The organization has *fully implemented* the best practices and may itself become a benchmark for others. - Continuous recalibration of the benchmark itself — *the benchmark is a moving target*. - Embedding benchmarking into the **DMAIC / PDCA cycle**. ## 2.3 The 10-Step Benchmarking Process (Spendolini Method) An alternative expanded model often tested: 1. Identify what to benchmark 2. Identify comparative companies 3. Determine data collection method 4. Determine data collection instrument 5. Collect the data 6. Determine the current performance gap 7. Project future performance levels 8. Communicate findings and gain acceptance 9. Establish functional goals 10. Take action and monitor progress ## 2.4 Code of Conduct for Benchmarking (Ethical Framework) Benchmarking partners exchange sensitive data; therefore the **International Benchmarking Clearinghouse** mandates: - **Principle of Legality:** Comply with antitrust laws. - **Principle of Exchange:** Only give what you are willing to receive. - **Principle of Confidentiality:** Treat all partner data as strictly confidential. - **Principle of Use:** Use information only for the stated benchmarking purpose. - **Principle of First-Contact:** Initiate contact only through senior management. - **Principle of Preparation:** Prepare thoroughly before any benchmarking meeting. - **Principle of Completion:** Complete the study professionally to the end. - **Principle of "Don't Ask":** Never ask a partner for proprietary data that would put them at a legal or competitive disadvantage. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Performance Gap (Absolute) | $G = P_{\text{current}} - P_{\text{best}}$ | Same units as the metric (e.g., %, ppm, DPMO) | | Performance Gap (Relative) | $G_{\%} = \dfrac{P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** | | Benchmarking Ratio (BR) | $BR = \dfrac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}}$ | Dimensionless; ideal value approaches **1.0** | | Improvement Factor (IF) | $IF = \dfrac{P_{\text{best}}}{P_{\text{current}}}$ | Dimensionless; for defect-type metrics (lower is better) | | Process Capability Comparison | $\Delta C_{pk} = C_{pk,\text{ours}} - C_{pk,\text{best}}$ | Dimensionless index | | Defect Rate (DPMO) | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units, $O$ = opportunities | | Process Sigma Level | $\sigma = Z_{\text{score}} + 1.5$ | **1.5 sigma shift** rule (Motorola) | | Yield Benchmark | $Y = e^{-DPMO / 10^{6}}$ | Exponential yield model | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for SQC Students:** When metrics are "smaller is better" (e.g., defects, DPMO, scrap %), the **Performance Gap is the *reduction* required**, not the addition. When metrics are "larger is better" (e.g., Cpk, yield, first-pass yield), the gap is the *increase* required. KTU examiners explicitly test for this directional logic. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Sector:** Benchmarking weld seam strength against Japanese OEMs drove Indian auto component manufacturers from 3-sigma to 4.5-sigma within five years. - **Software Industry:** Google's site reliability engineering (SRE) practices were benchmarked and adapted by Netflix, Amazon, and Microsoft to improve cloud uptime metrics. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Process Analytical Technology (PAT) benchmarks established by FDA drive continuous benchmarking of tablet-coating uniformity in generic drug manufacturing. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Benchmarking Defect Rate in a Production Line > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (KTU 14-Mark Style)** > An Indian automotive component manufacturer *AutoParts Ltd.* produces piston rings. The current production process has the following performance metrics for the month of September 2024: > - Total units produced: **45,000** > - Total defects found: **1,800** > - Defect opportunities per unit: **8** > - Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$): **1.10** > - Process Standard Deviation: **0.0045 mm** > - Specification width (USL – LSL): **0.030 mm** > > The company's R&D division, after participating in an international industry survey, identifies the **global best-in-class benchmark** as follows: > - Industry best DPMO: **233** (corresponds to **5.0-sigma**) > - Industry best $C_{pk}$: **1.67** > - Industry best First-Pass Yield: **99.977%** > > **Required:** > 1. Calculate the *current* DPMO and process sigma level of AutoParts Ltd. > 2. Compute the absolute and relative *performance gap* in DPMO and in $C_{pk}$. > 3. Determine the *benchmarking ratio* and the *improvement factor*. > 4. Comment on the strategic implications for the company. ### Solution (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step) #### Step 1 — Calculate Total Defect Opportunities The total number of defect opportunities available in the production run is the product of the number of units, the number of opportunities per unit, and (in some definitions) the number of characteristics inspected. Here, the problem gives a per-unit opportunity count. $$ \text{Total Opportunities} = U \times O = 45{,}000 \times 8 = 360{,}000 \text{ opportunities} $$ This value is the denominator of the DPMO fraction. Logically, it represents the maximum possible number of places a defect could occur in the entire production run. #### Step 2 — Calculate Current DPMO $$ DPMO_{\text{current}} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{1{,}800 \times 10^{6}}{360{,}000} $$ Performing the arithmetic: $$ DPMO_{\text{current}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{9}}{3.6 \times 10^{5}} = 5{,}000 \text{ DPMO} $$ Therefore, AutoParts Ltd. currently operates at **5,000 defects per million opportunities**. #### Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Process Sigma Level (Current) The relationship between DPMO and sigma level uses the standard normal table. From published Six-Sigma conversion tables, **5,000 DPMO corresponds to a short-term sigma of approximately 4.15σ** (without the 1.5σ shift) or equivalently a long-term sigma of approximately 4.15 − 1.5 = **2.65σ**. $$ \sigma_{\text{current, long-term}} \approx 4.15 - 1.5 = 2.65\sigma $$ The 1.5σ shift is the empirically observed mean-drift between short-term capability and long-term performance (Motorola's classical assumption). #### Step 4 — Absolute Performance Gap in DPMO Since DPMO is a "smaller-is-better" metric, the gap is the *reduction* required. $$ \Delta DPMO = DPMO_{\text{best}} - DPMO_{\text{current}} = 233 - 5{,}000 = -4{,}767 \text{ DPMO} $$ The negative sign indicates that the current state is *worse* than the benchmark by 4,767 DPMO. #### Step 5 — Relative Performance Gap in DPMO (%) $$ G_{\%} = \frac{DPMO_{\text{best}} - DPMO_{\text{current}}}{DPMO_{\text{best}}} \times 100 = \frac{233 - 5{,}000}{233} \times 100 $$ $$ G_{\%} = \frac{-4{,}767}{233} \times 100 \approx -2{,}046.78\% $$ This is a *gap of over 2,000%*. In other words, AutoParts Ltd. has approximately **21.5 times** more defects than the world-class benchmark. #### Step 6 — Performance Gap in $C_{pk}$ (Larger-is-Better Metric) $$ \Delta C_{pk} = C_{pk,\text{best}} - C_{pk,\text{current}} = 1.67 - 1.10 = 0.57 $$ A positive gap of 0.57 indicates AutoParts Ltd. needs to improve its process capability by 0.57 units to match the benchmark. #### Step 7 — Benchmarking Ratio (BR) $$ BR = \frac{C_{pk,\text{current}}}{C_{pk,\text{best}}} = \frac{1.10}{1.67} \approx 0.6587 $$ AutoParts Ltd. is currently operating at approximately **65.87%** of the benchmark's capability. Ideal value is 1.000 (100%). #### Step 8 — Improvement Factor (IF) for DPMO $$ IF = \frac{DPMO_{\text{current}}}{DPMO_{\text{best}}} = \frac{5{,}000}{233} \approx 21.46 $$ The company needs to **reduce its defect rate by a factor of 21.46** to reach the world-class benchmark. #### Step 9 — Strategic Implications > [!IMPORTANT] > The performance gap of ~2,047% in DPMO and 0.57 units in Cpk places AutoParts Ltd. in the **bottom-decile** of the global industry. The benchmarking study reveals a *systemic capability gap*, not a *random fluctuation gap*. The recommended strategic action is to **shift from local problem-solving to a full Six-Sigma DMAIC deployment** targeting 5.0σ within 36 months, with the first milestone being 4.0σ in 12 months. ### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | Current | Best-in-Class | Gap (Absolute) | Gap (Relative) | Direction | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | DPMO | 5,000 | 233 | −4,767 | −2,047% | Reduce | | Process Sigma (long-term) | 2.65σ | 5.00σ | +2.35σ | +88.7% | Increase | | $C_{pk}$ | 1.10 | 1.67 | +0.57 | +51.8% | Increase | | First-Pass Yield | ~99.5% | 99.977% | +0.477% | +0.48% | Increase | | Benchmarking Ratio (BR) | 0.6587 | 1.0000 | — | — | Approaching 1.0 | | Improvement Factor | 21.46× | 1.00× | — | — | Reduce toward 1.0 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Five-Phase Benchmarking Process Flow (Camp's Model) > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below renders the **canonical Robert C. Camp 5-Phase Model** as a linear flow with an embedded feedback loop from Phase 5 back to Phase 1, demonstrating that benchmarking is a *continuous cycle*, not a one-time project. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["PHASE 1: PLANNING"]:::phaseNode A2["PHASE 2: ANALYSIS"]:::phaseNode A3["PHASE 3: INTEGRATION"]:::phaseNode A4["PHASE 4: ACTION"]:::phaseNode A5["PHASE 5: MATURITY"]:::phaseNode B1["Identify subject of benchmarking"]:::subNode B2["Identify comparative organizations"]:::subNode B3["Define metrics and data collection plan"]:::subNode C1["Collect internal performance data"]:::subNode C2["Collect external benchmark data"]:::subNode C3["Compute performance gap using SQC formulas"]:::subNode D1["Present findings to management"]:::subNode D2["Set revised performance targets"]:::subNode D3["Gain organizational acceptance"]:::subNode E1["Develop action plan and pilot"]:::subNode E2["Verify via SPC control charts"]:::subNode E3["Roll out organization wide"]:::subNode F1["Embed best practices as standard"]:::subNode F2["Become a benchmark source for others"]:::subNode F3["Recalibrate benchmark target"]:::subNode A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> A2 A2 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> A3 A3 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> A4 A4 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> A5 A5 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3 F3 -. "Continuous Recalibration" .-> A1 classDef phaseNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold classDef subNode fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Benchmarking Types > [!NOTE] > The block diagram below maps the **five types of benchmarking** to their respective comparator scope and typical SQC use cases. It is the standard answer diagram expected by KTU examiners for a 7-mark "classify and explain" question. ```mermaid flowchart LR ROOT["BENCHMARKING TYPES
KTU 2024 Taxonomy"]:::rootNode T1["INTERNAL
Within organization"]:::typeNode T2["COMPETITIVE
Direct market rivals"]:::typeNode T3["FUNCTIONAL
Same function, any industry"]:::typeNode T4["GENERIC PROCESS
Best process globally"]:::typeNode T5["STRATEGIC
Long term strategy"]:::typeNode C1["Example: Plant A vs Plant B"]:::exNode C2["Example: Maruti vs Hyundai"]:::exNode C3["Example: Hospital admission vs Airline check in"]:::exNode C4["Example: Order to cash process"]:::exNode C5["Example: Toyota Lean vs GE Six Sigma"]:::exNode S1["SQC Use: Process control limits"]:::sqcNode S2["SQC Use: Competitive defect rate"]:::sqcNode S3["SQC Use: Workflow cycle time"]:::sqcNode S4["SQC Use: Capability index Cpk"]:::sqcNode S5["SQC Use: Quality culture maturity"]:::sqcNode ROOT --> T1 ROOT --> T2 ROOT --> T3 ROOT --> T4 ROOT --> T5 T1 --> C1 --> S1 T2 --> C2 --> S2 T3 --> C3 --> S3 T4 --> C4 --> S4 T5 --> C5 --> S5 classDef rootNode fill:#0b2545,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold classDef typeNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef exNode fill:#a6c5e2,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545 classDef sqcNode fill:#fce4a6,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545 ``` ## 4.3 Benchmarking–DMAIC Integration Schematic > [!NOTE] > This block schematic demonstrates how benchmarking provides the **external reference standard** that feeds the *Measure* and *Analyze* phases of the DMAIC cycle. It is the conceptual bridge between benchmarking (strategic) and SPC tools (operational) — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU. ```mermaid flowchart TD EXT["External Benchmark
Best in Class Data"]:::extNode INT["Internal Performance Data"]:::intNode D1["D: DEFINE"]:::dmaicNode M1["M: MEASURE"]:::dmaicNode A1["A: ANALYZE"]:::dmaicNode I1["I: IMPROVE"]:::dmaicNode C1["C: CONTROL"]:::dmaicNode GAP["Performance Gap
Computed in Analysis Phase"]:::gapNode XBAR["X bar and R Charts
Verify Improvement"]:::spcNode CPK["Cpk and DPMO
Monitor Long Term"]:::spcNode EXT --> M1 INT --> M1 M1 --> A1 A1 --> GAP GAP --> I1 I1 --> XBAR XBAR --> C1 C1 --> CPK CPK -. "Continuous Feedback" .-> D1 D1 --> M1 classDef extNode fill:#bf9000,stroke:#7f6000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef intNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef dmaicNode fill:#548235,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef gapNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef spcNode fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3a0a5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > Define benchmarking. List and briefly explain the **five major types** of benchmarking as per the KTU 2024 SQC syllabus. **Model Answer (3 marks — 90 words):** Benchmarking is the systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against best-in-class performers to identify performance gaps and implement improvements. The five major types are: **(1) Internal Benchmarking** — comparison within the same organization; **(2) Competitive Benchmarking** — direct comparison with market rivals; **(3) Functional Benchmarking** — comparison of a specific function across industries; **(4) Generic/Process Benchmarking** — comparison of discrete business processes against world-class performers; **(5) Strategic Benchmarking** — comparison of long-term strategic approaches of leading organizations. > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > State and explain the **Performance Gap** and **Benchmarking Ratio** with suitable formulas. Why is the *direction* of the gap important in SQC? **Model Answer (3 marks — 80 words):** The **Performance Gap** is the absolute difference between an organization's current performance and the best-in-class performance, given by $G = P_{\text{current}} - P_{\text{best}}$. The **Benchmarking Ratio** is the relative measure given by $BR = P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{best}}$, with the ideal value approaching 1.0. The **direction** of the gap is critical because metrics in SQC are of two types: *larger-is-better* (e.g., Cpk, Yield) and *smaller-is-better* (e.g., DPMO, scrap %). Misinterpreting the direction leads to incorrect improvement targets. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` > **(a)** Explain in detail the **Robert C. Camp's Five-Phase Benchmarking Model**, with a clear description of each phase and its key activities. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** A manufacturing company produces 50,000 units per month with 1,250 total defects and 5 defect opportunities per unit. The industry best-in-class operates at 230 DPMO. Calculate the company's current DPMO, the absolute and relative performance gap, the benchmarking ratio, and the improvement factor. Comment on the strategic position of the company. *(7 marks)* ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Camp's Five-Phase Benchmarking Model (7 marks) **Phase 1 — Planning (1.5 marks):** The planning phase is the *foundation* of any benchmarking study. It begins by identifying the *subject* of the benchmarking — for SQC this is typically a process metric such as DPMO, Cpk, or yield. Next, *comparative organizations* are identified (best-in-class performers). The data collection method is finalized, metrics are operationalized, and senior management sponsorship is obtained to legitimize the study. **Phase 2 — Analysis (1.5 marks):** In this phase, internal performance data is collected (typically via SPC control charts, Pareto analysis, and process capability studies) and external benchmark data is collected through site visits, surveys, and industry consortium data. The **performance gap** is computed using $G = P_{\text{current}} - P_{\text{best}}$ and $BR = P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{best}}$. Root-cause analysis is performed using SQC tools (fishbone, 5-Why, regression). **Phase 3 — Integration (1 mark):** Findings are communicated to all stakeholders using gap charts, box plots, and control-chart overlays. New performance targets are established (these become the *benchmarked* targets). Organizational acceptance is obtained through presentations and workshops. **Phase 4 — Action (1.5 marks):** Specific, time-bound action plans are developed, piloted in a controlled process zone, and statistically validated using **X-bar, R, and p-charts** to confirm that the new process is *in statistical control* and matches the benchmarked capability. Successful pilots are then rolled out organization-wide. **Phase 5 — Maturity (1.5 marks):** The organization has *fully absorbed* the best practices and may itself become a benchmark. The benchmark is *recalibrated* periodically (typically annually) because best-in-class performance is a moving target. Benchmarking is embedded into the DMAIC/PDCA cycle to ensure continuous improvement. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** - $U = 50{,}000$ units/month - $D = 1{,}250$ total defects/month - $O = 5$ opportunities per unit - $DPMO_{\text{best}} = 230$ **Step 1 — Total Defect Opportunities:** $$ U \times O = 50{,}000 \times 5 = 250{,}000 \text{ opportunities} $$ **Step 2 — Current DPMO:** $$ DPMO_{\text{current}} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{1{,}250 \times 10^{6}}{250{,}000} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{9}}{2.5 \times 10^{5}} = 5{,}000 \text{ DPMO} $$ **Step 3 — Absolute Performance Gap (DPMO):** $$ \Delta DPMO = DPMO_{\text{best}} - DPMO_{\text{current}} = 230 - 5{,}000 = -4{,}770 \text{ DPMO} $$ **Step 4 — Relative Performance Gap (%):** $$ G_{\%} = \frac{DPMO_{\text{best}} - DPMO_{\text{current}}}{DPMO_{\text{best}}} \times 100 = \frac{230 - 5{,}000}{230} \times 100 = -2{,}073.91\% $$ **Step 5 — Benchmarking Ratio:** $$ BR = \frac{DPMO_{\text{current}}}{DPMO_{\text{best}}} = \frac{5{,}000}{230} \approx 21.74 $$ For a "smaller-is-better" metric, a BR greater than 1.0 indicates underperformance. **Step 6 — Improvement Factor:** $$ IF = \frac{DPMO_{\text{current}}}{DPMO_{\text{best}}} = \frac{5{,}000}{230} \approx 21.74 $$ **Step 7 — Strategic Commentary (1 mark):** The company operates at **~2.65σ (long-term)** versus the world-class **5.0σ**, indicating a *systemic capability gap* of approximately 2.35σ. A reduction in DPMO by a factor of **21.74** is required to reach the world-class benchmark. This is not achievable through routine problem-solving and demands a **full Six-Sigma DMAIC deployment** with a 24–36 month horizon, with the first milestone of reaching 4.0σ within 12 months. --- > **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` > **(a)** Define benchmarking. Explain in detail the **Internal, Competitive, and Functional** types of benchmarking with two real-world examples each. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** The current Cpk of a CNC turning process is 1.20 with a process standard deviation of 0.008 mm and specification width of 0.060 mm. The industry best-in-class operates at Cpk = 1.67 (Six-Sigma level). Calculate: (i) the absolute gap in Cpk, (ii) the relative gap in %, (iii) the benchmarking ratio, and (iv) the corresponding improvement required in standard deviation to reach the benchmarked Cpk (keeping specification width constant). *(7 marks)* ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Definition and Types (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Benchmarking is the systematic, continuous, and structured process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and performance metrics against those of recognized best-in-class organizations to identify performance gaps and implement improvements. **Internal Benchmarking (2 marks):** Comparison of similar processes, departments, or units *within the same organization*. It is the easiest form of benchmarking because data sharing is not restricted by confidentiality or competition. *Examples:* 1. Comparing the *first-pass yield* of Plant A (Chennai) and Plant B (Pune) of the same automotive OEM. 2. Comparing the *mean cycle time* of three different packaging lines within a single FMCG factory. **Competitive Benchmarking (2 marks):** Direct head-to-head comparison against the *specific competitors* serving the same market. It is the *most actionable* form for SQC because it directly measures competitive standing. *Examples:* 1. Comparing the *defect rate per million vehicles* of Tata Motors versus Mahindra in the Indian SUV segment. 2. Comparing the *on-time delivery percentage* of FedEx versus DHL in the Indian express-logistics market. **Functional Benchmarking (2 marks):** Comparison of a *specific function* against the best-in-class performer of that function, **regardless of industry**. It enables breakthrough innovations by importing best practices from unrelated sectors. *Examples:* 1. A hospital benchmarking its *patient-admission workflow* against the *check-in process* of Singapore Airlines. 2. An e-commerce company benchmarking its *order-fulfillment process* against the *warehouse operations* of Amazon, even though Amazon may not be a direct competitor. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** - $C_{pk,\text{current}} = 1.20$ - $\sigma_{\text{current}} = 0.008$ mm - $(USL - LSL) = 0.060$ mm - $C_{pk,\text{best}} = 1.67$ **Step 1 — Verify Consistency of Given Data:** Theoretical $C_{pk} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{0.060}{6 \times 0.008} = \dfrac{0.060}{0.048} = 1.25$ The given $C_{pk} = 1.20$ differs slightly due to off-centering; we use the given value of **1.20** as the *true* current value. **Step 2 — Absolute Gap in Cpk:** $$ \Delta C_{pk} = C_{pk,\text{best}} - C_{pk,\text{current}} = 1.67 - 1.20 = +0.47 $$ **Step 3 — Relative Gap in %:** $$ G_{\%} = \frac{C_{pk,\text{best}} - C_{pk,\text{current}}}{C_{pk,\text{best}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.47}{1.67} \times 100 \approx 28.14\% $$ **Step 4 — Benchmarking Ratio:** $$ BR = \frac{C_{pk,\text{current}}}{C_{pk,\text{best}}} = \frac{1.20}{1.67} \approx 0.7186 $$ The company is at approximately **71.86%** of the world-class capability. **Step 5 — Required Standard Deviation for Benchmark Cpk:** Keeping the specification width constant at 0.060 mm, the required standard deviation to achieve $C_{pk} = 1.67$ is: $$ \sigma_{\text{required}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6 \times C_{pk,\text{best}}} = \frac{0.060}{6 \times 1.67} = \frac{0.060}{10.02} \approx 0.00599 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 6 — Required Reduction in Standard Deviation:** $$ \Delta \sigma = \sigma_{\text{current}} - \sigma_{\text{required}} = 0.008 - 0.00599 = 0.00201 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 7 — % Reduction in Standard Deviation:** $$ \% \Delta \sigma = \frac{0.00201}{0.008} \times 100 \approx 25.13\% $$ The company must reduce its process standard deviation by approximately **25.13%**, which requires tighter process control (e.g., improved CNC precision, better tooling, statistical process control on critical parameters). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Benchmarking Questions:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Error (2–3 marks lost):** Students often write $G = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}$ regardless of metric type. The correct convention is $G = P_{\text{current}} - P_{\text{best}}$ for *all* gaps; the *sign* of the result then indicates direction. Negative sign for "smaller-is-better" means the company is worse; positive sign for "larger-is-better" means the company is worse. > > 2. **Formula Misapplication in Benchmarking Ratio (2 marks lost):** $BR = P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{best}}$ works for *larger-is-better* metrics directly. For *smaller-is-better* metrics, students must write $BR = P_{\text{best}} / P_{\text{current}}$ so that the ideal value of 1.0 is consistent. > > 3. **Confusing 1.5σ Shift (1 mark lost):** When asked for "process sigma level", always clarify whether you are quoting *short-term* (without 1.5σ shift) or *long-term* (with 1.5σ shift). The KTU convention is *long-term* unless otherwise stated. > > 4. **Skipping Units and Direction in Final Answer (1 mark lost):** Always state the unit of the gap (DPMO, %, mm, σ) and explicitly say "reduce by" or "increase by". KTU examiners allocate dedicated marks for this clarity. > > 5. **Confusing Benchmarking with Process Capability (2 marks lost):** $C_{pk}$ is a *capability* measure (process-focused); benchmarking is a *comparative* measure (organization-focused). They are related but not identical. A high $C_{pk}$ does not guarantee a good benchmarking ratio if the entire industry has moved to 6-sigma. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Benchmarking** - **Definition:** Systematic, continuous comparison against best-in-class to identify gaps and implement improvements. - **Origin:** The word "benchmark" comes from land surveying; Camp's 5-phase model is the gold standard. - **Five Types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic/Process, Strategic. - **Camp's 5 Phases:** Plan → Analyze → Integrate → Act → Maturity (with continuous feedback to Plan). - **Spendolini's 10 Steps:** A more granular expansion of the 5-phase model. - **Code of Conduct:** 8 principles, primarily Legality, Exchange, Confidentiality, Use, First-Contact, Preparation, Completion, "Don't Ask". - **Performance Gap (Absolute):** $G = P_{\text{current}} - P_{\text{best}}$. Sign indicates direction. - **Performance Gap (Relative %):** $G_{\%} = (P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}) / P_{\text{best}} \times 100$. - **Benchmarking Ratio (BR):** $BR = P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{best}}$ — ideal = 1.0. - **Improvement Factor (IF):** $IF = P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{best}}$ for "smaller-is-better"; $IF = P_{\text{best}} / P_{\text{current}}$ for "larger-is-better". - **DPMO Formula:** $DPMO = (D \times 10^{6}) / (U \times O)$. - **Sigma Level:** Long-term sigma = short-term Z-score − 1.5 (Motorola 1.5σ shift rule). - **Process Capability:** $C_{pk} = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$ (simplified form). - **World-Class Targets:** 3.4 DPMO = 6σ; 233 DPMO = 5σ; 6,210 DPMO = 4σ. - **Linkage to DMAIC:** Benchmarking feeds *Measure* (best-in-class data) and *Analyze* (gap analysis) phases. - **Linkage to SPC:** Control charts (X-bar, R, p) verify whether the post-benchmarking process is in statistical control. - **Continuous Nature:** A benchmark is a *moving target*; recalibration is required at least annually. - **Internal Benchmarking Advantage:** Easy data access. Limitation: cannot break the *internal* capability ceiling. - **Competitive Benchmarking Advantage:** Directly actionable. Limitation: data is proprietary. - **Functional Benchmarking Advantage:** Breakthrough innovation possible. Limitation: practices may not be industry-transferable. - **Strategic Benchmarking Advantage:** Long-term positioning. Limitation: execution is slow. - **Generic Benchmarking Advantage:** Most transferable best practices. Limitation: requires significant adaptation. - **Common Pitfall:** Treating benchmarking as a one-time project rather than a continuous cycle — fails the "Maturity" phase. - **Ethical Principle:** "Don't Ask" — never request data that would place the partner at a legal or competitive disadvantage. - **Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always structure as: Definition → Types/Phases → Numerical (if any) → Strategic Implications. ---

topics.title: Kaizen and Kairyo Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kaizen and Kairyo Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kaizen (改善)** is a Japanese management philosophy that literally translates to *"change for the better"* (Kai = change, Zen = good). In the context of Statistical Quality Control and Total Quality Management (TQM), Kaizen refers to a **continuous, incremental, and systematic approach to process improvement** that engages every employee — from the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) to the frontline shop-floor worker — in identifying and eliminating waste (*Muda*), irregularity (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*).

**Kairyo (改良)**, in contrast, translates to *"innovation" or "radical improvement."* It denotes **discontinuous, large-scale, technology-driven, breakthrough improvements** — typically led by R&D specialists, engineers, or senior management — that fundamentally transform processes, products, or systems rather than refining them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEPET415 Module 2):**
> The two systems represent **complementary poles** of the Japanese quality improvement spectrum. Kaizen is the **"compass"** of daily operations, while Kairyo is the **"engine"** of strategic transformation. Together they form the **Kaizen–Kairyo Paradigm** central to lean manufacturing and Six Sigma frameworks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden & Renovations" Metaphor

Imagine you own a small home garden:

- **Kaizen** is like the **daily care** you give the garden — watering the plants, removing wilted leaves, pulling out tiny weeds, mulching the soil a little, trimming overgrown branches. Each action is small, cheap, and frequent. Over time, the garden flourishes.
- **Kairyo** is like **renovating the entire backyard** — installing a drip-irrigation system, building a greenhouse, switching to hydroponics, or replacing the soil altogether. This requires planning, capital investment, and expert help, but it delivers a quantum leap in output.

If you rely only on Kaizen, the garden remains traditional; if you rely only on Kairyo, you may bankrupt yourself before the renovation finishes. **Mature quality systems need both** — that is the Kaizen–Kairyo duality taught under KTU Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin & Standard References:**
> - **Kaizen** philosophy was popularized globally by **Masaaki Imai** in his seminal work *Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success* (1986).
> - **Kairyo** concepts are deeply embedded in the Japanese innovation doctrine traced to post-WWII industrial reconstruction and Deming's 14 Points (1982).
> - Both are foundational to the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and the **Lean Six Sigma** methodology that KTU students encounter in Operations Management and Quality Engineering electives.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Approximate Translation |
|---|---|---|
| **Muda (無駄)** | Waste | Anything that does not add value to the customer |
| **Mura (斑)** | Unevenness | Irregularity or inconsistency in workload/flow |
| **Muri (無理)** | Overburden | Excessive strain on workers or machines |
| **Gemba (現場)** | The actual place | The shop floor where value is created |
| **5S** | Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain | Workplace organization methodology |
| **PDCA** | Plan–Do–Check–Act | Deming/Shewhart improvement cycle |
| **Kaizen Event** | Blitz Kaizen | A focused 3–5 day rapid improvement project |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Kaizen

The Japanese Kaizen framework rests on five interlocking operational pillars. KTU examiners frequently ask students to enumerate and explain these pillars.

### Pillar 1 — Teamwork & Employee Involvement
Kaizen is **democratic improvement**. Quality circles (originated under Ishikawa) are the formal vehicle. Every operator is empowered to:
- Stop the line on detecting a defect (*Jidoka* principle).
- Submit improvement suggestions (typically **20–50 suggestions per employee per year** in mature Japanese firms).
- Participate in weekly quality-circle meetings.

### Pillar 2 — Personal Discipline
Discipline (*Shitsuke*, the 5th "S") ensures that newly established standards are not eroded over time. The cost of indiscipline is measured as **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**.

### Pillar 3 — Improved Morale
Improvement activities boost self-esteem and ownership, reducing absenteeism and labour turnover.

### Pillar 4 — Quality Circles
Small groups of 6–12 workers from the same area who meet weekly to solve workplace problems using simple statistical tools (Pareto charts, cause-and-effect diagrams, histograms, scatter diagrams, control charts).

### Pillar 5 — Suggestions for Improvement
A structured suggestion system with monetary and non-monetary recognition. A mature Kaizen culture typically processes **> 90% of suggestions** with feedback.

## 2.2 The 5S Methodology (Foundational to Kaizen)

| Step | Japanese | English | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seiri | Sort | Remove unnecessary items from the workplace |
| 2 | Seiton | Set in order | Arrange items for easy access (a place for everything) |
| 3 | Seiso | Shine | Clean the workplace; inspect during cleaning |
| 4 | Seiketsu | Standardize | Establish procedures and visual standards |
| 5 | Shitsuke | Sustain | Maintain discipline through audits and training |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** 5S is the **gateway to statistical process control** — you cannot construct a reliable $\bar{X}$-chart or $R$-chart on a chaotic, disorganized shop floor. The KTU Module 2 syllabus positions 5S as a *prerequisite* for any SPC implementation.

## 2.3 The Seven Wastes (Muda) of Kaizen

Originally identified by Taiichi Ohno (Toyota), extended to seven by subsequent Lean scholars:

1. **Transport** — unnecessary movement of materials
2. **Inventory** — excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods
3. **Motion** — unnecessary human movement (e.g., walking to fetch a tool)
4. **Waiting** — idle time due to unbalanced flow
5. **Overproduction** — producing more than the next process needs
6. **Over-processing** — doing more work than the customer values
7. **Defects** — rework, scrap, warranty claims

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A frequently asked KTU question: *"List the seven wastes and give one engineering example for each."* Memorize the mnemonic **TIMWOOD** (Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects).

## 2.4 The PDCA / Deming Cycle

Kaizen operates iteratively through the **PDCA cycle**:

$$
\text{Plan} \longrightarrow \text{Do} \longrightarrow \text{Check} \longrightarrow \text{Act} \longrightarrow \text{(re-enter Plan)}
$$

- **Plan:** Identify the problem, analyse with Pareto/Ishikawa, propose a countermeasure.
- **Do:** Implement on a small scale (pilot).
- **Check:** Validate using SPC tools ($\bar{X}$, $R$, $p$, $c$ charts).
- **Act:** Standardize if successful; if not, loop back to Plan.

## 2.5 The Kairyo Paradigm

Kairyo is invoked when Kaizen's incremental gains are insufficient. Typical triggers:

- **Technological discontinuity** (e.g., shift from analog to digital instrumentation).
- **Market disruption** (e.g., new regulatory standard like ISO 9001:2015 transition).
- **Capacity breakthrough** (e.g., installation of a CNC machining centre replacing a manual lathe).
- **Cost structure transformation** (e.g., reshoring or automation via Industry 4.0).

Kairyo is usually executed through:
- **R&D projects** with formal stage-gate reviews.
- **Capital investment** in new machinery (CAPEX-heavy).
- **Re-engineering initiatives** (BPR — Business Process Re-engineering).
- **Six Sigma / DMAIC** at a *Black-Belt* level.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Kaizen vs Kairyo)

| Dimension | Kaizen (改善) | Kairyo (改良) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale of change** | Small, incremental | Large, radical |
| **Frequency** | Continuous (daily) | Discontinuous (project-based) |
| **Driver** | All employees | Specialists, R&D, senior management |
| **Investment** | Low (minimal CAPEX) | High (significant CAPEX) |
| **Time horizon** | Short to medium (weeks/months) | Medium to long (months/years) |
| **Risk** | Low | High |
| **Primary toolset** | 5S, PDCA, suggestion system | DMAIC, QFD, FMEA, TRIZ, BPR |
| **Measured by** | Number of suggestions, % implemented | ROI, NPV, breakthrough KPIs |
| **Statistical link** | Control charts for monitoring | Design of Experiments (DOE) for optimization |
| **Philosophy** | "Many small steps compound" | "One giant leap transforms" |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System | Japanese industrial R&D tradition |
| **Failure tolerance** | High (learn from small wins) | Low (high-stakes projects) |

## 2.7 The Mathematical Bridge to SPC

The economic gain from Kaizen can be quantified by the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** reduction formula:

$$
\text{COPQ} \;=\; C_{\text{prevention}} \;+\; C_{\text{appraisal}} \;+\; C_{\text{internal failure}} \;+\; C_{\text{external failure}}
$$

A successful Kaizen event targets lowering $C_{\text{internal failure}}$ and $C_{\text{external failure}}$ — both of which directly translate to a **decrease in out-of-control signals** on Shewhart control charts and a **reduction in process capability index deviation** from the target $C_p = 1.33$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Kaizen is **"C"**ontinu**O**us — associated with **C**ontrol charts. Kairyo is **"B"**rea**K**through — associated with **D**esign of **E**xperiments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Because the topic of *Kaizen & Kairyo* is conceptual-management in nature (rather than computational-calculus), Section 3 focuses on **analytical frameworks, worked examples of COPQ calculation, and the structured mapping of improvement ideas to SPC tools** — all of which are the most frequently tested quantitative aspects in KTU examinations.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quantifying Kaizen Savings Using COPQ

**Problem Statement:**
A small-scale automotive parts manufacturer in a KTU industrial cluster has the following annual quality costs:

- Prevention cost ($C_P$): **₹ 4,00,000**
- Appraisal cost ($C_A$): **₹ 3,00,000**
- Internal failure cost ($C_{IF}$): **₹ 12,00,000**
- External failure cost ($C_{EF}$): **₹ 21,00,000**

The company runs a **Kaizen event** that successfully:
- Eliminates 40% of internal failure costs
- Eliminates 30% of external failure costs
- Requires an additional ₹ 1,50,000 in prevention investment
- Adds ₹ 50,000 in appraisal cost (new gauge purchases)

**Compute the COPQ before and after the Kaizen event, and the net annual savings.**

### Step 1 — Define the COPQ identity

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} \;=\; C_P \;+\; C_A \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given data

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \;+\; 3{,}00{,}000 \;+\; 12{,}00{,}000 \;+\; 21{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the reduction in failure costs

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{IF} &= 0.40 \times 12{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 \text{ INR} \\[4pt]
\Delta C_{EF} &= 0.30 \times 21{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}30{,}000 \text{ INR} \\[4pt]
\Delta C_{\text{failure,total}} &= 4{,}80{,}000 + 6{,}30{,}000 = 11{,}10{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Add the new prevention and appraisal cost increments

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_P &= +1{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR} \\[4pt]
\Delta C_A &= +50{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute COPQ after the Kaizen event

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} &= (C_P + \Delta C_P) \;+\; (C_A + \Delta C_A) \;+\; (C_{IF} - \Delta C_{IF}) \;+\; (C_{EF} - \Delta C_{EF}) \\[4pt]
&= (4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000) + (3{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000) \\[2pt]
&\quad + (12{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000) + (21{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}30{,}000) \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}000 + 14{,}70{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 30{,}90{,}000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the net annual savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} - \text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} \\[4pt]
&= 40{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}90{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 9{,}10{,}000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification (Pareto Logic):** A 70% reduction in failure-mode costs (combined 40% + 30% weighted) offsets a smaller investment in prevention/appraisal — the **classical Juran "gold-mining" principle** that Kaizen must be financially self-justifying.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mapping Kaizen Ideas to SPC Charts

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU viva-style question often asks: *"For each of the following Kaizen scenarios, identify the most appropriate SPC control chart and justify the choice."*

Provide a structured mapping matrix.

| Kaizen Scenario | Data Type | Sample Size | Recommended Chart | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monitoring diameter of a turned shaft | Continuous | $n=5$ | $\bar{X}$ and $R$ chart | $\bar{X}$ tracks process mean, $R$ tracks variability within subgroups |
| Counting weld defects per chassis | Discrete (count) | $n=1$ | $c$-chart | Defects per unit of constant area/length; $c$-chart assumes constant inspection unit |
| Tracking fraction of defective PCB lots | Discrete (proportion) | Variable $n$ | $p$-chart | Sample size varies by lot; $p$-chart handles varying $n$ |
| Tracking number of defective units in fixed $n=100$ sample | Discrete (proportion) | $n=100$ constant | $np$-chart | Constant subgroup size simplifies the centre line |
| Monitoring process when subgroup $=1$ | Continuous | $n=1$ | $X$-MR (Individuals & Moving Range) chart | Suitable for low-volume or destructive testing |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Tree — When to use Kaizen vs Kairyo

This is a frequently tested reasoning question in KTU Module 2:

```
START
  │
  ├── Is the problem a routine operational inefficiency?
  │       YES → KAIZEN (small, team-driven, PDCA)
  │       NO  ↓
  │
  ├── Is the root cause technological or systemic?
  │       YES → KAIRO (large, R&D-driven, DMAIC/DOE)
  │       NO  ↓
  │
  ├── Is the proposed fix low-cost and reversible?
  │       YES → KAIZEN
  │       NO  → KAIRO
  │
  └── Is the timeline < 3 months?
          YES → KAIZEN
          NO  → KAIRO
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision tree is **not mutually exclusive** — mature organizations run **parallel Kaizen and Kairyo tracks**. Toyota's famous "double-track" improvement system (daily Kaizen + strategic Kairyo) is the gold-standard model cited in KTU pedagogy.

## 3.4 Linking Kaizen Events to Process Capability Indices

A Kairyo-type breakthrough often aims to move a process from $C_p < 1$ to $C_p \geq 1.33$ (the "capable" threshold). The capability index is given by:

$$
C_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6 \sigma}
$$

A Kaizen event typically targets a **sigma-reduction** of 10–20% over a quarter, which is mathematically related to the yield improvement:

$$
Y_{\text{normal}} \;=\; 2 \Phi(3 C_p) - 1
$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution. A movement from $C_p = 1.00$ to $C_p = 1.33$ takes the process yield from **99.73%** to **99.993%** (a *3.4 DPMO* leap) — a classic Kairyo-level result achievable only via structural change.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Kaizen–Kairyo Improvement Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    strat["Strategic Kairyo Breakthrough"]:::top
    proj["Project-level DMAIC / Six Sigma"]:::mid
    evt["Kaizen Events 3-5 days"]:::mid2
    daily["Daily 5S and Suggestion Kaizen"]:::base
    pdca1["PDCA Loop 1"]:::cycle
    pdca2["PDCA Loop 2"]:::cycle
    pdca3["PDCA Loop 3"]:::cycle

    strat --> proj
    proj --> evt
    evt --> daily
    daily -.-> pdca1
    daily -.-> pdca2
    daily -.-> pdca3

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000000
    classDef mid2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    classDef base fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000000
    classDef cycle fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#666666,color:#000000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top tier (gold)** = Strategic Kairyo — large, infrequent, top-down.
- **Middle tier (blue / green)** = Project-level improvements — DMAIC, Six Sigma, Kaizen events.
- **Bottom tier (orange)** = Daily 5S, PDCA cycles — the foundation of Kaizen culture.
- **Dashed loops** = Continuous iterative refinement feeding back into the base.

## 4.2 The PDCA Cycle in Detail (Linked to Kaizen)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PLAN\nIdentify problem\nPareto + Ishikawa\nHypothesize cause"]:::plan
    D["DO\nPilot on small scale\nCollect data\nRun SPC chart"]:::do
    C["CHECK\nAnalyse with control chart\nCompare with standard\nTest hypothesis"]:::check
    A["ACT\nStandardize if success\nDocument SOP\nTrain workforce"]:::act
    Pnext["RE-ENTER PLAN\nfor next problem"]:::next

    P --> D --> C --> A --> Pnext
    Pnext -.-> P

    classDef plan fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000
    classDef do fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000000
    classDef check fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000000
    classDef act fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef next fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Kaizen Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        G["Gemba Observations\nShop-floor data"]:::in
        S["Worker Suggestions\nQuality circles"]:::in
        CUS["Customer Complaints\nWarranty data"]:::in
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        PAR["Pareto Analysis\n80/20 prioritization"]:::proc
        ISH["Ishikawa Fishbone\nCause categorization"]:::proc
        SPC["SPC Charts\nX-bar, R, p, c"]:::proc
        DEC{"Type of fix?"}:::dec
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        K["KAIZEN PATH\nSmall, daily, PDCA\nLow cost, low risk"]:::outK
        KR["KAIRO PATH\nLarge, R&D, DMAIC\nHigh cost, high reward"]:::outR
    end

    G --> PAR
    S --> PAR
    CUS --> PAR
    PAR --> ISH
    ISH --> SPC
    SPC --> DEC
    DEC -- routine --> K
    DEC -- strategic --> KR

    classDef in fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    classDef dec fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000000
    classDef outK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef outR fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.4 The Seven Wastes — Visual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W["SEVEN WASTES TIMWOOD"]:::title
    W --> W1["T - Transport\nMove materials less"]:::w
    W --> W2["I - Inventory\nReduce WIP and stock"]:::w
    W --> W3["M - Motion\nErgonomic layout"]:::w
    W --> W4["W - Waiting\nBalance the line"]:::w
    W --> W5["O - Overproduction\nPull not push"]:::w
    W --> W6["O - Over-processing\nValue stream map"]:::w
    W --> W7["D - Defects\nPoka-yoke + SPC"]:::w

    classDef title fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef w fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**Define Kaizen. State any four of its operational pillars.**

**Model Answer:**
Kaizen (改善) is a Japanese management philosophy meaning *"continuous improvement."* It emphasizes small, incremental, employee-driven changes to processes, products, and services on a continuous basis.

**Four operational pillars of Kaizen:**
1. **Teamwork and employee involvement** — quality circles, suggestion systems.
2. **Personal discipline** — adherence to newly established standards (5S Shitsuke).
3. **Improved morale** — recognition and reward systems.
4. **Quality circles** — small groups of 6–12 workers meeting weekly to solve workplace problems.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Suggestions for improvement with structured feedback.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for 4 pillars]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**List the seven wastes (Muda) of Lean manufacturing using the TIMWOOD mnemonic and give one example for any three.**

**Model Answer:**

| Letter | Waste | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **T** | Transport | Forklift moving WIP across the plant unnecessarily |
| **I** | Inventory | Excess raw material stacked near a CNC machine |
| **M** | Motion | Operator walking 10 m to fetch a torque wrench |
| **W** | Waiting | CNC machine idle while operator changes tools manually |
| **O** | Overproduction | Producing 1,000 units when the next process needs only 600 |
| **O** | Over-processing | Hand-polishing a part that does not require cosmetic finish |
| **D** | Defects | Weld porosity requiring rework |

**[3 marks: 1 mark list + 2 marks for two explained examples]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Kaizen-Centric Question*
**[KTU University Exam — Past Year Pattern, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain the philosophy of Kaizen in detail. Discuss its five operational pillars and the role of the 5S methodology as its foundation.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A manufacturing unit has the following annual quality cost data:*
- *Prevention cost: ₹ 5,00,000*
- *Appraisal cost: ₹ 3,50,000*
- *Internal failure cost: ₹ 14,00,000*
- *External failure cost: ₹ 17,50,000*

*After implementing a Kaizen programme, the firm achieves a 50% reduction in internal failure cost and a 40% reduction in external failure cost, while spending an additional ₹ 2,00,000 on prevention and ₹ 1,00,000 on appraisal. Calculate the COPQ before and after, and comment on the financial viability of the Kaizen initiative.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Philosophy of Kaizen (3 marks):**
Kaizen, originating from post-WWII Japanese industrial practice and popularized by Masaaki Imai, is a *continuous improvement* philosophy built on the premise that **small, daily, employee-driven changes compound into transformational results over time.** Unlike Western "big-bang" re-engineering, Kaizen emphasizes *process* over *outcome*, *people* over *systems*, and *standardization* over *creativity*. It is deeply intertwined with the Toyota Production System and is the cultural bedrock on which Deming's PDCA cycle and Juran's quality planning trilogy rest.

**Five Operational Pillars (2 marks):**
1. **Teamwork and employee involvement** — Quality circles, suggestion schemes.
2. **Personal discipline** — Sustaining standards through audits.
3. **Improved morale** — Recognition, transparent communication.
4. **Quality circles** — Weekly problem-solving teams (6–12 members).
5. **Suggestions for improvement** — Structured feedback with measurable implementation rates.

**Role of 5S (2 marks):**
The 5S methodology (Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) is the **operational foundation** of Kaizen. Without a clean, organized, standardized workplace, no statistical measurement (control chart, Pareto analysis) can be reliably performed. 5S eliminates the *Muda* of motion and waiting, and it is the first step in every Kaizen event agenda worldwide.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute COPQ before Kaizen:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 14{,}00{,}000 + 17{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute savings in failure costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{IF} &= 0.50 \times 14{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\Delta C_{EF} &= 0.40 \times 17{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\Delta C_{\text{failure,total}} &= 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add new prevention and appraisal costs:**

$$
\Delta C_P + \Delta C_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute COPQ after Kaizen:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} &= (5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000) + (3{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000) \\[2pt]
&\quad + (14{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000) + (17{,}50{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000) \\[4pt]
&= 7{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 29{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute net savings and comment:**

$$
\text{Net Annual Savings} = 40{,}00{,}000 - 29{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The Kaizen initiative delivers a **net saving of ₹ 11,00,000 per year** with a **27.5% reduction in total COPQ**. The 14 lakhs saved in failure costs comfortably absorbs the 3 lakhs additional investment in prevention and appraisal — confirming strong financial viability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Kairyo-Centric Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam — Past Year Pattern, Module 2 — Internal Choice Option]**

**(a)** *Define Kairyo. Compare Kaizen and Kairyo across any six parameters. Justify why both systems must coexist in a mature quality organization.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A process has USL = 12.05 mm, LSL = 11.95 mm, and the process standard deviation is σ = 0.025 mm. Compute the process capability index $C_p$. If a Kairyo programme reduces the standard deviation to σ' = 0.015 mm, recalculate $C_p'$. Comment on the practical significance of the improvement using the standard quality levels.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Kairyo (1 mark):**
Kairyo (改良) is a Japanese term for *radical, innovation-driven, large-scale improvement*, typically led by R&D, engineering specialists, or senior management. It is *discontinuous* — implemented as a project rather than a daily routine.

**Six-Parameter Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Kaizen | Kairyo |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | Small / incremental | Large / breakthrough |
| Frequency | Continuous (daily) | Discontinuous (project-based) |
| Investment | Low CAPEX | High CAPEX |
| Leadership | All employees | Specialists / R&D / top management |
| Risk | Low | High |
| Time horizon | Short–medium | Medium–long |

**Why Both Must Coexist (2 marks):**
A mature quality organization requires **both small daily refinements (Kaizen) and periodic transformational leaps (Kairyo)**. Without Kaizen, a Kairyo breakthrough will erode over time as the workforce reverts to old habits. Without Kairyo, the organization stagnates and cannot leapfrog competitors. This **dual-track philosophy** is the cornerstone of Toyota's competitive success and aligns with the **KTU Module 2 emphasis on integrated quality management**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$ before Kairyo:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{12.05 - 11.95}{6 \times 0.025} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.10}{0.150} \\[4pt]
&= 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C_p < 1$, the process is **not capable** of meeting specification.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_p'$ after Kairyo:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p' &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6 \sigma'} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.015} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.10}{0.090} \\[4pt]
&= 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the results (3 marks):**

| $C_p$ Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $C_p < 1.00$ | Process is not capable — high defect rate |
| $1.00 \leq C_p < 1.33$ | Marginal — capable but needs monitoring |
| $1.33 \leq C_p < 1.67$ | Good — industry benchmark |
| $C_p \geq 1.67$ | Excellent — Six-Sigma territory |

- **Before:** $C_p = 0.667$ → process produces approximately **44,600 DPMO** (defects per million opportunities) — a typical 2-sigma process.
- **After:** $C_p' = 1.111$ → process moves to the **3-sigma band** (~2,700 DPMO) — a **16-fold quality improvement**, which is the hallmark of an effective Kairyo intervention. To reach the gold standard $C_p \geq 1.33$, a complementary Kaizen track would be required to fine-tune the centring (drift towards target value $T$).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Kaizen with Kairyo** — A frequent 1-mark loss. Memorize: *Kaizen = small/continuous; Kairyo = large/discontinuous.*
> 2. **Forgetting to add the new prevention/appraisal costs in COPQ problems** — Examiner traps students who only subtract failure savings.
> 3. **Misidentifying the appropriate SPC chart** — A $c$-chart assumes a *constant inspection unit*; if $n$ varies, use a $u$-chart, not a $c$-chart.
> 4. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$** — $C_p$ measures *potential* capability (centred process); $C_{pk}$ measures *actual* capability (accounting for off-centring). A KTU question mentioning "the process is off-centre" requires $C_{pk}$, not $C_p$.
> 5. **Misspelling the seven wastes in random order** — Always use the **TIMWOOD** mnemonic in the same sequence; examiners reward the standard ordering.
> 6. **Writing "Kaizen Event" without explaining its duration and structure** — It is a *focused 3–5 day workshop*, not an open-ended meeting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Kaizen** = small, continuous, employee-driven improvement (改善 — *kai* change + *zen* good).
- [x] **Kairyo** = large, radical, innovation-driven improvement (改良 — *kai* change + *ryo* good/refine).
- [x] **5S** = Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — the *prerequisite* for any Kaizen event.
- [x] **Seven Wastes** (TIMWOOD) = Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- [x] **PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act — Deming's iterative improvement cycle.
- [x] **Gemba** = the actual place where work happens (shop floor).
- [x] **Muda** = waste, **Mura** = unevenness, **Muri** = overburden.
- [x] **COPQ formula** = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — must be recomputed *after* every Kaizen intervention.
- [x] **$C_p$ threshold** for a capable process is **$\geq 1.33$**; for Six Sigma it is **$\geq 1.67$**.
- [x] **Quality circles** originated in Japan (1962) under **Kaoru Ishikawa** — small groups of 6–12 workers meeting weekly.
- [x] **Kaizen event** = 3–5 day focused improvement project; **Kairyo** = multi-month, CAPEX-heavy project.
- [x] **Masaaki Imai** (1986) is the global popularizer of Kaizen; Toyota Production System is the most cited real-world exemplar.
- [x] **Dual-track philosophy** = *mature organizations run Kaizen and Kairyo in parallel*, not as alternatives.
- [x] **SPC linkage** — Kaizen monitors via control charts; Kairyo optimizes via Design of Experiments (DOE).
- [x] **KTU module mapping** — this topic integrates with Module 3 (control charts for variables/attributes) and Module 4 (acceptance sampling) as the *cultural and managerial foundation*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brainstorming
topics.content:
