> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Brainstorming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Brainstorming** is a structured, group-based creative ideation technique developed by **Alex F. Osborn** in **1939** (popularized in his 1953 book *Applied Imagination*) in which a team of participants generates a large quantity of ideas, solutions, or alternatives to a specific problem **without immediate criticism or judgment**, with the goal of producing novel, high-quality outputs that can later be filtered, evaluated, and refined.

In the context of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** and **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, brainstorming is classified as one of the **"Seven Management and Planning Tools"** and is considered a foundational **cause-analysis** and **problem-solving technique** used extensively during the *Define* and *Measure* phases of the **DMAIC (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control)** methodology of Six Sigma, and during the *Plan* phase of the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPET415 – Module 2):**
> Brainstorming is treated as a *qualitative quality improvement tool* that complements the *quantitative* statistical tools (control charts, Pareto analysis, Ishikawa diagrams, etc.). It is the *first step* in the structured problem-solving workflow before data-driven statistical analysis is applied.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **fishing net** thrown into a vast ocean of possibilities. Brainstorming is the act of casting the net **wide and freely**, catching every fish — big, small, weird-shaped, even inedible ones. The fisherman does **not** judge the fish while the net is being pulled in. Only *after* the net is full does the fisherman sort, grade, and keep only the best fish.

In quality control, the "ocean" is the team's collective knowledge about a defect, the "net" is the brainstorming session, the "fish" are ideas about root causes, and the "sorting" is the later statistical or cause-effect analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** *Quantity breeds quality.* The primary objective of brainstorming is to maximize the **number of ideas**, deferring all judgment to a later stage. **Criticism is strictly prohibited** during idea generation.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning in SQC Context |
|------|------------------------|
| **Facilitator** | The neutral person who guides the session, enforces rules, and records ideas. |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | The person who captures every idea verbatim on a board/sheet. |
| **Stimulus** | The problem statement or question that triggers ideation. |
| **Freewheeling** | Encouragement to propose wild, unconventional ideas. |
| **Piggybacking / Hitchhiking** | Building on another participant's idea to create a new one. |
| **Deferment of Judgment** | No criticism, evaluation, or "yes-but" statements during ideation. |
| **Cross-fertilization** | Combining ideas from different domains/disciplines. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Osborn's original guideline suggested a session of **45–60 minutes** with **6–12 participants**, targeting a minimum of **50–100 ideas** per session. KTU questions often quote this benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Cardinal Rules of Brainstorming (Osborn's Original)

Osborn codified brainstorming into **four non-negotiable rules**. Every KTU answer on brainstorming must reproduce these:

1. **No Criticism (Defer Judgment):** Every idea, no matter how absurd, is recorded without evaluation. Criticism kills creativity and causes *production blocking*.
2. **Freewheeling is Welcomed:** The wilder the idea, the better. Outrageous thoughts often trigger breakthrough ideas through piggybacking.
3. **Quantity is Desired:** The greater the number of ideas, the greater the probability of finding a brilliant one. A target of 50–100 ideas is standard.
4. **Combination and Improvement:** Participants are encouraged to *piggyback* on others' ideas, merge them, or refine them into superior concepts.

### 2.2 Types / Variants of Brainstorming

The following taxonomy is **high-yield** for KTU Part A and Part B questions:

| Type | Description | When Used |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Structured Brainstorming** | Each member presents an idea in turn (round-robin). | When senior/dominant members might otherwise dominate. |
| **Unstructured Brainstorming** | Free-flowing, any member speaks at any time. | When the group is mature and equally empowered. |
| **Silent / Written Brainstorming** (e.g., *6-3-5* method) | Ideas are written silently on paper, then passed. | When introverts are present or for cross-cultural teams. |
| **Electronic / Online Brainstorming** | Ideas posted via shared digital workspace. | Distributed/global teams; reduces production blocking. |
| **Nominal Group Technique (NGT)** | Silent idea generation, then round-robin sharing, then voting. | When structured ranking is required immediately. |
| **Affinity Grouping** | Ideas are clustered into natural groups after generation. | Used with KJ Method to organize ideas. |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Process of a Brainstorming Session

The **operational workflow** expected in a KTU 14-mark answer:

| Step | Action | Responsible |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **1. Define the Problem** | Frame a clear, specific, focused problem statement (the *stimulus*). | Facilitator |
| **2. Select the Team** | Recruit 6–12 diverse members (cross-functional in SQC contexts). | Quality Manager |
| **3. Appoint Facilitator & Scribe** | Facilitator enforces rules; scribe records verbatim. | Quality Manager |
| **4. Warm-up Session** | 5–10 min practice on an unrelated topic to relax the group. | Facilitator |
| **5. Generate Ideas** | 30–45 min of uninterrupted idea generation following the 4 rules. | All |
| **6. Record Ideas Verbatim** | Every idea is captured on flip-chart / whiteboard, numbered. | Scribe |
| **7. Clarify & Combine** | Briefly explain ambiguous ideas; combine similar ones. | All |
| **8. Evaluate & Prioritize** | Apply multi-voting, dot voting, or weighted scoring later. | Team |
| **9. Document & Action** | Record outputs, assign follow-up owners, integrate with Fishbone / 5-Why / Pareto. | Facilitator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Linkage:** Brainstorming is **never** the final step. Its output always feeds into a downstream tool — most commonly the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram**, the **Pareto Chart**, or a **5-Why analysis**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since brainstorming is a *qualitative* technique, the "formulas" here are the **structured parameters and metrics** examiners love to test:

| Parameter / Metric | Standard Value / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| Optimal team size | **6 – 12 members** | Balance between diversity and coordination cost. |
| Session duration | **45 – 60 minutes** | Prevents fatigue; maintains creative energy. |
| Target idea count | **≥ 50 ideas per session** | Statistically increases probability of high-quality ideas. |
| No-criticism window | **Entire ideation phase** | Eliminates *production blocking* and *evaluation apprehension*. |
| Warm-up duration | **5 – 10 minutes** | Reduces social anxiety, primes lateral thinking. |
| **Pareto Integration** | Top **20 %** of ideas → **80 %** of value | Use 80/20 rule to prioritize ideas after brainstorming. |
| **6-3-5 Method** | 6 people × 3 ideas × 5 rounds = **90 ideas** | Silent written variant for introverted teams. |
| **NGT Voting Weight** | Each member gets **3–5 votes** | Quantitative prioritization of qualitative ideas. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In manufacturing SQC, brainstorming is the *upstream* input that defines **what to monitor** on a control chart. For example, "What causes piston-ring defects?" → brainstorm → feed causes into Fishbone → identify measurable variables → design **X̄–R** or **p-chart**. Brainstorming therefore **scopes the statistical analysis**.

### 2.5 Advantages and Limitations (Direct Exam-Ready Points)

**Advantages**
- Generates a **large volume of ideas** rapidly.
- Promotes **cross-functional collaboration** and team ownership.
- Encourages **lateral thinking** and uncovers non-obvious root causes.
- Low cost — requires only a meeting room, flip-chart, and marker.
- Builds **psychological safety** in the team.

**Limitations**
- Susceptible to **production blocking** (members wait for their turn).
- Vulnerable to **groupthink** and dominance by vocal members.
- Risk of **social loafing** in unstructured settings.
- Quality of output depends heavily on **facilitator competence**.
- Can produce redundant or unfeasible ideas requiring heavy post-processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Expected Idea Yield (Quantitative Model)

Although brainstorming is qualitative, KTU may ask you to **quantify** expected output. A standard probabilistic model used in quality literature is the **6-3-5 Silent Brainstorming** productivity model.

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Style):**
In a 6-3-5 silent brainstorming session conducted for a quality improvement project, 6 engineers each write 3 ideas in 5 successive rounds. Each round, every sheet is passed to the next participant, who reads prior ideas and "hitches" new ideas from them.

Let:
- $N$ = number of participants
- $I$ = ideas per person per round
- $R$ = number of rounds

The **total expected idea count** $T$ is given by:

$$
T = N \times I \times R
$$

For the standard 6-3-5 configuration:

$$
T = 6 \times 3 \times 5
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$
T = 6 \times 3 \times 5
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply stepwise:**

$$
T = 18 \times 5
$$

**Step 3 — Final result:**

$$
T = 90 \text{ ideas}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for the piggybacking / hitchhiking factor:**
Empirical studies show that **20–30 %** of the 90 ideas are *unique original* ideas and the rest are *derived*. The duplication rate $\phi$ is typically **0.20 to 0.30**.

$$
\text{Unique ideas} = T \times (1 - \phi)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\phi = 0.25$ (typical):**

$$
\text{Unique ideas} = 90 \times (1 - 0.25)
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$
\text{Unique ideas} = 90 \times 0.75 = 67.5 \approx 68 \text{ ideas}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for SQC:** Out of ~90 raw ideas, roughly 68 are distinct root-cause hypotheses that can be entered into a Fishbone diagram or scored with Pareto weighting.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Brainstorming Variants

This matrix is **directly useful** for KTU Part B comparative questions (e.g., *"Compare structured and unstructured brainstorming"* or *"Justify the use of NGT in your industry project"*).

| Criterion | Structured | Unstructured | Silent (6-3-5) | NGT | Electronic |
|-----------|:----------:|:------------:|:--------------:|:---:|:----------:|
| **Idea Volume (per hour)** | Medium | High | Very High | Medium | High |
| **Production Blocking** | Low | High | Zero | Zero | Zero |
| **Evaluation Apprehension** | Medium | High | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Dominance Effect** | Low | High | Zero | Low | Zero |
| **Equal Participation** | High | Low | Very High | High | Very High |
| **Facilitator Skill Required** | Medium | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Documentation Ease** | Medium | Difficult | Easy | Easy | Very Easy |
| **Cross-functional Use** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (global) |
| **Suitability for SQC Teams** | High | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Time Required** | 45 min | 45 min | 30 min | 60 min | 45 min |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Applying Brainstorming in an SQC Project

**Scenario (typical KTU 14-mark stem):**
> A bottling plant observes that the *fill volume* in 500 mL bottles has a mean of 498 mL and standard deviation 1.5 mL. The lower specification limit (LSL) is 495 mL. As Quality Engineer, you suspect multiple causes. Design a brainstorming-driven problem-solving plan and compute the process capability index $C_{pk}$.

**Step 1 — Frame the stimulus (brainstorming problem statement):**
*"What are all the possible causes of under-filling in our 500 mL bottle line?"*

**Step 2 — Convene a cross-functional team:**
Production, Maintenance, Quality, R&D, Suppliers (6–12 members).

**Step 3 — Run a 6-3-5 silent brainstorming session:**

$$
T = 6 \times 3 \times 5 = 90 \text{ ideas}
$$

**Step 4 — Cluster ideas using Affinity (KJ) Diagram** — typical clusters: *Machine*, *Method*, *Material*, *Manpower*, *Measurement*, *Environment* (the **5M + E** of the Ishikawa fishbone).

**Step 5 — Pareto-prioritize** the 90 ideas. Suppose the top 20 % of ideas (18 ideas) account for 80 % of the cumulative cause-weight.

**Step 6 — Compute Process Capability:**
Given:
- Process mean $\bar{X} = 498$ mL
- Process standard deviation $\sigma = 1.5$ mL
- LSL $= 495$ mL
- USL (assumed) $= 505$ mL

Compute $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Upper term:**

$$
\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{505 - 498}{3 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{7}{4.5}
$$

**Step 8 — Lower term:**

$$
\dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{498 - 495}{4.5} = \dfrac{3}{4.5}
$$

**Step 9 — Compare and select the minimum:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{7}{4.5},\; \dfrac{3}{4.5} \right) = \min(1.556,\; 0.667)
$$

**Step 10 — Final value:**

$$
C_{pk} = 0.667
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Since $C_{pk} < 1.0$, the process is **NOT capable**. The brainstorming outputs (root causes — likely *nozzle wear*, *viscosity variation*, *operator timing*) must now be converted into **measurable variables** and tracked on an **X̄–R chart** for the *Improve* and *Control* phases of DMAIC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Brainstorming Workflow in the SQC Problem-Solving Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Identification\nCustomer Complaint / Defect Data] --> B[Frame the Stimulus\nDefine clear problem statement]
    B --> C[Select Team\n6-12 cross-functional members]
    C --> D[Appoint Facilitator and Scribe]
    D --> E[Warm-up Session\n5-10 minutes]
    E --> F[Idea Generation Phase\n45-60 min, No Criticism]
    F --> G[Record Ideas Verbatim\nNumbered list on flip chart]
    G --> H[Clarify and Combine Ideas]
    H --> I[Cluster via Affinity Diagram\nKJ Method / 5M+E]
    I --> J[Prioritize via Pareto / Multi-voting]
    J --> K{Top Causes Identified?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Build Ishikawa Fishbone]
    K -- No --> F
    L --> M[5-Why Root Cause Analysis]
    M --> N[Design Statistical Experiment\nor Control Chart]
    N --> O[Implement Improvement]
    O --> P[Monitor with Control Chart\nX-bar R or p-chart]
    P --> Q[Standardize and Document]
    Q --> R[Continuous Improvement\nPDCA Cycle]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Brainstorming Variants and Their Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Brainstorming Technique Selection] --> Q1{Team size and\nlocation?}
    Q1 -- Co-located, balanced --> S1[Structured Round-Robin]
    Q1 -- Co-located, mixed personalities --> S2[Unstructured Free-Flow]
    Q1 -- Distributed global team --> S3[Electronic Brainstorming]
    Q1 -- Dominant members present --> S4[Silent 6-3-5]
    Q1 -- Immediate voting needed --> S5[Nominal Group Technique]
    
    S1 --> Out[Output: List of Ideas]
    S2 --> Out
    S3 --> Out
    S4 --> Out
    S5 --> Out
    
    Out --> Next[Downstream Tool: Ishikawa / Pareto / 5-Why]
    Next --> Stat[Statistical Analysis: Control Charts / DOE]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Integration of Brainstorming with SQC Tools

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Qualitative["QUALITATIVE LAYER - Ideation"]
        BS[Brainstorming]
        ISH[Ishikawa Fishbone]
        WHY[5-Why Analysis]
    end
    
    subgraph Transitional["TRANSITIONAL LAYER - Prioritization"]
        AF[Affinity Diagram]
        PR[Pareto Chart 80-20]
        MV[Multi-voting NGT]
    end
    
    subgraph Quantitative["QUANTITATIVE LAYER - SQC"]
        CC[Control Charts\nX-bar R, p, c]
        CAP[Process Capability\nCp, Cpk]
        DOE[Design of Experiments]
    end
    
    subgraph Action["ACTION LAYER - Improvement"]
        PDCA[PDCA Cycle]
        DMAIC[DMAIC Six Sigma]
        SOP[Standard Operating Procedures]
    end
    
    BS --> AF
    AF --> PR
    PR --> ISH
    ISH --> WHY
    WHY --> CC
    CC --> CAP
    CAP --> DOE
    DOE --> PDCA
    PDCA --> DMAIC
    DMAIC --> SOP
    SOP -.->|Feedback loop| BS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram left-to-right and top-to-bottom:** Brainstorming (qualitative ideation) feeds prioritization tools, which scope the statistical tools, which trigger the action layer. The feedback arrow shows that continuous improvement **loops back** to re-brainstorm when new defects appear.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define brainstorming. State any two rules to be followed during a brainstorming session.*

**Model Answer:**

Brainstorming is a group creativity technique developed by **Alex F. Osborn (1939)** in which a team of 6–12 members generates a large number of ideas to solve a specific problem **without criticism or judgment**, with the objective of producing novel solutions that can later be evaluated and refined.

Two cardinal rules:
1. **No criticism** of any idea during the generation phase — defer all judgment to a later stage.
2. **Quantity is desired** — the greater the number of ideas, the higher the probability of finding a brilliant solution. A target of 50–100 ideas per session is typical.

*(Any other two rules — Freewheeling welcomed, Combination and improvement — also accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four types of brainstorming techniques used in quality management.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Structured (Round-Robin) Brainstorming** — ideas are presented one at a time in a fixed order.
2. **Unstructured Brainstorming** — free-flowing discussion with no turn order.
3. **Silent Brainstorming (6-3-5 method)** — ideas are written silently and passed between members.
4. **Nominal Group Technique (NGT)** — silent generation followed by structured sharing and voting.

*(Electronic / Online Brainstorming is also a valid fourth option.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the four rules of brainstorming as proposed by Alex Osborn. Discuss how these rules influence the quality of ideas generated during a quality improvement project. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare structured and unstructured brainstorming with suitable examples. Under what conditions would you recommend the **Nominal Group Technique** over conventional brainstorming? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Osborn's Four Rules and Their Influence on Idea Quality**

| # | Rule | Description | Influence on Idea Quality |
|---|------|-------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **No Criticism** | All ideas are recorded without judgment during the generation phase. | Eliminates *evaluation apprehension* and *production blocking*, encouraging shy members to contribute. |
| 2 | **Freewheeling** | Wild, unconventional ideas are welcomed. | Triggers *cross-fertilization* and lateral thinking — often the wildest idea sparks the most practical solution. |
| 3 | **Quantity is Wanted** | Aim for 50–100+ ideas per session. | Statistically increases the probability of a high-quality idea (Osborn's law of *quantity breeds quality*). |
| 4 | **Combination and Improvement** | Members piggyback on others' ideas. | Produces *synergistic* ideas that no individual could have produced alone. |

**Influence on a quality project:** These rules create **psychological safety**, enabling cross-functional teams (e.g., Production, Quality, Maintenance) to surface latent root causes that structured interviews might miss. The output then feeds a Fishbone or Pareto chart.

**(b) Comparison: Structured vs Unstructured Brainstorming**

| Aspect | Structured | Unstructured |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| Turn order | Round-robin, fixed | Free-for-all |
| Dominance effect | Reduced | High risk |
| Idea volume | Medium | High (if well facilitated) |
| Suitable for | Hierarchical teams, large groups | Small, mature, equal teams |
| Risk | May feel mechanical | May be hijacked by vocal members |
| Time per idea | Predictable | Variable |

**Example (SQC context):**
- *Structured:* A 10-member team in a tier-1 automotive supplier discussing weld defects takes turns presenting one idea each.
- *Unstructured:* A 5-member R&D team openly discusses solder-paste defects on a PCB line.

**When to use NGT over conventional brainstorming:**
- When **immediate prioritization** is required (NGT includes a voting round).
- When the group has **conflicting interests** (e.g., Production vs. Quality).
- When participants are **geographically dispersed** in a hybrid setting.
- When the **problem is politically sensitive** and anonymity is needed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for conducting a brainstorming session in a manufacturing firm. What roles are played by the *facilitator* and the *scribe*? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a 6-3-5 silent brainstorming session conducted for quality improvement of a CNC machining process, calculate the total number of ideas generated. If the duplication rate is 25 %, find the number of *unique* ideas. How would you then prioritize these ideas for further statistical investigation? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure for Conducting a Brainstorming Session**

| Step | Activity |
|------|----------|
| 1 | **Define the problem** — frame a clear, specific stimulus question. |
| 2 | **Select the team** — 6–12 cross-functional members. |
| 3 | **Appoint a facilitator** — enforces rules, manages time, encourages quiet members. |
| 4 | **Appoint a scribe** — records every idea verbatim on a flip-chart. |
| 5 | **Warm-up** — 5–10 min practice round on an unrelated topic. |
| 6 | **Generate ideas** — 30–45 min of no-criticism, freewheeling ideation. |
| 7 | **Record** — number each idea; capture exactly as spoken. |
| 8 | **Clarify and combine** — briefly explain ambiguous ideas. |
| 9 | **Cluster** — group related ideas (Affinity / KJ method). |
| 10 | **Prioritize** — multi-vote or Pareto the clustered ideas. |
| 11 | **Action plan** — assign owners, integrate with Fishbone / 5-Why. |

**Roles:**
- **Facilitator:** Neutral leader; enforces the four rules; time-keeper; stimulates silent members; prevents digression.
- **Scribe:** Records ideas *verbatim* (no paraphrasing); ensures visibility (large handwriting, post-it notes); assigns numbers.

**(b) Quantitative Computation**

Given:
- $N = 6$ participants
- $I = 3$ ideas per round
- $R = 5$ rounds
- $\phi = 25\% = 0.25$ duplication rate

**Step 1 — Total ideas generated:**

$$
T = N \times I \times R = 6 \times 3 \times 5
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply:**

$$
T = 90 \text{ ideas}
$$

**Step 3 — Unique ideas:**

$$
U = T \times (1 - \phi) = 90 \times (1 - 0.25)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
U = 90 \times 0.75 = 67.5 \approx 68 \text{ unique ideas}
$$

**Step 5 — Prioritization strategy:**
- Apply the **Pareto principle**: rank the 68 ideas by their *frequency of mention* across the 5 rounds.
- The top **20 % (≈ 14 ideas)** typically account for ~80 % of the cumulative weight.
- These 14 ideas become the **root-cause categories** for an **Ishikawa Fishbone**.
- The most critical 2–3 are then subjected to **5-Why analysis**, and the resulting measurable variables are tracked on an **X̄–R control chart** to confirm statistical significance before corrective action.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating brainstorming with brainwriting or NGT** — they are *related but distinct* techniques. Always state which one you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the post-brainstorming linkage** — KTU specifically wants to see brainstorming placed in the **DMAIC / PDCA / 7 QC tools** context. A standalone answer is incomplete.
> 3. **Skipping the role of the facilitator/scribe** — examiners explicitly test this; missing either costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit / rounding errors** in the 6-3-5 calculation — show the $\phi$ substitution step explicitly. Do not write $90 \times 0.25 = 68$ (this is a common slip).
> 5. **Treating brainstorming as statistical** — it is *qualitative*. Do not claim it produces statistically significant outputs on its own; the statistical work happens *downstream*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Brainstorming**

- **Founder:** Alex F. Osborn, **1939** (book: *Applied Imagination*, 1953).
- **Definition:** Group-based, no-criticism idea generation technique.
- **Team size:** **6–12 members**; duration **45–60 min**; target **50–100 ideas**.
- **Four Cardinal Rules (must memorize verbatim):**
  1. No criticism / defer judgment.
  2. Freewheeling is welcomed.
  3. Quantity is desired.
  4. Combination and improvement (piggybacking).
- **Two key roles:** *Facilitator* (rules + flow) and *Scribe* (verbatim recording).
- **Five major variants:** Structured, Unstructured, Silent (6-3-5), NGT, Electronic.
- **6-3-5 formula:** $T = 6 \times 3 \times 5 = 90$ ideas; unique $\approx 90 \times (1-\phi)$.
- **NGT voting:** Each member gets **3–5 votes** to rank ideas.
- **Pareto integration:** Top **20 %** of ideas drive **80 %** of improvement value.
- **Position in SQC workflow:** *Upstream qualitative tool* → feeds Ishikawa, Pareto, 5-Why → enables selection of measurable variables for *Control Charts* and *DOE*.
- **Position in Six Sigma:** *Define* and *Measure* phases of **DMAIC**.
- **Position in continuous improvement:** *Plan* phase of **PDCA**.
- **Psychological benefits:** Builds psychological safety, cross-functional bonding, ownership.
- **Main risks:** Production blocking, groupthink, dominance effect, social loafing — mitigated by skilled facilitator and structured variants.
- **Key link to statistics:** Brainstorming *scopes* what to measure; statistics then *validates* what to fix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DMAIC Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** DMAIC Cycle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **DMAIC** stands for **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control**. It is a **data-driven improvement cycle** used in **Six Sigma** methodology for improving, optimizing, and stabilizing business processes. The DMAIC framework is an integral part of the **Six Sigma Quality Management Philosophy** and forms the structural backbone of the *Improve* and *Control* modules of the **KTU 2024 Scheme – Statistical Quality Control (PEPET415)** syllabus. In the context of **KTU Statistical Quality Control**, the DMAIC cycle is defined as a **closed-loop, phased problem-solving methodology** in which each phase contains prescribed tools, statistical techniques, and exit criteria (often called *gate reviews* or *tollgate reviews*). It converts real-world operational problems into quantifiable statistical statements. The five sequential phases are: 1. **D – Define:** Identify the problem, the customer (Voice of Customer – *VOC*), and the project scope. 2. **M – Measure:** Quantify the current performance of the process using baseline data. 3. **A – Analyze:** Identify the root causes of defects and variation. 4. **I – Improve:** Implement solutions that address the root causes. 5. **C – Control:** Standardize the improved process to sustain the gains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DMAIC is not a one-time event — it is a **cyclic, iterative improvement loop**. Once Control is achieved, the cycle may restart at Define to drive *continuous improvement (Kaizen)*, eventually leading to higher sigma capability levels. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you visit a doctor with a persistent headache: | Doctor's Step | DMAIC Equivalent | What Happens | |---|---|---| | **Listen** to your symptoms | **Define** | Identify the problem and what "good" looks like | | **Run tests** (BP, MRI) | **Measure** | Collect data on current state | | **Diagnose** the cause | **Analyze** | Find the root cause | | **Prescribe** medicine / therapy | **Improve** | Apply and test a solution | | **Schedule follow-ups** | **Control** | Monitor to ensure the cure sticks | **Another Analogy — The GPS Navigation:** Think of DMAIC as a **GPS rerouting system** for a process that has gone off-course. - **Define** = You enter your destination (project goal). - **Measure** = The GPS shows your current location. - **Analyze** = The GPS finds why you are off-route (traffic, wrong turn). - **Improve** = The GPS suggests a better route. - **Control** = The GPS keeps recalculating until you reach the destination. ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants > [!NOTE] > The following metrics are central to evaluating any DMAIC project and are **frequently asked in KTU University Examinations**: > > - **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — the universal metric of Six Sigma. > - **σ (Sigma Level)** — measured in standard deviations between the mean and the nearest specification limit. > - **Yield (Y)** — the percentage of defect-free units produced. > - **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)** — the probability that a unit passes through all process steps defect-free. > - **Six Sigma Goal:** **3.4 DPMO** (i.e., **3.4 defects per million opportunities**), corresponding to **4.5σ** long-term capability. ## 1.4 Origin & Context in Six Sigma - DMAIC was popularized by **Motorola** in the 1980s and later institutionalized by **General Electric (GE)** under **Jack Welch** in 1995. - It is a refined version of the older **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle introduced by **Walter Shewhart** and later popularized by **W. Edwards Deming**. - DMAIC is **statistically heavier** than PDCA: every phase has formal statistical tools attached (control charts, hypothesis tests, DOE, regression, FMEA, etc.). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Phase-by-Phase Logical Breakdown ### 🔹 Phase 1 — DEFINE **Purpose:** Convert a vague business problem into a **structured project charter**. **Key Activities:** - Capture the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)**. - Develop a **Project Charter** (problem statement, scope, goal, timeline, team). - Create a **SIPOC Diagram** (Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers) — a high-level process map. - Identify **CTQs (Critical-to-Quality characteristics)** — the measurable features that customers care about. - Build a **High-Level Process Map** (also called a *Top-Down Flowchart*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exit Criterion of Define Phase:** A signed-off **Project Charter** and a clear, measurable problem statement (e.g., *"Reduce billing errors from 8 % to 2 % within 6 months"*). --- ### 🔹 Phase 2 — MEASURE **Purpose:** Quantify the **current state** of the process and establish a **baseline**. **Key Activities:** - Develop a **Data Collection Plan** (what, how, when, where, who). - Validate the **Measurement System** using **Gage R&R (Repeatability & Reproducibility)** studies. - Compute baseline metrics: **DPMO, Yield, Sigma Level, Defect Rate**. - Construct **Process Capability Indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$). - Construct **Run Charts** and initial **Control Charts** to assess process stability. **Statistical Tools Used:** - Descriptive statistics (mean, standard deviation, range). - Control Charts (X-bar, R, p, c, u charts — from **Module 1 & 2**). - Process Capability Analysis. - Measurement System Analysis (MSA). --- ### 🔹 Phase 3 — ANALYZE **Purpose:** Identify the **root causes** of defects and process variation. **Key Activities:** - Perform **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) Analysis**. - Conduct **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 rule) to prioritize the vital few causes. - Apply **Hypothesis Testing** (t-test, ANOVA, Chi-Square, proportion tests). - Use **Regression Analysis** and **Correlation Studies**. - Construct **Multi-Vari Charts** to identify the type of variation (positional, cyclical, temporal). - Perform **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** to rank potential failure modes by **RPN (Risk Priority Number)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exit Criterion of Analyze Phase:** A statistically validated list of **Vital Few Root Causes (Xs)** affecting the **CTQ (Y)**. --- ### 🔹 Phase 4 — IMPROVE **Purpose:** Develop, pilot, and implement **solutions** that eliminate or reduce the root causes. **Key Activities:** - **Generate Solutions** using brainstorming, **TRIZ**, or **Design of Experiments (DOE)**. - **Pilot / Test** solutions on a small scale. - Use **DOE** (Factorial, Fractional Factorial, Response Surface Methodology) to optimize settings. - Use **Lean Tools** (5S, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, Value Stream Mapping) to remove waste. - **Validate Improvement** by recomputing DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Level. - Perform **Cost–Benefit Analysis** to ensure financial viability. **Statistical Tools Used:** - Design of Experiments (DOE). - Regression & Correlation. - Optimization techniques (RSM). - Hypothesis testing to confirm the improvement is statistically significant. --- ### 🔹 Phase 5 — CONTROL **Purpose:** **Lock in** the gains and prevent regression to the old (poor) process performance. **Key Activities:** - **Standardize** the new process via Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Work Instructions, and Training. - Implement **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — install control charts on the Vital Few Xs and the CTQ Y. - Develop a **Control Plan** (similar to APQP / IATF 16949 format). - Create a **Reaction Plan** (what to do if the control chart signals an out-of-control condition). - Hand over to the **Process Owner**. - **Document Lessons Learned** and close the project. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exit Criterion of Control Phase:** A documented **Control Plan** with active SPC monitoring and a process capability of at least the target $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (or as per customer requirement). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **complete formula reference** for solving DMAIC-related numerical problems in KTU University Examinations. All equations are written using LaTeX and pipe symbols are replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` for markdown safety. | **#** | **Metric / Concept** | **Formula** | **Unit / Range** | **Used In Phase** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Defects per Unit (DPU) | $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | defects/unit | Measure, Analyze | | 2 | Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | defects/million opps | Measure, Improve | | 3 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units Passed First Time}}{\text{Total Units Entered}}$ | fraction or % | Measure | | 4 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \text{FPY}_{1} \times \text{FPY}_{2} \times \dots \times \text{FPY}_{n}$ | fraction | Measure, Improve | | 5 | Process Yield (Y) | $Y = 1 - \text{DPMO} \times 10^{-6}$ | fraction | Measure, Improve | | 6 | Sigma Level (using yield table) | $\sigma = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5$ (with 1.5σ shift) | dimensionless | Measure, Improve | | 7 | Process Capability ($C_p$) | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Measure, Control | | 8 | Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Measure, Control | | 9 | Defect Rate (%) | $\text{Defect \%} = \dfrac{\text{Defectives}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$ | percent | All | | 10 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | currency | Define, Improve | | 11 | RPN (FMEA) | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ (Severity × Occurrence × Detection) | integer 1–1000 | Analyze | | 12 | Sample Size for Proportion | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | integer | Measure | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The DMAIC framework is the **universal language of process improvement** in: - **Manufacturing** (automotive, semiconductor, pharmaceutical, aerospace). - **Service Industries** (banking, healthcare, IT, BPO, logistics). - **R&D and New Product Introduction** (via its cousin **DMADV** — Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify). - **Regulatory Compliance** (IATF 16949, ISO 9001, AS9100, Six Sigma Green/Black Belt certifications). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Note:** In **IATF 16949:2016 (automotive QMS)**, the DMAIC structure is embedded inside the **Control Plan**, making DMAIC literacy a **mandatory skill** for KTU Mechanical / Production / Automobile engineering graduates entering core manufacturing roles. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Pattern:** The most frequently asked calculation in KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) for this topic is the **DPMO → Yield → Sigma Level conversion** and the **RTY computation**. The full step-by-step derivations are shown below. ## 3.1 Derivation: From Defect Data to Sigma Level ### **Problem Context (KTU Standard Pattern):** > *A process produces 10,000 units. Each unit has 4 opportunities to be defective. A total of 120 defects were found during inspection. Compute the DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Level (assuming a 1.5σ long-term shift).* --- ### **Step 1 — Compute DPU (Defects Per Unit)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPU} &= \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \\[6pt] \text{DPU} &= \frac{120}{10{,}000} \\[6pt] \text{DPU} &= 0.012 \ \text{defects per unit} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** DPU is the *raw* defect intensity. It does not account for the *number of opportunities* per unit, so we cannot yet compare it to other processes. --- ### **Step 2 — Compute DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6} \\[6pt] \text{DPMO} &= \frac{120}{10{,}000 \times 4} \times 10^{6} \\[6pt] \text{DPMO} &= \frac{120}{40{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\[6pt] \text{DPMO} &= 0.003 \times 10^{6} \\[6pt] \text{DPMO} &= 3{,}000 \ \text{defects per million opportunities} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** DPMO normalizes the defect rate by the number of ways a unit can fail, allowing **fair comparison between processes of different complexity**. --- ### **Step 3 — Compute Process Yield (Y)** $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} \\[6pt] Y &= 1 - \frac{3{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[6pt] Y &= 1 - 0.003 \\[6pt] Y &= 0.997 \quad \text{or} \quad 99.7\,\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Yield is the *defect-free* fraction. At Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO), Yield ≈ **99.99966 %** — the famous "**Six Sigma Quality**" benchmark. --- ### **Step 4 — Convert Yield to Short-Term Sigma (Z)** The short-term Z-score is obtained from the standard normal table: $$ Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(Y) $$ For Y = 0.997, the standard normal table gives: $$ Z_{ST} = 2.967 \approx 2.97 $$ --- ### **Step 5 — Apply the 1.5σ Long-Term Shift (Motorola Convention)** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{\text{long-term}} &= Z_{ST} + 1.5 \\[6pt] \sigma_{\text{long-term}} &= 2.97 + 1.5 \\[6pt] \sigma_{\text{long-term}} &= 4.47 \ \sigma \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Motorola empirically observed that over time, process means drift by ~1.5σ. Hence the **industry-standard convention** is to add 1.5σ to convert short-term capability into long-term performance. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) ### **Problem Context:** > *A product passes through 4 sequential stations. The First Pass Yields are 0.95, 0.98, 0.92, and 0.99. Compute the Rolled Throughput Yield and the implied DPMO per station (assume 1 opportunity per station).* --- $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \text{FPY}_3 \times \text{FPY}_4 \\[6pt] \text{RTY} &= 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 \times 0.99 \\[6pt] \text{RTY} &= 0.8496 \quad \text{or} \quad 84.96\,\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** RTY captures the **multiplicative penalty** of process complexity. Even though each station has high yield, the cumulative effect drops the overall yield to ~85 %. --- ### **Step 2 — Defect Probability per Station** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defect prob. (Station 1)} &= 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 \\[6pt] \text{Defect prob. (Station 2)} &= 1 - 0.98 = 0.02 \\[6pt] \text{Defect prob. (Station 3)} &= 1 - 0.92 = 0.08 \\[6pt] \text{Defect prob. (Station 4)} &= 1 - 0.99 = 0.01 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Highest defect rate is at Station 3 (8 %) — this is the **Vital Few** candidate for the *Improve* phase of DMAIC. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Process Capability Indices ### **Given:** - USL = 24.0 mm, LSL = 16.0 mm - Process mean $\mu = 19.5$ mm - Standard deviation $\sigma = 1.0$ mm ### **Step 1 — Compute $C_p$ (Process Capability — Potential)** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\[6pt] C_p &= \frac{24.0 - 16.0}{6 \times 1.0} \\[6pt] C_p &= \frac{8.0}{6.0} \\[6pt] C_p &= 1.333 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** $C_p = 1.33$ means the **process spread is exactly 1.33× smaller** than the specification width — a common minimum industry target. --- ### **Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (Process Capability — Actual, Centering Considered)** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) \\[6pt] C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{24.0 - 19.5}{3 \times 1.0},\ \frac{19.5 - 16.0}{3 \times 1.0}\right) \\[6pt] C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{4.5}{3.0},\ \frac{3.5}{3.0}\right) \\[6pt] C_{pk} &= \min(1.50,\ 1.167) \\[6pt] C_{pk} &= 1.167 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** $C_{pk} < C_p$ because the process is **off-center** (μ = 19.5 is closer to LSL than USL). The bottleneck is the **lower-tail** capability. --- ### **Step 3 — Interpret for DMAIC Control Phase** | $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Status | DMAIC Action | |---|---|---| | $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | **Not Capable** | Stop production; go back to *Improve* | | $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginally Capable** | Tighten control; reduce variation | | $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | **Capable** | Maintain SPC; document Control Plan | | $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | **Highly Capable** | Best-in-class; consider Six Sigma target | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The diagrams below follow the **Mermaid v10+ syntax**, with strictly alphanumeric node IDs and no markdown formatting inside node labels (to ensure **clean compilation** in GitHub, VS Code, and KTU digital notes). ## 4.1 DMAIC Cyclic Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Project Kickoff]) --> nodeD nodeD["D - DEFINE
Problem Charter
VOC and CTQ
SIPOC Diagram
Project Scope"] nodeM["M - MEASURE
Data Collection Plan
Gage R&R Study
Baseline DPMO
Control Charts"] nodeA["A - ANALYZE
Ishikawa Diagram
Pareto Analysis
Hypothesis Tests
FMEA and RPN"] nodeI["I - IMPROVE
Design of Experiments
Pilot Solutions
Lean Tools
Validate Sigma Gain"] nodeC["C - CONTROL
Control Plan
SPC Monitoring
Reaction Plan
Process Owner Handover"] nodeGateD{{"Gate 1
Charter Sign-off"}} nodeGateM{{"Gate 2
Baseline Confirmed"}} nodeGateA{{"Gate 3
Root Cause Validated"}} nodeGateI{{"Gate 4
Improvement Proven"}} nodeGateC{{"Gate 5
Sustained Gains"}} nodeNext(["Next Cycle: Continuous Improvement"]) nodeD --> nodeGateD --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeGateM --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeGateA --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeGateI --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeGateC --> nodeNext nodeNext -.-> nodeD ``` ## 4.2 Tools-to-Phase Mapping Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeDefine["DEFINE PHASE"] nodeMeasure["MEASURE PHASE"] nodeAnalyze["ANALYZE PHASE"] nodeImprove["IMPROVE PHASE"] nodeControl["CONTROL PHASE"] nodeT1["Project Charter"] nodeT2["SIPOC Diagram"] nodeT3["VOC and CTQ Tree"] nodeT4["Gage R&R"] nodeT5["Control Charts"] nodeT6["Process Capability"] nodeT7["Ishikawa Fishbone"] nodeT8["Pareto Chart"] nodeT9["Hypothesis Testing"] nodeT10["Design of Experiments"] nodeT11["Regression Analysis"] nodeT12["Pilot Run"] nodeT13["Control Plan"] nodeT14["SPC Dashboard"] nodeT15["Reaction Plan"] nodeDefine --- nodeT1 nodeDefine --- nodeT2 nodeDefine --- nodeT3 nodeMeasure --- nodeT4 nodeMeasure --- nodeT5 nodeMeasure --- nodeT6 nodeAnalyze --- nodeT7 nodeAnalyze --- nodeT8 nodeAnalyze --- nodeT9 nodeImprove --- nodeT10 nodeImprove --- nodeT11 nodeImprove --- nodeT12 nodeControl --- nodeT13 nodeControl --- nodeT14 nodeControl --- nodeT15 ``` ## 4.3 DPMO → Sigma Level Conversion Logic (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeInput["Input: Defect Count,
Units, Opportunities"] nodeDPU["Compute DPU
= Defects / Units"] nodeDPMO["Compute DPMO
= DPU / Opps x 10^6"] nodeYield["Compute Yield Y
= 1 - DPMO/10^6"] nodeZST["Look up Z short term
from Normal Table"] nodeShift["Add 1.5 sigma shift
(Motorola convention)"] nodeSigma["Output: Long Term Sigma Level"] nodeInput --> nodeDPU --> nodeDPMO --> nodeYield --> nodeZST --> nodeShift --> nodeSigma ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the DMAIC cycle and list its five phases. Why is it called a "closed-loop" methodology?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > DMAIC stands for **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control**. It is a structured, data-driven problem-solving methodology used in Six Sigma to improve processes. > > The five phases are: > 1. **Define** – Identify the problem, customer (VOC), and project scope. > 2. **Measure** – Quantify the current process performance. > 3. **Analyze** – Identify root causes of defects and variation. > 4. **Improve** – Implement and validate solutions. > 5. **Control** – Standardize the gains using SPC and control plans. > > It is called a **closed-loop** methodology because the *Control* phase feeds back into *Define* for the next improvement cycle, ensuring **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** rather than a one-time fix. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between DPMO and Yield. How are they related mathematically?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is a *normalized* defect rate that allows comparison between processes of different complexity. > - **Yield** is the *percentage* of defect-free units (or opportunities) produced by the process. > > The mathematical relationship is: > > $$Y = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}$$ > > Equivalently, $\text{DPMO} = (1 - Y) \times 10^{6}$. DPMO is preferred in Six Sigma because it accounts for the *number of opportunities* per unit, whereas Yield alone can be misleading. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Many students lose **2–3 marks** in Part B by: > (a) Skipping the **logical justification** for choosing a particular tool — *always state "why" you are using a tool, not just "what"*. > (b) Forgetting the **1.5σ shift** when computing long-term sigma level. > (c) Not showing the **unit consistency** (defects vs. defectives; per-unit vs. per-opportunity). > (d) Confusing **$C_p$** (potential capability) with **$C_{pk}$** (actual capability) — they are *not* the same when the process is off-center. --- ### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]** > > *A manufacturing process produces **5,000 units per day**. Each unit has **6 opportunities** to be defective. During a 5-day audit, the following defects were recorded: Day 1 = 35, Day 2 = 40, Day 3 = 30, Day 4 = 45, Day 5 = 50.* > > **(a)** Compute the **DPU, DPMO, and Process Yield** for the process. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Determine the **long-term Sigma Level** (assume 1.5σ shift) and recommend the **appropriate DMAIC phase action**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Total Defects and Total Units** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Defects} &= 35 + 40 + 30 + 45 + 50 = 200 \ \text{defects} \\[6pt] \text{Total Units} &= 5{,}000 \times 5 = 25{,}000 \ \text{units} \\[6pt] \text{Opportunities per Unit} &= 6 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute DPU (Defects Per Unit)** $$ \text{DPU} = \frac{200}{25{,}000} = 0.008 \ \text{defects per unit} $$ **Step 3: Compute DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** $$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{200}{25{,}000 \times 6} \times 10^{6} = \frac{200}{150{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 1{,}333.33 $$ **Step 4: Compute Process Yield** $$ Y = 1 - \frac{1{,}333.33}{10^{6}} = 1 - 0.001333 = 0.998667 = 99.8667\,\% $$ > **Marks Allocation:** Total Defects (1), DPU (2), DPMO (2), Yield (2). --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Look up Short-Term Sigma from Yield Table** For $Y = 0.998667$, the corresponding short-term Z (from standard normal table) is: $$ Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(0.998667) \approx 3.00 $$ **Step 2: Apply the 1.5σ Long-Term Shift** $$ \sigma_{\text{long-term}} = 3.00 + 1.5 = 4.50 \ \sigma $$ **Step 3: DMAIC Recommendation** | Sigma Level | Industry Interpretation | Recommended Action | |---|---|---| | 4.5σ | World-class (between 4σ and 5σ) | Tighten control; aim for 5σ+ via Improve phase | > **Recommendation:** The process is in the **Improve phase** — apply **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to reduce variation and pilot a **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** device to bring sigma level toward 5σ. Establish **SPC control charts** in the Control phase to sustain the gain. > **Marks Allocation:** Z-lookup (2), 1.5σ shift (2), Interpretation (1), DMAIC action (2). --- ### **🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]** > > *(a)* Describe the **five phases of DMAIC** with the **statistical tools** used in each phase. **(7 marks)** > *(b)* A product passes through **5 sequential stations** with First Pass Yields of **0.97, 0.93, 0.95, 0.99, and 0.90**. Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and identify the **station needing immediate DMAIC intervention**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks** | Phase | Purpose | Key Statistical / Quality Tools | |---|---|---| | **Define** | Identify problem, customer, and project scope | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree, Kano Model | | **Measure** | Establish baseline performance | Control Charts (X-bar, R, p, c), Gage R&R, Process Capability ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$), DPMO baseline | | **Analyze** | Identify root causes | Ishikawa (Fishbone), Pareto, 5-Whys, Hypothesis Testing, Regression, FMEA | | **Improve** | Implement and validate solutions | Design of Experiments (DOE), Response Surface Methodology, Pilot Studies, Lean Tools (5S, Poka-Yoke) | | **Control** | Sustain the improvements | Control Plan, SPC charts, Reaction Plan, Mistake-Proofing, Documentation | > **Marks Allocation:** 1 mark per phase purpose + 0.4 marks per correct tool (≈ 5 phases × 1.4 = 7). --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Compute RTY** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \text{FPY}_3 \times \text{FPY}_4 \times \text{FPY}_5 \\[6pt] \text{RTY} &= 0.97 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \times 0.90 \\[6pt] \text{RTY} &= 0.7660 \quad \text{or} \quad 76.60\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Identify the Defect Probability per Station** | Station | FPY | Defect Probability (1 − FPY) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.97 | 0.03 | | 2 | 0.93 | 0.07 | | 3 | 0.95 | 0.05 | | 4 | 0.99 | 0.01 | | 5 | 0.90 | **0.10 ← Highest** | **Step 3: Conclusion** > Station 5 has the **highest defect rate (10 %)** and is the **Vital Few** root cause. The DMAIC recommendation is to **immediately enter the Improve phase for Station 5** — conduct root cause analysis (Ishikawa + 5-Whys), apply **Poka-Yoke**, run **DOE** to optimize settings, and then lock in via a **Control Plan**. > **Marks Allocation:** RTY formula (2), calculation (2), defect probabilities (1), identification of Station 5 + DMAIC action (2). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 High-Yield Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:** - **DMAIC = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control.** Mnemonic: *D-M-A-I-C = "Do Measure Analyze Improve Control"*. - **DPMO** normalizes defects by *opportunities*; **Yield** does not. - The **1.5σ shift** is the **Motorola convention** for converting short-term to long-term sigma — **always add it** unless the question explicitly says "short-term". - **Six Sigma goal:** **3.4 DPMO ≈ 4.5σ long-term ≈ 6σ short-term ≈ 99.99966 % Yield**. - **$C_p \geq C_{pk}$ always**; $C_p = C_{pk}$ only when the process is perfectly centered ($\mu = $ midpoint of spec). - **RTY** is *multiplicative* — every additional station multiplies the cumulative yield, making long production lines extremely sensitive to low station yields. - The **Vital Few (Pareto)** drives the **Improve** phase — focus on the 20 % of causes that create 80 % of defects. - **FMEA RPN = S × O × D** — prioritize fixes with the **highest RPN**. - **Control phase tools:** Control Charts, Reaction Plans, SOPs, Training, Visual Management. - The cycle is **iterative and continuous** — once Control is achieved, restart at Define (this is **Kaizen** in DMAIC language). - **SIPOC** is a *Define*-phase tool; **Fishbone** is an *Analyze*-phase tool; **DOE** is an *Improve*-phase tool; **Control Plan** is a *Control*-phase tool. - **Gage R&R** is a *Measure*-phase prerequisite — **never skip** measurement system validation before baseline data collection.

topics.title: Poka-Yoke
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Poka-Yoke # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Poka-Yoke ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Poka-Yoke** (ポカヨケ), pronounced *POH-kah YOH-keh*, is a Japanese quality engineering term coined by **Shigeo Shingo** (an industrial engineer at Toyota) in the 1960s. The literal translation is **"mistake-proofing"** or **"error-proofing"**, derived from *poka* (inadvertent mistake) and *yoke* (to prevent or avoid). In KTU 2024 Scheme SQC terminology, Poka-Yoke is formally defined as: > A **lean manufacturing / quality engineering technique** designed to **eliminate human errors** by designing the process, equipment, or tooling in such a way that: > (i) It is **impossible** to commit an error, OR > (ii) The error is **detected immediately** at the source before it propagates into a defect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core KTU Definition to Memorise:** > "Poka-Yoke is any mechanism in a lean manufacturing process that prevents incorrect operations by the operator from occurring, or immediately flags an error so that corrective action can be taken before the part moves further downstream." — Adapted from Shigeo Shingo's *Zero Quality Control: Source Inspection and the Poka-Yoke System* (1986). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are inserting a **USB cable into your laptop in the dark**. Even without seeing, you instinctively know it will only fit **one way** — the trapezoidal slot physically rejects any wrong orientation. The moment you flip it correctly, it slides in smoothly. This "you cannot mess it up" design is a perfect everyday example of **Poka-Yoke**. Another analogy: A **SIM card tray** in a smartphone. The tray is shaped so the SIM card can only be placed in one specific orientation; any other way simply does not fit. If you push too hard the wrong way, the tray won't even close. The system **guides** you to do it right and **blocks** you from doing it wrong. > [!NOTE] > **The Big Idea (First-Read Friendly):** > Traditional quality control **inspects** defects **after** they are produced (reactive). Poka-Yoke **prevents** defects from being produced in the first place (proactive). Instead of finding the bad part, it makes the bad part **physically impossible** to create. ## 1.3 Origin and Historical Context - **Year of Inception:** **1961** — Shigeo Shingo designed the first Poka-Yoke system at the **Yamaha plant** in Hamamatsu, Japan. - **Original Name:** The term was originally *"Baka-Yoke"* (idiot-proofing), but was later softened to **"Poka-Yoke"** (mistake-proofing) for cultural sensitivity. - **Toyota Adoption:** Toyota integrated Poka-Yoke as a pillar of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**. It complements **Jidoka** (automation with a human touch) and **Kaizen** (continuous improvement). - **Statistical Significance:** Enables **100% inspection** rather than **sampling inspection**, since the check is mechanical/inherent in the process itself. > [!TIP] > **Why KTU Asks This:** Poka-Yoke bridges *quality management philosophy* (Module 1–2 of SQC) with *practical process engineering*. It is one of the highest-weightage topics in Module 2 because questions test both **conceptual understanding** and **applied design thinking**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Strategic Categories of Poka-Yoke Poka-Yoke mechanisms are classified into **two strategic categories** based on **when** they act in the error lifecycle: ### 2.1.1 Prevention (Type 1) Poka-Yoke The mechanism makes it **physically impossible** for the error to occur in the first place. | Aspect | Description | |---|---| | **Philosophy** | "Make the mistake impossible." | | **Cost** | Higher upfront design cost, near-zero running cost. | | **Reliability** | Very high (99.99% effectiveness in well-designed systems). | | **Example** | Asymmetric USB connector; car key that only fits one ignition slot; specific-shape bolts. | ### 2.1.2 Detection (Type 2) Poka-Yoke The mechanism **allows** the mistake to occur momentarily but **detects and signals it immediately** so the operator can correct it before defect propagation. | Aspect | Description | |---|---| | **Philosophy** | "Catch the mistake the instant it is made." | | **Cost** | Lower design cost, moderate running cost (sensors, alarms). | | **Reliability** | High, but depends on operator response. | | **Example** | Car dashboard beep when seatbelt not fastened; dashboard warning light for low fuel; missing-component sensor on a conveyor. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** > Prevention > Detection. Prevention eliminates the error class entirely, while detection only flags individual occurrences. In KTU 14-mark answers, always **justify** which category you would prefer and why. ## 2.2 The Three Functional Methods of Poka-Yoke Shigeo Shingo classified the **physical implementation methods** into three families: ### 2.2.1 Contact Method - A physical **sensor, pin, or shape** is touched or engaged by the part. - **Example:** A pin on a fixture that physically protrudes if a part is missing; pressing a part completes a microswitch circuit. - **KTU Use Case:** In machining fixtures, a missing bolt means the locator pin cannot descend, jamming the cycle. ### 2.2.2 Fixed-Value (Counting) Method - The process counts **how many** operations have been performed (e.g., screws tightened, parts placed). - If the count does not match the expected value (e.g., **4 screws = OK**, **3 screws = ALARM**), the process **auto-stops**. - **Example:** A torque wrench that only releases when **exactly N rotations** are completed. An automated screw-driving system that fires exactly 4 screws per cycle. ### 2.2.3 Motion-Step (Sequence) Method - The mechanism enforces a **specific order** of operations. - **Example:** A car that will not start unless the gear is in **Park** AND the brake pedal is pressed. A copier that will not scan until the lid is fully closed. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet Although Poka-Yoke is qualitative, the following **decision matrix and equations** are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme questions: ### 2.3.1 Quality Cost Trade-Off $$ C_{total} \;=\; C_{prevention} \;+\; C_{appraisal} \;+\; C_{internal\ failure} \;+\; C_{external\ failure} $$ Poka-Yoke primarily **reduces** $C_{internal\ failure}$ and $C_{external\ failure}$ dramatically. The payback period: $$ \text{Payback Period (months)} \;=\; \frac{C_{Poka\text{-}Yoke\ implementation}}{C_{savings\ per\ month}} $$ ### 2.3.2 Inspection Effectiveness Comparison | Inspection Type | Coverage | Error Detection Rate | KTU Notation | |---|---|---|---| | **No Inspection** | **0%** | $\approx 0\%$ | Acceptable only for non-critical features | | **Sampling Inspection** | **5–10%** of lots | $\approx 85\%$ (typical AOQL) | Acceptable Quality Level (AQL) | | **100% Manual Inspection** | **100%** | $\approx 70\text{–}80\%$ (human fatigue) | Costly, error-prone | | **Poka-Yoke (Automated)** | **100%** | $\approx 99.99\%$ | Lowest long-run cost | > [!NOTE] > **Sampling vs Poka-Yoke — Important:** > Sampling Inspection (Modules 3 & 4) accepts a **small probability of accepting bad lots (β risk)** and **rejecting good lots (α risk)**. Poka-Yoke eliminates **both risks simultaneously** because each individual part is verified. ### 2.3.3 Classification Cheat-Sheet Table | Parameter | Prevention Poka-Yoke | Detection Poka-Yoke | |---|---|---| | **Action Trigger** | Before mistake occurs | After mistake occurs | | **Signal Type** | None (silence = OK) | Alarm / Light / Stop | | **Operator Skill Needed** | Low | Moderate (must respond to signal) | | **Best Suited For** | High-volume, dangerous, irreversible errors | Variable errors, complex assemblies | | **KTU Word Limit Hint** | Use for "design a fixture" type questions | Use for "add a sensor" type questions | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Poka-Yoke Example | |---|---| | **Automotive** | Gear-shift interlock — car cannot start unless in Park/Neutral. | | **Electronics** | Trapezoidal USB-A connector; keyed M.2 SSD slot on motherboards. | | **Aviation** | Cockpit control yokes shaped differently for left vs right hand. | | **Pharmaceuticals** | Pill bottle caps requiring push-down-and-turn (child-proof). | | **Aviation Assembly** | Missed-rivet detector; pins that fit only when all holes are aligned. | | **Medical Devices** | IV lines with unique male-female connectors (no cross-mixing). | | **Software/UI** | "Are you sure?" dialog box before destructive operations. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Implementation Procedure ## 3.1 The 5-Step Poka-Yoke Implementation Roadmap (KTU Board-Examiner Favourite) When a 14-mark KTU question asks *"Design a Poka-Yoke system for ..."*, examiners expect the following **five-step analytical structure**. Each step is described in exhaustive detail below. ### Step 1 — Identify the Operation and Potential Errors Begin by selecting the **specific operation** in the process where defects originate. Then enumerate **every possible error mode** the operator or machine could commit. For example, in a *4-screw tightening operation on a laptop chassis*: - Possible errors: forgetting a screw, over-tightening, under-tightening, wrong screw size, wrong orientation, missing washer. Document this in an **Error Mode Matrix**: | Error ID | Error Description | Consequence | Current Detection? | |---|---|---|---| | E1 | Missing screw 1 | Structural failure | None | | E2 | Missing screw 2 | Structural failure | None | | E3 | Over-tightened | Stripped threads | None | | E4 | Wrong screw | Rattle / breakage | None | | E5 | Missing washer | Leakage (if sealing) | None | ### Step 2 — Determine the Root Cause (5-Why Analysis) Apply **5-Why analysis** (from Toyota Production System) to each error. **Example for E1 (Missing screw 1):** - *Why 1:* Screw was not placed. → *Why 2:* Operator forgot. → *Why 3:* Operator was distracted. → *Why 4:* Four screws look identical; operator cannot tell when all are done. → *Why 5:* **There is no visual or physical cue that all four screws are placed.** **Root Cause:** No feedback mechanism at the operation level. ### Step 3 — Design the Poka-Yoke Device Choose between **Prevention** or **Detection**, and between the **three functional methods**: **Chosen Design:** *Detection-type, Fixed-Value Counting Method* - Install an **automatic screw counter** wired in series with the assembly fixture. - Counter increments **+1** each time the torque-controlled screwdriver completes a screw. - When counter reaches **exactly 4**, a green LED illuminates and the fixture unlocks. - If counter ≠ 4 after a fixed **cycle timeout (e.g., 60 seconds)**, a red LED + buzzer activates and the conveyor stops. ### Step 4 — Test, Validate, and Iterate - **Pilot Run:** Run 200 cycles. Record all error events. - **Validation Metrics to Record:** $$ \text{Effectiveness } E \;=\; \frac{\text{Errors Detected by Poka-Yoke}}{\text{Total Errors Made}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{False Alarm Rate } F \;=\; \frac{\text{False Alarms}}{\text{Total Operations}} \times 100\% $$ - **Target Effectiveness:** $E \geq 99.9\%$. - **Target False Alarm Rate:** $F \leq 0.1\%$. If $E < 99.9\%$, return to Step 3 and **redesign** (e.g., add a photo-electric sensor to physically detect screw head presence). ### Step 5 — Standardise and Scale - Document the procedure in the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)**. - Train all operators. - Roll out to all similar stations (Kaizen horizontal deployment). - Monitor for **Six Months** and compute actual cost savings: $$ \text{Monthly Savings} \;=\; (C_{scrap,before} - C_{scrap,after}) + (C_{rework,before} - C_{rework,after}) + (C_{warranty,before} - C_{warranty,after}) $$ $$ \text{ROI (\%)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Annual Savings} - \text{Annual Poka-Yoke Cost}}{\text{Implementation Cost}} \times 100 $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Distinction: Poka-Yoke vs SPC vs Sampling Inspection This is the **most frequently asked KTU comparison** in Module 2: | Criterion | Poka-Yoke | SPC Control Charts | Acceptance Sampling | |---|---|---|---| | **Approach** | Mistake prevention | Process variation monitoring | Lot-by-lot inspection | | **Coverage** | **100%** of units | Process-level (not unit-level) | **Sample** of lot | | **Stage** | **During** operation | **After** operation (real-time) | **After** lot production | | **Error Type** | Human errors | Process shifts / drifts | Lot-level defectives | | **Reaction Time** | Instantaneous | Real-time (chart) | Delayed (after sampling) | | **Best Module in KTU** | Module 2 | Modules 3 & 4 | Module 5 | | **Cost per Unit** | Very low (after setup) | Low | High (labour intensive) | | **Philosophy** | "Right the first time, every time" | "Listen to the process" | "Verify the outcome" | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ROI Justification **Problem:** A factory produces **10,000 laptops/month**. Before Poka-Yoke, the missing-screw defect rate is **2%** (200 defective units/month). Each defect costs **₹500** in rework. A Poka-Yoke counter system costs **₹3,00,000 to install** and **₹20,000/month** to maintain. **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:** $$ \text{Defects before} = 0.02 \times 10{,}000 = 200 \text{ units/month} $$ $$ \text{Monthly rework cost before} = 200 \times 500 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 $$ After installing the Poka-Yoke system, defects drop to **0.02%** (a 99% reduction, conservative estimate): $$ \text{Defects after} = 0.0002 \times 10{,}000 = 2 \text{ units/month} $$ $$ \text{Monthly rework cost after} = 2 \times 500 = \text{₹}1{,}000 $$ $$ \text{Monthly savings} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 - \text{₹}1{,}000 - \text{₹}20{,}000 = \text{₹}79{,}000 $$ $$ \text{Payback Period} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{79{,}000} \approx 3.8 \text{ months} $$ $$ \text{Annual ROI} = \frac{(79{,}000 \times 12) - (20{,}000 \times 12)}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{9{,}48{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Annual ROI} \approx 236\%} $$ **Interpretation:** The system pays for itself in under 4 months and yields an annual ROI of **236%**, justifying implementation under any standard capital budgeting criterion. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Poka-Yoke Decision Flow Diagram The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **decision logic** of an integrated Poka-Yoke system (prevention + detection layers) inside a typical assembly station. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Operation] --> B{Part Loaded Correctly?} B -- No --> C[Prevention Layer: Asymmetric Fixture Blocks Loading] C --> C1[Operator Cannot Proceed] C1 --> A B -- Yes --> D[Step 1: Action A] D --> E{Counter Increments Correctly?} E -- No --> F[Detection Layer: Red LED + Buzzer] F --> F1[Cycle Auto-Stop] F1 --> F2[Operator Corrects Mistake] F2 --> D E -- Yes --> G[Step 2: Action B] G --> H{Sequence Order Followed?} H -- No --> I[Detection Layer: Sequence Sensor Trips] I --> I1[Alarm + Lockout] I1 --> G H -- Yes --> J{All Steps Complete?} J -- No --> D J -- Yes --> K[Detection Layer: Green LED] K --> L[Fixture Unlocks] L --> M[Part Ejects to Next Station] M --> N[End] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a Poka-Yoke system, broken into its hardware, sensing, control, and feedback subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"] A1[Operator Action] A2[Material Feed] A3[Machine Cycle Signal] end subgraph SENSE["Sensing Layer"] B1[Contact Sensors
Microswitches, Pins] B2[Counting Sensors
Photoelectric, Encoders] B3[Sequence Sensors
Limit Switches, PLC Tags] end subgraph CONTROL["Control Layer - PLC or Microcontroller"] C1{Logic Comparator} C2[Expected State Memory] C3[Threshold Logic
e.g., count equals 4] end subgraph OUTPUT["Feedback Layer"] D1[Green LED - OK] D2[Red LED - Error] D3[Buzzer - Audible Alarm] D4[Conveyor Stop Signal] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B2 A3 --> B3 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 C2 --> C1 C1 --> C3 C3 -- Match --> D1 C3 -- Mismatch --> D2 C3 -- Mismatch --> D3 C3 -- Mismatch --> D4 ``` ## 4.3 Poka-Yoke Hierarchy — When to Choose What ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Identify Error Mode] --> Q1{Is the error due to wrong SEQUENCE?} Q1 -- Yes --> Seq[Motion-Step Method
e.g., Process Sequence Lock] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the error due to wrong COUNT or QUANTITY?} Q2 -- Yes --> Count[Fixed-Value Method
e.g., Counter Sensor] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the error due to wrong PRESENCE or POSITION?} Q3 -- Yes --> Contact[Contact Method
e.g., Microswitch / Pin Sensor] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is the operation hazardous or high-volume?} Q4 -- Yes --> Prevent[Prevention Poka-Yoke
Redesign the Tooling] Q4 -- No --> Detect[Detection Poka-Yoke
Sensor + Alarm] ``` > [!TIP] > **How to Use These Diagrams in KTU Answers:** > Sketch these flows **by hand** during the exam. Examiners award bonus marks for **clear, labelled diagrams** even if the question does not explicitly ask for one. Use boxes and arrows; label all decision diamonds and feedback loops. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define Poka-Yoke. Who developed it and what is its primary objective?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Poka-Yoke, coined by **Shigeo Shingo** in the 1960s, is a Japanese quality engineering technique meaning **"mistake-proofing"**. It is a mechanism designed to either prevent errors from occurring or detect them immediately at the source before they become defects. - **Developer (1 mark):** **Shigeo Shingo**, while working at Toyota and Yamaha in Japan. - **Objective (1 mark):** To achieve **zero defects** by enabling **100% inspection** at the operation level and eliminating the possibility of human or process errors propagating downstream. ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Distinguish between Prevention and Detection types of Poka-Yoke with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Prevention Poka-Yoke (1.5 marks):** A mechanism that makes the error **physically impossible**. The system is redesigned so that the wrong action cannot be initiated. *Example:* A USB Type-A connector that physically fits only one way; car ignition that only accepts the correct coded key. - **Detection Poka-Yoke (1.5 marks):** A mechanism that allows the mistake to happen momentarily but **immediately signals** the error so the operator can correct it. *Example:* A dashboard beep when the seatbelt is not fastened; a missing-component sensor that stops the conveyor and lights a red LED. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Q: (a)** Explain the **three functional methods** of Poka-Yoke with a suitable industrial example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **role of Poka-Yoke in achieving Zero Quality Control (ZQC)** and compare it with **Acceptance Sampling** as an inspection strategy. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer to (a): **Three Functional Methods of Poka-Yoke (7 marks):** **1. Contact Method (2 marks):** In this method, a part, pin, or sensor physically contacts the workpiece, fixture, or tool. If the contact condition is satisfied, the process continues; if not, the cycle is interrupted. *Industrial Example:* In a machining fixture, a **locator pin** is designed to descend only if all clamps engage correctly. If a part is missing or misaligned, the pin cannot enter the locating hole, and the machine cycle auto-aborts. A microswitch wired in series confirms the pin position before permitting spindle rotation. **2. Fixed-Value (Counting) Method (2 marks):** The process is instrumented to **count** a fixed number of operations — for example, the number of screws tightened, the number of rivets placed, or the number of tablets filled into a bottle. *Industrial Example:* On a motorcycle assembly line, an **automatic screw-driving system** tightens exactly **6 bolts** on the engine cover. The PLC counter must read exactly 6 for the green "OK" signal. If the count reads 5 or 7, the conveyor stops, a red beacon illuminates, and an operator must intervene. **3. Motion-Step (Sequence) Method (2 marks):** This method enforces a **mandatory sequence** of operations. The next operation is locked until the previous one is verified complete. *Industrial Example:* In a CNC machining cell, the spindle cannot start until the **chuck-close limit switch** AND the **guard-door limit switch** are both actuated. If the operator opens the door mid-cycle, the spindle stops instantly (interlock). On a passenger car, the engine cannot start unless the **gear is in Park** AND the **brake pedal is pressed** — this is a sequence-enforced Poka-Yoke. **Conclusion (1 mark):** All three methods aim for **100% inspection** at the operation level, ensuring that any error — be it contact-related, count-related, or sequence-related — is caught **before** the part moves to the next station, thereby upholding the zero-defect objective. --- #### Model Answer to (b): **Role of Poka-Yoke in Zero Quality Control (4 marks):** **Zero Quality Control (ZQC)**, conceptualised by Shigeo Shingo, is the philosophy that defects must be **prevented at the source** rather than discovered after the fact. Poka-Yoke is the **operational mechanism** that enables ZQC by: - **(i) Source Inspection (1 mark):** Inspecting every part **at the workstation where it is produced**, not at a downstream quality lab. The operator becomes the inspector, with the Poka-Yoke device acting as the verification tool. - **(ii) 100% Inspection (1 mark):** Unlike sampling inspection, every single unit is verified, eliminating the Producer's Risk (α) and Consumer's Risk (β). - **(iii) Immediate Feedback (1 mark):** The feedback loop between error and correction is **zero delay**, so errors cannot accumulate. - **(iv) Cause-Based Correction (1 mark):** When a Poka-Yoke fires, the underlying process cause is investigated via 5-Why analysis and **permanently eliminated** — aligning with **Kaizen** philosophy. **Comparison with Acceptance Sampling (3 marks):** | Parameter | Poka-Yoke | Acceptance Sampling | |---|---|---| | Inspection Coverage | 100% of units | Sample of lot (typically 5–10%) | | Timing | During operation | After lot completion | | Cost per Unit | Low (after setup) | High (labour intensive) | | Risk of Defect Escape | Essentially zero | α and β risks exist | | Feedback Speed | Real-time | Delayed (hours/days) | | Suitability | High-volume, repetitive | Low-volume, mixed-product | **Conclusion (within allocated marks):** Poka-Yoke is fundamentally **superior** to acceptance sampling for **in-process control** of high-volume operations because it provides 100% coverage at near-zero marginal cost. Acceptance sampling is reserved for situations where 100% inspection is **physically impossible or economically unjustified** (e.g., destructive testing). --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Q: (a)** With the help of a **flowchart**, describe the **step-by-step procedure for implementing a Poka-Yoke system** in a manufacturing plant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A factory produces **8,000 mobile phone covers per day**. Currently, **3%** of covers are rejected because the SIM tray is inserted in the wrong orientation. Each rejection costs **₹80** in rework. Design a Poka-Yoke system and calculate the **payback period** if the Poka-Yoke fixture costs **₹4,00,000** to install and **₹15,000/day** to operate. Assume the Poka-Yoke reduces rejections to **0.05%**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer to (a): **Step-by-Step Poka-Yoke Implementation Procedure (with flowchart, 7 marks):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 1: Identify Operation │ │ & Potential Errors │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 2: 5-Why Root-Cause │ │ Analysis │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 3: Choose Method │ │ (Contact / Count / Seq.) │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 4: Design the Poka- │ │ Yoke Device (Hardware + │ │ PLC / Sensor logic) │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 5: Pilot Test & │ │ Validate (Effectiveness ≥ │ │ 99.9%, False Alarm ≤ 0.1%) │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 6: Standardise SOP & │ │ Train Operators │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 7: Scale Across Plant │ │ (Kaizen Horizontal Deploy) │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ STEP 8: Monitor ROI & │ │ Iterate │ └─────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Step-wise Explanation:** 1. **Identify Operation and Errors (1 mark):** Select the specific operation (e.g., SIM tray insertion).
