- **Hospital Operating Theatres:** Adapted as **5S-OR** (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) to reduce surgical instrument misplacement and OR turnaround time.
- **Software Development (Kanban boards, clean repos):** The *Sort* and *Set in Order* principles are mirrored in version control hygiene.
- **KTU SQC Context:** 5S is a *prerequisite* for valid $\bar{X}$-R chart data collection, because uncontrolled floor noise introduces *non-random patterns* (e.g., tool-wear trends mistakenly attributed to raw-material variation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 5S Audit Scoring Workflow

The scoring of a 5S audit follows a structured, auditable procedure. We derive the standard step-by-step process used in industry and frequently referenced in KTU-style problem statements.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Audit

A standard 5S audit evaluates a work cell against a **checklist of N checkpoints**, each assigned a binary pass/fail or a 0–5 Likert rating. For an unweighted composite score:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{N_i} s_{ij} \right)
$$

where:

- $i$ indexes the five pillars (1 = Seiri, …, 5 = Shitsuke).
- $j$ indexes the checkpoints within pillar $i$.
- $s_{ij}$ is the score of checkpoint $j$ under pillar $i$.
- $N_i$ is the number of checkpoints for pillar $i$.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A 5S audit is conducted on a CNC machining cell. The five pillars have the following number of checkpoints and earned points (each checkpoint is worth up to 10 points):
>
> | Pillar | Checkpoints ($N_i$) | Total Earned |
> |--------|---------------------|--------------|
> | Seiri | 8 | 65 |
> | Seiton | 10 | 78 |
> | Seiso | 6 | 50 |
> | Seiketsu | 4 | 28 |
> | Shitsuke | 5 | 32 |
>
> Compute: (a) the unweighted total 5S audit score, (b) the maximum possible score, (c) the overall compliance percentage, and (d) interpret the result.

#### Part (a) — Unweighted Total Score

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 65 + 78 + 50 + 28 + 32
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 253
$$

#### Part (b) — Maximum Possible Score

$$
S_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (N_i \times 10) = 10 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{5} N_i
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} N_i = 8 + 10 + 6 + 4 + 5 = 33
$$

$$
S_{\max} = 10 \times 33 = 330
$$

#### Part (c) — Overall Compliance Percentage

$$
C_{\%} = \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{S_{\max}} \times 100
$$

$$
C_{\%} = \frac{253}{330} \times 100
$$

$$
C_{\%} = 0.7666\ldots \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\%} \approx 76.67\%}
$$

#### Part (d) — Interpretation

| Compliance Range | Rating | Action |
|------------------|--------|--------|
| $\ge 90\%$ | World-class (5S mature) | Sustain & mentor others |
| $75\% \le C_{\%} < 90\%$ | Good, with gaps | Targeted CAPA on weak pillars |
| $50\% \le C_{\%} < 75\%$ | Developing | Re-training + re-audit in 30 days |
| $< 50\%$ | Unacceptable | Full 5S re-implementation |

At $76.67\%$, the cell is in the **"Good, with gaps"** band. The auditor should now examine *which* pillars under-performed to direct **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)**.

---

### 3.1.3 Pillar-Wise Compliance Breakdown

For the same cell:

$$
C_{\text{Seiri}} = \frac{65}{8 \times 10} \times 100 = \frac{65}{80} \times 100 = 81.25\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Seiton}} = \frac{78}{100} \times 100 = 78.00\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Seiso}} = \frac{50}{60} \times 100 = 83.33\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Seiketsu}} = \frac{28}{40} \times 100 = 70.00\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shitsuke}} = \frac{32}{50} \times 100 = 64.00\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Insight:** The weakest pillar is **Shitsuke (64%)** — the cultural, sustainment pillar. This is the most common pattern in industry: the first three S's (physical) are easier to fix than the last two (behavioral). The CAPA must therefore focus on **management commitment, audit cadence, and recognition systems**, not on more physical reorganization.

---

## 3.2 The 5S Maturity Model (Analytical Matrix)

A continuous-improvement roadmap that aligns with the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle:

| Stage | Phase Name | Focus | SQC Connection |
|-------|------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | **Chaos** | No 5S, no baselines | Data collection is unreliable |
| 2 | **Initial Sort** | Seiri deployed | Waste physically removed |
| 3 | **Order & Shine** | Seiton + Seiso | Equipment failure modes surface |
| 4 | **Standardization** | Seiketsu documented | SOPs created; visual management |
| 5 | **Discipline** | Shitsuke entrenched | Stable process — SQC charts valid |
| 6 | **Improvement** | Kaizen on 5S | 5S feeds the DMAIC/DMADV engine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kaizen** (改善) — Japanese for "continuous improvement" — is the cultural companion to 5S. While 5S *stabilizes* the workplace, Kaizen *improves* it. Both are required for mature SQC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 5S Dependency Cycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["S1 Seiri SORT"]:::sort --> node2["S2 Seiton SET IN ORDER"]:::order
    node2 --> node3["S3 Seiso SHINE"]:::shine
    node3 --> node4["S4 Seiketsu STANDARDIZE"]:::standard
    node4 --> node5["S5 Shitsuke SUSTAIN"]:::sustain
    node5 --> node6["Stable Workplace READY FOR SQC"]:::ready
    node5 -.audit feedback.-> node1

    classDef sort fill:#FFD1D1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef order fill:#D1E5FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef shine fill:#D1FFD1,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef standard fill:#FFF4D1,stroke:#8B7500,color:#000
    classDef sustain fill:#E5D1FF,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef ready fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 5S → SQC Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs: Raw Material, Tools, Manpower] --> B[5S Layer 1: Seiri]
    B --> C[5S Layer 2: Seiton]
    C --> D[5S Layer 3: Seiso]
    D --> E[5S Layer 4: Seiketsu]
    E --> F[5S Layer 5: Shitsuke]
    F --> G[Stable Process]
    G --> H[SQC Tools: Control Charts, Pareto, Cp/Cpk]
    H --> I[Quality Output]
    I -.feedback.-> F
```

**Read as:** The 5S pipeline transforms a chaotic input stream into a *stable* process, which is the *only* valid input domain for the statistical quality control tools studied in the rest of PEPET415. Without the 5S layer, SQC tools will generate misleading signals (false alarms, missed trends).

---

## 4.3 Audit-Feedback Loop (PDCA Embedding)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PLAN: Set 5S standards, red-tag rules"] --> Do["DO: Train staff, deploy pillars"]
    Do --> Ch["CHECK: Monthly 5S audit, score compute"]
    Ch --> Ac["ACT: CAPA on weak pillars, re-audit"]
    Ac -.continuous.-> P
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Shitsuke** pillar is what *closes* the PDCA loop on a permanent basis. Without Shitsuke, the cycle resets every quarter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the 5S methodology. List the five Japanese terms with their English equivalents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

5S is a Japanese workplace organization methodology that establishes a disciplined, clean, and standardized environment as a prerequisite for quality and productivity improvement. The five terms are:

1. **Seiri (Sort)** — remove unnecessary items from the workplace.
2. **Seiton (Set in Order)** — arrange necessary items for easy identification and retrieval.
3. **Seiso (Shine)** — clean the workplace and equipment to reveal abnormalities.
4. **Seiketsu (Standardize)** — institutionalize the first three S's through visual standards and SOPs.
5. **Shitsuke (Sustain)** — maintain discipline through audits, training, and habit formation.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for listing all five pillars correctly with English equivalents.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Red-Tag Method used in the Seiri (Sort) step of 5S.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Red-Tag Method is the standard tool used in the **Seiri** step. Every item in the workplace is evaluated against a usage-frequency criterion (e.g., not used in the last 30 days). Items that fail this test are affixed with a **red tag** and quarantined to a holding area for a defined period (typically 30–90 days). During this hold, if no one claims the item, it is **disposed, recycled, or relocated** to an off-floor store. This method forces a conscious decision and prevents passive accumulation of clutter. *(Award 1 mark each for: red-tag definition, holding period, and disposition logic.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question B1 **or** Question B2.

---

### ⭐ Question B1 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the sequential logic of the 5S methodology. Why is Seiketsu (Standardize) considered the "bridge" pillar between the physical S's and the cultural S? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5S audit on an assembly line yielded the following data. Compute the **total audit score**, the **maximum possible score**, the **overall compliance percentage**, and identify the **weakest pillar** with a suitable corrective action. **(7 marks)**

| Pillar | Checkpoints ($N_i$) | Points Earned |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|
| Seiri | 10 | 72 |
| Seiton | 12 | 90 |
| Seiso | 8 | 50 |
| Seiketsu | 6 | 32 |
| Shitsuke | 7 | 30 |

---

#### Model Solution to B1(a) — Sequential Logic of 5S (7 marks)

The 5S methodology is **strictly sequential** because each pillar's success depends on the previous one. The logic flow is:

1. **Seiri → Seiton:** You *cannot* "set in order" items that have not first been *sorted*. Clutter must be removed before arrangement is meaningful.
2. **Seiton → Seiso:** You *cannot* effectively clean a chaotic workplace. An ordered layout exposes surfaces to clean.
3. **Seiso → Seiketsu:** Cleaning *reveals* abnormalities (leaks, wear). Only when the workplace is consistently clean can the **standard** of cleanliness be defined and codified.
4. **Seiketsu → Shitsuke:** Standards are the *precondition* for sustained discipline. Without written/visual standards, there is nothing for staff to be disciplined *against*.

**Why Seiketsu is the "bridge" pillar (4 marks):**

Seiketsu (Standardize) is the transitional step because it converts the **physical interventions** of Seiri, Seiton, and Seiso (which deal with objects and space) into **organizational rules** (SOPs, checklists, visual standards). These rules, in turn, are the *target* of Shitsuke — the **cultural** pillar that enforces them through human behavior. Hence Seiketsu is the hinge between "what is done to the workplace" and "what is demanded of the people."

---

#### Model Solution to B1(b) — Audit Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total Audit Score:**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 72 + 90 + 50 + 32 + 30 = 274
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Possible Score:**

$$
\sum N_i = 10 + 12 + 8 + 6 + 7 = 43
$$

Assuming each checkpoint is worth 10 points:

$$
S_{\max} = 43 \times 10 = 430
$$

**Step 3 — Overall Compliance Percentage:**

$$
C_{\%} = \frac{274}{430} \times 100 \approx 63.72\%
$$

**Step 4 — Pillar-Wise Compliance:**

$$
C_{\text{Seiri}} = \frac{72}{100} \times 100 = 72.00\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Seiton}} = \frac{90}{120} \times 100 = 75.00\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Seiso}} = \frac{50}{80} \times 100 = 62.50\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Seiketsu}} = \frac{32}{60} \times 100 = 53.33\%
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shitsuke}} = \frac{30}{70} \times 100 \approx 42.86\%
$$

**Step 5 — Identify Weakest Pillar:**

The weakest pillar is **Shitsuke (42.86%)**, followed by **Seiketsu (53.33%)**.

**Step 6 — Corrective Action:**

Since both weak pillars are *behavioral/cultural*, the corrective action should focus on:

- Instituting a **monthly 5S audit calendar** with management participation (Gemba walks).
- Creating **photographic standards** for each workstation and posting them visibly.
- Linking 5S compliance to **operator performance appraisals**.
- Conducting **re-training sessions** on the standardized SOPs.

> **Marks distribution for B1(b):** Total score (1) + Max score (1) + Compliance % (1) + Pillar-wise breakdown (2) + Weakest pillar + CAPA (2).

---

### ⭐ Question B2 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain how the 5S methodology serves as a **prerequisite** for the successful application of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** tools. Use at least two specific examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing cell is implementing 5S with a weighted audit scheme. The weights and earned scores are as follows:

| Pillar | Weight $w_i$ | Earned Score $S_i$ (out of 100) |
|--------|--------------|----------------------------------|
| Seiri | 0.15 | 80 |
| Seiton | 0.20 | 75 |
| Seiso | 0.15 | 85 |
| Seiketsu | 0.25 | 60 |
| Shitsuke | 0.25 | 50 |

Compute the **weighted audit score** and determine whether the cell passes the management benchmark of **70%**. If not, suggest a **targeted CAPA** for the two lowest-scoring pillars. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B2(a) — 5S as Prerequisite for SQC (7 marks)

SQC tools — control charts, Pareto analysis, process-capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$), and acceptance sampling — all assume that the underlying process is **stable** (only common-cause variation present) and that the data collected is **trustworthy**. 5S creates the conditions for these assumptions to hold. **Two specific examples:**

1. **Effect on Control Charts (e.g., $\bar{X}$-R chart):** A control chart monitors process variation by distinguishing *common-cause* (random) variation from *assignable-cause* (special) variation. In a non-5S workplace, tool misplacement, oil leaks, and inconsistent setups act as **persistent assignable causes** that the chart cannot "see" because they are *always present*. The chart will then show chronic out-of-control signals (false alarms), leading operators to ignore the chart entirely. A 5S-cleaned workplace removes these persistent assignable causes, leaving only the common-cause variation that the chart is *designed* to monitor.

2. **Effect on Data Collection & Pareto Analysis:** To build a Pareto chart of defect causes, operators must log defects honestly and consistently. In a cluttered, disorganized environment, defect logging is erratic and incomplete, producing a Pareto chart that misleads prioritization. 5S *Seiketsu* mandates **standardized logbooks and visual defect-categorization boards**, ensuring that the Pareto data is reliable. Similarly, 5S *Shitsuke* (sustained discipline) guarantees that logging is a *habit*, not a one-time event.

> **Conclusion:** 5S is the *environmental and behavioral foundation* on which the entire edifice of SQC is built.

---

#### Model Solution to B2(b) — Weighted Audit Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Verify the Weight Sum:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Weighted Audit Score:**

$$
S_w = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i
$$

$$
S_w = (0.15)(80) + (0.20)(75) + (0.15)(85) + (0.25)(60) + (0.25)(50)
$$

$$
S_w = 12.0 + 15.0 + 12.75 + 15.0 + 12.5
$$

$$
S_w = 67.25
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Benchmark:**

$$
S_w = 67.25 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Benchmark} = 70.00
$$

The cell **fails** the benchmark by $70.00 - 67.25 = 2.75$ points.

**Step 4 — Identify Two Lowest Contributors (Weighted Contribution):**

| Pillar | $w_i \cdot S_i$ | Rank |
|--------|------------------|------|
| Seiri | 12.00 | 4 |
| Seiton | 15.00 | 1 (tie) |
| Seiso | 12.75 | 3 |
| Seiketsu | 15.00 | 1 (tie) |
| **Shitsuke** | **12.50** | **5 (lowest)** |
| **Seiketsu** | **15.00** (but Seiketsu's *raw* score is 60, the second lowest) | — |

**The two lowest-scoring pillars by raw score are Seiketsu (60) and Shitsuke (50).**

**Step 5 — Targeted CAPA:**

- **For Seiketsu (Standardize):** Introduce **photographic 5S standards** at every workstation, develop a **5S audit checklist** with binary pass/fail criteria, and conduct a **two-day SOP training workshop** for all cell operators.
- **For Shitsuke (Sustain):** Launch a **monthly 5S audit calendar** with cross-functional auditors, integrate 5S scores into the **operator KRA (Key Result Area)** dashboard, and implement a **"Best 5S Cell of the Month"** recognition program to sustain cultural momentum.

> **Marks distribution for B2(b):** Weight-sum check (1) + Weighted score (2) + Benchmark comparison (1) + Identification of weak pillars (1) + CAPA (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 5S Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Conflating the S's:** Students frequently mix up **Seiton (Set in Order)** and **Seiketsu (Standardize)**. Remember: *Seiton* is **physical arrangement** of items; *Seiketsu* is the **standardization of procedures and rules**.
> 2. **Skipping units / percentage signs:** A bare number like "67.25" without units is incomplete. Always write **$67.25\% \text{ or } 67.25/100$** when the metric is a percentage.
> 3. **Ignoring the "weakest pillar" diagnostic:** Computing only the *total* compliance and stopping is worth 1–2 marks. Full marks require *pillar-wise breakdown* and a *targeted CAPA* — this is the 3–4 mark differentiator.
> 4. **Treating 5S as a one-time event:** Examiners specifically test whether you understand that 5S is **cyclical** and that **Shitsuke is the make-or-break pillar**. Always mention *PDCA* and *sustainment* in long answers.
> 5. **Forgetting the link to SQC:** In PEPET415, the 5S question is *not* just a lean-tool question — it is a **prerequisite for the statistical methods** of the rest of the course. Failing to mention this link costs the "application" marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ 5S = **Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke** (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain).
- ☐ The five S's are **strictly sequential** — skipping one breaks the chain.
- ☐ **Seiketsu is the bridge** between the physical S's and the cultural S.
- ☐ **Shitsuke** is the hardest pillar (cultural, not physical) — and the most common failure point in audits.
- ☐ **Red-Tag Method** is the canonical tool of Seiri; typical hold period is **30–90 days**.
- ☐ **30-Second Search Rule** is the canonical standard of Seiton.
- ☐ **Operator-led daily cleaning** is the canonical standard of Seiso (not outsourced to janitors).
- ☐ Audit scoring is typically on a **0–100 per pillar** basis; total possible = **500** (unweighted).
- ☐ Compliance % formula: $C_{\%} = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$.
- ☐ Weighted score formula: $S_w = \sum w_i S_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ☐ **5S is the environmental prerequisite for SQC** — without it, control charts and Pareto analyses are built on unreliable data.
- ☐ **5S embeds the PDCA cycle**; Shitsuke is what keeps the cycle alive.
- ☐ In KTU PEPET415, always link 5S discussion back to **process stability** and **data reliability** for downstream SQC tools.
- ☐ Mature 5S enables **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) and feeds the **DMAIC/DMADV** Six-Sigma framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Just in Time Manufacturing (JIT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Just in Time Manufacturing (JIT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Just In Time (JIT) Manufacturing** is a Japanese production philosophy, originated by **Taiichi Ohno** at the **Toyota Motor Company** (1950s–1970s), that seeks to produce and deliver finished goods, components, or services **exactly when they are needed**, **in the exact quantity required**, and **free from defects** — thereby eliminating all forms of waste (*Muda*) from the production system.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, JIT is treated as a **Lean Manufacturing philosophy** deeply integrated with **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**. It is classified as a **pull-based demand-driven production system** where each process produces only what is required by the *next* (downstream) process, triggered by visual signals called **Kanbans**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Statistical Quality Control):** JIT is studied as a *systems approach* that minimizes **variation**, **inventory variation**, and **process variation** — the three pillars that classical SQC theory (Shewhart, Deming, Juran) attempts to control statistically.

The core **production-trigger metric** in JIT is:

$$\text{Takt\ Time} = \frac{\text{Available\ Production\ Time\ per\ Day}}{\text{Customer\ Demand\ per\ Day}}$$

Every workstation must complete its operation in **≤ Takt Time** to maintain a synchronous flow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine walking into a busy **South Indian restaurant** during lunch hour. The waiter does **not** cook all 50 *dosas* in advance and stack them on a counter. Instead:

| Restaurant Step | JIT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Customer places order | Customer demand (downstream pull) |
| Waiter carries the order slip to the kitchen | **Kanban signal** (information card) |
| Cook prepares only **one** dosa at a time | **One-piece flow** (batch size = 1) |
| Dosa served fresh, hot, in 4 minutes | **Lead time = Takt time** |
| No leftover cold dosas at 2 PM | **Zero inventory** at end of day |
| If a customer complains, that specific recipe is corrected | **Jidoka** (autonomation + built-in quality) |

This is precisely how **JIT** operates in a factory — **no work is started until a downstream signal (Kanban) requests it**, no inventory is hoarded, and any defect is stopped at the source using statistical signals (P-charts, control charts, mistake-proofing devices called *Poka-Yoke*).

---

## 1.3 The Seven Wastes (*Muda*) — The Antithesis of JIT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Muda** (Japanese: 無駄) literally means *uselessness* or *waste*. **Taiichi Ohno** originally identified **seven wastes** in manufacturing. JIT aims to **eliminate every one of them**.

| # | Waste (Muda) | Japanese Term | Statistical Q.C. Link |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overproduction** | *Seisan* | Leads to overstock, hides defects (P-chart blind spots) |
| 2 | **Waiting** | *Taima* | Non-value-added time — increases **process spread** |
| 3 | **Transportation** | *Unten* | Material handling without value-add |
| 4 | **Over-processing** | *Sagyo* | Excess precision or steps beyond customer need |
| 5 | **Inventory** | *Zaiko* | Hides process variability — defeats statistical control |
| 6 | **Motion** | *Ugoki* | Worker movement without value-add |
| 7 | **Defects** | *Hikaku* | Triggers **Pareto analysis**, **control charts**, **Poka-Yoke** |

> [!NOTE]
> An **8th waste** — *Non-utilized human talent* (*Sontaku*) — is sometimes added in modern JIT/Lean literature.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

The following metrics are **central to any KTU 14-mark answer** on JIT:

- **Takt Time** — pace of production aligned to demand
- **Lead Time (LT)** — total time from order to delivery
- **Cycle Time (CT)** — actual processing time per unit
- **Kanban Number (N)** — number of circulating signals
- **Container Size (C)** — units per Kanban card
- **Setup Time / SMED** — Single Minute Exchange of Die
- **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** — fraction of units defect-free at first attempt
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** — `Cumulative throughput / Average inventory`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Pillars of JIT — Operational Logic Steps

JIT is not a *single technique*; it is a **philosophy backed by 12 inter-locking tactical tools**. Below is the **causal logic chain** that every KTU answer must follow:

### Step 1 — Establish Stable, Standardized Processes
Before pull-flow is possible, processes must be **statistically stable and capable**. This is verified using:
- **$\bar{X}$ and R control charts** (variables data) → centeredness and dispersion
- **$p$, $np$, $c$, $u$ charts** (attributes data) → defect monitoring
- **Process Capability Indices** — **$C_p$** and **$C_{pk}$**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \qquad C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

> For JIT viability, $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is typically required.

---

### Step 2 — Reduce Setup Times (SMED)
Originally, **SMED = Single-Minute Exchange of Die** (setup in < 10 minutes, i.e., *single-digit* minutes). Modern extensions (DMED = *Deca-Minute*) accept up to 99 minutes. Why is this critical?

> **Why?** When setup time → 0, **economic lot size → 1**, enabling true *one-piece flow*.

The relationship between **setup cost**, **lot size**, and **holding cost** follows the classic square-root formula (analogous to EOQ):

$$Q_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

where  
$D$ = annual demand (units), $S$ = setup cost per batch, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.

When **SMED reduces $S$ drastically**, $Q_{\text{opt}}$ collapses towards 1, the mathematical heart of *one-piece flow*.

---

### Step 3 — Implement the Pull System (Kanban)
Replace *forecast-driven push* with *demand-driven pull*. The **upstream process** only produces when:
1. A **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban)** arrives at the supermarket, **AND**
2. A **Production Kanban (P-Kanban)** is freed and posted to the upstream machine.

This creates the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** dual-card flow.

---

### Step 4 — Level the Production (Heijunka)
**Heijunka** smooths production volume *and mix* over time. Instead of producing 100 units of Model A on Monday and 100 of Model B on Tuesday, mix them throughout the day.

> **Statistical Implication:** Heijunka **reduces the coefficient of variation of demand** at each workstation, making SPC charts more sensitive to real assignable causes.

---

### Step 5 — Build in Quality (Jidoka + Poka-Yoke)
*Jidoka* = automation with a human touch. When a defect is detected, the **machine auto-stops**, and the **worker pulls the Andon cord** to signal upstream. This is the operational embodiment of Shewhart's *Western Electric Rules* — no defect shall pass.

---

### Step 6 — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
**Kaizen** = continuous, incremental improvement involving *every* worker. PDCA cycle (Plan–Do–Check–Act, Deming cycle) is applied daily to remove the *root causes* of waste revealed by statistical analysis.

---

### Step 7 — Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
Statistical tools like **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** and **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** are used to drive **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** above **85%** — a JIT prerequisite.

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** expected in KTU ESE Module-2 answers on JIT. **No vertical pipe characters** are used inside the table to preserve markdown rendering.

| # | Formula Name | Expression | Variables / Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{avail}}}{D_{\text{day}}}$ | $T_t$ (sec/unit), $T_{\text{avail}}$ (sec/day), $D_{\text{day}}$ (units/day) | $T_t > 0$; $D_{\text{day}} \neq 0$ |
| 2 | **Number of Kanbans** | $N = \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$ | $N$ (integer), $d$ (units/time), $L$ (time), $S$ (safety fraction, 0–1), $C$ (units/container) | Round **UP** to next whole Kanban |
| 3 | **Container Size Optimization** | $C = \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{N}$ | $C$ (units/container) | Often fixed by bin dimensions |
| 4 | **Lead Time** | $LT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{CT}_i + \text{Queue}_i + \text{Wait}_i$ | $LT$ (time) | JIT aims $LT \to T_t$ |
| 5 | **Optimal Lot Size (EOQ analog)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $Q^*$ (units), $D$ (units/yr), $S$ (\$), $H$ (\$/unit/yr) | As $S \to 0$, $Q^* \to 1$ |
| 6 | **Total Annual Cost (setup + holding)** | $TC = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}$ | $TC$ (\$/yr) | Min at $Q = Q^*$ |
| 7 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$ | $ITR$ (dimensionless) | JIT target $ITR \geq 12$ |
| 8 | **OEE** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A, P, Q \in [0,1]$ | World-class $OEE \geq 0.85$ |
| 9 | **First Pass Yield** | $FPY = 1 - DPMO \times 10^{-6}$ | $DPMO$ = defects per million opportunities | Six-Sigma target $FPY \geq 0.99966$ |
| 10 | **Process Capability** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_p$ (dimensionless) | $C_p \geq 1.33$ for JIT |
| 11 | **Cycle Time vs Takt Time** | $CT \leq T_t$ | Both in time/unit | Violation → bottleneck |
| 12 | **Cost of Poor Quality** | $COPQ = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}}$ | Currency units | JIT reduces COPQ to < 1% of sales |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | JIT Application | Statistical Tool Integrated |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (Toyota, Maruti, Tata Motors)** | Body-shop sequencing, mixed-model assembly | $p$-chart for weld defects |
| **Electronics (Foxconn, Apple)** | SMT line component replenishment | $C_{pk}$ on solder-paste thickness |
| **Pharma (Cipla, Sun Pharma)** | Tablet batch scheduling with Kanban | $c$-chart on tablet weight |
| **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL)** | Lean MRO with Heijunka | R-chart on turbine-blade dimensions |
| **Textile (Arvind, Vardhman)** | Dye-house pull from weaving | $\bar{X}$-chart on GSM |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **complete mathematical derivations** of the two most examination-critical formulas in JIT: (A) the **Optimal Kanban Number** and (B) the **Takt Time–Cycle Time bottleneck analysis**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — Number of Kanbans Required in a Production Line

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a single-stage production cell producing part **X** with:
- Average demand rate = $d$ (units per unit time)
- Replenishment lead time = $L$ (unit time)
- Safety factor = $S$ (a fraction, 0 ≤ S ≤ 1)
- Each Kanban container holds $C$ units (integer)

We need to find $N$, the number of Kanban cards circulating in the loop.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Expected demand during lead time**

During the replenishment lead time $L$, the average demand that must be met is:

$$D_L = d \times L$$

This is the *expected* consumption before replenishment arrives.

**Step 2 — Add safety stock to absorb variability**

Statistical fluctuation in demand (or lead time) demands a buffer. The *safety stock* required is a fraction $S$ of $D_L$:

$$SS = S \times D_L = S \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Total stock the system must hold**

The total quantity of parts in transit + in-process + in safety stock equals:

$$T_{\text{stock}} = D_L + SS = d \cdot L + S \cdot d \cdot L = d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)$$

**Step 4 — Divide by container size**

Each Kanban card controls exactly $C$ units. Hence the number of Kanbans:

$$N = \frac{T_{\text{stock}}}{C} = \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$$

**Step 5 — Apply integer constraint**

Since $N$ must be a whole number of physical cards, always **round up**:

$$\boxed{N = \left\lceil \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} \right\rceil}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Given: $d = 200$ units/day, $L = 0.5$ day, $S = 0.10$, $C = 25$ units.

$$N = \frac{200 \times 0.5 \times (1 + 0.10)}{25} = \frac{200 \times 0.5 \times 1.10}{25} = \frac{110}{25} = 4.4$$

Therefore, $N = \lceil 4.4 \rceil = \mathbf{5}$ Kanbans are required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Bottleneck Identification Using Takt Time

### 3.2.1 Setup

A 4-station serial line produces at the following cycle times (in seconds/unit):  
$\text{CT}_1 = 40$, $\text{CT}_2 = 55$, $\text{CT}_3 = 50$, $\text{CT}_4 = 45$.

Daily available time = 7 hours = 25 200 seconds.  
Daily demand = 600 units.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Takt Time**

$$T_t = \frac{T_{\text{avail}}}{D_{\text{day}}} = \frac{25\,200}{600} = 42\ \text{seconds/unit}$$

**Step 2 — Compare each station's cycle time with Takt Time**

| Station | CT (s) | $T_t$ (s) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 | 42 | OK (Slack = 2 s) |
| 2 | 55 | 42 | **BOTTLENECK** (Deficit = 13 s) |
| 3 | 50 | 42 | OK (Slack = -8 s) |
| 4 | 45 | 42 | OK (Slack = -3 s) |

**Step 3 — Identify the bottleneck**

The **maximum cycle time** determines the effective line capacity:

$$T_t^{\text{eff}} = \max_i(\text{CT}_i) = 55\ \text{s/unit}$$

**Step 4 — Compute true daily output**

$$D_{\text{day}}^{\text{true}} = \frac{25\,200}{55} \approx 458\ \text{units/day}$$

**Step 5 — Compute demand shortfall**

$$\Delta D = 600 - 458 = 142\ \text{units/day (shortfall)}$$

### 3.2.3 JIT Resolution

Under JIT, **Station 2** must be improved through:
- **SMED** to reduce setup-driven micro-stops
- **Kaizen** to reduce motion, walking, and tool-search waste
- **Paralleling** (splitting one station into two identical sub-stations)
- **Adding a buffer (conWIP) between Station 2 and Station 3** — *Drum–Buffer–Rope* (DBR) method from **Goldratt's Theory of Constraints**, a complementary JIT tool.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Total Cost Reduction via SMED

### 3.3.1 Setup

A stamping cell has:
- Annual demand $D = 100\,000$ units
- Original setup cost $S_1 = ₹25\,000$ per setup
- Holding cost $H = ₹50$ per unit per year
- Original setup time $T_{su,1} = 90$ minutes

After SMED, setup time drops to $T_{su,2} = 9$ minutes, reducing setup cost proportionally to $S_2 = ₹2\,500$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Original optimal lot size**

$$Q_1^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS_1}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 100\,000 \times 25\,000}{50}} = \sqrt{1.0 \times 10^{8}} = 10\,000\ \text{units}$$

**Step 2 — Original total cost**

$$TC_1 = \frac{DS_1}{Q_1^*} + \frac{HQ_1^*}{2} = \frac{100\,000 \times 25\,000}{10\,000} + \frac{50 \times 10\,000}{2} = 250\,000 + 250\,000 = ₹500\,000$$

**Step 3 — Post-SMED optimal lot size**

$$Q_2^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 100\,000 \times 2\,500}{50}} = \sqrt{1.0 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3\,162\ \text{units}$$

**Step 4 — Post-SMED total cost**

$$TC_2 = \frac{100\,000 \times 2\,500}{3\,162} + \frac{50 \times 3\,162}{2} \approx 79\,057 + 79\,050 \approx ₹158\,107$$

**Step 5 — Annual savings**

$$\text{Savings} = TC_1 - TC_2 = 500\,000 - 158\,107 = \mathbf{₹341\,893\ (68.4\%\ reduction)}$$

This dramatic reduction mathematically demonstrates **why SMED is a foundational pillar of JIT**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — JIT vs. Traditional (Push) System

| Performance Dimension | Traditional Push | JIT Pull | % Improvement (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Level** | High (WIP buffer) | Near zero | -85 to -95% |
| **Lead Time** | 4–6 weeks | 1–5 days | -80 to -90% |
| **Setup Time** | 60–180 min | < 10 min (SMED) | -85 to -95% |
| **Defect Rate (PPM)** | 3 000–10 000 | < 50 (Six-Sigma) | -99% |
| **Floor Space** | 100% baseline | 50–60% | -40 to -50% |
| **Labour Productivity** | 1.0× baseline | 1.5–2.0× | +50 to +100% |
| **OEE** | 45–60% | 85–95% | +40 to +50 pp |
| **Inventory Turnover** | 4–6 | 20–100+ | +500% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The JIT Pull System with Dual Kanban Architecture

The following **block-level functional architecture flow** illustrates the operational flow of the **Withdrawal Kanban (W)** and **Production Kanban (P)** in a Toyota-style supermarket production cell.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    custA[Customer Order] --> supM[Supermarket Withdrawal Point]
    supM -->|W Kanban pulled| line1[Assembly Line A1]
    line1 -->|Empty container returned| supM
    supM -->|W Kanban posted| machP[Press Shop Station]
    machP -->|Full container pushed| supM
    supM -->|W Kanban posted| storF[Finished Goods Store]
    storF -->|Dispatched| custA

    supM -->|P Kanban to upstream| machP

    machP -->|Production triggered| supR[Raw Material Supplier]
    supR -->|Milk run delivery| machP

    style custA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style supM fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style line1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style machP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style storF fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style supR fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** The **W-Kanban** authorizes *withdrawal* from the supermarket; the **P-Kanban** authorizes *production* at the upstream station. The two cards are physically exchanged to maintain the closed loop.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — JIT Implementation Roadmap (Pyramid Hierarchy)

This block diagram shows the **hierarchical dependency** of the 12 JIT tools, where each upper layer requires the lower layer to be in place.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    foundation[Foundation: Stable Capable Process] --> stable[Statistical Process Control]
    foundation --> jidoka[Jidoka and Poka Yoke]
    foundation --> tpm[Total Productive Maintenance]

    stable --> smed[SMED Setup Reduction]
    jidoka --> smed
    tpm --> smed

    smed --> flow[One Piece Flow]
    smed --> kanban[Kanban Pull System]
    smed --> heijunka[Heijunka Production Leveling]

    flow --> kaizen[Kaizen Continuous Improvement]
    kanban --> kaizen
    heijunka --> kaizen

    kaizen --> goal[Zero Defects Zero Inventory Zero Waste]

    style foundation fill:#8B0000,color:#fff
    style goal fill:#006400,color:#fff
    style stable fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    style jidoka fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    style tpm fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    style smed fill:#DAA520,color:#000
    style flow fill:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style kanban fill:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style heijunka fill:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style kaizen fill:#DC143C,color:#fff
```

> **Reading guide:** Bottom-up dependency — you cannot implement Kanban (middle layer) without first stabilizing the process (foundation).

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — JIT vs. Traditional Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph trad[Traditional Push System]
        fc1[Forecast] --> mrp1[MRP Explosion]
        mrp1 --> ws1[Workstation 1]
        ws1 --> ws2[Workstation 2]
        ws2 --> ws3[Workstation 3]
        ws3 --> fg1[Finished Goods]
        fg1 --> cust1[Customer]
    end

    subgraph jitSys[JIT Pull System]
        cust2[Customer] --> sup2[Supermarket]
        sup2 -->|W Kanban| wsA[Workstation A]
        sup2 -->|W Kanban| wsB[Workstation B]
        sup2 -->|W Kanban| wsC[Workstation C]
        wsA -->|P Kanban| supRaw[Upstream Process]
        wsB -->|P Kanban| supRaw
        wsC -->|P Kanban| supRaw
    end

    style fc1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style cust2 fill:#90EE90
```

> **Key contrast:** In the **push** system, information flows from forecast → production → customer. In the **pull** system, information flows from **customer → production** (reverse direction).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A carries **2 marks for accuracy** and **1 mark for presentation/diagram**. Answers must be **between 80 and 120 words** for full marks.

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define Just-In-Time (JIT) manufacturing. List the four primary objectives of JIT.**

**Model Answer (105 words):**

**Just-In-Time (JIT)** is a Japanese production philosophy, pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota, that produces and delivers **the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time** — eliminating all forms of waste (*Muda*).

The **four primary objectives of JIT** are:
1. **Zero Inventory** — produce only what is needed, when needed
2. **Zero Defects** — build quality into the process via *Jidoka* and *Poka-Yoke*
3. **Zero Lead Time** — minimize time from order to delivery
4. **Zero Waste** — eliminate all seven *Muda* (overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, defects)

Together, these objectives drive a **continuous flow** of defect-free products synchronized with customer demand.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is a Kanban? Differentiate between the two types of Kanban cards used in a pull system.**

**Model Answer (115 words):**

A **Kanban** (Japanese: 看板, meaning *visual signal card*) is the **information system** that triggers production and material flow in a JIT environment. It is a simple physical card (or electronic signal in modern E-Kanban) that travels between processes, carrying authorization data.

| Feature | **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban)** | **Production Kanban (P-Kanban)** |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Authorizes the *downstream* process to **withdraw** parts from the upstream *supermarket* | Authorizes the *upstream* process to **produce** a specified quantity |
| Trigger | Empty bin in downstream process | Free card returned to upstream machine |
| Direction of flow | Downstream → Upstream (replenishment request) | Upstream → Upstream (production order) |

Together they form the **dual-card closed loop** that is the heart of the Toyota Production System.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions follow the **Module Internal Choice** pattern. Two fully-worked alternatives are provided. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Include **at least one diagram** for full marks in the relevant sub-part.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **concept and philosophy of JIT manufacturing**. Discuss its **key principles and strategic objectives** in the context of modern lean production. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. Introduction (1.5 marks)**  
JIT, developed at **Toyota** (1950s–1970s) under **Taiichi Ohno** and **Eiji Toyoda**, is a pull-based production philosophy that synchronizes manufacturing precisely with customer demand, eliminating all non-value-added activities. It is a **total systems approach** that integrates quality, inventory, lead time, and workforce into one continuous improvement engine.

**2. The 12 Key Principles of JIT (3.5 marks)**  

| # | Principle | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer-driven pull** | Produce only what the next operation takes |
| 2 | **Zero inventory** | No raw material, WIP, or FG storage beyond immediate need |
| 3 | **One-piece flow** | Lot size = 1; units move singly, not in batches |
| 4 | **Takt time orientation** | Pace = $T_{\text{avail}}/D$ |
| 5 | **SMED** | Setup < 10 minutes to enable small lots |
| 6 | **Jidoka** | Auto-stop on defect, Andon cord for visual alert |
| 7 | **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing devices prevent errors at source |
| 8 | **Heijunka** | Level production volume *and* mix |
| 9 | **Kaizen** | Daily incremental improvement by all employees |
| 10 | **5S Workplace organization** | Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain |
| 11 | **TPM** | 100% employee ownership of machine maintenance |
| 12 | **Supplier partnerships** | Long-term relations, few reliable suppliers, milk-runs |

**3. Strategic Objectives (2 marks)**  
- **Cost reduction** through inventory carrying cost elimination  
- **Quality improvement** by *built-in* statistical control  
- **Lead time compression** for higher responsiveness  
- **Flexibility** to handle demand variety  
- **Employee empowerment** through problem-solving autonomy

---

**(b)** Explain the **Kanban system** in detail. **Derive the formula for calculating the number of Kanbans** required in a production line and solve: a workstation has demand rate 400 units/day, lead time 0.25 day, safety factor 0.20, container size 40 units. Find the number of Kanbans. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. Kanban System Explained (2.5 marks)**  

The **Kanban system** is the **information infrastructure** of JIT. It is a **demand-pull signaling mechanism** that:
- **Replaces** forecast-driven material requirements planning (MRP)  
- **Visualizes** work-in-process using physical cards  
- **Limits** inventory through the number of cards in circulation  
- **Triggers** production *only* when a downstream signal arrives  

**Two types:** *Withdrawal Kanban (W)* and *Production Kanban (P)*. When an operator at Station B empties a bin of part X, the empty bin is sent back to Station A's supermarket with a **W-Kanban**, which authorizes withdrawal of a full bin. A **P-Kanban** is simultaneously posted at Station A, authorizing production of the consumed quantity. This **closed loop** ensures production equals consumption at every instant.

**2. Derivation of N (2.5 marks)**  

| Step | Reasoning | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average demand during lead time | $D_L = d \cdot L$ |
| 2 | Safety stock for variability | $SS = S \cdot d \cdot L$ |
| 3 | Total stock to be buffered | $T_{\text{stock}} = d L (1 + S)$ |
| 4 | Divide by container capacity | $N = \dfrac{d L (1 + S)}{C}$ |
| 5 | Round up to integer | $N = \lceil N \rceil$ |

**3. Numerical Solution (2 marks)**  

Given: $d = 400$ units/day, $L = 0.25$ day, $S = 0.20$, $C = 40$ units.

$$N = \frac{400 \times 0.25 \times (1 + 0.20)}{40} = \frac{400 \times 0.25 \times 1.20}{40} = \frac{120}{40} = 3$$

Therefore, the line requires **exactly 3 Kanbans** (no rounding required).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **seven wastes (Muda)** in manufacturing as identified by Taiichi Ohno. Describe, with examples, how **JIT tools eliminate each waste**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. The Seven Wastes Framework (1.5 marks)**  
Taiichi Ohno's **Seven Wastes (Muda)** are the *non-value-adding* activities that consume resources without adding benefit to the customer. The mnemonic is often remembered as **"DOWNTIME"** (Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing).

**2. Waste-by-Waste Analysis with JIT Counter-Measures (4 marks)**

| # | Waste | Example | JIT Tool to Eliminate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defects** (Hikaku) | Rework of misaligned engine block | **Jidoka** + **Poka-Yoke** + **SPC charts** ($\bar{X}, R, p$) |
| 2 | **Overproduction** (Seisan) | Producing 1 000 parts when only 800 ordered | **Pull system (Kanban)** — only produce against signals |
| 3 | **Waiting** (Taima) | Machine idle while operator fetches tools | **SMED** + **5S** + **TPM** to ensure availability |
| 4 | **Non-utilized talent** (Sontaku) | Engineer's skills wasted on clerical work | **Kaizen teams** + **Suggestion systems** + **Job rotation** |
| 5 | **Transportation** (Unten) | Forklifts moving WIP across plant | **Cellular layout** + **Point-of-use storage** |
| 6 | **Inventory** (Zaiko) | Piles of raw material blocking aisles | **Kanban** + **Just-in-Sequence delivery** |
| 7 | **Motion** (Ugoki) | Operator walking 10 m to reach a tool | **5S** + **Ergonomic layout** + **U-shaped cells** |
| 8 | **Extra-processing** (Sagyo) | Polishing surface beyond customer spec | **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** to define *true* specs |

**3. Statistical Linkage (1.5 marks)**  
Each waste is *measurable*. For example, **Waiting** is measured by **takt-time gap analysis**; **Defects** by **DPMO**; **Inventory** by **ITR**. JIT converts these abstract wastes into **measurable metrics** amenable to **statistical control**.

---

**(b)** **Compare JIT with the traditional manufacturing system** under 8 specific parameters. Discuss **5 major benefits and 4 limitations** of JIT implementation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. Comparison Table (3 marks)**

| Parameter | Traditional (Push) | JIT (Pull) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast / MRP | Real customer demand (Kanban) |
| Inventory | High buffer stock | Near zero |
| Lead time | Long (weeks) | Short (days) |
| Lot size | Large (economic batch) | Small (often 1) |
| Setup time | Long (60–180 min) | Short (< 10 min, SMED) |
| Quality control | Inspection at end | Built-in (Jidoka, Poka-Yoke) |
| Suppliers | Many, competitive bidding | Few, long-term partnership |
| Workforce | Specialized, narrow | Multi-skilled, flexible |
| Focus | Efficiency of single station | Flow efficiency of entire line |

**2. Five Major Benefits of JIT (2.5 marks)**

1. **Dramatic inventory reduction** (50–90%) → frees working capital  
2. **Lead time compression** (50–90% reduction) → faster market response  
3. **Quality improvement** (defect PPM drops 10–100×) → customer satisfaction  
4. **Cost reduction** (carrying, rework, scrap costs plummet) → better margins  
5. **Higher workforce engagement** (Kaizen participation) → innovation culture

**3. Four Limitations of JIT (1.5 marks)**

1. **Brittleness to disruption** — single supplier or transport glitch halts the line (Toyota 2011 Tōhoku earthquake lesson)  
2. **High initial setup cost** — SMED, training, supplier development require capital  
3. **Demand volatility sensitivity** — JIT is fragile to sudden demand spikes  
4. **Limited variety in some cells** — pure one-piece flow suits repetitive production more than job-shops

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 
> | # | Common Student Mistake | Marks Lost | How to Avoid |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Writing JIT = "Jitna chahiye utna banao" without naming the *seven wastes* | 2–3 | Always **explicitly list the 7 Muda** and link each to a JIT tool |
> | 2 | Confusing **Withdrawal Kanban (W)** and **Production Kanban (P)** — calling both "Kanban" generically | 1–2 | Use the **table format** distinguishing *function*, *direction*, and *trigger* of each card type |
> | 3 | Forgetting to **round up** $N$ in the Kanban calculation | 1 | Always write $N = \lceil N_{\text{calc}} \rceil$ explicitly |
> | 4 | Skipping the **derivation** of Kanban number formula and only writing the final equation | 2–3 | Show **5 derivation steps** as demonstrated in Section 3.1 |
> | 5 | No **diagram** in the 14-mark answer | 1–2 | Insert at least one **mermaid-style** or hand-drawn JIT flow diagram |
> | 6 | Writing JIT benefits but **omitting limitations** in the comparison question | 1–2 | Always give a **balanced view** — pros *and* cons |
> | 7 | Confusing **Takt Time** with **Cycle Time** | 1 | State clearly: $T_t$ = *demand* rhythm, $CT$ = *process* rhythm |
> | 8 | Spelling "Kanban" as "Karnban" or "Canban" — minor, but examiners deduct | 0.5 | Use the **correct Japanese romanization** |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — JIT (Module 2, SQC)**

**A. Core Philosophy**
- JIT = **Pull-based, demand-driven, zero-inventory, zero-defect** production philosophy  
- Origin: **Toyota Motor Corporation**, **Taiichi Ohno** (1950s)  
- Integral to the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — predecessor of modern **Lean Manufacturing**  
- Goal: produce the **right item, in the right quantity, at the right time**

**B. The 7 (+1) Wastes (Muda) — Mnemonic "DOWNTIME"**
- **D**efects, **O**verproduction, **W**aiting, **N**on-utilized talent, **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **E**xtra-processing

**C. The 12 JIT Tools**
- Kanban (W & P), SMED, Jidoka, Poka-Yoke, Heijunka, Kaizen, 5S, TPM, cellular layout, supplier partnership, visual management, one-piece flow

**D. Must-Know Formulas (in 30 seconds)**
- $T_t = T_{\text{avail}} / D_{\text{day}}$  
- $N = \lceil d L (1+S) / C \rceil$  
- $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$  
- $OEE = A \times P \times Q$  
- $C_p = (USL - LSL)/6\sigma$  
- $ITR = \text{COGS}/\text{Avg. Inventory}$

**E. Must-Know Numerical Triggers**
- Takt time is in **seconds/unit**; daily demand is in **units/day**; available time is in **seconds/day**  
- $N$ is **always rounded UP** to next integer  
- $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the **JIT minimum**; $C_p \geq 1.67$ is **Six-Sigma territory**  
- $OEE \geq 0.85$ is **world-class**

**F. Must-Name Concepts in Answers**
- **TPS** (Toyota Production System)  
- **Muda**, **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden) — the **3M** of Toyota  
- **Andon** (visual alert board)  
- **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing)  
- **Heijunka box** (level scheduling)  
- **Gemba** (the actual workplace) — *Go to Gemba* for problem-solving

**G. Statistical Quality Control Linkage**
- JIT **demands** statistical stability → uses **$\bar{X}, R, p, c, u$ charts** as pre-requisites  
- JIT **produces** statistical data via SPC → enables **Cpk-driven acceptance** of suppliers  
- JIT and **Six-Sigma** are **complementary**: JIT removes *Muda*, Six-Sigma removes *variation* in retained processes

**H. KTU ESE Quick-Win Tips**
- Always **draw a flow diagram** in a 14-mark JIT answer — 1–2 marks reserved for it  
- Always **state a formula** and **plug a number** in — examiners reward quantitative depth  
- Always **compare with traditional system** — examiners test for breadth of understanding  
- Always **conclude with a balance** — mention at least one **limitation** to demonstrate critical thinking

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management for Excellence (TQMEX)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management for Excellence (TQMEX)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Quality Management for Excellence (TQMEX)** is an integrated, organization-wide management philosophy that combines the principles of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** with structured **Excellence Models** (such as the EFQM Model and the Malcolm Baldrige Criteria) to achieve superior business performance, customer satisfaction, and continuous organizational improvement.

**TQM** itself is defined as a **management approach** focused on long-term success through **customer satisfaction**, achieved by the participation of all members of the organization at all levels in improving products, services, processes, and the culture in which they work.

**TQMEX** extends this philosophy by embedding it within a **results-oriented excellence framework** that includes criteria for leadership, strategy, people management, resources, processes, and measurable outcomes. The "**EX**" emphasizes the pursuit of excellence, not just quality assurance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** TQMEX sits at the intersection of statistical quality control and strategic management. While SQC provides the data-driven tools, TQMEX provides the organizational framework, culture, and leadership philosophy required to deploy those tools effectively.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are the **head chef of a large restaurant chain** with 50 branches across Kerala. You want every customer, no matter which branch they visit, to receive the same delicious, perfectly cooked, hygienic meal.

- **Without TQM:** You personally taste-test every dish at every branch. You cannot scale this. Quality is inconsistent.
- **With TQM:** You create a **standard recipe (process)**, train every chef to follow it (people), collect customer feedback (customer focus), taste-test small samples statistically (SPC), and continuously refine the recipe (Kaizen).
- **With TQMEX:** You go further — you benchmark against the **best restaurant in the world**, you get an external award (like a Michelin star) to certify your excellence, you build a **culture** where every staff member, from the dishwasher to the manager, is empowered to suggest improvements, and you measure success not just by taste, but by profitability, employee happiness, and social impact.

**TQM = the kitchen system. TQMEX = the kitchen system PLUS the Michelin star framework PLUS the culture PLUS the continuous improvement engine.**

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology with Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Metric / Framework |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality** | Degree to which a product/service meets customer requirements | Conformance to specifications (in **%**) |
| **TQM** | Total Quality Management | Philosophy + Tools |
| **Excellence** | Outstanding practice in managing the organization | **EFQM / Baldrige** scores |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous small-step improvement | **PDCA** cycle |
| **Six Sigma** | 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO) | **DPMO = 3.4** |
| **Benchmarking** | Learning from the best-in-class | Gap Analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core TQMEX Principle (the "Three Cs"):** **Customer**, **Continuous Improvement**, and **Culture**. Every TQMEX implementation must address all three to be successful.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Pillars of TQM (Foundational Building Blocks)

TQMEX rests on **eight universally accepted pillars**. These are direct KTU exam high-yield points.

1. **Customer Focus**
   - **Why:** The customer defines quality. Without customers, there is no business.
   - **How:** Capture Voice of Customer (VOC) via surveys, interviews, complaints analysis, Net Promoter Score (NPS).
   - **KTU Keyword:** *Customer satisfaction index (CSI)*.

2. **Total Employee Involvement / Participation**
   - **Why:** Quality is made by people, not by machines alone.
   - **How:** Quality circles, suggestion systems, cross-functional teams, empowerment.

3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**
   - **Why:** Static processes decay; competitors innovate.
   - **How:** PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act), DMAIC, root cause analysis.

4. **Process-Centric Approach**
   - **Why:** Better processes produce better outputs consistently.
   - **How:** SIPOC diagrams, process mapping, statistical process control (SPC), FMEA.

5. **Integrated System**
   - **Why:** Departments working in silos create local optimization and global chaos.
   - **How:** Horizontal deployment of quality across all functions (marketing, R&D, production, finance, HR).

6. **Strategic and Systematic Approach**
   - **Why:** Quality must be a strategic goal, not a side project.
   - **How:** Quality policy, quality objectives aligned to business strategy, Hoshin Kanri.

7. **Fact-Based Decision Making**
   - **Why:** Opinions vary; data is verifiable.
   - **How:** SPC charts, hypothesis testing, regression analysis, Pareto analysis.

8. **Effective Communication**
   - **Why:** A strategy not communicated is a strategy not executed.
   - **How:** Visual management, daily stand-ups, quality newsletters, training programs.

---

### 2.2 The Quality Gurus (KTU High-Yield — Frequently Asked)

| Guru | Key Contribution | Famous Phrase / Concept |
|---|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; PDSA cycle; System of Profound Knowledge | *"In God we trust, all others bring data"* |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Juran Trilogy (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement); Pareto Principle in quality | *"Fitness for use"* |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Zero Defects; Quality is Free; 4 Absolutes of Quality | *"Quality is conformance to requirements"* |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control concept | *"Quality is everyone's responsibility"* |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles; Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram; Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC) | *"Next to the worker, the foreman is most important"* |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss Function; Robust Design | Quality loss begins the moment specification is偏离 |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Process Control (Control Charts) | Father of SPC |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Questions on "Deming's 14 Points" or "Juran's Trilogy" appear almost every semester. Memorize the keyword phrases.

---

### 2.3 TQM Tools and Techniques Matrix

| Tool / Technique | Purpose | Category | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pareto Chart** | Identify the vital few causes (80/20 rule) | Diagnostic | High |
| **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone)** | Identify root causes of a problem | Diagnostic | High |
| **Check Sheet** | Data collection in structured format | Data Capture | Medium |
| **Histogram** | Visualize distribution of data | Data Analysis | High |
| **Scatter Diagram** | Study correlation between two variables | Data Analysis | Medium |
| **Control Charts** (Shewhart) | Monitor process stability over time | SPC Tool | **Very High** |
| **Flowchart** | Map a process visually | Process Mapping | Medium |
| **Stratification** | Separate data into meaningful subgroups | Data Analysis | Medium |
| **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) | Identify potential failures proactively | Preventive | High |
| **QFD** (Quality Function Deployment) | Translate customer needs into specs | Design | Medium |
| **5S Methodology** | Workplace organization | Foundation | High |
| **Poka-Yoke** (Error Proofing) | Prevent errors at source | Mistake Proofing | Medium |
| **Benchmarking** | Compare with best-in-class | Strategy | High |
| **Six Sigma / DMAIC** | Drive near-zero defect processes | Improvement | High |

---

### 2.4 The TQMEX Excellence Frameworks

TQMEX is incomplete without a reference excellence model. The two globally dominant models are:

**A. Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA) — USA**
- **Seven Criteria Categories:**
  1. Leadership
  2. Strategic Planning
  3. Customer Focus
  4. Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management
  5. Workforce Focus
  6. Operations Focus
  7. Results

**B. EFQM Excellence Model — Europe**
- **Nine Criteria:**
  1. Leadership
  2. Strategy
  3. People
  4. Partnerships & Resources
  5. Processes, Products & Services
  6. Customer Results
  7. People Results
  8. Society Results
  9. Business Results
- **Logic:** Enablers (1–5) → Results (6–9), scored via **RADAR** logic (Results, Approach, Deploy, Assess, Refine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The EFQM RADAR matrix is a high-yield topic. Each element scores up to a maximum percentage, and organizations must demonstrate evidence-based assessment and refinement.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Threshold | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Six Sigma Defect Rate | $\text{DPMO} = 3.4$ per million opportunities | 4.5 sigma $\approx$ 1 defect per 5,000 |
| Process Capability (Cp) | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | $\geq 1.33$ generally acceptable |
| Process Capability Index (Cpk) | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is industry standard |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost}$ | Excludes prevention cost in some models |
| Quality Cost Categories | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | PAF Model |
| Pareto Cumulative % | $\%\text{Cumulative} = \dfrac{\text{Cum. Frequency}}{\text{Total Frequency}} \times 100$ | 80% line separates vital few from trivial many |
| Deming's $1/\sqrt{n}$ Rule | Standard error $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | Reducing variability through sampling |
| FMEA Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Severity $\times$ Occurrence $\times$ Detection |
| Juran's Pareto Principle | $80\% \text{ of problems from } 20\% \text{ of causes}$ | Vital few / Trivial many |
| Taguchi's Loss Function | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ | $k$ = constant; $y$ = actual; $m$ = target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** Cp and Cpk are dimensionless ratios. DPMO is in defects per million. RPN is a unitless score. Loss $L$ is in monetary units (e.g., ₹, $).

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of TQMEX

- **Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System (TPS) integrates TQM, Kaizen, and Just-in-Time. Achieves 99.999% quality levels.
- **Software Engineering:** CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is essentially a TQMEX framework adapted for software.
- **Healthcare:** NABH accreditation in Indian hospitals uses TQMEX principles to reduce medical errors.
- **Service Sector:** ISO 9001 + EFQM adoption in banking, aviation, and IT services (TCS, Infosys).
- **Defense/Aerospace:** AS9100 standard borrows heavily from TQMEX excellence criteria.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** When answering KTU questions, always cite at least one real-world example (e.g., Toyota, Tata Steel, Bosch, or ISRO) to earn full conceptual marks.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive analytical derivations for the key TQMEX mathematical and logical tools. **No steps are skipped.**

---

### 3.1 Derivation of Process Capability Index (Cpk)

The Cpk index measures how well a process delivers output within specification limits, accounting for **process centering**.

**Step 1 — Define the problem.** A process has a mean $\bar{X}$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The specifications are USL (Upper Specification Limit) and LSL (Lower Specification Limit).

**Step 2 — Define the one-sided capability.** The distance from the process mean to the upper spec, expressed in standard deviations:

$$
Z_{\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{\sigma}
$$

This represents how many standard deviations fit between $\bar{X}$ and USL.

**Step 3 — Define the lower-sided capability.**

$$
Z_{\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into Cpk.** By convention, Cpk is the *minimum* of the two one-sided measures divided by 3 (to normalize to $\pm 3\sigma$):

$$
C_{pk} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \min(Z_{\text{upper}}, Z_{\text{lower}})
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.** Expanding:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation of values.**

| Cpk Value | Interpretation | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | Excellent — Six Sigma capability | Monitor |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | Capable | Standard operation |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Marginal | Improvement needed |
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | Not capable | Immediate corrective action |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** If the process is perfectly centered ($\bar{X}$ = midpoint of specs), then $C_p = C_{pk}$. Off-centering always reduces $C_{pk}$ below $C_p$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Six Sigma DPMO Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A manufacturing line produces 12,500 mobile phone casings. During a 30-day audit, 27 defects are detected across 5 opportunity types. Calculate DPMO and the corresponding sigma level.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**
- Units produced (U) = 12,500
- Total defects (D) = 27
- Number of opportunities per unit (O) = 5
- Total opportunities (TO) = $U \times O = 12{,}500 \times 5 = 62{,}500$

**Step 2 — Calculate Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO).**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{TO} \times 10^6 = \frac{27}{62{,}500} \times 10^6
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$
\text{DPMO} = 27 \times \frac{10^6}{62{,}500} = 27 \times 16 = 432
$$

**Step 4 — Convert DPMO to Sigma level using the Six Sigma conversion table.** The standard Six Sigma conversion is:

$$
\text{Sigma} = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times \ln(\text{DPMO})}
$$

Wait — for $\text{DPMO} < 3.4$ we use the standard form. For higher DPMO, a more practical conversion is:

$$
\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - \frac{432}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.999568
$$

**Step 5 — Convert Yield to Defects per Opportunity (DPO), then to Sigma.**

$$
\text{DPO} = 432 \times 10^{-6} = 0.000432
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the standard Z-transformation for process capability with 1.5 sigma shift.** Using the inverse normal distribution:

$$
Z_{\text{shifted}} = Z_{\text{ideal}} - 1.5
$$

From the standard normal table, $Z = 4.74$ corresponds to yield $\approx 0.999568$.

Therefore:

$$
\text{Sigma Level} = 4.74 + 1.5 = 6.24 \text{ sigma}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** The process operates at approximately **6.24 sigma**, which is world-class. Equivalent to 432 DPMO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Whenever you see a DPMO or sigma level question, always show all six steps: identify variables → calculate total opportunities → compute DPMO → derive yield → convert to sigma. Marks are awarded for each step, not just the final answer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN) with Decision Logic

**Step 1 — Define the three risk factors.**
- **S (Severity):** Impact of the failure on the customer (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic).
- **O (Occurrence):** Likelihood the failure occurs (1 = rare, 10 = very frequent).
- **D (Detection):** Difficulty of detecting the failure before reaching the customer (1 = easy, 10 = impossible to detect).

**Step 2 — Compute the Risk Priority Number.**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 3 — Classify the RPN value.**

| RPN Range | Action |
|---|---|
| $\text{RPN} \leq 30$ | Low risk — monitor |
| $30 < \text{RPN} \leq 100$ | Moderate — improvement recommended |
| $100 < \text{RPN} \leq 300$ | High — corrective action required |
| $\text{RPN} > 300$ | Critical — stop production, redesign |

**Step 4 — Worked Example.** A printed circuit board (PCB) has:
- $S = 9$ (severe functional failure)
- $O = 5$ (occurs in 1 in 200 boards)
- $D = 7$ (detected only by end-of-line test)

$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 5 \times 7 = 315
$$

Since $\text{RPN} > 300$, the failure mode is **critical** and requires immediate redesign or poka-yoke implementation.

---

### 3.4 Pareto Analysis: Full Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Collect raw data.** Count occurrences of each defect type over a period.

**Step 2 — Sort in descending order** of frequency.

**Step 3 — Compute cumulative count and cumulative percentage.**

**Step 4 — Plot the Pareto chart** (bar for frequency, line for cumulative %).

**Step 5 — Apply the 80/20 rule:** Defect types contributing to the first ~80% of cumulative frequency are the **"vital few"** that require immediate action.

**Worked Example (KTU typical):**

| Defect Type | Frequency | Cumulative Frequency | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Scratch | 42 | 42 | 52.5% |
| Dimensional Error | 18 | 60 | 75.0% |
| Color Mismatch | 10 | 70 | 87.5% |
| Packaging Tear | 6 | 76 | 95.0% |
| Label Misprint | 4 | 80 | 100.0% |
| **Total** | **80** | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** "Surface Scratch" and "Dimensional Error" together account for 75% of all defects (vital few). Focus 80% of improvement resources on these two categories.

---

### 3.5 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function — Full Derivation

**Step 1 — State the philosophy.** Taguchi rejected the "on-target = no loss" view. He argued that any deviation from target $m$ incurs loss to society.

**Step 2 — State the functional form.** Loss is proportional to the *square* of the deviation:

$$
L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the constant $k$.** Let $L_0$ be the loss at the specification limit (when $y = \Delta_0$, where $\Delta_0$ is the tolerance). Then:

$$
L_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the final form.**

$$
L(y) = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot (y - m)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** A bearing has target diameter $m = 50.00$ mm. Tolerance $\Delta_0 = 0.05$ mm. If the loss at the tolerance limit is $L_0 = \text{₹}400$, find the loss when $y = 50.03$ mm.

**Step 6 — Apply the formula.**

$$
L(50.03) = \frac{400}{(0.05)^2} \cdot (50.03 - 50.00)^2
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate.**

$$
L(50.03) = \frac{400}{0.0025} \cdot (0.03)^2 = 160{,}000 \times 0.0009 = \text{₹}144
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation.** A deviation of 0.03 mm (within tolerance!) still causes a loss of ₹144 to the customer and to society. This is the core of Taguchi's philosophy.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which TQM Tool

| Problem Type | Primary Tool | Secondary Tool | KTU Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process out of control | Control Chart | Fishbone + Pareto | 7–14 marks |
| High defect rate | Six Sigma DMAIC | FMEA + Kaizen | 14 marks |
| Customer dissatisfaction | QFD / VOC analysis | Kano Model | 7–14 marks |
| Variable deviation from target | Taguchi Loss Function | Robust Design | 7 marks |
| Workplace disorganization | 5S | Visual Management | 3–7 marks |
| Strategic improvement | EFQM / Baldrige | Benchmarking | 14 marks |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The TQMEX Pyramid — Hierarchical Foundation of Excellence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base1[Foundation: 5S and Visual Management]
    base2[Foundation: Employee Training and Quality Culture]
    base1 --> m1
    base2 --> m1
    m1[Layer 1: ISO 9001 Quality Management System]
    m1 --> m2
    m2[Layer 2: Statistical Process Control SPC and Six Sigma DMAIC]
    m2 --> m3
    m3[Layer 3: Continuous Improvement Kaizen PDCA]
    m3 --> m4
    m4[Layer 4: Excellence Frameworks EFQM and Baldrige]
    m4 --> apex
    apex[Apex: World Class Excellence and Business Results]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pyramid shows that TQMEX is *built* from the bottom up. You cannot achieve world-class excellence (apex) without first establishing 5S, then ISO 9001, then SPC, then continuous improvement, then a recognized excellence model. This is a KTU high-yield schematic.

---

### 4.2 Deming's PDCA (PDSA) Cycle — The Engine of Continuous Improvement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan: Identify problem and analyze root cause]
    D1[Do: Implement small scale pilot solution]
    C1[Check: Measure results against expectations]
    A1[Act: Standardize if successful or revise plan]
    A1 --> P1
    D1 --> C1
    C1 --> A1
    P1 --> D1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Improvement is *cyclic*, not linear. Each completed cycle raises the process to a higher baseline. This is the heart of Kaizen and the foundation of all ISO 9001 and EFQM deployment.

---

### 4.3 TQMEX Implementation Roadmap — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Stage 1: Management Commitment and Leadership]
    start --> s2
    s2[Stage 2: Vision Mission and Quality Policy Definition]
    s2 --> s3
    s3[Stage 3: Customer Requirement Capture VOC]
    s3 --> s4
    s4[Stage 4: Process Mapping and Baseline Measurement]
    s4 --> s5
    s5[Stage 5: Statistical Tools Deployment SPC Pareto Fishbone]
    s5 --> s6
    s6[Stage 6: Employee Training and Quality Circles]
    s6 --> s7
    s7[Stage 7: Supplier Partnership and Integrated Supply Chain]
    s7 --> s8
    s8[Stage 8: Performance Review using EFQM or Baldrige Criteria]
    s8 --> s9
    s9[Stage 9: Recognition Benchmarking and Continuous Improvement]
    s9 --> loop{Improvement Achieved?}
    loop -->|No| s4
    loop -->|Yes| award[Stage 10: Excellence Award Certification and Sustenance]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the standard 10-stage TQMEX implementation roadmap. The feedback loop from Stage 9 back to Stage 4 ensures that improvement is never declared "complete" — it is a continuous cycle.

---

### 4.4 TQMEX Cause-and-Effect Framework (Fishbone of Excellence)

```mermaid
fishbone
    Title[TQMEX Failure Causes]
    Outcome[Excellence Not Achieved]
    Outcome --> Cat1
    Outcome --> Cat2
    Outcome --> Cat3
    Outcome --> Cat4
    Cat1[Methods: Poor processes no SPC]
    Cat2[Manpower: No training low involvement]
    Cat3[Machines: Old equipment no calibration]
    Cat4[Materials: Poor supplier quality no inspection]
    Cat5[Measurement: No data wrong metrics]
    Cat6[Environment: Culture resistant to change]
    Outcome --> Cat5
    Outcome --> Cat6
```

**Reading the diagram:** A typical Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram showing the six main categories of why TQMEX initiatives fail. This diagram type is a **very high frequency KTU question** in both short-answer and 14-mark formats.

---

### 4.5 The Juran Trilogy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QP[Quality Planning: Identify customers and their needs]
    QC[Quality Control: Evaluate actual performance vs goals]
    QI[Quality Improvement: Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement]
    QP --> QC
    QC --> QI
    QI -.-> QP
```

**Reading the diagram:** Juran's three managerial processes form a continuous loop, mirroring the Deming cycle but with different focus areas.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four key principles of TQM."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Total Quality Management (TQM) is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization, from top management to frontline workers, with the ultimate goal of achieving long-term customer satisfaction and organizational excellence.

**Four Key Principles (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Customer Focus:** The customer is the ultimate judge of quality. All activities revolve around understanding and meeting customer needs.
2. **Total Employee Involvement:** Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor worker, is responsible for and empowered to improve quality.
3. **Process-Centered Approach:** Quality is built into the process; statistical process control is used to monitor and improve processes.
4. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Improvement is a never-ending journey, achieved through the PDCA cycle and incremental innovations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "quality control" or "quality assurance" as their definition. TQM is a *management philosophy*, not a tool. Always emphasize the *organization-wide* and *continuous* aspects in your definition.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the concept of Kaizen and its role in TQMEX."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept of Kaizen (2 marks):**
Kaizen is a Japanese term meaning "continuous improvement." It is a long-term, systematic approach to small, incremental improvements in processes, products, and services involving all employees. The philosophy is that small, frequent improvements (typically 1–10% gains) compound into major gains over time, and are more sustainable than large, disruptive changes. The two pillars of Kaizen are: (i) **Process Kaizen** (improving how work is done) and (ii) **Product Kaizen** (improving the output itself).

**Role in TQMEX (1 mark):**
In TQMEX, Kaizen is the *operational engine* that drives the excellence models (EFQM/Baldrige) from "good" to "great." Without Kaizen, the TQMEX framework remains a static compliance document; with Kaizen, it becomes a living, breathing system of continuous value creation. The PDCA cycle used in Kaizen is the same PDCA used in EFQM RADAR logic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse **Kaizen** with **Six Sigma**. Kaizen focuses on *small, frequent, cultural* improvements; Six Sigma focuses on *large, data-driven, project-based* reductions in defects. Both are part of TQMEX, but they are not the same.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
