> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Process Capability and Process Control (Cp, Cpk & State of Statistical Control)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Statistical Quality Control (SQC), the **Relationship Between Process Capability and Process Control** describes the critical engineering connection between two distinct statistical assessments of a manufacturing process:

1. **Process Control (State of Statistical Control):** Assessed using **Control Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c charts). A process is deemed "in control" if only common-cause (random) variation is present, meaning all plotted points fall within the **Control Limits** (UCL, LCL) with no non-random patterns.

2. **Process Capability:** Assessed using **Capability Indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$). It measures the inherent ability of a stable process to produce output within **Engineering Specification Limits** (USL, LSL) set by the customer/design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Process capability indices are **mathematically valid ONLY when the process is first proven to be in statistical control.** Using $C_p$ or $C_{pk}$ on an unstable process is a fundamental error that KTU examiners heavily penalize.

### 1.2 The Two Dimensions of Process Performance

| Dimension | Tool Used | Limits Involved | What it Tells Us |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Stability** | Control Charts (X̄-R, X̄-S) | Control Limits (UCL, LCL) | Is the process consistent over time? |
| **Capability** | Capability Indices | Specification Limits (USL, LSL) | Can the process meet customer requirements? |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shooting Target" Intuition

Imagine you are an archer shooting arrows at a target:

- **🎯 Process Control (Stability):** Asks *"Are your arrows landing in a TIGHT cluster?"* — Even if you are consistently missing the bullseye, if your arrows land close to each other, you are **in statistical control** (consistent, predictable, repeatable).
- **🎯 Process Capability:** Asks *"Is your cluster inside the bullseye (specification limits)?"* — If your tight cluster is positioned perfectly on the bullseye, your process is both **in control AND capable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A process can be **in control but not capable** (tight cluster, wrong location), and a process can be **capable on average but not in control** (wide cluster that occasionally hits the target). The relationship between these states defines the four quadrants of process performance.

## 1.4 The Four States of a Process (Fundamental Relationship)

The relationship between control and capability produces **four possible process states**:

| State | Control Status | Capability | Description | Action Required |
|-------|---------------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **State 1** | In Control | Capable | Ideal State | Continue monitoring |
| **State 2** | In Control | Not Capable | Consistent but off-target | Adjust process mean (re-center) |
| **State 3** | Out of Control | Capable | Wide variation occasionally hits target | Reduce variation (eliminate special causes) |
| **State 4** | Out of Control | Not Capable | Chaos | Complete process overhaul |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capability Index $C_p$ — Theoretical Foundation

The **Process Capability Index $C_p$** quantifies the relationship between the **specified tolerance (engineering allowance)** and the **actual process spread (natural variation)**. It is the ratio of the "allowable width" to the "actual width" of the process.

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

where:
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit
- $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of the process (estimated from sample data)
- $6\sigma$ = The natural process spread covering **99.73%** of all measurements (per the Empirical Rule)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $C_p$ assumes the process mean ($\mu$) is perfectly centered between USL and LSL. It is a measure of **potential capability** — the best-case scenario. It does NOT detect off-center processes.

### 2.2 The Capability Index $C_{pk}$ — The "Real-World" Index

The **$C_{pk}$ index** corrects the major flaw of $C_p$ by accounting for **process centering**. It measures the actual one-sided capability relative to the nearest specification limit.

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

where $\bar{X}$ is the process mean.

**The Critical Relationship Between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the **centering index (process shift factor)**:

$$k = \frac{\vert \bar{X} - M \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2} = \frac{2\vert \bar{X} - M \vert}{USL - LSL}$$

and $M = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$ is the midpoint of the specification range.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---------|------------|--------------------|-------|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Ratio of tolerance to process spread | Dimensionless |
| **One-Sided Upper Capability** | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ | Distance from mean to upper limit | Dimensionless |
| **One-Sided Lower Capability** | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ | Distance from mean to lower limit | Dimensionless |
| **Overall Capability** | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Worst-case one-sided capability | Dimensionless |
| **Centering Index** | $k = \dfrac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}$ | Degree of mean shift from center | Dimensionless |
| **$C_{pk}$ in terms of $C_p$** | $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ | Capability adjusted for shift | Dimensionless |
| **Estimated Sigma (from R-bar)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Standard deviation estimate | Same as data |
| **Estimated Sigma (from S-bar)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{s}}{c_4}$ | Standard deviation estimate | Same as data |
| **Control Limits (X̄ chart)** | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$ | Upper control limit | Same as data |
| **Control Limits (X̄ chart)** | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$ | Lower control limit | Same as data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** When using $C_{pk}$ formula, students often forget to use $\hat{\sigma}$ (estimated sigma) rather than the population $\sigma$. Use $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ for X̄-R charts.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

| Industry | Typical $C_{pk}$ Required | Why? |
|----------|--------------------------|------|
| **Automotive (Safety parts)** | $\geq 1.67$ | Six-sigma level quality |
| **Semiconductor Manufacturing** | $\geq 1.50$ | Zero-defect expectations |
| **General Manufacturing** | $\geq 1.33$ | Standard industrial benchmark |
| **Non-critical Components** | $\geq 1.00$ | Minimum acceptable (3σ on each side) |
| **Existing Process (Pilot)** | $\geq 1.00$ | Process just meets limits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Six-Sigma Connection:** A $C_{pk} = 2.00$ corresponds to a Six-Sigma process (allowing 1.5σ drift over time), yielding only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. This is the ultimate goal of the relationship between capability and control.

## 2.5 Why the Relationship Matters: The Sequential Logic

```
Step 1: Achieve Statistical Control FIRST (remove special causes)
            ↓
Step 2: Verify Stability using Control Charts
            ↓
Step 3: THEN Calculate Capability Indices
            ↓
Step 4: If Cp ≥ 1.33 AND Cpk ≥ 1.33 → Process is capable
            ↓
Step 5: If Cp is high but Cpk is low → Mean is shifted → Re-center
            ↓
Step 6: If both are low → Reduce common-cause variation
```

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Empirical Rule to $C_p$ Formula

The relationship between process capability and process control is rooted in the **Empirical Rule of Normal Distribution** (also called the 68-95-99.7 Rule).

**Step 1 — Establish the Process Spread (6σ Width):**

For any stable, normally distributed process, the natural variation spans $\mu - 3\sigma$ to $\mu + 3\sigma$, covering 99.73% of all output. The total width is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process Width} &= (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) \\
&= \mu + 3\sigma - \mu + 3\sigma \\
&= 6\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the Specification Width (Tolerance):**

The engineering tolerance is the distance between the design specification limits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specification Width} &= USL - LSL
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Capability Ratio ($C_p$):**

A process is capable when its natural spread (6σ) is **smaller** than the specification width. The ratio is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result:**

- $C_p = 1.00$ → Process spread exactly equals specification width (zero margin)
- $C_p > 1.00$ → Process fits inside specifications
- $C_p < 1.00$ → Process produces defects even when perfectly centered

## 3.2 Derivation: From $C_p$ to $C_{pk}$ (Accounting for Centering)

**Step 1 — Define the Midpoint of Specifications:**

$$
M = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express One-Sided Distance to Upper Limit:**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 3 — Express One-Sided Distance to Lower Limit:**

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Limitation of $C_p$:**

$C_p$ assumes $\bar{X} = M$. If the mean shifts, $C_p$ remains unchanged but defects increase. We need an index that captures the **worst-case** one-sided capability.

**Step 5 — Define $C_{pk}$ as the Minimum:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})
$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic Derivation of $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \cdot \min\left(USL - \bar{X}, \bar{X} - LSL\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \cdot \left[\frac{(USL - LSL)}{2} - \mid \bar{X} - M \mid\right] \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}\right] \\
&= C_p \cdot (1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **centering factor** is:

$$
k = \frac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}, \quad 0 \leq k \leq 1
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Examination Style

> **Problem:** A manufacturing process produces piston rings with specifications $USL = 2.010$ cm and $LSL = 1.990$ cm. Twenty-five samples of size 5 yielded $\bar{\bar{X}} = 1.998$ cm and $\bar{R} = 0.008$ cm. The process is verified to be in statistical control. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and interpret the result. ($d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$)

**Step 1 — Estimate the Process Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.008}{2.326} \\
&= 0.003439 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $C_p$ (Potential Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} \\
&= \frac{2.010 - 1.990}{6 \times 0.003439} \\
&= \frac{0.020}{0.020636} \\
&= 0.9691
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Midpoint:**

$$
M = \frac{USL + LSL}{2} = \frac{2.010 + 1.990}{2} = 2.000 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Centering Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{2 \mid \bar{\bar{X}} - M \mid}{USL - LSL} \\
&= \frac{2 \mid 1.998 - 2.000 \mid}{0.020} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.002}{0.020} \\
&= 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ (Actual Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= C_p(1 - k) \\
&= 0.9691 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
&= 0.9691 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.7753
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Using Direct $C_{pk}$ Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{2.010 - 1.998}{3 \times 0.003439} = \frac{0.012}{0.010318} = 1.1630 \\
C_{pl} &= \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{1.998 - 1.990}{3 \times 0.003439} = \frac{0.008}{0.010318} = 0.7753 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.1630, 0.7753) = 0.7753 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- $C_p = 0.9691 < 1.00$ → Process spread is **wider** than the specification width. Even if perfectly centered, the process would produce defects.
- $C_{pk} = 0.7753 < C_p$ → Indicates the process is **off-center** (mean shifted toward LSL).
- $C_{pl}$ is the binding constraint → Process is encroaching on the **lower specification limit**.
- **Recommendation:** Reduce common-cause variation AND re-center the process mean toward 2.000 cm.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules

| Condition | Relationship | Diagnosis | Recommended Action |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| $C_p \geq 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | Capable & Centered | Excellent process | Maintain current controls |
| $C_p \geq 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Capable but Shifted | Off-center | Adjust process mean |
| $C_p < 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} \approx C_p$ | Not Capable, Centered | Excess variation | Reduce σ (improve equipment) |
| $C_p = C_{pk}$ | Perfectly Centered | $k = 0$ | No centering adjustment needed |
| $C_{pk} < 0$ | Mean outside specifications | Catastrophic | Stop production immediately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Invariant Relationship:** Since $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ and $0 \leq k \leq 1$, it is mathematically guaranteed that $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ **always**. This is a critical KTU conceptual point.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Sequential Relationship Between Control and Capability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Under Study] --> B[Phase 1: Data Collection]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Construct Control Charts X-bar and R]
    C --> D{Is Process In Statistical Control?}
    D -->|No - Special Causes Present| E[Investigate and Remove Assignable Causes]
    E --> C
    D -->|Yes - Stable Process| F[Phase 3: Estimate Process Sigma]
    F --> G[Sigma hat = R bar divided by d2]
    G --> H[Phase 4: Calculate Cp Index]
    H --> I[Phase 5: Calculate Cpk Index]
    I --> J{Cpk greater than or equal to 1.33?}
    J -->|No| K{Cp greater than Cpk significantly?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Process Off-Center: Re-center Mean]
    K -->|No| M[Process Has Excess Variation: Reduce Sigma]
    L --> F
    M --> F
    J -->|Yes| N[Process is Capable and Stable]
    N --> O[Continue Routine Monitoring with Control Charts]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Two-Dimensional Process Performance Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dimension1[Dimension 1 - Process Stability]
        SC1[In Control] 
        SC2[Out of Control]
    end
    subgraph Dimension2[Dimension 2 - Process Capability]
        CP1[Capable - Cpk greater than 1.33]
        CP2[Not Capable - Cpk less than 1.33]
    end
    SC1 -->|State 1| IDEAL[State 1: Ideal - Both Stable and Capable]
    SC1 -->|State 2| RE_CENTER[State 2: Stable but Not Capable]
    SC2 -->|State 3| REDUCE_VAR[State 3: Capable on Average but Unstable]
    SC2 -->|State 4| CHAOS[State 4: Unstable and Incapable - Worst Case]
    CP1 --> IDEAL
    CP2 --> RE_CENTER
    CP1 --> REDUCE_VAR
    CP2 --> CHAOS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Relationship Between Cp, Cpk, and k

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA[Process Data: X-double-bar, R-bar, USL, LSL] --> EST[Estimate Sigma hat]
    EST --> CPCALC[Calculate Cp = USL minus LSL divided by 6 Sigma hat]
    DATA --> MID[Calculate Midpoint M]
    MID --> KCALC[Calculate Centering Factor k]
    KCALC --> REL[Apply Relationship: Cpk equals Cp times 1 minus k]
    CPCALC --> REL
    REL --> CPKVAL[Final Cpk Value]
    DATA --> CPKDIRECT[Direct Calculation: Cpk equals min of Cpu and Cpl]
    CPKDIRECT --> CPKVAL
    CPKVAL --> DECISION{Decision Rule: Cpk greater than 1.33?}
    DECISION -->|Yes| ACCEPT[Accept Process]
    DECISION -->|No| REJECT[Improve Process]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Cp vs Cpk Visualization

```
        USL (Upper Specification Limit)              M (Midpoint)         LSL (Lower Specification Limit)
              |                                            |                        |
   ───────────●════════════════════════════════════════════●═══════════════════════●───────────
              ▲                                            ▲                        ▲
              │  USL - X̄ (Upper distance)                 │  X̄ - LSL (Lower dist)  │
              │                                            │                        │
              └──────────────── 6σ Process Spread ─────────┴────────────────────────┘
                                         (μ ± 3σ)

  Cp = (USL - LSL) / 6σ
  Cpk = min[(USL - X̄)/3σ , (X̄ - LSL)/3σ]
  
  When process is centered: Cp = Cpk
  When process shifts:     Cpk < Cp  (the greater the shift, the smaller Cpk becomes)
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Process Capability Index $C_p$ and explain its limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Process Capability Index $C_p$ is defined as the ratio of the specification width to the process width:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

It measures the **potential capability** of a process, assuming the mean is perfectly centered. **(1 Mark)**

**Limitations:** **(2 Marks)**
1. It does not account for **process centering** — a process can have a high $C_p$ but still produce defects if the mean is shifted.
2. It assumes the process is in statistical control; it is invalid for unstable processes.
3. It does not distinguish between one-sided and two-sided specifications.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. When is $C_p = C_{pk}$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The relationship is:

$$C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the centering index. **(2 Marks)**

$C_p = C_{pk}$ when **$k = 0$**, i.e., when the process mean $\bar{X}$ exactly equals the specification midpoint $M = (USL + LSL)/2$. This means the process is **perfectly centered**. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the relationship between statistical process control and process capability. Discuss the four possible states of a process with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A drilling machine produces holes with $USL = 12.60$ mm and $LSL = 12.40$ mm. From 20 samples of size 4, the grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.48$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.05$ mm. The process is in statistical control. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and interpret the result. Use $d_2 = 2.059$ for $n = 4$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Statistical Process Control (SPC) and Process Capability are two complementary but distinct evaluations. SPC uses **control charts** to verify **stability** (consistency over time), while process capability uses **indices** to verify the ability to meet **specifications** (customer requirements).

**Sequential Relationship (2 Marks):**
1. **First**, achieve statistical control by removing all assignable (special) causes using X̄-R or X̄-S control charts.
2. **Then**, calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ only on the stable process.

**Four States of a Process (3 Marks):**

| State | Control | Capability | Meaning | Action |
|-------|---------|------------|---------|--------|
| 1 | In Control | Capable | Ideal — tight cluster inside specs | Continue monitoring |
| 2 | In Control | Not Capable | Off-center or too wide | Re-center / reduce σ |
| 3 | Out of Control | Capable | Unstable but on target | Remove special causes |
| 4 | Out of Control | Not Capable | Worst case | Complete overhaul |

State 1 is the desired state. State 2 indicates the process mean has shifted. State 3 indicates special causes are creating wide swings. State 4 requires immediate intervention.

**Diagrammatic Representation (1 Mark):** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the four-state matrix)

---

### **Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Estimate Process Standard Deviation (1 Mark):**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.05}{2.059} = 0.02428 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $C_p$ (2 Marks):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{12.60 - 12.40}{6 \times 0.02428} = \frac{0.20}{0.14570} = 1.3727
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Midpoint and Centering Index (1 Mark):**

$$
M = \frac{12.60 + 12.40}{2} = 12.50 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
k = \frac{2 \mid \bar{\bar{X}} - M \mid}{USL - LSL} = \frac{2 \mid 12.48 - 12.50 \mid}{0.20} = \frac{0.04}{0.20} = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ (2 Marks):**

$$
C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k) = 1.3727 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1.3727 \times 0.80 = 1.0982
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation (1 Mark):**

- $C_p = 1.37 > 1.33$ → Process is potentially capable.
- $C_{pk} = 1.10 < 1.33$ → Actual capability is below industrial benchmark.
- The process is **off-center** (shifted toward LSL by 0.02 mm).
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process mean to $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.50$ mm to improve $C_{pk}$ to 1.37.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for $C_{pk}$ in terms of $C_p$ and the centering index $k$. Explain why $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ always. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a CNC turning process, the specification limits are $USL = 25.05$ mm and $LSL = 24.95$ mm. Twenty samples of size 5 give $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.99$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.025$ mm, and the process is in control. Calculate (i) Process capability index $C_p$ (ii) Process capability index $C_{pk}$ (iii) The percentage of parts within specifications. Use $d_2 = 2.326$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of $C_p$ (1 Mark):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Definition of $C_{pk}$ (1 Mark):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \min(USL - \bar{X}, \bar{X} - LSL) \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \left[\frac{USL - LSL}{2} - \mid \bar{X} - M \mid\right] \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \left[1 - \frac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}\right] \\
&= C_p(1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k = \dfrac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}$ is the centering index and $0 \leq k \leq 1$.

**Proof that $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ (2 Marks):** Since $0 \leq k \leq 1$, the term $(1 - k)$ lies between 0 and 1. Therefore:

$$
0 \leq (1 - k) \leq 1 \implies C_{pk} = C_p(1-k) \leq C_p
$$

Equality holds only when $k = 0$ (process perfectly centered).

---

### **Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Estimate σ and Calculate $C_p$ (3 Marks):**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.025}{2.326} = 0.010748 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.010748} = \frac{0.10}{0.06449} = 1.5505
$$

**(ii) Calculate $C_{pk}$ (2 Marks):**

$$
M = \frac{25.05 + 24.95}{2} = 25.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
k = \frac{2 \mid 24.99 - 25.00 \mid}{0.10} = \frac{0.02}{0.10} = 0.20
$$

$$
C_{pk} = 1.5505 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1.5505 \times 0.80 = 1.2404
$$

**(iii) Percentage of Parts Within Specifications (2 Marks):**

The Z-scores for each specification limit:

$$
Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 24.99}{0.010748} = 5.585
$$

$$
Z_{LSL} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{24.99 - 24.95}{0.010748} = 3.724
$$

From the standard normal table:
- $P(Z \leq 5.585) \approx 1.0000$
- $P(Z \leq 3.724) \approx 0.99990$

Percentage within specifications:

$$
P(LSL \leq X \leq USL) = 0.99990 - (1 - 1.0000) = 0.99990 = 99.99\%
$$

Approximately **99.99% of parts are within specifications.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\hat{\sigma}$ (estimated sigma)** instead of population σ. Always use $\bar{R}/d_2$ for X̄-R charts.
> 2. **Calculating capability before establishing control.** KTU explicitly penalizes this — capability is meaningless on an unstable process.
> 3. **Misapplying $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$:** Use $C_p$ only when the process is perfectly centered. Use $C_{pk}$ for real-world off-center processes.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Ensure USL, LSL, $\bar{R}$, and $\bar{\bar{X}}$ are all in the **same units** before substitution.
> 5. **Sign errors in k:** Always take the **absolute value** of $(\bar{X} - M)$.
> 6. **Forgetting the "1-" in the relationship:** A frequent error is writing $C_{pk} = C_p \cdot k$ instead of $C_p(1-k)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Relationship Between Process Control and Process Capability:**

- ⭐ **Precedence Rule:** Control FIRST, Capability SECOND. Never calculate $C_p$ or $C_{pk}$ on an unstable process.
- ⭐ **$C_p$ Formula:** $C_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$ — Assumes perfect centering.
- ⭐ **$C_{pk}$ Formula:** $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ — Worst-case one-sided capability.
- ⭐ **Master Relationship:** $C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$ where $k$ is the centering index.
- ⭐ **Centering Index:** $k = 2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid / (USL - LSL)$, with range $0 \leq k \leq 1$.
- ⭐ **Invariant Inequality:** $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ (always true, mathematically guaranteed).
- ⭐ **Equality Condition:** $C_{pk} = C_p$ **only** when $k = 0$ (process perfectly centered).
- ⭐ **Sigma Estimation:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ for X̄-R charts; $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{s}/c_4$ for X̄-S charts.
- ⭐ **Industrial Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for capable process; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for Six-Sigma quality.
- ⭐ **Four States:** (1) In Control & Capable, (2) In Control & Not Capable, (3) Out of Control & Capable, (4) Out of Control & Not Capable.
- ⭐ **Decision Rule:** If $C_p$ high but $C_{pk}$ low → Re-center. If both low → Reduce variation. If both high → Excellent, maintain.
- ⭐ **Six-Sigma Link:** $C_{pk} = 2.00$ corresponds to 3.4 DPMO (Six-Sigma quality standard).
- ⭐ **Practical Tip:** Always report $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ together to reveal both potential and actual capability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Process Capability and Process Control (Cp, Cpk & State of Statistical Control)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Statistical Quality Control (SQC), the **Relationship Between Process Capability and Process Control** describes the critical engineering connection between two distinct statistical assessments of a manufacturing process:

1. **Process Control (State of Statistical Control):** Assessed using **Control Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c charts). A process is deemed "in control" if only common-cause (random) variation is present, meaning all plotted points fall within the **Control Limits** (UCL, LCL) with no non-random patterns.

2. **Process Capability:** Assessed using **Capability Indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$). It measures the inherent ability of a stable process to produce output within **Engineering Specification Limits** (USL, LSL) set by the customer/design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Process capability indices are **mathematically valid ONLY when the process is first proven to be in statistical control.** Using $C_p$ or $C_{pk}$ on an unstable process is a fundamental error that KTU examiners heavily penalize.

### 1.2 The Two Dimensions of Process Performance

| Dimension | Tool Used | Limits Involved | What it Tells Us |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Stability** | Control Charts (X̄-R, X̄-S) | Control Limits (UCL, LCL) | Is the process consistent over time? |
| **Capability** | Capability Indices | Specification Limits (USL, LSL) | Can the process meet customer requirements? |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shooting Target" Intuition

Imagine you are an archer shooting arrows at a target:

- **🎯 Process Control (Stability):** Asks *"Are your arrows landing in a TIGHT cluster?"* — Even if you are consistently missing the bullseye, if your arrows land close to each other, you are **in statistical control** (consistent, predictable, repeatable).
- **🎯 Process Capability:** Asks *"Is your cluster inside the bullseye (specification limits)?"* — If your tight cluster is positioned perfectly on the bullseye, your process is both **in control AND capable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A process can be **in control but not capable** (tight cluster, wrong location), and a process can be **capable on average but not in control** (wide cluster that occasionally hits the target). The relationship between these states defines the four quadrants of process performance.

## 1.4 The Four States of a Process (Fundamental Relationship)

The relationship between control and capability produces **four possible process states**:

| State | Control Status | Capability | Description | Action Required |
|-------|---------------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **State 1** | In Control | Capable | Ideal State | Continue monitoring |
| **State 2** | In Control | Not Capable | Consistent but off-target | Adjust process mean (re-center) |
| **State 3** | Out of Control | Capable | Wide variation occasionally hits target | Reduce variation (eliminate special causes) |
| **State 4** | Out of Control | Not Capable | Chaos | Complete process overhaul |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capability Index $C_p$ — Theoretical Foundation

The **Process Capability Index $C_p$** quantifies the relationship between the **specified tolerance (engineering allowance)** and the **actual process spread (natural variation)**. It is the ratio of the "allowable width" to the "actual width" of the process.

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

where:
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit
- $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of the process (estimated from sample data)
- $6\sigma$ = The natural process spread covering **99.73%** of all measurements (per the Empirical Rule)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $C_p$ assumes the process mean ($\mu$) is perfectly centered between USL and LSL. It is a measure of **potential capability** — the best-case scenario. It does NOT detect off-center processes.

### 2.2 The Capability Index $C_{pk}$ — The "Real-World" Index

The **$C_{pk}$ index** corrects the major flaw of $C_p$ by accounting for **process centering**. It measures the actual one-sided capability relative to the nearest specification limit.

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

where $\bar{X}$ is the process mean.

**The Critical Relationship Between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the **centering index (process shift factor)**:

$$k = \frac{\vert \bar{X} - M \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2} = \frac{2\vert \bar{X} - M \vert}{USL - LSL}$$

and $M = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$ is the midpoint of the specification range.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---------|------------|--------------------|-------|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Ratio of tolerance to process spread | Dimensionless |
| **One-Sided Upper Capability** | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ | Distance from mean to upper limit | Dimensionless |
| **One-Sided Lower Capability** | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ | Distance from mean to lower limit | Dimensionless |
| **Overall Capability** | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Worst-case one-sided capability | Dimensionless |
| **Centering Index** | $k = \dfrac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}$ | Degree of mean shift from center | Dimensionless |
| **$C_{pk}$ in terms of $C_p$** | $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ | Capability adjusted for shift | Dimensionless |
| **Estimated Sigma (from R-bar)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Standard deviation estimate | Same as data |
| **Estimated Sigma (from S-bar)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{s}}{c_4}$ | Standard deviation estimate | Same as data |
| **Control Limits (X̄ chart)** | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$ | Upper control limit | Same as data |
| **Control Limits (X̄ chart)** | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$ | Lower control limit | Same as data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** When using $C_{pk}$ formula, students often forget to use $\hat{\sigma}$ (estimated sigma) rather than the population $\sigma$. Use $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ for X̄-R charts.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

| Industry | Typical $C_{pk}$ Required | Why? |
|----------|--------------------------|------|
| **Automotive (Safety parts)** | $\geq 1.67$ | Six-sigma level quality |
| **Semiconductor Manufacturing** | $\geq 1.50$ | Zero-defect expectations |
| **General Manufacturing** | $\geq 1.33$ | Standard industrial benchmark |
| **Non-critical Components** | $\geq 1.00$ | Minimum acceptable (3σ on each side) |
| **Existing Process (Pilot)** | $\geq 1.00$ | Process just meets limits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Six-Sigma Connection:** A $C_{pk} = 2.00$ corresponds to a Six-Sigma process (allowing 1.5σ drift over time), yielding only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. This is the ultimate goal of the relationship between capability and control.

## 2.5 Why the Relationship Matters: The Sequential Logic

```
Step 1: Achieve Statistical Control FIRST (remove special causes)
            ↓
Step 2: Verify Stability using Control Charts
            ↓
Step 3: THEN Calculate Capability Indices
            ↓
Step 4: If Cp ≥ 1.33 AND Cpk ≥ 1.33 → Process is capable
            ↓
Step 5: If Cp is high but Cpk is low → Mean is shifted → Re-center
            ↓
Step 6: If both are low → Reduce common-cause variation
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Empirical Rule to $C_p$ Formula

The relationship between process capability and process control is rooted in the **Empirical Rule of Normal Distribution** (also called the 68-95-99.7 Rule).

**Step 1 — Establish the Process Spread (6σ Width):**

For any stable, normally distributed process, the natural variation spans $\mu - 3\sigma$ to $\mu + 3\sigma$, covering 99.73% of all output. The total width is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process Width} &= (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) \\
&= \mu + 3\sigma - \mu + 3\sigma \\
&= 6\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the Specification Width (Tolerance):**

The engineering tolerance is the distance between the design specification limits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specification Width} &= USL - LSL
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Capability Ratio ($C_p$):**

A process is capable when its natural spread (6σ) is **smaller** than the specification width. The ratio is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result:**

- $C_p = 1.00$ → Process spread exactly equals specification width (zero margin)
- $C_p > 1.00$ → Process fits inside specifications
- $C_p < 1.00$ → Process produces defects even when perfectly centered

## 3.2 Derivation: From $C_p$ to $C_{pk}$ (Accounting for Centering)

**Step 1 — Define the Midpoint of Specifications:**

$$
M = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express One-Sided Distance to Upper Limit:**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 3 — Express One-Sided Distance to Lower Limit:**

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Limitation of $C_p$:**

$C_p$ assumes $\bar{X} = M$. If the mean shifts, $C_p$ remains unchanged but defects increase. We need an index that captures the **worst-case** one-sided capability.

**Step 5 — Define $C_{pk}$ as the Minimum:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})
$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic Derivation of $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \cdot \min\left(USL - \bar{X}, \bar{X} - LSL\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \cdot \left[\frac{(USL - LSL)}{2} - \mid \bar{X} - M \mid\right] \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}\right] \\
&= C_p \cdot (1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **centering factor** is:

$$
k = \frac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}, \quad 0 \leq k \leq 1
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Examination Style

> **Problem:** A manufacturing process produces piston rings with specifications $USL = 2.010$ cm and $LSL = 1.990$ cm. Twenty-five samples of size 5 yielded $\bar{\bar{X}} = 1.998$ cm and $\bar{R} = 0.008$ cm. The process is verified to be in statistical control. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and interpret the result. ($d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$)

**Step 1 — Estimate the Process Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.008}{2.326} \\
&= 0.003439 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $C_p$ (Potential Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} \\
&= \frac{2.010 - 1.990}{6 \times 0.003439} \\
&= \frac{0.020}{0.020636} \\
&= 0.9691
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Midpoint:**

$$
M = \frac{USL + LSL}{2} = \frac{2.010 + 1.990}{2} = 2.000 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Centering Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{2 \mid \bar{\bar{X}} - M \mid}{USL - LSL} \\
&= \frac{2 \mid 1.998 - 2.000 \mid}{0.020} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.002}{0.020} \\
&= 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ (Actual Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= C_p(1 - k) \\
&= 0.9691 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
&= 0.9691 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.7753
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Using Direct $C_{pk}$ Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{2.010 - 1.998}{3 \times 0.003439} = \frac{0.012}{0.010318} = 1.1630 \\
C_{pl} &= \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{1.998 - 1.990}{3 \times 0.003439} = \frac{0.008}{0.010318} = 0.7753 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.1630, 0.7753) = 0.7753 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- $C_p = 0.9691 < 1.00$ → Process spread is **wider** than the specification width. Even if perfectly centered, the process would produce defects.
- $C_{pk} = 0.7753 < C_p$ → Indicates the process is **off-center** (mean shifted toward LSL).
- $C_{pl}$ is the binding constraint → Process is encroaching on the **lower specification limit**.
- **Recommendation:** Reduce common-cause variation AND re-center the process mean toward 2.000 cm.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules

| Condition | Relationship | Diagnosis | Recommended Action |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| $C_p \geq 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | Capable & Centered | Excellent process | Maintain current controls |
| $C_p \geq 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Capable but Shifted | Off-center | Adjust process mean |
| $C_p < 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} \approx C_p$ | Not Capable, Centered | Excess variation | Reduce σ (improve equipment) |
| $C_p = C_{pk}$ | Perfectly Centered | $k = 0$ | No centering adjustment needed |
| $C_{pk} < 0$ | Mean outside specifications | Catastrophic | Stop production immediately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Invariant Relationship:** Since $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ and $0 \leq k \leq 1$, it is mathematically guaranteed that $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ **always**. This is a critical KTU conceptual point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Sequential Relationship Between Control and Capability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Under Study] --> B[Phase 1: Data Collection]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Construct Control Charts X-bar and R]
    C --> D{Is Process In Statistical Control?}
    D -->|No - Special Causes Present| E[Investigate and Remove Assignable Causes]
    E --> C
    D -->|Yes - Stable Process| F[Phase 3: Estimate Process Sigma]
    F --> G[Sigma hat = R bar divided by d2]
    G --> H[Phase 4: Calculate Cp Index]
    H --> I[Phase 5: Calculate Cpk Index]
    I --> J{Cpk greater than or equal to 1.33?}
    J -->|No| K{Cp greater than Cpk significantly?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Process Off-Center: Re-center Mean]
    K -->|No| M[Process Has Excess Variation: Reduce Sigma]
    L --> F
    M --> F
    J -->|Yes| N[Process is Capable and Stable]
    N --> O[Continue Routine Monitoring with Control Charts]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Two-Dimensional Process Performance Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dimension1[Dimension 1 - Process Stability]
        SC1[In Control] 
        SC2[Out of Control]
    end
    subgraph Dimension2[Dimension 2 - Process Capability]
        CP1[Capable - Cpk greater than 1.33]
        CP2[Not Capable - Cpk less than 1.33]
    end
    SC1 -->|State 1| IDEAL[State 1: Ideal - Both Stable and Capable]
    SC1 -->|State 2| RE_CENTER[State 2: Stable but Not Capable]
    SC2 -->|State 3| REDUCE_VAR[State 3: Capable on Average but Unstable]
    SC2 -->|State 4| CHAOS[State 4: Unstable and Incapable - Worst Case]
    CP1 --> IDEAL
    CP2 --> RE_CENTER
    CP1 --> REDUCE_VAR
    CP2 --> CHAOS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Relationship Between Cp, Cpk, and k

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA[Process Data: X-double-bar, R-bar, USL, LSL] --> EST[Estimate Sigma hat]
    EST --> CPCALC[Calculate Cp = USL minus LSL divided by 6 Sigma hat]
    DATA --> MID[Calculate Midpoint M]
    MID --> KCALC[Calculate Centering Factor k]
    KCALC --> REL[Apply Relationship: Cpk equals Cp times 1 minus k]
    CPCALC --> REL
    REL --> CPKVAL[Final Cpk Value]
    DATA --> CPKDIRECT[Direct Calculation: Cpk equals min of Cpu and Cpl]
    CPKDIRECT --> CPKVAL
    CPKVAL --> DECISION{Decision Rule: Cpk greater than 1.33?}
    DECISION -->|Yes| ACCEPT[Accept Process]
    DECISION -->|No| REJECT[Improve Process]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Cp vs Cpk Visualization

```
        USL (Upper Specification Limit)              M (Midpoint)         LSL (Lower Specification Limit)
              |                                            |                        |
   ───────────●════════════════════════════════════════════●═══════════════════════●───────────
              ▲                                            ▲                        ▲
              │  USL - X̄ (Upper distance)                 │  X̄ - LSL (Lower dist)  │
              │                                            │                        │
              └──────────────── 6σ Process Spread ─────────┴────────────────────────┘
                                         (μ ± 3σ)

  Cp = (USL - LSL) / 6σ
  Cpk = min[(USL - X̄)/3σ , (X̄ - LSL)/3σ]
  
  When process is centered: Cp = Cpk
  When process shifts:     Cpk < Cp  (the greater the shift, the smaller Cpk becomes)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Process Capability Index $C_p$ and explain its limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Process Capability Index $C_p$ is defined as the ratio of the specification width to the process width:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

It measures the **potential capability** of a process, assuming the mean is perfectly centered. **(1 Mark)**

**Limitations:** **(2 Marks)**
1. It does not account for **process centering** — a process can have a high $C_p$ but still produce defects if the mean is shifted.
2. It assumes the process is in statistical control; it is invalid for unstable processes.
3. It does not distinguish between one-sided and two-sided specifications.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. When is $C_p = C_{pk}$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The relationship is:

$$C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the centering index. **(2 Marks)**

$C_p = C_{pk}$ when **$k = 0$**, i.e., when the process mean $\bar{X}$ exactly equals the specification midpoint $M = (USL + LSL)/2$. This means the process is **perfectly centered**. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the relationship between statistical process control and process capability. Discuss the four possible states of a process with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A drilling machine produces holes with $USL = 12.60$ mm and $LSL = 12.40$ mm. From 20 samples of size 4, the grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.48$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.05$ mm. The process is in statistical control. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and interpret the result. Use $d_2 = 2.059$ for $n = 4$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Statistical Process Control (SPC) and Process Capability are two complementary but distinct evaluations. SPC uses **control charts** to verify **stability** (consistency over time), while process capability uses **indices** to verify the ability to meet **specifications** (customer requirements).

**Sequential Relationship (2 Marks):**
1. **First**, achieve statistical control by removing all assignable (special) causes using X̄-R or X̄-S control charts.
2. **Then**, calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ only on the stable process.

**Four States of a Process (3 Marks):**

| State | Control | Capability | Meaning | Action |
|-------|---------|------------|---------|--------|
| 1 | In Control | Capable | Ideal — tight cluster inside specs | Continue monitoring |
| 2 | In Control | Not Capable | Off-center or too wide | Re-center / reduce σ |
| 3 | Out of Control | Capable | Unstable but on target | Remove special causes |
| 4 | Out of Control | Not Capable | Worst case | Complete overhaul |

State 1 is the desired state. State 2 indicates the process mean has shifted. State 3 indicates special causes are creating wide swings. State 4 requires immediate intervention.

**Diagrammatic Representation (1 Mark):** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the four-state matrix)

---

### **Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Estimate Process Standard Deviation (1 Mark):**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.05}{2.059} = 0.02428 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $C_p$ (2 Marks):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{12.60 - 12.40}{6 \times 0.02428} = \frac{0.20}{0.14570} = 1.3727
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Midpoint and Centering Index (1 Mark):**

$$
M = \frac{12.60 + 12.40}{2} = 12.50 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
k = \frac{2 \mid \bar{\bar{X}} - M \mid}{USL - LSL} = \frac{2 \mid 12.48 - 12.50 \mid}{0.20} = \frac{0.04}{0.20} = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ (2 Marks):**

$$
C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k) = 1.3727 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1.3727 \times 0.80 = 1.0982
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation (1 Mark):**

- $C_p = 1.37 > 1.33$ → Process is potentially capable.
- $C_{pk} = 1.10 < 1.33$ → Actual capability is below industrial benchmark.
- The process is **off-center** (shifted toward LSL by 0.02 mm).
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process mean to $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.50$ mm to improve $C_{pk}$ to 1.37.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for $C_{pk}$ in terms of $C_p$ and the centering index $k$. Explain why $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ always. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a CNC turning process, the specification limits are $USL = 25.05$ mm and $LSL = 24.95$ mm. Twenty samples of size 5 give $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.99$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.025$ mm, and the process is in control. Calculate (i) Process capability index $C_p$ (ii) Process capability index $C_{pk}$ (iii) The percentage of parts within specifications. Use $d_2 = 2.326$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of $C_p$ (1 Mark):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Definition of $C_{pk}$ (1 Mark):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \min(USL - \bar{X}, \bar{X} - LSL) \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sigma} \left[\frac{USL - LSL}{2} - \mid \bar{X} - M \mid\right] \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \left[1 - \frac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}\right] \\
&= C_p(1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k = \dfrac{2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid}{USL - LSL}$ is the centering index and $0 \leq k \leq 1$.

**Proof that $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ (2 Marks):** Since $0 \leq k \leq 1$, the term $(1 - k)$ lies between 0 and 1. Therefore:

$$
0 \leq (1 - k) \leq 1 \implies C_{pk} = C_p(1-k) \leq C_p
$$

Equality holds only when $k = 0$ (process perfectly centered).

---

### **Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Estimate σ and Calculate $C_p$ (3 Marks):**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.025}{2.326} = 0.010748 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.010748} = \frac{0.10}{0.06449} = 1.5505
$$

**(ii) Calculate $C_{pk}$ (2 Marks):**

$$
M = \frac{25.05 + 24.95}{2} = 25.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
k = \frac{2 \mid 24.99 - 25.00 \mid}{0.10} = \frac{0.02}{0.10} = 0.20
$$

$$
C_{pk} = 1.5505 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1.5505 \times 0.80 = 1.2404
$$

**(iii) Percentage of Parts Within Specifications (2 Marks):**

The Z-scores for each specification limit:

$$
Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 24.99}{0.010748} = 5.585
$$

$$
Z_{LSL} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{24.99 - 24.95}{0.010748} = 3.724
$$

From the standard normal table:
- $P(Z \leq 5.585) \approx 1.0000$
- $P(Z \leq 3.724) \approx 0.99990$

Percentage within specifications:

$$
P(LSL \leq X \leq USL) = 0.99990 - (1 - 1.0000) = 0.99990 = 99.99\%
$$

Approximately **99.99% of parts are within specifications.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\hat{\sigma}$ (estimated sigma)** instead of population σ. Always use $\bar{R}/d_2$ for X̄-R charts.
> 2. **Calculating capability before establishing control.** KTU explicitly penalizes this — capability is meaningless on an unstable process.
> 3. **Misapplying $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$:** Use $C_p$ only when the process is perfectly centered. Use $C_{pk}$ for real-world off-center processes.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Ensure USL, LSL, $\bar{R}$, and $\bar{\bar{X}}$ are all in the **same units** before substitution.
> 5. **Sign errors in k:** Always take the **absolute value** of $(\bar{X} - M)$.
> 6. **Forgetting the "1-" in the relationship:** A frequent error is writing $C_{pk} = C_p \cdot k$ instead of $C_p(1-k)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Relationship Between Process Control and Process Capability:**

- ⭐ **Precedence Rule:** Control FIRST, Capability SECOND. Never calculate $C_p$ or $C_{pk}$ on an unstable process.
- ⭐ **$C_p$ Formula:** $C_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$ — Assumes perfect centering.
- ⭐ **$C_{pk}$ Formula:** $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ — Worst-case one-sided capability.
- ⭐ **Master Relationship:** $C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$ where $k$ is the centering index.
- ⭐ **Centering Index:** $k = 2 \mid \bar{X} - M \mid / (USL - LSL)$, with range $0 \leq k \leq 1$.
- ⭐ **Invariant Inequality:** $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ (always true, mathematically guaranteed).
- ⭐ **Equality Condition:** $C_{pk} = C_p$ **only** when $k = 0$ (process perfectly centered).
- ⭐ **Sigma Estimation:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ for X̄-R charts; $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{s}/c_4$ for X̄-S charts.
- ⭐ **Industrial Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for capable process; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for Six-Sigma quality.
- ⭐ **Four States:** (1) In Control & Capable, (2) In Control & Not Capable, (3) Out of Control & Capable, (4) Out of Control & Not Capable.
- ⭐ **Decision Rule:** If $C_p$ high but $C_{pk}$ low → Re-center. If both low → Reduce variation. If both high → Excellent, maintain.
- ⭐ **Six-Sigma Link:** $C_{pk} = 2.00$ corresponds to 3.4 DPMO (Six-Sigma quality standard).
- ⭐ **Practical Tip:** Always report $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ together to reveal both potential and actual capability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choice of Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Choice of Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Statistical Quality Control (SQC), the **Choice of Variables** refers to the systematic process of identifying, selecting, and prioritizing measurable quality characteristics (quantitative parameters) of a product, process, or service that best represent conformance to specifications and customer requirements. According to KTU 2024 SQC syllabus framing, "variables" here denote continuous numerical measurements (e.g., diameter in mm, weight in grams, tensile strength in MPa, voltage in V) as opposed to "attributes" (pass/fail, good/defective).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Choice of Variables is the engineering decision-making process that determines which measurable quality characteristics of a manufactured product or process output should be monitored, measured, and controlled using statistical methods such as $\bar{X}$-charts, $R$-charts, $\sigma$-charts, $C_p$/$C_{pk}$ indices, and process capability analysis.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and want to judge the "quality" of a biryani dish. You could ask binary questions: *Is it served hot? (Yes/No)*, *Does it have a lid? (Yes/No)* — these are **attributes**. Or, you could measure: *Temperature in °C, weight in grams, oil content in ml, spice level on a 1–10 scale* — these are **variables**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnostic Test":**
> When a doctor checks your health, she doesn't just ask "Are you sick? (attribute)". She measures **Blood Pressure (mmHg)**, **Sugar Level (mg/dL)**, **Cholesterol (mg/dL)**, **Heart Rate (bpm)**. The "Choice of Variables" decision is: *Which numbers give the most reliable picture of your health with the least cost and effort?* A factory engineer faces the same dilemma: *Which measurements best capture product quality?*

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Standards

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Variable** | A measurable quality characteristic that yields continuous numerical data | Foundation of $\bar{X}$, $R$ control charts |
| **Attribute** | A quality characteristic expressed as pass/fail or counts of defects | Foundation of $p$, $np$, $c$, $u$ charts |
| **Quality Characteristic** | An identifiable property of a product/process that bears on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs | ISO 9001 / KTU Module 3 anchor |
| **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** | A characteristic that directly impacts customer satisfaction or regulatory compliance | Six-Sigma / Lean integration |
| **Measurement System** | The complete set of operations, instruments, and procedures used to quantify a variable | Gauge R&R studies |
| **Specification Limit (SL)** | The target value (nominal) and tolerance bounds (USL, LSL) defining acceptability | Process Capability comparison |

### 1.4 The Two Broad Categories of Quality Data

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Classification:**
> 1. **Variables Data (Continuous):** Length, weight, hardness, viscosity, concentration, voltage, torque
> 2. **Attributes Data (Discrete):** Number of defects, number of defectives, go/no-go judgement, ordinal ratings

This classification is critical because the choice of variable dictates which control chart family is applicable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Decision Framework for Choosing Variables

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes a **multi-criteria decision framework** for variable selection. The choice is never arbitrary; it is governed by seven interlocking criteria:

#### 2.1.1 Step 1 — Customer / End-User Requirements Analysis
- Identify what the customer values most (performance, durability, aesthetics, safety).
- Translate vague customer needs ("the car should feel smooth") into measurable engineering variables (e.g., steering torque in N·m, gear-shift time in ms).
- Use tools like the **House of Quality (QFD)** and **Voice of Customer (VOC)** matrices.

#### 2.1.2 Step 2 — Functional / Engineering Significance
- Select variables that have a **causal relationship** with product function.
- Example: For a pressure vessel, *wall thickness* and *seam integrity* are functional; *paint color* is not (in a regulatory sense).

#### 2.1.3 Step 3 — Process Sensitivity Analysis
- Choose variables that **respond detectably** to process changes.
- Conduct **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to identify which inputs significantly affect which outputs.

#### 2.1.4 Step 4 — Economic Feasibility
- Cost of measurement vs. cost of failure.
- Variables requiring **expensive sensors**, destructive testing, or rare expertise are down-weighted unless critical.

#### 2.1.5 Step 5 — Measurement System Capability (GR&R)
- Apply **Gauge Repeatability & Reproducibility (GR&R)** analysis.
- A variable is only useful if its measurement system is **< 30% GR&R** of the tolerance.

#### 2.1.6 Step 6 — Statistical Control Compatibility
- The variable must be amenable to Normal-distribution-based control charts ($\bar{X}$, $R$) or suitable transformations.
- Verify with **normality tests** (Anderson–Darling, Shapiro–Wilk).

#### 2.1.7 Step 7 — Regulatory / Safety Compliance
- Variables tied to safety, health, environmental compliance (e.g., brake-pad friction coefficient, pharmaceutical assay %, emission levels) are **non-negotiable** CTQs.

### 2.2 Variables vs. Attributes — Comparative Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Variables Data | Attributes Data |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Information richness** | High (gives magnitude) | Low (gives only count) |
| **Sample size needed** | Small (n=2 to 5 typical) | Large (n=50 to 100+) |
| **Statistical power** | High | Moderate |
| **Cost of inspection** | High (instruments, time) | Low (visual, go/no-go) |
| **Conversion** | Can be converted → attribute (e.g., "in spec?" yes/no) | Cannot be reversed to give magnitude |
| **KTU chart linkage** | $\bar{X}$, $R$, $S$, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ | $p$, $np$, $c$, $u$ |
| **Best use case** | Critical dimensions, continuous processes | Cosmetic defects, large-volume inspection |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield — they will appear in derivations, numericals, and Part A definitions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index (potential) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index (actual, centred) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$ | Capability w.r.t. target $T$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_{\text{within}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Short-term process std. dev. | same as variable |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{c_4}$ or $\dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Long-term process std. dev. | same as variable |
| 6 | $P(pk) = P(X < LSL) + P(X > USL)$ | Expected proportion non-conforming | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{DPMO} = P(pk) \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opportunities | dimensionless |
| 8 | $USL = T + \text{tolerance}/2$ | Upper spec limit from target | same as variable |
| 9 | $LSL = T - \text{tolerance}/2$ | Lower spec limit from target | same as variable |
| 10 | $\%GR\&R = 5.15 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{GRR}}}{\text{Tolerance}} \times 100$ | Measurement system capability | % |
| 11 | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Sample mean of variable | same as variable |
| 12 | $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Sample range (for $R$-chart variable selection) | same as variable |
| 13 | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Control limit for $\bar{X}$-chart | same as variable |
| 14 | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Control limit for $\bar{X}$-chart | same as variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Rule for KTU Board Exam:**
> Always distinguish between **Specification Limits** ($USL$, $LSL$) — set by customer/design — and **Control Limits** ($UCL$, $LCL$) — derived from process data. Mixing them is a **common 2-mark loss** in board exams.

### 2.4 Why "Choice of Variables" Matters in Engineering

1. **In Manufacturing:** Determines whether $\bar{X}$-$R$ charts (variables) or $p$-charts (attributes) are deployed on the shop floor.
2. **In Process Improvement:** Drives Six-Sigma DMAIC projects — the *Measure* phase begins with correct variable selection.
3. **In Cost Management:** Wrong variable choice leads to either over-inspection (wasted ₹) or under-inspection (costly recalls).
4. **In Capability Assessment:** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are **only computable for variables**; attribute data forces a separate binomial/poisson analysis.
5. **In Digital Twins / Industry 4.0:** Modern MES/SCADA systems stream only those variables selected during SQC design — wrong selection = blind digital twin.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why $C_p$ Uses $6\sigma$ in the Denominator

The Six-Sigma framework assumes a stable, approximately Normal process. Under the empirical rule, a Normal distribution places:

- $68.27\%$ of observations within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$
- $95.45\%$ of observations within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$
- $99.73\%$ of observations within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$
- $99.9937\%$ of observations within $\mu \pm 4\sigma$
- $99.999943\%$ of observations within $\mu \pm 4.5\sigma$
- $99.9999998\%$ of observations within $\mu \pm 6\sigma$

For a process centred at $\mu$ with standard deviation $\sigma$, the spread across the natural process limits is therefore:

$$
\text{Natural process spread} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma
$$

The specification spread is the engineering tolerance:

$$
\text{Specification spread} = USL - LSL
$$

The **process capability index** $C_p$ is defined as the ratio of the specification spread (what we are *allowed*) to the natural process spread (what we *naturally achieve*):

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Specification spread}}{\text{Natural process spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Interpretation Step:**
- $C_p < 1$ → process spread **exceeds** specification → incapable.
- $C_p = 1$ → process barely fits inside specs.
- $C_p \geq 1.33$ → KTU/industry minimum acceptable.
- $C_p \geq 1.67$ → preferred for new processes.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From $C_p$ to $C_{pk}$ (Centring Adjustment)

$C_p$ assumes the process is perfectly centred. Real processes drift. Define:

$$
C_{p,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \quad C_{p,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

Each one-sided index measures how many $3\sigma$ "steps" of process variation fit between the mean and one specification limit. The actual capability is governed by the **worse** of the two — the one closer to its limit. By convention, we take the minimum:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

A more compact equivalent form, useful for KTU board answers, is:

$$
C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k), \quad \text{where } k = \frac{2\mu - (USL + LSL)}{(USL - LSL)}
$$

Here $k$ is the **centring factor**; $k = 0$ means perfectly centred, $k \to 1$ means process mean at the spec limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Mini-Example for KTU Board Style:**
> A shaft has $USL = 12.00$ mm, $LSL = 11.90$ mm, $\mu = 11.96$ mm, $\sigma = 0.015$ mm.
>
> Step 1: $C_p = \dfrac{12.00 - 11.90}{6 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.09} = 1.111$
>
> Step 2: Upper side: $\dfrac{12.00 - 11.96}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.045} = 0.889$
>
> Step 3: Lower side: $\dfrac{11.96 - 11.90}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.045} = 1.333$
>
> Step 4: $C_{pk} = \min(0.889,\ 1.333) = 0.889$
>
> Conclusion: Although $C_p = 1.11$ suggests marginal capability, $C_{pk} = 0.889 < 1$ confirms the process is **not capable** and is drifting toward the upper limit — a process-recentring action is required.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion of Variables to Attribute Data (Thresholding)

Sometimes an engineering decision forces the conversion of a continuous variable into a binary attribute. Given a specification limit $L$ and a measured variable $X$:

$$
Y = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{(Defective)} \quad \text{if } X < L \text{ or } X > U \\ 0 & \text{(Conforming)} \quad \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The proportion non-conforming is the area under the Normal curve outside $[L, U]$:

$$
P(\text{Defective}) = P(X < L) + P(X > U) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{L - \mu}{\sigma}\right) + \left[1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{U - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]
$$

$$
P(\text{Defective}) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{L - \mu}{\sigma}\right) + \Phi\!\left(\frac{\mu - U}{\sigma}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Loss Theorem (qualitative):**
> Once a variable is converted to an attribute, the *magnitude* of non-conformance is lost. Two defectives, one with deviation $0.01$ mm and another with $0.10$ mm, are counted identically. KTU boards reward students who explicitly state this trade-off in their answers.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — DPMO and Yield Relationship

In Six-Sigma projects, the metric **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** standardizes quality across products with different complexity:

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6
$$

For a single critical variable with proportion defective $p$:

$$
\text{DPMO} = p \times 10^6
$$

The **First Pass Yield (FPY)** is:

$$
\text{FPY} = (1 - p) \times 100\%
$$

And the relationship between DPMO and the **Sigma level** is given by the inverse of the Normal tail:

$$
\text{DPMO} = 10^6 \times \left[1 - \Phi(6\sigma_{\text{level}} + 1.5)\right] \times 2
$$

where $1.5$ is the conventional long-term mean-shift correction. For example, $4.5\sigma$ quality $\Rightarrow$ approximately $3.4$ DPMO, the Six-Sigma benchmark.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Variable Selection Decision Tree

| Engineering Scenario | Recommended Variable Type | KTU Chart to Use |
|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Critical dimension of a machined shaft (continuous, tight tolerance) | **Variable** | $\bar{X}$–$R$ chart |
| Surface finish of a piston (micrometre measurable) | **Variable** | $\bar{X}$–$R$ chart, $C_{pk}$ |
| Visual cosmetic defect on a painted panel | **Attribute** | $c$-chart or $u$-chart |
| Number of solder joints defective on a PCB | **Attribute (count of defects)** | $c$-chart |
| pH of a pharmaceutical solution | **Variable** | $\bar{X}$–$R$ chart, $C_{pk}$ |
| Go/No-Go pin gauge clearance | **Attribute** | $p$-chart |
| Concrete compressive strength (MPa) | **Variable** | $\bar{X}$–$R$ chart |
| Number of customer complaints per day | **Attribute (count)** | $c$-chart |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — End-to-End Variable Choice & Capability (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem (14-Mark Format):**
> A bearing-race inner diameter is specified as $25.000 \pm 0.050$ mm. Twenty-five samples of size $n=5$ yield $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.012$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.030$ mm. Compute the process capability indices and comment on the choice-of-variables aspect.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the variable:
The inner diameter is a **continuous measurable variable** — best monitored with $\bar{X}$–$R$ control charts (variable data), not $p$-charts.

Step 2 — Compute the process standard deviation using $\bar{R}$ (since $n=5$):
For $n=5$, the constant $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$
\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.030}{2.326} = 0.01290\ \text{mm}
$$

Step 3 — Compute $C_p$:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.050 - 24.950}{6 \times 0.01290} = \frac{0.100}{0.0774} = 1.292
$$

Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.050 - 25.012}{3 \times 0.01290} = \frac{0.038}{0.0387} = 0.982
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.012 - 24.950}{3 \times 0.01290} = \frac{0.062}{0.0387} = 1.602
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.982,\ 1.602) = 0.982
$$

Step 5 — Interpretation:

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $C_p = 1.29$ (marginally capable) but $C_{pk} = 0.98$ (just below 1.0, drifting toward $USL$). The process is **centred too high**. Recommendation: re-centre the CNC lathe tool offset downward by $\approx 0.012$ mm. The diameter is correctly chosen as a **variable** because it is continuous, normally distributed, and economically measurable with a bore gauge. Using it as an attribute (go/no-go) would lose the centring information — a key lesson of the "Choice of Variables" module.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Choice of Variables Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Requirements VOC] --> B[Engineering Functional Analysis]
    B --> C[Identify Candidate Quality Characteristics]
    C --> D{Continuous and Measurable?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Classify as VARIABLE]
    D -- No --> F[Classify as ATTRIBUTE]
    E --> G[Conduct GR and R Study]
    G --> H{GR and R less than 30 percent?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Variable ACCEPTED for X-bar and R chart]
    H -- No --> J[Improve Gauge or Reject Variable]
    F --> K[Define Defect Classification Logic]
    K --> L[Variable Converted to ATTRIBUTE]
    L --> M[Deploy p-chart or c-chart]
    I --> N[Process Capability Analysis Cp and Cpk]
    M --> O[Attribute Capability Analysis DPMO]
    N --> P[Decision Capability greater than 1.33]
    O --> P
    P -- Yes --> Q[Production Monitoring Active]
    P -- No --> R[Root Cause Analysis and Process Improvement]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Variable vs Attribute Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: New Quality Characteristic Identified] --> T{Can it be measured on a continuous numeric scale?}
    T -- Yes --> U{Is the measurement cost justified by criticality?}
    U -- Yes --> V[SELECT AS VARIABLE]
    U -- No --> W{Is the defect visible and countable?}
    V --> X[Use X-bar and R charts plus Cp Cpk]
    T -- No --> Y{Is it a count of defects per item?}
    W -- Yes --> Z[SELECT AS ATTRIBUTE]
    W -- No --> AA[Re-engineer measurement method]
    Y -- Yes --> AB[Use c-chart or u-chart]
    Y -- No --> AC{Is it pass or fail judgement?}
    AC -- Yes --> AD[Use p-chart or np-chart]
    AC -- No --> AE[Reject characteristic or redesign]
    X --> AF[Continuous SPC loop]
    AB --> AF
    AD --> AF
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — How the Chosen Variable Feeds the SQC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Input Material] --> P2[Manufacturing Operation]
    P2 --> P3[Chosen Quality Variable Measured]
    P3 --> P4[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    P4 --> P5[Real time Database]
    P5 --> P6[Control Chart Engine]
    P6 --> P61[X-bar Chart]
    P6 --> P62[R Chart]
    P5 --> P7[Capability Engine]
    P7 --> P71[Cp Index]
    P7 --> P72[Cpk Index]
    P7 --> P73[DPMO Computation]
    P61 --> P8[Out of Control Action OCC]
    P62 --> P8
    P71 --> P9[Management Review Report]
    P72 --> P9
    P73 --> P9
    P8 --> P10[Corrective Action Dispatch]
    P9 --> P11[Strategic Quality Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Students:**
> The architecture shows that the **Choice of Variables** is not an isolated decision; it sits at the *upstream* input of the entire SQC system. A wrong choice (e.g., selecting an attribute when a variable was measurable) reduces the data fed into the capability engine, lowering the resolution of every downstream calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define "Choice of Variables" in Statistical Quality Control. Why is the choice critical in modern manufacturing?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The "Choice of Variables" refers to the engineering decision of selecting which measurable, continuous quality characteristics of a product or process should be monitored using statistical methods such as $\bar{X}$-charts, $R$-charts, and process capability indices. The choice is critical because (i) variables data carries **more information** per observation than attribute data, (ii) it enables **precise process capability assessment** via $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, (iii) it allows **earlier detection** of process drift, and (iv) it forms the foundation of Six-Sigma DMAIC measurement systems. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any two criticality points.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Differentiate between **variables** and **attributes** with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Variables | Attributes |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Data type | Continuous numerical | Discrete / categorical |
| Examples | (1) Bearing inner diameter in mm, (2) Tablet weight in mg | (1) Number of scratches on a surface, (2) Go/No-Go pin test result |

Variables yield a numerical magnitude of deviation from target; attributes yield a count or classification. (1 mark for differentiation, 2 marks for examples.)

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the seven-step framework for choosing variables in a manufacturing process. List the criteria and the rationale behind each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A critical bolt diameter has specifications $12.00 \pm 0.10$ mm. From 20 samples of size 5, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.04$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.045$ mm. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and DPMO. Comment on the process capability and recommend a corrective action.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Customer Requirements Analysis (1 mark):** Identify CTQs through VOC, QFD.
2. **Functional Significance (1 mark):** Select variables causally linked to function.
3. **Process Sensitivity (1 mark):** Use DOE to identify sensitive variables.
4. **Economic Feasibility (1 mark):** Cost of measurement vs. cost of failure.
5. **Measurement System Capability (1 mark):** GR&R < 30 % of tolerance.
6. **Statistical Control Compatibility (1 mark):** Verify normality.
7. **Regulatory / Safety Compliance (1 mark):** Mandate non-negotiable variables.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Step 1 — Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.045}{2.326} = 0.01935\ \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Process capability:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.20}{6 \times 0.01935} = \frac{0.20}{0.1161} = 1.723
$$

Step 3 — One-sided capabilities:

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{12.10 - 12.04}{3 \times 0.01935} = \frac{0.06}{0.0581} = 1.033
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{12.04 - 11.90}{3 \times 0.01935} = \frac{0.14}{0.0581} = 2.412
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.033,\ 2.412) = 1.033
$$

Step 4 — Proportion non-conforming (upper-tail only, since lower is far away):

$$
Z_{\text{upper}} = \frac{12.10 - 12.04}{0.01935} = 3.10
$$

$$
P(X > USL) = 1 - \Phi(3.10) \approx 0.000968
$$

Step 5 — DPMO:

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.000968 \times 10^6 \approx 968
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** $C_p = 1.72$ (excellent potential) but $C_{pk} = 1.03$ indicates the process is **drifting toward $USL$**. Recommended action: re-centre the bolt-making machine to bring $\bar{\bar{X}}$ closer to $12.00$ mm (shift mean down by $0.04$ mm). The variable (diameter) was correctly chosen as a continuous measurable characteristic, enabling this rich analysis — had it been treated as a Go/No-Go attribute, the centring drift would have been invisible.

---

**Q3(b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the criteria for converting a variable to an attribute. Under what circumstances is such a conversion acceptable in industrial practice?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A capacitor manufacturer measures capacitance (in µF) as a continuous variable. Specifications are $100 \pm 10$ µF. From a stable process: $\mu = 102$ µF, $\sigma = 2.5$ µF. The company wants to switch to a Go/No-Go LCR-meter test (attribute data) using limits 90 µF and 110 µF. Calculate (i) the original proportion non-conforming and DPMO under the variable measurement, and (ii) the proportion non-conforming under the attribute conversion. Comment on the information lost.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Criteria for accepting variable-to-attribute conversion:

1. **High measurement cost vs. low failure cost** (1 mark)
2. **Large sample size is feasible** (1 mark)
3. **Process is highly capable** ($C_p \geq 2$); centring drift is small (1 mark)
4. **Customer only needs pass/fail** (no downstream re-work benefit) (1 mark)
5. **Skilled measurement labour unavailable** (1 mark)
6. **No regulatory requirement for magnitude data** (1 mark)
7. **Destruction test** (cannot be repeated as variable) (1 mark)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

(i) Variable data computation:

$$
Z_{\text{upper}} = \frac{110 - 102}{2.5} = 3.20
$$

$$
Z_{\text{lower}} = \frac{90 - 102}{2.5} = -4.80
$$

$$
P(X > 110) = 1 - \Phi(3.20) \approx 0.00069
$$

$$
P(X < 90) = \Phi(-4.80) \approx 0.00000079
$$

$$
P(\text{Defective}) = 0.00069 + 0.00000079 \approx 0.000691
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = 691
$$

(ii) Attribute conversion: same as (i) because the Go/No-Go threshold is mathematically identical to the spec limits. Numerically identical in this case. (1 mark for stating this carefully.)

**Information lost (2 marks):**
- Magnitude of deviation lost (a 92 µF and a 89 µF capacitor both classified "defective").
- Centring drift invisible (you cannot tell from attributes that the process mean has shifted to 102 µF).
- Hence, attribute data is **acceptable for outgoing quality** (final inspection) but **not for in-process SPC** during manufacture — a classic KTU trade-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common 14-Mark Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ assumes centring; the board will deduct 2 marks if you compute only $C_p$ and call the process "capable".
> 2. **Forgetting the $1.5\sigma$ shift** when comparing to Six-Sigma DPMO benchmarks.
