<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEPET415]

**"Differentiate between statistical control and process capability."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Statistical Control | Process Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **Concerns** | Variability / Stability | Ability to meet specs |
| **Defined by** | Control limits ($\mu \pm 3\sigma$) | Specification limits (USL, LSL) |
| **Metric** | Points within limits, no patterns | $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ |
| **Implication** | Predictable variation | Conformance to requirements |
| **Action if poor** | Remove special cause / reduce common cause | Redesign the system |

A process can be in control but incapable (e.g., a stable process whose $\pm 3\sigma$ band is wider than the spec window). **(1 mark each for two clear differences, 1 mark for the example.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEPET415]

**"What is the in-control ARL of a 3-sigma Shewhart control chart? Why is it chosen as 370 instead of infinity?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The in-control ARL is the **expected number of plotted points between successive false alarms** when the process is genuinely stable. With $p = 0.0027$:

$$ \text{ARL}_0 = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.4 $$

It is finite (not infinite) because there is always a 0.27% chance per sample that a *truly in-control* point will randomly fall outside the $\pm 3\sigma$ band, by definition of the normal distribution. **(2 marks for computation, 1 mark for the conceptual explanation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PEPET415 Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **four logical states** of a process based on control chart behaviour and capability indices. For each state, state the appropriate corrective action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a $\bar{X}$ chart, **25 subgroups of size 5** give $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.0$ mm and $\bar{R} = 2.0$ mm. Specifications are USL = 54 mm and LSL = 46 mm. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ and state whether the process is capable and in control. Assume the chart shows no out-of-control points. Take $A_2 = 0.577$ and $d_2 = 2.326$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The four states form a $2 \times 2$ matrix (1 mark for the matrix concept):

1. **State I — In Control & Capable** ($C_{pk} \ge 1.33$): Ideal. Continue monitoring; pursue Six Sigma targets.
2. **State II — In Control & Not Capable** ($C_{pk} < 1$): Only common cause. **Do NOT tamper**. Redesign equipment / material / method.
3. **State III — Out of Control & Capable**: Spurious special cause or mixed lots. Find and eliminate the assignable cause; verify capability persists.
4. **State IV — Out of Control & Not Capable**: Both. Stop the line. Full investigation: 5-Why, Ishikawa, stratification.

(1.5 marks per state with correct action.)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute control limits (verify stability assumption):**

$$ \text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.0 + 0.577 \times 2.0 = 51.154 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50.0 - 0.577 \times 2.0 = 48.846 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Estimate process standard deviation:**

$$ \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{2.0}{2.326} = 0.8598 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$ C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{54 - 46}{6 \times 0.8598} = \frac{8}{5.159} = 1.551 $$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$:**

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{54 - 50}{3 \times 0.8598} = \frac{4}{2.5794} = 1.551 $$

$$ C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{50 - 46}{2.5794} = 1.551 $$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.551,\ 1.551) = 1.551 $$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

Since $C_{pk} = 1.551 > 1.33$ and the process shows no out-of-control signals, the process is **in statistical control AND capable** (State I). It is comfortably meeting the customer's specifications with margin for future tightening. Routine monitoring is sufficient.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, PEPET415]**

**(a)** Define **Average Run Length (ARL)**. Calculate the ARL$_1$ of a 3-σ Shewhart chart when the process mean shifts by **1.5σ**. Comment on the sensitivity of the chart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Western Electric Rules** for detecting non-random patterns on a control chart. With suitable sketches, explain what each of the **first four rules** indicates. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** ARL is the **expected number of subgroups** plotted before a control chart gives an out-of-control signal. Mathematically $ARL = 1/p$ where $p$ is the probability of a single point falling outside the control limits.

**Computation (4 marks):** For $\delta = 1.5\sigma$ shift, with the new mean at $\mu + 1.5\sigma$:

$$ p_1 = P(Z > 3 - 1.5) + P(Z < -3 - 1.5) = P(Z > 1.5) + P(Z < -4.5) $$

$$ p_1 = [1 - \Phi(1.5)] + \Phi(-4.5) $$

$$ p_1 = (1 - 0.9332) + 0.0000034 = 0.0668 $$

$$ \text{ARL}_1 = \frac{1}{0.0668} \approx 14.97 \approx 15 $$

**Comment (1 mark):** The Shewhart 3-sigma chart detects a **1.5σ shift in ~15 samples**, which is reasonable for moderate shifts but **slow for shifts ≤ 0.5σ** (ARL$_1$ ≈ 161). For faster small-shift detection, CUSUM/EWMA charts are recommended.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Discussion (2 marks):** The Western Electric Rules, codified in the *Statistical Quality Control Handbook* (1956, 1958), supplement Rule 1 (points outside limits) by detecting **non-random patterns within the limits**, which Rule 1 alone misses.

**Rule-wise explanation (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

| Rule | Pattern | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Rule 1** | One point beyond $\pm 3\sigma$ | Single assignable cause; immediate investigation |
| **Rule 2** | 9 consecutive points on one side of CL | Process mean has shifted (new material, operator, machine) |
| **Rule 3** | 6 consecutive points trending up or down | Tool wear, temperature rise, systematic drift |
| **Rule 4** | 14 consecutive points alternating | Two distinct sources alternating (e.g., two machines, two shifts) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to **confuse the WECO rules with specification limits**. WECO patterns apply to **control limits (statistical)**, NOT to USL/LSL. Also, students often forget that Rule 1 is the **primary** rule — Rules 2-4 are supplementary sensitivity boosters. Marks are deducted if you treat them as equal in importance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conclusions from Control Charts**

- **Control chart answers ONE question only:** *"Is the variation predictable?"* It does **not** answer *"Does the process meet specifications?"*
- **4 logical states:** (In Control ∩ Capable), (In Control ∩ Not Capable), (Out of Control ∩ Capable), (Out of Control ∩ Not Capable).
- **In-control ARL = 370.4** at 3-sigma. 1σ shift ARL ≈ 44. 2σ shift ARL ≈ 6.
- **WECO Rule 1** = point beyond $\pm 3\sigma$ (special cause). **Rule 2** = 9 same side. **Rule 3** = 6 trending. **Rule 4** = 14 alternating.
- **$C_p$** measures *potential* capability (perfectly centered assumption). **$C_{pk}$** measures *actual* capability (off-center correction).
- **$C_p = C_{pk}$** ⇒ perfectly centered process. **$C_p > C_{pk}$** ⇒ off-center.
- **Industry thresholds:** $C_{pk} < 1$ = incapable, $1 \le C_{pk} < 1.33$ = marginally capable, $1.33 \le C_{pk} < 1.67$ = capable, $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ = excellent / Six Sigma.
- **Tampering** = adjusting an in-control process — this *increases* variation. It is **management's most common mistake** (Deming's 4th Deadly Disease).
- **Deming's 94/6 rule:** 94% of process problems are common causes (system), 6% are special causes (assignable). Match the **action to the cause type**.
- **Voice of the Process** ($\mu \pm 3\sigma$) vs. **Voice of the Customer** (USL/LSL) — never confuse the two.
- **Process improvement priority:** first achieve stability, then improve capability. A capable but unstable process is a ticking time-bomb.
- **CUSUM / EWMA** charts detect small shifts faster than Shewhart charts (not in KTU Module 3, but mentioned for context).
- **Sampling must be rational:** subgroups should be chosen so that within-subgroup variation = common cause and between-subgroup variation = special cause opportunity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked *"What conclusions do you draw from a control chart?"*, **always structure your answer in two layers**:
> 1. **Layer 1 — Stability:** Comment on points outside limits + WECO patterns.
> 2. **Layer 2 — Capability:** Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ and compare to thresholds.
>
> This two-layer structure guarantees full marks in any 7-mark or 14-mark KTU Module 3 question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Chart Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Chart Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, a **Control Chart Pattern** refers to the systematic, non-random arrangement or clustering of plotted points on a Shewhart control chart that signals the presence of an **assignable cause** of variation, even when the points technically lie within the calculated control limits (Upper Control Limit – **UCL** and Lower Control Limit – **LCL**).

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (PEPET415), control chart patterns are classified into two broad categories:

1. **Random (Natural) Patterns** — Points scattered above and below the **Center Line (CL)** with no recognizable sequence, indicating the process is **in statistical control**.
2. **Non-Random (Unnatural) Patterns** — Points exhibiting trends, runs, cycles, or systematic grouping, indicating the process is **out of statistical control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> "Students must be able to identify unnatural control chart patterns (trends, runs, cycles, mixtures, stratification, over-control) and apply the **Western Electric Rules** and the **Zone Tests (A, B, C)** for pattern recognition — a high-weightage, frequently asked topic in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor monitoring a patient's heartbeat on an **ECG (Electrocardiogram) monitor**:

* If the waveform goes up and down **irregularly but within a healthy band**, the heart is healthy — this is a **random pattern**.
* If you suddenly see **seven consecutive peaks all rising upward**, you immediately suspect a problem — this is a **trend pattern**.
* If the peaks **suddenly shift upward to a new average level**, you suspect the heart rate has changed — this is a **shift pattern**.
* If the readings are **squeezed too close to the center line**, it means the monitor might be artificially dampening the signal — this is **over-control / hugging the centerline**.

A **control chart** is essentially the **"ECG of a manufacturing process"**. The control limits are the "normal heartbeat band", and any pattern tells you what kind of illness (assignable cause) the process has.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** A process is judged "in control" not just by the *3-sigma rule* (points outside limits) but also by the *pattern rule* (sequence/arrangement of points). Both must be satisfied for statistical control.

---

## 1.3 Why Pattern Recognition Matters in Industry

* **Early Warning System:** Patterns emerge *before* points actually fall outside the control limits, allowing **predictive corrective action**.
* **Diagnosis of Assignable Causes:** Each pattern has a typical real-world root cause (tool wear, operator change, material batch variation, etc.).
* **Six Sigma and Lean Manufacturing:** Modern quality frameworks (DMAIC) rely heavily on pattern analysis to drive **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**.
* **Cost Reduction:** Detecting patterns early reduces **scrap, rework, and warranty costs**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Statistical Metrics Used

* **σ (sigma)** — standard deviation of the quality characteristic.
* **CL (Center Line)** — process mean $\mu$ (or $\bar{\bar{X}}$ for $\bar{X}$-charts).
* **UCL** = $\mu + 3\sigma$.
* **LCL** = $\mu - 3\sigma$.
* **Zone A** = between $2\sigma$ and $3\sigma$ from CL.
* **Zone B** = between $1\sigma$ and $2\sigma$ from CL.
* **Zone C** = between $0$ and $1\sigma$ from CL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All patterns are evaluated using these standardized zones.** The Western Electric Handbook (1956) formalized these rules, and they are the **gold standard** referenced in KTU examination questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Two States of a Process

A manufacturing process is either:

* **State I — In Control (Common Cause Variation Only):** Only natural, inherent, random variation exists. Points fall within UCL and LCL in a **random pattern**.
* **State II — Out of Control (Assignable Cause Present):** Some external or systematic factor has changed the process mean $\mu$ or spread $\sigma$. This produces **patterns**.

The core statistical question is:
> "Given a sequence of $n$ plotted points, is the probability of this specific sequence occurring under a truly random process high enough to be plausible, or so low that an assignable cause must be present?"

This leads to the use of **Runs Analysis** and **Zone Tests**.

---

## 2.2 Major Non-Random Control Chart Patterns

### Pattern 1 — **Trend (Drift)**
* **Description:** A continuous, steady movement of points either **upward** or **downward**.
* **Detection Rule:** **Seven (or more) consecutive points** all moving in the same direction (all increasing or all decreasing).
* **Typical Root Cause:** Gradual **tool wear**, raw material depletion, temperature drift, progressive deterioration of a machine, operator fatigue.
* **Engineering Analogy:** The slope of a hillside path — you keep stepping up.

### Pattern 2 — **Shift (Level Change)**
* **Description:** A sudden jump in the process mean to a new level, which then stays there.
* **Detection Rule:** **Seven (or more) consecutive points** lying on the same side of the center line.
* **Typical Root Cause:** New batch of raw material, change of operator, new machine setting, change in inspection method.
* **Engineering Analogy:** A staircase — the floor level suddenly changes.

### Pattern 3 — **Cycle (Periodic Variation)**
* **Description:** Points rise and fall in a regular, repeating wave-like pattern.
* **Detection Rule:** Visual inspection; often identified when peaks/troughs occur at **regular intervals**.
* **Typical Root Cause:** **Seasonal effects**, shift changes (morning/evening operator), periodic maintenance cycles, ambient temperature cycles.
* **Engineering Analogy:** A sine wave like ocean tides.

### Pattern 4 — **Mixture (Over-Dispersion from Two Sources)**
* **Description:** Points are excessively scattered, frequently approaching or crossing the control limits, often forming two interleaved streams.
* **Detection Rule:** Many points in **Zone A** (beyond $2\sigma$), or lack of points near CL.
* **Typical Root Cause:** **Two different sources** of raw material, two different machines, two different shifts producing in the same chart.
* **Engineering Analogy:** Mixing two rivers — the chart reflects two different process means simultaneously.

### Pattern 5 — **Stratification (Under-Dispersion)**
* **Description:** Points cluster **too tightly** around the center line; very few points in Zones A or B.
* **Detection Rule:** **15 consecutive points** all within Zone C ($ \pm 1\sigma$), or excessive hugging of CL.
* **Typical Root Cause:** **Over-inspection** averaging, computed data smoothing, incorrect calculation of sigma, or grouping of dissimilar units.
* **Engineering Analogy:** A lake that is suspiciously still — maybe the wind has stopped, or the readings are fake.

### Pattern 6 — **Over-Control (Tampering)**
* **Description:** A special form of stratification where every deviation is "corrected", producing artificial smoothness.
* **Detection Rule:** Same as stratification; often identified alongside alternating small oscillations.
* **Typical Root Cause:** Operator adjusting the machine for every minor deviation → increases variation.
* **Engineering Analogy:** A driver who over-steers on a straight road — causes wobble.

### Pattern 7 — **Systematic Variable (Sawtooth Pattern)**
* **Description:** Points alternate in a regular up-down fashion around the CL.
* **Detection Rule:** Many alternating points; visually a **sawtooth wave**.
* **Typical Root Cause:** Two different machines alternating, two operators working in rotation, alternating die sets.
* **Engineering Analogy:** A heartbeat monitor showing regular pulses.

### Pattern 8 — **Out-of-Control Points (Beyond Limits)**
* **Description:** One or more points fall **outside** UCL or LCL.
* **Detection Rule:** Any point $x_i$ such that $x_i > \text{UCL}$ or $x_i < \text{LCL}$.
* **Typical Root Cause:** Sudden shock, power failure, broken tool, contaminated batch, measurement error.
* **Engineering Analogy:** A spike on the ECG — immediate alarm.

---

## 2.3 The Western Electric Rules (Zone Tests)

The **Western Electric Statistical Quality Control Handbook (1956)** standardized pattern detection. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to memorize and apply these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Zonal Definition (assumes 3-sigma control limits):**
> * **Zone A:** $2\sigma \le \vert x - \mu \vert \le 3\sigma$
> * **Zone B:** $1\sigma \le \vert x - \mu \vert < 2\sigma$
> * **Zone C:** $0 \le \vert x - \mu \vert < 1\sigma$

### The Four Primary Western Electric Rules

| Rule # | Description | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Rule 1** | Any single point outside the $3\sigma$ control limits | Process out of control |
| **Rule 2** | 2 out of 3 consecutive points in Zone A or beyond (same side) | Shift in process mean |
| **Rule 3** | 4 out of 5 consecutive points in Zone B or beyond (same side) | Shift in process mean |
| **Rule 4** | 7 or more consecutive points on the same side of CL | Shift in process mean |

### Extended Western Electric Rules (often tested)

| Rule # | Description | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Rule 5** | 7 consecutive points trending up or down | Trend (drift) |
| **Rule 6** | 15 consecutive points all within Zone C ($\pm 1\sigma$) | Stratification |
| **Rule 7** | 8 consecutive points on both sides of CL with none in Zone C | Mixture / stratification |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Center Line (X-bar chart) | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | Same as $X$ | Process mean estimate |
| Standard Deviation (X-bar chart) | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Same as $X$ | Process spread |
| Upper Control Limit (X-bar) | $\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Same as $X$ | $3\sigma$ upper bound |
| Lower Control Limit (X-bar) | $\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Same as $X$ | $3\sigma$ lower bound |
| Zone A boundary | $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 2\sigma$ | Same as $X$ | Outer $1\sigma$ band |
| Zone B boundary | $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 1\sigma$ | Same as $X$ | Middle $1\sigma$ band |
| Zone C boundary | $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 0\sigma$ | Same as $X$ | Inner $1\sigma$ band |
| Probability of any point in Zone A | $\approx 0.0428$ (4.28%) | — | Binomial context |
| Probability of any point in Zone B | $\approx 0.1359$ (13.59%) | — | Binomial context |
| Probability of any point in Zone C | $\approx 0.3413$ (34.13%) | — | Binomial context |
| Rule 1 false-alarm probability | $0.0027$ (0.27%) | — | One point outside $3\sigma$ |
| Rule 4 false-alarm probability | $(0.5)^7 = 0.0078125$ | — | 7 same-side run |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important for KTU Numerical Problems:** When asked to verify a pattern using the binomial probability formula, use
> $$P(\text{pattern}) = \binom{n}{r} p^r (1-p)^{n-r}$$
> where $p = 0.5$ for points above/below CL under randomness.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive Industry (Toyota Production System):** Pattern detection triggers **Jidoka** (autonomous stoppage of machines).
* **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Trend patterns in wafer thickness signal **etch time drift**.
* **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Cycle patterns in tablet weight indicate **die-press malfunction**.
* **Aerospace (AS9100 Standards):** Mandatory use of Western Electric rules in supplier quality audits.
* **Bottling Industry:** Mixture patterns in fill volume indicate **two different filling heads** working inconsistently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of 7 Consecutive Points on the Same Side (Rule 4)

This is a classic KTU derivation question. We will derive the probability of obtaining 7 consecutive points all on one side of the center line, assuming the process is genuinely in control (random variation only).

### Step 1 — Define the Event Space

Let $X_i$ be the $i^{\text{th}}$ plotted point. Under the assumption of a stable, in-control process, each $X_i$ is independently distributed with:
$$P(X_i > \bar{\bar{X}}) = P(X_i < \bar{\bar{X}}) = 0.5$$

This follows from the **symmetry of the Normal distribution** about its mean.

### Step 2 — Define a Success

Let a "success" be defined as: *a point lies above the center line*. Then:
$$p = 0.5, \quad q = 1 - p = 0.5$$

### Step 3 — Probability of a Single Point Being on a Specific Side

For any single observation, the probability of being above (or below) the center line is:
$$P(\text{single point above CL}) = 0.5$$
$$P(\text{single point below CL}) = 0.5$$

### Step 4 — Probability of $n$ Independent Consecutive Points All Above CL

Since points are independent, the joint probability is the product of marginal probabilities:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{7 points above CL}) &= P(X_1 > \text{CL}) \times P(X_2 > \text{CL}) \times \cdots \times P(X_7 > \text{CL}) \\
&= (0.5)^7 \\
&= \dfrac{1}{128} \\
&\approx 0.0078125
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Probability of 7 Consecutive Points on EITHER Side (Rule 4 Total)

By the **addition rule for mutually exclusive events** (all above OR all below):
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{7 consecutive same side}) &= P(\text{all above}) + P(\text{all below}) \\
&= (0.5)^7 + (0.5)^7 \\
&= 2 \times (0.5)^7 \\
&= \dfrac{2}{128} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{64} \\
&\approx 0.015625
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion / Engineering Interpretation

$$
P(\text{7 consecutive same side under randomness}) = \dfrac{1}{64} \approx 1.5625\%
$$

Since this probability is **very small (less than 2%)**, if we observe such a run in practice, we conclude with high confidence that the process is **out of control** (i.e., the mean has shifted).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Many textbooks quote only the one-sided probability $\left(\tfrac{1}{128}\right)$. KTU's official answer key accepts either form, but the **two-sided** probability $\left(\tfrac{1}{64}\right)$ is the more rigorous version, and is preferred for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Probability of 2 out of 3 Points in Zone A (Rule 2)

This is the most common Rule 2 numerical in KTU 2024 papers.

### Step 1 — Probability a Single Point Falls in Zone A

For a standard normal distribution, the probability a point lies **between $2\sigma$ and $3\sigma$ from the mean** (one side) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(2 \le Z \le 3) &= \Phi(3) - \Phi(2) \\
&= 0.99865 - 0.97725 \\
&= 0.02140
\end{aligned}
$$

The two-sided probability (Zone A on either side) is:
$$P(\text{Zone A, any side}) = 2 \times 0.02140 = 0.0428$$

### Step 2 — Probability a Single Point is NOT in Zone A (same side focus)

For Rule 2, we consider only **one side at a time** (e.g., "above" the CL). So the probability a point is in Zone A **above** CL is:
$$p_A = 0.02140$$
$$q_A = 1 - 0.02140 = 0.97860$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Binomial Distribution

We want exactly 2 out of 3 consecutive points in Zone A (above CL). With $n = 3$, $r = 2$, $p = 0.02140$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(r=2) &= \binom{3}{2} p^2 q^{3-2} \\
&= 3 \times (0.02140)^2 \times (0.97860)^1 \\
&= 3 \times 0.000458 \times 0.97860 \\
&\approx 0.001345
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Two-Sided Rule 2 Probability

For either side:
$$
P(\text{Rule 2 violation}) \approx 2 \times 0.001345 = 0.00269
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$
P(\text{Rule 2 violation under randomness}) \approx 0.00269 \approx 0.27\%
$$

This is comparable to the Rule 1 false-alarm rate, making Rule 2 a **highly sensitive** indicator of process shifts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pattern-to-Cause Diagnostic Table

| Pattern | Detection Rule | Probability of False Alarm | Probable Assignable Cause |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Single point out of limits | Rule 1 | 0.27% | Sudden shock, tool breakage, material defect |
| 2/3 in Zone A (same side) | Rule 2 | 0.27% | Gradual shift in process mean |
| 4/5 in Zone B (same side) | Rule 3 | ~0.5% | Slow shift, new operator |
| 7 same side of CL | Rule 4 | 1.56% | Level shift, material batch change |
| 7-point trend (up/down) | Rule 5 | 1.56% | Tool wear, temperature drift |
| 15 points in Zone C | Rule 6 | 0.30% | Over-control, stratification, wrong $\sigma$ |
| 8 points alternating, none in Zone C | Rule 7 | ~0.01% | Two populations (mixture) |
| Cyclical pattern | Visual | — | Seasonal, shift change |
| Sawtooth | Visual | — | Systematic alternating cause |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical

> **Problem:** A $\bar{X}$-chart has $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50$ and control limits $\text{UCL} = 56$, $\text{LCL} = 44$. The last 7 points are: 51, 52, 51, 53, 52, 51, 52. Identify the pattern, if any, using the Western Electric Rules.

### Step 1 — Locate Each Point

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Point 1} &= 51 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone C)} \\
\text{Point 2} &= 52 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone C)} \\
\text{Point 3} &= 51 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone C)} \\
\text{Point 4} &= 53 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone B)} \\
\text{Point 5} &= 52 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone C)} \\
\text{Point 6} &= 51 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone C)} \\
\text{Point 7} &= 52 \rightarrow \text{above CL (Zone C)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Count Consecutive Points Same Side

All 7 points are above the CL.

### Step 3 — Apply Western Electric Rule 4

Since 7 consecutive points are all on the same side of the center line, **Rule 4 is violated** → **Process is out of control**.

### Step 4 — Estimate the Probable Cause

The pattern is a **Shift (Level Change)** — the process mean has likely moved **upward** from 50. Possible causes:
* New raw material supplier.
* New machine calibration.
* New operator.

### Step 5 — Compute the Probability Under Randomness (for full KTU marks)

$$
P(\text{7 above CL}) = (0.5)^7 = \dfrac{1}{128} \approx 0.78\%
$$

Two-sided:
$$
P(\text{7 same side}) = 2 \times (0.5)^7 = \dfrac{1}{64} \approx 1.56\%
$$

Since this is far below typical significance levels (e.g., 5%), we **reject randomness** and conclude **assignable cause is present**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Full-Marks Tip:** In KTU 2024 evaluation, a complete answer must include: (a) pattern identification, (b) specific Western Electric Rule cited, (c) probability calculation, and (d) engineering interpretation. Missing any one leads to a 2–3 mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Decision Tree for Pattern Identification

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **logical decision process** a quality engineer follows when examining a control chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Plot All Points on Control Chart"] --> q1{"Any point outside 3 sigma limits?"}
    q1 -- "Yes" --> p1["PATTERN 1: Out-of-Control Point\nWestern Electric Rule 1 Violated\nAction: Investigate Sudden Shock"]
    q1 -- "No" --> q2{"7 or more consecutive points on same side of CL?"}
    q2 -- "Yes" --> p2["PATTERN 2: Shift or Level Change\nWestern Electric Rule 4 Violated\nAction: Check Material/Operator/Machine"]
    q2 -- "No" --> q3{"7 or more consecutive points trending up or down?"}
    q3 -- "Yes" --> p3["PATTERN 3: Trend or Drift\nWestern Electric Rule 5 Violated\nAction: Check Tool Wear/Temperature"]
    q3 -- "No" --> q4{"2 of 3 points in Zone A on same side?"}
    q4 -- "Yes" --> p4["PATTERN 4: Subtle Shift\nWestern Electric Rule 2 Violated\nAction: Re-verify Sigma"]
    q4 -- "No" --> q5{"4 of 5 points in Zone B or beyond on same side?"}
    q5 -- "Yes" --> p5["PATTERN 5: Mild Shift\nWestern Electric Rule 3 Violated\nAction: Audit Process Mean"]
    q5 -- "No" --> q6{"15 or more consecutive points all within Zone C?"}
    q6 -- "Yes" --> p6["PATTERN 6: Stratification or Over-Control\nWestern Electric Rule 6 Violated\nAction: Verify Sampling Method"]
    q6 -- "No" --> q7{"8 points alternating, none in Zone C?"}
    q7 -- "Yes" --> p7["PATTERN 7: Mixture of Two Populations\nWestern Electric Rule 7 Violated\nAction: Identify Two Process Sources"]
    q7 -- "No" --> q8{"Visible regular wave pattern?"}
    q8 -- "Yes" --> p8["PATTERN 8: Cycle or Periodic Variation\nAction: Check Shift Changes/Seasons"]
    q8 -- "No" --> q9{"Alternating up-down sawtooth pattern?"}
    q9 -- "Yes" --> p9["PATTERN 9: Systematic Variable\nAction: Identify Alternating Cause"]
    q9 -- "No" --> ok["Process Appears In Statistical Control\nContinue Routine Monitoring"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Zone-Test Engine

The following block diagram describes the functional architecture of an automated pattern detection system used in modern SPC software (such as Minitab, JMP, or Python-based SPC tools).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock["Data Acquisition Layer"]
        raw["Raw Measurement Data X1, X2, ..., Xn"]
    end

    subgraph computeBlock["Statistical Computation Engine"]
        meanEst["Estimate Process Mean mu_hat"]
        sigmaEst["Estimate Sigma via R_bar / d2"]
        cl["Compute Center Line CL"]
        ucl["Compute UCL = CL plus 3 sigma"]
        lcl["Compute LCL = CL minus 3 sigma"]
    end

    subgraph zoneBlock["Zone Classifier Module"]
        zA["Zone A: 2 sigma to 3 sigma band"]
        zB["Zone B: 1 sigma to 2 sigma band"]
        zC["Zone C: 0 to 1 sigma band"]
    end

    subgraph ruleBlock["Western Electric Rule Engine"]
        r1["Rule 1 Checker: Outside 3 sigma?"]
        r2["Rule 2 Checker: 2 of 3 in Zone A?"]
        r3["Rule 3 Checker: 4 of 5 in Zone B?"]
        r4["Rule 4 Checker: 7 same side?"]
        r5["Rule 5 Checker: 7 trend?"]
        r6["Rule 6 Checker: 15 in Zone C?"]
        r7["Rule 7 Checker: 8 alternating, none in Zone C?"]
    end

    subgraph outputBlock["Diagnostic Output Module"]
        pattern["Identify Pattern Type"]
        cause["Map to Assignable Cause"]
        alarm["Trigger SPC Alarm / Stop Production"]
    end

    raw --> meanEst
    raw --> sigmaEst
    meanEst --> cl
    sigmaEst --> ucl
    sigmaEst --> lcl
    cl --> zA
    cl --> zB
    cl --> zC
    ucl --> r1
    lcl --> r1
    zA --> r2
    zB --> r3
    zC --> r6
    r1 --> pattern
    r2 --> pattern
    r3 --> pattern
    r4 --> pattern
    r5 --> pattern
    r6 --> pattern
    r7 --> pattern
    pattern --> cause
    cause --> alarm
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Visual Catalog of All Major Patterns

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **textual visual schematic** describing each major control chart pattern. (For actual chart visualizations, students are encouraged to plot these in Minitab, R, or Python `matplotlib` as part of lab work.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    catalog["Control Chart Pattern Catalog"]

    catalog --> pattern1["Pattern 1: OUT-OF-CONTROL POINT\nSymbol: 1 isolated spike above UCL or below LCL\nShape: Dot far from CL\nCause: Sudden shock"]

    catalog --> pattern2["Pattern 2: SHIFT\nSymbol: 7+ points above OR below CL\nShape: Staircase step\nCause: Level change"]

    catalog --> pattern3["Pattern 3: TREND\nSymbol: 7+ points moving up or down\nShape: Diagonal line / slope\nCause: Gradual drift"]

    catalog --> pattern4["Pattern 4: CYCLE\nSymbol: Periodic peaks and troughs\nShape: Sine wave\nCause: Seasonal / shift-based"]

    catalog --> pattern5["Pattern 5: MIXTURE\nSymbol: High scatter, often near limits\nShape: Two interleaved clouds\nCause: Two process sources"]

    catalog --> pattern6["Pattern 6: STRATIFICATION\nSymbol: Tight cluster near CL\nShape: Narrow horizontal band\nCause: Over-control / wrong sigma"]

    catalog --> pattern7["Pattern 7: SYSTEMATIC VARIABLE\nSymbol: Alternating up-down\nShape: Sawtooth wave\nCause: Alternating machine / operator"]

    catalog --> pattern8["Pattern 8: RANDOM\nSymbol: Normal scatter around CL\nShape: Cloud centered on CL\nCause: Common cause only IN CONTROL"]
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flow: How Patterns Trigger Corrective Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Step 1: Continuous Data Collection from Sensors"] --> s2["Step 2: Compute Subgroup Statistics X-bar and R"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: Update Control Chart in Real Time"]
    s3 --> s4{"Step 4: Pattern Detection Engine Checks All 7 Western Electric Rules"}
    s4 -- "Rule Violated" --> s5["Step 5: Identify Specific Pattern Type"]
    s5 --> s6["Step 6: Lookup Assignable Cause from Pattern-Cause Database"]
    s6 --> s7["Step 7: Notify Operator and Quality Engineer"]
    s7 --> s8["Step 8: Stop Production and Apply Corrective Action"]
    s8 --> s9["Step 9: Verify Process Restoration with New Control Chart"]
    s4 -- "No Rule Violated" --> s10["Step 10: Continue Monitoring Process"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a "trend" pattern in a control chart. List two probable assignable causes for it. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **trend** is a non-random pattern in which **seven or more consecutive points move consistently in one direction** (either steadily increasing or steadily decreasing) on a control chart, while still possibly remaining within the control limits.

**Probable assignable causes:**
1. **Progressive tool wear** — for example, a drill bit gradually becoming blunt in a CNC machining operation.
2. **Gradual temperature drift** — for example, a furnace temperature slowly rising due to a faulty thermostat.

**Pattern Formula:** If $X_i$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ plotted point, a trend is present if:
$$X_{i+1} > X_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6 \quad \text{(upward trend)}$$
or
$$X_{i+1} < X_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6 \quad \text{(downward trend)}$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is stratification in a control chart? How is it detected? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Stratification** is a non-random control chart pattern in which plotted points are **excessively clustered around the center line (CL)**, with very few points in the outer zones (Zone A and Zone B). It indicates that the **process variation appears artificially small** compared to the true underlying variability.

**Detection Rule (Western Electric Rule 6):**
* **15 or more consecutive points** fall within Zone C, i.e., within $\pm 1\sigma$ of the center line.

**Probable Causes:**
* Incorrect computation of the standard deviation $\sigma$ (often underestimated).
* Sampling from a sub-population rather than the full process.
* Smoothing or averaging of raw data before plotting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Q (a): Explain the various types of unnatural control chart patterns with suitable sketches. Discuss the probable assignable causes for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

When a process is **out of statistical control** but points still lie within the control limits, they form **unnatural (non-random) patterns**. The major types are:

**1. Trend (Drift):** A continuous, steady increase or decrease of 7+ consecutive points.
* *Cause:* Tool wear, temperature drift, raw material depletion.

**2. Shift (Level Change):** A sudden jump in the process mean; 7+ consecutive points lie on the same side of CL.
* *Cause:* New operator, new material batch, new machine setting.

**3. Cycle (Periodic Variation):** A regular, repeating wave-like pattern.
* *Cause:* Seasonal effects, shift changes, periodic maintenance.

**4. Mixture:** Excessive scatter with many points in Zone A; indicates two process sources.
* *Cause:* Two different machines, two different material suppliers.

**5. Stratification:** Points cluster too tightly around the center line.
* *Cause:* Over-control, wrong computation of $\sigma$.

**6. Over-control:** Artificial smoothness from excessive adjustment.
* *Cause:* Operator over-reacting to small deviations.

**7. Systematic Variable:** Regular alternating up-down pattern (sawtooth).
* *Cause:* Alternating operators, alternating machine cycles.

**Sketch representation (textual):**

```
Trend:        /  /  /  /  /  /  /
Shift:   ___________
              ____________
Cycle:    /\    /\    /\
Mixture:  *  * *  **  *  * **  *
Stratif:  ---*--*--*--*--*--*---  (narrow band)
```

> *(For full marks, students should draw clean labeled sketches with CL, UCL, and LCL on a graph paper. The above is a textual proxy for explanation.)*

---

**Q (b): Compute the probability of obtaining 8 consecutive points on the same side of the center line in a Shewhart control chart, assuming the process is in control. Use this to comment on the Western Electric Rule for "8 same-side points". (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Setup**
Under the assumption of an in-control process, each plotted point is equally likely to fall above or below the center line:
$$P(\text{point above CL}) = P(\text{point below CL}) = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Probability of 8 Consecutive Points All Above CL**
$$
P(\text{all 8 above}) = (0.5)^8 = \dfrac{1}{256} \approx 0.003906
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of 8 Consecutive Points All Below CL**
$$
P(\text{all 8 below}) = (0.5)^8 = \dfrac{1}{256} \approx 0.003906
$$

**Step 4 — Combined Probability (Either Side)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{8 consecutive same side}) &= P(\text{all above}) + P(\text{all below}) \\
&= 2 \times (0.5)^8 \\
&= \dfrac{2}{256} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{128} \\
&\approx 0.0078125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on the Rule**
The probability of obtaining 8 consecutive points on the same side purely by chance is approximately **0.78%**, which is **less than 1%**. This is much smaller than typical significance thresholds (e.g., 5%). Hence, observing such a run in practice provides strong statistical evidence that the process is **out of control** due to an assignable cause (typically a shift in the process mean).

The Western Electric Rule corresponding to this is the generalized version of **Rule 4** — any run of 7+ (or 8+) consecutive points on the same side of CL is treated as evidence of a process shift.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> * **Do not** compute the probability for only one side and call it "the answer". Always include both sides unless the question explicitly specifies direction.
> * **Do not** confuse Rule 4 (7 same-side) with Rule 5 (7 trend). They are different — Rule 4 is for *shift*, Rule 5 is for *drift*.
> * **Do not** state the probability as a percentage without showing the binomial/multiplication working. Examiners award method marks for the derivation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q (a): Explain the Western Electric Rules for control chart pattern detection. State the false-alarm probability for each rule. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Western Electric Rules** are standardized statistical tests used to detect non-random patterns in Shewhart control charts, formalized in the *Western Electric Statistical Quality Control Handbook (1956)*. They are based on the **standard normal (Z) distribution** and the 3-sigma control limits.

| Rule | Description | False-Alarm Probability |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **Rule 1** | Any single point outside the $3\sigma$ control limits | $\approx 0.0027$ (0.27%) |
| **Rule 2** | 2 out of 3 consecutive points in Zone A or beyond (same side) | $\approx 0.0027$ (0.27%) |
| **Rule 3** | 4 out of 5 consecutive points in Zone B or beyond (same side) | $\approx 0.0054$ (0.54%) |
| **Rule 4** | 7 or more consecutive points on the same side of CL | $\approx 0.0156$ (1.56%) |
| **Rule 5** | 7 or more consecutive points trending up or down | $\approx 0.0156$ (1.56%) |
| **Rule 6** | 15 or more consecutive points within Zone C ($\pm 1\sigma$) | $\approx 0.0033$ (0.33%) |
| **Rule 7** | 8 consecutive points on both sides of CL with none in Zone C | $\approx 0.0001$ (0.01%) |

**Zone Definitions:**
* **Zone A** = band between $2\sigma$ and $3\sigma$ from CL.
* **Zone B** = band between $1\sigma$ and $2\sigma$ from CL.
* **Zone C** = band between $0$ and $1\sigma$ from CL.

**Interpretation:** If the false-alarm probability is small (typically < 5%), and a rule is violated, the process is judged **out of control** with that level of statistical confidence.

---

**Q (b): The last 9 points of an $\bar{X}$-chart with $\bar{\bar{X}} = 100$ and $\sigma = 4$ are: 102, 104, 106, 108, 110, 112, 114, 116, 118. Apply the appropriate Western Electric Rule and comment on the state of the process. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Verify the Control Limits**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma = 100 + 3(4) = 100 + 12 = 112 \\
\text{LCL} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma = 100 - 3(4) = 100 - 12 = 88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify Each Point**
All nine points (102, 104, 106, 108, 110, 112, 114, 116, 118) lie **within** the control limits (88, 112) and are all **above** the center line $\bar{\bar{X}} = 100$. The 9th point (118) is **outside UCL = 112** → Rule 1 is also violated.

**Step 3 — Check the Trend (Western Electric Rule 5)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
102 \to 104: & \text{ increase} \\
104 \to 106: & \text{ increase} \\
106 \to 108: & \text{ increase} \\
108 \to 110: & \text{ increase} \\
110 \to 112: & \text{ increase} \\
112 \to 114: & \text{ increase} \\
114 \to 116: & \text{ increase} \\
116 \to 118: & \text{ increase}
\end{aligned}
$$
There are **8 consecutive increases** → a clear **upward trend** is present → **Rule 5 is violated**.

**Step 4 — Compute Probability Under Randomness**
The probability of 8 consecutive increases purely by chance:
$$
P(\text{8 increases}) = (0.5)^8 = \dfrac{1}{256} \approx 0.0039 = 0.39\%
$$

The probability of 9 consecutive same-side points (Rule 4):
$$
P(\text{9 same side}) = 2 \times (0.5)^9 = \dfrac{2}{512} = \dfrac{1}{256} \approx 0.39\%
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
Since both Rule 1 (point outside UCL) and Rule 5 (8-point upward trend) are violated, with very low probability under random behavior, the process is **definitely out of control**. The pattern is a **trend (upward drift)** with a possible **shift in process mean**.

**Probable Assignable Causes:**
* Progressive tool wear.
* Gradual increase in machine temperature.
* Raw material property changing batch-by-batch.
* Operator gradually adjusting the machine setting (tampering).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> * Students often miss that **multiple rules can be violated simultaneously**. Always state *every* rule that is broken.
> * In trend questions, **state the direction explicitly** ("upward trend" vs "downward trend") — a vague "trend pattern" loses 1 mark.
> * Always compute the **probability under randomness** to justify your conclusion — without it, the answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPET415 — Module 3 (Control Chart Patterns):**

- [ ] **Two States of a Process:** In-control (random) vs. Out-of-control (assignable cause).
- [ ] **Pattern vs. Limit Rule:** Limits check *position*; patterns check *sequence*.
- [ ] **Trend:** 7+ consecutive points moving in one direction → tool wear, temperature drift.
- [ ] **Shift:** 7+ consecutive points on one side of CL → new material, new operator, new machine setting.
- [ ] **Cycle:** Repeating wave pattern → seasonal effects, shift changes.
- [ ] **Mixture:** Excess scatter, points in Zone A → two process sources.
- [ ] **Stratification:** Points hugging CL → wrong $\sigma$, over-control, data smoothing.
- [ ] **Over-Control:** Artificial smoothness → operator tampering.
- [ ] **Systematic Variable:** Regular alternating sawtooth → alternating machine/operator.
- [ ] **Zone A:** $2\sigma$ to $3\sigma$ band.
- [ ] **Zone B:** $1\sigma$ to $2\sigma$ band.
- [ ] **Zone C:** $0$ to $1\sigma$ band.
- [ ] **Rule 1 false alarm:** $0.0027$ (0.27%) — single point outside limits.
- [ ] **Rule 2 false alarm:** $\approx 0.0027$ (0.27%) — 2 of 3 in Zone A.
- [ ] **Rule 3 false alarm:** $\approx 0.0054$ (0.54%) — 4 of 5 in Zone B.
- [ ] **Rule 4 false alarm:** $2 \times (0.5)^7 = 0.0156$ (1.56%) — 7 same side.
- [ ] **Rule 5 false alarm:** $(0.5)^6 = 0.0156$ (1.56%) — 7-point trend (one direction).
- [ ] **Rule 6 false alarm:** $\approx 0.0033$ (0.33%) — 15 points in Zone C.
- [ ] **Rule 7 false alarm:** $\approx 0.0001$ (0.01%) — 8 alternating, none in Zone C.
- [ ] **Probability under randomness:** For $n$ consecutive same-side points → use $2 \times (0.5)^n$.
- [ ] **Engineering mapping:** Each pattern has a *typical* assignable cause → use as diagnostic tool.
- [ ] **Pattern detection precedes corrective action** in the SPC cycle.
- [ ] **Memorize zone boundaries** — examiners often give $\sigma$ values, not zone labels.
- [ ] **In KTU answers, always state the Rule number, the pattern name, AND the probability** — full-marks formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Run Sum Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Run Sum Test (Cumulative Sum / CUSUM Test):** The Run Sum Test is a sequential statistical procedure used in Statistical Quality Control (SQC) to detect small, sustained shifts in the **process mean** ($\mu$) by accumulating (summing) the deviations of sample observations from a target/reference value. Unlike control charts that use only the *current* sample, the CUSUM chart integrates historical information, making it superior for detecting shifts of magnitude **0.5$\sigma$ to 2.5$\sigma$** from the target.

The test was popularized by **E.S. Page (1954)** and is formally grounded in the theory of **sequential analysis** (Wald, 1945). It is categorized as a **"memory-type" control chart** because each plotted point carries information from all prior samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Distinction:**
> - **Run** → A sequence of consecutive points either all above or all below the centerline.
> - **Cumulative Sum (CUSUM, $S_i$)** → The running total of deviations from a reference ($K$) value.
> - **Decision Interval ($H$)** → The threshold above/below which an out-of-control signal is raised.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowball on a Hill"

Imagine you are standing at the base of a snow-covered mountain, and your **target** is the center of the slope. Each minute, you note whether a falling snowflake lands to the **left or right** of your target by some small distance. If the wind is calm, the flakes will randomly sprinkle on both sides and the **cumulative offset** stays near zero. But if a strong wind begins blowing consistently from the east, the offsets all start accumulating in one direction — like a **rolling snowball** that grows larger and larger with each passing minute.

A standard $\bar{X}$ control chart sees only the *last* snowflake (the current sample mean) and may miss the trend. The **Run Sum (CUSUM) chart**, however, *adds up* all the deviations — so even tiny, persistent drifts are quickly magnified into a visible "snowball" that crosses the **decision boundary** $H$, alerting the operator that the process mean has shifted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical / Engineering Constants in this Topic:**
> - **Reference value $K$ (Slack value / Allowance)** → Typically chosen as $K = \frac{\delta}{2}\sigma_{\bar{X}}$, where $\delta$ is the shift (in $\sigma$ units) you want to detect.
> - **Decision Interval $H$** → Typically $H = 4\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ or $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ for standardized tabulated ARL performance.
> - **In-control ARL (Average Run Length)** $\approx 370$ for $H = 5\sigma$ two-sided test.

---

## 1.3 Why the Run Sum Test Exists — Limitation of Shewhart Charts

| Feature | Shewhart $\bar{X}$-Chart | Run Sum (CUSUM) Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Information used per point** | Only the current subgroup mean | All past subgroup means (cumulative) |
| **Sensitivity to small shifts ($\le 1.5\sigma$)** | **Poor** (ARL very high) | **Excellent** (ARL short) |
| **Reaction speed to large shifts** | Fast | Comparatively slower |
| **Plot type** | Individual point vs. UCL/LCL | Cumulative sum vs. Decision interval $H$ |
| **Best application** | Large shifts ($\ge 2.5\sigma$) | Small-to-moderate persistent shifts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever a question asks *"Which chart is best for detecting small sustained shifts in process mean?"*, the official KTU answer is the **CUSUM / Run Sum Chart**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Concept Breakdown

The Run Sum Test operates on a beautifully simple principle: **transformation + accumulation + decision**.

1. **Step 1 — Standardize the observation.** Convert each sample mean $\bar{X}_i$ into a Z-score using the in-control parameters:
 $Z_i = \frac{\bar{X}_i - \mu_0}{\sigma_{\bar{X}}}$

 where $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ is the standard error of the mean for subgroup size $n$.

2. **Step 2 — Choose the reference (slack) value $K$.** This is **half the smallest shift** $\delta$ (in $\sigma$ units) you wish to detect with high probability. Standard choice:
 $K = \frac{\delta}{2}\sigma_{\bar{X}}$

3. **Step 3 — Form the one-sided cumulative sums.**
 - **Upper CUSUM** (detects upward shift):
 $S_i^+ = \max[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (Z_i - K)]$

 - **Lower CUSUM** (detects downward shift):
 $S_i^- = \min[0,\; S_{i-1}^- + (Z_i + K)]$

 The $\max[0, \cdot]$ operator is critical — it *resets* the cumulative path to zero whenever the deviation becomes unfavorable, preventing false alarms from random negative fluctuations.

4. **Step 4 — Apply the decision rule.**
 - If $S_i^+ \ge H$ → **OUT-OF-CONTROL** (mean has shifted upward).
 - If $S_i^- \le -H$ → **OUT-OF-CONTROL** (mean has shifted downward).
 - Otherwise → Continue sampling.

5. **Step 5 — Reset the path.** Once a signal is given, the operator takes corrective action and the cumulative path is reset to zero for the next investigation.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Intuitive Engineering Rationale

- **Why use $\max[0, \cdot]$?** It guarantees the CUSUM only accumulates evidence of the *specific* shift we are hunting. Random noise that points the "wrong way" gets discarded, keeping the test statistically clean.
- **Why subtract $K$?** It provides a "dead zone" near zero so that minor natural process noise doesn't accumulate into a false signal. Only deviations that exceed $K$ get integrated.
- **How is sensitivity tuned?** By choosing $\delta$ (the shift we care about). Smaller $\delta$ → smaller $K$ → more sensitive (but more false alarms).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standardized deviation | $Z_i = \dfrac{\bar{X}_i - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Standard error of mean | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Same units as $X$ |
| 3 | Slack / Reference value | $K = \dfrac{\delta}{2}\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ | Same units as $\bar{X}$ |
| 4 | Upper CUSUM recursion | $S_i^+ = \max[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 - K)]$ | Same units as $\bar{X}$ |
| 5 | Lower CUSUM recursion | $S_i^- = \min[0,\; S_{i-1}^- + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 + K)]$ | Same units as $\bar{X}$ |
| 6 | Decision Interval (two-sided) | $H = 4\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ or $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ | Same units as $\bar{X}$ |
| 7 | In-control ARL (two-sided) | $ARL_0 \approx 370$ (for $H=5\sigma$) | number of samples |
| 8 | Out-of-control ARL (approx.) | $ARL_1 \approx \dfrac{H}{\delta - K} + 1.4$ | number of samples |
| 9 | Initial conditions | $S_0^+ = 0,\; S_0^- = 0$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Signal conditions | $S_i^+ \ge H$ or $S_i^- \le -H$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Warning:** Some textbooks (e.g., Montgomery, 7th ed.) define the recursion using the *raw* deviation $\bar{X}_i - \mu_0$ (with $K$ in same units as $\bar{X}$). Others use the *standardized* form $Z_i - K$ (with $K$ as a pure number). **Both are mathematically equivalent.** Always verify which form the question uses.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Detecting subtle drift in tablet weight during long compression runs.
- **Semiconductor fabrication:** Monitoring thin-film thickness deposition where shifts of 0.1 µm matter.
- **Automotive piston machining:** Catching slow tool wear before it produces defective batches.
- **Chemical process control:** Refinery temperature/pressure monitoring where the standard Shewhart chart misses gradual catalyst deactivation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (Standard KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A process has target mean $\mu_0 = 100$, known $\sigma = 4$, and subgroup size $n = 4$. The engineer wishes to detect a shift of magnitude $\delta = 1.0\sigma$ (i.e., a 1-sigma shift). The 12 consecutive sample means are:
> $\bar{X} = \{101.0,\; 102.5,\; 100.8,\; 99.5,\; 103.2,\; 104.1,\; 105.0,\; 104.5,\; 106.0,\; 105.8,\; 104.0,\; 103.5\}$
>
> Construct a one-sided (upper-tail) CUSUM chart using $K = 0.5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$. Does the process signal out-of-control?

### Step 1 — Compute the Standard Error

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.000
$$

**Logic:** The standard error shrinks with $\sqrt{n}$ because averaging $n$ observations reduces variability. Here, $n=4$ halves the standard deviation of the mean.

### Step 2 — Compute $K$ and $H$

$$
K = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.000
$$

$$
H = 5 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} = 5 \times 2 = 10.000
$$

**Logic:** Since $\delta = 1.0\sigma$, the slack $K$ is half of one standard error — meaning only deviations exceeding 1.0 unit above target will start accumulating. The decision interval $H=10$ is the standard choice giving $ARL_0 \approx 370$.

### Step 3 — Form the Upper CUSUM Recursion Table

Apply the formula:

$$
S_i^+ = \max\Big[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 - K)\Big] = \max\Big[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - 100 - 1)\Big] = \max\Big[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - 101)\Big]
$$

| Sample $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $\bar{X}_i - 101$ | $S_{i-1}^+$ | $S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - 101)$ | $S_i^+ = \max[0,\;\cdot]$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 101.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | **0.0** |
| 2 | 102.5 | 1.5 | 0.0 | 1.5 | **1.5** |
| 3 | 100.8 | -0.2 | 1.5 | 1.3 | **1.3** |
| 4 | 99.5  | -1.5 | 1.3 | -0.2 | **0.0** (reset!) |
| 5 | 103.2 | 2.2 | 0.0 | 2.2 | **2.2** |
| 6 | 104.1 | 3.1 | 2.2 | 5.3 | **5.3** |
| 7 | 105.0 | 4.0 | 5.3 | 9.3 | **9.3** |
| 8 | 104.5 | 3.5 | 9.3 | 12.8 | **12.8** |
| 9 | 106.0 | 5.0 | 12.8 | 17.8 | **17.8** ⚠️ |
| 10 | 105.8 | 4.8 | 17.8 | 22.6 | **22.6** ⚠️ |
| 11 | 104.0 | 3.0 | 22.6 | 25.6 | **25.6** ⚠️ |
| 12 | 103.5 | 2.5 | 25.6 | 28.1 | **28.1** ⚠️ |

### Step 4 — Decision Rule Application

Compare $S_i^+$ against $H = 10$:

- **At sample 8:** $S_8^+ = 12.8 \ge 10.0$ → **OUT-OF-CONTROL SIGNAL!**

**Conclusion:** The first signal occurs at the 8th sample. Once the signal is raised, corrective action is initiated (look for tool wear, raw material change, etc.) and the CUSUM path is reset to zero for the next phase of monitoring.

### Step 5 — Compute ARL Performance

The out-of-control ARL for the chart can be estimated by:

$$
ARL_1 = \frac{H}{\delta - K} + 1.4 = \frac{10}{(1.0)(2) - 1.0} + 1.4 = \frac{10}{1.0} + 1.4 = 11.4 \text{ samples}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the chart will detect a 1$\sigma$ upward shift within roughly 11–12 samples — dramatically faster than a Shewhart $\bar{X}$-chart (which needs ~55 samples for a 1$\sigma$ shift). This is the headline advantage of CUSUM.

### Step 6 — Standardize for Comparison (Alternate Form)

If we re-express using the standardized form $Z_i = (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0)/\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and $K = 0.5$:

$$
S_i^+ = \max[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (Z_i - 0.5)]
$$

| $i$ | $Z_i$ | $Z_i - 0.5$ | $S_i^+$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 0.5  | 0.0  | 0.0  |
| 2 | 1.25 | 0.75 | 0.75 |
| 3 | 0.4  | -0.1 | 0.65 |
| 4 | -0.25| -0.75| 0.0  |
| 5 | 1.6  | 1.1  | 1.1  |
| 6 | 2.05 | 1.55 | 2.65 |
| 7 | 2.5  | 2.0  | 4.65 |
| 8 | 2.25 | 1.75 | **6.4 ≥ 5.0** ⚠️ |

**Logic:** Here $H = 5$ in standardized units, and the signal triggers at sample 8 — same answer, two equivalent representations.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix for Common KTU Cases

| Observation Pattern | Likely Cause | CUSUM Behavior | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_i^+$ drifts slowly upward over many samples | Tool wear, gradual drift | Smooth crossing of $H$ | Adjust tool/recalibrate |
| $S_i^+$ spikes high then resets | Isolated outliers, no trend | Frequent $\max[0,\cdot]$ resets | Check measurement system |
| $S_i^+$ oscillates near zero | In-control process | Stays below $H$ indefinitely | Continue monitoring |
| Both $S_i^+$ and $S_i^-$ cross $H$ | Process oscillating around target | Frequent two-sided signals | Investigate variance, not mean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When solving a CUSUM problem, always state the **initial condition** $S_0^+ = S_0^- = 0$, and the **units of $K$ and $H$** explicitly. Examiners specifically look for these.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — CUSUM Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sample Data Xi] --> B[Compute Subgroup Mean Xbar_i]
    B --> C[Compute Standard Error sigma_Xbar = sigma divided by sqrt of n]
    C --> D{Choose K and H from Shift Delta}
    D --> E[Compute Deviation di = Xbar_i minus mu0]
    E --> F[Subtract Slack K from di]
    F --> G{Is deviation positive?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Upper CUSUM Si+ = max of 0 and Si-1+ plus di minus K]
    G -- No --> I[Lower CUSUM Si- = min of 0 and Si-1- plus di plus K]
    H --> J{Is Si+ greater or equal to H?}
    I --> K{Is Si- less or equal to minus H?}
    J -- Yes --> L[OUT OF CONTROL SIGNAL UPWARD]
    J -- No --> M[Plot Si+ vs i Continue Monitoring]
    K -- Yes --> N[OUT OF CONTROL SIGNAL DOWNWARD]
    K -- No --> O[Plot Si- vs i Continue Monitoring]
    L --> P[Reset CUSUM Path to Zero]
    N --> P
    M --> Q[Next Sample]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 CUSUM vs Shewhart Sensitivity Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A[Shift Magnitude Delta in sigma units]
        A1[Delta less than 0.5]
        A2[Delta between 0.5 and 1.5]
        A3[Delta between 1.5 and 2.5]
        A4[Delta greater than 2.5]
    end
    A1 --> R1[Both charts slow but CUSUM better]
    A2 --> R2[CUSUM strongly preferred]
    A3 --> R3[Both comparable CUSUM slightly better]
    A4 --> R4[Shewhart competitive EWMA also viable]
```

## 4.3 CUSUM Path Geometry (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Start S0 equals 0] --> P1[Sample 1 deviation zero path flat at 0]
    P1 --> P2[Sample 2 small upward bump]
    P2 --> P3[Sample 3 small dip but path remains above 0]
    P3 --> P4[Sample 4 reset to 0 due to negative deviation]
    P4 --> P5[Sample 5 onward steady upward climb]
    P5 --> P6[Sample 6 climb accelerates]
    P6 --> P7[Sample 7 approaching H]
    P7 --> P8[Sample 8 crosses H SIGNAL fires]
    P8 --> P9[Corrective action taken path reset to 0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Run Sum (CUSUM) test. Why is it preferred over the Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart for detecting small process shifts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Run Sum Test is a sequential statistical procedure that accumulates deviations of sample means from a target value and plots the cumulative sum against a decision interval $H$. It is preferred over the Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart for detecting small shifts (typically 0.5$\sigma$ to 2.5$\sigma$) because the CUSUM uses **all historical data** (memory-type chart), giving a much lower Average Run Length (ARL) for small persistent drifts, whereas the Shewhart chart only uses the current subgroup and has a very high ARL for small shifts.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the role of the reference value $K$ and the decision interval $H$ in a Run Sum chart.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **reference value $K$** acts as a *slack allowance* — it is usually set to half the magnitude of the smallest shift $\delta$ (in $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ units) that the engineer wants to detect. It prevents trivial noise from accumulating into a false signal. The **decision interval $H$** is the threshold against which $S_i^+$ (or $S_i^-$) is compared. If $S_i^+ \ge H$, the process is declared out-of-control upward; if $S_i^- \le -H$, it is declared out-of-control downward. Typical $H = 4\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ or $5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction of a one-sided upper CUSUM chart with the help of the recursion formula. Define all symbols and state the decision rule clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process has $\mu_0 = 50$, $\sigma = 2$, $n = 5$. Detect a shift of $\delta = 1\sigma$. Use $K = 0.5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$. Given 10 sample means: $\{51.2,\; 50.8,\; 52.1,\; 51.5,\; 52.8,\; 53.4,\; 52.0,\; 53.9,\; 54.5,\; 53.0\}$, construct the CUSUM and find when the process signals. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — One-Sided Upper CUSUM Construction

**Step 1 — Recursion Formula (3 marks):**

$$
S_i^+ = \max\Big[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 - K)\Big], \quad S_0^+ = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Symbol definitions (2 marks):**
- $S_i^+$ = Upper cumulative sum at sample $i$
- $\bar{X}_i$ = $i$-th subgroup mean
- $\mu_0$ = Target / in-control mean
- $K$ = Slack value (reference allowance)
- $\max[0, \cdot]$ = Resets path to zero if cumulative sum goes negative

**Step 3 — Decision Rule (2 marks):**
- If $S_i^+ \ge H$ → **OUT-OF-CONTROL** (mean has shifted upward by $\ge \delta$).
- If $S_i^+ < H$ → Continue monitoring.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical CUSUM

**Step 1 — Compute parameters (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{2.236} = 0.8944
$$

$$
K = 0.5 \times 0.8944 = 0.4472
$$

$$
H = 5 \times 0.8944 = 4.472
$$

**Step 2 — Apply recursion:** $S_i^+ = \max[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - 50 - 0.4472)]$

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $\bar{X}_i - 50.4472$ | $S_{i-1}^+$ | $S_i^+$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 51.2 | 0.7528 | 0.0 | 0.7528 |
| 2 | 50.8 | 0.3528 | 0.7528 | 1.1056 |
| 3 | 52.1 | 1.6528 | 1.1056 | 2.7584 |
| 4 | 51.5 | 1.0528 | 2.7584 | 3.8112 |
| 5 | 52.8 | 2.3528 | 3.8112 | **6.1640** ⚠️ |

**Step 3 — Decision (2 marks):** At sample $i = 5$, $S_5^+ = 6.1640 \ge H = 4.472$, so the process **signals out-of-control upward at the 5th sample**.

**Step 4 — ARL estimate (1 mark):**

$$
ARL_1 = \frac{H}{\delta - K} + 1.4 = \frac{4.472}{0.8944 - 0.4472} + 1.4 = \frac{4.472}{0.4472} + 1.4 = 10.0 + 1.4 = 11.4 \text{ samples}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the Shewhart $\bar{X}$-chart and the CUSUM chart in terms of: (i) type of information used, (ii) sensitivity to small shifts, (iii) Average Run Length performance, and (iv) best engineering application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The inside diameter of a bearing is specified as $\mu_0 = 25.00$ mm with $\sigma = 0.10$ mm. Samples of size $n = 5$ are taken hourly. The engineer wants to detect a shift of $\delta = 0.5\sigma$. Use $K = 0.25\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and $H = 4\sigma_{\bar{X}}$. The first 8 sample means (in mm) are: $\{25.02,\; 25.05,\; 25.01,\; 25.08,\; 25.10,\; 25.06,\; 25.09,\; 25.11\}$. Construct a two-sided CUSUM and state whether the process is in control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Comparison

| Criterion | Shewhart $\bar{X}$-Chart | CUSUM / Run Sum Chart |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Information used | Only the **current** subgroup mean | **All past** subgroup means (cumulative) |
| (ii) Sensitivity to small shifts | Poor (high $ARL$ for $\delta \le 1.5\sigma$) | Excellent (low $ARL$ for $\delta \le 1.5\sigma$) |
| (iii) ARL for in-control | $ARL_0 \approx 370$ | $ARL_0 \approx 370$ (when $H=5\sigma$) |
| (iii) ARL for $\delta = 1\sigma$ | $\approx 55$ samples | $\approx 11$ samples |
| (iv) Best application | Large abrupt shifts ($\ge 2.5\sigma$) | Small sustained shifts ($0.5\sigma$ to $2.5\sigma$) |

**(7 marks awarded for clear tabular comparison with at least 4 criteria elaborated.)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Two-Sided CUSUM Construction

**Step 1 — Parameters (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{0.10}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.10}{2.236} = 0.04472 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
K = 0.25 \times 0.04472 = 0.01118 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H = 4 \times 0.04472 = 0.17888 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Two-sided recursion (1 mark):**

$$
S_i^+ = \max[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - 25.00 - 0.01118)]
$$

$$
S_i^- = \min[0,\; S_{i-1}^- + (\bar{X}_i - 25.00 + 0.01118)]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute table (4 marks):**

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $\bar{X}_i - 25.0112$ | $S_i^+$ | $\bar{X}_i - 24.9888$ | $S_i^-$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 25.02 | 0.0088 | 0.0088 | 0.0312 | 0.0312 |
| 2 | 25.05 | 0.0388 | 0.0476 | 0.0612 | 0.0924 |
| 3 | 25.01 | -0.0012 | 0.0464 | 0.0212 | 0.1136 |
| 4 | 25.08 | 0.0688 | 0.1152 | 0.0912 | 0.2048 |
| 5 | 25.10 | 0.0888 | **0.2040** | 0.1112 | 0.3160 |
| 6 | 25.06 | 0.0488 | 0.2528 | 0.0712 | 0.3872 |
| 7 | 25.09 | 0.0788 | 0.3316 | 0.1012 | 0.4884 |
| 8 | 25.11 | 0.0988 | **0.4304** | 0.1212 | 0.6096 |

**Step 4 — Decision (1 mark):**
- $H = 0.17888$
- $S_5^+ = 0.2040 \ge H$ → **OUT-OF-CONTROL upward at sample 5**.

The process mean has shifted upward, and corrective action must be taken at the 5th hour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $S_0^+ = 0$** — many students start the recursion with a non-zero initial value. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Using $\sigma$ instead of $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ in $K$ and $H$** — the formulas require the **standard error of the mean**, not the population/process standard deviation. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Not showing the $\max[0, \cdot]$ reset explicitly** — the examiner wants to see negative cumulative sums being reset to zero in the table. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Comparing against the wrong threshold** — for a one-sided chart, you only compare $S_i^+ \ge H$ (or only $S_i^- \le -H$). Mixing both into one threshold loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting units in $K$ and $H$** — always write "mm" or the relevant unit explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Full Name:** Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) Control Chart, also called **Run Sum Test**.
- ✅ **Inventors:** E.S. Page (1954) for the chart; A. Wald (1945) for the underlying sequential theory.
- ✅ **Primary Use:** Detection of **small, sustained shifts** in the process mean (0.5$\sigma$ to 2.5$\sigma$).
- ✅ **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
 - $S_i^+ = \max[0,\; S_{i-1}^+ + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 - K)]$
 - $S_i^- = \min[0,\; S_{i-1}^- + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 + K)]$
 - $K = \dfrac{\delta}{2}\sigma_{\bar{X}}$, $\;H = 4\sigma_{\bar{X}}\text{ or }5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$
- ✅ **Initial Condition:** $S_0^+ = S_0^- = 0$.
- ✅ **Signal Rule:** $S_i^+ \ge H$ → upward shift; $S_i^- \le -H$ → downward shift.
- ✅ **ARL Benchmark:** $ARL_0 \approx 370$ when $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.
- ✅ **ARL for $\delta = 1\sigma$:** Shewhart $\approx 55$, CUSUM $\approx 11$.
- ✅ **Type of Chart:** Memory-type / sequential (uses all historical data).
- ✅ **Always remember to use $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$, not $\sigma$**, when computing $K$ and $H$ (unless the question is in standardized form).
- ✅ **Reset on signal:** After firing the alarm and taking corrective action, the CUSUM path must be reinitialized to zero.
- ✅ **KTU Favored Comparison Question:** "Compare CUSUM with Shewhart chart" — use the 4-criterion matrix: information used, sensitivity, ARL, application.
- ✅ **Closely related alternative:** **EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) chart** — also good for small shifts but with different weighting geometry. KTU often pairs the two in the same module.
- ✅ **Reset Behavior is Unique:** Unlike Shewhart charts, the CUSUM path can *decrease* (back to zero) when unfavorable samples appear — this is a feature, not a bug.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Revising Control Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET415 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Revising Control Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Revising Control Limits** is the systematic, iterative procedure in **Phase I (Retrospective / Trial) Analysis** of a Statistical Process Control (SPC) study wherein the initially estimated trial control limits (UCL, CL, LCL) on a Shewhart control chart are refined, recomputed, and finalized. This revision is performed using only the subgroup data points that, after investigation, are found to be in a state of **statistical control** (i.e., points lying within the trial control limits and exhibiting no non-random patterns). 

The objective of revising control limits is to **eliminate the effect of assignable (special) causes of variation** from the historical dataset, so that the resulting limits accurately represent the **inherent (common-cause) variability** of the process. These revised limits then become the **standard reference** for ongoing **Phase II (Prospective / Real-Time) process monitoring** of future production output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A control chart is NEVER considered "final" after the very first plotting. Revision is a *mandatory* step in any KTU-endorsed SQC study unless the analyst can mathematically prove that no assignable cause was present in the original data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **fitness trainer** measuring the resting heart rate of 50 clients over a week to determine a "normal range" for your gym. 

- **First Pass (Trial Limits):** You compute the average and a range based on *all* 50 readings. Two readings come from clients who were running late (anxiety-induced spike) and two from clients who had just had heavy meals. These are **abnormal events** (assignable causes).
- **The Revision:** You discard those 4 abnormal readings and recompute the average heart rate and the "normal range" using only the 46 readings taken under typical, calm conditions.
- **Final Result:** The revised range is tighter, more accurate, and now reliably represents what a *normal* client looks like. Any future client whose heart rate falls outside this revised range is flagged for a closer look.

**The control chart works exactly the same way.** The trial limits are a rough first estimate. The revision cleans out the "noise from special events" so that the final limits are tight, reliable, and ready for prime-time monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** The **3-sigma (3σ)** limit is the universally accepted boundary for the standard Shewhart control chart — it corresponds to a **0.27%** probability of a point falling outside the limits when the process is truly in control. This is why the revision process aims to keep *all* remaining points within the ±3σ envelope.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Phases of Control Chart Implementation

A complete SPC study using control charts is divided into two distinct phases:

| Phase | Name | Purpose | Limits Used |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
| **Phase I** | Retrospective / Trial Analysis | Establish *reliable* control limits from historical/process-startup data | **Trial limits** → **Revised limits** |
| **Phase II** | Prospective / Real-Time Monitoring | Monitor *future* production to detect new assignable causes | **Revised limits (from Phase I)** |

**Revising Control Limits is the culminating activity of Phase I.**

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Revision Procedure

The KTU-mandated iterative procedure for revising control limits is:

1. **Step 1 — Collect Preliminary Data:** Draw $m$ preliminary samples (subgroups) of size $n$ from the process. Compute subgroup statistics (e.g., $\bar{X}_i$, $R_i$).
2. **Step 2 — Compute Trial Limits:** Using *all* the preliminary data, compute the grand average $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and the average range $\bar{R}$. Derive the **trial control limits** using the standard SPC constants.
3. **Step 3 — Plot Trial Chart:** Plot the subgroup statistics on the control chart using the trial limits.
4. **Step 4 — Investigate Out-of-Control Points:** Examine every point that falls **outside** the trial control limits. For each such point, conduct a root-cause analysis. If a real, identifiable assignable cause is found, **document** it and **remove** that subgroup from the dataset.
5. **Step 5 — Recompute Revised Limits:** Using only the subgroups that *remained within* the trial limits and showed no non-random pattern, recompute $\bar{\bar{X}}_{new}$, $\bar{R}_{new}$, and the new control limits.
6. **Step 6 — Iterate:** Plot the previously excluded (and other) points against the new revised limits. If additional points now fall outside, repeat Steps 4–5.
7. **Step 7 — Finalize:** Stop iterating only when **no points fall outside the limits** and the chart shows no non-random behaviour. The resulting limits are the **final revised control limits**, which are then used for Phase II.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Revision Necessary?** Trial limits are biased — they are inflated by the very assignable causes they are supposed to detect. This makes the chart *less sensitive* (a phenomenon called **masking**). Revision removes this bias, giving sharper, more accurate limits.

## 2.3 The Eight Western Electric / Nelson Rules (Non-Random Patterns)

A point may lie *within* the control limits but still indicate an assignable cause via a non-random **pattern**. KTU examiners frequently test knowledge of these:

- **Rule 1:** One point beyond ±3σ.
- **Rule 2:** 2 out of 3 successive points beyond ±2σ (same side).
- **Rule 3:** 4 out of 5 successive points beyond ±1σ (same side).
- **Rule 4:** 8 successive points on the same side of the center line.
- **Rule 5:** 6 successive points steadily increasing or decreasing (trend).
- **Rule 6:** 15 successive points in Zone C (±1σ inner band) — stratification.
- **Rule 7:** 14 successive points alternating up and down — systematic variation.
- **Rule 8:** 8 successive points beyond ±1σ on either side — mixture.

If any of these patterns is observed, the affected points/subgroups must be investigated even if they are not outside the ±3σ limits.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Chart Type | Center Line (CL) | Upper Control Limit (UCL) | Lower Control Limit (LCL) | Constants Involved |
|---|------------|-----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\bar{X}$ (Mean) Chart | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ |
| 2 | $R$ (Range) Chart | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3, D_4$ |
| 3 | $s$ (Std. Dev.) Chart | $\bar{s}$ | $B_4 \bar{s}$ | $B_3 \bar{s}$ | $B_3, B_4$ |
| 4 | $p$ (Fraction Defective) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | — |
| 5 | $np$ (Number Defective) | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | — |
| 6 | $c$ (Defects/Unit) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | — |
| 7 | $u$ (Defects/Unit, variable n) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | — |
| 8 | Individuals ($X$) Chart | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + 3\dfrac{\bar{MR}}{d_2}$ | $\bar{X} - 3\dfrac{\bar{MR}}{d_2}$ | $d_2 = 1.128$ for $n=2$ |
| 9 | CUSUM / EWMA | Depends on design | Depends on $h, k$ | Depends on $h, k$ | Reference parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tabulated Constants (for samples of size $n$):** $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $B_3$, $B_4$, $d_2$ are all tabulated in KTU-approved SQC textbooks and are **independent of the process data**. They depend only on the subgroup size $n$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Revising control limits is the practical bridge between research/pilot-scale data and **full-scale manufacturing**. It is indispensable in:

- **Automotive Industry:** Finalizing torque, paint-thickness, and dimension limits after pilot runs.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Establishing validated process windows (PAT — Process Analytical Technology).
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Defining baseline control limits for Critical Dimension (CD) metrology.
- **Six Sigma / Lean Industry:** A non-negotiable step in the **DMAIC** *Measure* and *Analyze* phases (Green Belt & Black Belt certifications).
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Establishing pH, Brix, and fill-volume limits on packaging lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Trial & Revised Control Limits for an $\bar{X}$–$R$ Chart

### Setup
Consider a process where $m = 10$ preliminary samples, each of size $n = 5$, are drawn. The subgroup means ($\bar{X}_i$) and ranges ($R_i$) are computed as:

$$
\bar{X}_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}, \quad R_i = \max(X_{ij}) - \min(X_{ij})
$$

### Step A: Compute Trial (Initial) Control Limits

The **grand average** of subgroup means is the trial center line:

$$
