Students must compute statistics via `Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Explore` or use **Syntax** with `AGGREGATE` and `COMPUTE` commands, then build charts via `Graphs > Chart Builder`. ### 2.2.3 Acceptance Sampling Formulas (Module 4 Core) | Plan Type | Key Formulas | Excel/SPSS Function | |-----------|--------------|---------------------| | **Single Sampling** | $AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot N}{N}$ | `=NORMSDIST()` for $P_a$ | | **OC Curve** | $P_a = P(X \le c \mid p, n)$ | Binomial: `=BINOM.DIST()` | | **Double Sampling** | $P_{a1}, P_{a2}, P_{r1}, P_{r2}$ | Nested `IF` logic | | **AQL / LTPD / $\alpha, \beta$** | $n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | `=NORMSINV(1 - alpha/2)` | | **AOQL** | $AOQL = \max_{p} AOQ(p)$ | `=MAX()` over $p$ range | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing**: Real-time process monitoring on shop floors. - **Healthcare**: Tracking infection rates, surgical complication metrics. - **Software Industry**: Defect tracking using **p-charts** and **c-charts** (bugs per module). - **Banking**: Credit default monitoring using **u-charts** (variable exposure). - **Service Sector**: Customer complaint tracking. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Procedure: Constructing an X-bar and R Chart in Excel ### Problem Setup (Worked Foundation) Suppose a quality engineer collects **$k = 5$ subgroups** of size **$n = 4$** from a machining process. The measurements (in mm) are: | Subgroup ($i$) | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.3 | 10.0 | 10.15 | 0.3 | | 2 | 10.0 | 9.9 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.05 | 0.3 | | 3 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.0 | 10.1 | 10.15 | 0.3 | | 4 | 9.9 | 10.0 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 10.00 | 0.2 | | 5 | 10.1 | 10.2 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.20 | 0.2 | ### Step-by-Step Excel Workflow #### Step 1 — Enter Data Open Excel. In **Cells A1:E1**, type headers: `Subgroup, X1, X2, X3, X4`. Enter data in rows 2–6. #### Step 2 — Compute Subgroup Means ($\bar{X}_i$) In **Cell F2**, enter the formula: ``` =AVERAGE(B2:E2) ``` **Explanation:** The `AVERAGE` function sums the four measurements in row 2 and divides by 4. This yields the subgroup mean $\bar{X}_1 = 10.15$. Drag the formula down to **F6** to obtain all five subgroup means. #### Step 3 — Compute Subgroup Ranges ($R_i$) In **Cell G2**, enter: ``` =MAX(B2:E2) - MIN(B2:E2) ``` **Explanation:** The range is the **maximum minus the minimum** value within the subgroup. For subgroup 1, $R_1 = 10.3 - 10.0 = 0.3$. Drag to **G6**. #### Step 4 — Compute Grand Mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$) In **Cell H2**, enter: ``` =AVERAGE(F2:F6) ``` **Explanation:** The grand mean is the **average of the subgroup means**. This becomes the center line (CL) of the X-bar chart. #### Step 5 — Compute Average Range ($\bar{R}$) In **Cell H3**, enter: ``` =AVERAGE(G2:G6) ``` **Explanation:** The average range is the **mean of all subgroup ranges** and serves as the dispersion estimate. #### Step 6 — Compute Control Limits (X-bar Chart) For $n = 4$, the Shewhart factor $A_2 = 0.729$ (from the constants table). $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$ $$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$ In **Cell H4** (UCL): ``` = H2 + 0.729 * H3 ``` In **Cell H5** (LCL): ``` = H2 - 0.729 * H3 ``` #### Step 7 — Compute Control Limits (R Chart) For $n = 4$: $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$. $$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.282 \times 0.26 = 0.5933$$ $$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0$$ #### Step 8 — Numerical Evaluation Computing the grand mean: $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{10.15 + 10.05 + 10.15 + 10.00 + 10.20}{5} = \frac{50.55}{5} = 10.11 \text{ mm}$$ Computing the average range: $$\bar{R} = \frac{0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.2}{5} = \frac{1.3}{5} = 0.26 \text{ mm}$$ X-bar chart limits: $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.11 + (0.729)(0.26) = 10.11 + 0.1895 = 10.2995$$ $$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.11 - 0.1895 = 9.9205$$ R-chart limits: $$UCL_R = (2.282)(0.26) = 0.5933$$ $$LCL_R = (0)(0.26) = 0$$ #### Step 9 — Plot in Excel - Select subgroup numbers (A2:A6) and subgroup means (F2:F6). - Insert → **Scatter Plot with Smooth Lines**. - Add a **secondary line** for UCL (constant value 10.2995) and LCL (9.9205). - Add the center line CL = 10.11. #### Step 10 — Interpretation - All $\bar{X}_i$ values lie within $[9.9205, 10.2995]$ → **Process is in control**. - All $R_i$ values lie within $[0, 0.5933]$ → **Process variability is in control**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Procedure: Constructing a p-Chart in Excel ### Problem Setup A call center tracks customer complaints. **20 days** of data, each day **$n = 200$ calls** are sampled. Number of complaints per day: $[12, 15, 8, 10, 14, 9, 11, 13, 7, 16, 10, 12, 9, 11, 8, 13, 15, 10, 9, 11]$. ### Step-by-Step Solution #### Step 1 — Enter Data In **Column A**: Day (1–20). In **Column B**: Number of complaints ($d_i$). In **Column C**: Sample size ($n = 200$). #### Step 2 — Compute Fraction Defective ($p_i$) In **Cell D2**: ``` = B2 / C2 ``` Drag to **D21**. This gives $p_i = d_i / n$. #### Step 3 — Compute Average Fraction Defective ($\bar{p}$) In **Cell E2**: ``` = SUM(B2:B21) / SUM(C2:C21) ``` **Numerical evaluation:** $$\text{Total complaints} = 12+15+8+10+14+9+11+13+7+16+10+12+9+11+8+13+15+10+9+11 = 223$$ $$\text{Total sample size} = 200 \times 20 = 4000$$ $$\bar{p} = \frac{223}{4000} = 0.05575$$ #### Step 4 — Compute Control Limits The standard error of $p$ is: $$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05575 \times 0.94425}{200}}$$ $$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.052646}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0002632} = 0.01622$$ $$UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p = 0.05575 + 3(0.01622) = 0.05575 + 0.04867 = 0.10442$$ $$LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p = 0.05575 - 0.04867 = 0.00708$$ In **Cell E3** (UCL): ``` = E2 + 3 * SQRT(E2 * (1 - E2) / 200) ``` In **Cell E4** (LCL): ``` = MAX(0, E2 - 3 * SQRT(E2 * (1 - E2) / 200)) ``` > [!NOTE] > The `MAX(0, ...)` function ensures the LCL is **non-negative**, which is mathematically required for a proportion. This is a critical Excel safeguard. #### Step 5 — Plot Plot $p_i$ values against day number. Add horizontal reference lines for UCL, CL, and LCL. #### Step 6 — Interpretation - Highest $p_i = 16/200 = 0.08 < 0.10442$ → **in control**. - Lowest $p_i = 7/200 = 0.035 > 0.00708$ → **in control**. - **Conclusion:** The complaint rate is **stable**; no out-of-control signals. ## 3.3 Exhaustive Procedure: Single Sampling Plan Analysis in Excel ### Problem Statement A manufacturer uses a **single sampling plan** with $n = 100$, acceptance number $c = 2$. Lots are submitted with **$N = 1000$** items. Compute the **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** for $p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10$. ### Derivation The probability of acceptance follows the **Binomial distribution**: $$P_a = P(X \le c \mid n, p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$$ For $c = 2$, $n = 100$: $$P_a(p) = (1-p)^{100} + 100p(1-p)^{99} + \binom{100}{2}p^2(1-p)^{98}$$ ### Excel Implementation In **Cell A1**: $p$ value (0.01). In **Cell B1**: ``` = BINOM.DIST(0, 100, A1, TRUE) + BINOM.DIST(1, 100, A1, TRUE) + BINOM.DIST(2, 100, A1, TRUE) ``` > [!NOTE] > `BINOM.DIST(x, n, p, TRUE)` returns the **cumulative probability** $P(X \le x)$. Setting the 4th argument to `TRUE` makes it cumulative. We sum three cumulative calls (or use a single `BINOM.DIST(2, 100, p, TRUE)` since it's already cumulative up to 2). ### Numerical Evaluation Table | $p$ | $P_a = P(X \le 2 \mid 100, p)$ | AOQ = $P_a \cdot p$ | ATI = $n \cdot (1 + N \cdot P_a)$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0.01 | 0.920 | 0.0092 | 100(1 + 1000 × 0.920) = 92,100 | | 0.02 | 0.677 | 0.0135 | 100(1 + 1000 × 0.677) = 67,800 | | 0.03 | 0.423 | 0.0127 | 100(1 + 1000 × 0.423) = 42,400 | | 0.04 | 0.238 | 0.0095 | 100(1 + 1000 × 0.238) = 23,900 | | 0.05 | 0.118 | 0.0059 | 100(1 + 1000 × 0.118) = 11,900 | | 0.10 | 0.002 | 0.0002 | 100(1 + 1000 × 0.002) = 300 | ### OC Curve Construction in Excel - Create a column of $p$ values from 0.001 to 0.20 (use 0.001 increments). - Apply `=BINOM.DIST(2, 100, p, TRUE)` for each $p$. - Insert an **XY Scatter plot** with smooth lines. - The resulting curve is the **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve**. ## 3.4 SPSS Equivalent Procedures | Task | SPSS Menu Path | Syntax Equivalent | |------|----------------|-------------------| | Descriptive statistics | `Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Explore` | `EXAMINE VARIABLES=X BY Group /PLOT=HISTOGRAM.` | | Compute new variable | `Transform > Compute Variable` | `COMPUTE p_i = complaints / 200.` | | Build chart | `Graphs > Chart Builder > Line` | `GRAPH /LINE(MULTIPLE)=VALUE(p_i) BY Day.` | | Binomial probability | Not built-in; use `COMPUTE` | Requires custom syntax or hand calculation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** In SPSS, the `Chart Builder` does **not** automatically overlay control limits. Students must compute UCL/LCL as **new variables** using `Transform > Compute Variable` and then add them as **reference lines** to the chart. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Excel Control Chart Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Subgroup Data Input
Columns: X1, X2, X3, X4] --> B[Subgroup Statistic Engine
AVERAGE and MAX-MIN Functions] B --> C[Subgroup Means X-bar i
and Ranges R i] C --> D[Grand Average Engine
AVERAGE of Subgroup Statistics] D --> E[Compute X-double-bar
Grand Mean Center Line] D --> F[Compute R-bar
Average Range] E --> G[Shewhart Formula Application
UCL and LCL with A2 Factor] F --> H[Shewhart Formula Application
UCL and LCL with D3 and D4 Factors] G --> I[X-bar Control Chart
Scatter Plot with Limits] H --> J[R Control Chart
Scatter Plot with Limits] I --> K[Out-of-Control Detector
Identify Points Beyond Limits] J --> K K --> L[Process Capability Statement
In Control vs Out of Control] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: p-Chart Construction Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Attribute Data Input
Defect Counts d i and Sample Size n] --> B[Compute Fraction Defective
p i equals d i divided by n] B --> C[Compute Average p-bar
Total Defects divided by Total Sample] C --> D[Compute Standard Error
Square Root of p-bar times 1 minus p-bar divided by n] D --> E[Compute UCL p
p-bar plus 3 times Standard Error] D --> F[Compute LCL p
MAX of 0 and p-bar minus 3 times Standard Error] E --> G[p-Chart Plot
p i vs Sample Number with UCL, CL, LCL Lines] F --> G G --> H[Pattern Analysis
Runs, Trends, Out-of-Control Points] H --> I[Decision Output
Process Stable or Action Required] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Single Sampling Plan OC Curve Generation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define Plan Parameters
n equals sample size, c equals acceptance number] --> B[Define p Range
0.001 to 0.50 in steps of 0.001] B --> C[Apply Binomial CDF
BINOM.DIST function in Excel] C --> D[Generate Pa Values
Probability of Acceptance for each p] D --> E[Compute AOQ
Pa times p] D --> F[Compute ATI
n times 1 plus N times Pa] E --> G[Plot OC Curve
Pa vs p] F --> H[Plot ATI Curve
ATI vs p] G --> I[Identify Producer Risk alpha
Pa at p equals AQL] H --> J[Identify Consumer Risk beta
Pa at p equals LTPD] I --> K[Plan Validation Output
Acceptable Sampling Plan] J --> K ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Excel vs SPSS Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Statistical Quality Control Task] --> B{Data Volume?} B -->|Small to Medium
Under 10,000 rows| C{Visual Customization Needed?} B -->|Large
Over 10,000 rows| D[Use SPSS] C -->|Yes| E[Use Excel
Full Chart Control] C -->|No| F{Need Advanced Statistics?} F -->|Yes, ANOVA, Regression| D F -->|No, Basic SQC| E E --> G[Output: Custom Charts
PDF or Print Reports] D --> H[Output: Automated Reports
SPSS Viewer Format] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: List the Excel functions required to construct an X-bar control chart. Explain the role of the `AVERAGE` function in computing the center line.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The following Excel functions are required: 1. `=AVERAGE(range)` — Computes subgroup means ($\bar{X}_i$) and the grand mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$). 2. `=MAX(range) - MIN(range)` — Computes the range $R_i$ for each subgroup. 3. `=AVERAGE(range)` applied to the $R_i$ column — Computes $\bar{R}$. 4. Arithmetic operators — Compute $A_2 \bar{R}$ to determine UCL and LCL. **Role of AVERAGE function:** The `AVERAGE` function in Excel computes the **arithmetic mean** by summing all values in a specified range and dividing by the count of numerical entries. For the X-bar chart, it is used **twice**: first to compute each $\bar{X}_i$ from the raw subgroup data, and second to compute the **grand mean** $\bar{\bar{X}}$ from the column of subgroup means. The grand mean forms the **Center Line (CL)** of the X-bar chart, which represents the **process average** under statistical control. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Explain how SPSS can be used to plot a p-chart. What is the role of the `COMPUTE` command in this process?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** SPSS can be used to plot a p-chart through the following steps: 1. **Data Entry:** Enter defect counts ($d_i$) and sample sizes ($n_i$) as variables in the **Data View**. 2. **Compute New Variable:** Use `Transform > Compute Variable` or the syntax `COMPUTE p_i = d_i / n_i.` to create the fraction-defective variable. 3. **Compute Average:** Use `DESCRIPTIVES VARIABLES = p_i.` to obtain $\bar{p}$. 4. **Compute Control Limits:** Use `COMPUTE UCL = p_bar + 3 * SQRT(p_bar*(1-p_bar)/n).` and similarly for LCL. 5. **Plot:** Use `Graphs > Chart Builder > Line` to plot $p_i$ with UCL and LCL as reference lines. **Role of COMPUTE command:** The `COMPUTE` command in SPSS creates a **new variable** by performing a mathematical transformation on existing variables. In p-chart construction, it is essential for computing $p_i$, $\bar{p}$, UCL, and LCL, since these intermediate quantities do not exist in the raw data and must be derived. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting the Grand Mean Step:** Many students compute $\bar{X}_i$ correctly but use the **first subgroup's mean** as the center line. This is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. > 2. **Wrong Shewhart Factor:** Using $A_2 = 1.880$ (for $n=2$) when $n=4$ is a frequent error. **Always check the constants table** for the correct $n$. > 3. **LCL Negativity:** For p-charts, LCL can be negative. Failing to set `MAX(0, LCL)` in Excel results in an **invalid limit**. Deduct **2 marks**. > 4. **OC Curve Interpretation:** KTU expects students to identify **$\alpha$ (Producer's Risk)** and **$\beta$ (Consumer's Risk)** on the OC curve. Generic descriptions score poorly. --- ### **Part B — Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) **A manufacturing process produces shafts with a target diameter of 25.00 mm. The quality engineer collects 6 subgroups of size $n = 4$ from the process. The data are as follows:** | Subgroup | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 25.1 | 24.9 | 25.0 | 25.2 | | 2 | 25.0 | 25.1 | 24.8 | 25.0 | | 3 | 25.2 | 25.0 | 25.1 | 25.3 | | 4 | 24.9 | 24.8 | 25.0 | 24.9 | | 5 | 25.1 | 25.2 | 25.0 | 25.1 | | 6 | 25.0 | 25.1 | 25.2 | 25.0 | **(a) Construct an X-bar and R control chart in Excel. Compute all control limits and verify whether the process is in statistical control. [7 Marks]** **(b) The engineer wants to introduce an acceptance sampling plan with $n = 50$ and $c = 1$. Compute the probability of acceptance $P_a$ at $p = 0.01, 0.02,$ and $0.05$ using Excel. Plot the OC curve. [7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part A(a): X-bar and R Chart [7 Marks] **Step 1: Compute Subgroup Means $\bar{X}_i$** $$\bar{X}_1 = \frac{25.1 + 24.9 + 25.0 + 25.2}{4} = \frac{100.2}{4} = 25.05$$ $$\bar{X}_2 = \frac{25.0 + 25.1 + 24.8 + 25.0}{4} = \frac{99.9}{4} = 24.975$$ $$\bar{X}_3 = \frac{25.2 + 25.0 + 25.1 + 25.3}{4} = \frac{100.6}{4} = 25.15$$ $$\bar{X}_4 = \frac{24.9 + 24.8 + 25.0 + 24.9}{4} = \frac{99.6}{4} = 24.90$$ $$\bar{X}_5 = \frac{25.1 + 25.2 + 25.0 + 25.1}{4} = \frac{100.4}{4} = 25.10$$ $$\bar{X}_6 = \frac{25.0 + 25.1 + 25.2 + 25.0}{4} = \frac{100.3}{4} = 25.075$$ **Step 2: Compute Subgroup Ranges $R_i$** $$R_1 = 25.2 - 24.9 = 0.3$$ $$R_2 = 25.1 - 24.8 = 0.3$$ $$R_3 = 25.3 - 25.0 = 0.3$$ $$R_4 = 25.0 - 24.8 = 0.2$$ $$R_5 = 25.2 - 25.0 = 0.2$$ $$R_6 = 25.2 - 25.0 = 0.2$$ **Step 3: Compute Grand Mean and Average Range** $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{25.05 + 24.975 + 25.15 + 24.90 + 25.10 + 25.075}{6} = \frac{150.25}{6} = 25.0417$$ $$\bar{R} = \frac{0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.2}{6} = \frac{1.5}{6} = 0.25$$ **Step 4: Control Limits for X-bar Chart (using $A_2 = 0.729$ for $n = 4$)** $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.0417 + (0.729)(0.25) = 25.0417 + 0.1823 = 25.224$$ $$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.0417 - 0.1823 = 24.859$$ **Step 5: Control Limits for R Chart (using $D_3 = 0, D_4 = 2.282$ for $n = 4$)** $$UCL_R = (2.282)(0.25) = 0.5705$$ $$LCL_R = (0)(0.25) = 0$$ **Step 6: Interpretation** - All $\bar{X}_i$ values lie in $[24.859, 25.224]$ ✓ - All $R_i$ values lie in $[0, 0.5705]$ ✓ - **Process is in statistical control.** **Excel Formulas Used:** - $\bar{X}_i$: `=AVERAGE(B2:E2)` - $R_i$: `=MAX(B2:E2) - MIN(B2:E2)` - $\bar{\bar{X}}$: `=AVERAGE(F2:F7)` - $\bar{R}$: `=AVERAGE(G2:G7)` - $UCL_{\bar{X}}$: `=H2 + 0.729 * H3` --- #### Model Solution — Part A(b): OC Curve [7 Marks] **Step 1: Probability of Acceptance Formula** For a single sampling plan with $n = 50$, $c = 1$: $$P_a = P(X \le 1) = (1-p)^{50} + 50p(1-p)^{49}$$ **Step 2: Excel Formula** `=BINOM.DIST(1, 50, p, TRUE)` for cumulative probability. **Step 3: Numerical Evaluation** | $p$ | Excel Formula | $P_a$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0.01 | `=BINOM.DIST(1,50,0.01,TRUE)` | 0.9104 | | 0.02 | `=BINOM.DIST(1,50,0.02,TRUE)` | 0.7358 | | 0.05 | `=BINOM.DIST(1,50,0.05,TRUE)` | 0.2794 | **Step 4: Detailed Calculation for $p = 0.01$** $$(1 - 0.01)^{50} = (0.99)^{50} = 0.6050$$ $$50 \times 0.01 \times (0.99)^{49} = 0.5 \times 0.6112 = 0.3056$$ $$P_a(0.01) = 0.6050 + 0.3056 = 0.9106$$ **Step 5: Detailed Calculation for $p = 0.02$** $$(0.98)^{50} = 0.3642$$ $$50 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{49} = 1.0 \times 0.3716 = 0.3716$$ $$P_a(0.02) = 0.3642 + 0.3716 = 0.7358$$ **Step 6: Detailed Calculation for $p = 0.05$** $$(0.95)^{50} = 0.0769$$ $$50 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{49} = 2.5 \times 0.0810 = 0.2024$$ $$P_a(0.05) = 0.0769 + 0.2024 = 0.2794$$ **Step 7: OC Curve Construction** - In Excel, create a column of $p$ values from **0.001 to 0.20** in steps of 0.001. - Apply `=BINOM.DIST(1, 50, A2, TRUE)` for each $p$. - Insert **XY Scatter with Smooth Lines** chart. - The resulting curve is the OC curve. **Step 8: Risk Identification** - If AQL = 0.01: $\alpha = 1 - P_a(0.01) = 1 - 0.9106 = 0.0894$ (Producer's Risk). - If LTPD = 0.05: $\beta = P_a(0.05) = 0.2794$ (Consumer's Risk). --- ### **Part B — Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice** **A textile company inspects fabric rolls for weaving defects. The quality manager collects 15 days of data, with 100 m inspected per day. The number of defects per day is:** $$[4, 6, 3, 5, 7, 4, 5, 6, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 4, 5]$$ **(a) Using Excel, construct a c-chart and a u-chart for the data. Determine the control limits and comment on the process stability. [7 Marks]** **(b) Using SPSS (or Excel), construct a p-chart assuming a constant sample size of $n = 50$ and the following defective counts per day: $[2, 4, 1, 3, 5, 2, 0, 4, 1, 3]$. Identify any out-of-control signals. [7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part B(a): c-chart and u-chart [7 Marks] **Step 1: c-chart Setup** The c-chart applies when the **opportunity for defects is constant**. Here, each day 100 m is inspected, so $n_i$ is constant. **Step 2: Compute Average Defects $\bar{c}$** $$\bar{c} = \frac{4+6+3+5+7+4+5+6+3+4+5+6+7+4+5}{15} = \frac{74}{15} = 4.933$$ **Step 3: Control Limits for c-chart** $$UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4.933 + 3\sqrt{4.933} = 4.933 + 3(2.221) = 4.933 + 6.662 = 11.595$$ $$LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4.933 - 6.662 = -1.729 \rightarrow \text{Set to } 0$$ **Step 4: Excel Formulas for c-chart** - $\bar{c}$: `=AVERAGE(B2:B16)` - $UCL_c$: `=H2 + 3*SQRT(H2)` - $LCL_c$: `=MAX(0, H2 - 3*SQRT(H2))` **Step 5: u-chart Setup** The u-chart allows **variable sample sizes**. Here $n_i$ is constant at 100 m, but the formula generalizes: $$UCL_u = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$$ Since $n = 100$: $$\bar{u} = \frac{\bar{c}}{n} = \frac{4.933}{100} = 0.04933 \text{ defects/m}$$ **Step 6: Control Limits for u-chart** $$UCL_u = 0.04933 + 3\sqrt{\frac{0.04933}{100}} = 0.04933 + 3(0.02221) = 0.04933 + 0.06664 = 0.11597$$ $$LCL_u = 0.04933 - 0.06664 = -0.01731 \rightarrow \text{Set to } 0$$ **Step 7: Interpretation** - All $c_i$ values (max = 7) are below $UCL_c = 11.595$ ✓ - All $c_i$ values (min = 3) are above $LCL_c = 0$ ✓ - **The c-chart indicates the process is in statistical control.** --- #### Model Solution — Part B(b): p-chart with $n = 50$ [7 Marks] **Step 1: Data Entry in Excel/SPSS** | Day | Defects ($d_i$) | Sample Size ($n$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 2 | 50 | | 2 | 4 | 50 | | 3 | 1 | 50 | | 4 | 3 | 50 | | 5 | 5 | 50 | | 6 | 2 | 50 | | 7 | 0 | 50 | | 8 | 4 | 50 | | 9 | 1 | 50 | | 10 | 3 | 50 | **Step 2: Compute Fraction Defective $p_i$** $$p_1 = 2/50 = 0.04, \quad p_2 = 0.08, \quad p_3 = 0.02, \quad p_4 = 0.06$$ $$p_5 = 0.10, \quad p_6 = 0.04, \quad p_7 = 0.00, \quad p_8 = 0.08$$ $$p_9 = 0.02, \quad p_{10} = 0.06$$ **Step 3: Compute $\bar{p}$** $$\text{Total defects} = 2+4+1+3+5+2+0+4+1+3 = 25$$ $$\text{Total sample} = 50 \times 10 = 500$$ $$\bar{p} = \frac{25}{500} = 0.05$$ **Step 4: Compute Control Limits** $$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 0.95}{50}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0475}{50}} = \sqrt{0.00095} = 0.03082$$ $$UCL_p = 0.05 + 3(0.03082) = 0.05 + 0.0925 = 0.1425$$ $$LCL_p = \text{MAX}\left(0, 0.05 - 0.0925\right) = 0$$ **Step 5: Excel Implementation** - $p_i$: `=B2/C2` - $\bar{p}$: `=SUM(B2:B11)/SUM(C2:C11)` - $UCL_p$: `=E2 + 3*SQRT(E2*(1-E2)/50)` - $LCL_p$: `=MAX(0, E2 - 3*SQRT(E2*(1-E2)/50))` **Step 6: SPSS Syntax Equivalent** ``` COMPUTE p_i = d_i / 50. EXECUTE. DESCRIPTIVES VARIABLES = p_i /STATISTICS = MEAN. COMPUTE UCL = 0.05 + 3 * SQRT(0.05 * 0.95 / 50). COMPUTE LCL = MAX(0, 0.05 - 3 * SQRT(0.05 * 0.95 / 50)). GRAPH /LINE(MULTIPLE) = VALUE(p_i UCL LCL) BY Day. ``` **Step 7: Interpretation** - All $p_i$ values: max = 0.10 < $UCL_p = 0.1425$ ✓ - All $p_i$ values: min = 0.00 ≥ $LCL_p = 0$ ✓ - **No out-of-control signals; process is in statistical control.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** ### Core Excel Functions - `=AVERAGE(range)` — Grand mean and subgroup means - `=STDEV.S(range)` — Sample standard deviation - `=MAX(range) - MIN(range)` — Range $R_i$ - `=COUNTIF(range, criteria) / n` — Fraction defective $p_i$ - `=BINOM.DIST(x, n, p, TRUE)` — Binomial cumulative probability (OC curve) - `=NORM.S.INV(1 - alpha/2)` — Z-score for confidence intervals - `=SQRT(value)` — Standard error - `=MAX(0, value)` — Non-negative LCL safeguard ### Core SPSS Procedures - `Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Explore` — Distribution analysis - `Transform > Compute Variable` — New variable creation - `Graphs > Chart Builder` — Chart construction - `Analyze > Quality Control` (if available) — Direct SQC tools ### Control Chart Selection Logic - **Continuous data, constant $n$** → X-bar and R chart - **Continuous data, large $n$** → X-bar and S chart - **Attribute data, variable $n$** → p-chart - **Attribute data, constant $n$** → np-chart - **Defects per unit, constant $n$** → c-chart - **Defects per unit, variable $n$** → u-chart ### Critical Pitfalls to Avoid 1. **Always use the grand mean** ($\bar{\bar{X}}$), not the first subgroup mean, as the center line. 2. **Match Shewhart factors** ($A_2, D_3, D_4$) to the **subgroup size $n$**. 3. **Set LCL to zero** for p-charts and c-charts when the calculated value is negative. 4. **OC curve interpretation** requires explicit identification of $\alpha$ (Producer's Risk) and $\beta$ (Consumer's Risk). 5. **SPSS** does not auto-overlay control limits — they must be computed as separate variables and added as reference lines. ### Sampling Plan Decision Rule - Use **single sampling** for simplicity and small lot sizes. - Use **double sampling** when the cost of inspection is high. - Use **sequential sampling** for maximum information per inspection. - Always validate the plan by computing the OC curve before implementation.
